objHaz: icefall from the upper mountain, worst in the afternoon sun
routes.face: Coleman Headwall, left of the Roman Nose (north side)
routes.overview: Arguably the most complex route regularly climbed on Mount Baker: a sustained two-tool ice climb up the north-side headwall, mostly 45-50 degrees with short steeper steps, considered a Cascade classic.
routes.beta: From around 8,500 ft, climb predominantly 45-50 degree snow and ice with occasional short steep sections (up to 70 degrees, near-vertical); the mid-route follows either an aesthetic ice arete or long moderate slopes broken by big crevasses, with terrain kicking back to 55+ degrees near the top. Up to 14 pitches depending on conditions and line chosen.
routes.approach: Via the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead and the Coleman Glacier, angling toward the base of the headwall left of the Roman Nose feature at roughly 8,500 ft.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the Coleman-Deming route
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Coleman-Deming line (Roman Wall, Deming and Coleman Glaciers) back to Heliotrope Ridge camp; the headwall itself is technically skiable but has produced fatalities and is not a standard descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike in and camp near 6,000-7,000 ft below Heliotrope Ridge. Day 2: Very early alpine start, climb the headwall (8-12 hrs) to the summit, descend via Coleman-Deming.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once committed to the ice pitches; below the schrund/lower slopes, downclimbing or rappelling on ice screws back toward the Coleman Glacier is the main option.
routes.turnaround: Given serious icefall exposure that worsens through the day, commit to a conservative turnaround and prioritize being off the route before the sun softens the upper mountain.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed spring through early summer (roughly April-June); guides advise against the right-hand line beyond July 1 as melt-out increases objective hazard.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the route; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: 2 ice tools suitable for placing in snow anchors, 2-3 pickets, 3-4 ice screws (more if climbing later in the season), slings and carabiners, glacier rope and crevasse-rescue kit.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets protect the moderate ice slopes and crevasse crossings; a rack of screws increases with later-season firmer ice.
whatToBring: extra ice screws for late-season ice
proTips: Start very early to be off the route before the sun begins shedding ice from the upper mountain
The right-hand line is the standard, safer route with less objective hazard; the left-hand line's first half is threatened by icefall
watchOut: Even marble-sized falling ice can knock a climber off steep terrain
The route has produced fatalities when skied or descended
knownHazards: Icefall from the upper mountain, worst in the afternoon sun
Crevasses and bergschrunds mid-route

objHaz: overhanging bergschrund below the headwall (30-50 ft high in places)
routes.overview: A long, remote headwall route on Baker's northeast flank, with a route-finding crux around large crevasses before ice pitches gain the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Climbers work through numerous large crevasses on the approach glacier, then gain the face of the right-hand ridge and climb roughly three 50m ice pitches (up to about 60 degrees, with an overhanging schrund to negotiate) to the ridge crest, which is followed to the summit.
routes.approach: From Artist Point (end of Mt. Baker Highway/SR-542), hike the Ptarmigan Ridge Trail about 5 miles past Camp Kiser; drop down on snow to the climber's right below the ridgeline and traverse to a rocky saddle to gain the ridge's left side.
routes.descent: Descend via the Coleman-Deming route
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the standard Coleman-Deming line down to Heliotrope Ridge, which requires a long traverse back to the Ptarmigan Ridge approach or a car shuttle/second vehicle.
routes.itinerary: Given the long approach, plan 2 long days minimum (3 for a more leisurely pace): Day 1 hike in via Ptarmigan Ridge to a camp near Camp Kiser or below the headwall; Day 2 (or 3) climb the headwall and descend.
routes.bail: Retreat down the approach glacier before committing to the headwall pitches; above the schrund, downclimbing or rappelling the ice pitches on screws may be necessary.
routes.turnaround: Given the remote, long approach and crevasse route-finding, set a conservative turnaround time and avoid getting caught on the headwall in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed early season (roughly June) before the approach glacier becomes heavily crevassed and broken up later in summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this remote route; a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Ice tools, ice screws, pickets, a 50m rope, standard glacier gear, helmet and crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws protect the headwall's ~60-degree ice pitches and schrund crossing; pickets protect the approach glacier.
whatToBring: extra food/fuel for a 2-3 day itinerary
wands for the long, featureless approach glacier
proTips: This is a long route for Mount Baker; plan on 2 long days minimum, 3 for a more leisurely pace
Climb early season before the glacier opens up
watchOut: Numerous large crevasses make route-finding the crux of the climb
The schrund below the headwall can be 30-50 ft high and overhanging
knownHazards: Crevasse fall hazard on the approach glacier
Icefall/rockfall on the headwall

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse a steep snow/ice ramp, cross a narrow snow ledge, then make difficult moves to surmount the schrund lip
Three roughly 50m ice pitches gain the ridge crest, which is followed to the summit

routes.fa: Joe Morovits and party, July 2, 1894
routes.face: Boulder Glacier / Boulder-Park Cleaver, east side
routes.overview: A quieter, moderate east-side glacier route that avoids the crowds of the Coleman-Deming and Easton routes, following the cleaver between the Boulder and Park Glaciers.
routes.beta: From camp, ascend the Boulder Glacier alongside the Boulder-Park Cleaver, negotiating crevasses and a bergschrund; the cleaver itself is largely crevasse-free and offers good bivy sites with North Cascades views.
routes.approach: From Baker Lake Highway (FR 11), pass Boulder Creek Campground and turn onto FR 1130, then FR 1131, to the Boulder Ridge Trailhead. Hike the Boulder Ridge Trail (#605), including a short rock scramble, about 2 miles to Boulder Glacier basecamp (~5,500-5,700 ft); some parties continue to a sheltered camp at ~7,500 ft below the cleaver.
routes.descent: Same route (Boulder Glacier / cleaver)
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line down the cleaver and Boulder Glacier to basecamp, then the Boulder Ridge Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Boulder Ridge Trailhead, hike in to basecamp (~5,500-7,500 ft). Day 2: Alpine start, climb the Boulder Glacier/cleaver to the summit (5-7 hrs from camp), descend and hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down the cleaver or glacier is straightforward below the 9,300 ft buttress; avoid lingering under the buttress on the way down.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before leaving camp based on weather, party pace, and remaining daylight.
routes.bestSeason: Early-to-mid summer (June-July) is favored; by mid-to-late summer crevasse terrain becomes more broken.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the glacier or summit plateau; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier rope, one picket per person, crevasse-rescue kit, ice axe, crampons, helmet.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and crevasse-rescue gear are the main protection concern; largely crevasse-free on the cleaver itself.
whatToBring: extra layers for an exposed, less-traveled route
proTips: A good moderate route that avoids the crowds found on the Easton or Coleman-Deming routes
Excellent bivy sites along the cleaver with North Cascades views
watchOut: Rockfall while traversing below the 9,300 ft buttress, or on the direct line north of it
Helmets highly recommended
knownHazards: Rockfall near the 9,300 ft buttress
Crevasse rescue hazard in the upper glacier

objHaz: extremely loose rock in the wrong season
bergschrunds and crevasses in the icefall section

routes.face: Cockscomb Ridge, north side (between the Coleman/Roosevelt and Park/Rainbow Glaciers)
routes.overview: A long, moderate rock-and-ice ridge climb up Baker's north side, bypassing junky towers and bergschrunds via ramps within an icefall before following the ridge crest to the summit.
routes.beta: After gaining the ridge near 7,500 ft (roughly 2-3 hours of glacier travel), climb moderate snow bypassing a pinnacle on the right, navigate loose rock and bergschrunds via low-to-moderate-angle ramps within the icefall, pass the Cockscomb itself on the left via steep snow, then continue to the summit. Later in the season parties may need to drop back to the Park Glacier and climb its headwall (AI3, several screws) instead.
routes.approach: From Heliotrope Ridge, cross the Coleman and Roosevelt Glaciers on low-to-moderate-angle snow to gain the ridge around 7,500 ft.
routes.descent: Descend via the Coleman-Deming route (easy-to-moderate glacier)
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the standard Coleman-Deming line down the Roman Wall and Deming/Coleman Glaciers to Heliotrope Ridge and the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike in to a Heliotrope Ridge-area camp. Day 2: Alpine start, cross the Coleman/Roosevelt Glaciers to gain the ridge, climb Cockscomb Ridge to the summit, descend via Coleman-Deming.
routes.bail: Retreat back down the ridge and glacier crossing to Heliotrope Ridge is the most direct option below the crux towers.
routes.turnaround: Given loose rock and icefall sections, set a conservative turnaround and avoid the ridge in warm, late-day conditions when rockfall risk increases.
routes.bestSeason: Best in spring or early summer, when the loose rock sections are still snow-covered and more stable; conditions become notably looser and less secure later in the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this remote route; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: A few ice screws and pickets, standard glacier gear, plus ice tools if a Park Glacier headwall (AI3) variation is needed late season.
routes.proNeeds: Screws and pickets protect the icefall and bergschrund sections; helmet essential for the loose rock towers.
gear: ice tools (for possible Park Glacier headwall option, AI3)
whatToBring: helmet for rockfall on loose towers
extra layers for a long, exposed ridge day
proTips: Time the climb for spring/early summer before the rock dries out and becomes loose
Bypass the Cockscomb tower itself on the left via steep snow
watchOut: Insanely loose and sketchy rock in the wrong season
Multiple bergschrunds and crevasses demand careful route-finding
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose terrain on the ridge
Bergschrund/crevasse crossings in the icefall section

routes.corrections: No reliable source confirms a first-ascent date for the full Cockscomb Ridge; AAC records note a 1906 Mazama attempt that reached the Rainbow Glacier below Cockscomb Tower, and a full ridge ascent later by a party including Chuck Murley and John Musser, but the exact year was not found in available sources, so 'fa' is left null rather than guessed.

blurb: Glacier Peak (Dakobed) is Washington's most remote stratovolcano, rising to roughly 10,540 ft deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, ringed by the White Chuck, Suiattle, and Sauk River drainages. Built from andesite-to-dacite lava domes and pyroclastic deposits over roughly the last 300,000 years, it is mantled by more than a dozen named glaciers and reached only by long wilderness-trail approaches, with no road coming within many miles of the mountain. It was first climbed in June 1897 by a USGS topographic survey party — Thomas Gerdine, Sam Strom, A.H. Dubor, and Darcy Bard — who reportedly summited a sub-peak in fog before the true summit was revealed.

routes.fa: Darcy Bard, A.H. Dubor, Thomas Gerdine, and Sam Strom (USGS survey party) — June 1897, first ascent of Glacier Peak
objHaz: heavily crevassed lower/mid glacier requiring route-finding around seracs
icy sections on the glacier tongue
rockfall/loose pumice near the summit ridge
long, exposed wilderness approach with no cell coverage
washed-out access road adding extra approach mileage
routes.face: Northeast side, via Boulder Basin and Sitkum Ridge
routes.overview: The historic and most popular route on Glacier Peak: a long wilderness approach to Boulder Basin followed by an ascent of the moderately-to-heavily crevassed Sitkum Glacier on the mountain's northeast side, finishing on the pumice Sitkum Ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: From Boulder Basin, head SE onto the Sitkum Glacier and climb a corridor on its left (climber's) side. The lower glacier and the tongue connecting the lower and upper glacier are heavily crevassed and largely bare ice — expect to weave and re-route repeatedly. Continue climbing NE to a saddle near 9,500 ft just east of Sitkum Spire, then follow the pumice Sitkum Ridge (Class 2-3) to the summit block via a snow chute.
routes.approach: From Darrington, the historic approach used the White Chuck River Road (FR 23) to the White Chuck trailhead; hike ~5.5 mi to Kennedy Hot Springs (~3,300 ft) on well-maintained trail through forest, continue ~1.5 mi to the PCT junction near Sitkum Creek, then take the climbers' path steeply up to Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), the standard base camp, roughly 9-10 miles from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down Sitkum Ridge and back down the Sitkum Glacier corridor to Boulder Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the pumice ridge SW to the saddle east of Sitkum Spire, then downclimb/glissade the established corridor on the Sitkum Glacier's climber's-left side back to Boulder Basin, staying well clear of the crevassed tongue in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-day trip: Day 1 hike in to Boulder Basin; Day 2 alpine start for the glacier climb and summit, return to camp; Day 3 hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same glacier corridor to Boulder Basin is the only realistic bail option; do not attempt to descend the crevassed tongue in whiteout — wait for visibility to improve.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before departing camp based on forecast weather and party pace, and stick to it — afternoon cloud buildup and cross-glacier navigation in whiteout are the primary reasons parties get into trouble low on this peak.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is typical, once the White Chuck approach trail has melted out and crevasses on the glacier are more predictably bridged or open (rather than snow-hidden); early-season attempts require more snow-travel skill but face fewer open-crevasse routefinding problems.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boulder Basin or on the Sitkum Glacier; this is a deep wilderness route and a satellite communicator/inReach is strongly advised.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: ice axe, crampons, harness, rope (one per rope team), 2-3 pickets or ice screws per team for crevasse rescue and running belays, prusiks/mechanical ascenders, and a basic crevasse-rescue kit (pulleys, carabiners, cordage).
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection; carry pickets for glacier running belays and crevasse-rescue anchors.
proTips: The lower glacier tongue is largely bare ice by mid-late summer — expect to route-find around open crevasses rather than follow a fixed line.
Boulder Basin is the standard, scenic base camp with reliable water.
watchOut: Crevasse danger increases through the summer as snow bridges deteriorate.

The historic White Chuck River Road approach has been repeatedly washed out since 2003 flooding, adding significant extra hiking distance — verify current road status before relying on this trailhead.
knownHazards: Open crevasses on the lower and mid glacier by mid-to-late season.
Loose pumice/rockfall on the final ridge to the summit block.
Long, remote approach with no rescue nearby.

road.name: White Chuck River Road (FR 23)
road.status: closed
road.driveNote: Damaged by flooding since 2003 and further impacted by a December 2025 storm; Forest Service closure orders have shut sections of FR 23 (through at least Dec 31, 2025, with ongoing damage reported into 2026). Confirm current status with the Darrington Ranger District before planning around this trailhead — the closure can add several extra miles of road-walking.

climate.typical: High alpine glaciated-volcano climate; the peak generates its own localized weather, and thunderstorms/rapid changes are common in summer.

bySeason.spring: Approach trails typically snowbound into June; avalanche/cornice hazard on steeper slopes.
bySeason.summer: Jul-Sep is prime climbing season; glaciers are most negotiable but crevasses open progressively through the summer.
bySeason.fall: Early snow and post-storm road/trail washouts become more likely by October.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible for climbing — deep snow and unplowed, often-damaged approach roads.

routes.corrections: Route elevation for the summit is cited variably as 10,525-10,568 ft across sources (Wikipedia ~10,525+ ft; USGS 3,214 m/10,544 ft; Mountain Project/Topozone 10,541 ft; listsofjohn 10,549 ft); the given 10,550 ft falls within this common range. Mountain Project's 'Sitkum Glacier' page labels the route 'Grade III' while The Mountaineers/Willhite describe it as a 'Grade II' Basic Alpine Climb — this entry uses Grade II per the majority of sources.

routes.rockGrade: 3rd-4th class (occasional 5th class moves reported on variant lines)
objHaz: significant rockfall — the loose rock face below Disappointment Peak sheds early in the day
glacier crevasses on the Suiattle and Cool Glaciers
long approach with route-finding on scree/moraine
routes.face: South side, via Suiattle Glacier col and the Disappointment Cleaver rock rib below Disappointment Peak
routes.overview: A south-side route that climbs the rock rib (cleaver) below Disappointment Peak from a col on the Suiattle Glacier, traverses under Disappointment Peak, and joins the Cool Glacier col before finishing up the summit ridge — commonly descended via the Cool Glacier/Gerdine Ridge line.
routes.beta: From a col at ~7,300 ft on the edge of the Suiattle Glacier, descend ~100 ft to the nose of the cleaver. When melted out there is a climbers' trail on the cleaver's right side; ascend until it steepens near 8,600 ft, then make a rising traverse on the glacier under Disappointment Peak (moving quickly — this slope sheds rock as it warms), arcing up and left to steepening snow slopes and the col adjoining the Cool Glacier at ~9,100 ft. Continue NNW on snow or pumice, then take an obvious moderate slope straight up to the summit ridge for a final 200-300 ft.
routes.approach: From Darrington, drive the Mountain Loop Highway then Sloan Creek Road (FR 49) to the North Fork Sauk River Trailhead (~2,050 ft). Hike the North Fork Sauk Trail through old growth ~3.5 mi to Red Creek, then ~1.5 mi to Mackinaw Shelter (good camping), then climb steeply ~3 mi to the PCT junction (~6,000 ft) and continue toward White Pass/Glacier Gap to reach the Suiattle Glacier approach col.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the Cool Glacier/Gerdine Ridge route rather than reversing the cleaver.
routes.descentText: From the col at the top of the Cool Glacier, descend the Cool Glacier and Gerdine Ridge south back toward Glacier Gap and the White Chuck Basin/Foam Creek approach, or reverse the ascent line back to the Suiattle Glacier col if conditions on Cool Glacier are poor.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-4 day trip using the North Fork Sauk approach: 1-2 days hiking in and establishing high camp, a summit day, and 1 day hiking out.
routes.bail: Retreat to the Suiattle Glacier col and back down toward the PCT is the primary bail option; avoid lingering under the loose rock face below Disappointment Peak on retreat.
routes.turnaround: Because the traverse below Disappointment Peak is an active rockfall zone that worsens as the day warms, set and honor an early turnaround time so that traverse is crossed while still cold/frozen.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, once the North Fork Sauk trail and PCT approach have melted out; the rock-fall hazard below Disappointment Peak is worst in warm afternoon conditions regardless of season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the approach or route; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons, and standard glacier travel/crevasse-rescue kit (rope, pickets, prusiks) for the Suiattle/Cool Glacier sections; helmet strongly recommended for the cleaver traverse.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection; pickets/screws for glacier sections, helmet mandatory given rockfall.
proTips: Move fast under the loose rock face below Disappointment Peak — locals note it 'breaks off early in the day.'
Many parties climb up the cleaver and descend via Cool Glacier as a loop.
watchOut: Rockfall on the traverse under Disappointment Peak — do not linger.
Crevasses on the Suiattle and Cool Glaciers require standard glacier travel precautions.
knownHazards: Active rockfall zone below Disappointment Peak.
Loose, unstable rock throughout the cleaver.
Crevassed glacier sections on approach and descent.

Currently the most reliable driving approach into Glacier Peak Wilderness, though 2025-26 storm damage has left overall wilderness access reported as 'extremely limited' — call the Darrington Ranger District, (360) 436-1155, for current conditions.

climate.typical: High alpine glaciated-volcano climate with localized weather and frequent summer thunderstorm risk.

bySeason.spring: Trail typically snowbound into June; avalanche hazard on steeper slopes.
bySeason.summer: Jul-Sep primary season; rockfall below Disappointment Peak worsens with afternoon warming.
bySeason.fall: Early snow and possible storm damage to trail/road by October.
bySeason.winter: Not a viable climbing season.

routes.corrections: No route-specific first-ascent party is documented for this named line (distinct from the 1897 peak first ascent, which historical sources associate with the Sitkum Glacier side); fa left null rather than reusing the peak's overall FA.

routes.rockGrade: 4th class (some sources describe occasional harder moves in variable conditions)
routes.alpineGrade: AD-/PD+ (multiple sources rate this Grade II, easier than the AD given here)
objHaz: steep snow/ice near the 'Rabbit Ears' feature
long, committing approach with difficult retreat options
routes.season: Jun-Aug (early-season snow climb; becomes more of an ice route late season)
routes.face: North side, via the Kennedy Glacier saddle and the Frostbite/Rabbit Ears ridge crest
routes.overview: A classic, more technical line up Glacier Peak's north side: approach via the Kennedy Glacier to a saddle below Frostbite Ridge, then follow the corniced ridge crest over/through the 'Rabbit Ears' rock notch and up steep snow or alpine ice to the summit area, most often descended via the Cool Glacier/Gerdine Ridge.
routes.beta: From the North Fork Sauk/PCT approach, ascend the Kennedy Glacier (a few small crevasses) to an ~8,800 ft saddle between the Kennedy and Ermine Glaciers. From there Frostbite Ridge is obvious for roughly 1,000 ft; running belays with pickets are advisable on steeper sections as you climb pumice or snow up the ridge to the 'Rabbit Ears' rock feature. Climb through the Rabbit Ears and descend the far side (Class 3) to a saddle, then climb ~150 ft of steep snow (up to ~45°) to the false summit, with 1-2 pitches of low-angle alpine ice possible depending on conditions.
routes.approach: Historically via Suiattle River Road to Sulfur Creek Campground, the Suiattle Pass Trail (~7 mi) to a Suiattle River bridge, then the PCT south (~9 mi) to the East Fork Milk Creek basin; alternatively via the North Fork Sauk Trail and PCT north ~19-20 mi to Glacier Ridge, with a climbers' path departing the PCT near Glacier Creek toward the Ptarmigan Glacier and Kennedy Peak.
routes.descent: Standard descent is via the Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route on the south side rather than reversing Frostbite Ridge.
routes.descentText: From near the summit, descend south onto the Cool Glacier and down Gerdine Ridge toward Glacier Gap, then out via the White Chuck Basin/Foam Creek or North Fork Sauk approach depending on which trailhead the party used.
routes.itinerary: Most parties take 2-3 days; total mileage for an up-Frostbite/down-Cool-Glacier loop is slightly over 40 miles.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once committed above the Kennedy Glacier saddle; downclimbing the ridge back to the saddle and Kennedy Glacier is the main option — this is considered a long, committing approach with limited bail options.
routes.turnaround: Given the committing nature of the ridge and the descent-by-a-different-route norm, set a conservative turnaround based on weather and rope-team pace before starting up from the Kennedy Glacier saddle.
routes.bestSeason: Late June-August as an early-season snow climb (Basic Alpine Climb difficulty); by late season the route becomes more of an ice climb (Intermediate difficulty) as snow melts to reveal ice on the ridge's steeper sections.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this remote north-side approach; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Rope, pickets, ice screws, and optional small-to-medium nuts for belays on the ridge and Rabbit Ears sections; standard glacier kit (ice axe, crampons, harness, crevasse-rescue gear) for the Kennedy Glacier approach.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and ice screws for running belays on the ridge; small-to-medium nuts optional for the Rabbit Ears rock step.
whatToBring: Lightweight trail runners for the long hiking approach, swapped for mountaineering boots at the glacier
GPS/waypoints for Glacier Gap and route junctions
proTips: Total round-trip mileage (up Frostbite, down Cool Glacier) runs slightly over 40 miles — plan food/fuel accordingly.
Save GPS waypoints, particularly for Glacier Gap, given the featureless glacier terrain in poor visibility.
watchOut: The ridge is corniced and the rock crumbling — stay off cornices and test holds.
Ice conditions on the upper ridge vary significantly by season; what is a snow climb early season becomes real AI1-2 ice climbing late season.
knownHazards: Corniced ridge crest with crumbling rock.
Crevasses on the Kennedy and Vista Glaciers.
Long, committing route with limited retreat options once above the saddle.

bySeason.spring: Approach trails snowbound; avalanche hazard.
bySeason.summer: Jun-Aug is prime season, transitioning from snow to ice climbing conditions as summer progresses.
bySeason.fall: Early snow returns ice/mixed conditions; approach roads prone to storm damage.
bySeason.winter: Not viable for climbing.

routes.overview: This catalog entry's name conflates two distinct, separately-documented Glacier Peak routes and could not be matched to a single named line in any source — see corrections.
routes.turnaround: Whichever route this entry actually represents, standard practice on Glacier Peak is to set and honor a pre-departure turnaround time given the long glaciated approaches and afternoon weather/rockfall risk common to all its routes.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep across all standard Glacier Peak routes.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage anywhere on Glacier Peak's approaches; satellite communicator advised.
watchOut: This entry's name pairs 'Disappointment Cleaver' (a distinct south-side route, see wa_glacier_peak_disappointment_peak_cleaver) with 'Sitkum Glacier' (a distinct northeast-side route, see wa_glacier_peak_sitkum_glacier) — these are two different lines on opposite sides of the mountain, not one route.

routes.corrections: This entry appears to be a data-quality duplicate/conflation: 'Disappointment Cleaver' is a real, distinct south-side Glacier Peak route (already captured as wa_glacier_peak_disappointment_peak_cleaver) and 'Sitkum Glacier' is a real, distinct northeast-side route (already captured as wa_glacier_peak_sitkum_glacier) — no source describes a single combined 'Disappointment Cleaver / Sitkum Glacier' line, and the two named routes do not share a common line or the 1897 FA (historical accounts of the 1897 ascent are generally associated with the peak's easier glacier lines, not specifically a route called 'Disappointment Cleaver'). Recommend merging or removing this entry rather than treating it as a sixth distinct route; left most fields null/generic pending that cleanup.

objHaz: glacier crevasses on the Suiattle and Cool Glaciers
loose rock on the alternative South Ridge/Disappointment Peak variant
routes.face: South side, via Glacier Gap and Gerdine Ridge
routes.overview: The current standard south-side route: from Glacier Gap, follow Gerdine Ridge north, traverse onto the upper Suiattle Glacier and then the Cool Glacier, and finish on the summit ridge past Disappointment Peak — widely considered Glacier Peak's easiest and most popular line as of recent years.
routes.beta: From Glacier Gap (~7,300 ft), ascend north, side-hilling the left slope then the right slope below Glacier Gap Peak (7,739 ft), before regaining the ridge. Continue along the ridge to ~8,400 ft, traverse right (NE) onto the upper Suiattle Glacier, pass below cliffs, and reach the upper Cool Glacier around 9,200 ft. Ascend the Cool Glacier westward to regain the ridge at its top, then follow the ridge north on scree or snow past Disappointment Peak to the summit. An alternative avoids glacier travel by ascending the South Ridge directly to Disappointment Peak (9,755 ft) before dropping slightly to the saddle above the Cool Glacier.
routes.approach: As of 2023 the most common approach is the North Fork Sauk Trail (FR 49 trailhead) north on the PCT to White Pass and into the White Chuck Basin toward Glacier Gap; an older approach uses the PCT from White Pass via the Foam Creek Trail (~2 mi) and a saddle at ~6,600 ft into the White Chuck Glacier basin to reach Glacier Gap.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the Cool Glacier and Gerdine Ridge back to Glacier Gap.
routes.descentText: From the summit ridge, descend south past Disappointment Peak, down the Cool Glacier, traverse back onto the upper Suiattle Glacier, and follow Gerdine Ridge south to Glacier Gap.
routes.itinerary: Typically 3-4 days via the North Fork Sauk approach: 1-2 days in to high camp near Glacier Gap/White Chuck Basin, a summit day, and a day out.
routes.bail: Retreat down Gerdine Ridge to Glacier Gap is straightforward in good visibility; the rock face below Disappointment Peak sheds rock early in the day, so avoid lingering there on retreat.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before leaving high camp; the rockfall zone near Disappointment Peak and the glacier crossings are the main reasons to stay disciplined about timing.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep; later in the season the Cool/Suiattle Glacier crossing becomes more broken up, making the South Ridge variant to Disappointment Peak an attractive glacier-free alternative.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this route; satellite communicator advised.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe and crampons required; standard glacier gear (rope, harness, pickets/screws, crevasse-rescue kit) for the Suiattle/Cool Glacier crossings.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection; pickets/screws for glacier travel and crevasse rescue.
proTips: Much of the rock in this area is loose — the face below Disappointment Peak breaks off early in the day, so move quickly through that section.
The South Ridge direct variant to Disappointment Peak lets teams skip glacier travel late season.
watchOut: Rockfall below Disappointment Peak, especially as the day warms.
Crevasses on the upper Suiattle and Cool Glaciers.
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout the Glacier Gap-Disappointment Peak area.
Crevassed glacier crossings on the Suiattle and Cool Glaciers.

pitchDetail.notes: Side-hilling below Glacier Gap Peak, regaining Gerdine Ridge

routes.face: North side, ascending the Kennedy Glacier directly from the Kennedy Creek/Glacier Creek approach
routes.overview: A north-side glacier route that climbs the Kennedy Glacier directly, typically approached from the North Fork Sauk/PCT corridor via a moraine bivy near the old Kennedy Glacier terminal moraine; often combined with or used as an approach variant for the neighboring Frostbite Ridge route.
routes.beta: From the moraine bivy area (~6,100 ft), drop down to the toe of the Kennedy Glacier. Climbers move over a mix of rock, sand, and boulders on and along the ice for roughly 2 hours to about 7,500 ft, then rope up and put on crampons to ascend the bare glacier ice, which has open crevasses requiring careful route-finding.
routes.approach: Via the North Fork Sauk Trail (#649) north on the PCT; a climber's cutoff trail departs the PCT just before crossing Glacier Creek, ascending scree and bushy slopes onto the crest of the old Kennedy Glacier moraine, where there are good bivy sites around 6,100 ft, before dropping down to the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the Kennedy Glacier to the moraine bivy, or exit via the neighboring Frostbite Ridge/Cool Glacier route depending on conditions and party plan.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the Kennedy Glacier's ascent line back to the moraine crest bivy area, then retrace the scree/bushy-slope cutoff back to the PCT and North Fork Sauk Trail.
routes.itinerary: Typically 2-3 days, consistent with other Glacier Peak north-side routes.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same glacier line to the moraine bivy is the main option; avoid descending in whiteout given open crevasses.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround based on rope-team pace across the crevassed glacier ice and forecast weather before leaving the moraine bivy.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Aug, when the Kennedy Creek log crossing and PCT cutoff trail are passable and before late-season ice becomes too broken up.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; satellite communicator advised.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons, rope, harness, pickets/ice screws, and a crevasse-rescue kit for the bare-ice glacier travel.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets/screws for glacier running belays and crevasse-rescue anchors on open bare ice.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the rock/sand/boulder section before roping up
proTips: The log crossing of Kennedy Creek may be washed out depending on recent conditions — have a wading/alternate-crossing plan.
The old Kennedy Glacier moraine crest around 6,100 ft has good bivy sites and views before dropping to the glacier.
watchOut: The glacier is bare ice with open crevasses by mid-summer — crevasse-rescue readiness is essential.
Kennedy Creek crossing conditions vary and may require route-finding around a washed-out log.
knownHazards: Open crevasses on the Kennedy Glacier.
Loose scree/moraine terrain on approach.
Seasonal creek-crossing hazard at Kennedy Creek.

blurb: Mount St. Helens (8,325 ft) is an active stratovolcano in the Cascade Range of Skamania County, Washington, about 50 miles northeast of Portland, Oregon, built mainly of dacite and andesite lavas over roughly the last 40,000 years. Once a symmetrical cone nicknamed the "Fujiyama of America," its catastrophic May 18, 1980 lateral eruption removed about 1,300 feet of the summit and created the mile-wide crater climbers now skirt, with renewed lava-dome growth occurring from 2004–2008. The mountain's first recorded ascent was by Thomas J. Dryer's party on August 26, 1853, and today it is climbed year-round on two established non-technical routes.

routes.overview: The standard summer line up Mount St. Helens' south flank: a forested approach trail to timberline, then an exposed climb along a rocky, wooden-post-marked ridge and volcanic ash slope to the crater rim.
routes.beta: Monitor Ridge is a non-technical scramble that gains about 4,500 ft in 5 miles one-way from Climber's Bivouac. It starts on Ptarmigan Trail #216A through forest to timberline (4,800 ft), then follows Monitor Ridge over blocky lava boulders (passing beneath Monitor Peak, 5,994 ft) with wooden-post route markers to about 7,000 ft. The final ~1,300 ft is unmarked, loose rock/pumice/ash nicknamed the 'Vertical Beach.' Most parties finish the round trip in 7–12 hours. Early-season (through roughly mid-July) the route can be largely snow-covered and requires an ice axe and crampons/microspikes; by August it is typically dry trail, boulders, and ash.
routes.approach: From Cougar, WA, drive east on Forest Road 90 about 1 mile past Swift Dam, turn left (north) on FR 83, continue to the FR 81 junction, turn left, drive 1.6 miles, then turn right on FR 830 to the Climber's Bivouac trailhead (3,700 ft) — the highest vehicle access on the mountain.
routes.descent: Descend the same way (Monitor Ridge to the Ptarmigan Trail); many parties glissade snowfields below the ridge with an ice axe in early season.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line: follow the wooden posts back down Monitor Ridge to timberline, then Ptarmigan Trail #216A back to Climber's Bivouac. Most serious accidents occur while glissading — descend only on soft snow that lets your heels dig in, keep the ice axe ready for self-arrest, remove crampons before glissading, and watch for glissade chutes that diverge from the main route or run into rock/boulder fields, since it is easy to descend an unintended drainage in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: Single-day car-to-car climb: pre-dawn start from Climber's Bivouac, ~1.5–2 hr to timberline, several hours up the boulder field/ash slope to the crater rim, brief time at the summit, then 3–4 hr descent, for a typical 7–12 hr round trip.
routes.bail: There is no technical descent required anywhere on the route — retreat is simply reversing the ridge and posts back down to timberline and the Ptarmigan Trail from any point.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before you leave and honor it: descend if weather or visibility deteriorates, winds and wind chill become dangerous, thunderstorms threaten the exposed upper ridge, or any member of the party is moving too slowly to safely complete the round trip in daylight.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions are mid-July through August/September, when the route is typically dry trail, boulders, and ash rather than snow. Climber's Bivouac usually opens by late June or July depending on snowpack (it has opened as early as June and as late as mid-July in recent years), and the route generally remains climbable into October before snow returns; outside that window climbers use the Worm Flows winter route instead.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable on the drive in and through the forested lower trail; several trip reports note intermittent-to-good reception (especially Verizon) above treeline and on the summit, but do not rely on a phone for emergencies — carry a satellite communicator/PLB or leave a detailed trip plan with a contact.
objHaz: rockfall and loose volcanic rock/ash underfoot
high wind and rapid weather changes
knownHazards: The crater rim drops over 1,000 feet to the crater floor and its edges are unstable, crumbly, and prone to sudden collapse — sources direct climbers to stay at least 30 ft back from the visible edge.
Most serious accidents on the mountain happen when climbers glissade or slide on snowfields and are unable to self-arrest.
Fog and cloud can drop visibility from good to under 10 feet within minutes above treeline, and new or blowing snow can erase footprints in a few minutes.
watchOut: Do not approach or stand on the crater rim edge — it is corniced/undercut and has caused fatalities.
Carry and know how to use an ice axe for early-season snow; most injuries stem from uncontrolled glissades.
GPS or a downloaded track is strongly recommended — many people get off-route on the descent in the boulder field or in cloud.
proTips: Start pre-dawn to beat both the heat/thunderstorm risk and the daily climber quota crowding.
Trekking poles are considered close to essential for the ash/sand 'Vertical Beach' section on both ascent and descent.
Gaiters keep ash, pumice, and snow out of boots and help on the loose upper slope.
Bring 3+ liters of water — there is little to no reliable water on route except early-season snowmelt.
routes.detailedRack: No technical protection is used. Bring an ice axe and crampons or aggressive microspikes for the seasonal snow/ice that typically persists into early-to-mid July; trekking poles for the ash/boulder terrain; sturdy ankle-supportive boots; and gaiters.
routes.proNeeds: None — this is a non-technical scramble with no roped climbing. Ice axe/crampons serve as self-arrest and traction tools on seasonal snow and ice, not placed protection.
whatToBring: layered synthetic clothing with waterproof shell
map/compass or GPS with route track loaded
waste pack-out bag (human waste must be packed out on the upper route)

road.name: Forest Roads 90 / 83 / 81 / 830
road.status: Seasonal — snow-closed in winter/spring, typically opens as snowpack allows
road.seasonalGate: Climber's Bivouac access via FR 81-830 is generally inaccessible until the road is plowed/melted out, often not until June or July
road.driveNote: From Cougar: FR 90 east ~1 mi past Swift Dam, left on FR 83, north to the FR 81 junction, left 1.6 mi, then right on FR 830 to the Climber's Bivouac trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Zone 8 (South Washington Cascades, west of crest)
climate.typical: Alpine conditions persist even in summer — expect cold, wind, and rapidly changing weather at the crater rim regardless of valley forecasts; use a summit-elevation forecast (e.g., Mountain-Forecast.com or NWS point forecast for ~6,000-8,000 ft), not the town forecast.

bySeason.spring: Still largely winter conditions; use the Worm Flows route.
bySeason.summer: Best weather window; still subject to afternoon wind and thunderstorm buildup at elevation.
bySeason.fall: Shortening daylight and returning snow by October; conditions revert toward winter route requirements.
bySeason.winter: Climber's Bivouac and Monitor Ridge are snow-bound; climbers use the Worm Flows winter route instead.

access.landManager: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument, Gifford Pinchot National Forest (USDA Forest Service)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day (or valid Northwest Forest Pass) at Climber's Bivouac; waived if a valid climbing permit is displayed
access.permit: Climbing permit required year-round above 4,800 ft. April 1–November 30: purchased online via Recreation.gov, $15/person plus a $6 transaction fee, released monthly. December 1–March 31: free, self-issued at the trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee (summer) plus a climbing permit above 4,800 ft
access.closures: Climbing is confined to designated Closure Area #2; daily quotas of 350 climbers (Apr 1–May 14) and 110 climbers (May 15–Nov 30); group size capped at 12 per permit; drones prohibited above 4,500 ft.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (roughly 4–6 a.m.) to allow the full 7–12 hr round trip, beat afternoon wind/thunderstorm buildup, and reduce exposure to daily crowding on the quota-limited route.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Climber's Bivouac (3,700') to timberline/Loowit Trail junction (4,800')
sectionBreakdown.note: Ptarmigan Trail #216A, 2.25 mi through forest; toilet at the Loowit Trail crossing.
Follows Monitor Ridge over blocky lava boulders, marked by large wooden posts.
Unmarked, loose rock/pumice/ash — steepest and most tiring section.

waypoints.name: Mount St. Helens Summit / Crater Rim

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skamania County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 427-9490; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument office, 42218 NE Yale Bridge Rd, Amboy, WA 98601, (360) 449-7800
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through much of the route (varies by carrier); do not depend on a phone to call for help. Leave a written trip plan and expected return time with a contact.

routes.corrections: Elevation-gain and distance figures vary slightly by source: WTA lists 10 mi round trip / 4,500 ft gain to an 8,365 ft summit; USFS lists 5 mi one-way / 4,500 ft gain; Mount St. Helens Institute lists ~9 mi round trip / 4,598 ft gain to 8,363 ft. Current summit elevation per the 2009 USGS LiDAR survey is roughly 8,325-8,330 ft (matching the catalog's 8,325 ft) rather than the older 8,363-8,365 ft figure still quoted by most trail guides, reflecting post-1980 crater-rim erosion/collapse since earlier surveys.

routes.overview: The standard winter/spring line up Mount St. Helens, following the Swift Creek drainage past Chocolate Falls and then a lava-flow ridge on the mountain's south-southeast flank to the crater rim; the default route whenever the summer Climber's Bivouac road is snow-closed.
routes.beta: Worm Flows is a non-technical snow climb of about 12 miles round trip from Marble Mountain Sno-Park, gaining roughly 5,500-5,700 ft to the crater rim. It follows the Swift Ski Trail #244 through forest, crosses to the west side of Swift Creek above Chocolate Falls (3,700 ft), then climbs open slopes and an old lava-flow ridge (the 'worm' flows for which the route is named) past a seismic station near 6,200 ft to the crater rim. It requires an ice axe, crampons, and avalanche awareness; skis with skins or snowshoes are strongly recommended. Round trip typically takes 7-10 hours, though many winter parties report longer days.
routes.approach: From Cougar, WA, drive east on Lewis River Road (becomes Forest Road 90). At the FR 90/FR 83 junction, turn left onto FR 83 and continue about 6 miles to Marble Mountain Sno-Park (FR 83 is gated at the Sno-Park in winter and early spring).
routes.descent: Descend the same line back down the lava-flow ridge to Chocolate Falls and the Swift Ski Trail to Marble Mountain Sno-Park; glissading on suitable soft snow is common on the lower slopes.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent ridge, staying off wind-loaded slopes and gully bottoms. Above ~5,600 ft, whiteout can drop visibility to under 10 feet in minutes and new/blowing snow erases footprints quickly, so a GPS track loaded ahead of time is strongly advised rather than relying on your ascent tracks. Glissade only on soft snow where your heels can dig in — never on hard snow or ice — and watch for melting glissade chutes that cross rock, holes, or boulder fields, or that diverge from the correct descent line.
routes.itinerary: Single-day car-to-car climb: early start from Marble Mountain Sno-Park, ~2 mi of forest travel to Chocolate Falls/timberline, then several hours up the open ridge and snowfields to the crater rim, descend the same line, for a typical 7-10+ hour round trip depending on snow conditions.
routes.bail: No technical descent is required — retreat at any point simply by reversing the ridge/snowfield line back down to Chocolate Falls and the Swift Ski Trail to the Sno-Park; in whiteout, staying on a pre-loaded GPS track is the safest way to avoid dropping into the wrong drainage.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the NWAC avalanche danger is elevated, winds exceed roughly 25 mph, precipitation or a wind chill below about 15°F is forecast, or visibility deteriorates — descending your ascent line before conditions worsen is safer than pushing for the rim.
routes.bestSeason: This is the go-to route whenever the summer Climber's Bivouac road is snow-closed, roughly December through June. Big avalanches are possible on Mount St. Helens any time from October through May, with the highest risk after major storms, on wind-loaded slopes, during rain-on-snow events, and on warm sunny days — check the NWAC forecast (Zone 8) before every climb. Ideal weather is sunshine, winds under about 15 mph, and temperatures above 25°F.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through the forested approach and much of the route; do not rely on a phone for emergency communication — carry a satellite communicator/PLB or leave a detailed trip plan with a contact.
objHaz: unstable crater-rim cornices extending 20-30 ft over the crater with overhangs up to 10 ft in winter
whiteout/loss of visibility and navigation error above ~5,600 ft
falls while glissading or on steep icy slopes
hypothermia from cold, wind, and wet conditions
knownHazards: The crater edge is a cliff exceeding 1,000 feet above the Crater Glacier; in winter, cornices extend 20-30 feet out over the crater with potential 10-foot overhangs, and cornice collapses have caused fatalities.
Mount St. Helens can produce large avalanches any time from October through May; risk is highest following major storms, on wind-loaded snow, during rain-on-snow events, and on warm sunny days.

In winter fog and snow, visibility can drop from good to under 10 feet within minutes, and new or blowing snow can erase your own footprints in a few minutes, making the ascent track unreliable for the descent.
watchOut: Never walk out onto the crater-rim cornice, even though it may look solid — collapses have killed climbers.
Steep, icy slopes (this route and the east aspect of neighboring Monitor Ridge in particular) create serious fall/slide hazard; carry and know how to use an ice axe for self-arrest.
Spring and early-summer skiing/glissading are a leading cause of injury on the mountain — descend only on soft snow and stop before reaching rocks or boulder fields.
proTips: Check the NWAC avalanche forecast (Zone 8) before every climb, not just the trailhead weather.

Use summit-elevation weather sources (e.g., Mountain-Forecast.com or an NWS point forecast around 6,000-8,000 ft) rather than valley forecasts, and expect winter forecasts to change dramatically within 24 hours.
Bring an avalanche beacon, shovel, and probe (with training) for winter travel.
Arrive early on nice winter weekends — the Marble Mountain Sno-Park lots fill quickly — and park diagonally in the lot center rather than at the edges.
routes.detailedRack: No roped protection is used. Bring snowshoes or skis with climbing skins, true crampons fitted to stiff boots, a mountaineering ice axe, and avalanche safety gear (beacon, shovel, probe) with training to use them; adjustable trekking/ski poles.
routes.proNeeds: None — non-technical snow climb with no placed protection. Ice axe self-arrest technique and crampons/traction are the primary safety tools on icy sections; avalanche gear is recommended for any travel off the main ridge crest.
gear: snowshoes or skis with climbing skins
map/compass or GPS with route track loaded
whatToBring: layered wool/synthetic clothing with a breathable waterproof shell
warm waterproof gloves or mittens plus a spare pair
close-fitting sunglasses or goggles for snow glare and blowing snow
thermos with hot food/drink and high-calorie snacks
Washington State Sno-Park permit for the vehicle (Dec 1-Mar 31)

road.name: Forest Road 90 to Forest Road 83
road.status: Plowed by Washington State Dec-Mar; occasional Oct/Nov/Apr snowstorms may close it temporarily
road.seasonalGate: FR 83 is gated at Marble Mountain Sno-Park in winter and early spring, making the Sno-Park the effective winter trailhead
road.driveNote: From Cougar: Lewis River Road/FR 90 east to the FR 83 junction, left on FR 83, ~6 miles to Marble Mountain Sno-Park. Chains or traction tires are needed on the steep, icy final stretch.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Zone 8 (South Washington Cascades, west of crest)
climate.typical: Full winter mountaineering conditions are possible at any point in this route's Dec-Jun season — cold, wind, whiteout, and avalanche hazard are all in play; use a summit-elevation forecast and the NWAC avalanche advisory rather than the valley weather.

bySeason.spring: Prime Worm Flows season with lengthening days but still significant avalanche and cornice hazard, especially after warm/sunny spells or rain-on-snow.
bySeason.summer: Route is normally abandoned once Climber's Bivouac/Monitor Ridge opens (roughly June/July).
bySeason.fall: Snow returns and Worm Flows becomes the default route again by roughly November/December.
bySeason.winter: Coldest, shortest-daylight conditions; avalanche danger is highest after storms and wind-loading events.

access.landManager: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument, Gifford Pinchot National Forest (USDA Forest Service)
access.fees: Washington State Sno-Park Permit required at Marble Mountain Sno-Park Dec 1-Mar 31 ($22/day or $40/seasonal, purchased in advance, not at the trailhead)
access.permit: Climbing permit required year-round above 4,800 ft. Dec 1-Mar 31: free, self-issued at the Marble Mountain Sno-Park kiosk. Apr 1-Nov 30: purchased online via Recreation.gov, $15/person plus a $6 transaction fee.
access.passRequired: Washington State Sno-Park permit (winter) plus a climbing permit above 4,800 ft
access.closures: Climbing confined to designated Closure Area #2; group size capped at 12 per permit; drones prohibited above 4,500 ft.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start recommended given the shorter winter daylight window and to allow time to reassess conditions/turn around before afternoon weather or whiteout sets in.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Marble Mountain Sno-Park (~2,800') to Chocolate Falls/timberline (3,700')
sectionBreakdown.note: Swift Ski Trail #244; cross to the west side of Swift Creek above Chocolate Falls.
Follows ridges/open slopes on the old Worm Flows lava; wooden route markers to ~4,800 ft.
Navigation above ~5,600 ft is the crux in poor visibility; watch for cornices at the rim.

waypoints.name: Mount St. Helens Summit / Crater Rim

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skamania County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 427-9490; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument office, 42218 NE Yale Bridge Rd, Amboy, WA 98601, (360) 449-7800
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable through the forested approach and much of the route; do not depend on a phone to call for help. Skamania County EMS & Rescue can also be reached at (509) 427-5065. Leave a written trip plan and expected return time with a contact.

routes.corrections: Round-trip elevation-gain figures vary by source: WTA lists 5,699 ft gain over a 12-mi round trip; Mount St. Helens Institute lists 5,563 ft gain over ~10 mi round trip; USFS describes it as 'nearly 5,700 ft.' This entry uses WTA's more granular trail-database figure. Summit elevation also varies across sources (8,363-8,365 ft in most trail guides vs. ~8,325-8,330 ft per the 2009 USGS LiDAR survey, matching the catalog value used here) due to post-1980 crater-rim erosion since older surveys.

blurb: Mount Stuart is the second-highest non-volcanic peak in Washington and the highest point in the Stuart Range, rising within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Cascades between Leavenworth and the Teanaway valley. It is a single massive granitic pluton (the Late Cretaceous Mount Stuart batholith) whose extensive jointing produces unusually clean, sound granite on its ridges and faces, with the south face rising nearly 5,000 feet over about two horizontal miles and three glaciers (Stuart, Ice Cliff, and Sherpa) occupying the north slopes. A wooden stick inscribed "Angus McPherson—1873" found on the summit suggests an earlier ascent, but the first confirmed ascent was by Frank Tweedy and Richard Goode in August 1883.

routes.overview: The standard, mostly non-technical route up Mount Stuart from the south: a broad couloir/gully system climbed from the Ingalls Creek basin to the false summit (~9,200'), followed by class 2-3 scrambling along the summit ridge to the true summit (9,415').
routes.beta: The Cascadian Couloir has become the standard route from the south and is probably the most-climbed line on the peak, giving scramblers and climbers non-technical access to the summit. It is class 2-3 when snow-free; in early season the upper section holds steep, often corniced snow. From the false summit, scramble east along the left side of the ridge through broken rock (class 2-3) to the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda Trailhead (4,290 ft, end of FR-9737 in the North Fork Teanaway), climb east to Longs Pass (6,210 ft) or northeast to Ingalls Pass (~6,500 ft). Descend/contour into the Ingalls Creek drainage at about 4,800 ft and pick up one of the gully lines leading into the Cascadian Couloir, ascending on a bearing of roughly 348° toward Sherpa Col or the false summit.
routes.descent: Nearly all Mount Stuart routes descend the Cascadian Couloir back to the Ingalls Creek basin and out over Longs Pass or Ingalls Pass to the Esmeralda Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, reverse the class 2-3 ridge scramble west to the false summit, then descend the broad couloir/gully system to the southeast (heavily corniced early season), following cairns where present down to the Ingalls Creek basin, then climb back over Longs Pass or Ingalls Pass to the Esmeralda Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in one very long day (documented example ~13 hrs) by fit parties, or as a 2-day trip with a camp in the Ingalls Creek basin or near Longs Pass.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the couloir at almost any point; the main hazards on bail are loose rock and, in early season, steep snow — down-climb facing in on snow sections.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets the party off the upper couloir and summit ridge before typical Cascades afternoon thunderstorms build, and before other parties above start kicking down rockfall later in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. An ice axe (and often crampons) is commonly recommended into July because snow lingers in the couloir; by August the couloir is usually snow-free but loose and rocky. Snowy years can hold snow later into the season.
whatToBring: Extra water (limited sources high on route)
Map/compass or GPS for the ambiguous upper couloir and ridge
proTips: Scope the couloir and false-summit ridge from Longs Pass before committing — route-finding through the broken rock between the false and true summit is much easier seen from a distance.
Start early to avoid other parties kicking rock down the couloir and to beat afternoon thunderstorms.
watchOut: Rockfall is the single biggest hazard on this route, much of it climber-triggered.
Steep, often corniced snow near the false summit lingers into early summer.
Loose rock and ambiguous route-finding between the false and true summits.
knownHazards: Rockfall from parties climbing above in the couloir
Steep, occasionally corniced snow early season
Afternoon thunderstorms common over the Cascade crest in summer
routes.fa: Not a first-ascent line itself; the peak's presumed early ascent is marked by a wooden stick inscribed "Angus McPherson—1873" found at the summit, and the first confirmed ascent was by Frank Tweedy and Richard Goode, August 1883.
routes.corrections: The supplied FA text conflated the peak's overall first-ascent history with this specific route; the Cascadian Couloir itself only became the accepted "standard" line later and was not necessarily the 1873/1883 line of ascent.

road.name: Forest Road 9737 (North Fork Teanaway Road)
road.status: Gravel, seasonal
road.seasonalGate: Typically not snow-free until mid-to-late May; can be affected by wildfire-season closures in dry years
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum: WA-970 east ~8.5 mi, left on North Fork Teanaway Rd ~13 mi (paved), then FR-9737 ~9.6 mi (gravel) to the Esmeralda Trailhead (4,290 ft).

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Central Cascades
climate.typical: Warmer, drier summers than the west-slope Cascades; afternoon thunderstorms common July-August

bySeason.spring: Snow lingers in the couloir into June; access road may still be snowed in
bySeason.summer: Warm and mostly dry with possible afternoon convective storms
bySeason.fall: Cooling but generally stable; early snow possible by October
bySeason.winter: Deep snow; road generally inaccessible

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest / Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required to park at the Esmeralda Trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: North Fork Teanaway roads/trails have had temporary wildfire closures in past fire seasons — check current Okanogan-Wenatchee NF alerts

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start for a single-day push

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Esmeralda TH to Ingalls Creek basin/camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Longs Pass or Ingalls Pass
Via Cascadian Couloir and false-summit ridge

emergency.county: Kittitas County (Teanaway approach); the summit area is near the Chelan/Kittitas county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff/Kittcom non-emergency (509) 925-8534; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee NF (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg (Level IV trauma); Central Washington Hospital, Wenatchee (509) 662-1511 for higher-level trauma
emergency.notes: Rescue response on this side is typically coordinated through Kittitas County SAR; cell coverage is unreliable in the Ingalls Creek basin and couloir.

routes.overview: A Grade IV alpine rock climb following the jagged North Ridge crest for roughly 11 pitches of low/mid 5th class to the Great Gendarme, then two crux 5.9 pitches over the gendarme, finishing with easier climbing to the summit — one of Fifty Classic Climbs of North America.
routes.beta: From the notch above Stuart Glacier, about 11 pitches of low/mid-5th class climbing (one move of 5.7) lead to the base of the Great Gendarme. The gendarme itself is the technical crux: a steep fingers-and-hands dihedral (5.9) followed by an offwidth pitch (5.8-5.9, harder than the first). Above the gendarme, roughly 3 more low-5th pitches lead to the summit. Total route length is commonly cited around 18 pitches from the notch, though older sources describing the full route from the lower buttresses give ~3,000 ft and 30+ pitches.
routes.approach: Two main approaches: (1) from the Longs Pass Trailhead via Ingalls Lake and Goat Pass, then traversing Stuart Glacier to the notch marking the start of the ridge; or (2) via Mountaineer Creek from the Stuart Lake Trailhead into the basin below Stuart and Ice Cliff Glaciers.
routes.descent: Standard descent is the Cascadian Couloir.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend east toward the false summit through blocky class 2-3 terrain, then follow cairns to a large snowfield and descend the Cascadian Couloir extensively down to the Ingalls Creek basin.
routes.itinerary: A long single day for fast parties (documented 9-11 hrs for the upper ridge including descent), or more commonly a 1-2 night trip with a bivy near the base of the ridge or on route.
routes.bail: Rappel/downclimb options exist along the ridge crest before the Gendarme; above the Gendarme, retreat is more involved and continuing to the summit and descending Cascadian is often the fastest way off.
routes.turnaround: Given the exposed, committing nature of the upper ridge and the Gendarme's belayed pitches, set a turnaround time that allows completing the gendarme and reaching the summit ridge well before dark or incoming weather.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep; best conditions are typically July-September once approach snow has consolidated or melted from the glacier/notch area.
gear: Rack to ~3.5 in (a wide piece is useful for the offwidth)
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light boots for scrambling sections
Bivy gear if not doing it in a single push
Extra water — little to no water on the ridge itself
proTips: Much of the low/mid-5th terrain is commonly simul-climbed to save time; belay the Gendarme's two 5.9 pitches.
A 3.5" (or optional #4) cam is considered essential for the offwidth pitch on the Gendarme.
watchOut: Exposed, sustained ridge climbing with limited retreat options once past the Gendarme.
The Gendarme pitches can be wet or icy depending on conditions and time of day.
Loose rock in sections, particularly on variations off the main crest.
knownHazards: Extended commitment/exposure on the upper ridge
Afternoon thunderstorms
Wet/icy conditions on the Gendarme pitches in shoulder season
routes.fa: North Ridge established by John Rupley & Don Gordon, 1956 (original line, skirting the Great Gendarme via a gully to the North Ridge Notch).
routes.corrections: Sources disagree on who first completed the Gendarme direct to make the "Complete"/"Direct" North Ridge: Mountain Project credits James Wickwire & Fred Stanley (though other sources put a related Wickwire/Stanley aid variation, the Direct North Buttress, in 1965); a separate source credits Fred Beckey & Steve Marts (1963) with the first ascent of the upper North Ridge; and another credits Mead Harris & Jay Ossiander with the direct/complete variation roughly seven years after 1963 (~1970). The given data's attribution of the complete route solely to Rupley/Gordon 1956 is not well supported — that pair established only the original (Gendarme-skirting) line.

pitchDetail.notes: Ridge-crest climbing on solid granite to the base of the Great Gendarme
Steep fingers-and-hands dihedral with good rests
Offwidth crack, harder than P1
~3 easier pitches to the true summit

road.name: FR-9737 (Longs Pass TH) or Icicle Rd/FR-7601 (Stuart Lake TH)
road.status: Gravel, seasonal
road.seasonalGate: Both approaches generally snow-free by June
road.driveNote: See Cascadian Couloir (south approach) or Ice Cliff Glacier (north approach) entries for driving directions.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Central Cascades
climate.typical: Alpine granite ridge exposed to wind and afternoon convection

bySeason.spring: Approach glacier/snow travel required; cornices possible at the notch
bySeason.summer: Best window; watch for afternoon storms
bySeason.fall: Good rock conditions but shorter days
bySeason.winter: Not a viable season

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest / Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass at either trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit; overnight camping in the Stuart Zone (May 15-Oct 31) requires an Enchantment Permit Area quota permit if approaching via Stuart Lake/Mountaineer Creek
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn if climbing the upper ridge in a day from a high camp

sectionBreakdown.section: Upper ridge (from notch) round trip incl. descent
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Notch to summit and down Cascadian
sectionBreakdown.note: Documented range 9-11 hrs for strong parties

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency (509) 667-6851; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River (Icicle) Ranger Station, Leavenworth (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth (Level V trauma); Central Washington Hospital, Wenatchee (509) 662-1511 for higher-level trauma
emergency.notes: Chelan County Mountain Rescue responds to Stuart Range callouts; cell service is unreliable to absent on the ridge.

routes.overview: A long, moderate ridge climb on excellent granite: mostly 3rd/4th-class scrambling for its lower 1,000+ feet, with the crux at the summit pyramid where a couple of short, well-protected 5.6 sections must be climbed.
routes.beta: A Cascades classic and one of the range's best long, easy ridge climbs — many parties solo or simul-climb the lower ridge and bivy on route for a 3-day round trip. Unlike much Cascades rock, the abundant 3rd/4th class is on sound granite. Route-finding, particularly between Long John Tower and the West Ridge Notch, is considered the crux of the trip alongside the technical climbing near the top.
routes.approach: From Ingalls Lake, hike the ridge toward Stuart Pass (staying mainly on the ridge crest). Cut right across the talus slope toward the second prominent gully, where the technical climbing begins.
routes.descent: The Cascadian Couloir is the easiest way down; from its base, follow the Ingalls Creek trail back to Ingalls Lake.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Cascadian Couloir (see that route entry) to the Ingalls Creek basin, then pick up the Ingalls Creek Trail back toward Ingalls Lake and the Esmeralda Trailhead — a long, unglamorous but straightforward walk-off.
routes.itinerary: Frequently done as a 3-day trip (approach and bivy, climb and descend, walk out), though strong parties do it car-to-car in one very long day.
routes.bail: The lower ridge offers many walk-off options on 3rd/4th class terrain; above the West Ridge Notch, retreat is more committing and continuing over the top is often the practical choice.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, route-finding crux below the summit, and long walk-off, set a turnaround time that keeps the technical summit pyramid climbing out of the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep, once the approach ridge and gullies are free of significant snow.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for the common overnight itinerary
Approach shoes for the long 3rd/4th class sections
proTips: The lower ridge is fast on foot in approach shoes; save the rack for the summit pyramid.
Route-finding between Long John Tower and the West Ridge Notch is the trickiest navigation on the route — study beta/photos beforehand.
watchOut: Easy to wander onto harder terrain off the ridge crest if route-finding is off.
Long, complicated ridge with many false leads.
The Ingalls Creek walk-off descent is long and considered tedious/unaesthetic.
knownHazards: Extended, complex ridge route-finding
Loose rock in variations off the crest
Long descent/approach exposure to weather
routes.fa: Lex Maxwell, Fred Llewellyn, and John Vertrees, August 1935.
routes.corrections: The given grade listed 5.4 as the technical crux; Mountain Project's route page lists the crux as 5.6, which is the widely cited figure for the summit-pyramid section. FA was previously listed as null — sourced above from Mountain Project (cross-referenced with SummitPost).

emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg (Level IV trauma); Central Washington Hospital, Wenatchee (509) 662-1511 for higher-level trauma

routes.overview: A technically moderate but committing glacier/ice route on the north side: climb the Ice Cliff icefall, cross the upper cirque, and ascend a ~50° gully to a cornice exit onto the south-facing ridge just below the false summit.
routes.beta: Starting from the moraine below the North Ridge, traverse beneath the cliff around 6,600 ft and ascend the icefall via the left side (early season, AI2 ice) or right side (late season, up to a 5.8 slab). From the top of the icefall, cross the cirque toward the upper couloir, pass the bergschrund on the right, and climb a roughly 50° gully, taking the right fork near the top and surmounting a cornice to reach the ridge below the 8,600-ft false summit, then traverse to the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead, follow the main trail, staying right at the first fork onto the Mountaineer Creek path. A climbers' trail branches off around 47.49742°N, 120.86218°W; ascend talus/snow staying east of Mountaineers Ridge to upper valley campsites (~5,400 ft) below Sherpa Glacier.
routes.descent: Descend via Sherpa Glacier, east of the icefall/couloir exit, beginning around 8,200 ft.
routes.descentText: From the false summit area, descend southeast onto the upper snowfields and drop into the Sherpa Glacier/Sherpa Couloir system east of the Ice Cliff route, then continue down to the Mountaineer Creek basin and out the Stuart Lake Trail.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 2-day trip: approach and camp near 5,400 ft below Sherpa Glacier, climb and descend the next day.
routes.bail: Retreat down the icefall/couloir is possible early in the climb but becomes more difficult and hazardous higher up due to crevasses and the ice cliff itself; committing once established on the upper couloir.
routes.turnaround: Because conditions (crevasse bridges, ice-cliff stability, cornice size) change through the season and through the day, set a conservative turnaround for the cornice exit and reassess if the exit looks heavily corniced or unstable.
routes.bestSeason: Conditions vary greatly by year and month; typically climbed June-August. Type and severity of difficulties (crevasses, ice-cliff exposure, cornice size) can differ significantly month to month, so current beta/recent trip reports should be checked.
whatToBring: Glacier travel gear (rope, crevasse rescue kit)
Extra ice screws for the upper couloir
Bivy gear for the standard 2-day itinerary
proTips: Early season the left side of the icefall is generally easier (short AI2 sections); late season the right side may require a 5.8 rock slab.
Passing the bergschrund on the right in the upper cirque is the typical line.
watchOut: The route sits directly beneath/adjacent to an active ice cliff and glacier — icefall and crevasse hazard are real and route-defining.
The summit cornice exit can be large and unstable, especially early season.
Conditions vary dramatically month to month; stale beta can be dangerously wrong.
knownHazards: Icefall/serac hazard from the Ice Cliff Glacier
Crevasses on the glacier and in the upper cirque
Cornice at the top of the exit couloir
routes.corrections: Multiple sources (Mountain Project, general route summaries) grade this route Grade II-III rather than the Grade IV given; the given alpineGrade of D and overall commitment appear to overstate the grade relative to Mountain Project's Grade III listing.
routes.face: North side, above the Ice Cliff Glacier
routes.fa: Bill Prater, Gene Prater, and Dave Mahre, August 5, 1957.

routes.overview: A classic steep snow/ice couloir on the northwest side, climbing Stuart Glacier to a narrow ice step (up to 60°) that gains the West Ridge near the summit.
routes.beta: From the top of Stuart Glacier (~7,800 ft), the couloir climbs at mostly 40-50° with a crux narrow section about halfway up that frequently holds ~100 ft of water ice to 60°. The route tops out at or near the small notch on the West Ridge; exit rock is low-5th on the normal finish (about 5.7 on a left-side variation), with additional low-5th climbing on the West Ridge to the summit.
routes.approach: From Icicle Road, follow the Stuart Lake Trail about 3 miles, continue another mile, then veer left staying in the main valley; after 1.5 miles ascend a steep slope to a small flat valley at 5,400 ft near Sherpa Glacier, then continue to the base of Stuart Glacier.
routes.descent: Typically down Sherpa Glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the east ridge, bypass the false summit, traverse the southeast snowfield, and descend Sherpa Couloir to Sherpa Glacier and back to the Mountaineer Creek basin.
routes.itinerary: Usually a 2-day trip with a camp near 5,400 ft below Sherpa Glacier; the route is a favored early-season objective while the couloir still holds continuous snow/ice.
routes.bail: Downclimbing the couloir is an option below the ice crux but becomes harder and more hazardous once past the narrow ice step; a couple of short screws aid retreat anchors if needed.
routes.turnaround: Given the couloir's narrow ice crux and cornice/exit conditions, turn around if the ice step or exit rocks look unreasonably hard for conditions or time of day.
routes.bestSeason: Best as an early-season snow/ice climb, typically May-July; conditions shift from full winter-like dry-tooling potential in early season to a softer M3-M4 couloir with easier route-finding later in the season.
gear: Standard alpine rock rack (nuts + cams to BD #3)
whatToBring: Glacier travel gear for Stuart Glacier approach
proTips: A couple of short ice screws are handy for the crux water-ice step; pickets are useful lower on the couloir in early season.
One trip report notes bringing 5 cams up to a BD #3, 8 nuts, and 4 pins and using all of it on the exit rock/West Ridge section.
watchOut: Conditions vary dramatically by season — from full winter dry-tooling conditions early to softer, easier mixed terrain later.
Narrow ice crux midway up the couloir can be thin or unformed depending on the year.
Glacier travel and bergschrund hazards on the approach across Stuart Glacier.
knownHazards: Crevasses on Stuart Glacier approach
Narrow, sometimes thin ice crux
Cornice/exit hazard at the top of the couloir
routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists this as Grade IV rather than the Grade III given; noting the conflict here.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Larry Strathdee, June 1944 (per the supplied data; Mountain Project confirms the pairing and year but does not specify the month).
