routes.overview: A striking crack-climbing pillar above the Ice Cliff Glacier on Stuart's northeast side: four easy pitches across the glacier reach the base, followed by three sustained crux pitches of steep crack climbing on the pillar itself.
routes.beta: The Girth Pillar sits above the Ice Cliff Glacier on the northeast face. After traversing roughly four easy pitches across the glacier to the pillar's base, climbers tackle three engaging, sustained crack pitches (the crux) before easier terrain leads to the false summit above the glacier. Loose rock is noted on the final pitch.
routes.approach: Via Mountaineer Creek from the Stuart Lake Trail to the basin below Ice Cliff Glacier, then onto the glacier itself to reach the base of the pillar (see Ice Cliff Glacier route entry for approach detail).
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir, reached via the false summit above the glacier.
routes.descentText: From the top of the pillar, easier terrain leads to the false summit; from there descend the Cascadian Couloir to the Ingalls Creek basin or continue to the true summit first per party goals.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-day trip: approach and camp below the glacier, climb and descend the following day.
routes.bail: Rappelling the pillar itself (fixed or natural anchors) is the main retreat option once established on the crux pitches; retreat across the glacier below is comparatively straightforward in good conditions.
routes.turnaround: Given glacier travel on the approach and the sustained crux pitches, set a turnaround that accounts for glacier conditions changing through the day and leaves margin before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Aug, once the Ice Cliff Glacier approach is reasonably consolidated and the pillar is dry.
gear: Ice/glacier travel gear for the approach pitches
whatToBring: Crampons/axe for the glacier approach pitches
Bivy gear for the standard 2-day itinerary
proTips: The crux three pitches on the pillar are sustained crack climbing — come with a solid rack of hand-to-fist-size gear.
There is an option to cut left at a white sling into the original 5.11c line, but that section collapsed in the 1990s.
watchOut: Loose rock noted on the final pitch.
Glacier travel hazards (crevasses) on the approach across Ice Cliff Glacier.
Sustained, committing crack climbing with limited easy retreat mid-pillar.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Ice Cliff Glacier approach
Loose rock on the upper pillar
Historic rockfall/collapse in part of the original line (1990s)
routes.corrections: The given data listed 6 pitches; Mountain Project's route page describes 11 pitches total (approximately 4 easier pitches across the glacier plus 3 crux pitches on the pillar plus additional pitches above to the false summit) over 2,000 ft, which is used here.
routes.face: Northeast face, above Ice Cliff Glacier
routes.fa: Kit Lewis and Jim Nelson, 1983.

pitchDetail.notes: 130 ft; nice but immediately steep hand crack, jogs right at the top; belay on a small, poor ledge
100 ft; shuffle left ~10 ft to a thin crack, finger jams lead to a rest before the crux
120 ft; perfect hands on steep, somewhat dirty rock to the top of the pillar

routes.overview: No named "North Face" route distinct from the peak's cataloged north-side lines (North Ridge, Ice Cliff Glacier, Stuart Glacier Couloir) could be confirmed in current guidebook/route databases; this entry likely refers generically to Stuart's broad north aspect, where those established routes are located.
routes.beta: Extensive searching of Mountain Project's Mt Stuart area page, SummitPost, and Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide summaries turned up no separately named "North Face" route matching this grade (IV, 5.8, AI2). The north side of the mountain is instead divided among the named North Ridge, Ice Cliff Glacier, Stuart Glacier Couloir, and Girth Pillar routes, plus the Lara Kellogg Memorial Route on the adjacent northeast face above Ice Cliff Glacier.
routes.corrections: Could not independently verify a distinct "North Face" route on Mount Stuart matching the supplied grade/stats. This may be a database entry for the general north aspect rather than a specific named line, or may correspond to one of the already-cataloged north-side routes (most likely Stuart Glacier Couloir or Ice Cliff Glacier, both of which carry similar AI2-class grades). Recommend verifying this entry against the original data source before publishing route-specific numbers.
routes.face: North side (general aspect; no distinct named line confirmed)
knownHazards: Rockfall and icefall hazard typical of Stuart's north-side glacier routes
Route-finding uncertainty given the lack of a confirmed distinct line

routes.overview: The Great Gendarme is the technical crux feature of Mount Stuart's North Ridge, not an independent route: two sustained 5.9 pitches (a fingers-and-hands dihedral, then an offwidth) taken directly over the gendarme roughly two-thirds of the way up the ridge.
routes.beta: Above the base of the pillar/gendarme are two crux 5.9 pitches. The first is a steep fingers-and-hands dihedral with good rests between harder sections — described elsewhere as a series of 5.8 laybacks, underclings, and corners, each harder than the last. The second pitch, harder than the first, climbs an offwidth crack. A short additional 5.9 crack pitch follows before easier simul-climbing continues to the summit.
routes.approach: Reached via the North Ridge itself (see North Ridge route entry) — roughly 11 pitches of low/mid-5th climbing from the notch above Stuart Glacier lead to the base of the Gendarme.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir (same as North Ridge).
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend east to the false summit through blocky terrain, then down the Cascadian Couloir to Ingalls Creek.
routes.bail: As part of the North Ridge, retreat past this point is committing; rappel anchors on the Gendarme pitches are limited and downclimbing the offwidth/dihedral is difficult.
routes.turnaround: Because the Gendarme pitches are the technical and psychological crux of a long ridge day, reaching their base with ample daylight/weather margin is essential — treat this as the go/no-go point for continuing to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep, matching the North Ridge season; the Gendarme pitches can be wet or icy depending on conditions and time of day.
gear: A wide piece (3-3.5 in / optional #4 cam) for the offwidth
Standard rock rack for the dihedral pitch
whatToBring: Comfortable offwidth technique/gear — this pitch is often the day's hardest
Light rack given the alpine, simul-heavy nature of the surrounding ridge
proTips: The offwidth pitch is considered the harder of the two 5.9 pitches — a #4-sized cam or equivalent wide gear is commonly recommended.
Pitches here can be wet/icy even when the rest of the ridge is dry, given the shaded, exposed position.
watchOut: Limited retreat options once committed to the Gendarme pitches.
Can be wet or icy independent of overall route conditions.
Sustained difficulty after already climbing ~11 pitches of ridge to reach it.
knownHazards: Committing position with limited rappel/retreat options
Localized wet/icy rock
General North Ridge exposure and afternoon-storm risk
routes.corrections: This is best understood as the crux feature within the North Ridge (see wa_mount_stuart_north_ridge) rather than a standalone route; sources differ on who first free-climbed it directly (candidates include Fred Beckey & Steve Marts, 1963; Mead Harris & Jay Ossiander, ~1970; and a related aid variation by James Wickwire & Fred Stanley, 1965) — no single FA could be confirmed with confidence, so it is left null here.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep fingers-and-hands dihedral; a series of laybacks, underclings, and corners with good rests between harder moves
Offwidth crack, harder than pitch 1 — the route's technical crux

blurb: Sherpa Peak (8,630 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range within Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, immediately east of Mount Stuart and part of the Mount Stuart batholith. It is known for a distinctive "balanced rock" formation along its east ridge and for generally looser, more "scrappy" rock than its taller neighbor, with an involved, multi-rappel descent on every route. The peak was first climbed in 1955 by Dave Mahre, Bill Prater, and Gene Prater, and was later named for a local 1950s-era climbing club.

routes.fa: Fred Dunham, Ray Lilleby, and James Wick, August 13, 1961
routes.rappels: 4 single-rope rappels retracing the ascent route
routes.face: West Ridge (crux climbs a steep SW-facing headwall above the Stuart-Sherpa col)
routes.overview: The standard and most popular technical line on Sherpa Peak: from the Stuart-Sherpa col, three pitches of 4th-class-to-5.4 climbing lead up the west ridge to an exposed, airy summit block.
routes.beta: From the Stuart-Sherpa col (~8,100 ft), climb the ridge staying near the crest to a small notch, traverse sandy benches to a steep SW-facing headwall, and ascend broken rock to a small cave about a third of the way up. The crux crack out of the cave (5.4-5.5) leads to a sheltered belay ledge; from there, traverse left to a second anchor, scramble down, then climb a short 4th-class pitch to the notoriously airy summit block. Descend by rappelling the same line.
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda trailhead at the end of North Fork Teanaway River Road, hike the Longs Pass Trail over Longs Pass (~2,100 ft of gain), descend to Ingalls Creek, and follow the same climbers' path used for Mount Stuart's Cascadian Couloir into the basin below Stuart and Sherpa (staying left of the ridge, crossing streams). Camp in the boulder-filled basin around 6,200 ft, or continue to the Table Rock bivy at ~7,400 ft — roughly 4.5 miles from the trailhead. From camp, climb to the Stuart-Sherpa col (~8,100 ft) to start the route.
routes.descent: Rappel the ascent route (4 single-rope rappels) back to the Stuart-Sherpa col, then reverse the approach.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb to the first anchor and make a single-rope rappel, then a second, to a scramble section; a further single-rope rappel and a final double-rope rappel (or two combined single-rope rappels with a harder pull) complete the descent to the col. Descent involves loose rock; trip reports describe rappel stations that can be hard to relocate and descents finishing near dark.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike Longs Pass Trail to the Ingalls Creek basin and set camp (6,200 ft basin or 7,400 ft Table Rock bivy), ~4.5 miles. Day 2: climb to the Stuart-Sherpa col and up the West Ridge (3 pitches) to the summit, rappel the route back to camp, then hike out (often combined with Mount Stuart in a 2-3 day trip).
routes.bail: Rappel the ascent route back to the Stuart-Sherpa col; there is no walk-off once established on the technical ridge.
routes.turnaround: This is a long alpine day with a multi-stage rappel descent; several trip reports describe finishing at or after dusk. Set a turnaround time based on your team's pace on the climbing and rappels, and stick to it.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed June through September once the approach and col are free of snow; early-season attempts can encounter snow/ice in the approach gullies and around the col.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack suffices: finger-to-hand cams (~0.4-1 in) for the crux crack out of the cave, a couple of larger 2-3 in pieces, a set of nuts, and extra slings/draws. One 60 m rope covers the roped pitches and the four single-rope rappels on descent.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine rack weighted toward finger/hand cams for the crux crack, plus a few larger cams and a nut set; slings for rappel anchors.
whatToBring: Bivy/camp gear for the overnight camp
proTips: Stay left of the obvious cave for the standard 5.4 line — the right-hand variation goes at a sustained 5.7
Table Rock (~7,400 ft) is a scenic, sheltered bivy option below the col
The crux crack takes small-to-medium cams; rack light
watchOut: Loose rock on the descent rappels has caused close calls in trip reports
Rappel stations can be hard to relocate on the way down — note them on the way up
knownHazards: Loose rock
Multi-stage rappel descent
Exposed, airy summit block

pitchDetail.notes: Crack out of the cave to a sheltered belay ledge that holds 4-5 climbers
Traverse left to a second anchor station
Scramble down then a short exposed pitch to the airy summit block

bySeason.spring: Approach gullies and the col often still snow-covered into June; creek crossings can be high with runoff

bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures, first snow possible by October, shorter daylight

routes.corrections: Mountain Project rates the overall route 5.5 (vs. the 5.4 given), consistent with several trip reports citing the crux crack as 5.4-5.5; treat 5.4-5.5 as the accepted range. MP also documents 3 pitches / 500 ft while some older trip reports describe six shorter pitches from the col — pitch-count conventions vary by source. Some sources (Mountain Project, WTA) list the summit at 8,605 ft rather than the 8,630 ft used here (which matches Wikipedia/USGS).

routes.rappels: ~4 rappels down the ascent gully system (or 2 rappels down the West Ridge line, per some parties)
routes.face: East Ridge, past the peak's namesake balanced rock
routes.overview: The easiest way to Sherpa's summit: a long Class 3-4 ridge scramble past the peak's famous balanced-rock formation, roped mainly for the rappel descent.
routes.beta: From a basin around 7,000 ft, climb a broad gully trending NNE to the ridge crest east of the balanced rock, then follow the ridge westward over blocks and sandy benches, past the balanced rock, to the summit. Rated Class 3-4 with some low-5th-class moves; existing rappel slings mark key downclimb/rappel sections. Considered the least technical and fastest way to the top, but a rope is still needed for the descent.
routes.approach: Same approach as the West Ridge to Ingalls Creek and the basin below the peak (~7,000 ft): Esmeralda trailhead, Longs Pass Trail, down to Ingalls Creek, then the climbers' path toward Stuart/Sherpa.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the same gully system using existing slings, or traverse over and rappel the West Ridge line.
routes.descentText: Trip reports describe two general options: rappelling down through the ascent gully itself (roughly 4 rappels off a mix of fixed slings and gear anchors, with class 3-4 downclimbing between them), or descending 2 rappels plus downclimbing via the West Ridge before traversing a rock field back to camp. Expect loose rock and, in poor conditions, sketchy footing near the balanced rock.
routes.bail: Downclimb or rappel the same gully system using existing anchors, or traverse to the West Ridge rappel line if conditions deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Several parties have finished this descent after dark due to route-finding among the gullies and rappel stations; turn around with enough daylight margin to manage the full descent.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep; new snow or wet rock near the balanced rock section turns the easy Class 3-4 scrambling into a serious proposition, so this route is best attempted in settled summer conditions.
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rack — mostly a scramble with existing rappel slings in the gully system. Carry a 50-60 m rope and a handful of pieces plus extra tat/cord to back up or replace old anchors on the descent rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack for occasional protection on the low-5th sections; rope primarily for the rappel descent.
whatToBring: Bivy/camp gear for the basin camp
Sturdy approach shoes for talus and gully terrain
proTips: Existing rappel slings mark the standard descent gully — inspect old tat before trusting it
This is the fastest, least technical way to the summit and lets you tag the famous balanced rock along the way
watchOut: New snow or wet rock near the balanced rock turns easy Class 3-4 into a serious proposition
Several parties have finished the descent after dark
knownHazards: Loose, exposed ridge terrain
Weather-sensitive rock
Multiple rappels required on descent despite the route's easy overall grade
routes.corrections: The source data listed this route as 'Grade III, 5.7' with 5 pitches and an AD alpine grade. Multiple independent sources (SummitPost, Eric's Base Camp trip report, onehikeaweek, general Stuart Range beta) instead describe the East Ridge as the EASIEST route on Sherpa Peak — Class 3-4 scrambling with mostly low-5th-class moves, roped chiefly for ~4 rappels on descent, not a sustained Grade III 5.7 technical climb. rockGrade, alpineGrade, and pitches have been corrected to null/removed rather than carry forward unsupported figures; commitment corrected to II.

routes.fa: Rick La Belle and Pat Derr, 1971
routes.season: Jun-Sep (summer ascents most commonly reported, e.g. August)
routes.face: North Ridge (not to be confused with the North Ridge of neighboring Mount Stuart)
routes.overview: A long, serious three-section ridge from the Sherpa-Argonaut saddle with two 5.8 pitches through loose, hard-to-protect rock — the hardest and least-traveled of Sherpa's three ridges.
routes.beta: The ridge splits into three sections divided by deep notches. The lower section is mostly Class 2-4 with some 5th class, best climbed on the east side of the crest. The middle section involves rappelling into notches, scrambling around spires, and climbing two 5.8 pitches (a dirty chimney pitch and a crack pitch with some loose blocks). The final section is lower-fifth-class scrambling, including a long pitch with a hand crack, to the summit. Large detached blocks and difficult protection make this route feel serious for its grade.
routes.approach: From the Colchuck Lake/Stuart Lake trailhead (Icicle Creek Road), follow the boot path up Mountaineer Creek, then ascend talus slopes west of the creek toward the Argonaut-Sherpa saddle (~5,800 ft+); from the Sherpa-Argonaut basin, contour climber's right to the base of the route's lower notch.
routes.descent: Rappel southwest into gullies south of the summit, downclimb (max Class 4), then descend benches and scree via the Sherpa-Argonaut col back to the basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, a short rappel leads to Class 3 terrain down a gully, with optional further rappels around obstacles. The route traverses southeast below ribs to reach the Sherpa-Argonaut saddle; the roughly 1,000-foot descent from the saddle features loose rock and sand-covered ledges.
routes.bail: Retreat back down the ridge itself is slow and hazardous due to loose blocks; most parties continue to the summit and use the standard gully descent to the Sherpa-Argonaut col rather than reversing the route.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, complex ridge with variable pitch counts depending on simul-climbing and route-finding through the notches; budget extra time and set a firm turnaround given the descent's own complexity.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack to 3 in with doubles in the 0.5-1 in range, plus many long runners to manage rope drag and protect around loose blocks. A 60 m rope is recommended, though shorter ropes (40-50 m) can work given the short pitch lengths.
routes.proNeeds: Doubled small-to-medium cams and abundant long slings; protection is sparse and awkward around the route's loose blocks, so the climbing feels harder than the 5.8 grade suggests.
whatToBring: Bivy/camp gear (long day from Colchuck approach)
Extra rappel cord for loose-rock anchors
proTips: Bring doubles of hand-sized cams and many long runners — protection is difficult around the loose blocks
This is NOT the North Ridge of Mount Stuart — don't mix up beta between the two peaks
watchOut: Large, detached loose blocks throughout the upper pitches
Difficult, sparse protection despite the moderate 5.8 grade — climbs harder than its number
knownHazards: Loose, detached blocks
Difficult protection
Long, complex descent via the Sherpa-Argonaut col

pitchDetail.notes: Best climbed on the east side of the ridge crest
Rappel to notches, scramble around spires; two 5.8 pitches (dirty chimney, then a crack pitch with loose blocks)
Long scramble pitch with a hand crack to the summit; large detached blocks and difficult protection

blurb: Argonaut Peak (8,457 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, part of the Mount Stuart batholith, standing on the ridge crest between Colchuck Peak and Sherpa Peak, east of Mount Stuart. It offers a range of moderate alpine rock routes on generally clean, sound granite, mostly in the 5.4-5.8 range. The peak was first climbed via its south side in September 1955 by Lex Maxwell, Bob McCall, and Bill Prater.

objHaz: Loose rock where the approach gully/couloir has melted out
Exposed steep snow crossing (roughly 45-55 degrees) with rock below
Long, brushy alternate approach via Ingalls Creek/Fourth Creek Pass if not using the Colchuck Lake side
routes.face: Northeast side, gained from the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the Colchuck Lake approach
Extra water for the long approach
proTips: Climbing earlier in the season (June) gives firmer, more continuous snow in the approach couloir; by late summer the gully can be snow-free and loose/rubbly.

Approaching via Colchuck Lake and Colchuck Col is reported to be far more efficient than the alternate Ingalls Creek/Fourth Creek Pass approach, which involves miles of brushy 'green purgatory' bushwhacking.
watchOut: A steep, narrow snow-filled couloir must be crossed en route to the ridge and carries real fall exposure over rock below.
When the upper gully melts out later in summer it exposes loose, rubbly rock some parties find harder than the nominal grade suggests.
knownHazards: Loose rock, especially where snow has melted out of the approach gully/couloir
Exposed steep snow crossing with rock hazard below
Long, brushy alternate approach via Ingalls Creek/Fourth Creek Pass

road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road ~8.5 mi, turn left on Eightmile Road/FR 7600, then stay right on FR 7601 for ~4 mi to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; high-clearance recommended.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades rain-shadow climate, generally drier and sunnier than the west-side Cascades; afternoon thunderstorms are a summer hazard.

bySeason.spring: Snow lingers in the approach couloir and at Colchuck Col into June.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window; some snow may persist into July in shaded terrain.
bySeason.fall: Good conditions can hold into September with shorter daylight.
bySeason.winter: Access road closed; deep snow and avalanche terrain on the approach.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Stuart Lake Trailhead to Colchuck Col
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Colchuck Lake Trail and the Colchuck Col couloir/talus, per one GPS-tracked trip report
Traverse to the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle, then up the northeast ridge/gully
Reverse of ascent route, per the same trip report

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or Chelan County dispatch (Rivercom) 509-663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth: 509-548-2550, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA - 24-hour ER, 509-548-5815
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach with no maintained trail beyond Colchuck Lake.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine route gaining Argonaut's northeast ridge from the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle, mixing 4th-class scrambling with a short section of low 5th-class rock and a steep snow/couloir crossing.
routes.beta: Approached from Colchuck Lake via Colchuck Col and a traverse around Colchuck Peak's south side to the saddle between Colchuck and Argonaut, the route climbs the crest and northwest flank of Argonaut's northeast ridge. A narrow, steep (roughly 45-55 degree) snow-filled couloir must be crossed below a short section of 4th-class-to-5.4 rock that reportedly protects well to about 1.5 inches; the final approach to the true summit is an easy 4th-class scramble. One GPS-tracked trip report logged roughly 16 miles and 7,200 ft of gain/loss round trip from the Stuart Lake Trailhead, in a 9.2-hour ascent and 5.5-hour descent.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,400 ft) follow the Colchuck Lake Trail about 4 miles to Colchuck Lake (~5,570-5,580 ft), then continue via the Colchuck Col couloir/talus to Colchuck Col (~8,050 ft, about 4.2 hrs from the trailhead per one trip report). From the col, traverse below Colchuck Peak's south side to the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle to reach the base of Argonaut's northeast ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb the ridge and couloir back to the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle, then retrace the approach over Colchuck Col to Colchuck Lake and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descent follows the ascent line in reverse. From the summit, downclimb the 4th-class upper ridge, cross back over the steep snow couloir, and regain the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle. From there, contour back around Colchuck Peak to Colchuck Col and descend the couloir/talus to Colchuck Lake and out the Colchuck Lake/Stuart Lake Trail. One reported descent took about 5.5 hours from summit to trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in a long day; can also be split into a 2-day trip with a camp at Colchuck Lake (overnight permit required) to shorten the summit day.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally back down the ridge/couloir to the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle and back over Colchuck Col; there is no shorter bail option once established on the upper ridge.
routes.turnaround: The approach and descent are both long (roughly 4+ hours each way to/from Colchuck Col alone per available trip reports) and cross exposed snow terrain, so set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to re-cross the couloir and regain Colchuck Col before dark, and watch for building afternoon cloud or thunderstorm activity common to the Cascades in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed June through September. Earlier in the season the approach couloir holds more continuous snow, which eases travel but requires axe and crampons; by late summer the gully can melt out to loose rock underfoot.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack is sufficient: a handful of cams/nuts up to about 1.5 inches for the brief 5.4 step, plus a rope for the short technical section and for belaying the steep snow crossing if desired.
routes.proNeeds: The short 5.4 section is reported to protect well with gear up to about 1.5 inches.

objHaz: Lichen-covered holds reducing friction and protection quality
Long bushwhack approach if taken from the Ingalls Creek side
routes.face: East/southeast side, via the SE spire (false summit) to the true summit
whatToBring: Extra runners/slings for lichen-covered, awkward protection
Approach shoes for the scramble sections
Bivy gear if approaching via the long Ingalls Creek bushwhack
proTips: The approach via Colchuck Col is reported to be considerably better than the long, strenuous Ingalls Creek bushwhack approach.
Expect blocky, somewhat awkward climbing on the lower pitches with lots of lichen, so protection can be harder to find than the grade alone suggests.
watchOut: This line tops out on Argonaut's southeast spire, a false summit, before a further scramble/climb to the true high point.
Loose blocks and lichen on the lower pitches call for extra care with protection placement and rope management.
knownHazards: Loose, lichen-covered rock on the lower section
Long, brushy alternate approach via Ingalls Creek if Colchuck Col is not used

routes.overview: A technical rock line up Argonaut's east side that gains the southeast spire (a false summit) before continuing to the true summit, on blocky, lichen-covered granite.
routes.beta: Several east-side lines exist on Argonaut (East Gully, Southeast Ridge, Northeast Buttress) and naming is inconsistent between sources; none matches the app data's 'East Ridge, Grade III, 6 pitches' exactly. The best-documented analog, The Mountaineers' Southeast Ridge, is graded Grade II, 5.6, with blocky, somewhat awkward, hard-to-protect lower pitches covered in lichen, topping out on the SE spire (false summit) before continuing to the true summit.
routes.approach: Best approached via Colchuck Lake and Colchuck Col, as for the peak's other technical routes. An alternative approach up Ingalls Creek from the Teanaway/Beverly Creek side is a long, strenuous bushwhack and is not recommended.
routes.descent: Descend by downclimbing and/or rappelling the route back toward the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle.
routes.bail: Retreat down the climbed pitches (rappelling as needed) back toward the Argonaut-Colchuck saddle.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach shared with the peak's other technical routes, set a turnaround time that leaves enough daylight and margin to complete all rappels and regain the saddle/col before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep, once approach snow on the col/saddle terrain has consolidated or melted out.
routes.corrections: No source located documents a route named exactly 'East Ridge' at Grade III with 6 pitches on Argonaut Peak. The closest documented analogs are The Mountaineers' 'Southeast Ridge' (Grade II, 5.6 -- blocky, lichenous lower pitches, tops out on the SE spire/false summit before the true summit) and other sources' 'East Gully' (II, 5.6, a single dihedral pitch gaining the summit ridge) and 'Northeast Buttress' (II, 5.8 A1, 4 pitches). Based on these sources, commitment grade is corrected here to II (from the given III); alpineGrade, exact pitch count, and route length could not be independently confirmed and are left null rather than guessed. Note also that a separate, easier Class 3-4 scramble line (part of the standard South Route, via a 50-ft chockstone gully) is sometimes loosely described as reaching 'the east ridge' before heading west to the summit -- this is a different, non-technical line and should not be conflated with a III/5.6 six-pitch route.

blurb: Colchuck Peak (8,705 ft) is the westernmost major summit of the Stuart Range in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, carved from the coarse Cretaceous-age granite of the Mount Stuart batholith. Its northeast flank holds the Colchuck Glacier, which feeds turquoise Colchuck Lake below and forms the peak's standard route. The peak was first climbed in August 1948 by Elvin and Norma Johnson and William and Kathy Long via the glacier line that still bears its name.

routes.rappels: None typical — descend the same route (Colchuck Col to glacier to lake)
routes.fa: Elvin & Norma Johnson, William & Kathy Long — August 1948 (this glacier line was the peak's original first-ascent route)
routes.overview: The standard route on Colchuck Peak: moderate snow up the east lobe of the Colchuck Glacier to Colchuck Col, followed by third-class scrambling to the summit.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Lake, gain the crest of the lateral moraine early to avoid loose scree, then ascend easy snow up the glacier's east (left) lobe past the base of Dragontail's Serpentine and Backbone Ridges. The glacier steepens to roughly 40 degrees for about 100 ft just below the Colchuck-Dragontail col. From the col, scramble sandy third-class steps up to the summit plateau (~8,500 ft) and continue west/north across it to the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead, hike the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trail (#1599/#1599A) about 4 miles and 2,280 ft of gain to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft). Continue along the lake's north/east shore toward the base of the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route via Colchuck Col and the glacier back to the lake and trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the scramble down to Colchuck Col, then descend the glacier's east lobe, staying well above any bergschrund/crevasse features, back to the lateral moraine and the lake shore trail.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in a long day (10–14 hrs reported) or as an overnight with camp at Colchuck Lake, climbing the glacier the following morning while snow is firm.
routes.bail: Straightforward to retreat by reversing the glacier descent at almost any point above the col; below the steepened section near the col there is no technical down-climbing required.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before committing to the steeper snow below the col — solar warming softens the glacier surface through the day and increases the chance of postholing, wet-slide activity, and rockfall off the surrounding ridgelines.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a snow climb from June through August when the glacier is consolidated; by late summer the route can go largely dry with more loose talus and scree exposed, and crevasses may open on the glacier later in the season.
routes.comms: No cell service beyond Icicle Creek Canyon near Leavenworth; carry a satellite communicator (e.g. Garmin inReach) for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack needed for the standard line; parties carry an ice axe and crampons for the glacier and col.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needs — this is an unroped snow/scramble route for most parties in normal conditions; a rope and basic crevasse-rescue gear are prudent later in the season as the glacier opens up.
whatToBring: extra water (no reliable water above the lake late season)
proTips: Gain the lateral moraine crest as soon as possible from the lake to avoid loose scree and talus.
Climb early while the glacier snow is firm; it can be done car-to-car in a day with an early start.
watchOut: Loose sandy/talus scrambling on the final steps to the summit plateau.
Glacier crevasse hazard increases later in the season.
knownHazards: Crevasses can open on the Colchuck Glacier by late summer.
The moraine approach has loose, ankle-twisting scree and talus.

pitchDetail.notes: Talus/scree along the lateral moraine crest above Colchuck Lake.
East lobe of the Colchuck Glacier, steepening near the col.
Sandy, low-angle rock scrambling across the summit plateau to the true summit.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Central zone

bySeason.summer: Warm, mostly dry; glacier softens by midday.
bySeason.fall: Cooling fast, early-season snow possible above the lake.
bySeason.winter: Deep snow, road gated, approach becomes a full ski/snowshoe tour.

sectionBreakdown.note: 4 miles, 2,280 ft gain

sectionBreakdown.section: Lake to Colchuck Col via glacier

Class 3 scrambling

routes.rappels: 1 rappel from the summit rock band to a ledge on the SE face, then 2–3 single-rope rappels down a gully just east of the couloir's top to reach the snow slopes below
routes.fa: Skip Edmunds, Clark Gerhardt, Paula Kregel, Greg Markov — January 1975
routes.face: Northeast Face, above the Colchuck Glacier, left of the Northeast Buttress
objHaz: thin ice/mixed terrain in variable conditions
routes.overview: A deep, sustained snow-and-ice couloir splitting the Northeast Face higher up the Colchuck Glacier than the North Buttress Couloir, finishing near steep flutes below the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Climb the couloir from the glacier, watching for a steeper mid-route section that can melt out early in the year; near the top, traverse left along the base of a wall to find the exit. Conditions vary widely year to year — from a straightforward snow climb to a thin-ice/mixed pitch, with a cornice (up to ~20 ft reported) sometimes requiring tunneling or a fluted-snow finish onto the summit plateau.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead, hike to Colchuck Lake (~4 miles, 2,280 ft gain), then continue onto the Colchuck Glacier and locate the couloir entrance higher on the glacier's true right side, above the North Buttress Couloir.
routes.descent: Descend from the summit rock band via a short rappel to a SE-facing ledge, then rappel a gully east of the couloir's top back to the glacier, and descend the standard Colchuck Glacier route to the lake.
routes.descentText: From the A-frame summit blocks, downclimb/rappel to the upper rock band, then make a single rappel to a ledge on the southeast face. From the ledge, traverse to the top of the NE Couloir and find a gully just east of it; 2–3 single-rope rappels down this gully regain the snow slopes below the couloir. From there, cross to the Colchuck-Dragontail col area and descend the Colchuck Glacier back to the lake and trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed from a Colchuck Lake camp with an alpine-start couloir ascent, summit, and rappel/glacier descent back to camp the same day.
routes.bail: Retreat down-couloir is possible early via existing rock or bollard rappel anchors used for the entrance, but becomes progressively harder once above the mid-route narrows; above that point it is generally safer to continue to the exit and use the standard rock-band rappels than to reverse the couloir.
routes.turnaround: This NE-facing couloir receives direct morning sun and has produced a fatal slab avalanche; treat any signs of recent new/wind-loaded snow, warming, or unstable slab as a hard turnaround, and plan to be off the couloir before the face is in full sun.
routes.bestSeason: Late May and early June typically offer the most consistent snow conditions; the couloir's midsection can melt out and go icy/thin by mid-to-late summer, turning the climb into a mixed route.
routes.comms: No cell service beyond Icicle Creek Canyon near Leavenworth; carry a satellite communicator (e.g. Garmin inReach) for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A small alpine rock rack including pitons, plus ice screws and pickets for snow/ice protection; parties report belaying steeper mixed sections near the top.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed snow and rock protection: pickets and screws for the couloir body, a light rack with pitons/cams for the rock exit and rappel anchors.
gear: small alpine rock rack incl. pitons
whatToBring: extra layers for the exposed summit ridge
proTips: Best conditions are typically late May–early June before the couloir's mid-section melts out.
Some parties ski or walk across the still-frozen lake earlier in the season to save time — this window closes as the ice breaks up (roughly mid-May on average years).
Bollard or rock anchors are commonly used to protect the couloir entrance.
watchOut: A 2023 avalanche on this route killed three climbers when a lead climber triggered a slab that carried four people roughly 1,000 vertical feet down the couloir.
A cornice up to ~20 ft has been reported at the couloir's top, sometimes requiring tunneling.
Conditions swing dramatically year to year between an easy snow slog and a thin-ice mixed climb.
knownHazards: On February 19, 2023, a climber-triggered slab avalanche in the Northeast Couloir killed three of six climbers on the route, the deadliest U.S. avalanche of that winter.
Rockfall and icefall risk rises quickly once the face gets direct sun.
Narrow constrictions partway up the couloir can force short sections of steeper, technical climbing.

road.status: Gravel, rough and washboarded later in the season; high-clearance recommended

road.driveNote: From west Leavenworth, take Icicle Creek Road ~8.4–8.5 miles, turn left onto FR 7600/7601, then continue ~3.7–4 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Central zone
climate.typical: Continental east-slope Cascades climate: colder and drier than west-side zones, with strong solar warming on NE aspects by mid-morning in spring.

bySeason.spring: Key avalanche-hazard season for this route; wind slabs and warming can destabilize the couloir.
bySeason.summer: Couloir melts out and can go icy/thin by mid-late summer.
bySeason.fall: Marginal; early snow can re-cover the line but conditions are inconsistent.
bySeason.winter: Rarely climbed; deep snow and long approach on foot/ski.

sectionBreakdown.note: Via Colchuck Glacier's true right side
Steep snow/ice to 60+ degrees, mixed exit through the rock band

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency: (509) 667-6851 (dispatched via RiverCom 911)
emergency.rangerStation: Leavenworth Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-2550

emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin; the Feb 2023 avalanche rescue here required 22 SAR responders from five agencies and a multi-month, multi-agency body-recovery effort — illustrating how remote and slow rescue response is on this route.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists this route's grade as Grade II (not Grade III as given); treat the technical rating as highly condition-dependent, ranging from an easy Grade II snow climb to a harder mixed/ice route in thin years. A slab avalanche killed three of six climbers on this route on Feb 19, 2023 (NWAC/Chelan County Sheriff), confirming it as a genuine avalanche-terrain route despite its moderate technical grade.

routes.rappels: None typical — most parties walk off via the Colchuck Glacier/Col; a short roped pitch (not a rappel) is sometimes used near the summit
routes.fa: Ray Lilleby and Jim Wickwire — July 15, 1962
routes.overview: A moderate, popular steep-snow couloir on Colchuck Peak's north side, described as a good introduction to steep snow and easy mixed climbing, with a harder direct-line variation for parties wanting more technical climbing.
routes.beta: From the far side of Colchuck Lake, climb up to and along the lateral moraine, then drop into the large north-facing couloir. Ascend steep snow, with a short section of low-5th-class mixed climbing near the summit on the standard line; a harder direct variation goes at roughly AI2 M2+ 5.5 R/X. Most parties move together or solo the couloir when conditions are firm and safe.
routes.approach: From Colchuck Lake's south/far shore, hike up to gain the lateral moraine, follow it to the right, and drop into the couloir.
routes.descent: Descend via the backside of the peak and down the Colchuck Glacier back to the lake.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the standard Colchuck Glacier route (reversing the glacier's east lobe to Colchuck Col) rather than down-climbing the couloir; afternoon snow on the glacier can turn to slush, so an early exit is preferable.
routes.itinerary: Commonly climbed car-to-car in one long day with camp at or near Colchuck Lake, or as a single big day from the trailhead; parties often start climbing the couloir by 3–4am to beat solar warming.
routes.bail: Retreat by down-climbing the lower, lower-angle portion of the couloir is feasible early; above the steeper upper section, continuing to the top and walking off via the glacier is generally preferred to reversing exposed snow/mixed terrain.
routes.turnaround: Because rockfall and icefall here are driven by direct morning sun on the buttress, set a hard turnaround for entering or remaining in the couloir once the face comes into full sun, rather than climbing on a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: An early-season route — typically in good condition as soon as the access road and trailhead open in spring (April–May) through June; it can still be climbed later but with more exposed rock and less consolidated snow.
routes.comms: No cell service beyond Icicle Creek Canyon near Leavenworth; carry a satellite communicator (e.g. Garmin inReach) for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack is typical: a few cams (~0.5–2"), a small selection of pitons, a tricam, nuts, plus 1–2 pickets and ice screws (screws are often unnecessary in good snow).
routes.proNeeds: Most parties carry minimal protection and move quickly on firm snow; a rack becomes more useful if the couloir is icy or if climbing the harder direct-start variation.
gear: rope for the short summit step
light rack: cams, small pitons, nuts, tricam
whatToBring: extra layers for an early alpine start
overnight camping gear if basing at Colchuck Lake
proTips: Start climbing very early (around 3am) to minimize exposure to rockfall and icefall as the sun hits the buttress.
This route is usually in good condition as soon as the trailhead/road opens for the season.
The standard line is often soloed in firm conditions; save the rope/rack for the harder direct variation or icy years.
watchOut: Loose, snow-covered rock blocks in the couloir.
Ice and rockfall from solar warming can arrive quickly once the sun hits the north-facing buttress.
Afternoon glacier descent can turn to slush, slowing the walk-off considerably.
knownHazards: Rockfall and icefall triggered by morning solar warming are the primary objective hazard reported by multiple parties.
Committing mixed moves near the top can have poor ("abysmal") gear placements in certain conditions.

bySeason.spring: Prime season — firm, continuous snow in the couloir, but road may still be gated adding miles to the approach.
bySeason.summer: Route degrades — more exposed rock, less reliable snow, higher rockfall risk.
bySeason.fall: Rarely in condition; inconsistent early snow.
bySeason.winter: Not typically climbed; road closed and avalanche hazard high.

routes.corrections: Given data lists alpine grade "IV" (a multi-day commitment grade), but Mountain Project and multiple trip reports (Spokalpine, Have Tent Will Travel) describe the standard line as Grade II — a single-day steep-snow/easy-mixed route often soloed in good conditions; only a harder direct-start variation reaches roughly AI2 M2+ 5.5 R/X. Corrected commitment/alpineGrade reflects the standard route; treat "D"/"IV" as applicable only to the harder variation, if at all.

blurb: Dragontail Peak (8,840 ft) is the second-highest summit in the Stuart Range within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, rising above Colchuck Lake near Leavenworth, Washington. Built of Cretaceous-age granite, it takes its name from the serrated rock needles on its southwest ridge, which early climbers (Lex Maxwell, Bob McCall, Bill Prater) thought resembled a dragon's tail — the name was officially accepted in 1955. Its steep, glaciated north face above the Colchuck Glacier holds some of the Enchantments' most celebrated alpine objectives, from long moderate ridge rock climbs to sustained ice-and-mixed couloir routes.

routes.overview: A long, aesthetic ridge climb that weaves about 2,000 feet up the massive rock arête bounding the right (north/northwest) side of Dragontail's north face, above the Colchuck Glacier, to the summit.
routes.beta: The most popular moderate rock route on Dragontail: mostly low-5th-class ridge scrambling bracketing two well-protected 5.8 crux pitches low on the route. Position and exposure, more than sustained hard climbing, are the draw — only a couple of pitches out of roughly 13 are described as genuinely 'good' climbing.
routes.fa: Tom Hargis and Jay Ossiander, 1973
objHaz: loose rock on low-5th ridge sections
routes.face: North face, right (NW) side — above the Colchuck Glacier
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead (end of FR 7601), hike the Colchuck Lake Trail about 4 miles / 2,280 ft gain to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft); a ~2-hour hike via the Mountaineer Creek drainage. Continue along the south shore, camp, then in the morning ascend the lateral moraine to the base of the arête at the Colchuck Glacier.
routes.descent: Walk-off south side to Aasgard Pass
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the snow/scree slope on Dragontail's south side toward Aasgard Pass, then follow the steep, sandy/rocky Aasgard Pass trail back down to Colchuck Lake. Crampons and an ice axe are recommended for the descent snowfield, especially late season when it can turn to hard ice.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike in to Colchuck Lake and camp. Day 2: alpine start, climb the arête (roughly 9-10 hours base to summit), descend via Aasgard Pass, hike out (car-to-car in a long single day is also common for fit parties).
routes.bail: Multiple notches along the ridge allow rappel or downclimb retreat onto the Colchuck Glacier on the north side of the ridge before committing to the upper pitches.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, committing ridge climb with a slow, loose descent — set a firm turnaround time before starting based on daylight and party speed, and bail via the ridge notches rather than push into the descent after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a summer/early-fall rock climb, roughly July through September/October, once the approach trail and glacier crossing are mostly snow-free; Dragontail is one of the more reasonable large Cascades peaks to climb early in the season.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rack to 3 inches with doubles to 1.5 inches (excluding very small sizes); tri-cams/offset cams help in parallel, flared cracks. One party's kit: six cams, a large hex, a full nut set, and about 10 sling anchors. About 12 draws including runners. A 70m rope lets you link the two 5.8 crux pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Gear-intensive in the small-to-medium (up to 2in) range for the crux pitches; long slings help manage rope drag on the wandering ridge line; the low-5th-class ridge pitches are often simul-climbed or run out on gear.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the moraine/glacier approach
Light bivy gear if splitting the climb over two days
Sun protection for the glacier approach
proTips: Link the two 5.8 crux pitches with a 70m rope to save time
Bring extra long runners to reduce rope drag on the wandering ridge line
Start early — climbing plus descent typically takes 10-12 hours
watchOut: Loose rock through much of the low-5th-class ridge climbing — helmet mandatory
Late-season snow on the descent slope can turn to hard ice, increasing fall consequences
knownHazards: Rockfall on loose sections of the ridge
Glacier travel/crevasse hazard on the approach via Colchuck Glacier
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer

pitchDetail.notes: Two easy pitches protected with running belays before technical climbing begins
Ledge traverse into corner system
Move to surmount a flake; 3rd-class terrain around a tower
Crux 1 — steep, exposed climbing on excellent rock in a crack system
Crux 2 — double cracks with good stemming and dihedrals to an airy belay; the two cruxes can be linked with a 70m rope
Right-facing corner, steep crack/face climbing through chimneys; full 50m pitches
Roughly eight pitches of moderate ridge climbing/scrambling; notches provide belay stations
Cracks lead to the base of the summit block; most parties take an easier 4th-class traverse left to the ridge crest rather than the direct crack

Set to 13 per Mountain Project as the most current single formal count. FA (Tom Hargis & Jay Ossiander, 1973) was not present in the original data and is added here.

routes.overview: A three-tiered snow-ice-mixed line splitting the center of Dragontail's north face, climbing three couloirs separated by steep rock/ice bands (the Runnels) directly to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: A Cascades ice-climbing testpiece, usually done in spring: moderate 40-50° snow/ice fills the three couloirs, bookending a short but sustained 70-80° ice/mixed crux (the Runnels) linking the first ('Hidden') and second couloirs.
routes.fa: Bill Joiner, Leslie Nelson, and Dave Seman, May 1974
routes.season: Mar-May (spring); some winter ascents when avalanche conditions allow
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike/snowshoe the Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) to Colchuck Lake's north shore for a base camp. From the lake, head toward the lowest protrusion of rock roughly in the middle of the face and bear slightly left to the entrance of the first (Hidden) couloir.
routes.descent: Aasgard Pass walk-off
routes.descentText: From the top of the third couloir, angle right along the summit ridge to the true summit, then descend right to a notch and take a direct line down to Aasgard Pass, following it back to Colchuck Lake.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single long day from a Colchuck Lake camp: pre-dawn start, 5-9 hours from the lake to the summit, then descend via Aasgard Pass.
routes.bail: Downclimb or rappel the couloir just climbed back to the Colchuck Glacier before committing to the steep Runnels; retreat becomes harder once above the Runnels crux.
routes.turnaround: The Runnels crux and upper couloirs are exposed to rockfall/icefall that increases through the day as the face warms — set a turnaround time and be prepared to retreat down the couloir already climbed rather than push through deteriorating conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is spring (roughly March-May) when the couloirs and Runnels hold consolidated snow/ice; some parties climb in winter if avalanche conditions are stable, but the route is not recommended for beginners given the runout snow/ice sections.
routes.detailedRack: Medium rack including small nuts, 4-5 pitons, cams .5-2in, plus 4-5 ice screws and a few pickets for the moderate snow sections.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pitons for the steep Runnels pitches (70-80°); rock gear mainly for belay anchors; much of the moderate snow in the three couloirs is soloed or simul-climbed with picket protection, so this climb demands comfort with sustained runout terrain.
whatToBring: Extra layers — the route is on a shaded north face
proTips: Climb early in the season before the ice runnels melt out
Evaluate the Runnels' ice coverage from Colchuck Lake before committing — look for complete ice coverage on the rock band
Move fast/simul-climb the moderate snow sections to limit exposure to rockfall
watchOut: Avalanche hazard on the approach and lower couloirs
The crux Runnels can be thin or verglassed depending on the year
Increasing rockfall/icefall as the face warms through the day
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain
Rockfall/icefall in the Runnels
Variable ice thickness year to year

pitchDetail.notes: About 800 ft, trends south from ~6,400 ft; retains snow into midsummer
~3 pitches (140m) of steep ice/mixed climbing linking the first and second couloirs — the technical crux
Over 600 ft
A few hundred feet, exits via mixed or steep snow pitch depending on conditions
From the top, go right along the ridge to the true summit

routes.corrections: Pitch count corrected from 12 to 20 per Mountain Project's route page (matches the Mountaineers.org area description of a '20-pitch' route). FA identified as Bill Joiner, Leslie Nelson, and Dave Seman, May 1974 (not present in the original data), corroborated by American Alpine Institute's route profile.

routes.overview: A long ridge line up the lower-right side of Dragontail's north face, trending left to the summit on clean granite interspersed with looser scrambling.
routes.beta: A sustained IV 5.9 alpine rock outing with a well-known offwidth crux early on (needs a #6 Camalot), several good crack/face pitches near the 'Ball Bearing Amphitheater' (a year-round water source), and long stretches of low-5th ridge climbing/scrambling to the top. The Fin Direct variation (added 1975) offers harder, more sustained climbing.
routes.fa: FA 1970 — M. Weigelt and J. Bonneville (Fin Direct variation 1975 — P. Cruver and K. Lewis).
routes.rappels: 2 rappels (~30m) from a cairned station used in icy conditions in lieu of the walk-off
objHaz: loose rock on upper ridge scrambling
long, committing ridge with limited retreat
routes.face: North face, lower right side (ridge left of the white slabs), trending to the summit
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike the Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) to Colchuck Lake, then continue to the base of the ridge via the Colchuck Glacier moraine.
routes.descent: Same as Serpentine Arête — south-side walk-off to Aasgard Pass
routes.descentText: Descend snow/scree on the south side to Aasgard Pass and down to Colchuck Lake; in icy conditions, two ~30m rappels from a cairned station near the top of the ridge (~47.479°N, 120.832°W) reach a ledge system leading north to Aasgard Pass.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: approach to Colchuck Lake, camp. Day 2: alpine start, climb the ridge, descend via Aasgard Pass.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ridge is difficult once past the lower pitches; the two-rope rappel option from the cairned station near the top of the ridge is the main documented bail/alternate-descent route.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, committing ridge climb with limited retreat options above the lower pitches — set a firm turnaround time based on daylight and party speed, and favor the rappel-descent option over pushing on if conditions turn icy.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a summer rock climb, roughly July-September, once the glacier approach is in reasonable condition.
routes.detailedRack: Medium rack to #3.5 Camalot plus a #6 Camalot specifically for the P2 offwidth.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack for the clean granite lower pitches; the offwidth pitch is the main gear-specific crux, requiring a big cam (#6 Camalot).
whatToBring: Extra long runners for the ridge traverse sections
proTips: Bring a #6 Camalot for the offwidth on P2
Simul-climb the low-5th ridge sections (P5-6, P11-12) to save time
The Fin Direct variation adds sustained 5.9 climbing for parties wanting more technical climbing
watchOut: Loose rock on upper ridge scrambling sections
Route-finding can be tricky through the ridge traverse sections
knownHazards: Loose rock
Long, committing ridge with limited retreat options

routes.corrections: Pitch count conflicts across sources: Mountain Project lists 12 (used here, corrected from the original 15); a 1971 American Alpine Journal account of the FA describes 21 pitches at NCCS IV, F9, and dates the first ascent to August 1971 rather than 1970. The given 1970 Weigelt/Bonneville FA was kept since it is corroborated independently by Mountain Project, but the AAJ date/pitch-count conflict is flagged here.

routes.overview: The lower, 'hidden' entry couloir of the Triple Couloirs system — not visible from Colchuck Lake — gaining Dragontail's north face before the technical Runnels crux.
routes.beta: About 800 vertical feet of 40-50° snow/ice starting near 6,400 ft, trending south, that retains coverage into midsummer. It ends at a steep rock band (the base of the Runnels) where it joins the rest of the Triple Couloirs line. No source reviewed documents it as an independent, standalone route.
routes.face: North face (lower/entry couloir of the Triple Couloirs line)
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike the Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) to Colchuck Lake, then approach the couloir entrance above the south end of the lake at ~6,400 ft.
routes.descent: Aasgard Pass (if continuing to the summit via Triple Couloirs)
routes.descentText: If continuing to the summit via the full Triple Couloirs line, descend via Aasgard Pass back to Colchuck Lake; if bailing from partway up the Hidden Couloir, downclimb the snow/ice back to the Colchuck Glacier.
routes.bail: Downclimb the moderate snow/ice back to the Colchuck Glacier before the steep Runnels band above.
routes.turnaround: Assess ice/snow coverage on the rock band above (the Runnels) from Colchuck Lake before committing; turn around if that section looks thin or if conditions are deteriorating, since retreat becomes harder once above the couloir.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is spring (roughly March-May) when the couloir holds consolidated snow/ice, as for Triple Couloirs overall.
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack (screws/pickets); typically climbed as part of the full Triple Couloirs rack rather than alone.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly moderate snow/ice (40-50°) protected with pickets/screws; a short steep step near the top of the couloir is the main technical section.
whatToBring: Early start to catch firm snow
Avalanche assessment tools if conditions are questionable
proTips: Best climbed as the entry to the full Triple Couloirs line rather than as a standalone objective
Assess ice coverage in the rock band above from Colchuck Lake before starting
watchOut: Retains snow/ice into midsummer but coverage becomes inconsistent late season
A steep step near the top of the couloir
knownHazards: Avalanche hazard
Funnels rockfall/icefall from the terrain above

pitchDetail.notes: Trends south for ~800 vertical ft from ~6,400 ft
Near the top of the couloir before reaching the rock band that separates it from the second couloir

routes.corrections: No source documents 'Hidden Couloir' as an independent named route with its own FA/grade history. Every source (Mountain Project, American Alpine Institute, alpinedave.com) describes it as the lower, unseen-from-the-lake entry couloir of the Triple Couloirs line (the first of the three couloirs). Fields here describe that couloir section rather than a verified standalone climb; pitches/FA left null accordingly.

routes.overview: A direct line up the lowest, tallest point of Dragontail's north face, between Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge — climbable as a moderate rock route in summer or a sustained ice/mixed line in winter-spring.
routes.beta: Originally a summer rock route with a different start (4th/low-5th class with a couple of 5.7 crux moves); in recent years it has become popular as a IV WI3+ M4 ice-mixed outing following runnels that split the dark rock band two-thirds up the face, finishing through steep snow or granite slabs to the summit.
routes.season: Feb-Apr (winter/spring ice-mixed); also climbable as a summer rock route
routes.face: North face, at the low point of the face between Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike/snowshoe the Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) to Colchuck Lake, then approach the base of the route just right of the toe of the north face via the Colchuck Glacier.
routes.descent: Aasgard Pass
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via Aasgard Pass; parties report glissading much of the snowfield below the pass in good conditions, reaching Colchuck Lake basecamp in under 2.5 hours from the summit.
routes.itinerary: Single long day from a Colchuck Lake camp: pre-dawn start, sustained climbing up the runnels/mixed terrain, summit, then descend/glissade via Aasgard Pass.
routes.bail: Rappel the line of ascent using ice-screw/rock anchors; the route has limited natural bail points on the steep central face once above the lower ramp.
routes.turnaround: Conditions on the runnels vary significantly year to year and can be thin or wet — assess ice quality low on the route and be willing to retreat rather than push into deteriorating mixed terrain higher up.
routes.bestSeason: Winter/spring (roughly February-April) for the ice-mixed line, when the runnels are well-frozen; the original rock line can also be climbed in summer.
routes.detailedRack: Summer: medium rack to 3in. Winter/spring: single rack, 4+ ice screws, pitons as conditions dictate, 70m rope.
routes.proNeeds: Highly conditions-dependent protection — ice screws when climbing frozen, rock gear when dry; the crux mixed pitch (M4) wants a mix of both.
whatToBring: Approach skis or snowshoes possible in winter
Extra layers for the shaded north face
proTips: Popular as a winter/spring ice-mixed testpiece — check recent trip reports for current conditions before committing
Glissade the Aasgard Pass snowfield on descent when conditions allow
watchOut: Highly conditions-dependent — ice/neve coverage varies year to year
Sustained steep climbing directly up the tallest part of the north face with limited bail options
knownHazards: Avalanche hazard in winter/spring
Rockfall/icefall
Sparse or variable protection in the runnels

pitchDetail.notes: Starts just right of the toe of the face
1,000+ ft through runnels splitting the dark rock band two-thirds up the face
Includes an ice chimney; multiple variations exist, some traversing right toward rock features or into the third couloir
Granite slabs/snow to the summit

routes.corrections: Original data listed only '5.9 alpine rock.' Sources (Mountain Project, trip reports) show the summer rock line is closer to 4th/low-5th class with a couple of 5.7 crux moves, and the route is now primarily climbed as a IV WI3+ M4 ice/mixed line in winter-spring; rockGrade corrected to 5.7 and iceGrade WI3+ added, alpineGrade set to IV. No reliable FA party/year could be sourced despite the descriptive 'Gerber-Sink' name implying two first ascensionists.

routes.overview: A distinct, less-traveled ice-mixed couloir on Dragontail's northeast aspect. Note: this is a different feature from 'Pandora's Box' (the W Couloir near Colchuck Col), which sources describe as a separate, much easier snow couloir — see corrections.
routes.beta: About 2,000 feet of technical terrain culminating in a sustained, loose, sparsely-protected mixed crux (WI2, M5 R) before easier terrain leads to the summit ridge.
routes.rappels: 1 rappel (~30-40m) from an anchor near the exit notch
objHaz: high wind exposure near the summit
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike to Colchuck Lake, staying on the right side of the moraine to reach the couloir entrance; many parties approach in the dark to reach the couloir before the sun softens the snow/ice.
routes.descent: Rappel into couloir, then Aasgard Pass
routes.descentText: A single ~30-40m rappel from an anchor near the exit notch regains easier terrain; downclimb to easy ground above the glacier and descend via Aasgard Pass back to Colchuck Lake.
routes.itinerary: Single long day from the trailhead or a Colchuck Lake camp: pre-dawn approach, climb the couloir, rappel/downclimb to the exit notch, traverse to the summit ridge, descend via Aasgard Pass.
routes.bail: Rappel from the established anchor near the exit notch (~30-40m) back into the couloir.
routes.turnaround: The mixed crux is loose and sparsely protected — turn around before committing to it if conditions look drier/looser than expected or if the party is behind schedule, since retreat above the crux is limited.
routes.bestSeason: Condition dependent; documented ascents span late fall into spring. Assess recent conditions closely before attempting, as the route can be very different between seasons.
routes.detailedRack: Light rack: two short ice screws, a set of nuts, and cams .2-1in.
routes.proNeeds: Sparse, R-rated protection on the mixed crux — expect long runouts on loose rock; screws are mainly useful in the lower/upper snow-ice sections.
whatToBring: Warm layers — the northeast aspect stays shaded and cold
proTips: Start in the dark to reach the couloir before the sun softens snow/ice
Stay on the right side of the moraine on approach
watchOut: Loose, friable rock on the mixed crux pitches
Sparse protection on the crux (R-rated)
High wind exposure near the summit ridge
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose holds
Runout/sparse protection on crux mixed terrain
Avalanche hazard on the approach couloir

pitchDetail.notes: Entry section from the Colchuck Glacier
Loose rock, sparse protection
Steep snow leading to the ridge below the summit
~30-40m rappel from an anchor to regain easier terrain

routes.corrections: The given route name conflates two different, non-equivalent features on Dragontail. 'Pandora's Box' (also called the W Couloir) is an easier snow couloir/notch near Colchuck Col on the peak's SW/south side — described in trip reports as not too technical, climbable with just an axe and strap-on crampons, roughly a moderate snow scramble. The 'Northeast Couloir' is a separate, harder ice-mixed climb (WI2 M5 R, ~2,000 ft) on the north/northeast aspect. Since the given grade (Grade III-IV, AI3) matches the harder NE Couloir rather than Pandora's Box, this entry is filled using NE Couloir sources. A Mountain Project 'Northeast Couloir' page listing an FA (Skip Edmunds, Clark Gerhardt, Paula Kregel, Greg Markov, Jan 1975) could not be confidently confirmed as this same Dragontail route — its own description places it above the 'North Buttress Couloir,' which is a known Colchuck Peak feature — so that FA was not used here and fa is left null.

blurb: Little Annapurna (8,485 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range, within the Enchantments area of Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Cascade Range). It was first climbed in 1947 by Ellensburg's Sherpa Climbing Club, who named it for the resemblance between its south face and that of the Himalayan Annapurna. Unlike the technical rock routes on neighboring Dragontail and Colchuck Peaks, Little Annapurna is reached by a non-technical scramble, making it one of the most frequently climbed summits in the Enchantments core zone.

routes.overview: A long, non-technical Class 2-3 scramble up Little Annapurna's broad south-facing slopes from the upper Enchantment basin above Aasgard Pass — widely regarded as the easiest of the major summits in the Enchantments core zone.
routes.beta: From the Stuart/Colchuck Lake Trailhead, hike to Colchuck Lake and grind up the steep boulder-and-scree gully of Aasgard Pass to reach the upper Enchantment basin. From the pass, head roughly south-southwest past Isolation and Tranquil Lakes toward Little Annapurna's northeast ridge, then follow cairns up open granite slabs and talus on the peak's south side to the summit -- one of three rocky high points on the ridge, with the true summit being the middle one. The line itself involves minimal exposure and only occasional Class 3 moves, but Aasgard Pass is the physical and route-finding crux of the day.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road about 8.5 mi, turn left on Eightmile Road (FR 7600), then stay right onto FR 7601 and continue about 4 mi to the Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,701 ft). Hike the Lake Stuart Trail 2.5 mi, then turn left (south) onto the Colchuck Lake Trail for 1.8 mi to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft) -- about 4.3 mi and roughly 1,900 ft of gain from the car.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: back down through the upper basin, down the Aasgard Pass boulder field to Colchuck Lake, then out the Colchuck Lake/Lake Stuart Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: There is no shortcut -- descend the same South Slopes line back to the saddle above Aasgard Pass, then carefully down-climb the loose boulder-and-scree gully of the pass itself, which trip reports consistently describe as slower and more tedious than the ascent, especially with lingering snow or ice. Continue past Colchuck Lake and back out the Colchuck Lake/Lake Stuart Trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most commonly done as a very long single day (many parties report 11-13+ hours car-to-car) with a pre-dawn trailhead start around 4-5 AM. Alternatively, parties holding an Enchantment Permit Area overnight permit camp at Colchuck Lake or in the Core Zone/upper basin and day-trip the summit from camp.
routes.bail: Above Aasgard Pass there is no faster way down than retracing the ascent line back to the pass and down to Colchuck Lake; turn back toward the pass early if weather or time is a concern, since down-climbing Aasgard Pass after dark or in a storm is hazardous.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if afternoon thunderstorms are building, if the party cannot be off the exposed summit slopes and back down Aasgard Pass before dark, or if the pass holds hard snow/ice beyond the group's comfort, experience, or gear.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the most reliable window, once seasonal snow has cleared from Aasgard Pass and the upper basin; the pass can hold hard, unconsolidated snow or ice into July in high-snowpack years, and fast-building afternoon thunderstorms are a common summer hazard above treeline.
routes.comms: Multiple trip reports note no cell service at the trailhead or anywhere along the approach and route; a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is recommended for emergencies.
objHaz: lingering snow/ice on Aasgard Pass into early summer
routes.fa: Sherpa Climbing Club of Ellensburg, 1947 -- first recorded ascent of Little Annapurna (peak first ascent; the exact line is not documented, but this South Slopes/basin approach is the peak's easiest and most commonly used line, and the one that gave rise to the peak's name).
routes.detailedRack: No technical climbing rack is needed -- this is an unroped scramble. Bring trekking poles for the steep, loose terrain on Aasgard Pass, and carry an ice axe with microspikes or crampons if snow or hard neve persists on the pass, which is common into July in heavier snow years.
whatToBring: sun hat and sunscreen (route is south-facing and largely exposed)
extra layers for wind and afternoon storms
Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
self-issued day-use permit from the trailhead kiosk
microspikes and/or ice axe if snow lingers on Aasgard Pass
proTips: Start pre-dawn (many parties car-camp at the trailhead) to be off the exposed south-facing rock and summit slopes before afternoon thunderstorms build.
The summit ridge has three rocky high points -- the true summit is the middle one, so don't stop at the first outcrop reached.
Follow cairns closely through the Core Zone to avoid unnecessary bushwhacking and backtracking.
Budget extra time for the descent of Aasgard Pass -- it is consistently reported as more tedious and slower than the climb up.
watchOut: Aasgard Pass gains roughly 2,000 ft in under a mile up a loose boulder/scree gully and can hold hard, unconsolidated snow or ice well into summer.
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly above treeline in the Enchantments -- plan to be off exposed terrain by early-to-mid afternoon.
There is no maintained trail above Aasgard Pass; route-finding across granite slabs and talus is required, and cairns can be hard to follow in poor visibility.
No cell service has been reported at the trailhead or along the route -- carry a satellite communicator.
knownHazards: Loose talus and scree, especially on Aasgard Pass, create rockfall and slip hazards for parties above and below.
Remnants of the Snow Creek Glacier persist on Little Annapurna's north slope, though the standard South Slopes route stays clear of them.
Exposure to lightning above treeline during summer convective storms is a recurring concern on this peak.

pitchDetail.notes: 4.3 mi via the Lake Stuart Trail (2.5 mi) then the Colchuck Lake Trail (1.8 mi), gaining roughly 1,900 ft to the lake at 5,570 ft.
Gains roughly 2,270 ft in under a mile up a loose boulder-and-scree gully between Dragontail Peak and Little Annapurna; can hold hard snow/ice into summer.

pitchDetail.pitch: Aasgard Pass to the upper basin below Little Annapurna
pitchDetail.notes: Cross the open upper Enchantment basin near Isolation and Tranquil Lakes, then turn south/southwest toward Little Annapurna's northeast ridge.

Ascend granite slabs and blocks on the peak's south side/slopes to the summit, following cairns; occasional Class 3 moves are possible depending on the exact line chosen. The true summit is the middle of three rocky high points.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Road and Eightmile Road/FR 7600) to Stuart Lake Trailhead
road.status: Gravel Forest Service road, passenger-car accessible in summer
road.seasonalGate: Not gated, but typically snow-impassable in winter and spring; usually clear by June
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road about 8.5 mi, turn left on Eightmile Road (FR 7600), stay right onto FR 7601, and continue about 4 mi to the trailhead. Parking is very limited and fills before sunrise in summer.

climate.typical: Dry, sunny summers with cold, snowy winters typical of the east-slope Washington Cascades; the route is mostly snow-free July-September but afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack commonly lingers into June on Aasgard Pass and in the upper basin; travel there requires mountaineering skills and gear.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep); warm, sunny days with a real risk of fast-building afternoon thunderstorms above treeline.
bySeason.fall: Cooler, often more stable weather into early October, but early-season snow and ice are possible at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Deep snowpack and avalanche terrain on Aasgard Pass, plus short daylight, make this a serious winter mountaineering objective rather than a scramble.

access.permit: Free self-issued day-use permit at the trailhead kiosk for day trips; overnight camping anywhere within the Enchantment Permit Area (Colchuck Zone or Core Zone) requires a reserved permit via the recreation.gov lottery during the quota season (roughly May 15-Oct 31)

access.closures: Dogs are prohibited throughout the Enchantment Permit Area, including the Stuart Lake and Colchuck Lake Trails; no campfires above 5,000 ft or within 1/2 mile of any lake; the access road and trail are typically not snow-free until June

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (many parties start by 4-5 AM from the trailhead) to be off Aasgard Pass and the summit before afternoon thunderstorms build
