routes.corrections: Given elevation of 8,485 ft matches USGS-derived figures (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn); some hiking-guide/trip-report sites (WTA, various trip reports) cite an older/rounded 8,440 ft, which appears superseded. Approach distance and elevation-gain figures also vary noticeably by source (one-way distance to summit reported as 7.3 mi by WTA vs. ~8.1 mi implied by other guides; gain reported from 5,018 ft (WTA) up to 5,800-6,600 ft round-trip cumulative in other trip-report sources) -- likely due to different measurement start points and cumulative-vs-net gain methodology. gainFt/distKm here use WTA's dedicated trail-data figures as the most consistent single source; treat exact mileage/gain as approximate.

blurb: McClellan Peak (8,368 ft) forms part of the south rim of the Enchantments basin in the Stuart Range, a subset of the Cascade Range, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Chelan County, Washington. The peak is composed of Late Cretaceous tonalitic pluton (granite-like) rock typical of the Enchantment batholith. It was first climbed July 5, 1946 by Ken Prestrud, Keith Rankin, and William Herston via its easiest (Class 3) line, and is named for Army officer George B. McClellan, who explored the area in 1853.

routes.fa: Ken Prestrud, Keith Rankin, William Herston — July 5, 1946 (peak first ascent, via this easiest/standard Class 3 line; not confirmed as line-specific in sources)
objHaz: loose rock/talus on the unmarked approach
exposed traverse near Perfection Lake and the summit block
icy/wet slab sections in early and late season
avalanche and wet-slide hazard on approach snow slopes into June
residual snow patches with runouts above cliff bands
routes.season: Jul-Sep prime; some parties go before mid-June in low-snow years (ice axe/crampons required then) or in October for larch color
routes.face: Southwest side of the summit block, above Perfection Lake in the Enchantments core basin
routes.overview: A long, scenic alpine scramble across the south rim of the Enchantments: gain the Upper Enchantments basin via Aasgard Pass (or the Snow Lakes Trail), cross talus and slabs past Perfection Lake toward McClellan Ridge, then climb an exposed Class 3-4 pitch on the summit block's southwest side to the small, airy summit.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Lake, climb the steep Aasgard Pass gully to the pass, then drop gently into the gentler, rocky Upper Enchantments basin. Traverse east past Perfection Lake toward the ridge connecting to McClellan, keeping west of the distinct rock tower known as 'The Prong.' Gain the ridge on Class 2-3 talus/slabs, cross to the south side, and finish with a short, exposed Class 3 (some report low Class 4) scramble up the summit block's SW side. Descend via an easier Class 2-3 line on the block's south/east side.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (FR 7601, ~3,400 ft), hike the Colchuck Lake Trail past Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft) and climb the steep Aasgard Pass gully to the pass (7,841 ft). Alternatively, approach from the north via the Snow Lakes Trail (~10 miles) into the core Enchantments basin. Both approaches converge in the Upper Enchantments basin below McClellan's south rim.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the SW side of the summit block to the ridge, then either retrace the route back over Aasgard Pass to Colchuck Lake, or continue an Enchantments traverse down through the core basin and out the Snow Lakes Trail (this requires a car shuttle or a long road walk between the two trailheads).
routes.descentText: Down-climb the Class 3 summit block on its south/east side (easier than the SW ascent line), regain the ridge, and drop back toward Perfection Lake and Aasgard Pass. One documented option continues north through the Enchantments core basin and down the Snow Lakes Trail to the Icicle Creek road corridor instead of retracing to Colchuck Lake; this point-to-point option needs two vehicles or a long roadside return. Avoid the alternate line to the east of the summit block, which trip reports describe as having 'much more exposure than necessary.'
routes.itinerary: Most parties backpack in and camp one to two nights (Colchuck Lake, the Enchantments core, or Snow Lakes zone, via the Enchantment Permit Area lottery) and day-climb McClellan from camp. Strong parties have completed the full Stuart Lake TH -> Aasgard Pass -> McClellan summit -> Snow Lakes TH traverse car-to-car in a single day, documented at roughly 11.5 hours.
routes.bail: The route is a straightforward reversal below the final summit block — down-climb the SW scramble to the ridge and retreat the way you came toward Aasgard Pass (or back into the basin); there is no fixed rappel/bail gear on route, so turn back before deteriorating weather or wet rock makes the exposed scrambling near the summit block unsafe.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach (multiple hours just to reach Aasgard Pass or the Enchantments basin from either trailhead), set a firm turnaround time before committing to the exposed summit-block scramble so the descent back over Aasgard Pass or out through the Enchantments/Snow Lakes isn't finished after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Prime window is roughly July through September once the approach has melted out. Parties going before mid-June face deep snow on Aasgard Pass and the basin (ice axe and crampons/microspikes effectively mandatory, with wet-slide and avalanche hazard). A handful of parties climb in October for the Enchantments' larch color, though early-season snow can return by then.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent through most of the Enchantments basin and on the approach; a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is recommended rather than relying on a phone.
routes.detailedRack: This is unroped scrambling terrain — no climbing rack is carried. A helmet is considered necessary for the summit block and for any party traveling below others on the loose talus/scree. Ice axe (plus crampons or microspikes) is required whenever snow covers the Aasgard Pass gully or basin approach, typically into June.
gear: map/compass or GPS (off-trail travel above the basin)
whatToBring: extra water (none available once off-trail above the basin)
layers for wind and weather on the exposed ridge
trekking poles for the talus/scree approach
proTips: Keep west of the distinctive rock tower known as 'The Prong' on the ascent toward McClellan Ridge, per multiple trip reports.
The SW/west side of the summit block (Class 3, some report short Class 4 moves) is the recommended line; an alternate line to the east has 'much more exposure than necessary' and is not recommended.
In low-snow years before mid-June, climbing the snow slopes directly can save time versus talus-hopping, but bring traction and an axe.
Many parties combine McClellan with neighboring Enchantment peaks (Little Annapurna, Cannon Mountain) in one long day from a basin camp.
watchOut: The steep snow/rock section below the ridge becomes icy and dangerous late in the season.
A large wet, down-sloping slab near the summit block has been described as having 'unforgivable exposure' in wet conditions.
There is no maintained trail beyond the basin — route-finding across boulders and talus is required.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer; the ridge and summit block are exposed to lightning.
knownHazards: Loose rock (talus/scree) on the unmarked approach to the summit block.
Avalanche and wet-slide hazard on the Aasgard Pass gully and basin snow slopes in spring/early summer.
Exposed, airy Class 3-4 scrambling on the summit block with real fall consequences.
Residual snow patches with runouts above cliff bands persisting into early summer.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Road and Eightmile Road/FR 7600)
road.status: gravel, rough and washboarded; high-clearance recommended
road.seasonalGate: gated closed to vehicles roughly November through May
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road about 8.5 miles, turn left on Eightmile Road (FR 7600), stay right on FR 7601, then continue about 4 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead parking area (roughly 20-30 spaces).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Cascade East / Stevens Pass forecast zone
climate.typical: Dry, sunny summers with cool nights at 7,000-8,000 ft; afternoon thunderstorms are common July-August; snow can linger on north-facing gullies and the Aasgard Pass approach into June.

bySeason.spring: Deep snow on Aasgard Pass and the basin approach through May-June; avalanche and wet-slide hazard; ice axe/crampons mandatory.
bySeason.summer: Prime season; mostly snow-free by July with warm days, but be ready for sudden afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Larches turn gold through the Enchantments in early-mid October; the route is often still climbable in dry years but early snow is possible.
bySeason.winter: Access road (FR 7601) is gated closed; the peak is effectively inaccessible without a long winter approach.

access.landManager: USFS Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Enchantment Permit Area)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass required for trailhead parking; Recreation.gov reservation fee applies to overnight permits
access.permit: Enchantment Permit Area overnight permit required May 15-Oct 31 (lottery via Recreation.gov, Stuart/Colchuck zone gives access to McClellan); free self-issued day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead otherwise
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Dogs are prohibited throughout the Enchantment Permit Area; FR 7601 access road is closed to vehicles in winter

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start given the long approach

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom regional 911 dispatch for Chelan County Sheriff SAR: (509) 663-9911 (non-emergency) or 911
emergency.rangerStation: Leavenworth Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24/7 emergency department, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through most of the Enchantments basin and approach. Chelan County Mountain Rescue (volunteer) assists the Sheriff's SAR unit on technical alpine rescues in this area.

routes.corrections: Sources vary on summit elevation: Wikipedia and most peak-list sites give 8,368 ft (close to the 8,367 ft supplied), but a LiDAR-based resurvey cited by trailcatjim.com gives 8,363-8,364 ft. Elevation-gain figures for this route also vary widely by chosen approach and starting camp — as low as ~3,200 ft round-trip from a basin camp near Snow Lake, versus ~5,900-8,200 ft for a full trailhead-to-summit push via different trailheads (Stuart/Colchuck, Snow Lakes, or Ingalls Creek). The 5,929 ft gainFt reported here reflects one documented Stuart Lake TH-to-summit ascent (yellowleaf.org) and should be treated as approximate, not a fixed figure for every approach option.

blurb: Enchantment Peak (8,538 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range of the Cascades, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness' famed "Enchantments" basin near Leavenworth, WA. It has two summit points — a slightly higher NE point (a short, broad 3rd-class block) and a narrow, more technical SW point roughly 1,500 ft away — confirmed by a 2019 theodolite/LIDAR survey to differ by only 6-7 feet. Like most of the surrounding Enchantment peaks, it was first climbed by Fred Beckey during his 1948 exploration of the Cashmere Crags.

routes.overview: An exposed 2nd/3rd-class scramble that gains Enchantment Peak's true (NE) summit from Prusik Pass via the peak's south/southwest slopes — the natural, easiest line up the mountain and the one used on most ascents.
routes.beta: From a high camp in the Core Enchantment Zone (commonly near Inspiration/Sprite/Perfection Lakes), climbers cross to Prusik Pass and work west and then up the southwest/south flank of the peak. The going is a straightforward talus/rock walk-up with occasional hands-on 2nd-to-3rd-class scrambling and no sustained technical difficulty until a short, airy 3rd-class (some parties call it 3rd/4th) summit block. The higher NE summit is a compact, flat ~6x6 ft platform; a separate, more serious SW summit lies about 1,500 ft away and involves sustained 4th-class-to-low-5th climbing on a narrow fin — most parties do not attempt it.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (end of FR 7601, off Icicle Creek Road) hike the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trail ~4 mi to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft), then climb the steep, loose Aasgard Pass talus/scree gully (~1.1 mi, ~2,200-2,400 ft gain) to the pass (7,841 ft) and the Core Enchantment Zone. From there traverse the basin past the Enchantment lakes to Prusik Pass (~7,500 ft) and turn up the south/southwest slopes of Enchantment Peak to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back to Prusik Pass and down through the Core Zone to Aasgard Pass and Colchuck Lake.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the summit block and southwest slopes back to Prusik Pass, then retrace the traverse through the Core Enchantment Zone to the top of Aasgard Pass. Descend Aasgard Pass with care — it is steep, loose scree/talus and a common source of falls and rockfall injuries — then follow the trail back past Colchuck Lake to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties combine Enchantment Peak with a 2-3 day backpacking permit trip through the Core Enchantment Zone (Colchuck Lake -> Aasgard Pass -> core basin camp -> day-scramble Enchantment Peak, often paired with Little Annapurna and/or McClellan Peak -> exit via Aasgard Pass or the Snow Lakes trail). Strong parties have also done it as a very long single day (one recorded car-to-car time was roughly 13 hours).
routes.bail: Below the short summit block, retreat is simply downclimbing the way you came; there is no established rappel line on the standard route. From anywhere on the peak or in the Core Zone, the primary bail-out is back over Aasgard Pass to Colchuck Lake and the Stuart Lake Trailhead — there is no shorter emergency exit from the core basin.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and return both cross Aasgard Pass's steep, exposed scree — which is far more hazardous descending in the dark or in bad weather — set a firm turnaround time that guarantees getting back over the pass and off exposed terrain well before dark or the onset of afternoon thunderstorms.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is roughly July through October, once seasonal snow has cleared from Aasgard Pass and the upper mountain. Many climbers specifically target the first one to two weeks of October for the larches' peak gold color, though alpine conditions (freezing nights, fast-moving weather, possible early snow) return quickly at elevation by then. Spring and early-summer ascents are possible but involve steep, consolidated or icy snow on Aasgard Pass and the peak's rock sections, requiring an ice axe/crampons and avalanche judgment.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in the Core Enchantment Zone or on the peak; several trip reports note coverage only returns near the Snow Lakes trailhead on the opposite side of the range. A satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin inReach) is commonly recommended given the lack of any short bail-out from the core basin.
routes.proNeeds: The standard route is an unroped scramble for competent parties; no fixed protection or rack is used. A rope, a few slings/biners, and basic protection are sometimes carried for the exposed summit block or if attempting the harder SW summit, which one party described as beyond what a 30 m rope and a light rack were comfortable for.
whatToBring: Enchantment Permit Area overnight permit (if camping in season) or day-use wilderness permit
satellite communicator (no cell service in the Core Zone)
water treatment (lake water available at camps, but carry capacity for the dry basin traverse)
layers for fast-changing alpine weather and afternoon thunderstorms
proTips: The direct scramble line between Lake Reginleif and Lake Sigrun is shorter than going all the way out to Prusik Pass and back, but trades distance for more exposure.

The NE summit (true high point) is the short, obvious 3rd-class scramble; do not mistake the more dramatic-looking SW summit block for the objective unless prepared for sustained 4th/low-5th climbing.
Snow lingering into early summer on the peak's rock steps can be more hazardous than the bare-rock scramble itself — several parties turned back on snow-covered class 3/4 terrain near the SW summit.
watchOut: Aasgard Pass is loose, steep scree/talus with real rockfall risk to parties above and below — helmets are advised.
The SW summit's narrow fin has caught out under-equipped parties; it is significantly harder and more exposed than the standard NE summit route.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Cascades in summer; the peak and Aasgard Pass are fully exposed with no quick retreat.
Spring/early-season snow can obscure the class 3 rock and turn straightforward scrambling into icy, exposed climbing.
knownHazards: Loose rock and talus on Aasgard Pass and the peak's slopes, with rockfall danger to parties both above and below.
No short emergency exit from the Core Enchantment Zone; any bail funnels back over Aasgard Pass.
Avalanche and steep snow hazard on Aasgard Pass and the peak in spring/early-season conditions.
Exposure on the class 3 (and class 3/4 in spots) summit block terrain.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (off Icicle Creek Road) to Stuart Lake Trailhead
road.status: Dirt, sometimes potholed/bumpy forest road; passable to most passenger vehicles in season
road.seasonalGate: Closed to vehicles roughly November through May (winter closure)
road.driveNote: From US-2 at the west edge of Leavenworth, take Icicle Creek Road 8.4 mi, then turn left (south) on FR 7601 and follow it 3.7 mi to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Slopes Central (per one route-report citation; verify current NWAC zone naming before relying on it operationally)
climate.typical: High alpine granite basin climate: cold nights and rapidly changing weather even in summer, afternoon thunderstorm potential, and a short snow-free window roughly July-October.

bySeason.spring: Aasgard Pass and upper mountain typically still snow/ice covered; avalanche hazard on the pass; ice axe/crampons generally needed.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; snow mostly cleared by July; afternoon thunderstorms are the main weather hazard.
bySeason.fall: Cool, often stable weather into September; early October brings peak larch color but also faster-moving storms and possible early snow at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Access road (FR 7601) gated; approach becomes a long winter mountaineering undertaking with avalanche terrain on Aasgard Pass.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or $5/vehicle/day at the Stuart Lake Trailhead; separate Enchantment Permit Area fee for overnight permits in season.
access.permit: Overnight camping anywhere in the Enchantment Permit Area (including the Core Zone) requires a permit May 15-Oct 31, awarded via a recreation.gov lottery that opens in February, with limited daily walk-up permits available through a geofenced mobile lottery near the Leavenworth Ranger Station; free self-issue wilderness permits are required year-round for day use.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking) plus an Enchantment Permit for overnight stays in season.
access.closures: FR 7601 (the final approach road to Stuart Lake Trailhead) is gated and closed to vehicles roughly November-May; dogs and campfires are prohibited within the Enchantment Permit Area.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start, especially for a single-day car-to-car attempt, to be off Aasgard Pass and the peak before afternoon thunderstorms and to avoid descending the pass in the dark.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Ralph Widrig, September 20, 1948 (per American Alpine Club 'New Climbs, Further Firsts in Cashmere Crags' report)
routes.rockGrade: Class 3 (standard NE-summit line); the separate SW summit is sustained class 4 to low 5th
objHaz: rockfall on Aasgard Pass and approach talus
avalanche/steep snow on Aasgard Pass in early season
route-finding through the Core Enchantment Zone
routes.face: South/southwest flank, accessed via Prusik Pass
routes.corrections: Given peak data listed the first ascent as 'May 1948 by Fred Beckey' (solo/unspecified partner). The American Alpine Club's own 'New Climbs, Further Firsts in Cashmere Crags' report instead dates Enchantment Peak's first ascent to September 20, 1948, by Fred Beckey and Ralph Widrig — a specific, later date and a named second climber. The peak's elevation (8,538 ft) and the route's Class 2-3 grade in the supplied data are both corroborated by multiple sources (Wikipedia/USGS-derived elevation; SummitPost, ericsbasecamp.net, and other trip reports on the standard NE-summit scramble) and required no correction. Note also that Enchantment Peak has two summits (NE, the true high point by 6-7 ft per a 2019 theodolite/LIDAR survey, and a separate, harder SW summit ~1,500 ft away); the supplied route is the standard line to the NE (true) summit, not the technical SW summit.

blurb: Prusik Peak (8,008 ft) is a striking granite spire at the west end of The Temple in the Stuart Range, within the Enchantment Lakes core zone of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Leavenworth, Washington. Named for Austrian mountaineer Karl Prusik (inventor of the prusik knot, used by the first-ascent party to gain the summit horn), it is renowned for unusually solid, clean granite. Fred Beckey and Art Holben made the first ascent via the East Route in May 1948; the peak's south face and West Ridge have since become classic Cascades alpine-rock testpieces.

routes.fa: Unknown party, 1957 (guidebook-cited); often attributed to Fred Beckey.
routes.rappels: 4 single-rope raps to the north side, then walk back over Prusik Pass
objHaz: unprotected slab crux (only an old fixed piton for pro)
party traffic/queuing on the popular crux pitch
routes.overview: A moderate, four-pitch ridge line on excellent granite, widely regarded as one of the best easy alpine rock climbs in the Cascades, finishing with an exposed slab and a short summit chimney.
routes.beta: Mostly class 4/low 5th climbing gains the crest of the ridge; the technical crux is a short, unprotected 5.7 friction slab (frequently felt as harder than the grade due to exposure and a single old ring piton), followed by ledge traversing, a 5.6 layback corner, a thin flake, and a narrow summit chimney.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike to Colchuck Lake, climb Aasgard Pass into the Core Enchantment Zone, then traverse toward Prusik Pass on the west side of the peak and gain the base of the West Ridge; alternatively approach via the longer, more gradual Snow Lakes trail from Icicle Creek.
routes.descent: 4 single-rope rappels down the north side back to the start of the route, then walk over Prusik Pass.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make four single-rope rappels down the north side of the peak, which return climbers to the base of the route near packs; from there walk back over Prusik Pass to regain the approach trail.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on most of the route by downclimbing the easy lower pitches; above the crux slab, rappel stations/slings can be built on gear since there are no bolts.
routes.turnaround: Begin early enough to be off the ridge and below treeline before afternoon thunderstorms build, and account for likely delays behind slower parties on the crux slab.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is June through September once the Enchantments basin is snow-free; the route is popular and can see significant crowding and queuing at the crux on summer weekends.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices: a set of nuts, cams to roughly 3 inches (including a #1-2 Camalot for an off-width option near the top), and a few slings for gear/sling anchors since there are no bolts on route.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse on the crux slab pitch (essentially just one old fixed ring piton); the rest of the route takes standard nut and small-to-medium cam placements.
whatToBring: extra layers for wind on the ridge
bug repellent (mosquitoes at Prusik Pass notch)
proTips: A single 60m rope is sufficient; a party of three can tie one climber in mid-rope for speed.
Get on the route early to avoid queuing behind slower parties at the exposed slab crux.
Most of the lower ridge can be simul-climbed on one rope to save time.
watchOut: The crux slab has essentially one piece of fixed gear and feels harder than 5.7 due to exposure.
Rockfall and dislodged blocks are possible where the route sees heavy traffic.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Stuart Range in summer.
knownHazards: Unprotected 12-25 ft friction slab crux with only an old ring piton.
Heavy party traffic can slow ascents and increase objective exposure to weather.

pitchDetail.notes: Easier terrain gaining the crest of the West Ridge, often simul-climbed.
Short, unprotected friction slab crux (~12-25 ft) with one old ring piton; traverse left on ledges under the summit.
Layback up a steep corner, thin flake climbing, then a short narrow chimney to the summit; an off-width 5.8 finger-crack variant exists.

bySeason.fall: Climbable into September/early October with colder temps and a rising chance of early snow.
bySeason.winter: Access road closed; route effectively inaccessible without a long winter approach.

routes.corrections: Several sources (Mountain Project, trip reports) list this as a Grade III route rather than the Grade II given in the source data; set commitment to III accordingly. FA party is not firmly documented in available sources beyond a 1957 date; some pages attribute it to Fred Beckey but this is not conclusively sourced as this specific route's first ascent.

routes.fa: Wayne Wallace and Tim Olsen, 1989.
routes.rappels: Single 60m rappel down the north side, then ~30 min scramble
objHaz: sustained crack/corner climbing with a hard roof move
routes.face: South Face (west side), topping onto the West Ridge
routes.overview: A sustained, high-quality 5-pitch crack-and-corner line up the south face, widely rated one of the best 5.11 alpine free routes in the Enchantments, finishing up the same perfect corner-crack top pitch as several neighboring lines.
routes.beta: Starts just right of the outside corner on the left flank of the south face; climbs a sustained corner and crack system through a hard roof (crux, 5.11a) to an off-width/mantle section, then finishes with a striking corner crack to the West Ridge crest.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass into the Core Enchantment Zone and approach the south face of Prusik Peak (near Gnome Tarn); alternatively approach via the longer Snow Lakes trail.
routes.descent: Single 60m rappel down the north side, then a short scramble back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route on the West Ridge, a single 60m rappel down the north side reaches easier ground, followed by about a 30-minute scramble back to the base of the south face.
routes.bail: Retreat below the roof pitch is difficult given sustained, sparsely-ledged climbing; rappelling the route on gear anchors is the main bail option once established above the first pitch.
routes.turnaround: Plan to top out and begin the descent with enough daylight margin to complete the rappel and scramble before dark or incoming weather.
routes.bestSeason: June through September once the basin is snow-free and the face is dry; the route holds excellent rock quality throughout.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack to 3-4 inches with doubles in finger-to-hand sizes for the sustained crack pitches, plus a set of nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Good protection throughout on gear; the roof pitch and corner crux take small-to-medium cams.
gear: cams to 3-4 in (doubles in mid sizes)
whatToBring: light rack for the approach scramble sections
layers for wind at the col
proTips: The first and last pitches are considered the standout pitches on the route.
Combine with an early start to beat crowds on the popular south face.
watchOut: The roof move on pitch 2 is the technical and psychological crux.
Sustained climbing with limited rest stances on the middle pitches.
knownHazards: Hard, technical roof move well off the ground.
Afternoon storms typical of the Stuart Range in summer.

pitchDetail.notes: ~100 ft crack to stembox, sustained corner; belay under a roof.
~100 ft; hard roof move, hand crack to a horizontal break; gear belay (crux).
~185 ft combined; off-width to a mantle, then an arching seam on a chimney wall.
~150 ft perfect corner crack to the West Ridge top.

routes.fa: FA 1968 — the Stanley-Burgner route.
routes.rappels: 5 raps off slung blocks down the south face (~1 hr with traffic)
objHaz: loose rock possible on gully sections
rope-team traffic on a popular line
routes.overview: The classic six-pitch line up the middle of Prusik's south face, widely called the best moderate route on the wall, finishing with a celebrated left-facing corner pitch to the summit.
routes.beta: Starts about the middle of the base of the south face; climbs a wide tree-lined crack, an exiting crack on knobs, a gully to a chockstone, steep double cracks through a chockstone tunnel, a flaring squeeze-chimney crux, then tops out via a striking left-facing corner to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass into the Core Enchantment Zone and approach the south face of Prusik Peak near Gnome Tarn; alternatively approach via the longer Snow Lakes trail.
routes.descent: Five rappels off slung blocks down the south face, roughly an hour with typical rope-team traffic.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via five rappels off slung anchor blocks down the south face back toward the base of the route; expect delays behind other parties on busy days.
routes.bail: The route can be bailed from most belays by rappelling the same slung-block anchors used for the standard descent, though some anchors may require additional gear/slings to supplement.
routes.turnaround: Track pace against the six pitches and five-rappel descent; turn around with enough margin to be down before dark or approaching storms, especially if queued behind other parties.
routes.bestSeason: June through September once the face is dry; this is one of the most popular lines on the peak and can see significant traffic on summer weekends.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine trad rack to 3-4 inches, including gear suited to the chimney/squeeze section and the chockstone tunnel pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Solid protection throughout on cams and nuts; the squeeze chimney pitch (5.9+) is the main technical and protection crux for many parties.
whatToBring: extra layers for wind at the base
proTips: The final left-facing corner pitch is considered the highlight of the route.
Expect company on this popular line; an early start helps avoid stacking behind other parties.
watchOut: The flaring squeeze chimney on pitch 5 is a common crux/pinch point for traffic.
The chockstone tunnel on pitch 4 requires care with pack/rope management.
knownHazards: Squeeze-chimney crux can be strenuous and awkward with a pack.
Popular route sees heavy traffic that can slow descent rappels.

pitchDetail.notes: ~180 ft wide crack with trees; belay at the tallest tree.
~100 ft crack exiting right on knobs to a gully.
~180 ft gully climbing to a ledge below a chockstone.
~60 ft steep double cracks, tunnel behind a chockstone.
~80 ft flaring squeeze chimney; crux for many parties.
~180 ft amazing left-facing corner to the summit.

routes.corrections: Steph Abegg's trip-report page rates this route 5.9+ overall (with individual pitches to 5.9+) rather than 5.10a; Wikipedia and the given source data both list 5.10a, which is retained here, but the 5.9+ alternate rating should be noted as it appears in at least one other reputable source.

routes.fa: Brooke Sandahl and party, established over a multi-year effort in the 1990s (documented in the Leavenworth guidebook).
routes.rappels: 5 single-rope raps down the north/back side
objHaz: sustained, technical crack crux low on route
loose block in the summit chimney
routes.overview: A sustained, technical six-pitch splitter-crack testpiece on the south face, considered one of the finest hard alpine free climbs in the Stuart Range, linking steep crack systems with airy, committing sequences on perfect white granite.
routes.beta: Six pitches of steep, splitter crack climbing on the south face: a stem-box/twin-crack crux low on the route, knobby corner climbing, an undercling flake/crack system, a wild exposed corner, a short crux section, and a final strenuous flaring hand crack to an easy chimney and the summit.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake trailhead, hike via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass into the Core Enchantment Zone and approach the south face of Prusik Peak near Gnome Tarn; alternatively approach via the longer Snow Lakes trail.
routes.descent: Five single-rope rappels down the back (north) side of the peak.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make five single-rope rappels down the north/back side of Prusik Peak, then traverse back to the base of the south face or over Prusik Pass to the approach trail.
routes.bail: Retreat from mid-route is difficult given sustained, sparsely-protected crack pitches; rappelling on gear anchors from fixed stations (where present) or building new anchors is the main option.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's sustained difficulty and 6-hour-plus round trip from the base, start early enough to top out and complete the descent well before dark or incoming weather.
routes.bestSeason: June through September once the face is dry; the route requires confident 5.11 crack climbing at altitude.
routes.detailedRack: A double rack from small to #2-sized cams (RPs and small wires useful for the stem-box crux), with 1-2 larger pieces for comfort on wider sections.
routes.proNeeds: Well-protected on gear throughout for a route of its difficulty, though the pitch-1 stem box and tips crack require small, precise placements.
whatToBring: extra layers for wind at the base
proTips: Fix-and-follow is a recommended climbing style to move efficiently through the sustained pitches.
Bring doubles in small-to-hand sizes; the route is stingy on rest stances.
watchOut: Pitch 1's stem-box/twin-crack section is the technical crux and is sustained near the route's grade.
A loose block is present in the summit chimney on the final pitch — test holds carefully.
knownHazards: Sustained, committing crack climbing with limited bail options mid-route.
Loose block in the final chimney section.

pitchDetail.notes: 50m; left-slanting crack, delicate tips crux, stem box, twin cracks with liebacks to a bolted belay.
PG13; corner climbing, knobby sections, fixed pin, two bolts, offset tips crack to a ledge anchor.
35m; flake/crack into a corner, jam climbing on quality granite; gear anchor on a large ledge.
30m; corner crack with a mid-pitch crack switch, traverse past a fixed pin, wild pillar mantle.
30m; steep corner crack, knobby slab, bulge section to a small ledge.
30m; offwidth splitter, hand crack, chimney with a loose block, top out to the summit.

road.name: Icicle Road (FR 76) to Eightmile Road (FR 7601)
road.status: Open seasonally, typically May-November
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 gated/closed by snow roughly November-May
road.driveNote: 8.4 mi paved Icicle Road, then ~3.8-4 mi steep washboarded dirt road to the Stuart Lake trailhead; high-clearance recommended.

climate.typical: Warm, dry alpine-summer days with cool nights are typical June-September; afternoon thunderstorm buildup is common in the Stuart Range.

bySeason.spring: Snow-covered through May; road and approach often impassable.
bySeason.summer: Prime window, June-September.
bySeason.fall: Climbable into September/early October, colder, rising early-snow risk.
bySeason.winter: Access road closed; effectively inaccessible.

access.landManager: USFS Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District
access.fees: $5/day or $30/yr Northwest Forest/Recreation Pass for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free self-issued day-use wilderness permit; overnight camping in the Core Enchantment Permit Area requires an advance-reservation permit May 15-October 31
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Recreation Pass
access.closures: Dogs prohibited in the Enchantment Permit Area; access road closed seasonally by snow.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start

sectionBreakdown.note: Approximate base-to-summit climbing time per trip report

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (509) 667-6851; RiverCom 911 dispatches emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth, WA: (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA (24/7 ER); advanced trauma transported to Wenatchee/Seattle/Spokane
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Enchantments core zone; Chelan County Sheriff's SAR and Chelan County Mountain Rescue respond to backcountry incidents, sometimes with helicopter evacuation.

routes.corrections: Given source data lists commitment grade IV and rockGrade 5.11; Mountain Project and multiple trip reports (cascadeclimbers.com title, etc.) grade this a Grade III route at 5.11+ overall (with a 5.11d/12a crux pitch), so commitment was corrected to III and rockGrade refined to 5.11+.

blurb: The Temple is an 8,295-foot granite spire in the Stuart Range of the Cascades, rising above Snow Lakes and Prusik Peak at the west end of Temple Ridge, within the core Enchantments basin of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness in Chelan County. Like the Enchantments' other named granite towers (Prusik Peak, The High Priest, Comet Spire), it offers clean granite scrambling and moderate rock climbing above a cluster of alpine lakes. Fred Beckey made the first ascent of the peak in 1942.

routes.fa: Peak first ascended 1942 by Fred Beckey
objHaz: Loose/rotten rock on the upper ridge and summit block
Exposure on class 4 to low-5th-class terrain with limited protection
Lingering snow/ice in gullies and on shaded slabs into early summer
Afternoon thunderstorms common over the Cascades crest in summer
routes.overview: The South Ridge is the peak's original 1942 line and today's standard route: a long approach through the Enchantments basin followed by class 3-4 scrambling up blocky granite ribs and slabs to a short, exposed class 4/low-5th crux just below the summit.
routes.beta: Parties approach via the Snow Lakes Trail into the Core Enchantments basin, then leave the trail to ascend talus and granite buttresses on Temple's south side. The line climbs mostly class 3-4 terrain - slabs, ledges, and blocky ribs - with exposure increasing near the top, finishing with a short low-5th-class (5.0-5.3, reported inconsistently between sources) section through a ramp/notch onto the small, airy summit. A helmet and a light rack are commonly carried for the final moves; most parties downclimb or make one or two short rappels to retreat.
routes.approach: From the Snow Lakes Trailhead (Icicle Road/Forest Road 7600, about 4.3 miles from Leavenworth), hike the Snow Lakes Trail past Nada Lake and Snow Lakes, then continue into the Core Enchantments basin toward Prusik Peak. From the basin, leave the trail and ascend talus and granite slabs on Temple's south side to gain the ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the south ridge line back to the basin.
routes.descentText: Most parties reverse the route: downclimbing the class 3-4 terrain and making one or two short rappels (a single 30-60m rope is sufficient) through the steepest section near the summit before regaining the basin and hiking out the Snow Lakes Trail.
routes.itinerary: Most parties backpack in and camp in the Core Enchantment zone (overnight permit required), then day-trip to Temple's summit via the South Ridge before continuing an Enchantments traverse or hiking out. A strong, permit-less party can also do it car-to-car in one long day (roughly 16 miles round trip with about 7,000 ft of gain).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward: downclimb back the way you came to the notch/basin. The route has no sustained technical section that can't be reversed by downclimbing or a short rappel.
routes.turnaround: As with any Enchantments objective, set a firm turnaround time that gets the party off exposed granite before afternoon thunderstorms build, and allow enough daylight for the long trail return to the Snow Lakes Trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: The Enchantments are typically snow-free and in prime scrambling condition from July through September; overnight permits are required May 15-October 31, but early-season (May-June) travel can still involve significant snow in approach gullies and on shaded slopes, requiring an ice axe and crampons for safe travel.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack is sufficient given the short technical crux: a few small-to-mid cams, a couple of nuts, and some slings for the low-5th-class section near the summit, plus a 30m (or longer) rope for a belayed move or short rappel.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal - the route is mostly unroped scrambling with one short, low-5th-class section near the summit that most parties either protect with a handful of cams/nuts or solo if comfortable with the exposure.
gear: Approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the long approach
Map/GPS for route-finding through the basin and talus
proTips: Combine Temple with a broader Enchantments traverse to make the long approach worthwhile.
Start early to be off the summit before afternoon thunderstorms common on Cascades granite in summer.
The Core Enchantment permit is extremely competitive - apply in the Feb 15-Mar 1 advance lottery or watch the daily lottery and cancellations on recreation.gov.
watchOut: Loose blocks and rotten rock on the upper ridge
Talus/buttress terrain below the ridge is easy to get off-route on
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Cascades crest in summer
Early-season snow can make gullies and slabs dangerous without an ice axe/crampons
knownHazards: Loose rock on the upper mountain, especially near the summit block
Exposure on class 4/low-5th-class terrain with limited protection
Snow and ice can linger into early summer on shaded approach terrain
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Enchantments during summer

road.name: Icicle Road / Forest Road 7600
road.status: Paved near Leavenworth, transitioning to gravel farther up the valley; generally passable to the Snow Lakes Trailhead in summer
road.seasonalGate: Can be gated/closed at or near the trailhead in winter for snow removal
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road/FR 7600 about 4.3 miles to the Snow Lakes Trailhead.

climate.typical: Dry, sunny alpine summers (Jul-Sep) typical of the Cascades' east-slope rain shadow, with occasional afternoon thunderstorms; winters bring heavy snowpack to the basin.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack lingers into June on approach gullies and shaded slopes; avalanche hazard possible into late spring.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season; generally dry and stable with occasional afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Cooler, often stable weather persists into October with fewer crowds and larch colors; early snow is possible by late October.
bySeason.winter: Deep snow covers the basin and route; the trailhead access road may be gated. Not a winter objective.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Snow Lakes Trailhead day-use parking fee ($5/vehicle/day or $30 annual Recreation Pass); Enchantment Permit Area overnight fees ($6 lottery application + $6 reservation, plus $5/person/day for extra days or added group size)
access.permit: Enchantment Permit Area overnight permit required May 15-October 31 for camping in the Core Enchantment zone (Recreation.gov advance lottery held Feb 15-Mar 1, plus a daily lottery); a self-issued wilderness permit covers day use.
access.passRequired: NW Forest Pass / America the Beautiful interagency pass, or the trailhead day-use fee
access.closures: Icicle Road/Snow Lakes Trailhead can close seasonally for snow removal; Core Zone overnight entry is capped per day under the permit system.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start recommended given the long approach and afternoon-thunderstorm risk

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff via RiverCom dispatch, non-emergency (509) 663-9911; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA - (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA - (509) 548-5815 (24-hour ER)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through most of the Enchantments basin; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended given the long, remote approach.

routes.corrections: Wikipedia lists The Temple's easiest route at YDS class 5.3, while the given route data lists rockGrade 5.0 for the South Ridge; the corrected value (5.3) is used here, but both likely describe the same short crux move near the summit and the discrepancy may reflect grading variation between sources/parties rather than a factual error. Multiple distinctly-named lines exist on this peak (the original 1942 Beckey line described here as the South Ridge, a NE Ridge involving a steep snow gully, and a 'West Side' 5.6 two-pitch route to Temple Ridge's high point), so route-specific approach stats found in individual trip reports vary by which line was climbed; the approach figures given here (~13 km one-way, ~7,000 ft gain) reflect the general Snow Lakes Trailhead approach shared by all of them (computed from the trailhead elevation of 1,300 ft and summit elevation of 8,295 ft), not a South-Ridge-specific survey measurement.

blurb: Cannon Mountain (8,652 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Chelan County, Washington, roughly a mile north of Enchantment Peak within The Enchantments. No maintained trail reaches the top; parties travel cross-country over open granite slabs and benches to a class 2-3 ridge scramble finishing on a friction-slab summit block. Peakbagger/Wikipedia record the first ascent in 1921, though the climbing party is not documented in available sources.

routes.overview: The South Slopes is Cannon Mountain's standard, non-technical line: cross-country travel across open granite terrain on the peak's south side to the Druid Plateau, then a short class 2-3 ridge scramble to a friction-slab summit block.
routes.beta: Cannon has no maintained trail. The most-documented approaches reach a common staging area near Druid Plateau (east of the summit) either from Stuart Lake Trailhead via the Mountaineer Creek valley and Cannon Creek basin (cross-country, brushy lower section with cliff bands), or from the Core Enchantment Zone via Prusik Pass after the longer Snow Lakes Trail approach. From Druid Plateau, parties contour/scramble to the base of the summit block and finish on class 2-3 friction slab and boulders. Snow and verglass can linger on shaded sections into July, adding real exposure to otherwise easy terrain.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road (FR 7600) about 8.5 miles, turn onto Eightmile Road, then FR 7601 about 4 miles to Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,400 ft). Follow the Stuart Lake/Mountaineer Creek trail roughly a mile to a horse-ford area, then leave the trail and head cross-country southeast/east into the Cannon Creek basin and cirque, gaining the Druid Plateau below Cannon's east side before turning to the south-side slopes for the final scramble to the summit block. An alternate, longer approach uses the Snow Lakes Trail (~9 mi, ~4,500 ft gain) into the Core Enchantment Zone, then up to Prusik Pass (~7,463 ft) and northwest across contouring slopes to the same plateau.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent slopes back to Druid Plateau and down into the Cannon Creek basin/Mountaineer Creek trail; no rappels are typically required. Some parties instead descend toward Prusik Pass/Lorelei Lakelets into the Core Enchantment Zone.
routes.descentText: Most parties down-climb the same class 2-3 south-side slopes used on ascent, dropping back to Druid Plateau and retracing the cross-country line through the Cannon Creek basin to the Mountaineer Creek trail and Stuart Lake Trailhead. Parties camping in the Core Enchantment Zone, or caught by weather, sometimes instead drop off Cannon's north/east side toward Lorelei Lakelets and Prusik Pass to regain the core trail network — a documented alternate that trip reports describe as scenic but not necessarily faster, since it trades known terrain for additional cross-country route-finding.
routes.itinerary: Feasible as a very long single day from Stuart Lake Trailhead for fit, experienced parties, but commonly done as an overnight from a Core Enchantment Zone or Colchuck Lake camp, often paired with McClellan Peak, Enchantment Peak, or a Core Zone traverse.
routes.bail: Terrain is class 2-3 throughout, so retreat by simply reversing the ascent line is straightforward in most conditions. Above Druid Plateau, parties can also escape toward Prusik Pass and the Core Enchantment Zone trail network rather than backtracking the full Cannon Creek approach.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before starting and stick to it — the approach is long, largely off-trail, and afternoon thunderstorms are common over the Enchantments in summer; being on the exposed summit block or upper slopes in a storm is a serious hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through October is the practical window: the FR 7601 access road is gated roughly November-May, and snow/avalanche hazard on the approach make earlier attempts inadvisable in most years. Shaded upper slopes can still hold snow or verglass into early July.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness backcountry approach and on the mountain; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.
objHaz: exposed class 3 moves near the summit block
lingering snow/ice on shaded slopes into early summer
cliff bands and dense brush on the lower approach
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the class 2-3 scrambling. A helmet is recommended for loose rock, and an ice axe (with crampons if snow/verglass lingers on the upper slopes) should be carried into early summer.
routes.proNeeds: None — the route is unroped scrambling throughout; a light rope/harness is sometimes carried by less-experienced parties as a personal safety margin near the exposed summit block, but is not standard.
gear: sturdy approach or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee for trailhead parking
Enchantments overnight permit (recreation.gov) if camping in the permit area May 15-Oct 31
extra water — few reliable sources on the upper mountain
proTips: Approach via Stuart Lake Trailhead and the Mountaineer Creek valley, then cut cross-country into the Cannon Creek basin toward Druid Plateau — this avoids the much longer Snow Lakes/Aasgard Pass approach into the Core Zone.
Many parties combine Cannon with McClellan Peak or Enchantment Peak from a single Core Zone or Colchuck Lake camp to justify the long approach.
watchOut: Snow and verglass can linger on north- and east-facing sections and the summit block into July, adding real exposure to otherwise easy class 2-3 terrain.
The lower approach through the Cannon Creek basin involves cliff bands and steep brush that require careful route-finding.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Enchantments in summer — start early and be off exposed terrain by early afternoon.
knownHazards: Loose rock and exposed class 3 moves on the final summit block
Lingering snow and ice on shaded approach slopes into early summer
Cliff bands and dense brush on the lower Cannon Creek approach
Limited to no cell service throughout the Alpine Lakes Wilderness backcountry
Mountain goats habituated to hikers throughout the Enchantments core

road.name: FR 7601 (Eightmile Road) via Icicle Road/FR 7600
road.status: gravel, steep in sections; high-clearance/4WD recommended
road.seasonalGate: gated seasonally, roughly November-May
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road ~8.5 mi, turn onto Eightmile Road/FR 7600, then FR 7601 ~4 mi to Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,400 ft); parking is very limited and carpooling is encouraged.

climate.forecastZone: Stevens Pass zone (NWAC winter avalanche forecasts; not issued for the summer scrambling season)
climate.typical: Dry, sunny summer days with cool nights and a real risk of afternoon thunderstorms; shaded terrain can hold snow/ice into early summer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy residual snowpack on the approach and upper mountain; access road typically still gated into May.
bySeason.summer: Prime season late June-September; warm days, cold nights, and recurring afternoon thunderstorm risk.
bySeason.fall: Generally stable but shorter days; first snow possible by October, and the Enchantment overnight permit season ends October 31.
bySeason.winter: Access road gated; full winter mountaineering/avalanche conditions on the approach and mountain.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District; Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day (or $30 annual) trailhead fee at Stuart Lake Trailhead; free self-issue day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead
access.permit: Enchantment Permit Area lottery permit (recreation.gov) required for overnight camping in the permit zones May 15-October 31; day trips do not require the lottery permit
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass
access.closures: FR 7601 access road closed seasonally, roughly November-May; dogs are prohibited within the Enchantment Permit Area

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start recommended given the long, largely off-trail approach and afternoon thunderstorm risk

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cannon Mountain summit - Stuart Lake Trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911; RiverCom 911 regional dispatch handles Chelan County Sheriff/Search and Rescue callouts
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24/7 ER, (509) 548-5807
emergency.notes: No reliable cell service through most of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness backcountry; carry a satellite communicator. Advanced trauma cases are transported from Cascade Medical to Wenatchee, Seattle, or Spokane.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation is well-corroborated at 8,652 ft (Wikipedia, Peakbagger), matching the provided data; note some secondary listings round to 8,638 ft (WTA) or list 8,657 ft (ListsOfJohn) for the same summit. Multiple distinctly named approach variants exist in sources (this 'South Slopes'/Class 2-3 line via Cannon Creek-Druid Plateau most closely matches the given grade; The Mountaineers separately document a 'Southeast Approach' via Snow Lakes/Prusik Pass, a 'North Slope Approach' via Coney Lake, and a Southwest Ridge/Mountaineer Creek variant used on winter ascents) — all reach broadly the same Druid Plateau staging area and summit block. Route-specific first-ascent party for this line is not documented; only the peak's overall 1921 first ascent year is recorded.

blurb: Cashmere Mountain is a massive, craggy granite (tonalitic pluton) summit in the Wenatchee Mountains subrange of the Cascade Range, rising above Icicle Creek and Eightmile Creek near Leavenworth, WA, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. It is a well-known viewpoint reflected in nearby Colchuck Lake and sits adjacent to the Cashmere Crags, a cluster of granite spires (the "Nightmare Needles") where Fred Beckey and other Northwest climbers established numerous first ascents in the 1940s-50s. The peak itself is reached by a long, remote day-trip approach (16-20 miles round trip) through the Eightmile-Caroline lakes basin, with the West Ridge as its standard, easiest line.

routes.overview: A long approach through the Eightmile-Caroline lakes basin to Windy Pass, followed by exposed Class 2-3 scrambling through a series of rock towers and gaps on Cashmere's upper west ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: The standard and easiest route on Cashmere Mountain. From Windy Pass the west ridge becomes the obvious line: descend to a saddle where the scrambling begins, then work through a series of tooth-like gendarmes separated by roughly five main gaps, weaving from the north side of the ridge to the south and back, rarely following the crest exactly. Cairns mark parts of the route but route-finding is required. Rock is generally solid granite but loose in spots near the summit; snow or rime can turn the Class 2-3 terrain into harder Class 4 climbing in early season or after storms, and parties have roped up with slings/chocks for running belays in those conditions.
routes.approach: From the Eightmile Lake Trailhead (end of Eightmile Creek Rd/FR 7601, about 3 miles south of Icicle Rd), follow Eightmile Lake Trail #1552 about 2.8 miles to Little Eightmile Lake, then branch onto the Eightmile-Trout Creek Trail toward Caroline Pass, Lake Caroline, and Little Caroline Lake, continuing into Windy Basin and up to Windy Pass. From Windy Pass, follow the ridge to the saddle below Cashmere's upper west ridge, then begin the Class 2-3 scramble to the summit, traversing past intermediate high points (reported as Point 7555 and Point 8279) along the way.
routes.descent: Descend the same West Ridge scramble route back to Windy Pass and retrace the approach trail.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line back through the gendarme gaps on the west ridge to the saddle and Windy Pass, then follow the approach trail back down through Windy Basin, Lake Caroline, Little Eightmile Lake, and out to the Eightmile Lake Trailhead. In poor conditions (snow/rime) some parties have instead descended the northwest face using running belays with snow pickets rather than reversing the ridge crest; this is a variation, not the standard descent, and requires snow travel gear and route-finding.
routes.bail: The west ridge scramble can be reversed at essentially any point back toward the saddle and Windy Pass; there is no fixed rappel line, so retreat means down-climbing the same Class 2-3 terrain, which becomes harder to reverse if snow or rime is present.
routes.turnaround: Given the 16-20 mile round-trip distance and mostly waterless upper mountain, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and party pace; the exposed ridge scrambling and long exposed ridge crest are not places to be caught by darkness, afternoon thunderstorms, or deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed as a long summer day trip, June through September, once the approach trail and upper mountain are mostly snow-free; the route has been done in late May but with significant lingering snow, rime, and reduced-visibility conditions that turn the scramble into harder, more serious climbing.
objHaz: snow/ice on ridge in early season
knownHazards: Loose, crumbly rock and scree near the summit increase rockfall risk, especially with multiple parties on route.
The upper west ridge's gaps and gendarmes can hold snow or rime well into early summer, turning Class 2-3 scrambling into harder, more exposed Class 4 climbing.
Water sources are scarce above Lake Caroline/Windy Basin, and the long approach with full sun exposure creates real dehydration risk on hot days.
Mosquitoes can be severe around Lake Caroline in early-to-mid summer.
watchOut: Route-finding on the upper ridge is genuinely tricky — the line switches from the north side to the south side of the crest through five main gaps and rarely follows the ridgeline directly, so cairns alone are not sufficient.
Don't underestimate the approach: at 16-20 miles round trip with 5,600+ feet of gain, most parties treat this as a full, long day.
Keep parties tight through loose terrain near the summit to reduce rockfall risk to those below.
proTips: Many parties do the whole route as a single long day given limited overnight permits in the Enchantment Permit Area; start early to allow a margin for route-finding on the ridge.
Carry extra water — reports describe the upper basin and ridge as drier than expected despite maps showing streams.
Check recent trip reports for snow conditions before a late-spring or early-summer attempt; rime/snow on the ridge significantly raises the difficulty.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed rack is required for the standard dry-season Class 2-3 scramble; parties occasionally carry a small selection of slings and a few small-to-medium chocks/nuts for optional running-belay protection on exposed sections or when snow/rime is present.
routes.proNeeds: Generally unprotected scrambling in summer conditions; optional light rack (slings, a few chocks) if roping up through exposed or snow-covered sections.
whatToBring: microspikes or ice axe if snow lingers on the ridge

road.name: Eightmile Creek Road (FR 7601)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service road to the Eightmile Lake Trailhead; passable to standard passenger vehicles in dry conditions
road.seasonalGate: Not gated at low elevation, but the road and upper trail are seasonally snow-affected; the route is generally described as accessible "once you can drive the Eightmile Road," typically late spring onward
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, take Icicle Road (Icicle Creek Rd) about 3-4 miles from Hwy 2, then turn onto Eightmile Creek Road (FR 7601) and follow it to its end at the Eightmile Lake Trailhead.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades climate: drier and sunnier than the west-side Cascades, with hot, exposed summer days on the approach and afternoon thunderstorm risk; the upper ridge is fully exposed to wind and weather.

bySeason.spring: Significant lingering snowpack on the upper mountain and ridge well into late May/June; approach road and lower trail may still have patchy snow.
bySeason.summer: The main climbing season (Jun-Sep); hot, dry, and sun-exposed on approach with minimal water and shade; afternoon thunderstorms possible.
bySeason.fall: Can remain climbable into September with stable weather but shortening daylight; early snow possible by late September/October at this elevation.
bySeason.winter: Not a standard climbing season for this route; deep snow, avalanche terrain on approach slopes, and short days make it a ski-mountaineering/winter objective rather than a scramble.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District), within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or Interagency/Digital Day Pass) required for trailhead parking; overnight camping in the Eightmile-Caroline zone requires an Enchantment Permit Area overnight permit ($5/person/day) from May 15-Oct 31.
access.permit: Free self-issue day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead for day trips; overnight stays require an Enchantment Permit Area lottery or same-day permit (the Eightmile-Caroline zone is generally the least competitive zone).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency America the Beautiful Pass / Digital Day Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early morning given the long round-trip distance and desire to be off the exposed ridge before afternoon thunderstorms

sectionBreakdown.note: Trail #1552, about 2.8 miles

emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826 — 24-hour Emergency Department, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent for much of the approach and on the ridge; the nearest reliable coverage is typically near Leavenworth/Hwy 2.

routes.corrections: Some older/secondary sources (Mountaineers.org page slug, Mountain Project) list the summit at 8,501 ft; current USGS/LiDAR-derived elevation and Wikipedia both give 8,514 ft, matching the input data, so 8,514 ft was retained. Wikipedia attributes a 'first ascent' to Robert A. Farmer in 1900 via a USGS survey record, but this is unconfirmed as a true climbing first ascent (versus a survey visit) and could not be corroborated by a second climbing-history source, so no peak or route FA was set.

blurb: Forbidden Peak is an 8,815-foot glacial horn in the Boston Basin area of North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington, prized for its clean Skagit Gneiss rock and knife-edge ridges. It was first climbed June 1, 1940 by Fred and Helmy Beckey, Lloyd Anderson, Jim Crooks, and Dave Lind via the West Ridge, which later became one of the most celebrated technical alpine climbs in North America after its inclusion in Steck and Roper's "Fifty Classic Climbs of North America." There is no easy way off the summit — nearly every route ends with a long, loose, and exposed descent.

routes.overview: A ~1,500-foot knife-edge ridge of clean, solid gneiss above the Quien Sabe/Forbidden glacier, reached via a steep snow couloir or the late-season Cat Scratch gullies; the finest and most popular line on the peak.
routes.beta: From the West Ridge Notch, climb 4th- and low-5th-class ridge crest with short roped sections; the crux is a steep step about two-thirds of the way up, passed on its left past a fixed piton, then the crest wraps left of the false summit to the true (east) summit.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead (3,200 ft) on Cascade River Road, hike an old mine roadbed then a steep climber's path (multiple creek crossings) to high camp near 6,200-6,400 ft (3-4 hours, ~3-3.5 mi, ~3,000 ft gain). From camp, cross glacier remnants to a prominent couloir at the left edge of Forbidden's south face and ascend it (or, late season, the Cat Scratch gullies) to the West Ridge Notch.
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge (downclimb/rappel) to the West Ridge Notch, then rappel the couloir or the Cat Scratch gullies (roughly 6 rappels) back to the glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb and rappel back along the ridge crest to the West Ridge Notch, swap climbing shoes for boots/crampons, then make a combination of rappelling, lowering, and belayed downclimbing — about 6 rappels — down the couloir or the loose Cat Scratch gullies to the glacier, where parties can unrope for the walk back to camp.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward almost anywhere below the crux by reversing the ridge crest back to the West Ridge Notch, then rappelling/downclimbing the couloir or Cat Scratch gullies to the glacier.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent off Forbidden is long, loose, and exposed regardless of route, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather that leaves enough margin to complete the full rappel/downclimb sequence before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August is the most reliable window. Snow often covers the rock until early July; the couloir gives good 40-45 degree snow climbing in early/mid season but can be melted out to loose rock with an open bergschrund by late August. Check current NPS Boston Basin climbing-conditions reports before going.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the peak; North Cascades cell service is generally described as unpredictable at best. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.fa: June 1, 1940 — Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Lloyd Anderson, Jim Crooks, Dave Lind (peak and route first ascent together)
routes.rappels: ~6 rappels down the couloir/Cat Scratch gullies from the West Ridge Notch to the glacier
objHaz: party-induced rockfall in the approach couloir/gullies
routes.detailedRack: A single light alpine rack works: cams roughly 0.3-3in (many parties bring doubles in the small-to-mid sizes), a set of nuts, and 10 or so slings (including several double-length) for the many rock horns on the ridge — horn slings see more use than cams. An old fixed piton protects the 5.6 crux.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly slung horns plus a light cam rack and nuts; the crux step has a fixed piton for protection.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the ridge rock
glacier travel kit (harness, prusiks) for the approach
bivy gear if not going car-to-car
proTips: Boston Basin permits are limited and can be hard to get for this popular Fifty Classic Climbs route — reserve early
The second pitch's exposed 'air step' across a rock gap looks worse than it climbs
A fixed piton marks/protects the 5.6 crux about two-thirds of the way up
watchOut: Loose, party-kicked rock in the approach couloir/gullies
Glacier-polished, wet slabs below the snowfield have caused a fatal slip on descent
Rockfall from parties above on this popular route
Bergschrund opening late season in the couloir
knownHazards: A climber died in September 2013 after being struck by rockfall and falling roughly 300 feet while descending the West Ridge
Crevasse hazard crossing the glacier remnants below the route

pitchDetail.notes: From the West Ridge Notch, simulclimb easy ridge crest on solid gneiss.
Includes the well-known exposed 'air step' across a rock gap; well protected.
Continue the knife-edge crest, weaving around gendarmes, mostly staying on the north side.
A steep step about two-thirds of the way up, climbed on its left side past a fixed piton.
Ridge crest passes left of the false summit to the true (east) summit.

road.driveNote: Small unmarked pullout with room for roughly 10 cars, opposite Johannesburg Mountain just past mile marker 22; Northwest Forest Pass required to park

climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate: wet winters with heavy snow, drier but changeable summers with occasional afternoon thunderstorms

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack still covers approach trail and couloir into June
bySeason.summer: Most stable window (Jul-Aug) but quick weather changes and afternoon storms still occur
bySeason.fall: Increasing storm frequency and new snow by mid-to-late September
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow; road typically ungated/inaccessible and avalanche terrain throughout

access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (parking) plus NPS wilderness permit (overnight)
access.closures: Cascade River Road is seasonally gated and can close for washouts/maintenance — check the NPS road-status page before driving

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 (after hours); NPS non-emergency (360) 854-7255
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, (360) 854-7245; Park HQ (360) 854-7200

emergency.notes: Call 911 for emergencies. North Cascades NP has one of the highest visitor death rates of any US national park; Forbidden Peak has had multiple climbing fatalities (rockfall 2013, fall 2018). No reliable cell coverage — carry a satellite communicator.

sectionBreakdown.note: ~3-3.5 mi, ~3,000 ft gain on a steep climber's path
Glacier crossing, couloir, and ridge
Rappel/downclimb the ridge and couloir/Cat Scratch gullies

routes.corrections: Reported pitch counts for the West Ridge vary widely across sources (roughly 4 to 8, depending how the ridge is broken up); 6 is used here as a reasonable middle figure, consistent with the given data.

routes.overview: A long, moderate knife-edge ridge above Boston Basin, separating the smooth south-face slabs from the crevassed Boston Glacier to the north; considered one of the finest arêtes of its kind in the Cascades.
routes.beta: From a bivy/notch on the ridge, climb a mix of 4th- and low-5th-class terrain over and around several gendarmes, occasionally rappelling into notches, to reach the summit. A more direct, harder start (5.8, known as the 'East Ridge Direct') was pioneered later and is climbed far more often today than the original 1946 line.
routes.approach: From Boston Basin high camp, approach to the base of the East Ridge is regarded as one of the faster, easier approaches on the peak (roughly 3-4 hours), via moraines and gullies to a snow finger leading to the ridge crest.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the East Ledges (a class 3-4 traverse) or by reversing/rappelling the West Ridge.
routes.descentText: A common option is a series of single-rope rappels down the northeast side to a ledge system, followed east across several loose, mostly 3rd-class rock ribs (with short 4th-class sections) to a gully that regains the ridge crest near a solitary gendarme, then snow slopes down to the small glacier below the south face. Alternatively, parties reverse/rappel the West Ridge.
routes.bail: Below the crux gendarmes, retreat by reversing rappels/downclimbing back along the ridge crest toward the approach notch.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that accounts for the long, loose East Ledges descent (or reversing the ridge) — do not let the technical climb eat into the daylight needed to get off the mountain safely.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August, once the approach snow finger has consolidated and before late-season icing/dryness makes the couloirs and ledges more hazardous.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the peak; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.fa: 1946 — Fred Beckey, Bob Craig, Cliff Schmidtke (original East Ridge line; rarely repeated in this form)
routes.rappels: Descent (East Ledges) involves roughly 5 single-rope rappels (~400 ft total) plus scrambling
objHaz: loose rock on the ridge and gendarmes
unprotectable terrain on the East Ledges descent
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack of cams and nuts plus abundant slings for horns and gendarme bypasses; ropes sufficient for single-rope rappels into notches.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly cams/nuts in the 0.2-2in range with plentiful slings; several notches require a short rappel rather than downclimbing.
whatToBring: extra webbing for tat-worn rappel notches
proTips: The approach to the East Ridge is among the fastest on the peak, often under 4 hours from high camp
Most parties today climb the harder, more direct 'East Ridge Direct' start (5.8) rather than the original 1946 line described here
watchOut: Loose rock throughout the ridge, especially on the East Ledges descent
A fatal 1975 fall occurred when a descending party mistakenly traversed too high on loose rock — stay low on the correct ledge line
knownHazards: On July 13, 1975, a climber fell roughly 400 feet to his death on the East Ledges descent after the party's leader was given incorrect route directions and traversed too high on loose rock

pitchDetail.notes: Broken ridge crest and gendarmes above the Boston Basin glacier approach.
Bypass several gendarmes on the north or south side of the crest; some short rappels into notches.
The technical high point of the original 1946 line, well below the 5.8 crux of the later East Ridge Direct start.
Knife-edge simul-climbing to the summit.
