routes.corrections: The given grade/pitch data (III, 5.6, 8 pitches) matches the original 1946 East Ridge line by Beckey/Craig/Schmidtke, not the more technical 'East Ridge Direct' (5.8, 6 pitches per Mountain Project) that most modern trip reports and guided parties actually climb. Sources disagree on the Direct variant's first-ascent credit (Mountain Project cites 1958, Beckey/Hieb/Cooper/Claunch; another account cites a 1970 Canadian party establishing a safer direct start) — not resolved here, and not applied to this 'East Ridge' entry.

routes.overview: The most alpine and committing of Forbidden's three ridges: a tricky approach over Sharkfin Col, a traverse of the crevassed Boston Glacier, then a half-mile jagged rock crest to the summit pyramid.
routes.beta: From a bivy where the ridge crest is gained above the Boston Glacier moat, mostly simul-climb 'walking to low fifth class' terrain along a nearly-horizontal knife edge, with a short steep corner (5.5-5.6) crux low on the route and a final short hand-crack pitch to the summit.
routes.approach: From Boston Basin high camp, ascend the northwest margin of the Quien Sabe Glacier, scramble loose gullies to a notch near Sharkfin Col, make two 30m rappels onto the heavily crevassed Boston Glacier, and traverse it to gain the North Ridge; two ledges near the moat crossing serve as a common (waterless) bivy.
routes.descent: Descend via the West Ridge: simul-climb down, then several rappels to the West Ridge Notch, followed by rappels down the Cat Scratch gullies (or the East Ledges) back to the basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, simul-climb down for several rope-lengths, make about 3 rappels, then a 4th-class scramble to the West Ridge Notch, followed by roughly 7 rappels down the Cat Scratch gullies to snow. Take care descending the wet, glacier-polished slabs below the snowfield — this stretch has caused at least one fatality.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: approach to Boston Basin high camp. Day 2: cross Sharkfin Col and the Boston Glacier to a ridge-crest bivy. Day 3: climb the North Ridge to the summit and descend via the West Ridge/Cat Scratch gullies. Few parties complete the route in fewer than 3 days, though strong teams have done it in one long day from camp.
routes.bail: Below the ridge crest, retreat is by reversing the Boston Glacier traverse and Sharkfin Col rappels back to Boston Basin; once established on the ridge above the bivy, there are few easy bail options short of continuing to the summit and descending the West Ridge.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour glacier traverse and rappels required just to reach the ridge, and the long descent via the West Ridge afterward, build in generous time margins and turn around early enough to reverse the glacier crossing in daylight if needed.
routes.bestSeason: July-August, when the Boston Glacier crevasses are more consolidated and the ridge rock is drier; conditions and crevasse bridging vary significantly by season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the Boston Glacier/ridge; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.fa: 1952 — Fred Beckey and party (reported in AAJ 1953); the full roster of the first-ascent party was not confirmed in available sources
routes.rappels: 2 x 30m rappels onto the Boston Glacier on approach; ~3 rappels off the summit plus ~7 rappels down the Cat Scratch gullies on descent
objHaz: crevasses on the Boston Glacier traverse
no water source at the ridge bivy
wet, glacier-polished slabs below the descent snowfield (at least one fatality)
routes.detailedRack: A light rack (singles 0.3-2in with doubles in 0.4-0.75in) on a 60m twin rope folded in half covers the technical sections; crampons and an ice axe/tools are needed for the Boston Glacier crossing.
routes.proNeeds: Light cam rack plus nuts for the low corner crux and occasional protection along the mostly unprotected simul-climbing on the ridge crest.
whatToBring: bivy gear (this is a multi-day route for most parties)
stove/fuel to melt snow for water
crevasse rescue kit for the Boston Glacier
proTips: Two good bivy ledges exist right where you gain the ridge after the moat crossing, but carry water up from the glacier — there's none at the bivy
Snow patches along the upper ridge can be melted for water later in the route
watchOut: Heavily crevassed Boston Glacier crossing
Loose rock in the Sharkfin Col approach gullies
Wet, glacier-polished slabs on the descent below the snowfield have been the site of at least one fatal slip
knownHazards: At least one climbing fatality has occurred on the wet, glacier-polished slabs below the snowfield on the standard descent

pitchDetail.notes: From the moat below Sharkfin Col, gain the ridge crest via ledges; two bivy ledges here (no water).
A short steep corner is the first technical crux, then mostly walking/low 5th along the crest.
Nearly-horizontal knife-edge simul-climbing; snowfields can be bypassed on rock.
Ledges and a chimney on the west side, then au cheval/knife-edge moves across narrow sections.
A short, solid hand crack leads to the summit.

sectionBreakdown.note: Via Sharkfin Col and Boston Glacier traverse; often a full day
One party's reported technical climbing time

routes.corrections: The given rockGrade of 5.8 does not match reputable sources: Mountain Project, Spokalpine, and Alpinedave's trip report all cite a 5.6 technical crux for the North Ridge. Corrected to 5.6 here.

routes.overview: The tallest, steepest, and hardest-to-reach established route on Forbidden Peak: a big, complex face route on excellent granite/gneiss reached by a circuitous glacier approach across the largest glacier in North Cascades National Park.
routes.beta: After crossing Sharkfin Col and the Boston Glacier, gain a low-fifth-class gendarme on the North Ridge, then steepen through a chimney crux onto the true Northwest Face, which offers remarkably good rock and excellent crack systems for four 60m pitches (mid-5th to 5.8) before joining the upper West Ridge for several more, easier pitches to the true summit.
routes.approach: From Boston Basin, ascend the left side of the Quien Sabe Glacier to a notch above/east of Sharkfin Col, make two single-rope rappels onto the heavily crevassed Boston Glacier, traverse it (refilling water at the moat), do a low-fifth boulder-problem sequence to the Forbidden Glacier, and downclimb tenuous crevassed terrain to the rock — roughly 8 hours total.
routes.descent: Multiple rappels down the upper ridge, some unroped downclimbing, then roughly 7 rappels down the Cat Scratch gullies to snow.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the upper West Ridge with a combination of rappels and unroped downclimbing to the West Ridge Notch, then make about 7 rappels down the Cat Scratch gullies to reach snow/glacier and unrope for the walk out.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip: approach to Boston Basin high camp, then a long approach day over Sharkfin Col and the Boston Glacier to the base of the face (or a bivy partway), followed by the technical climb and descent.
routes.bail: Below the face's midpoint, retreat requires reversing steep terrain and is difficult; most parties commit to the top once established on the wall. Escape from the Boston Glacier approach back over Sharkfin Col to Boston Basin is possible before starting the technical climbing.
routes.turnaround: This is the longest, most remote line on the peak — set an early turnaround given the multi-hour glacier approach, technical face climbing, and long descent via the Cat Scratch gullies.
routes.bestSeason: July is generally preferred (lighter aluminum crampons suffice on the approach); by August the glacier and snow sections are icier and firmer footwear/ice gear is recommended.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the Boston Glacier or the face; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.fa: July 1959 — Fred Beckey and Ed Cooper
routes.rappels: ~7 rappels down the Cat Scratch gullies on descent, plus 2 rappels onto the Boston Glacier on approach
objHaz: snow mushrooms and glacial remnants on the approach
tenuous downclimbing through crevasses to reach the rock
routes.detailedRack: Offset nuts and cams from about 0.2-2.0in (doubles 0.3-2.0in) cover the crack systems; a 60m rope is standard. Crampons and an axe (aluminum can suffice in July) are needed for the glacier approach, with pickets/screws optional in July but recommended by August.
routes.proNeeds: Good crack protection throughout on excellent rock; offset nuts/cams recommended for the crux chimney and crack pitches.
whatToBring: approach shoes early season; mountaineering boots by August
bivy gear for the long approach
crevasse rescue kit for the Boston Glacier
proTips: The Sharkfin Col approach is described as the least hazardous and most scenic way to reach the face
Refill water at the moat between the Boston Glacier and the North Ridge rock before starting the technical climbing
watchOut: Heavily crevassed Boston Glacier traverse
Snow mushrooms and unstable glacial remnants low on the approach
Long, complex descent via the Cat Scratch gullies
knownHazards: The approach crosses North Cascades National Park's largest glacier and involves objectively hazardous crevassed terrain

pitchDetail.notes: Four 60m pitches on solid flakes and crack systems gain the North Ridge junction; a chimney marks the crux.
Continues via the upper West Ridge for several more, easier pitches to the true summit.

Four 60m pitches to the crux chimney and beyond

routes.corrections: Pitches was null in the given data; Mountain Project lists 7 pitches for the Northwest Face (the technical wall section before joining the upper West Ridge for additional easier pitches to the true summit), which is used here.

routes.overview: No documented technical AI3/Grade IV ice route on Forbidden Peak's northeast face was found. The only well-sourced feature on this side of the mountain is the 'East Ledges' (aka 'NE Ledge'), a class 3-4 rock traverse below the summit, used almost exclusively as the standard descent rather than as an ascent line.
routes.beta: The East Ledges/NE Ledge is reached from the summit by roughly five single-rope rappels (~400 ft total) down the northeast face to a ledge system, which is then followed east — mostly 3rd class with short 4th-class sections — across several loose rock ribs to a gully that regains the ridge crest near a solitary gendarme, then down snow to the small glacier below the south face.
routes.approach: Same Boston Basin approach as the other routes (see West Ridge); this feature is accessed from the summit, not from a distinct base.
routes.descent: This IS the standard descent route for several other Forbidden Peak lines (West Ridge, East Ridge, North Ridge parties sometimes use it as an alternative to the Cat Scratch gullies).
routes.descentText: About five single-rope rappels (~400 ft total) lead from a summit horn down the northeast face to a ledge system; follow the ledges east, staying well below the ridge crest, crossing roughly five rock ribs (3rd class, short 4th-class sections) until a gully leads back up to the ridge crest at a solitary gendarme, then down snow to the glacier below the south face.
routes.turnaround: Route-finding errors on this terrain have been fatal — if visibility or conditions are poor, do not push on; the ledge line must be followed low and precisely.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Aug, matching the rest of the peak's climbing season; it is a rock scramble, not an ice route.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the peak; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.rappels: ~5 single-rope rappels (~400 ft total) from the summit
objHaz: route-finding difficulty on the ledge traverse
watchOut: Do not traverse high on the ledges — stay on the correct low line across the rock ribs
Rock is loose and largely unprotectable; a fall on much of this terrain would be fatal
knownHazards: On July 13, 1975, a climber fell roughly 400 feet to his death here after his party's leader mistakenly gave directions that led them to traverse too high on loose rock

Described by experienced Cascades climbers as technically the easiest way up or down the mountain but also the scariest, because most of the terrain cannot be reliably protected despite being steep enough that a fall is fatal

routes.corrections: The submitted grade (IV, AI3, D) does not match any documented Forbidden Peak route found in extensive research across Mountain Project, SummitPost, Mountaineers.org, CascadeClimbers, and guide-service sites. Every source describing a 'Northeast Face'/'NE Ledge' on this peak calls it a class 3-4 scramble/downclimb used almost exclusively as the standard descent — not a technical AI3 ice ascent. No AI3 ice route on Forbidden's Northeast Face was found anywhere. Grade/technical fields are left null rather than guessed; the real, verifiable East Ledges information is provided instead.

routes.overview: No documented 5.9/Grade IV 'East Face' summit route was found. The only well-sourced feature matching 'Catscratch' is the Cat Scratch Gullies — loose class-4 gullies on the south side of the peak, climbed as a late-season alternate way to the West Ridge Notch and used far more often as the standard rappel descent from the West Ridge.
routes.beta: Three small gullies sit left of the deep south-face couloir; the easternmost (closest to the couloir) is preferred in late season. Climbed in four long, easy (class 4) pitches that feel secure with only 2-3 pieces of gear per pitch, they top out at the West Ridge Notch.
routes.approach: Same Boston Basin approach as the West Ridge (see that entry); the gullies are reached from high camp by crossing the glacier to the base of the south face, at the left edge below the West Ridge Notch.
routes.descent: Also used as the standard descent from the West Ridge and other summit routes: roughly 6-7 rappels from the West Ridge Notch down to the glacier.
routes.descentText: From the West Ridge Notch, make a combination of rappels (roughly 6-7) and downclimbing through the loose gullies back to the glacier remnants below the south face, where parties can unrope.
routes.bail: Downclimb or rappel back down the gully at essentially any point — the terrain is moderate (class 4) throughout.
routes.bestSeason: Best used in the later season (Aug-Sep) once the adjacent main couloir has become too icy or dry for the standard West Ridge ascent; the easternmost of the three small gullies is preferred at that time.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the peak; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.rappels: ~6-7 rappels from the West Ridge Notch to the glacier when used as a descent
objHaz: party-induced rockfall (loose rock throughout the gullies)
routes.face: Cat Scratch Gullies (south-side gullies to the West Ridge Notch)
routes.detailedRack: A minimal rack suffices — most parties report needing only 2-3 pieces of protection per pitch on this easy, loose terrain.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed; the terrain is easy (class 4) but loose, so rope management to avoid knocking rock onto partners matters more than gear placements.
gear: rope for the gully pitches and descent rappels
proTips: Use the easternmost of the three small gullies (closest to the main couloir) in late season
Being first on route reduces the party-induced rockfall risk that's common here
watchOut: Loose rock throughout the gullies — rockfall from other parties above is a major concern
The adjacent main couloir can be icy/dry late season, which is why this variation exists
knownHazards: Loose, rockfall-prone terrain typical of all the gully variations on Forbidden's south side

pitchDetail.notes: Four long, easy pitches up loose rock in the easternmost gully, needing only 2-3 pieces of protection each, topping out at the West Ridge Notch.

bySeason.summer: Best window Jul-Aug for the main couloir; this gully variation is more relevant Aug-Sep
bySeason.fall: Storms increase by mid-late September
bySeason.winter: Road inaccessible

routes.corrections: The given rockGrade (5.9), alpineGrade (D), and commitment (IV) are not supported by any source found; the Cat Scratch Gullies are consistently described (SummitPost, guide-service pages) as easy class-4 terrain, not 5.9. The given 'East Face' label is also geographically off — the Cat Scratch Gullies are on the south side of the peak, accessed from Boston Basin below the West Ridge Notch, not on an east-facing wall. Technical grade fields are left null rather than guessed; the real, verifiable Cat Scratch Gullies information is provided instead.

blurb: Eldorado Peak (8,872 ft) rises above the Cascade River in North Cascades National Park, at the head of Marble Creek just west of the Inspiration Glacier — part of the largest contiguous non-volcanic ice sheet in the Lower 48. The peak is composed largely of Cretaceous-age biotite granodiorite orthogneiss, and its classic East Ridge route, topping out on a narrow snow knife-edge, has made it one of the most popular glacier climbs in the range. It was first climbed August 27, 1933 by Donald Blair, Norval Grigg, Arthur Winder, and Arthur Wilson.

routes.overview: The standard route: a moderate but committing glacier climb up the Eldorado and Inspiration glaciers followed by an exposed, narrow snow ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: The most popular route on Eldorado Peak because it has the shortest approach, least technical difficulty, and a spectacular payoff — a snowy knife-edge summit ridge above the Inspiration-McAllister-Klawatti icecap. From high camp, climbers ascend snowfields and the Eldorado Glacier just left (west) of the rocky ridge, continue onto the broad flat Inspiration Glacier around 7,500 ft, traverse it to the north, then follow a broad snow-covered ridge that narrows to a true knife-edge at the summit. Requires glacier travel skills and crevasse-rescue gear; can be done as a long day push or a two-day trip.
routes.approach: From the Eldorado Creek trailhead (mile 20, Cascade River Road, 2,100 ft), cross the river via a log/downed-tree crossing, then climb a steep climbers' path gaining about 1,500 ft to talus slopes. Follow cairns up the talus field (cliffs to the left), cross a small creek and waterfall, and enter Eldorado Creek Basin. Continue up to Roush Creek Basin (6,100-6,400 ft) or the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier high camp (7,500 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the same East Ridge/glacier route back to high camp and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line: down the summit ridge, across the Inspiration Glacier, down the Eldorado Glacier and snowfields to camp, then down the talus and climbers' path to the river crossing and trailhead. No rappels are typically required on this route.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike from trailhead to high camp (5-7 hrs, 3,500-5,500 ft gain). Day 2: summit via Inspiration Glacier and East Ridge (5-7 hrs round trip from camp) and return to camp or trailhead. Some parties add a 3rd day for the descent to the trailhead (2-4 hrs).
routes.bail: Retreat is generally straightforward by reversing the ascent line back across the glaciers to camp; the main constraints are whiteout navigation on the glacier and softening snow bridges later in the day.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting the summit push and stick to it — afternoon warming increases crevasse-bridge failure risk and wet-loose avalanche activity on the approach slopes, and the exposed summit ridge is no place to be caught by deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly June through early October; conditions vary through the season — the ridge is wider and less exposed in early summer, and becomes a true knife-edge of firmer snow/ice by September. The glacier is typically most passable and least crevassed in early-to-mid season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in this North Cascades backcountry; a satellite communicator is recommended for emergency contact.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier mountaineering kit: crevasse-rescue gear (prussiks/mechanical ascenders, pickets), rope for the team, ice axe, and crampons. No technical rock rack is generally needed on the standard line.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets for glacier crevasse protection; a rope per rope team for glacier travel.
proTips: Scout the river crossing near the trailhead in daylight before an early departure.
Stick to the climber's right side of the boulder field(s) below the glacier and follow cairns.
Camp options exist at three elevations (5,600 ft, 6,100-6,400 ft, 7,500 ft) — choose based on your itinerary and acclimatization.
watchOut: Glide cracks form on steep sections below the glacier; be ready to deviate from the established track.
Crevasse bridges become unreliable as temperatures rise through the day.
The summit ridge is a true knife-edge late season with exposure on both sides.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel on the Eldorado and Inspiration glaciers.
Wet loose avalanches on warm afternoons on the approach slopes.
High water at the trailhead river crossing during warm afternoons and after rain.
Bear activity in the high country; rodent problems at established camps.

pitchDetail.notes: Trail and boulder field to roughly 4,000-4,500 ft
Roped glacier travel to 'The Gap' on the lower East Ridge
Ridge-crest travel, roped for crevasse hazard
Narrow and sometimes corniced; a talus bypass on climber's left is sometimes available

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Gravel from ~mile 10 to road's end near mile 23; paved for the first ~10 miles
road.seasonalGate: Winter closure gate near Eldorado trailhead at mile 20; road typically reopens to the Cascade Pass trailhead area by late June (sometimes early July after heavy snow)
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road to the Eldorado Creek trailhead parking area at mile 20 (2,100 ft). Last ~10-13 miles are rough gravel, subject to ruts/washouts; high-clearance recommended. Trailers/vehicles over 22 ft or 8 ft wide are prohibited past the park boundary gate at milepost 18.

climate.typical: Maritime Cascades alpine climate: wet, snowy winters and a short weather-window summer climbing season; afternoon convective buildups are common in summer.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack, avalanche hazard on approach slopes, road may still be gated.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jun-Sep); glacier travel with crevasse-rescue gear required; afternoon thunderstorm risk.
bySeason.fall: Route becomes firmer/icier and the summit ridge narrows to a true knife-edge by September-October; shorter days.
bySeason.winter: Road gated, deep snow, avalanche terrain — not a standard climbing season.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex
access.fees: Backcountry camping permit ~$10/person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee; NPS entrance is fee-free but a National Park Pass/Northwest Forest Pass may be required for the trailhead
access.permit: Overnight backcountry permit required from the Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount) for the Eldorado Cross-Country Zone; camping restricted to designated sites (Roush Creek rib 5,400 ft, Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier apron 7,500 ft) — do not camp in Eldorado Basin itself due to heavy use.
access.passRequired: National Park Pass or Northwest Forest Pass at the trailhead
access.closures: Cascade River Road has a standard winter seasonal closure near the Eldorado trailhead (mile 20); it has also been temporarily closed in some years for storm damage or wildfire management — check current NPS road-conditions postings before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start recommended for the river crossing and to be off exposed glacier/ridge terrain before afternoon warming

sectionBreakdown.note: 5-7 hrs typical; river crossing then steep climbers' trail and talus

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: High camp to Eldorado summit (8,872 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: 3-6 hrs depending on conditions, pace, and camp location
4-6 hrs, losing ~5,300-6,800 ft

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit 911 non-emergency dispatch: (360) 336-3131; 911 for emergencies

routes.corrections: Trailhead elevation and MP20 location confirmed by NPS and WTA; total gain to summit is reported variably as 6,700-6,800 ft across sources (WTA: 6,716 ft round-trip figure differs from guide-service 6,800 ft one-way gain) — used 6,800 ft (guide-service + NPS-aligned figure).

routes.overview: A mixed/ice couloir climbing the Northwest Ice Couloir above the Eldorado Glacier through the notch between Eldorado Peak and Dean's Tower, joining the summit ridge from the west.
routes.beta: A long, complicated approach through the wild Marble Creek Cirque leads to a sustained ice/snow couloir (AI2 up to WI3/WI4-M4 depending on conditions) that starts around 7,800 ft and tops out near 8,800 ft just below the knife-edge summit ridge. Terrain is sustained 50-80 degree snice/ice with vertical bulges; the gully funnels ice fall so parties should minimize time in the runnel and climb it early/cold.
routes.approach: From the Eldorado trailhead, climb the steep climbers' trail (~1 hr, 2,000 ft gain), cross the boulder field on its right side following cairns, gain the ridgeline at first light, descend into the basin beneath the Eldorado Glacier, reach the glacier around 7,000 ft, cross the Inspiration Glacier, and traverse around past the East Ridge route to the notch between Eldorado Peak and Dean's Tower. Total approach is roughly 5.5 hours.
routes.descent: Descend the East Ridge route, or rappel the couloir.
routes.descentText: From the notch/couloir top, two ~33m rappels from a piton anchor with a 70m rope (three raps with a 60m rope) return climbers to the Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier basin; from there, retrace the glacier crossing and the approach trail to the trailhead. Alternatively descend via the East Ridge route.
routes.bail: Rappel the couloir from fixed/natural anchors near the notch back to the glacier basin if conditions deteriorate partway up.
routes.turnaround: Given the ice-fall funnel and sustained steep terrain, commit only with a stable weather window and be prepared to retreat via rappel before conditions (rockfall, wet snow, or approaching weather) worsen.
routes.bestSeason: Best in cold, stable conditions — typically spring through early summer or a cold snap in fall — when the couloir holds consolidated neve/ice rather than being a rockfall-prone runnel; conditions vary widely climb to climb.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is generally absent in this drainage; no reception reported at approach or route elevations.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the boulder field
Extra layers for a cold pre-dawn start
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel gear plus a technical ice/mixed rack: 7 ice screws, a set of passive rock protection (nuts, tricams), and snow pickets for those less comfortable on sustained 50-degree snice.
routes.proNeeds: 7 ice screws (a party used all 7 on one ascent); passive rock gear for mixed sections; pickets recommended if uncomfortable on sustained exposed snow/ice.
proTips: Climb the couloir early/cold to minimize ice-fall exposure — it's an extreme funnel.
Stick to the climber's right side of the boulder field(s) and follow cairns on approach.
A 70m rope makes the descent in 2 rappels versus 3 with a 60m rope.
watchOut: The couloir is an extreme ice-fall funnel — be careful of ice plates shedding onto followers.
Long, complicated approach through Marble Creek Cirque adds route-finding difficulty.
knownHazards: Ice fall in the couloir
Crevassed glacier approach
Rockfall on a warming couloir

pitchDetail.grade: AI2-3 (harder chockstone step possible in lean/early-season conditions)
pitchDetail.notes: Steep ice/neve entering the couloir, roughly 70-80 degree steps
Simul-climbing to gain the upper mountain near the Eldorado-Dean's Tower col

routes.corrections: Could not independently verify a distinct named 'East Face' route (5.7 rock / AI2 ice, Grade III) on Eldorado Peak from 2+ reputable sources. Mountain Project's Eldorado Peak area page and SummitPost list only East Ridge, Northwest Ice Couloir, West Arete, North Ridge (5.7), and Austera Peak/Klawatti Peak lines as named routes — no separate 'East Face' appears. It is possible this entry overlaps with, or is a variant name for, the well-documented Northeast Face (steep ice/snow up to 60 degrees, climbers' right of the East Ridge) or the North Ridge (5.7 rock line), but I did not find sources confirming this specific line/grade combination, so left most fields null rather than guess. Recommend verifying against a print guidebook (e.g., Nelson/Potterfield Cascade Alpine Guide) which may document a route not indexed online.

routes.overview: A steep snow/ice alternative to the standard East Ridge, climbing the ice face to the climbers' right of the East Ridge route before rejoining it below the summit knife-edge.
routes.beta: Described as a 'somewhat contrived' but worthwhile line for climbers with some ice experience: follow the East Ridge route until able to traverse climbers' right above steep cliffs to gain the Northeast Face, then climb several pitches of snow/ice up to 60 degrees (crossing a bergschrund and a rock band on a snowy ramp per one trip report), rejoining the East Ridge route below the summit ridge and finishing up the knife-edge to the top.
routes.approach: Same as the East Ridge: Eldorado Creek trailhead (2,100 ft) to high camp at Roush Creek Basin or the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier apron (7,500 ft), then onto the glacier toward the East Ridge before branching right onto the face.
routes.descent: Descend via the East Ridge route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, follow the East Ridge/Inspiration Glacier descent line back to camp and the trailhead — the same descent used for the standard route.
routes.bail: Retreat back down the face/East Ridge line to the glacier if conditions on the ice pitches deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround given the sustained 50-60 degree ice/snow pitches and bergschrund crossing — softening snow bridges and increased rockfall/icefall risk develop through the day.
routes.bestSeason: Best in early fall (late September to mid-October), before the first snowstorms, when the face holds consolidated snow and ice to 60 degrees; conditions are route-dependent and can vary widely earlier in the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this North Cascades backcountry.
whatToBring: Extra ice screws if conditions are icy rather than snowy
routes.detailedRack: An alpine ice rack: pickets (roughly six cited in one trip report) plus a light alpine rack for crossing the bergschrund and ascending the rock band/ice slope.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets for the snow/ice pitches; light rock protection for the rock band section.
proTips: Best conditions are typically late September-mid October, before the ridge gets buried by early winter storms.
Follow the East Ridge approach and glacier crossing before branching onto the face.
watchOut: Bergschrund crossing can be tricky depending on the season.
Steep ice/snow pitches up to 60 degrees demand solid ice technique.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier approach
Steep ice/snow to 60 degrees
Bergschrund crossing

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse in climbers' right off the East Ridge approach above steep cliffs
Several pitches, running belay with screws/pickets
Joins the East Ridge route below the summit

routes.corrections: Sources consistently grade this route as Grade II+ to Grade III (Mountaineers.org, SummitPost, trip reports) with pitches of snow/ice up to 60 degrees — not the Grade IV / AI3 given in the source data. Set commitment to III per the most commonly cited grading; could not confirm an AI3 ice grade or Grade IV commitment from any source, so alpineGrade/iceGrade were left as provided but flagged here as unconfirmed/likely overstated.

blurb: Sahale Mountain (8,680 ft) is a jagged, glaciated summit in North Cascades National Park at the head of the Stehekin/Cascade Pass corridor, ringed by the Sahale, Quien Sabe, and Davenport glaciers and standing just south of Boston Peak. Like most of the range, it is built from North Cascades crystalline terrane rock uplifted and carved by Pleistocene glaciation. It was first climbed in August 1897 by John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester, and today is one of the range's most popular moderate glacier climbs, reached via the classic Sahale Arm from Cascade Pass or via Boston Basin and the Quien Sabe Glacier.

routes.overview: The classic, most-traveled route on Sahale: a long ridge walk up the Sahale Arm from Cascade Pass onto the Sahale Glacier, finishing with a short 3rd/4th-class rock scramble to the summit block.
routes.beta: From the Cascade River Road trailhead, hike through forest to Cascade Pass, then follow the open Sahale Arm ridge north to Sahale Glacier Camp at the toe of the Sahale Glacier. Rope up and ascend the glacier toward its center (line varies with crevasse conditions) to the base of the summit pinnacle, then scramble 3rd/4th-class rock to the top, downclimbing or rappelling the same short section on descent. It is a non-technical but genuinely alpine outing requiring glacier-travel and basic rock-scrambling competence.
routes.approach: From the Cascade River Road trailhead (3,600 ft, 23.1 mi from Marblemount via SR-20), climb ~1,700-1,800 ft through heavy timber over about 35 short switchbacks in 3.7 mi to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). Take the left-hand fork and follow the Sahale Arm trail north, gaining the open ridge with views of Johannesburg Mountain, Magic Mountain, Mixup Peak, and Doubtful Lake, to Sahale Glacier Camp (~7,200-7,600 ft) at the toe of the Sahale Glacier, about 5.9 mi and ~3,900 ft gain from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb or make a short rappel off the summit block, descend the Sahale Glacier, then retrace the Sahale Arm ridge and Cascade Pass switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb or rappel the short 3rd/4th-class summit block back onto the glacier. Descend the Sahale Glacier toward camp, watching for crevasses that open later in the season, then follow the Sahale Arm trail south back to Cascade Pass and continue down the switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 2-day trip: Day 1, hike to Sahale Glacier Camp (4-5 hrs); Day 2, early-morning summit push (glacier + scramble, 2.5-3 hrs up) and return to camp, then hike out. Strong parties also do it car-to-car in a long single day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward and non-technical along the same line — descend the glacier and Sahale Arm ridge back to Cascade Pass; no fixed rappel stations or technical bail points beyond downclimbing the short summit block.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the glacier/summit-block section based on weather, party pace, and remaining daylight; afternoon thunderstorms and softening glacier snow are the main reasons to bail early.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-July through September, after the Cascade River Road opens (typically late June, sometimes delayed into July by snow) and once the approach switchbacks have melted out; an ice axe is often needed on steep snow as late as July, and Sahale Glacier Camp permits are highly competitive in peak summer weeks.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service along the Cascade Pass/Sahale Arm corridor; do not rely on phone coverage in this backcountry — carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
gear: A few slings/rappel gear for the summit block
whatToBring: Bear canister (required for overnight in North Cascades NP)
Overnight/bivy gear if doing it as a 2-day trip
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rack: a small glacier-travel kit (pickets, prusiks/rescue gear), a short rope for the roped glacier crossing and the summit block, and a few slings/a locking biner or two for a quick rappel or handline off the summit pinnacle. No traditional rock protection is generally needed.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel gear (rope, pickets, crevasse-rescue kit) plus a light rack of slings for a short rappel/handline on the summit block; no cams or nuts typically placed.
proTips: Backcountry permits for Sahale Glacier Camp are limited and competitive in summer — reserve early or be flexible on dates.
Start early to beat both crowds on the popular trail and softening snow on the glacier.
Microspikes or an ice axe are useful on the switchbacks below Cascade Pass into early-mid July.
watchOut: Crevasses on the Sahale Glacier open up as the season progresses — assess conditions before committing to a line.
The summit block is loose in places and exposed; helmets recommended.
Afternoon thunderstorms and fast-changing alpine weather are common in summer.
knownHazards: Crevasse hazard on the Sahale Glacier, worsening later in the season.
Exposed, moderately loose 3rd/4th-class summit block.
Steep snow requiring an ice axe on the approach switchbacks into early summer.

road.name: Cascade River Road

road.driveNote: 23.1 mi from SR-20 in Marblemount to the Cascade Pass trailhead

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades)

bySeason.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack; road to trailhead often still gated.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); still expect afternoon thunderstorms and possible snow above 7,000 ft.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing snow by October.
bySeason.winter: Road gated well below the trailhead; deep snow, avalanche terrain.

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.fees: No park entrance fee; overnight backcountry camping requires a permit ($10/person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee).
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight stays at Sahale Glacier Camp or Pelton Basin, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount; competitive advance reservation and walk-up lottery in summer.
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated at MP 20 (Eldorado Creek) outside the summer season.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start from camp (or an alpine start for a single-day car-to-car push) to have firm snow and daylight margin on the glacier and summit block.

sectionBreakdown.note: ~3.7 mi, forested switchbacks

Roped glacier travel then 3rd/4th-class summit block

emergency.county: Chelan / Skagit (summit sits on the county line; standard trailhead access is Skagit County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue, dispatched via 911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount - (360) 854-7245

routes.overview: A route from Boston Basin up the Quien Sabe Glacier's northwest side to the Boston-Sahale col, then an exposed snow-and-rock ridge traverse to the summit; more direct and slightly more sustained than the Sahale Arm route.
routes.beta: From Boston Basin, hike to the toe of the Quien Sabe Glacier and rope up. The easier line follows the left (north) side of the glacier toward Sharkfin Tower, then traverses right along the base of the rock face to the Boston-Sahale col; an alternate line up the glacier's right side is steeper (up to 40-45 degrees) and more exposed to crevasse hazard. The two lines merge partway up. From the col, traverse an exposed snow ridge (ice axe and crampons) to the base of the summit block, then scramble loose 3rd-class rock to the top. A cornice can persist at the col into early summer.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive east on Cascade River Road about 21.7 mi to the old Diamond Mine Road pullout (~3,200-3,400 ft). Walk the abandoned mining road about 1 mi, then follow the steep, unmaintained Boston Basin climbers' path (four stream crossings) roughly 2-3 more miles and 2,100+ ft of gain into Boston Basin, where camps sit around 5,300 ft (lower) and 6,400 ft (upper moraine sites), about 3-4 hours from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Either reverse the ascent line back down the Quien Sabe Glacier to Boston Basin, or traverse from the summit area over to the Sahale Glacier and descend via the Sahale Arm route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the loose class-3 block to the ridge, retrace the exposed snow traverse to the Boston-Sahale col, and descend the Quien Sabe Glacier back toward Boston Basin camp, watching for crevasses and the cornice near the col. Some parties instead descend the Sahale Glacier and Sahale Arm to Cascade Pass as an alternate exit, requiring a car shuttle or long road walk back to the Boston Basin trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-day trip: Day 1, approach to Boston Basin camp (3-4 hrs); Day 2, early glacier start, ~7 hrs camp-to-summit plus ~2 hrs back to camp, then hike out. Fit parties can push it into one long 12-14 hr car-to-car day.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same glacier line to Boston Basin is generally straightforward in good conditions; the steeper right-side glacier variant and the col traverse are the sections where retreating becomes more involved due to crevasse exposure.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before the col traverse and summit scramble based on cornice/crevasse conditions, weather, and party pace — the exposed ridge and loose summit rock are not places to be rushed or caught in worsening weather.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (roughly July-September) once the Cascade River Road and Boston Basin trail have melted out; a cornice at the Boston-Sahale col can linger into early summer, adding hazard and requiring more caution earlier in the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in Boston Basin or on the glacier; carry a satellite communicator/PLB and do not rely on phone coverage.
gear: A few pickets for steeper snow sections
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the steep approach trail
routes.detailedRack: Light glacier kit: rope, a handful of pickets for the steeper snow sections, and standard crevasse-rescue gear (prusiks, pulley, etc.); a few slings for protection or a short rappel/handline on the loose class-3 summit block. No traditional rock rack generally needed.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier-travel protection (pickets, rope, crevasse-rescue kit) is the main need; only minimal gear (slings) for the summit block.
proTips: Take the left (north) side of the Quien Sabe Glacier for an easier, less exposed line than the steeper right-side variant.
Start early while snow is firm, especially if using the steeper glacier line.
Check current conditions for the col cornice before committing in early season.
watchOut: Crevasses on the lower-to-mid glacier, worse on the steeper right-side line (up to ~40-45 degrees).
A cornice can persist at the Boston-Sahale col into early summer.
Loose rock on the final class-3 summit scramble.
knownHazards: Crevasse fields on the Quien Sabe Glacier, more severe on the steeper right-side variant.
Cornice hazard at the Boston-Sahale col in early season.
Loose, exposed rock near the summit block.

pitchDetail.notes: Boston Basin camp to glacier toe, roped up before stepping on ice.
Left side is broken but moderate; right side is steeper with more crevasse exposure.
Ice axe/crampons for the ridge traverse, then loose rock scramble to the top.

road.driveNote: About 21.7 mi from Marblemount to the old Diamond Mine Road pullout for the Boston Basin trail

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades)
climate.typical: Cool, wet shoulder seasons and a short summer climbing window; conditions change quickly at elevation.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack, road often still gated, heavy cornice/avalanche hazard.
bySeason.summer: Primary season (Jul-Sep); still expect afternoon storms and lingering snow patches.
bySeason.fall: Shortening windows, increasing snow by October.
bySeason.winter: Road access gated well below the trailhead.

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.fees: No park entrance fee; overnight camping in Boston Basin requires a backcountry permit ($10/person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee).
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight camping, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount; Boston Basin sites are limited and competitive in summer.
access.closures: Cascade River Road gated at MP 20 (Eldorado Creek) outside the summer season.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start from Boston Basin camp for firm snow on the glacier and margin for the col traverse and summit scramble.

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E. Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA - (360) 424-4111 (Level III ED); United General/Skagit Regional Health, Sedro-Woolley, is closer to Marblemount

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt data (8,680 ft) matches sourced summit elevation exactly; no correction needed on the peak's elevation. The provided route grade 'Grade II' and 'Class 3-4 summit block' are consistent with Mountain Project's listing of the summit block as low 4th class / Class 3 loose rock.

blurb: Boston Peak (8,894 ft) rises above Boston Basin in the North Cascades of Washington, within North Cascades National Park, near the Skagit-Chelan county line, flanked by the Quien Sabe, Boston, and Davenport glaciers with Sahale Peak just to its south and Buckner Mountain across Ripsaw Ridge to the east. The peak was named for the Boston Mine, which worked its southeast side in the late 1800s, and was first climbed in 1938 by Calder Bressler, Ray Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers. It has a strong reputation among North Cascades climbers for loose, friable rock and sustained exposure even on its technically easiest lines.

routes.rappels: Typically 2-3 rappels (60m rope), station-to-station, back down through the crux band to the Boston-Sahale col
routes.face: South-facing rock/ridge above the Boston-Sahale col (note: several primary trip-report sources label this same standard line the 'Southeast Face' or 'East Face' rather than 'Southwest Face' - see corrections)
routes.overview: Boston Peak's standard, non-technical line: glacier travel to the Boston-Sahale col, then a sustained, exposed Class 3-4 scramble up loose ledges and ribs to a short crux band just below the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From the Boston-Sahale saddle, climbers work up a steepening ridge/face on generally loose rock, weaving between small ledges. Climbing stays exposed Class 3 until roughly 50 feet below the summit, where one or two Class 4 (low 5th-class) moves - either a short chimney or the rib just to its right - form the crux. Above, the angle eases and a short, narrow, highly exposed summit ridge leads to the highpoint.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road to the Boston Basin trailhead (about 21.7 mi from Marblemount, elev. ~3,200 ft; gated at Eldorado Creek, MP 20, outside the snow-free season). Hike an old mine road and climbers' path roughly 2.5 mi and ~3,000-3,100 ft to treeline and the Boston Basin camps (low camp ~5,300 ft, high camp ~6,400 ft). From upper Boston Basin, rope up and cross the Quien Sabe Glacier to the Boston-Sahale col, the start of the face/ridge climbing. An alternate approach traverses in via Cascade Pass and the Sahale Arm/Sahale Glacier, then the connecting ridge from Sahale Peak to the same col.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to the Boston-Sahale col, downclimbing where reasonable and rappelling the steeper/crux terrain.
routes.descentText: Most parties downclimb the easier Class 3 sections and make 2-3 rappels with a 60m rope off fixed anchors/tat through the crux band and steepest terrain back to the Boston-Sahale col, then re-cross the Quien Sabe Glacier to Boston Basin. Trip reports note that existing rappel webbing on this peak can be old and weathered - back it up rather than trusting it outright.
routes.itinerary: Usually climbed in a single long day from a Boston Basin or Sahale Arm camp; parties who combine it with Sahale Peak via the connecting ridge have reported 12-15 hour car-to-car days.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the climbed terrain to the Boston-Sahale col; there is no shorter escape once established on the upper face/ridge, so decide before committing to the crux band if weather, conditions, or daylight are marginal.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting up from the col - the crux band and summit ridge are sustained, exposed, and slow to reverse as a group, and afternoon thunderstorms or wet rock substantially raise both difficulty and rockfall hazard on this loose peak.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, after the Quien Sabe Glacier and approach snow have consolidated; a first winter ascent (February 2024) was made via the separate, more technical West Face, but the standard face/ridge line described here is a summer scramble.
routes.detailedRack: A small alpine rock rack (a few small-to-mid cams/stoppers) is enough for the short crux; standard glacier-travel gear (harness, crevasse-rescue kit, and a picket or two, or screws late-season) is needed for the Quien Sabe Glacier crossing.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection overall - most of the route is unroped scrambling, with a single short low-5th-class crux that parties often protect with one or two small pieces (or solo); size the rack mainly for the glacier crossing and that one section.
whatToBring: Bear canister (required for camping in Boston Basin, Jun 1-Nov 15)
Extra webbing to back up old rappel anchors
proTips: Many parties combine Boston Peak with Sahale Peak via the connecting ridge in one long day.
Carry extra webbing to back up old, weathered rappel anchors on the descent.
Wilderness permits for Boston Basin/Cascade River Road trailheads must be reserved on Recreation.gov and picked up in person at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center.
A bear canister is mandatory for overnight camping in Boston Basin (Jun 1-Nov 15).
watchOut: Rock throughout is loose and friable - test every hold before weighting it.
Wet rock dramatically increases both difficulty and rockfall danger.
Old fixed rappel anchors/webbing have been reported in poor condition.
The connecting ridge from Sahale is exposed knife-edge scrambling over unstable talus and scree.
knownHazards: Persistent loose rock and rockfall, especially in gullies and along the ridge.
Crevasse hazard while roped-travel crossing the Quien Sabe Glacier.
High, sustained exposure on the summit ridge where a fall would likely be fatal.
Bears are frequently seen in Boston Basin; proper food storage is mandatory.

pitchDetail.notes: Boston Basin trail to upper basin, then roped glacier travel across the Quien Sabe Glacier to the Boston-Sahale col.
Steepening, loose ridge/face climbing from the col, weaving between small ledges.
One or two moves roughly 50 ft below the summit - a short chimney or the rib just to its right.
Short, narrow, highly exposed ridge walk to the true summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal - generally passable late spring through fall; subject to storm washouts, check current NPS road conditions before driving
road.seasonalGate: Gated at Eldorado Creek (MP 20) during late autumn, winter, and spring, about 3 miles before the Cascade Pass trailhead
road.driveNote: Gravel beyond the pavement's end; the Boston Basin trailhead pullout is at roughly MP 21.7 (elev. ~3,200 ft). The road has a history of storm washouts (e.g., near MP 20-23) that can force a longer hike- or bike-in when repairs are pending.

climate.forecastZone: NWS West Slopes North Cascades zone / NWAC Cascade Pass-area forecasts
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and drier, milder summers typical of the western Cascade Crest; easterly flow through Cascade Pass can bring locally colder or drier conditions than areas farther west.

bySeason.spring: Deep, consolidating snowpack on the approach and glacier; avalanche and cornice hazard remain into early summer.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul-Sep); glaciers open up with visible crevasses and afternoon thunderstorm risk increases by early afternoon.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and increasing chance of early snow; rock sections get colder and wetter, raising rockfall risk on this already loose peak.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall, avalanche hazard, and a gated access road (MP 20); a first winter ascent of the separate West Face route was made in February 2024 by a well-equipped party.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: Wilderness permit: $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee; youth 15 and under free
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay in Boston Basin. Reserve via Recreation.gov (permit ID 4675322) and pick up in person at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center - all Cascade River Road trailhead permits (Cascade Pass, Boston Basin) must be picked up there; about 40% of sites are held for walk-up/next-day issuance.
access.closures: Boston Basin is managed as a Cross-Country Zone with a party quota (multiple sources cite roughly 6 parties at a time); bear canisters required for camping Jun 1-Nov 15; Cascade River Road is gated seasonally at MP 20.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start from a Boston Basin or Sahale Arm camp to cross the glacier on firm snow and be off the exposed summit ridge before typical afternoon thunderstorm buildup

emergency.county: Skagit County on the approach/trailhead side; the summit sits on the Skagit-Chelan county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 (after-hours backup for park dispatch)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount - (360) 854-7245; park dispatch (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, after-hours communications center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley - (360) 856-6021; Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III trauma center), Mount Vernon - (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Call 911 for emergencies. Cell coverage is unreliable to absent in Boston Basin and on the peak itself.

routes.corrections: Given route name is 'Southwest Face,' but multiple primary trip-report sources (CascadeClimbers.com TRs from 2013, 2016, 2019) label essentially this same standard scramble line from the Boston-Sahale col as the 'Southeast Face' or 'East Face.' A separate, more technical West Face (5 roped pitches, first climbed Sept. 1956 by Anderson and Shonle; first winter ascent Feb 2024) and a Northwest Ridge (5.5, 10 pitches) also exist on the peak per Mountain Project but are distinct routes not covered here. Because of this aspect-naming inconsistency, 'aspect' was left null rather than guessed. Peak elevation is given/confirmed at 8,894 ft by Wikipedia, NPS-adjacent sources, and mountain-forecast.com; note ListsOfJohn.com shows a slightly different figure (8,888 ft) from an older survey/benchmark, not used here.

blurb: Sharkfin Tower (8,120 ft) is a striking granite tooth on the high ridge connecting Forbidden Peak and Boston Peak in North Cascades National Park's Boston Basin, with the Quien Sabe Glacier below its south side and the Boston Glacier sprawling beneath its north face. The peak is known for some of the cleanest, most solid granite in Washington State. It was first climbed in August 1947 by Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, and Joseph Vance via the Southeast Ridge, and the same gully system was the site of a fatal 2005 rockfall/rappel-anchor accident that killed three Mountaineers club members.

routes.fa: Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, Joseph Vance — August 1947 (first ascent of the peak, via this route)
routes.rappels: Downclimb the ascent line is standard; several fixed rappel anchors exist in the descent gully, but multiple trip reports and a fatal 2005 NPS accident investigation warn that anchors here are often marginal (single blocks/anchors with no backup) — inspect, back up, and avoid loading isolated rock features.
routes.face: Southeast Ridge/Southeast side of the tower (distinct from the Northwest Face and Southeast Face routes)
routes.overview: A short, moderate alpine rock route following the crest of Sharkfin Tower's southeast ridge, combining a bit of glacier travel and a steep snow/scree gully with several pitches of clean, low-fifth-class granite directly on the ridgeline above the Boston Glacier.
routes.beta: From Boston Basin, cross onto the Quien Sabe Glacier (as for Sahale Peak) and traverse right beneath Sharkfin Tower to a gully — snow in early season, loose scree by late summer — that climbs a steep rock band to a notch at the base of the southeast ridge. From the notch, 3 pitches of low 5th class (5.0, with one pitch that feels closer to 5.4 due to sustained exposure) follow the ridge crest in a spectacular position above the Boston Glacier to the small summit.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles from Marblemount to the Boston Basin trailhead (~3,200-3,400 ft). Follow an old abandoned mining road for about a mile, then a climbers' path across several stream crossings, emerging above treeline at Boston Basin low camp (~5,300 ft); high camp is at roughly 6,400 ft. From camp, ascend toward the Quien Sabe Glacier and traverse below Sharkfin Tower to the base gully.
routes.descent: Downclimb the ascent ridge back to the notch, then descend the approach gully to the glacier and camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the low-5th-class/4th-class ridge back to the notch at its base. Descend the steep gully below the notch — this is the most hazard-prone part of the route: it is loose scree by mid-to-late summer, and several parties have used fixed or improvised rappel anchors here. Treat any anchor with suspicion (a single-block anchor failed and caused three fatalities in July 2005); back up marginal anchors, avoid loading loose blocks, and consider downclimbing over rappelling where terrain allows. Continue across the Quien Sabe Glacier and back to Boston Basin camp, then retrace the approach trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Boston Basin trailhead and hike in to low or high camp (roughly 4 hours). Day 2: Alpine start; cross the Quien Sabe Glacier, climb the approach gully to the SE Ridge notch, climb the 3-pitch ridge to the summit, then reverse the route to camp — parties report 9-10.5 hour round trips from camp. Some fit teams combine Sharkfin Tower with Sahale Peak's Quien Sabe Glacier route in the same day. Day 3: Optional additional climbing or hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the ridge to the notch at any point and descending the approach gully; there is no fixed rappel route requirement, but existing fixed anchors in the gully have a documented history of failure — treat all anchors with skepticism and prefer downclimbing or building redundant anchors on multiple solid features.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before committing to the ridge pitches — afternoon thunderstorms build quickly in the North Cascades, and the descent gully is at its most dangerous (loose rock, rockfall onto parties below, marginal anchor options) when climbers are rushing or descending in a group; leave enough daylight and margin to downclimb deliberately rather than rappel under time pressure.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the standard window. Early season (late June-July) the approach gully still holds snow, requiring crampons and an ice axe but giving firmer, more predictable footing; by August the gully has melted to loose, unstable scree that is manageable but tedious, and the bergschrund/moat on the Quien Sabe Glacier can become difficult to cross late in the season.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in Boston Basin's rugged terrain but is sometimes available from higher, more exposed points such as the ridge and summit; do not count on a signal at camp or in the approach gully. If you do get through, state your name, callback number, and location immediately in case the call drops.
routes.detailedRack: A single 30-40m rope (or a pair of light 8mm ropes, as used by some parties for the rappel-capable descent) is standard, paired with a small alpine rock rack of cams and stoppers — the climbing is short, moderate (5.0, low 5th class) on clean, solid granite, so a full trad rack is unnecessary.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is straightforward on solid granite; a light set of cams (small-to-medium) and a few nuts covers the ridge pitches, which have good gear where needed despite feeling more exposed than the grade suggests.
gear: 30-40m rope (or two thin ropes for rappelling)
whatToBring: Approach or rock shoes for the ridge pitches (mountaineering boots also work)
IGBC-approved bear canister (required for camping below treeline in Boston Basin, June 1-Nov 15)
Extra insulating layers for exposed ridge climbing
Glacier glasses/sunglasses for the Quien Sabe Glacier crossing
proTips: Many parties combine Sharkfin Tower with Sahale Peak's Quien Sabe Glacier route from the same Boston Basin camp in one long day.
Climbing early season while the approach gully still holds snow (with crampons and an axe) is often faster and more secure footing than the loose scree of late summer.
Carry light rock shoes even though the grade is easy — the exposure on the ridge pitches makes secure footwear worthwhile.
watchOut: The descent gully has a documented history of serious rockfall and a fatal rappel-anchor failure (July 2005, three Mountaineers killed) — do not build or trust single-point anchors on isolated blocks here.
Route-finding into the wrong gully or notch has contributed to past accidents; positively identify the correct approach gully and ridge notch before committing.
Afternoon thunderstorms and rockfall risk both argue for an early start and a firm turnaround time.
knownHazards: A loose gully below the SE Ridge notch has produced serious rockfall and at least one fatal rappel-anchor failure that killed three climbers in July 2005.
The Quien Sabe Glacier crossing carries real crevasse hazard, particularly late in the season as it opens up and the moat near the tower can become difficult to cross.
Bears are regularly active in Boston Basin; IGBC-approved food canisters are required for camping below treeline from June 1 through November 15.

pitchDetail.notes: From the ridge notch, skirt right on large blocks then follow a short dihedral back to the ridge crest for a belay.

pitchDetail.grade: 5.0 (feels closer to 5.4 due to sustained exposure)
pitchDetail.notes: Follow solid rock directly on the ridge crest with good protection; steep and exposed in a striking position above the Boston Glacier.
Scramble on or just right of the ridge crest to the small summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Gravel road subject to seasonal washouts, rockslides, and construction closures; check current NPS/USFS road-condition pages before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens for the season by early-to-mid summer once snow/damage is cleared; can close temporarily for flooding or slides in spring or late summer.
road.driveNote: The Boston Basin trailhead is reached via Cascade River Road, about 21.7 miles from Marblemount off SR-20.

climate.typical: A maritime alpine climate with generally dry, warm summer days and cool nights, but fast-building afternoon thunderstorms and sudden weather changes are common even in July and August.

bySeason.spring: Still largely snowbound; not the standard climbing season for this rock route.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jun-Sep); early summer holds more snow in the approach gully (crampons/axe useful), late summer exposes loose scree and drier rock.
bySeason.fall: Climbable into September in stable weather; shorter days and early-season snow become a growing concern.
bySeason.winter: Not a winter climbing objective; full winter alpine conditions with significant avalanche hazard.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; overnight backcountry/wilderness permit costs $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee.
access.permit: A wilderness permit is required to camp overnight in Boston Basin, obtainable via Recreation.gov reservation or as a walk-up permit at the NPS Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: Cascade River Road and the Boston Basin approach can close seasonally for snow, flooding, rockslides, wildfire, or bear activity — check the NPS climbing-conditions and trail-conditions pages before heading out.

timing.recommendedStart: An early/alpine start from camp is standard to cross the glacier on firm snow and finish the descent gully before afternoon thunderstorms or softening conditions.

sectionBreakdown.note: Old mine road plus climbers' path with several stream crossings.
Quien Sabe Glacier traverse then the approach gully to the SE Ridge notch.
3 pitches of low 5th class plus scrambling.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Boston Basin low camp to summit and back
sectionBreakdown.note: One documented trip report cites 9 hours 12 minutes round trip including a ~1h43m break; another cites roughly 10.5 hours round trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7245; park headquarters (Sedro-Woolley): 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III Emergency Department) is the nearest full-service hospital; Cascade Valley Hospital in Arlington, WA has a Level IV ED and is also used regionally.
emergency.notes: Cell coverage in Boston Basin and on the route is unreliable; rescues typically require an NPS helicopter and technical rescue team dispatched from Marblemount. Give your name, callback number, and location immediately when you get a connection, in case the call drops.

routes.corrections: The given data listed rockGrade 5.4 and 4 pitches; three independent, mutually-corroborating sources (Wikipedia, Mountain Project's route database, and Steph Abegg's detailed trip report) consistently document the Southeast Ridge as YDS 5.0 over 3 pitches, so those were corrected. Note there is genuine disagreement in the field: two guide-service pages (North Cascade Mountain Guides and BC Adventure Guides) describe this same route as 5.4 (one calling it 'III 5.4' over 6 pitches), likely a more conservative/inclusive rating of the exposed crux moves and glacier/gully sections rather than the bare rock-climbing YDS grade — this is reflected in pitch 2's notes above. No source was found that assigns this route an explicit NCCS commitment grade or a French/UIAA alpine grade (the input's implied 'Grade II'/'PD' could not be confirmed), so both were left null. Elevation for the peak/summit varies slightly by source (8,120 ft per Wikipedia/Peakbagger/USGS-style listings vs. 8,160-8,165 ft per Mountain Project and Timberline Mountain Guides); the canonical 8,120 ft was retained as it matches the input data and the majority of sources. gainFt/lossFt/distKm are derived from sourced elevations/distances (trailhead ~3,200 ft, summit 8,120 ft, ~2.5 mi/3,100 ft to Boston Basin camp) rather than a single all-in-one sourced total, since no source states a complete trailhead-to-summit gain/distance figure for this specific route.

blurb: Johannesburg Mountain (8,200+ ft) rises immediately south of Cascade Pass in the North Cascades of Skagit County, Washington, and is famous for its immense, glaciated Northeast Face, which drops roughly 5,000 feet in under a mile and feeds several hanging glaciers prone to rock and icefall. It was first climbed July 26, 1938 by Calder Bressler, Bill Cox, Ray W. Clough, and Tom Myers via the East Ridge/Cascade-Johannesburg Couloir. Despite modest prominence, its steep relief and objective hazard have given it a fearsome reputation among North Cascades alpinists, with only a handful of ascents most years.

routes.rappels: ~7 rappels down the East Ridge to the C-J col, then continue via Doug's Direct back to Cascade Pass
objHaz: Rockfall and icefall from Johannesburg's hanging glaciers onto the approach/lower buttress
Loose rock throughout, especially the upper ridge and chimney
Breakable vegetation holds (tree limbs/roots) on the lower buttress
Crevasses/bergschrund on the Cache Glacier during the Doug's Direct descent
routes.overview: A long, sustained alpine line up Johannesburg's massive Northeast Buttress: thousands of feet of 3rd/4th-class terrain, including notorious near-vertical bushwhacking through trees, heather, and mossy streambeds, before the route breaks onto a summit snow arete and a short AI2 ice pitch to the top.
routes.beta: The longest line on one of the biggest walls in the range. From steep snow at the base, climb slabs and cross waterfalls, then fight up through a literal wall of brush and trees trending right toward the ridge. Above the trees the character changes to steep heather and 3rd/4th-class rock along the buttress crest, with two historical start variations (the steeper, more direct 1951 line and the lower, more belayed 1957 line) merging before a short 5.5-5.8 chimney leads to a bivy around 7,100 ft. Above the bivy, a exposed snow arete and a ~200-ft AI2 ice pitch (three screws) lead through notches to the summit. Trip reports describe roughly 30 roped/simul-climbed pitches given the route's sustained, unrelenting nature — noticeably more than a quick scramble.
routes.approach: Park at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (or the 'hairpin' pullout on Cascade River Road). Descend and ford the Cascade River, climb up and over the terminal moraine, and work up steep snow toward an obvious snow finger that leads rightward onto the toe of the buttress.
routes.descent: Doug's Direct: from the summit, descend the East Ridge (about 7 rappels) to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, then traverse toward the Triplets buttress and up to Mixup Peak's ridge before dropping to the Cache Glacier and the Cascade Pass climber's trail.
routes.descentText: From the C-J col, descend down and left toward the base of the Triplets buttress (~6,000 ft). Climb up and right across heather and talus, aiming for the right-hand 'heather tongue' leading up toward Mixup Peak (about 1,500 ft of gain from the Triplets buttress). From the ridgecrest, descend 3rd/4th-class slabs and blocks to the Cache Glacier, traverse left toward the Mixup Arm to pick up a climber's trail, and follow it back to Cascade Pass. Some parties instead descend via 8+ rappels off Mixup Peak's north ridge. The steep, 60-degree heather sidehilling near the start of this descent is widely described as the most dangerous part of the whole outing.
routes.itinerary: Most parties bivy partway up the buttress (around 7,100 ft) after a long first day of climbing, then finish to the summit and complete the Doug's Direct descent on day two; strong parties occasionally push it car-to-car in one very long day (roughly 11-24 hours total by published time estimates).
routes.bail: Retreat is genuinely difficult once committed to the buttress — the terrain is low-angle but continuously loose, vegetated, and poorly protected. Below the ~7,100-ft bivy, downclimbing or rappelling the line of ascent using improvised tree/heather anchors is essentially the only option.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's unrelenting length and the well-documented history of parties returning a day or more late, climbers should set a firm turnaround based on remaining daylight, water, and bivy gear rather than pushing to the summit at all costs.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, once the lower slabs and bushwhack sections have melted out enough to climb but before fall storms move in; the upper snow arete and ice pitch conditions vary meaningfully year to year.
routes.detailedRack: Small alpine rock rack to about a #2 Camalot (1 in), 2-3 knifeblade pitons (optional), 3 ice screws, 1-2 snow pickets, many long slings for improvised anchors in brush and heather, 60m rope, ice axe with hammer, crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is used as much for psychological security through loose rock and vegetation as for hard climbing; small cams and long slings for tree/heather belays low on the route, with screws and pickets reserved for the summit snow arete and short ice pitch.
whatToBring: approach shoes or light boots for the vegetated lower buttress
cell phone or satellite communicator for emergencies
proTips: The 1957 start (lower, with more belayed pitches on 'more logical terrain') is generally preferred over the steeper, scarier 5.7 1951 start.
Bring extra slings — most low-buttress anchors are improvised off trees and heather, not gear placements.
Study the Doug's Direct descent beta closely beforehand; multiple trip reports call it more dangerous and harder to navigate than the climb itself.
watchOut: The vegetated lower buttress carries real fall potential — climbers describe grabbing tree limbs and roots more than rock.
Rockfall and icefall from Johannesburg's hanging glaciers threaten the approach and lower slopes.
Parties commonly return one to two days late; don't let your trip plan/emergency contact assume a fast turnaround.
knownHazards: Loose rock is pervasive, especially on the upper ridge and summit chimney.
Icefall and rockfall from hanging glaciers affect the approach and lower buttress.
Crevasse and bergschrund crossings are required on the Cache Glacier during the Doug's Direct descent.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep snow to slabs; loose rock, minimal protection
Climbing through brush and trees, trending right toward the ridge
Heather slopes on the buttress proper; marginal protection
Short chimney leads to the ~7,100 ft bivy site
Significant exposure on both sides
~200 ft; 3 ice screws adequate for protection
Final scrambling through notches to the summit

road.name: Cascade River Road (FR 15 / NPS)
road.status: Paved to about mile 10, then gravel; final ~3 miles narrow with steep drop-offs
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June, sometimes not until early July in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from Marblemount (SR 20) to Cascade Pass Trailhead; trailers/vehicles over 22 ft long or 8 ft wide are prohibited past the park boundary (mile 18); high-clearance recommended for washouts/ruts near the end.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime climate typical of the west-side North Cascades; the July-September window offers the most stable weather for this route.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack and avalanche/icefall hazard from the hanging glaciers; road often still gated by snow.
bySeason.summer: Warmest, driest window and the standard climbing season, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and increasing storm frequency by October.
bySeason.winter: Deep snow, road closed, high avalanche hazard.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS); lower Cascade River Road managed by Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.fees: No entrance fee at North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Overnight/bivy requires a wilderness camping permit ($10/person + $6 reservation fee); climbing permits are available at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.closures: Final miles of Cascade River Road are typically snow-gated into June (sometimes later); check current NPS road conditions before driving up

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start given the route's overall length

sectionBreakdown.note: Published range roughly 8-12 hours
Published range roughly 1-3 hours

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Summit to base of East Ridge/C-J col
sectionBreakdown.note: Published range roughly 3-5 hours

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: East Ridge via Doug's Direct to Cascade Pass Trailhead
sectionBreakdown.note: Published range roughly 4-6 hours

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office coordinates Search and Rescue for this area; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA 98267 — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in the Cascade River valley and on the peak; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. This route has a well-documented history of parties returning a day or more later than planned.

routes.corrections: Given pitch count of 20 is likely low: cascadeclimbers.com/summitpost trip-report accounts describe roughly 30 roped/simul-climbed pitches on this route given its sustained, unrelenting character. No source found identifies a specific first-ascent party/year distinct from the peak's 1938 FA (via the separate East Ridge/C-J Couloir line) or the named 1951/1957 rib-start variations, so 'fa' is left null rather than guessed.

routes.fa: Calder Bressler, Bill Cox, Ray W. Clough, Tom Myers — July 26, 1938 (also the peak's first ascent)
routes.rappels: Up to 3 rappels through the steeper upper gully sections on descent, using existing slings
objHaz: Continuous rockfall/icefall from the Sill Glacier onto the lower two-thirds of the couloir
Crevasses and bergschrunds requiring route-finding, worse in late season
Avalanche hazard on the approach and within the couloir
Historically overhung by an active serac (has since retreated per recent reports)
routes.overview: The most direct line up Johannesburg and its original route: a sustained ~3,000-3,400-ft snow/ice couloir rising to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, followed by Class 4 scrambling up the East Ridge to the summit — historic and 'straightforward' in line, but with serious, near-continuous overhead rock/icefall exposure from the Sill Glacier.
routes.beta: Described by multiple sources as the most direct but also most dangerous line on the mountain due to rock/icefall potential. The route boots up firm snow in the lower couloir, narrows through a ~300-ft 'bottleneck' (20-50 ft wide, 40+ degrees) with a short water-ice step (about AI2), then continues through variable upper snow past multiple crevasses/bergschrunds to the Cascade-Johannesburg col. From the col, Class 4 scrambling along the East Ridge leads to the summit. As the Sill Glacier breaks up through the season the couloir gets progressively harder and more hazardous, so it is best treated as an early-season (spring) objective when snow still bridges the crevasses.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead or pullouts further down Cascade River Road (near the 'hairpin'/Eldorado parking area), descend into the valley, cross the creek/avalanche fan, and work up talus and brush to the base of the couloir below the Sill Glacier.
routes.descent: Most parties downclimb the same couloir (or an adjacent 'northern chute' variant), moving quickly through the exposed bottleneck; a few rappels off existing slings are common through the steepest sections. Spring ascents are frequently descended on skis or a splitboard.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent line down the upper couloir to the col area, then descend the bottleneck and lower couloir, using ice screws/pickets for protection through the technical steps and rappelling steeper sections off existing slings where present. In stable spring snow the couloir can be skied nearly top to bottom with cautious jump turns; descent of the couloir alone has been reported to take roughly 2.5 hours.
routes.itinerary: Commonly climbed car-to-car in a single long day; some parties bivy near the col. Spring ski-mountaineering parties often combine it with a same-day ski descent.
