routes.bail: Once committed to the couloir, retreat is essentially limited to downclimbing or rappelling the same line — moving quickly through the exposed bottleneck to minimize time under overhead hazard is the priority in any retreat.
routes.turnaround: Because the couloir is exposed to rockfall and icefall for nearly its full length, climbers should commit only in cold, stable conditions and turn around if daytime warming softens the snow/ice or if rockfall/icefall activity increases.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly April through July while snow still bridges the crevasses/bergschrunds and overnight refreeze holds rock and ice in place; by late summer the couloir becomes discontinuous, icier, and considerably more hazardous.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe (a second tool is useful for the steeper bottleneck), crampons, a few ice screws, 1-2 snow pickets for the col and bottleneck sections, and a rope for glacier travel and any rappels; a light rock rack is sometimes carried for the upper gullies but often goes unused.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is almost entirely snow/ice (screws and pickets); the upper gully rock is loose and takes protection poorly.
gear: ice axe (second tool for steep sections)
whatToBring: early alpine start to beat daytime rock/icefall
extra layers for a cold col
skis or splitboard optional for a spring descent
proTips: Climb it early season (spring) when snow still bridges the crevasses — the route gets progressively more dangerous and discontinuous through summer.
Move fast through the ~300-ft bottleneck, which narrows to 20-50 ft at 40+ degrees under direct overhead exposure.
A cold, stable morning start meaningfully reduces rock/icefall risk compared with an afternoon ascent.
watchOut: This is one of the most heavily-warned-about lines in the Cascades for overhead hazard — rock and ice fall from the Sill Glacier threaten the lower two-thirds nearly continuously.
Multiple crevasse/bergschrund crossings can be difficult to pass, especially in low-snow years.
Conditions and hazard level swing significantly year to year and even week to week as the glacier breaks up.
knownHazards: Near-continuous rock/icefall exposure from the Sill Glacier for most of the couloir's length.
Crevasse and bergschrund crossings, more difficult and dangerous late season.
Avalanche hazard on the approach and within the couloir, especially in spring.

pitchDetail.notes: Creek crossing and brush to roughly 3,300 ft
Steepening snow climbing toward the bottleneck
~300 ft long, narrows to 20-50 ft wide, direct overhead exposure
Variable snow, multiple crevasse/bergschrund crossings to the C-J col
Ridge scrambling from the col to the summit

road.name: Cascade River Road (FR 15 / NPS)
road.status: Paved to about mile 10, then gravel; final ~3 miles narrow with steep drop-offs
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June, sometimes not until early July in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from Marblemount (SR 20) to Cascade Pass Trailhead; some parties park lower (near the Eldorado/"hairpin" pullouts around mile 20) for a shorter approach to this route; trailers/vehicles over 22 ft or 8 ft wide are prohibited past the park boundary (mile 18).

climate.typical: Wet, maritime climate typical of the west-side North Cascades; spring cold snaps produce the most stable snow/ice conditions for this route.

bySeason.spring: Prime season — cold, stable freeze-thaw cycles and crevasse bridging, but road access may still be limited by snow lower down.
bySeason.summer: Couloir becomes discontinuous and more rockfall-prone as the Sill Glacier breaks up; less favorable by mid-to-late summer.
bySeason.fall: Increasing storm frequency; not a typical season for this route.
bySeason.winter: Deep snow and high avalanche hazard; occasionally climbed/skied in rare stable winter freeze windows.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS); lower Cascade River Road managed by Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.fees: No entrance fee at North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Overnight/bivy requires a wilderness camping permit ($10/person + $6 reservation fee); climbing permits available at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.closures: Final miles of Cascade River Road are typically snow-gated into June (sometimes later); check current NPS road conditions before driving up

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start essential to minimize time in the couloir before daytime warming increases rock/icefall risk

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office coordinates Search and Rescue for this area; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA 98267 — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in the Cascade River valley and on the peak; carry a satellite communicator/PLB given the overhead hazard on this route.

blurb: Mount Torment (8,120+ ft) rises on the west side of Boston Basin in North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, WA, immediately west of its more famous neighbor Forbidden Peak. Built of gneiss and granite sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, it is a double-summited alpine peak reached via the gentle Taboo Glacier. It was first climbed August 23, 1946 by Jack Schwabland and Herb Staley, who — per the standard naming account — named it for "the torture of a hot day with only one orange for quenching thirst"; today the peak is best known as the start of the classic Torment-Forbidden Traverse.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper & Walt Sellers, 1958 (per Mountain Project's route page). Note: this is distinct from Mount Torment's overall peak first ascent (Jack Schwabland & Herb Staley, Aug 23, 1946), which likely used this or a very similar line but is not confirmed as the same route.
routes.season: Jun-Sep (best Jul-Aug); a short entry snow couloir favors an early-season ascent
objHaz: Loose, crumbly rock in the gully below the upper notch creates rockfall hazard for parties below.
Boston Basin has documented bear activity; food storage in an approved canister is required June 1-Nov 15.
Rapid weather changes and afternoon thunderstorms are common in this alpine zone.
routes.overview: A moderate 4th/low-5th-class ridge line up Torment's south side from Boston Basin — the most popular independent route to the summit and the standard start of the Torment-Forbidden Traverse.
routes.beta: From Boston Basin, cross the gentle Taboo Glacier and climb a short snow couloir to gain the South Ridge. The route makes an ascending traverse of several hundred feet on ledges and chimneys on the ridge's left side (no harder than 5.4, sustained 4th/low-5th class, with real route-finding) toward a notch marked by a patch of bright yellow-orange lichen. From the notch, some of the route's most solid rock leads to the central section of the peak below the summit headwall, then easy ledge traversing across the southeast face and broken 4th/easy-5th climbing reach the summit.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road ~21.7-22.5 miles from Marblemount (SR 20) to the small Boston Basin trailhead pullout (elev. ~3,200 ft). Follow an old mine road roughly 1 mile, then a steep, eroded climber's path with several creek crossings up to Boston Basin (low camp ~5,300 ft, high camp ~6,400 ft), about 2.6 miles and ~3,000 ft of gain, 3-4 hours. From high camp continue up the Taboo Glacier and a short snow couloir to the base of the South Ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the South Ridge back to the notch, or descend the loose SE Face gully system.
routes.descentText: Two options are commonly used: (1) reverse the South Ridge, downclimbing and building rappel anchors on gear/slings where needed, or (2) descend the Southeast Face gully system directly to the Taboo Glacier (looser rock, but avoids re-climbing the ridge). Parties continuing on to the Torment-Forbidden Traverse instead rappel from the notch toward the Forbidden Glacier.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 1-night trip: hike to Boston Basin camp day one, climb and return day two. Strong parties do it car-to-car in a long day.
routes.bail: The ridge itself offers straightforward retreat at almost any point — downclimb or build a rappel anchor on gear/slings on any of the ledge systems back down toward the Taboo Glacier notch. Avoid downclimbing the loose central gully unroped.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before you start based on your team's pace, the forecast, and remaining daylight — the loose central gully and ledge traverses are considerably harder to downclimb safely in the dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with July-August the most reliable climbing window; going earlier keeps the entry couloir snow-covered and easier to climb, while very late season can leave it as bare, loose scree/talus.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in Boston Basin and on Torment is unreliable to nonexistent per the National Park Service; do not plan on calling out mid-route.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack: a small set of stoppers, a few cams up to ~2in, and long slings for the rappel/notch stations (some webbing needs replacing).
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly natural gear placements on ledges and in the notch/headwall cracks; a light rack suffices given the moderate grade.
gear: Light alpine rack (stoppers + a few cams to 2in)
ice axe & crampons for the entry snow couloir (early season)
whatToBring: Bug repellent/head net (biting flies common on the approach)
Bear canister for overnight camps (required in Boston Basin, Jun 1-Nov 15)
Map/GPS — route-finding is the main crux
proTips: Aim for the notch marked by a patch of bright yellow lichen when route-finding up from the Taboo Glacier.
Many parties pair the South Ridge ascent with a Southeast Face descent to avoid reversing the ridge.
Climb earlier in the season while the entry couloir still holds snow; by late summer it can be bare, loose scree.
watchOut: The gully/notch section is very crumbly, rockfall-prone rock — parties are advised not to unrope here despite the easy grade.
Route-finding is non-trivial; several sub-parallel ledge systems can lead climbers astray.
Biting flies can be intense in Boston Basin in early-mid summer.
knownHazards: Loose, crumbly rock in the gully below the upper notch creates rockfall hazard for parties below.
Boston Basin has documented bear activity; food storage in an approved canister is required June 1-Nov 15.
Rapid weather changes and afternoon thunderstorms are common in this alpine zone.

pitchDetail.notes: From high camp, cross the Taboo Glacier and climb a short snow couloir to gain the South Ridge.
Ascending traverse of several hundred feet on the ridge's left side via ledges and chimneys — sustained 4th class/low 5th — toward the notch marked by yellow lichen.
From the notch, climb some of the route's most solid rock to the central section below the summit headwall.
Easy traverse on grassy ledges across the southeast face, then broken 4th and easy 5th-class climbing to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal gravel road; paved to mile 10, then gravel, with a narrow high-clearance-recommended section past Eldorado (mile 20)
road.seasonalGate: Gated in winter/spring; typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June or early July, later in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: Boston Basin trailhead pullout is at roughly mile 21.7-22.5, elevation ~3,200 ft; small pullout opposite Johannesburg Mountain, room for a handful of cars.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Boston Basin-Forbidden Peak area (check NWS Seattle mountain point forecast or mountain-forecast.com)
climate.typical: Cool alpine climate; the access road typically opens late June-early July and the range holds snow into early summer.

bySeason.spring: Road and approach often still snow-covered below high camp into June.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window July-September; conditions are most favorable earlier in summer.
bySeason.fall: Cooler temperatures, fewer crowds, possible early-season snow; road usually stays open into September-October.
bySeason.winter: Road gated, deep snowpack — not a winter objective.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS) / Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass may apply for parking; NPS backcountry permit fee of $10/person plus a $6 reservation fee for overnight stays.
access.permit: A backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay in Boston Basin; obtain via recreation.gov reservation or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (parking) and NPS backcountry permit (overnight camping)
access.closures: Camping restricted to designated low camp (~5,300 ft) and high camp (~6,400 ft); approved bear canister required June 1-November 15 in Boston Basin.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start from high camp (or trailhead if doing it car-to-car)

sectionBreakdown.section: Trailhead to Boston Basin high camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead (3,200 ft) to high camp (6,400 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~2.6 mi, ~3,000 ft gain on a steep, eroded climber's path

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: (360) 336-9450 (per NPS); 911 also works but may route to a distant center
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center — (360) 854-7245 (Marblemount / Sedro-Woolley)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111 (24-hr); PeaceHealth United General, Sedro-Woolley — (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable to nonexistent in this remote, rugged terrain per NPS; do not rely on it for rescue coordination. NPS and county Sheriff's Offices coordinate SAR, often with volunteer Mountain Rescue units.

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade 5.6 and 6 pitches. Mountain Project's South Ridge route page (fetched directly) lists YDS 5.4, Overall Grade III, and 4 pitches (1000 ft), with FA credited to Cooper & Sellers, 1958. A separate Beckey Cascade Alpine Guide-derived source describes the same route as Grade II, 5.4, in as many as 8 pitches depending on how parties rope it up — pitch count clearly varies a lot by source/party technique, but 5.4 (not 5.6) is corroborated by two independent sources, so rockGrade was corrected to 5.4.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper & Walt Sellers, July 1958. Cooper first scouted the line in May 1958 while climbing Forbidden's East Ridge Direct with Fred Beckey, Joe Hieb, and Don Gordon, then returned with Sellers that July to complete the traverse.
routes.face: Torment's South Ridge -> Forbidden Glacier crossing -> West Ridge of Forbidden Peak
routes.season: Jul-early Sep; conditions are best done earlier in summer, before the Forbidden Glacier's bergschrund and moats open up
routes.rappels: Roughly 7 rappels down the Cat Scratch Gullies (standard West Ridge of Forbidden descent), plus an overhanging rappel off Torment's notch onto the Forbidden Glacier and additional glacier-crossing rappels as bergschrund/moat conditions require.
objHaz: Crevasses, bergschrunds, and moats on the Forbidden Glacier crossing vary greatly by season and can require technical, exposed rappels and traverses.
Steep snow/ice sections — up to roughly 50 degrees in some years — on the glacier traverse demand solid axe/crampon technique.
Rockfall risk on both Torment's south side and in the Cat Scratch Gullies descent.
Bear activity is documented in Boston Basin; an approved canister is required June 1-November 15.
routes.overview: A committing, highly regarded traverse linking Mount Torment's South Ridge to Forbidden Peak's classic West Ridge across the Forbidden Glacier — one of the most sought-after moderate alpine outings in the North Cascades.
routes.beta: From Boston Basin, ascend Torment's South Ridge or SE Face (loose 4th/low-5th class) to a grassy ledge and V-shaped notch below Torment's summit; tag the summit and downclimb back to the notch. An overhanging rappel on fixed slings drops onto the Forbidden Glacier, which is crossed — negotiating bergschrunds, moats, and snow bridges that vary greatly by season — to a saddle below Forbidden. From there the exposed 'Sidewalk in the Sky' ridge crest is followed, bypassing two gendarmes, to the West Ridge Notch, where the route joins Forbidden Peak's classic West Ridge (a Fifty Classic Climbs route): 4th-class scrambling with a crux 5.6 tower pitch to the summit. Descent is via the West Ridge and the Cat Scratch Gullies.
routes.approach: Same Boston Basin approach as the South Ridge: Cascade River Road to the trailhead (~3,200 ft), then ~2.6 miles and ~3,000 ft of gain on a steep climber's path to Boston Basin camp (2-3 hours), then roughly another hour over talus and slabs to the base of Torment's South Ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel Forbidden's West Ridge, then descend roughly 7 rappels down the Cat Scratch Gullies to snowfields below the South Face and back to Boston Basin.
routes.descentText: From Forbidden's summit, downclimb the West Ridge (typically with one rappel) then descend the Cat Scratch Gullies — reported as about 7 rappels with serviceable but aging fixed anchors — to snowfields below the South Face, then continue down snow/trail back to Boston Basin. NPS notes this descent approach is normally snow-free by late summer, so crampons/axe are often unnecessary for it specifically (though needed for the glacier crossing earlier in the route).
routes.itinerary: Most parties take 2-3 days: hike in and camp in Boston Basin, climb Torment and bivy on the connecting ridge before Forbidden, then finish and hike out the next day. Strong, fit teams complete it car-to-car in a single very long day — one reported single-push time was about 23 hours (2:30am to 1:15am).
routes.bail: Retreat options shrink once committed to the glacier crossing and ridge traverse — the route is considered a serious commitment with limited easy bail points between Torment's notch and the West Ridge Notch. The most realistic bail is back over Torment's South Ridge/SE Face before starting the glacier crossing; once on the connecting ridge, continuing to Forbidden and descending the Cat Scratch Gullies is usually faster than reversing.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting based on your team's pace and forecast — the glacier crossing, ridge traverse, and Cat Scratch Gullies rappels are all significantly more hazardous in the dark, in bad weather, or when fatigued; many completions happen well into the night, so a strong headlamp and margin for error are essential.
routes.bestSeason: July through early September; going earlier in the season is favored because the Forbidden Glacier's bergschrund and moats become progressively more difficult and hazardous to cross as summer progresses.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in Boston Basin and across the traverse is unreliable to nonexistent per the National Park Service; a satellite communicator is a reasonable backup given the route's remoteness and commitment.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack suffices: a single set of stoppers/offset nuts, cams to about 2in, several double-length slings for the notch/glacier and Cat Scratch rappels, 1-2 snow pickets and/or an ice screw for the glacier crossing (conditions-dependent), and a 60m rope.
routes.proNeeds: Protection mixes trad rock gear on Torment's ridge and Forbidden's West Ridge with snow/glacier protection (pickets, occasionally a screw) for the connecting glacier traverse; existing rappel-station webbing at the notch and in the Cat Scratch Gullies should be backed up or replaced if worn.
gear: ice axe & crampons (light/aluminum acceptable most of the season)
whatToBring: Bivy gear if not going car-to-car (a ridge bivy is common)
Bear canister for the Boston Basin camp
Headlamp — many parties finish in the dark
Extra food/water — long day with limited water on the ridge
Sun and wind protection for the exposed ridge crossing
proTips: Most parties plan 2-3 days with a bivy on the connecting ridge; very strong teams do it car-to-car in one long day (roughly 20-24 hours reported).
Climb it earlier in the summer — by late season the Forbidden Glacier bergschrund/moats open up and become notably harder and more hazardous to cross.
The Cat Scratch Gullies rappel anchors are serviceable but aging; carry extra cord/webbing to back them up.
watchOut: A fatal accident occurred on the upper glacier bergschrund during a 2009 traverse attempt — the glacier crossing is the route's most serious objective hazard.
The waterfall slabs below the connecting glacier have caused fatalities and should be avoided.
Loose rock is present throughout, especially on Torment's south side and in the Cat Scratch Gullies descent.
knownHazards: Crevasses, bergschrunds, and moats on the Forbidden Glacier crossing vary greatly by season and can require technical, exposed rappels and traverses.
Steep snow/ice sections — up to roughly 50 degrees in some years — on the glacier traverse demand solid axe/crampon technique.
Rockfall risk on both Torment's south side and in the Cat Scratch Gullies descent.
Bear activity is documented in Boston Basin; an approved canister is required June 1-November 15.

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend Torment's South Ridge (or SE Face) on loose 4th/low-5th terrain to a grassy ledge and V-shaped notch below the summit; leave packs, tag the summit, and downclimb back to the notch.
An overhanging rappel on fixed slings from the notch drops onto the Forbidden Glacier.
Cross the Forbidden Glacier past bergschrunds, moats, and snow bridges to a saddle below Forbidden's north side; the route's most serious and season-dependent hazard.
Follow the exposed 'Sidewalk in the Sky' ridge crest, bypassing two gendarmes (first on the left, second on the right), to the West Ridge Notch.
Join Forbidden Peak's classic West Ridge (Fifty Classic Climbs route): 4th-class scrambling with a crux 5.6 tower pitch near the summit.
Descend the West Ridge (partial downclimb, ~1 rappel) then roughly 7 rappels down the Cat Scratch Gullies back toward Boston Basin.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal gravel road; paved to mile 10, then gravel, with a narrow high-clearance-recommended section past Eldorado (mile 20)
road.seasonalGate: Gated in winter/spring; typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June or early July, later in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: Boston Basin trailhead pullout is at roughly mile 21.7-22.5, elevation ~3,200 ft.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Boston Basin-Forbidden Peak area (check NWS Seattle mountain point forecast or mountain-forecast.com)
climate.typical: Cool alpine climate; the access road typically opens late June-early July, and glacier/snow conditions on the traverse change significantly through the season.

bySeason.spring: Road and approach often still snow-covered below high camp into June; route not in condition.
bySeason.summer: Prime window July-early September; earlier is favored for glacier conditions.
bySeason.fall: Bergschrunds/moats most open and difficult; cooler temps and possible early snow; road usually open into September-October.
bySeason.winter: Road gated, deep snowpack — not a winter objective.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS) / Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass may apply for parking; NPS backcountry permit fee of $10/person plus a $6 reservation fee for overnight stays.
access.permit: A backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay in Boston Basin or on the connecting ridge bivy; obtain via recreation.gov reservation or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (parking) and NPS backcountry permit (overnight camping/bivy)
access.closures: Camping restricted to designated sites in Boston Basin (low camp ~5,300 ft, high camp ~6,400 ft); approved bear canister required June 1-November 15.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start (~2:30am reported) whether going car-to-car or from a ridge bivy

sectionBreakdown.note: steep climber's path, ~2.6 mi

sectionBreakdown.section: Basin to Torment's South Ridge notch
sectionBreakdown.note: talus and slabs

sectionBreakdown.section: Torment summit, glacier crossing, and ridge traverse
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: South Ridge notch to West Ridge Notch (Forbidden)
sectionBreakdown.note: highly variable with glacier/snow conditions; often includes a ridge bivy

sectionBreakdown.section: West Ridge of Forbidden to summit and Cat Scratch Gullies descent
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: West Ridge Notch to Forbidden summit to Boston Basin
sectionBreakdown.note: ~7 rappels down Cat Scratch Gullies

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: (360) 336-9450 (per NPS); 911 also works but may route to a distant center
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center — (360) 854-7245 (Marblemount / Sedro-Woolley)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111 (24-hr); PeaceHealth United General, Sedro-Woolley — (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable to nonexistent in this remote, rugged terrain per NPS; do not rely on it for rescue coordination. NPS and county Sheriff's Offices coordinate SAR, often with volunteer Mountain Rescue units.

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade 5.7. Mountain Project's route page (fetched directly, YDS 5.6) and multiple independent sources describing Forbidden's West Ridge crux tower pitch (Wikipedia, climberkyle.com, Mountaineers) consistently cite 5.6 for that pitch — corrected to 5.6, though a couple of secondary trip-report summaries do mention 5.7/5.8 variations. Commitment grade is given as IV-V reflecting a real split between sources (Mountain Project cites Alpine Grade IV; at least one detailed trip report cites Grade V) rather than a single confirmed value. Pitches left null because the route is largely simulclimbed/scrambled outside the discrete 4-pitch West Ridge finish, and no source gives one authoritative total pitch count for the full traverse.

blurb: The Triad is a rugged, three-summited peak in the North Cascades of Skagit County, Washington, on a southwest ridge extension of Eldorado Peak within North Cascades National Park. Composed of granodioritic orthogneiss and rarely climbed compared to its more famous neighbor, it was first ascended in 1949 by Dick Eilertsen, Dick Lowery, Dick Scales, and Don Wilde, who jokingly named it "Three Dicks" for their shared first names before the more formal "Triad" stuck.

routes.overview: A long cross-country approach over Hidden Lake Trail and the heather ridge above Hidden Lake Basin leads to a small glacier and a loose gully descent, followed by a scramble up The Triad's East Ridge to a short, exposed low-5th-class pinnacle just below the summit.
routes.beta: From the Hidden Lake trailhead, hikers follow the maintained trail toward Hidden Lake Lookout, then leave the trail near the pass to traverse east across a broad, heather-covered basin/ridge with views of Baker, Shuksan, and the Pickets. The route descends the north side of the ridge to cross a small glacier (crampons/axe often needed on firm snow or bare ice), then drops through a steep, loose gully (nicknamed the "Drainage of Despair" by at least one party) to a high saddle that marks the start of the East Ridge proper. From the saddle, parties gain the ridge crest via ramps and a slabby switchback section, then face a short, exposed but well-featured low-5th-class (5.4) rock pinnacle guarding the final approach to the true summit; most parties rope up for this section.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road roughly 10.2 miles from Marblemount, then turn onto Forest Service Road 1540 (Sibley Creek/Hidden Lake Road) and continue about 4.5 miles to the small Hidden Lake trailhead (no formal lot). From the trailhead, follow Hidden Lake Trail #745 toward the pass below Hidden Lake Lookout, then branch east off-trail into Hidden Lake Basin and up onto the ridge connecting toward The Triad's east and middle peaks.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: rappel the low-5th-class pinnacle, downclimb/scramble the East Ridge back to the high saddle, re-ascend the loose gully, re-cross the glacier, and retrace the heather ridge and trail back to the Hidden Lake trailhead.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend by reversing the route of ascent. From the true summit, downclimb to the top of the low-5th-class pinnacle and rappel it (a single rope length is generally sufficient given the short pitch). Continue down the East Ridge via the same ramps/switchback slabs used on the way up to the high saddle, then re-climb the loose "Drainage of Despair" gully back up to the ridge crest. Re-cross the small glacier (crampons recommended if snow/ice is firm) and follow the heather ridge and Hidden Lake Trail back to the trailhead. Total car-to-car time is commonly reported around 10-11 hours.
routes.bail: The route is a simple out-and-back along a single ridge/gully line, so retreat at any point (short of the pinnacle) is straightforward reversal of the ascent; from the pinnacle itself, a rappel is the standard way off if conditions or time force a retreat before the summit.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, glacier crossing, and loose terrain on the "Drainage of Despair," set a firm turnaround time based on daylight and party pace so the glacier and gully sections aren't downclimbed in the dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September, once seasonal snow has cleared enough for the Cascade River Road/FR 1540 approach and the ridge terrain to be mostly dry; the small glacier can still hold firm snow or bare ice into summer, so crampons and a light axe/tool are commonly carried even in the core season.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to nonexistent along Cascade River Road within a few miles of Marblemount and is not to be relied upon once on the mountain; parties should plan for no reception on the approach, glacier, or ridge.
gear: Light ice axe or ice tool
whatToBring: Navigation (map/compass or GPS) for the off-trail basin and ridge sections
Extra layers for exposed, wind-affected ridge terrain
Sufficient water/food for a long car-to-car day (approx. 10+ hours)
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack is sufficient for the short low-5th-class pinnacle: a handful of cams and nuts plus slings for the brief technical section, one rope, harness, and helmet. Aluminum crampons and a light ice axe/tool are commonly carried for the small glacier crossing when it holds firm snow or bare ice.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal and confined to the short 5.4 pinnacle near the summit; the rest of the route is class 2-4 scrambling on ridge, ramps, and slabs.
proTips: Rope up for the low-5th-class pinnacle even though the holds are good, given the exposure below it.
Expect a long, mostly off-trail day; budget extra time for route-finding through Hidden Lake Basin and along the ridge.
Aluminum crampons and a light axe are worth carrying even in summer if the small glacier still holds firm snow or bare ice.
watchOut: The steep, loose gully below the East Ridge saddle (nicknamed the "Drainage of Despair" in at least one trip report) is a notable loose-rock hazard on both ascent and descent.
The small glacier crossing can have firm snow or bare ice that requires crampons and an axe/tool rather than just trekking poles.
Bears have been reported in the basin/ridge area; standard backcountry food storage practices apply.
knownHazards: Loose rock and dirt on the steep gully descent/re-ascent between the ridge and the East Ridge saddle.
Firm snow or bare ice on the small glacier traverse, especially early or late season.
Exposure on the low-5th-class pinnacle below the true summit.

pitchDetail.notes: Short, low-5th-class rock step with good holds but real exposure; most parties rope up and rappel it on descent.

road.name: Cascade River Road to Forest Service Road 1540 (Sibley Creek/Hidden Lake Road)
road.status: FR 1540 has been reported washed out around mile 2.4-2.6; confirm current status with the Mt. Baker Ranger District before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Snow typically blocks FR 1540 until mid-summer; conditions improve later in the season.
road.driveNote: Cascade River Road is paved then gravel; FR 1540 is steep, rocky, rutted, narrow, and switchbacked with no formal trailhead parking lot — high-clearance/AWD strongly recommended.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skagit County alpine zone
climate.typical: Typical North Cascades alpine climate: wet, snow-heavy winters and springs, with a relatively short dry-ish window in summer that offers the most reliable climbing conditions.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists on the approach and ridge; road access to the trailhead is often still snow-blocked.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jun-Sep); trail and ridge are typically mostly snow-free, though the small glacier can still hold firm snow/ice.
bySeason.fall: Generally stable but shortening days and possible early-season snow; road and trail conditions can deteriorate after storms.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible for climbing; heavy snowpack and avalanche hazard on the approach and road.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (peak/upper mountain) and Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker Ranger District (trailhead/lower approach)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at the Hidden Lake trailhead.
access.permit: No permit required for a day climb; a National Park Service backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay, obtained in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (or via limited advance reservation on Recreation.gov).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: FR 1540 has been reported washed out near mile 2.4-2.6 in recent seasons; verify current road status with the Mt. Baker Ranger District before driving.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead to Hidden Lake Basin ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Main trail for ~1 hour, then ~15 minutes to the saddle starting the ridge traverse.
Scenic ridge walk with views of Baker, Shuksan, and the Pickets.

sectionBreakdown.section: Glacier and gully to East Ridge saddle
sectionBreakdown.note: Includes glacier crossing and descent/traverse of the loose gully ("Drainage of Despair"); saddle reached ~4.5 hrs from car in one trip report.
Ramps and slabby switchbacks to the low-5th-class pinnacle, then a short technical section to the top; summit reached ~6 hrs from car in one trip report.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit 911 (emergency: 911; non-emergency: 360-336-3131) dispatches Skagit County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue / Skagit Mountain Rescue Unit for backcountry incidents.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — 360-854-7245 (climbing/backcountry) or 360-854-7200.
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (closest ER on the drive route); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA is a larger regional option farther down-valley.
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable to absent along Cascade River Road within a few miles of Marblemount and on the mountain itself; do not rely on a cell signal for emergency communication from the route.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt for the peak (7,570 ft) does not match reputable sources: Wikipedia and PeakVisor list The Triad's East Peak at 7,520+ ft (coordinates 48.5195, -121.1630), and ListsOfJohn lists the peak's highpoint at 7,540 ft. Used 7,520 ft (sourced specifically to the East Peak) for highPointFt. The 1949 first-ascent party (Eilertsen, Lowery, Scales, Wilde) is documented for "The Triad" as a whole, but no source specifies which of the three summits (east/middle/west) or which line they climbed, so 'fa' was left null for this East Peak route rather than assumed.

blurb: Cascade Peak is a 7,428-foot summit in the North Cascades of Skagit County, Washington, rising above Cascade Pass on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and the Glacier Peak Wilderness, wedged between Johannesburg Mountain and The Triplets. It is built largely of volcanic breccia and still carries small glacier remnants on its north side, and it sees far less traffic than famous neighbors like Eldorado, Forbidden, or Johannesburg. Fred Beckey, Pete Schoening, and Phil Sharpe made the first recorded ascent on July 23, 1950, via what sources describe only as glacier travel and Class 3-4 scrambling.

routes.overview: The East Ridge is the standard, easiest line up Cascade Peak — a lightly-visited summit above Cascade Pass involving Class 3-4 scrambling with some glacier travel on the approach.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Pete Schoening, Phil Sharpe — July 23, 1950 (documented as the peak's first ascent; sources describe the line only as "glacier travel, Class 3-4 scrambling" and do not explicitly name it "East Ridge," but it is the peak's sole well-documented route and matches this route's stated grade)
objHaz: crevasse hazard where the approach crosses glacier remnants
steep snow/scree on approach gullies early season
routes.approach: Hike the Cascade Pass Trail (3.7 mi one-way, ~1,700 ft gain) from the Cascade River Road trailhead to Cascade Pass. From the pass, the standard climbers' corridor into this basin continues off-trail across Mixup Arm and the Cache Glacier basin toward Cache Col and The Triplets; Cascade Peak's east side is reached from this basin. Published route-finding beta specific to gaining the East Ridge itself is sparse online — confirm current conditions/line with a recent trip report before attempting.
routes.bestSeason: Snow typically keeps Cascade River Road and the Cascade Pass Trail closed into June; the road generally reopens to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June. July-August is the driest, most consolidated window; by September early-season snow can return at this elevation.
routes.comms: No cell coverage along Cascade River Road or in the Cascade Pass backcountry; carry a satellite communicator/PLB.
gear: crevasse-rescue kit if crossing the glacier remnants
watchOut: This is a lightly-traveled peak with sparse published beta for the East Ridge specifically — carry a map/altimeter and be ready to route-find.
Breccia rock is loose and prone to handhold/rockfall failure.
Small glacier remnants persist on the peak's north side; assess crevasse hazard before crossing snow/ice.
knownHazards: Glacier remnants on the north side of the peak present crevasse hazard on approach.
Loose volcanic breccia composition increases rockfall risk.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved to mile 10, then rough gravel; final ~3 miles to the Cascade Pass trailhead are narrow and steep with limited passing room
road.seasonalGate: Winter closure gate at milepost 18 (park boundary); typically reopens to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June, weather/damage permitting
road.driveNote: High-clearance recommended beyond MP20; vehicles over 22 ft or towing trailers are prohibited; check the NPS road-conditions page before driving, as storm damage has caused extended closures in recent years

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy winter snowfall; wetter and cooler than the east-slope Cascades

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June; road and trail typically still closed or snow-covered
bySeason.summer: July-August is the primary climbing window, driest and most stable
bySeason.fall: September can be clear, but early snow is possible by October
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow, road gated, not a winter objective

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; no day-use parking fee at the Cascade Pass trailhead (inside the park boundary)
access.permit: Free backcountry/climbing permit required for any overnight stay, including bivies; reserve via recreation.gov or obtain first-come, first-served at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.passRequired: None for day use at Cascade Pass TH; a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass is only needed at nearby USFS-managed trailheads
access.closures: Cascade River Road has a seasonal gate at MP18; also subject to unscheduled closures for storm/flood damage — check the NPS road-conditions page before driving

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); (360) 416-1911 (Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA — (360) 424-4111 (roughly 1.5-2 hr drive from the trailhead)
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Cascade River Road corridor or the backcountry approach/summit; carry a satellite messenger for emergencies

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,415 ft) differs from multiple reputable sources (Wikipedia, PeakVisor), which both list 7,428 ft — 7,428 ft appears to be the better-supported figure. The route 'fa' field reflects the peak's documented first ascent (Beckey/Schoening/Sharpe, 7/23/1950); sources describe that line only as "glacier travel, Class 3-4 scrambling" and do not use the name "East Ridge," though no other route on this peak is documented, so it is very likely the same line.

blurb: Mix-up Peak (7,440 ft) is a granite/gneiss wedge in the Cascade Pass area of North Cascades National Park, rising nearly 4,000 feet above Cascade Pass at the northern end of the Ptarmigan Traverse. Its broad East Face — often called the "white staircase" — gives popular, moderate class 3-4 scrambling on solid rock with a short mid-5th-class finish, though it carries a serious rockfall reputation despite the easy grade. Wesley Grande and Jack Kendrick made the first recorded ascent of the peak in 1947.

routes.rappels: 5 rappels back to Gunsight Notch, 60 m rope sufficient; includes one overhanging double-rope rappel; check/back up old sling anchors before trusting them.
routes.overview: A moderate, popular alpine rock route up Mix-up Peak's broad East Face: a roped glacier/snowfield approach to Gunsight Notch, two short roped pitches (up to mid-5th class) through a loose gully, then class 3-4 scrambling up a wide, solid-rock 'staircase' to a short, exposed finish on the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From Cascade Pass, contour south up Mixup Arm and across a roughly 6,000 ft bench to a small glacier/snowfield below Gunsight Notch; rope up to cross it. From the notch's south side, two roped pitches (a loose gully, then a short chimney/scramble) gain a shoulder below the main summit pyramid. From there, easy class 3-4 scrambling up the light-gray 'white staircase' leads to a short mid-5th-class step through a notch in the exposed summit ridge, then a short class 3-4 ridge walk to the true summit. Descend the same line via 5 rappels.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road 23.1 mi from Marblemount (paved to about mile 10, then gravel/washboard, narrow with drop-offs in the final miles) to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~3,600 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 mi with about 1,700 ft of gain to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). From the pass, go south/right up Mixup Arm, follow a bench at roughly 6,000 ft southeast to a small glacier/snowfield below the ridge, rope up, and cross to the right-hand (Gunsight) notch in the ridge southeast of Mix-up Peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: 5 rappels (60 m rope) from near the summit back down to Gunsight Notch, then re-cross the glacier/snowfield and Mixup Arm bench back to Cascade Pass and the trail.
routes.descentText: From a small ledge on the east side just below the summit, make a 50 m rappel. From the bottom of the shoulder, a single 60 m rappel reaches a belay station atop the second climbing pitch; from there an overhanging double rappel drops directly to just below Gunsight Notch — 5 rappels total gets you back to the notch, and a 60 m rope is adequate throughout. Rappel-station webbing is old in places, so inspect and back it up. From Gunsight Notch, re-cross the glacier/snowfield and retrace the Mixup Arm bench back to Cascade Pass.
routes.itinerary: Most parties climb it car-to-car in one very long day (reported times range roughly 12-19+ hours depending on party size/conditions) with a car camp at the trailhead the night before; some split it into two days with a backcountry camp (permit required) to shorten the summit day.
routes.bail: Retreat via the same 5-rappel line back to Gunsight Notch — the existing (aging) rap stations are the standard bail points; from the notch, re-cross the glacier/snowfield back to the Mixup Arm bench and Cascade Pass Trail.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent depends on 5 rappels down a loose gully and a roped glacier/snowfield crossing, keep enough margin to finish the technical descent and re-cross the glacier before afternoon warmth softens snow and raises rockfall risk; treat falling behind schedule as a cue to retreat rather than push for the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, once the Cascade River Road and Cascade Pass Trail are snow-free (the road usually reopens by late June, occasionally not until early July in heavy-snow years). Earlier in the season the glacier/snowfield below Gunsight Notch is more continuous but avalanche and snow-bridge conditions need more care; later in the season a moat can open between snow and rock in the approach gully, and the glacier becomes more broken.
routes.detailedRack: A small trad rack suffices for the two roped pitches — a light set of nuts and a few cams (finger to hand size) plus slings; the low technical grade means many parties link sections with running protection rather than full pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Light protection needs given the easy-to-mid 5th-class grade; the crux is more about loose, exposed terrain than sustained hard climbing. Bring extra webbing/cord to back up old rappel-station anchors.
whatToBring: approach shoes or light boots for the scramble sections
extra rappel webbing/cord to back up old anchors
sun protection for the long glacier/snow approach
headlamp for an alpine start/late finish
proTips: Rope up before crossing the glacier/snowfield below Gunsight Notch — crevasses and a bergschrund are present even in mid/late summer.
Carry a 60 m rope; the standard descent is 5 rappels including one overhanging double rappel.
Inspect and back up the existing rappel-station webbing, which is old in places.
Expect a long day — reported car-to-car times vary widely (one reported trip took 19 hours car-to-car), so start early.
watchOut: Rockfall is a serious, well-documented hazard on both the ascent and descent gullies despite the moderate technical grade.
The snow tongue leading to Gunsight Notch can be steep (roughly 50-65 degrees over a short section) with bergschrunds on either side.
A moat can open between snow and rock in the approach gully later in the season.
The final summit-ridge section is narrow and exposed even though it's only class 3-4.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier/snowfield crossing below Gunsight Notch requiring roped travel.
Loose rock in the gully below Gunsight Notch and at the start of the technical pitches.
Rockfall exposure on both the approach and descent lines.
Steep (50-65°) snow tongue with bergschrunds just below Gunsight Notch.
Exposed, narrow summit ridge.

pitchDetail.notes: From the south side of Gunsight Notch, descend ~100 ft west down a side gully, then climb a wide, loose gully northwest; pitch ends just above a chimney.
Short pitch/scramble up the chimney to a flat shoulder below the main summit pyramid.
Traverse the shoulder and ascend the broad, gray-colored 'white staircase' on the right-hand side of the summit pyramid — easy, solid-rock scrambling with big exposure.
Route steepens just below the summit ridge; a short mid-5th-class step gains a notch in the exposed summit ridge.
Follow the ridge south a short distance, exposed in spots, to the true summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open to the Cascade Pass Trailhead as of NPS's most recent 2026 update; paved to about mile 10, then gravel/washboard beyond, narrow with drop-offs in the final ~3 miles.
road.seasonalGate: Winter closure near Eldorado (~milepost 20); typically reopens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June, occasionally not until early July in heavy-snow years.
road.driveNote: 23.1 mi from SR 20 in Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead; no turnaround areas until the road's end, vehicles over 22 ft long/8 ft wide prohibited past milepost 18, high-clearance recommended for the gravel sections.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades); the area also falls within NWAC's 'West Slopes North' avalanche-forecast region during winter/spring.
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy orographic precipitation, especially winter snowfall on west-facing slopes; summers are comparatively dry and are the main climbing window.

bySeason.spring: Deep, lingering snowpack; avalanche danger and unstable snow bridges on the glacier approach.
bySeason.summer: Driest, most stable period; the standard July-September climbing window.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows; early snow can arrive by October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall; Cascade River Road is gated and the approach is effectively inaccessible.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No entrance fee for North Cascades National Park; day climbing requires no fee.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required only for overnight stays ($10/person plus a $6 reservation fee; ages 15 and under free); Cascade Pass-area permits must be picked up in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: None confirmed for day-use parking at the Cascade Pass Trailhead, which lies inside the park boundary; bring photo ID if picking up an overnight permit.
access.closures: Cascade River Road has a seasonal winter closure near Eldorado (~MP 20), and occasional washout-related closures have occurred in past years; check current NPS road conditions before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start recommended given the long roped approach and 5-rappel descent; one reported party started at 5:40 AM.

sectionBreakdown.note: 3.7 mi, ~1,700 ft gain via the Cascade Pass Trail (NPS).
Up Mixup Arm, across a bench at ~6,000 ft, then a roped glacier/snowfield crossing to Gunsight Notch.
2 roped pitches (to mid-5th) plus class 3-4 scrambling up the 'white staircase.'
The Mountaineers' route page estimates 7-9 hrs trailhead-to-summit; midpoint used here.
One CascadeClimbers trip report (party of 4, left trailhead 5:40 AM) logged 19 hrs car-to-car; highly conditions/pace dependent.

emergency.county: Skagit County (the peak sits on the Skagit/Chelan county line)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 428-3211; dial 911 for emergencies.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245; park emergency line (360) 856-5700 ext. 515 (business hours) or (360) 873-4590 ext. 37 (after hours).
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III trauma center) — (360) 424-4111; Cascade Valley Hospital (formerly United General), Sedro-Woolley — (360) 856-6021.

routes.corrections: Route length (1,000 ft over 2 pitches, Mountain Project) is consistent with the given pitch count. The input's rockGrade of '5.0' is described by multiple sources as 'low-to-mid 5th class,' so a '5.0-5.4' range is used instead of a single grade. Approach/elevation-gain of ~3,900 ft and a high point of ~7,400 ft come from The Mountaineers' route stat block; the more precise summit elevation (7,440 ft, per Wikipedia/USGS-derived data and Mountain Project GPS) is used for highPointFt/gainFt reference. A specific first-ascent party for the East Face route itself was not found (Mountain Project lists it as unknown); the peak's overall first ascent (Wesley Grande and Jack Kendrick, 1947) is documented but not confirmed to be this exact line, so route 'fa' is left null.

blurb: Magic Mountain (7,610 ft) rises just southeast of Cascade Pass on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and Glacier Peak Wilderness, anchoring the north end of the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. The peak is built of Magic Mountain Gneiss and carries small glaciers (the Yawning and Cache Col glaciers) on its northern flank. It was first climbed in July 1938 by Calder Bressler, Ralph (Ray) Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers, the Ptarmigan Climbing Club party that pioneered the original Ptarmigan Traverse.

routes.fa: Calder Bressler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, Tom Myers, July 1938 (documented as the peak's first ascent via its easiest scrambling/glacier line, made during the first Ptarmigan Traverse; not explicitly confirmed in sources as this exact modern South Ridge line)
objHaz: rockfall / loose rock on summit ledges
glacier travel and moderate-angle snow (Cache Glacier)
exposed class 3-4 scrambling near the false summit
route-finding difficulty in fresh snow or whiteout
routes.face: South Ridge (via the false summit and east-side summit ledges)
routes.overview: A Class 3, briefly Class 4, scramble up Magic Mountain's south ridge, reached from Cascade Pass over the Cache Glacier and Cache Col to Kool-Aid Lake at the north end of the Ptarmigan Traverse; the crux is a short, exposed rock/ledge traverse past a false summit to the true summit.
routes.beta: From Kool-Aid Lake, descend about 600 ft on the Ptarmigan Traverse path, then traverse and climb to a 7,100 ft col east of the lake. From that col, ascend the South Ridge on heather and rock (Class 3) toward a false summit roughly 50 ft below and southeast of the true summit. Drop off the false summit and cross broken, exposed Class 3-4 ledges/gully on the east side of the summit tower to reach the true summit; fresh snow can force a detour into the summit moat.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive the Cascade River Road 23.1 miles (paved for roughly the first 10 miles, then gravel and increasingly steep/narrow) to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~3,600 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail (~3.5-3.7 mi) up switchbacks to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). From the pass, follow a climber's trail bearing SSE on a rising traverse of snow/heather across Mixup Peak's east flank to the base of the Cache Glacier (~6,000 ft), crossing a spur ridge en route, then ascend moderate snow (up to about 35°) to Cache Col (~6,900-6,960 ft). Cross the col and make a descending traverse SSE to Kool-Aid Lake (6,120 ft), the standard camp for the climb.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: back across the false summit and the 7,100 ft col, over Cache Col, and down the climber's trail to Cascade Pass and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Down-climb the same broken ledges/gully back to the false summit, then retrace the South Ridge to the 7,100 ft col and back up/over toward Cache Col. Re-cross the moderate snow slopes below Cache Col and descend the climber's traverse below Mixup Peak's east flank back to Cascade Pass, then follow the maintained trail down to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 2-day trip: Day 1 hike in to Kool-Aid Lake (or Cache Col area) and camp; Day 2 climb Magic Mountain and hike out. Strong parties have also done it car-to-car in a long single day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the route at any point; there are no fixed rappels or technical anchors on the standard line, so descend the same terrain you climbed back to Cache Col and Cascade Pass.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before committing to the exposed ledges/gully past the false summit if the party lacks a comfortable time and weather margin to reverse the full traverse over Cache Col and back to Cascade Pass before dark or before incoming weather; fresh snow on the summit ledges or on the Cache Glacier traverse is a reasonable turnaround trigger.
routes.bestSeason: The Cascade River Road typically doesn't open to the Cascade Pass Trailhead until late June or early July, and the climb is generally done July through September when the Cache Glacier traverse and summit ledges are snow-free or firm. The road can close again with early fall storms.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in this part of North Cascades National Park is unreliable to nonexistent per NPS guidance; do not rely on a phone for emergency communication, and consider carrying a PLB or satellite messenger.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel kit (rope, harness, and crevasse-rescue gear) plus ice axe and crampons for the Cache Glacier crossing and any lingering snow on the approach; a helmet is recommended for rockfall on the ridge and summit ledges. A rack is not needed for the Class 3-4 scrambling, though a rope and a few slings/light pieces can back up the exposed summit ledges for less experienced parties.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection exists on the route; it is typically climbed unroped as a scramble. A rope with a small rack of slings/light gear can be used to protect the short exposed Class 3-4 section near the false summit if desired.
gear: light rack or slings (optional, for the summit ledges)
whatToBring: extra insulating layer for the exposed ridge and col
proTips: Expect a rising traverse on snow or heather across Mixup Peak's east flank before reaching the Cache Glacier; carry an axe even late in the season.
The false summit sits about 50 ft above and short of the true summit — don't mistake it for the top.
Kool-Aid Lake (6,120 ft) is the standard camp for a two-day ascent and also serves parties on the Ptarmigan Traverse.
watchOut: A short, exposed Class 3-4 gully/ledge system past the false summit guards the true summit; fresh snow can force an awkward detour into the moat.
Loose, blocky rock on the summit pyramid is a real hazard — a dislodged rock caused a serious fall and head injury here in a documented 1964 accident.
Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in this part of the park; carry a PLB/satellite device.
knownHazards: Glacier travel on the Cache Glacier crosses moderate-angle snow (up to roughly 35 degrees).
Loose, blocky rock on the summit ledges creates rockfall danger for climbers below.
Route-finding across the heather/snow traverse and summit ledges becomes difficult in whiteout or fresh-snow conditions.

pitchDetail.notes: Rising traverse on climber's trail/heather/snow across Mixup Peak's east flank to the Cache Glacier, then moderate snow to Cache Col (~6,900-6,960 ft).
Descending traverse SSE to Kool-Aid Lake (6,120 ft), the standard camp.
Ascend roughly 1,000 ft to the saddle between Magic Mountain and Hurry-Up Peak, then traverse/descend slightly to a 7,100 ft col east of Kool-Aid Lake.
Climb heather and rock on the South Ridge to a false summit roughly 50 ft below and southeast of the true summit.
Drop roughly 50-80 ft and cross exposed, broken ledges/gully on the east side of the summit block to reach the true summit; fresh snow can force a detour through the moat.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 10 miles from Marblemount, then gravel; narrow and steep in the final few miles
road.seasonalGate: Gated at milepost 20 (Eldorado) for winter; typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June or early July (sometimes not until early July after heavy snow)
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from SR-20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead; roughly an hour's drive. Trailers and vehicles over 22 ft long or 8 ft wide are prohibited beyond the park boundary at mile 18; oversized vehicles turn around at the Eldorado parking area (mile 20).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North avalanche zone; NWS zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced west-slope North Cascades climate with a heavy winter snowpack and a short, more stable summer weather window.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June on the approach and Cache Glacier; the access road is often still gated.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (July-September); Cascade River Road is typically open, though afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving weather are possible.
bySeason.fall: Freezing levels and snow depth drop quickly after September; the road can close for the season with early storms.
bySeason.winter: Road closed at Eldorado (mile 20); the peak becomes a ski-mountaineering/avalanche-terrain objective rather than a standard summer scramble.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS); the approach also touches Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest) near the summit boundary
access.fees: No entrance fee for North Cascades National Park. Overnight backcountry camping requires a permit costing $10/person (youth 15 and under free) plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee.
access.permit: NPS wilderness/backcountry permit required year-round for any overnight stay in the park; parties are limited to a maximum of 6 people.
access.passRequired: Not required at the NPS Cascade Pass Trailhead itself; a Northwest Forest Pass may be required at nearby USFS trailheads.
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated at Eldorado (mile 20) for winter and typically doesn't reopen to the Cascade Pass Trailhead until late June or early July.

timing.recommendedStart: Early or pre-dawn start for a car-to-car day; alternatively camp at Kool-Aid Lake for a two-day ascent

sectionBreakdown.note: 23.1 miles on Cascade River Road, paved then gravel; about an hour.
Maintained trail, roughly 3.5-3.7 miles.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass to Kool-Aid Lake via Cache Col
sectionBreakdown.note: Cross-country traverse and glacier crossing; standard camp.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Kool-Aid Lake to summit and return
sectionBreakdown.note: South Ridge scramble to the false summit, then exposed ledges to the true summit.

emergency.county: Skagit County (summit sits on the Skagit/Chelan county line)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office: (360) 336-9450 (after-hours dispatch for the park)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA; park communications center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37, business hours (360) 856-5700 ext. 515
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA, (360) 856-6021; Skagit Valley Hospital (24-hr, higher level of care), Mount Vernon, WA, (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell phone coverage in this part of the North Cascades is limited or nonexistent per NPS guidance; a Personal Locator Beacon or satellite communicator is recommended but does not replace proper training and experience.

routes.corrections: Most sources (Mountaineers, Peakware, trip reports) describe the standard line as Class 3 heather/rock with only a short Class 3-4 section (broken ledges/gully) past the false summit, rather than a sustained Class 4 route as the input grade implies — the given 'Class 4' rating is consistent with that crux section but should be understood as a brief crux, not the character of the whole climb. The listed FA (Bressler/Clough/Cox/Myers, 1938) is documented by Wikipedia as the peak's first ascent via its easiest scrambling/glacier line during the first Ptarmigan Traverse; it is highly likely but not explicitly confirmed in sources to be this specific modern-day South Ridge line.

blurb: Buckner Mountain (9,114 ft) rises in the Cascade Pass area of North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, straddling the Chelan-Skagit county line; it is the highest peak in Skagit County and the third-highest in the park. The mountain has near-twin summits (SW 9,114 ft, NE 9,112 ft) and its massive north-side Boston Glacier — one of the largest and best-known ice climbs in the range — feeds the technical North Face route, while the southwest side offers a much gentler snow-and-scramble line above Horseshoe Basin. Lewis Ryan made the first recorded ascent of the peak on August 1, 1901.

objHaz: Long, exposed falls on steep hard snow
routes.overview: Buckner's standard, least-technical line climbs steepening southwest-facing snowfields and rock bands above Horseshoe Basin to the summit ridge, using a distinctive cowskull-shaped snow patch as a landmark below the top.
routes.beta: From Sahale Glacier Camp, parties descend Sahale Peak's south ridge and traverse roughly 2 miles across Horseshoe Basin, passing the abandoned Davenport Mine (~7,000 ft), before angling up steepening snowfields on Buckner's SW face. The final approach threads a snow chute past the cowskull-shaped snow patch, then loose scree/rubble to Class 2-3 rock on the summit block. On firm/icy snow many parties favor the adjacent rock bands over the snow gully for 3rd/4th class scrambling instead.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road (FR 15) ~22.9 mi from Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~3,660 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass trail (~3.5 mi, +1,700 ft) to Cascade Pass, then continue up the Sahale Arm (~2.15 mi, +2,200 ft) to Sahale Glacier Camp (~7,600 ft), the standard base for the SW approach.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down the SW face/rock bands into Horseshoe Basin, then back over Sahale Arm to Cascade Pass.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the Class 2-3 summit block and retrace the ascent snow chute/rock bands to Horseshoe Basin. Recross the basin below Davenport Mine, regain Sahale Peak's south ridge, and descend to Sahale Glacier Camp before continuing down the Sahale Arm and Cascade Pass trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: drive-in and hike to Sahale Glacier Camp. Day 2: pre-dawn start, traverse Horseshoe Basin, climb the SW face/slopes to the summit and return to camp (trip reports log roughly 7 hours each way for the climbing day). Day 3: hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally a straightforward downclimb of the ascent line back into Horseshoe Basin; on icy snow, favor the rock bands over the open snow gully.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets the party off the exposed SW snowfields and back to camp before afternoon thunderstorms build or the snow softens excessively; a slip on the steep hard snow here has been described as potentially fatal.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed June through September once the SW snowfields have consolidated; a strong, direct snow climb favors early-mid summer, while a drier late-season ascent shifts more onto the loose rock bands.
routes.detailedRack: Minimal technical rack — this is a snow climb/scramble, not a roped rock route. Crampons and an ice axe for the SW snowfields; a rope and a few pickets for the Quien Sabe/Sahale/Boston Glacier crossings en route if used as a connecting approach; helmet for the loose rock bands and summit block.
routes.proNeeds: Little to no fixed rock protection is used; a light glacier rack (pickets, occasional screw) covers the connecting glacier terrain, and most parties travel unroped on the summit scrambling once off the snow.
proTips: The cowskull-shaped snowfield high on the face is the key landmark below the summit.
Sahale Glacier Camp is the standard, spectacular base for the SW approach, a short walk from Cascade Pass via the Sahale Arm.
When the snow is icy, the adjacent rock bands offer safer 3rd/4th class scrambling than the open snow gully.
watchOut: A fall on the steep SW snow slopes above Horseshoe Basin could mean a long, potentially fatal slide.
Cascade River Road can wash out or close seasonally from flooding/rockslides — check current NPS road-condition updates before driving in.
Overnight camping is prohibited at the trailheads themselves; a backcountry permit and designated camp (e.g. Sahale Glacier Camp) are required.
knownHazards: Steep, hard-packed snow on the SW face has produced long falls in trip reports.
Loose scree and rubble near the summit ridge require careful footing.
Crevasses on the Quien Sabe/Sahale/Boston Glaciers along connecting approaches require roped glacier travel.

road.name: Cascade River Road (FR 15)
road.status: Seasonally open
road.seasonalGate: Gated at Eldorado Creek (milepost 20) through winter/shoulder season; can wash out or close temporarily from flooding/rockslides even in summer
road.driveNote: ~22.9 mi from Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead parking area (~3,660 ft); paved for roughly the first 10 mi, then gravel/washboard; no RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft

climate.typical: Cool, often cloudy/wet North Cascades alpine climate with afternoon thunderstorm risk in summer; snow can persist on the SW face into July

bySeason.spring: Deep, avalanche-prone snowpack; road/trail access often still closed by gate or snow
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; snowfields firm overnight, soften by afternoon
bySeason.fall: Drier but shorter days and increasing new-snow risk by October
bySeason.winter: Road gated at Eldorado Creek; approach becomes a full winter mountaineering undertaking

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park, Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: $10/person plus a $6 nonrefundable reservation fee for an overnight backcountry permit; no day-use fee
access.permit: Backcountry permit required year-round for any overnight stay in the Cascade Pass/Sahale Glacier cross-country zone; peak-season (May 15-Oct 10) slots are allocated by an early-access lottery via recreation.gov
access.closures: Cascade River Road can close seasonally or temporarily due to washouts/flooding/rockslides; camping banned at the trailhead itself

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start from Sahale Glacier Camp

emergency.county: Peak straddles the Chelan/Skagit county line; primary road access is from the Skagit County side (Marblemount)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911; Chelan County Rivercom Dispatch 509-663-9911 for Chelan-side incidents
emergency.rangerStation: NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley (360-856-6021); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (360-424-4111)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness with no road access to the peak; NPS and county Search & Rescue (e.g. Chelan County Volunteer SAR, 509-433-7171) coordinate ground/helicopter rescue

routes.corrections: One older source (mountaineers.org) lists the summit at 9,080 ft; current sources (Wikipedia/Lists of John) give 9,114 ft for the SW summit and 9,112 ft for the NE summit — the more recent figure (matching the peak's given elevationFt) was used.

objHaz: crevasses on the Quien Sabe and Boston Glaciers
cornices near the Sahale-Boston col/Davenport Glacier
afternoon-softened snow causing postholing and wet-slide risk
routes.face: North Face above the Boston Glacier
routes.overview: A classic North Cascades ice route: 40-50 degree snow/ice climbing up Buckner's north-facing wall directly above the massive Boston Glacier, reached by a committing glacier approach over Sharkfin Col or the Sahale-Boston col.
routes.beta: From Boston Basin, cross the Quien Sabe Glacier and either the Sahale-Boston col or Sharkfin Col to reach the Boston Glacier, then traverse to the base of Buckner's north face (~7,800 ft). Climb steep snow/ice on the line of least resistance — mountainschool.com describes 8-13 pitches of 40-50 degree snow/ice — negotiating a bergschrund and any exposed rock bands, to the summit ridge between the two summits. The North Face Couloir variant offers similarly moderate climbing nearby.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road ~21.7 mi from Marblemount to the Eldorado/Boston Basin trailhead pullout (~3,200 ft). Hike into Boston Basin (~2.5 mi, +2,500 ft to ~5,700 ft), then ascend via moraine to the Quien Sabe Glacier and cross the Sahale-Boston col or Sharkfin Col onto the Boston Glacier, traversing to the base of the north face.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the Southwest Slopes/Horseshoe Basin and Sahale Arm back to Cascade Pass rather than reversing the technical ascent line; some reverse the route back across the Boston Glacier and Sharkfin/Sahale-Boston col.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the SW Slopes into Horseshoe Basin, regain Sahale Peak's south ridge, and continue down Sahale Arm to Cascade Pass and the trailhead (a common through-hike exit). Alternatively, reverse the ascent by boot-skiing or downclimbing the Boston Glacier and recrossing Sharkfin Col or the Sahale-Boston col back to Boston Basin.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike into Boston Basin and establish camp (or continue to a bivy at the Sahale-Boston col / on the Boston Glacier). Day 2: pre-dawn crossing of the Quien Sabe and Boston Glaciers to the north face, climb to the summit (trip reports log roughly 1.5-2 hours base-to-summit once at the face), then descend via the SW Slopes/Horseshoe Basin/Sahale Arm to Cascade Pass or back over the col to Boston Basin. Day 3: hike out if not completed on Day 2.
routes.bail: Retreat once committed on the face is difficult; parties can traverse off toward the Boston Glacier and retreat over Sharkfin Col or the Sahale-Boston col rather than attempting to reverse steep sections of the face directly.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time to be off the face and back across the Boston/Quien Sabe glaciers before afternoon sun softens the snow significantly, increasing postholing, wet-slide, and crevasse-bridge risk.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in June-July while the face still holds continuous snow/ice; trip reports note that once the north side melts out later in summer, the underlying rock is loose and of poor quality, making the route much harder and more hazardous.
routes.detailedRack: Crampons and an ice axe (or two tools in the steeper upper couloir); a rope for the Quien Sabe/Boston Glacier crossings and any belayed sections on the face; snow pickets for glacier travel and running belays; harness, helmet, and crevasse-rescue kit for the col/glacier legs.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly snow/ice-based — pickets and occasional ice screws where the snow firms up — rather than rock gear; the route is often simul-climbed on moderate snow with a rope out primarily for the glacier approach and any steeper bergschrund crossing.
whatToBring: Extra layers for an exposed col bivy
proTips: The North Face and North Face Couloir both offer moderate, sustained 40-50 degree snow/ice above the Boston Glacier and are considered classic North Cascades ice routes.
Many parties break the approach with a bivy at the Sahale-Boston col or a camp on the Boston Glacier.
A popular exit loops down the SW Slopes/Horseshoe Basin and Sahale Arm rather than reversing the ascent.
watchOut: Natural rockfall is a documented hazard: in 1984 a Mountaineers climber was seriously injured by a rockslide from ~100 m upslope while sheltered in a moat below a rock wall; his helmet is credited with saving his life.
Snow/ice conditions are reported best in June-July; later in summer the melted-out rock underneath is described as very loose.
Afternoon sun significantly softens the Quien Sabe/Boston Glacier snow, causing postholing and raising wet-slide and crevasse-bridge risk — favor an early start.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Quien Sabe and Boston Glaciers require roped travel and crevasse-rescue readiness.
A bergschrund guards the base of the face and its coverage varies by season.
Cornices have been specifically noted above the Davenport Glacier/Sahale-Boston col area, prompting parties to avoid camping directly beneath them.

emergency.rangerStation: NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley (360-856-6021); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (360-424-4111)
emergency.notes: Remote glaciated terrain with no road access; NPS and county Search & Rescue (e.g. Chelan County Volunteer SAR, 509-433-7171) coordinate ground/helicopter rescue; the 1984 North Face rescue required a U.S. Navy helicopter at a record altitude

routes.corrections: Given data lists 'North Face' with ice grade AI2-3/alpine grade AD; sources describe it as moderate 40-50 degree snow/ice (Grade II-III depending on source/era), consistent with the given rating — no material conflict found, though pitch count is a range (8-13) rather than a fixed number.

blurb: Booker Mountain (8,284 ft) is a remote summit in North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, about 1.5 miles southeast of Mount Buckner on the crest of Park Creek Ridge between the Park Creek and Stehekin River drainages. It is built of granodioritic orthogneiss typical of the North Cascades' deeply glaciated core, and its northeast face is among the steepest in the state, while the southwest side is a long talus-and-scree slope. The peak was named for Booker T. Washington at the suggestion of landscape painter Abby Williams Hill around 1904; the first recorded technical ascent, via the Northeast Face, was made by Dan Davis and John Holland on August 22, 1964.

routes.rappels: None typically required in normal dry conditions; descend via the same line
routes.face: Southwest side, below the west summit ridge/col
routes.bestSeason: Trip reports cluster in August and late September, after the seasonal snowfield near Sahale Glacier's margin and the approach gullies have mostly melted out. Snow can persist into July on north-facing gullies and the glacier margin traverse, so an ice axe and light crampons/microspikes are typically still useful even in a 'snow-free' season.
routes.overview: A long, remote 3rd-class scramble up talus, scree, and low-angle slabs on Booker's southwest side; it is reached via a rugged high traverse from Mount Buckner rather than climbed as a standalone approach.
routes.beta: From the roughly 6,500' shoulder/col between Buckner and Booker, cross talus and scree, ascend low-angle slabs directly toward Booker, then scramble a short 3rd-class section in a gully below a col just west of the summit before finishing up low-angle talus and scree to the top.
routes.approach: Start at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (end of Cascade River Road) and climb the standard Cascade Pass / Sahale Arm trail. Continue up Sahale Arm toward Sahale Glacier Camp, then descend Sahale Peak's south buttress to a snow-free gully near 6,600 ft and enter Horseshoe Basin. Traverse the basin toward Mount Buckner (crossing talus and a permanent snowfield/narrow ledge below Sahale Glacier), work over or around Buckner's southwest slopes, then descend and angle to a roughly 6,500' shoulder between Buckner and Booker before beginning the final scramble up Booker's southwest slopes.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down the southwest slopes to the Buckner-Booker shoulder, then retrace the Horseshoe Basin traverse and Sahale Arm back to Cascade Pass Trailhead.
