routes.descentText: Down-climb the short 3rd-class gully and scree/talus slopes back to the ~6,500' shoulder between the two peaks, then re-cross the ridge/basin terrain toward Buckner's southwest slopes, drop into Horseshoe Basin, and climb back up to Sahale Arm before descending the maintained trail to the trailhead. Loose rock in the gullies is more hazardous underfoot on the way down; groups often rope up briefly for the exposed snowfield ledge if it has firmed up overnight.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as an overnight trip: Day 1, hike in via Cascade Pass and Sahale Arm to Sahale Glacier Camp (backcountry permit required). Day 2, very early alpine start for the long traverse over/around Buckner to Booker's summit and back to camp; one recorded combined Buckner+Booker round trip from Sahale Camp/trailhead ran roughly 3 a.m. to 7:30 p.m. Day 3 (or same day for strong parties), hike out. Some fit parties complete Buckner and Booker car-to-car in one very long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is along the same ridge/basin traverse used on approach — back over the Buckner-Booker shoulder, through Horseshoe Basin, and up to Sahale Arm; there is no significant shortcut, so deteriorating weather or injury requires committing to the full reversal.
routes.turnaround: Given the length and exposure of the Buckner-Booker traverse, set a firm turnaround time before starting and stick to it — afternoon thunderstorms and softening/icy conditions on the snowfield ledge and gullies both argue against a late summit push.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this part of North Cascades National Park; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended for this remote objective.
routes.detailedRack: Essentially none — this is an unroped scramble in normal dry conditions. A rope with a few slings/small cams can be carried for the short 3rd-class step below the summit col if it's wet, icy, or a party wants extra security.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal; the route is normally climbed unroped. Protection needs are limited to optional light gear for the short technical gully step near the summit.
gear: Sturdy approach or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: Bear-resistant food canister (required in designated NP backcountry camps)
Extra water (basin sections can be dry)
Sun protection for extended snow/slab travel
proTips: Start very early (pre-dawn) given the long ridge traverse from Buckner to Booker.
Most parties combine Mount Buckner and Booker in a single outing from Sahale Glacier Camp rather than climbing Booker alone.
Aluminum crampons and a whippet are useful backup even when most of the route is snow-free, especially for the permanent snowfield/ledge below Sahale Glacier.
Helmets are recommended through the loose gullies, particularly if other parties are traveling above.
watchOut: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in North Cascades summers on this exposed traverse.
Rockfall risk in the gullies, especially with parties above.
Route-finding across Horseshoe Basin and the Buckner-Booker shoulder gets difficult in poor visibility.
The permanent snowfield/narrow ledge below Sahale Glacier can be firm and icy in early morning.
knownHazards: Loose talus and scree with rockfall potential in the summit gully
Exposed narrow ledge traverse near the Sahale Glacier margin
Crevasse hazard where the route passes close to Sahale Glacier
Remoteness and lack of cell coverage extend any rescue timeline
Long, daylight-dependent traverse with no easy bail shortcut

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (road's end, elev. ~3,660 ft)
road.seasonalGate: Gated for winter at the North Cascades NP boundary about 5 miles below the trailhead; reopens for the season on a variable date each spring
road.driveNote: Paved for about the first 10 miles from Marblemount, then gravel with potholes and washboard to road's end; not recommended for RVs, large trailers, or low-clearance vehicles/sedans, and vehicles over ~22 ft are restricted

climate.forecastZone: No dedicated NWAC zone covers North Cascades NP peaks directly; nearest general public forecast guidance is the West Slopes North Cascades zone
climate.typical: Alpine climate with snow persisting into July on north-facing gullies and glacier margins; short reliable weather windows and frequent summer afternoon convective buildup

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack with avalanche/cornice hazard; access road often still gated
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); south/west aspects typically snow-free by mid-to-late July
bySeason.fall: Stable windows can persist into September, but days shorten and early snow is possible
bySeason.winter: Road closed and deep snow make this a non-winter objective

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No day-use fee; an overnight backcountry/wilderness permit fee applies (per-person nightly fee plus a non-refundable reservation fee)
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay in the park; reserve in advance via recreation.gov or obtain a walk-up permit at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated for winter at the park boundary about 5 miles below the Cascade Pass Trailhead and reopens on a variable date each spring

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn — one recorded combined Buckner+Booker round trip started around 3 a.m.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass Trailhead - Sahale Glacier Camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Standard Cascade Pass/Sahale Arm trail approach

sectionBreakdown.section: Sahale Camp to Booker summit and return
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Sahale Glacier Camp - Booker Mountain summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Long ridge/basin traverse over or around Mount Buckner; timing reflects a combined Buckner+Booker day, not Booker alone

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit); trailhead access is via Skagit County (Marblemount)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; NPS non-emergency line (360) 854-7255
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111 (Level III trauma center)
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage in this part of the park; NPS and local SAR groups recommend carrying a satellite communicator or PLB for backcountry trips in this area.

routes.corrections: Given grade (Grade II, Class 3 / PD) matches trip-report descriptions of a long talus/scree scramble with one short 3rd-class gully section, so no grade correction is made. Some trip reports round the summit to 8,286 ft versus the 8,284 ft figure used here (from Wikipedia/USGS-derived sources); 8,284 ft is treated as authoritative. Distance/timing figures are sourced from reports of the combined Mount Buckner + Booker Mountain outing from the same Cascade Pass/Sahale Arm trailhead (Booker is almost always climbed together with Buckner, not as a standalone trip), so distKm and totalHrs should be read as approximate for that combined itinerary rather than an isolated Booker-only round trip. No source documents a first-ascent party for the Southwest Slopes route specifically (only the Northeast Face FA, a different line, is recorded), so route 'fa' is left null.

blurb: Mount Formidable (8,325 ft) is a rugged summit in the North Cascades of Skagit County, Washington, on a spur ridge about half a mile west of the Cascade crest between Cascade Pass and Dome Peak. Originally igneous rock later metamorphosed in the Late Cretaceous (locally mapped as gneiss), it carries the Middle Cascade and Cache Glaciers on its northern flanks. Named by mountaineer Herman Ulrichs in 1935 for the forbidding look of its north face, it was first climbed on July 25, 1938 by Ptarmigan Club members Calder Bessler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers during the inaugural Ptarmigan Traverse.

routes.fa: Calder Bessler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, Tom Myers — July 25, 1938 (peak FA, made via this south-side line during the inaugural Ptarmigan Traverse)
routes.face: South face/rib and Southeast Ledges, between the Spider-Formidable col and the true summit
routes.overview: A long, remote alpine outing on Mount Formidable's south side, reached via the opening stage of the Ptarmigan Traverse: glacier and col travel to gain the mountain's south basin, then a scramble up broken ledges and a short low-5th-class rib to the summit.
routes.beta: Most parties take 2-3 days, approaching via Cascade Pass, Cache Col, and Kool-Aid Lake or the Spider-Formidable col. The route climbs a broken rib/ledge system (Class 3-4, with one low-5th, rope-protected step across a chasm) to the summit; the same line is used for the descent, and is the only practical way down.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass trailhead (3,650 ft, end of Cascade River Road), hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 mi to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). Pick up the Ptarmigan Traverse climbers' trail bearing SSE, making a rising traverse on the flanks of Mixup Peak to the Cache Glacier, and ascend snow to Cache Col. Descend on a traverse SSE to Kool-Aid Lake (common camp), then continue south past Red Ledge, descending across meadows, snow, and talus toward the Middle Cascade Glacier icefall. Ascend the glacier SSE to the Spider-Formidable col (~7,320 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the south route: down-climb the south rib/ledges back to the ~7,050 ft notch, regain the Spider-Formidable col, and retrace the approach over the Middle Cascade Glacier, Cache Col, and the Cascade Pass trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the broken south rib back through the gully system, watching for the crux chasm/cleft crossing where a short rappel or careful roped downclimb may be needed — rappel anchors are reported scarce, so be prepared to build your own. Regain the ~7,050 ft notch, climb back over the Spider-Formidable col, and descend the Middle Cascade Glacier icefall into the south basin. From there retrace the approach past Red Ledge to Kool-Aid Lake/Cache Col, over Cache Col, and down the climbers' trail to Cascade Pass and the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done in 2-3 days: Day 1 hike from the Cascade Pass trailhead over Cascade Pass and Cache Col to camp at Kool-Aid Lake or the Spider-Formidable col; Day 2 climb to the summit and return to camp (strong parties sometimes push straight through in one long day); Day 3 hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same south rib/gully system to the ~7,050 ft notch and back over the Spider-Formidable col; from there descend the Middle Cascade Glacier and retrace the approach to Cache Col and the Cascade Pass trail. There is no shorter alternate descent.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before committing to the crux chasm/cleft crossing on the south rib if route-finding is uncertain or the rock feels unusually loose or wet — this crossing has been the site of a fatal fall. Also turn back if afternoon thunderstorms are building, given how long and loose the retreat is.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions typically run from early July through late August, matching the window most parties use for the Ptarmigan Traverse; the Cascade River Road itself usually doesn't open until late June, sometimes later in heavy-snow years. Early season, the traverse across Mixup Peak's flanks can hold firm snow with bad runout; late season, Cache Col can moat out and become difficult to cross, and the south basin's snow ramps turn to slush in the afternoon heat.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack for the roped step across the crux chasm/cleft on the south rib: a handful of small-to-mid cams/nuts, a few slings, and a rope (30-60m). Helmet is essential given pervasive loose rock. Add standard glacier travel gear (harness, crevasse-rescue kit, ice axe, crampons) for the Cache and Middle Cascade Glacier crossings.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal until the chasm/cleft crossing on the south rib, where natural gear (small-to-mid cams/nuts) and a rope are used for a short belayed step or rappel; solid rappel anchors are reported scarce there.
whatToBring: Overnight pack for a 2-3 day trip
Camp/bivy gear for Kool-Aid Lake or the Spider-Formidable col
Extra water/filter (camps can be dry or windy)
proTips: Camp at Kool-Aid Lake or push on to the Spider-Formidable col to shorten the summit day.
Start early to cross the glacier and snow ramps while they're still firm; afternoon heat turns them to slush.
Carry a rope and a small rack even though most of the route is 3rd/4th class — the chasm crossing on the south rib is short but exposed.
watchOut: The chasm/cleft crossing on the south rib is the site of a documented fatality (2006); avoid exposed traversing on the loose rock around it.
Several near-parallel gullies cut the south basin and are easy to confuse — many parties pick the wrong one and top out on a false summit east of the true one.
Rock throughout is loose and repeatedly described by parties as 'chossy'; test holds carefully and wear a helmet.
Solid anchors for rappelling are scarce near the crux — be ready to build your own.
knownHazards: A large chasm/cleft bisects the south rib below the summit; crossing it on loose rock has caused at least one fatal fall (Ed Miller, August 2006).
Loose, broken rock ('chossy') is pervasive across the south face and basin.
Glacier travel with crevasse hazard on the Cache Glacier and the Middle Cascade Glacier icefall.
A complex, easily-confused gully system in the south basin leads to frequent route-finding errors.

pitchDetail.notes: Spider-Formidable col down to the ~7,050 ft notch and into the south basin
Rising traverse NW across two gullies and a spur rib toward the summit gully
Broken rock and a snowfield to the crux chasm/cleft crossing; sometimes protected with a rope
Broken rock for the final ~600 ft to the summit

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then gravel; narrower and steeper with some potholes near the end
road.seasonalGate: Gated in winter; typically opens by late June, sometimes delayed into July in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: About 23 miles from Marblemount (SR 20) to the road-end Cascade Pass trailhead (3,600-3,650 ft), a circular lot beneath Johannesburg Mountain

climate.forecastZone: NWS West Slopes North Cascades zone (WAZ567); NWAC West Slopes North avalanche zone in winter/spring
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short, drier alpine climbing season roughly July-September; even in summer, multi-day trips should expect a chance of rain

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; Cascade River Road typically still gated
bySeason.summer: The main climbing window (Jul-Aug); glaciers and snow ramps firm up overnight and soften by afternoon
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows; early snow can close the route by late September/October
bySeason.winter: Road gated and route effectively inaccessible outside serious winter mountaineering

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Complex / Stephen Mather Wilderness (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee
access.permit: Free backcountry/wilderness permit required for any overnight stay (self-issue or advance reservation via recreation.gov), available at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount; day climbs need no permit
access.passRequired: None (NPS unit — no Northwest Forest Pass needed at this trailhead)
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated for winter and usually opens late June to early July depending on snowpack

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start from high camp so glacier and snow-ramp crossings are done while firm, aiming to be off the loose upper mountain before afternoon thunderstorms or softening snow

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass TH (3,650 ft) to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: 3.7 mi on established trail
Climbers' trail traverse of Mixup Peak's flanks, then snow slopes

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cache Col to Kool-Aid Lake / Red Ledge camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Descending traverse SSE
Past Red Ledge, across talus/snow, up the Middle Cascade Glacier icefall
Descend to the 7,050 ft notch, traverse the south basin gullies, climb the south rib

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: (360) 336-9450
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA: (360) 854-7245; main park line (360) 854-7200
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent in this part of the North Cascades backcountry; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended. 911 will always trigger a response, but wilderness cell calls can be routed to a distant dispatch center, delaying handoff to the correct agency.

routes.corrections: Some sources (Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn) list the summit at 8,356 ft rather than 8,325 ft; the more commonly cited figure (Wikipedia, SummitPost) of 8,325 ft — matching the peak's existing elevationFt — was retained for highPointFt. No source gives a numeric one-way approach distance or a specific rappel count for this route, so distKm, maxAngle, and rappels were left null rather than estimated.

blurb: Spider Mountain (8,317 ft) rises in the North Cascades of western Chelan County, about 1.5 miles east of Mount Formidable along the classic Ptarmigan Traverse between Cascade Pass and Dome Peak. Built of notoriously loose schist, it is flanked by the Spider Glacier on its north face and the Middle Cascade Glacier to the west, and is regarded by traversers mainly as a chossy side-trip rather than a destination in itself. The peak was first climbed in 1938 by Calder Bressler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers.

routes.overview: From the Spider-Formidable Col (~7,300 ft) on the Ptarmigan Traverse, the standard route drops slightly onto the south side of the peak and climbs a long, narrow, badly loose gully to a rocky rib, then follows the summit ridge over a false summit to a short Class 3 scramble on the true summit block.
routes.beta: This is the easiest way up Spider Mountain and the line most Ptarmigan Traverse parties use if they detour to tag the summit. It is universally described as a chossy, unpleasant scramble rather than a quality climb: a steep, confined gully choked with loose rock and irregular wet Class 3 steps leads from the col area up onto the peak's south/southeast flank, with a final ridge traverse and easy summit block. Multiple trip reports independently warn that the rock is 'horribly loose' and that rockfall from parties above is a real hazard in the gully's narrow confines.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,660 ft, end of Cascade River Road/Forest Road 15, ~23 mi from Marblemount), hike to Cascade Pass, then follow the Ptarmigan Traverse south through Mix-up Arm, crossing the Cache Glacier to Cache Col, and descend to Kool-Aid Lake (6,100 ft) as a base camp. From Kool-Aid Lake continue south past Magic Mountain, across Red Ledge (an exposed, sometimes verglassed traverse), then rope up to cross the Middle Cascade Glacier (crevassed below an icefall) to reach the Spider-Formidable Col (~7,300 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the loose gully/rib back to the Spider-Formidable Col, then retrace the Middle Cascade Glacier - Red Ledge - Kool-Aid Lake - Cache Col line back to Cascade Pass and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the ridge and gully back to the Spider-Formidable Col, exercising extra caution on the loose rock and any wet or icy steps (parties report the descent is often finished in the dark on long summit days). At the col, rope up again to recross the Middle Cascade Glacier, watching for crevasses and moats near the icefall, then retrace Red Ledge and the Ptarmigan Traverse tread back to Kool-Aid Lake and out via Cache Col, Mix-up Arm, and Cascade Pass to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Cascade Pass Trailhead to Kool-Aid Lake camp via Cascade Pass, Mix-up Arm, Cache Glacier and Cache Col (~7 mi, ~3,500 ft gain). Day 2: Alpine start from camp, cross Red Ledge and the Middle Cascade Glacier to Spider-Formidable Col, climb the gully/rib to the summit and return to camp (long day, 8-16 hrs round trip per multiple trip reports). Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is essentially reversing the route: downclimb to the Spider-Formidable Col and back across the Middle Cascade Glacier. There is no faster alternate descent off the peak itself; the nearest true escape from the broader area is back out via Cascade Pass.
routes.turnaround: Because the gully and rib are loose and exposed to rockfall from any party above, and much of the descent is a straightforward reversal of exposed, glaciated terrain, set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit push and be willing to retreat if weather, visibility, or rockfall conditions deteriorate — the approach and descent both involve glacier travel that is far safer done with good visibility and stable snow conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Peak season is roughly July through September, when the Cascade River Road and Cascade Pass Trailhead are typically snow-free and the Middle Cascade Glacier crossing and Red Ledge are most passable; the marine-influenced climate of the North Cascades brings heavy winter snowpack and the most reliable clear weather windows are mid-summer.
routes.comms: This is a remote, glaciated alpine cirque well off any road; cell service is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the approach and on the peak itself. A satellite communicator or PLB is strongly recommended, and NPS backcountry rangers can be reached in advance via the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
whatToBring: headlamp (summit days often finish in the dark)
trekking poles for the long approach
extra water/snacks for the long gully scramble
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier-travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse-rescue gear, crampons, ice axe) is needed for the Middle Cascade Glacier crossing to and from the Spider-Formidable Col. The gully and rib climbing itself is essentially unprotected scrambling on loose rock; a small rack of slings/a few pieces can be carried but is rarely placed — the terrain is walked/scrambled rather than pitched out.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is used on the route itself; the real protection need is a rope and rescue gear for the glacier approach, plus a helmet at all times in the gully given constant rockfall risk from loose rock and parties above.
proTips: Use the talus ramp at the mouth of the gully to avoid crossing a deep erosion runnel.
Exit the gully about two-thirds of the way up onto the rib/buttress to either side rather than climbing it to the very top — this avoids the worst of the loose rock near its head.
Wet rock in the gully can actually offer the most solid holds, since dry sections are often coated in loose grit.
Keep climbing parties tight and avoid stacking people directly below one another in the gully because of constant rockfall.
watchOut: Persistently loose, 'horribly loose' rock throughout the ascent gully
Rockfall triggered by parties climbing above
Water seeping over rock steps in the gully, adding to instability
Crevasses and moats on the Middle Cascade Glacier, especially near the icefall
An exposed, sometimes verglassed traverse across Red Ledge
Corniced sections on the summit ridge
knownHazards: The ascent gully is confined and consistently loose, creating serious rockfall exposure for anyone below other climbers.
The Middle Cascade Glacier crossing has real crevasse hazard and requires roped travel.
Red Ledge, part of the standard approach, can be icy or hold open moats depending on season and snow conditions.
Long summit days (reported up to 16 hours round trip from camp) mean parties frequently descend the loose terrain in the dark.

pitchDetail.notes: From Spider-Formidable Col, descend ~200 ft and traverse onto the south side of the peak to reach the gully mouth; a talus ramp bypasses a deep erosion runnel.
~800 vertical feet of narrow, steep, badly loose gully with intermittent wet Class 3 rock steps; exit onto the rib/buttress about two-thirds of the way up.
Soft dirt/rock rib leads up to the summit ridge crest, crossing or bypassing a corniced snow arete depending on conditions.
Traverse over a false summit; the final summit block looks like Class 4-5 from a distance but goes at an easy Class 3 scramble.

routes.face: South/southeast gully and rib off the Spider-Formidable Col (not a true north ridge — see corrections)
routes.fa: Calder Bressler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, Tom Myers, 1938 (peak first ascent; presumed same easiest-line route, not separately documented as a route-specific FA)

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Open in summer season (confirmed open as of the most recent NPS road-conditions report)
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated in late autumn through spring around milepost 20-21 (Eldorado Creek area), several miles short of the road end; usually opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June or July
road.driveNote: Paved to about mile 10 from Marblemount, then gravel with potholes and washboard to the road end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,660 ft); not suitable for RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft, and not recommended for low-clearance sedans.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy orographic winter snowfall and generally clear summers; July through September offer the most favorable climbing weather.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack on approach and route; glacier crossings require caution.
bySeason.summer: Best window for the climb; Cascade River Road and trailhead typically snow-free, though the Middle Cascade Glacier and Red Ledge still require care.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early snow at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Road to the trailhead gated well below its end; the route is effectively inaccessible/mountaineering-only in full winter conditions.

access.landManager: Mixed: the Cascade Pass approach lies within North Cascades National Park Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness, NPS); Spider Mountain itself and the Spider-Formidable Col/Kool-Aid Lake area lie within the Glacier Peak Wilderness, managed by the Okanogan-Wenatchee/Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forests (USFS).
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness camping permit from the NPS is required for designated camps within North Cascades National Park (e.g. near Cache Glacier); camping at Kool-Aid Lake and the Spider-Formidable Col, which lie within the Glacier Peak Wilderness (USFS land), does not require a permit.
access.closures: A mandatory moratorium on installing new fixed anchors is in effect in North Cascades National Park wilderness; clean-climbing techniques (removable protection) are required.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start from Kool-Aid Lake or Spider-Formidable Col camp given the length of the glacier crossing, loose-rock gully, and long descent

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass Trailhead to Kool-Aid Lake
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Cascade Pass, Mix-up Arm, Cache Glacier and Cache Col; ~6.9 mi, ~3,550 ft gain

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Kool-Aid Lake to Spider Mountain summit and return
sectionBreakdown.note: Round trip reported as long as 16 hours including the Middle Cascade Glacier crossing, Red Ledge, the loose ascent gully, and a headlamp-lit descent

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit and standard route); approach via Skagit County (Cascade River Road/Marblemount)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (509) 667-6851; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (Level III trauma center) is the nearest hospital via the Marblemount approach; serious trauma cases from the North Cascades backcountry are frequently flown to Harborview Medical Center in Seattle.
emergency.notes: This is a remote, glaciated, cross-country area; self-rescue capability and a satellite communicator/PLB are strongly advised given long response times.

routes.corrections: The given route name 'North Ridge / Spider-Formidable Col' does not match sourced route beta. Multiple independent trip reports (trailcatjim.com, onehikeaweek.com, eileensadventures.com) describe the standard/easiest line as descending from the Spider-Formidable Col onto the south or southeast side of the peak and ascending a loose gully/rib to the summit ridge — not a north ridge. 'face' and 'aspect' have been set to S/SE to match sourced descriptions rather than the given 'North Ridge' name. gainFt/lossFt (9,100 ft) and the ~16 hr round-trip timing reflect one full round-trip trip report from the trailhead (17.6 mi total) rather than route-only elevation gain, since no source isolates gain for the technical portion alone.

blurb: Klawatti Peak is a glacially-carved nunatak of Eldorado orthogneiss in North Cascades National Park, ringed by the Klawatti Glacier (east), McAllister Glacier (northwest), and Inspiration Glacier (south). It sits deep in the Eldorado Peak massif and is reached only via multi-day glacier approaches. Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, and Tom Gorton made the first ascent on July 7, 1940.

routes.overview: A short, low-5th-class rock step on Klawatti's southeast side above the Klawatti Glacier, reached by a long multi-glacier approach from Klawatti Col; the technical climbing is brief, but the glacier approach is the crux of the outing.
routes.beta: Most parties combine Klawatti with an ascent of Eldorado Peak, approaching via the Eldorado Creek climbers' path and East Ridge, then traversing the Inspiration Glacier to a camp near Klawatti Col (~7,800 ft). From the col, the southeast side above the Klawatti Glacier gives a short Class 4 to low-5th-class (5.2) rock step of one to two pitches leading to Class 3 scrambling on the summit block. Sources describe this general line as Klawatti's Southeast Ridge: Class 4 near the bottom with Class 3 scrambling higher.
routes.approach: From the Eldorado/Inspiration trailhead (about mile 20 on Cascade River Road, ~2,100 ft), follow the Eldorado Creek climbers' path through timber and talus to the Eldorado Glacier, gain Eldorado's lower East Ridge (~7,500 ft notch), and traverse roughly 1.5 miles across the Inspiration Glacier icecap to Klawatti Col (7,800 ft) on Klawatti's west side. From the col, contour toward the Klawatti Glacier side to reach the base of the southeast face.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to Klawatti Col, then retrace the Inspiration Glacier traverse and Eldorado's East Ridge/Eldorado Creek route to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the low-5th-class step and Class 4 terrain back to the base of the southeast face, then re-cross to Klawatti Col. From the col, reverse the Inspiration Glacier traverse toward Eldorado's East Ridge, dropping through established camps (roughly 7,800 ft, then near 5,400 ft) before descending the Eldorado Creek climbers' path to the Cascade River Road trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-3 day plan: Day 1, hike in via Eldorado Creek to a camp between about 5,400 and 7,800 ft on Eldorado's East Ridge/Inspiration Glacier; Day 2, traverse to Klawatti Col, climb Klawatti's southeast side (often combined with Eldorado and/or Austera Peak), return to camp; Day 3, hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat means reversing the ascent line back to Klawatti Col, then the Inspiration/McAllister glacier traverse and Eldorado's East Ridge descent to the Eldorado Creek climbers' path. Deteriorating weather or whiteout on the broad, largely featureless icecap is the main reason parties bail, since route-finding across the glaciers becomes difficult without visibility.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround based on glacier and snow-bridge condition rather than a fixed clock time — the approach crosses several crevassed glaciers (Eldorado, Inspiration, McAllister) where snow bridges and moat/bergschrund crossings weaken through the day; plan to be off the icecap traverse before afternoon warming degrades them.
routes.bestSeason: Standard season runs roughly July through September, after the Eldorado/Inspiration icecap has consolidated enough for glacier travel but before fall storms arrive. Early-season (June) attempts face more crevasse hazard and snow-covered moats/bergschrunds; a well-timed start helps cross south- and southeast-facing snow and glacier terrain before it softens.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through most of this zone per NPS guidance; carrying a satellite phone or personal locator beacon as backup is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack suffices for the short low-5th-class step: a handful of nuts and cams up to about 2 inches, a few slings, and a 30-60m rope. The bulk of the gear list is glacier-travel equipment (crampons, ice axe, pickets, crevasse-rescue kit) for the multi-glacier approach rather than technical rock protection.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal and glacier-oriented rather than rock-oriented: small-to-medium cams/nuts protect the short low-5th-class band, while pickets and screws are for the approach glaciers, not the summit step itself.
whatToBring: Crevasse rescue kit (prusiks, pulley, ice screws)
Map/compass/GPS for icecap navigation in poor visibility
proTips: Camp at Klawatti Col (~7,800 ft) or a rock camp on the McAllister Glacier side to shorten the summit push
Cross moats and bergschrunds early in the day while snow bridges are firmer
The Eldorado orthogneiss rock can be loose in spots — test holds before committing on the technical step
watchOut: Crevasses on the Eldorado, Inspiration, and McAllister Glaciers require roped travel and careful route-finding
Afternoon softening of snow bridges and moats increases fall-through risk
Loose rock on the short low-5th-class step
Corniced terrain near the summit in early season
knownHazards: Glacier travel with crevasse-fall risk across the Eldorado/Inspiration/McAllister icecap
Bergschrund/moat crossing at the base of the rock step
Loose rock (Eldorado orthogneiss) on the short technical section
Whiteout/navigation hazard on the broad, largely featureless icecap

pitchDetail.notes: Glacier and scree approach from Klawatti Col across to the base of the southeast rock step, crossing the bergschrund/moat.
Short technical rock step on the southeast face/ridge above the Klawatti Glacier, then Class 3 scrambling to the summit block.

routes.face: Southeast Face, above the Klawatti Glacier

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal; open in summer 2026 per NPS road-conditions page
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead area by late June (sometimes early July after heavy snow/storm damage); closed at various points in winter
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, follow Cascade River Road roughly 19-20 miles to the Eldorado/Inspiration climbers' trailhead parking (~2,100 ft). Paved for about the first 10 miles, then gravel with potholes and washboard, narrowing with steep drop-offs near the road's end at Cascade Pass. No RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades Zone (NWS zone WAZ301)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy winter snowfall and generally drier, more stable summers; the Eldorado/Inspiration icecap holds snow well into summer.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; crevasses largely bridged but bridges weaken with warm afternoons.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); glaciers become more broken out and crevassed as the season progresses.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows; early snowstorms can arrive by late September/October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow accumulation; access effectively requires ski mountaineering and is far more serious/committing.

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.fees: Backcountry camping permit fee (reported as roughly $10/person plus a non-refundable reservation fee at the Wilderness Information Center; confirm current NPS.gov fee schedule)
access.permit: Backcountry/wilderness camping permit required, obtained at the Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount
access.passRequired: No Northwest Forest Pass required for this trailhead per WTA
access.closures: Cascade River Road is subject to seasonal winter closures and occasional washout-related closures/maintenance; check the NPS Cascade River Road conditions page before driving in. A mandatory moratorium on installing new fixed anchors applies park-wide.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office 360-336-9450 (or 911; response from this remote area can take hours)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park / Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount - 810 State Route 20, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284, 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III ED); serious trauma cases from this area have been flown to Harborview Medical Center, Seattle
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through much of this zone; NPS recommends a satellite phone or PLB as backup and advises stating name, phone number, and location first when a call does connect.

routes.corrections: Elevation is well corroborated at 8,485 ft across Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and WTA; an older SummitPost figure of 8,448 ft for the peak appears outdated and was not used. No source could confirm the exact line taken by the 1940 first-ascent party, so no route-level 'fa' is asserted here for the Southeast Face/Klawatti Glacier line specifically — the 1940 Anderson/Boyer/Gorton ascent is documented as the peak's first ascent, not confirmed as this particular route.

waypoints.name: Eldorado Creek / Cascade River Road Trailhead

blurb: Dorado Needle is a slender granodiorite-orthogneiss spire in North Cascades National Park, about 0.7 mile north of Eldorado Peak, standing as the high point between the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers. It combines an easy glacier approach with several long, moderate multipitch trad routes (up to 5.10) on generally sound rock, making it a popular alpine-rock objective. The peak was first climbed via its Northwest Ridge in July 1940 by Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, and Tom Gorton.

routes.fa: Joan and Joe Firey, Hans Hoesli, Dave Knudson, and Peter Renz — July 4, 1971 (per Alpine Institute's route profile, citing Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide; Mountain Project's route page lists the FA as unknown, so this is reported with that caveat)
routes.rappels: No single standard rappel line: most parties downclimb/rappel the Northwest Ridge back onto the McAllister Glacier (a few short raps plus a moat crossing); some parties instead make two long rappels (70m rope) straight down the north face to the glacier.
routes.approach: From the Eldorado Creek trailhead on Cascade River Road (about 19-20 miles from Marblemount), climb steeply through forest, cross Eldorado Creek, and ascend talus/slabs/heather benches to the Eldorado Glacier and a high camp near Eldorado Peak's East Ridge (roughly 7,400-7,800 ft). From camp, rope up, cross the Inspiration Glacier and a col onto the McAllister Glacier, then traverse to the col below Point 8386 at the base of Dorado Needle's East Ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb and rappel the Northwest Ridge back to the McAllister Glacier, or (in good weather) make two long rappels down the north face directly to the glacier; then retrace the approach to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, avoid the excess of old fixed tat and head toward the Northwest Ridge start. A roughly 30m rappel down the southwest side reaches a stance, then easy ledges lead back to the NW Ridge, which is downclimbed/rappelled to the glacier. Descend the skier's-right side of the McAllister Glacier through crevassed terrain back to the McAllister-Inspiration col, watching for deep moats where snow meets rock. Alternatively, some parties rappel twice (70m rope) straight down the north face onto the McAllister Glacier to avoid the 'lame' NW Ridge downclimb.
routes.itinerary: Typical 3-5 day trip: Day 1 — hike from the Eldorado Creek trailhead to a camp in the Eldorado Creek or Roush Creek basin (~5,400 ft). Day 2 — continue up the Eldorado Glacier to a high camp near Eldorado Peak's East Ridge (~7,400-7,800 ft). Day 3 — rope up, cross the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers, climb the East Ridge to the Dorado Needle summit, and descend to camp. Day 4(-5) — hike out.
routes.bail: Before starting the technical climbing, retreat down the glacier to camp is straightforward. Once on the ridge, the easiest bail is to reverse pitches by rappel from the horn/anchor near the base back to the col; higher up, continuing to the rappel notch partway up the ridge is generally faster than reversing loose terrain.
routes.turnaround: Most parties take 8-12 hours round trip from high camp (glacier crossings, ridge climbing, and a technical descent), so set a firm turnaround time before leaving camp so the glacier recrossing and ridge descent aren't done after dark or in deteriorating afternoon weather, which is common in North Cascades summers.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed mid-to-late summer, roughly July through September, once the Cascade River Road and approach trail have melted out and the Inspiration/McAllister Glaciers are more consolidated. Guide services report the road and trailhead are typically not reliably open until late June or early July.
routes.comms: No cell coverage should be expected anywhere in the Eldorado/Inspiration/McAllister Glacier cirque; occasional signal exists only along SR-20 near Marblemount/Newhalem. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
whatToBring: Bivy/camping gear for a 2-4 night trip
Sun protection for extended glacier travel
Satellite communicator or PLB (no cell coverage)
routes.detailedRack: A single 60m or 70m rope with a light rack to about 3 inches suffices for most parties: cams from 0.3/0.4 in up through 2-3 in, with doubles in the 0.5-1 in range recommended for the crack pitches near the summit headwall, plus a few slings and tricams for the looser lower ridge pitches. Add full glacier travel and crevasse-rescue gear (axe, crampons, prusiks/pickets) for the Inspiration-McAllister Glacier crossings.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is adequate overall but spread thin on some of the loose lower pitches — the first pitch directly off the col is notably run-out with a horn/sling anchor as the first real protection about 100 ft up. Doubles in the small-to-mid cam sizes pay off on the better rock higher on the ridge and on the two crux headwall pitches near the summit.
proTips: Many parties skip the loose, largely unprotectable first pitch off the col by instead walking up the adjacent glacier to join better rock higher on the ridge.
Start early — most parties need 8-12 hours round trip from the East Ridge/Eldorado high camp to the Dorado Needle summit and back.
Scout the safest McAllister Glacier crossing/moat before committing to a descent line, especially late season.
Climbing shoes are worth carrying for the cleaner 5.6-5.7 pitches on the summit headwall even though most of the route goes in boots.
watchOut: The first pitch above the Dorado Needle col is loose and largely unprotectable — treat it with caution or bypass via the glacier.
Excessive old fixed rappel anchors/tat near the summit and on the NW Ridge descent can route parties onto the wrong line — read anchors carefully.
Moats where the glacier meets rock can be deep and difficult to negotiate, especially late in the season.
knownHazards: Loose, lichen-covered rock on the lower ridge and the initial pitch off the col.
Crevasses and moats on the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers.
Afternoon thunderstorms and rapidly changing alpine weather typical of North Cascades summers.
Rockfall potential on the ridge, especially with other parties above.

pitchDetail.notes: Loose, nearly unprotectable pitch directly from the Dorado Needle col; best anchor is a horn with sling about 100 ft up. Many parties bypass this pitch via the adjacent glacier.
Scramble along or just below the ridge crest on better rock for several rope lengths to a notch, then downclimb to where the glacier meets the rock.
Rope up and cross the glacier to regain the ridge at a ledge marked by quartzite bands.
Continue several more rope lengths along the ridge, then make two short rappels into a notch.
First crux pitch of the summit headwall — loose rock but with decent protection.
Harder, more continuous climbing on good rock with better protection leads to the summit; never harder than 5.7.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then rough gravel with ruts/washouts that can require high clearance; the final few miles to the trailheads are narrow with steep drop-offs.
road.seasonalGate: Closed seasonally by snow; typically not reliably open until late June or early July, sometimes later after heavy snow or storm damage. Check NPS road-conditions page before driving.
road.driveNote: Total road length 23.1 miles from Marblemount; Eldorado Creek trailhead parking is around mile 20. Trailers and vehicles over 22 ft long/8 ft wide are prohibited past the park boundary (mile 18).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Typical North Cascades alpine-summer weather: cool mornings, potential for afternoon thunderstorms/showers, and rapid weather changes; snow is possible at this elevation in any month.

bySeason.spring: Road and approach are typically still snow-covered/closed; not a standard season for this route.
bySeason.summer: Primary season (Jul-Sep); glaciers are more consolidated but still crevassed, with afternoon thunderstorm risk.
bySeason.fall: Shorter days and colder temperatures, with possible early-season snow by late September/October.
bySeason.winter: Cascade River Road is closed by snow; the route is generally out of condition.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service) — Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier cross-country zone
access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for the required overnight wilderness permit
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for all overnight stays; obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (in person, or via advance reservation on recreation.gov)
access.closures: Cascade River Road and the Eldorado trailhead close seasonally with snow (typically drivable only roughly late June through October); verify current status on the NPS road-conditions page before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from high camp (roughly 5-7am) to leave a full day for the ridge climb, McAllister Glacier descent, and return.

sectionBreakdown.section: Trailhead to Eldorado/Roush Creek basin camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Eldorado Creek trailhead to ~5,400 ft camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep forest, log crossing of Eldorado Creek, then talus/slabs/heather; roughly 4 miles one-way with substantial elevation gain per multiple guide-service trip descriptions.

sectionBreakdown.section: Basin camp to East Ridge high camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Ascend the Eldorado Glacier to established high-camp sites near Eldorado Peak's East Ridge.

sectionBreakdown.section: High camp to Dorado Needle summit and back
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: ~7,400-7,800 ft camp via Inspiration/McAllister Glaciers to 8,440 ft summit and return
sectionBreakdown.note: Reported by multiple trip reports as roughly 8-12 hours round trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency: (360) 416-1911; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — park main line (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the approach/climb zone; carry a satellite communicator or PLB. Backcountry search and rescue in this area is coordinated by the NPS with Skagit County SAR support.

routes.overview: The East Ridge is a long, moderate alpine rock climb up Dorado Needle, following the crest (with glacier bypasses) from the col below Point 8386 to the summit, mixing 3rd-class scrambling with sustained 5.5-5.7 crack and slab pitches on generally sound rock, capped by a stouter crux headwall guarding the true summit.
routes.beta: A Grade III alpine rock route combining glacier travel on the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers with roughly a route-length of low-5th to 5.7 climbing along and just below Dorado Needle's East Ridge. The original guidebook rating of 5.5 has been revised upward by recent parties to 5.7 (some report 5.8) on the summit headwall pitches; the lower ridge is looser and less protectable than the clean rock higher up. Most parties climb it as a side trip from an Eldorado Peak East Ridge camp, taking 8-12 hours round trip.
routes.corrections: The seed data's grade/pitch stats (Grade III, 5.4, alpine grade PD+, 3 pitches) match Dorado Needle's Northwest Ridge route, not the East Ridge. Sourced East Ridge stats are Grade III, rock grade 5.7 (range 5.5-5.8 across sources), ~800 ft of roped climbing (Mountain Project); pitch counts are reported inconsistently across sources (roughly 8-14 depending on route-finding/rappel choices), so pitches was left null rather than guessed. No sourced French/Alps-style alpine grade (e.g. PD+) was found for this route, so alpineGrade was left null. Peak elevation of 8,440 ft (per Wikipedia and WTA) was used; Mountain Project's area page instead lists 8,488 ft. The route's first ascent is disputed between sources: Mountain Project's route page lists it as unknown, while Alpine Institute's route profile (citing Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide) attributes it to Joan and Joe Firey, Hans Hoesli, Dave Knudson, and Peter Renz on July 4, 1971.

blurb: Primus Peak (8,501-8,508 ft, sources vary) sits at the northeast edge of the Eldorado Icecap in North Cascades National Park, flanked by the North Klawatti Glacier to the south and the Borealis Glacier to the northeast, with Tricouni Peak immediately to its east. The peak is composed of Skagit Gneiss, and while the summit scrambling itself is largely non-technical (Class 2-4), Primus is regarded as a serious objective because both standard approaches require multiple days of remote, crevassed glacier travel with no maintained trail.

routes.face: South Ridge (final summit block, above the McAllister/North Klawatti Glacier system)
routes.overview: A remote, largely non-technical alpine summit reached by crossing the McAllister Glacier from the Eldorado Icecap and finishing up Primus's short south ridge; the glacier crossing and multi-day approach are the real difficulties, not the climbing itself.
routes.beta: From a high camp on the Eldorado Icecap (commonly near the Klawatti Col / North Klawatti Glacier), rope up and traverse the McAllister Glacier past Klawatti Peak toward the base of Primus's south side. Snow ramps lead up the lower peak; higher up climbers move onto broken rock, then ascend the final south ridge (Class 3-4 scrambling) to the broad, dry summit.
routes.approach: From the Eldorado Creek trailhead on Cascade River Road (~mile 18.5-20, elev. ~2,150 ft), follow the Eldorado Creek climbers' trail into Eldorado/Roush Basin, gain the Eldorado Glacier, and cross over Eldorado's east ridge onto the Inspiration Glacier toward Klawatti Col. From the col, drop onto the McAllister Glacier and traverse past Klawatti Peak's north side onto the North Klawatti Glacier below Primus's south flank before climbing the south ridge to the top.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the south ridge back onto the North Klawatti/McAllister Glacier system and retrace the Icecap traverse to Eldorado Creek.
routes.descentText: Descend the south ridge scrambling terrain back to the glacier, then re-rope for the return traverse across the North Klawatti and McAllister Glaciers toward Klawatti Col and the Inspiration/Eldorado Glaciers. Watch for softened, sun-affected snow bridges and widening moats on the return leg, especially in the afternoon; retrace the Eldorado Creek climbers' trail down to the Cascade River Road trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a multi-day trip: Day 1 hike in from Eldorado Creek to a camp in Eldorado/Roush Basin or on the Eldorado Glacier; Day 2 cross the Icecap (Eldorado-Inspiration-McAllister-North Klawatti glaciers) to a high camp near Klawatti Col or on the North Klawatti Glacier; Day 3 climb Primus's south ridge and return to high camp (often combined with nearby Klawatti, Austera, or Tricouni Peaks); Day 4 exit back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: In good weather, retreat is simply reversing the glacier traverse back toward Eldorado Creek. In poor visibility or storm, stay roped, navigate by GPS/compass across the featureless icecap plateau, and avoid pushing through deteriorating snow-bridge conditions on the McAllister/Inspiration Glaciers.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that leaves enough daylight and margin to re-cross the McAllister and Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier crevasse fields before afternoon snow softening, and before visibility or weather deteriorates on the exposed icecap plateau.
routes.bestSeason: Most commonly climbed July through September, when glacier travel is most straightforward and snow bridges are best consolidated in early-to-mid summer. The Cascade River Road to the Eldorado trailhead is often not snow-free and open until late June or early July, and by late September glacier crevasses are typically more open and route-finding across the icecap is less predictable.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this backcountry area; tree cover and steep terrain block signal in most of the park, and any successful cell call from the wilderness may route to a distant dispatch center. NPS advises never relying on a cell phone as the sole emergency plan and recommends a satellite communicator/PLB plus a filed itinerary.
routes.detailedRack: A glacier-travel kit is the core requirement: rope, harness, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks/mechanical ascenders, pickets), crampons, and ice axe for the icecap crossing. A light rock rack (a handful of slings and small cams/nuts) can be useful to protect the Class 3-4 scrambling on the final south ridge but is not standard for most parties.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are dominated by glacier travel gear (pickets/screws for crevasse rescue) rather than rock protection; the summit ridge scrambling is generally unroped and unprotected by parties in good conditions.
whatToBring: extra insulating layers for exposed glacier camps
proTips: Cross the McAllister/Inspiration glaciers early in the day, before sun softens snow bridges over crevasses.
Near the top, the summit area has a false summit face (~8,270 ft); traverse climber's left around it rather than pushing straight up steep snow that doesn't lead to the true summit.
Apply for the Klawatti Zone wilderness permit early at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center — quotas are small and this is a popular icecap-traverse objective.
Primus is frequently combined with Klawatti, Austera, Tricouni, or Eldorado Peaks from the same icecap camp, since all share the same approach.
watchOut: Thinly bridged crevasses and moats on the McAllister and North Klawatti Glaciers, especially late in the season.
Loose, chossy rock typical of North Cascades Class 3-4 terrain on the upper mountain.
Long, complex glacier-crossing approach with real routefinding and whiteout risk on the featureless icecap plateau.
No reliable cell coverage and a multi-day distance from the trailhead complicate any rescue.
knownHazards: Crevasse-fall hazard during roped glacier travel across the McAllister/Inspiration/North Klawatti glacier system.
Rockfall and loose rock on the upper south ridge and summit block.
Remote location, multiple days from a trailhead, with limited options for rapid rescue.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 10 miles, then rough gravel; condition beyond that varies year to year and should be checked with NPS before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Gated in late autumn through spring, typically near milepost 20 (Eldorado Creek); the road usually opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June, occasionally not until early July after a heavy snow year.
road.driveNote: Pavement ends around mile 10 (NPS park boundary at mile 18). The Eldorado parking area at mile 20 accommodates larger vehicles and is a common turnaround point; the final ~3 miles to the Cascade Pass trailhead are narrow with steep drop-offs. Trailers and vehicles over 22 ft long / 8 ft wide are prohibited within the park boundary.

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Cascades (NWS zone WAZ567); NWAC Cascade-region avalanche/mountain weather forecasts cover the broader area in winter/spring.
climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate with a heavy winter snowpack; drier, more stable weather typically July through September.

bySeason.spring: Heavy residual snowpack; Cascade River Road often still gated; glacier travel can have good, more consolidated snow bridges.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window; warmer, drier days but with afternoon thunderstorm risk and glacier bridges progressively degrading through the season.
bySeason.fall: Early snow possible; glacier crevasses more open and route-finding across the icecap less predictable; shorter daylight.
bySeason.winter: Road closed at the seasonal gate; extreme avalanche and glacier hazard; the peak is rarely climbed in winter.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS), within the Stephen Mather Wilderness.
access.fees: America the Beautiful (or equivalent NPS) pass required for parking at some trailheads; no separate per-peak fee.
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry camping permit is required for overnight stays (Klawatti climbing zone); issued in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, subject to a small nightly quota.
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful interagency pass (or park entrance equivalent) for trailhead parking.
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated seasonally (roughly late autumn through spring/early summer near milepost 20); a mandatory moratorium on new fixed climbing anchors applies park-wide. Check current NPS road-status and permit-availability pages before a trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (non-emergency, via Skagit 911); dial 911 for emergencies, though NPS notes County/911 calls take longer to reach Park Dispatch than calling Park Dispatch directly.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245 (permits) / main park line (360) 854-7200; Park Dispatch (360) 873-4500 ext. 37.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — the nearest full-service hospital, roughly 1.5+ hours from the Cascade River Road trailheads via SR-20.
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the park's backcountry; tree cover and steep terrain block signal, and a successful call may route to a distant (sometimes Canadian) dispatch center. NPS advises never relying on a cell phone as the sole evacuation plan and recommends a satellite communicator/PLB plus a filed trip itinerary.

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 8,501 ft matches ListsofJohn's LiDAR-derived figure; most other sources (Wikipedia, Mountain Project, PeakVisor, CountryHighpoints trip reports) list 8,508 ft, likely from older 40-ft-contour USGS topo rounding — both figures are in active use, so highPointFt is set to the given 8,501 ft with this note. Wikipedia's infobox DMS coordinate ("120°05'28"W") appears to be a transcription typo of its own decimal coordinate (121.09111°W); the correct longitude, corroborated by PeakVisor's GNIS-style data and by Primus's described position near Eldorado/Klawatti/McAllister/Borealis Glaciers, is approximately -121.09°W, which is what's used for the summit waypoint here. The route name "South Ridge / McAllister Glacier" is not a single named route on any one source but is a reasonable synthesis of the standard south/Eldorado-Icecap approach (which crosses the McAllister Glacier) finishing via Primus's south ridge, as described across Mountain Project's "South Approach" and a 2015 Inspiration Traverse trip report that explicitly climbs "the south ridge to the top." No first-ascent record (peak or this specific route) was found in available sources; the only sourced FA-adjacent fact is Mark Bebie's 1986 technical climb of Primus's separate North Ridge (5.6), which is not this route.

blurb: Austera Peak (8,339 ft / 2,542 m) is the highpoint of a sharp arête called Austera Towers in the south unit of North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, about a mile north of Klawatti Peak and a mile south of Primus Peak. The North Klawatti, Klawatti, and McAllister Glaciers all radiate off its granitic North Cascades slopes, and the peak sits at the heart of the classic Inspiration-McAllister-Klawatti icecap traverse. It was first climbed September 16, 1965 by Joe and Joan Firey, John and Irene Meulemans, and Anthony Hovey.

routes.fa: Joe & Joan Firey, John & Irene Meulemans, Anthony Hovey — Sept 16, 1965 (peak first ascent; likely also first ascent of this, the peak's easiest/standard line)
routes.rappels: One ~75 ft rappel across Klawatti Peak's north ridge (30-60m rope)
objHaz: loose/broken rock on the summit ridge
possible rime/verglas on the final face early season
routes.face: Southwest side/ridge of the peak, approached across the McAllister and Klawatti Glaciers; final moves are on the northeast face of the summit block
routes.overview: A glacier mountaineering line up Austera's southwest side, linking the Inspiration, McAllister, and Klawatti Glaciers from the standard Eldorado approach to a short, exposed 4th/low-5th-class scramble up the summit block.
routes.beta: From an Eldorado-area glacier camp, rope up and traverse the Inspiration Glacier to Klawatti col, pass below Klawatti Peak, and cross a lobe of the upper McAllister Glacier to the 7,900 ft Klawatti-Austera col. Drop packs, crampons, and axes there, then scramble a few hundred feet of 4th class terrain, trending and traversing left on a broken ridge before finishing with about 30 ft of low 5th class (5.5 in August conditions) around to the right on the northeast face to the summit. Expect roughly 4 hours one-way from camp, with about 1,280 ft of gain and 592 ft of loss over 2.8 miles.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road about 20 miles to the Eldorado Creek parking area (mile 20, ~2,100 ft). Ford the Cascade River near the lot, pick up the climbers' trail on the far bank, and climb through timber and open slopes to a camp on the Inspiration Glacier near Eldorado Peak's east ridge (roughly 7,500-7,600 ft); this leg takes about 5-6 hours.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb back over the Klawatti-Austera col and re-cross the McAllister, Klawatti-col, and Inspiration Glaciers to camp, making the ~75 ft rappel off Klawatti's north ridge along the way.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the low-5th-class step and the broken 4th-class ridge back to the Klawatti-Austera col. Re-rope for glacier travel, cross the McAllister Glacier lobe back past Klawatti Peak to Klawatti col, make the roughly 75 ft rappel across Klawatti's north ridge where needed, and retrace the Inspiration Glacier to the Eldorado-area camp, then the climbers' trail and river ford back to the Cascade River Road trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Eldorado Creek lot on Cascade River Road, ford the Cascade River, and hike/climb to a camp on the Inspiration Glacier (~7,500-7,600 ft), about 5-6 hours. Day 2: Rope up and travel the Inspiration Glacier to Klawatti col, cross the McAllister Glacier lobe to the Klawatti-Austera col (7,900 ft), and scramble the southwest ridge to the summit (~4 hours one-way); reverse the route to camp, or extend into Klawatti and/or Primus Peak as part of a longer icecap traverse before hiking out.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent — there is no shorter bailout once committed to the glacier crossing, so downclimb to the Klawatti-Austera col and re-cross the McAllister/Klawatti/Inspiration Glaciers to camp; turn around early if weather or visibility turns before committing to the glacier traverse.
routes.turnaround: Because the route crosses three glaciers with crevasse exposure before any technical climbing begins, set a firm weather/visibility-based turnaround rather than pushing for the summit — deteriorating visibility on the icecap makes crevasse navigation far more hazardous than the climbing itself.
routes.bestSeason: Guided trips run this route June through September; mid-summer (July-August) generally offers the most consolidated, best-bridged glacier travel, while conditions on the final rock step vary with the season — parties have found it low 5th class and dry in August but rime- or verglas-coated earlier in the year.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of North Cascades National Park's backcountry, including this route; carry a satellite messenger or PLB and a backup plan rather than relying on a phone signal.
routes.detailedRack: Full glacier travel kit (harness, crevasse-rescue gear, crampons, ice axe), a 30-60m rope, and a light alpine rack with a few long slings for the short rock step; some parties add small cams and a retrievable cordelette to back up the rappel anchor.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal — a light rack and slings suffice for the brief 4th/low-5th-class section; the technical emphasis of the route is glacier travel gear, not rock gear.
gear: glacier travel kit (harness, crevasse rescue gear)
whatToBring: sun protection for extended glacier exposure
bivy/camp gear for an overnight at the Inspiration Glacier camp
proTips: Drop packs, crampons, and axes at the Klawatti-Austera col before the summit scramble to move faster on the rock.
Many parties link Austera with neighboring Klawatti and/or Primus Peak via the Inspiration-McAllister-Klawatti icecap traverse rather than climbing it in isolation.
A 30-60m rope and a light rack with a few long slings is enough technical gear — most of the challenge is the glacier approach.
watchOut: Crevasses on the Inspiration, McAllister, and Klawatti Glaciers — travel roped.
Loose, broken rock on the summit ridge scramble.
Route-finding on the broken ridge below the summit block.
Rime or verglas can coat the final northeast-face step outside peak summer conditions.
knownHazards: Roped glacier travel with crevasse exposure across three glaciers on the approach.
Loose/broken rock on the final ridge scramble to the summit.
A short but exposed low-5th-class section on the northeast face just below the summit.

pitchDetail.notes: Roped glacier travel: Inspiration Glacier to Klawatti col, then a lobe of the McAllister Glacier to the 7,900 ft Klawatti-Austera col.
A few hundred feet from the col, trending and traversing left on a broken ridge.
Final ~30 ft, climbed around to the right on the northeast face.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Gravel road from Marblemount to the Eldorado Creek parking area (mile 20); the final ~3 miles beyond Eldorado to Cascade Pass trailhead (mile 23.1) is narrow with steep drop-offs and can be rutted or washed out. The road has seen significant storm damage in recent seasons with partial closures below mile 20 reported in 2025-2026 — check the NPS road-conditions page before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed/gated in winter; in a normal year opens to Cascade Pass trailhead by late June, sometimes delayed into July by snow or storm damage.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, follow Cascade River Road about 20 miles to the Eldorado Creek parking area, then ford the Cascade River near the lot to pick up the climbers' trail.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short, drier alpine climbing window; permanent glaciers and snowfields dominate the upper mountain year-round.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; the access road and river ford may still be difficult or closed.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jun-Sep); glacier crevasses open progressively through the season and afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and the first snow can arrive by October, adding complexity to glacier travel.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall, road closed, and the area is effectively inaccessible without ski/splitboard travel and avalanche skills.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: Backcountry camping permit fee of $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee (per WTA); no day-use fee.
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight camping, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: Fixed anchors are prohibited park-wide in the Stephen Mather Wilderness; Cascade River Road is subject to seasonal and storm-damage closures that can restrict access below the Eldorado trailhead.

timing.recommendedStart: Early/alpine start from the Inspiration Glacier camp to cross the approach glaciers on firm snow before afternoon softening.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Eldorado Creek TH (~2,100 ft) to Inspiration Glacier camp (~7,500-7,600 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Includes the Cascade River ford and climbers' trail through timber to the glacier.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Eldorado camp via Klawatti col and McAllister Glacier lobe to Klawatti-Austera col (7,900 ft) and the summit scramble
sectionBreakdown.note: Roped glacier travel plus roughly 150-300 ft of 4th/low-5th-class scrambling near the top; one-way estimate.

waypoints.name: Eldorado Trailhead (Cascade River Road, Eldorado Creek climbers' path)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — 360-424-4111 (Level III Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of this backcountry area; rescue response can take hours to days given the remote, glaciated terrain.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 8,370 ft does not match reputable sources: USGS/Wikipedia list 8,339 ft (2,542 m NAVD88), PeakVisor lists 8,339 ft, and WTA lists 8,334 ft — highPointFt corrected to 8,339 ft. Given route grade cites 'Class 4' as the crux, but Mountain Project's route description places the final ~30 ft on the northeast face at low 5th class (5.5 in August conditions), so rockGrade is set to 5.5 to reflect the true crux above the 4th-class scrambling.

blurb: Tricouni Peak (8,102 ft per USGS/Wikipedia; other sources list 8,105-8,106 ft) is a glacier-rimmed summit in North Cascades National Park's McAllister Mountains group, sitting between the Borealis and North Klawatti Glaciers just southeast of its taller neighbor Primus Peak. Composed of Eldorado Orthogneiss, it was first climbed and named in August 1951 by Elwyn Elerding, Jeanne Elerding, and Les Carlson during a traverse of the Inspiration Glacier. It is rarely climbed as a standalone objective, usually tagged as one summit among several (Klawatti, Primus, Austera, Dorado Needle) on the multi-day Ptarmigan/Inspiration/Isolation Traverse.

routes.fa: Elwyn Elerding, Jeanne Elerding, Les Carlson — August 1951, via a traverse of the Inspiration Glacier (the approach line matches this Klawatti Col/southwest side of the peak, though no source explicitly names it a 'Southwest Slopes' first ascent)
routes.overview: A glacier-approach-and-scramble line up Tricouni's southwest side, reached via Klawatti Col; most parties climb it as a short Class 4 side-summit from a high camp while traversing the McAllister Mountains group (Klawatti, Primus, Austera, Dorado Needle) on the Ptarmigan/Inspiration/Isolation Traverse.
routes.beta: From a high camp near Klawatti Col (7,800 ft), the southwest slopes and chutes of Tricouni give a moderate snow-and-rock scramble to the summit ridge, with short sections of loose Class 4 rock near the top. It is almost always done in conjunction with other McAllister-group peaks rather than as a dedicated objective, and the approach itself (glaciated, multi-day) is the crux of the trip rather than the summit scramble.
routes.approach: From the Eldorado Trailhead on Cascade River Road (near milepost 20, outside Marblemount), follow a steep bootpack through forest, cross Eldorado Creek on a log, and climb talus, slabs, and heather benches to a high camp on the edge of the Eldorado Glacier (~7,400 ft; roughly 4 miles and 5,300 ft of gain from the trailhead). From camp, rope up and travel north across the Inspiration Glacier past a notch on Eldorado's lower east ridge (~7,500 ft), continuing about 1.5 miles to Klawatti Col (7,800 ft) — the common high camp/base used to climb Klawatti, Primus, Austera, Tricouni, and Dorado Needle.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the southwest slopes/chutes back to Klawatti Col.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same southwest chutes and slopes used on the ascent back to Klawatti Col, taking care on loose rock and any firm or icy snow. From the col, reverse the glacier travel back across the Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier to high camp and down the approach trail to the Eldorado Trailhead, watching for softening snow bridges and increasing crevasse hazard as the day warms.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike/climb the Eldorado Creek approach to a high camp on the Eldorado Glacier (~7,400 ft). Day 2: rope up and cross the Inspiration Glacier to Klawatti Col (7,800 ft), establish camp, and climb Tricouni's southwest slopes as a same-day side excursion (often combined with Klawatti and/or Primus). Day 3+: continue on to Austera Peak and Dorado Needle, or reverse the approach to exit, depending on whether the trip is a dedicated Tricouni objective or part of a longer traverse.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same southwest slopes back to Klawatti Col, then reverse the Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier approach to the Eldorado Trailhead. Watch for softening snow bridges and rising crevasse hazard on the glacier as the day warms.
routes.turnaround: Start early and plan to be off the glaciated approach and summit slopes before afternoon warming increases crevasse and wet-snow hazard and before the North Cascades' typical afternoon cloud/thunderstorm buildup; turn back if visibility drops during the glacier crossing.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season for this glacier approach is roughly July through August, once Cascade River Road is open and the Inspiration/Klawatti Glacier has good snow coverage. Early July typically has snow bridges more completely filling crevasses and moats near the col; by late August the glacier can be more broken and icy, requiring more careful route-finding or ice travel skills. The peak's other (north-side, Thunder Creek) approach has also been climbed/skied in April on firm spring snow, but the Klawatti Col line is a summer glacier-traverse objective.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable to absent in this remote, glaciated section of North Cascades National Park; the NPS advises not to rely on cell phones as an evacuation plan.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: a rope (30-60m) for the team, 2-3 snow pickets, and crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks, pulley, a screw or two). A light rack of small cams and nuts is sometimes carried for the short Class 4 rock near the summit ridge, though many parties solo that scrambling unroped.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal on the peak itself — the crux is short, low-Class-4 scrambling that most parties climb unroped. The real protection concern is crevasse-rescue gear for the Inspiration/Klawatti Glacier crossing, plus a picket or two for any firm snow below the col.
gear: Light rack of small cams/nuts (optional, for the Class 4 section)
whatToBring: Approved food storage (bear canister/hang per NPS backcountry zone rules)
proTips: Tricouni is most efficiently combined with Primus, Austera, Klawatti, and/or Dorado Needle from a single high camp at or near Klawatti Col.

Early July typically has more complete snow coverage over crevasses and moats on the Inspiration/Klawatti Glacier than late August, when the same terrain can require travel on harder, more exposed ice.
Rope up for the glacier crossing even where it looks straightforward — parties have found deceptively thin, unsupportive snow bridges/snow fingers near the col that require a rock detour.
watchOut: Crevasse hazard on the Inspiration and North Klawatti Glaciers, worsening later in the season as snow bridges deteriorate.
Loose, blocky rock in the southwest-facing chutes leading to the summit ridge.
Afternoon cloud and thunderstorm buildup is common in this part of the range; plan an early start.
Cell coverage is unreliable in this remote part of the park.
knownHazards: The Inspiration/Klawatti Glacier crossing en route to Klawatti Col has active crevasses; rope up for glacier travel.
A thin, unsupportive snow finger has been encountered on the approach near Klawatti Col in some seasons, forcing parties onto a loose-rock detour.
Loose rock is present in the southwest chutes and gullies below the summit ridge.
Afternoon cloud buildup and thunderstorms are common in this part of the North Cascades in summer.

pitchDetail.notes: Eldorado Trailhead to high camp on the Eldorado Glacier: ~4 mi, ~5,300 ft gain on steep forest bootpack, talus, slabs, and heather.
High camp across the Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier, past a notch on Eldorado's lower east ridge, to Klawatti Col (7,800 ft): ~1.5 mi, roped travel.
Klawatti Col up the southwest slopes/chutes to the summit ridge and top; short sections of loose Class 4 rock near the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Gravel; rougher and narrower toward its end near the Eldorado Trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated for winter/spring near milepost 20 (Eldorado Creek) and reopened once snow and washout repairs allow, usually early-mid summer
road.driveNote: Reached from Marblemount; trailhead parking is at/near milepost 20 (Eldorado Trailhead) on Cascade River Road

climate.typical: Interior North Cascades alpine climate: cold, glaciated, and prone to fast-moving weather; summer days can be clear and hot at treeline but build afternoon cloud and thunderstorms over the high peaks.

bySeason.spring: Firm spring snow can allow ski mountaineering/snow-climb ascents (documented via the alternate north-side approach in April), but glacier crevasses are typically well bridged and access roads may still be gated.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Aug); Cascade River Road open, glacier travel to Klawatti Col most straightforward, though crevasses open up progressively through August.
bySeason.fall: A shorter, less-documented window before snow returns and roads close; conditions become icier and more broken on the glacier.
