bySeason.winter: Not a climbing season for this route — Cascade River Road is gated and glacier/avalanche hazard is severe.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS), within the Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: No park entrance fee; overnight backcountry permit costs $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.permit: Overnight backcountry/climbing permit required, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (or via recreation.gov reservation)
access.passRequired: A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass may be required for trailhead parking on Cascade River Road
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated seasonally (winter/spring) near Eldorado Creek at roughly milepost 20, and has been subject to additional closures from washouts; check current NPS road status before driving up

timing.recommendedStart: Early/alpine start recommended to cross the glaciated approach while the snow is still firm

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Eldorado Trailhead to Eldorado Glacier high camp
sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 mi, ~5,300 ft gain
~1.5 mi across the Inspiration/Eldorado Glacier to the 7,800 ft col; roped travel
Class 4 southwest slopes/chutes, typically climbed as a side-summit from camp

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff dispatch: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — 360-424-4111 (also PeaceHealth United General, Sedro-Woolley — 360-856-6021)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in this remote area per NPS guidance; do not rely on cell phones alone for emergency communication — carry a satellite communicator if possible.

routes.corrections: Elevation figures vary by source: USGS/Wikipedia list 8,102 ft, LiDAR-based listsofjohn.com lists 8,106 ft, and the given app data lists 8,105 ft — differences are within normal survey/DEM variance and no single figure is clearly authoritative. The documented 1951 first ascent approached via a traverse of the Inspiration Glacier, which matches this Klawatti Col/southwest side of the peak rather than the alternate north-side (Thunder Creek/Lucky Pass/West Ridge) route that most modern trip reports describe as the 'standard' route — but no source explicitly labels the 1951 climb a first ascent of a named 'Southwest Slopes' line, so this is a reasonable but not certain match. gainFt/lossFt/distKm reflect the shared approach corridor to Klawatti Col (the common camp used for Tricouni and neighboring peaks), not a Tricouni-specific surveyed route distance.

blurb: Little Tahoma Peak (11,138 ft) is a jagged, andesitic remnant perched on the east shoulder of Mount Rainier within Mount Rainier National Park, and is independently Washington's third-highest summit. Built of loose, crumbly volcanic lava eroded from an ancestral, larger Rainier, its unstable rock produced one of the largest recorded Cascade rockslides in December 1963 (roughly 14 million cubic yards onto the Emmons Glacier). The peak was first climbed August 29, 1894 by J.B. Flett and Henry H. Garrison via the East Shoulder from Summerland, the line that remains the standard route today.

routes.overview: Little Tahoma's original 1894 line and standard route: a roped glacier ascent up the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers to a gully on the peak's east side, finishing with an exposed Class 3-4 scramble on loose andesite to the summit notch.
routes.beta: From a high camp near Meany Crest above Summerland, rope up and cross the Fryingpan Glacier on a bearing of roughly 260 degrees toward the notch between the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers (about 9,000 ft), pass through the notch and contour southwest around the first rock outcrop, then continue up the Whitman Glacier. Exit the glacier at its upper left-hand corner into a gully, climb about two-thirds of the gully to a 6-ft step at a headwall, surmount the step, and ascend a gully-chimney to the summit notch. A short (about 30-ft), loose and exposed scramble along the ridge crest reaches the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Fryingpan Creek Trailhead on the White River/Sunrise Road (about 3 miles past the White River Entrance), hike the Wonderland Trail roughly 4.2 miles and 2,100 ft to Summerland (about 5,900 ft). Most parties continue off-trail up talus and snow to a high camp near Meany Crest, a large rock outcrop in the cliff band directly above Summerland cited variously at 7,000-7,573 ft.
routes.descent: Descend the East Shoulder route in reverse: down-climb the summit scramble and gully, then retrace the roped Whitman/Fryingpan glacier crossing back to the Meany Crest camp and out the Summerland trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the exposed ridge scramble and downclimb the gully-chimney and headwall step back onto the upper Whitman Glacier. Rope up and retrace the ascent route through the notch near 9,000 ft, then descend the Fryingpan Glacier on a reciprocal bearing back toward the Meany Crest high camp. From camp, descend past Summerland and hike out the Wonderland Trail to the Fryingpan Creek Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Fryingpan Creek Trailhead to a high camp near Meany Crest/Meany Snowfield (about 4.5 mi, 3,600 ft gain). Day 2: Alpine start, climb the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers to the summit, then descend back to camp. Day 3: Hike out from camp/Summerland to the trailhead.
routes.bail: On the glacier sections, retreat by reversing the rope team's ascent track back toward Meany Crest camp. Above the bergschrund and in the summit gully there are no fixed rappel anchors on the standard line, so downclimbing the ascent route is generally the only retreat option.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on current snow and rockfall conditions and party pace, and commit to it — the Fryingpan/Whitman glaciers and the summit gully become more hazardous as the day warms, softening snow bridges and increasing rockfall.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions are generally early-to-mid summer (July into early August), when the glacier is still consolidated enough to bridge crevasses safely but before extended melt-out increases rockfall and opens the bergschrund. Conditions deteriorate through late August and September as the route becomes more broken, icier, and looser underfoot.
routes.fa: J.B. Flett and Henry H. Garrison, August 29, 1894, via Summerland and the East Shoulder
knownHazards: Rockfall is the primary objective hazard on this route, driven by the peak's notoriously loose, crumbly andesitic geology.
In December 1963 a massive rockslide (roughly 14 million cubic yards) released from Little Tahoma's north side onto the Emmons Glacier, one of the largest recorded rockslides in the Cascades.
Roped glacier travel on the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers exposes parties to crevasse-fall risk that increases through the season.
watchOut: Serious rockfall funnels down from the upper Fryingpan Glacier through the summit gully; helmets are essential well before technical terrain begins.
Crevasse hazard on the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers grows later in the season as snow bridges deteriorate.
The final summit scramble is exposed, unprotected Class 3-4 climbing on friable andesite.
proTips: Start early to cross the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers on firm, refrozen snow before afternoon sun softens crevasse bridges.
Camping at Meany Crest rather than Summerland shortens the glacier day and puts you closer to the route.
Keep party size and time spent under the gully/headwall minimal given the persistent rockfall hazard there.
routes.detailedRack: A single rope per rope team (roughly 30-50m) for glacier travel, a couple of pickets for belays/anchors on the steeper snow of the Whitman Glacier, and a personal crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks, pulley, spare carabiners/slings). No technical rock rack is standard, since the summit scramble is climbed unroped on loose, unprotectable rock.
routes.proNeeds: Full glacier-travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse-rescue gear) plus a few pickets; helmets are essential given persistent rockfall. No rock protection is typically carried, as the summit block is climbed as an unprotected scramble.
whatToBring: Extra water/filter (glacier meltwater available near high camp)

road.name: Sunrise Road / White River Road (off SR 410)
road.status: Seasonal — generally open late June/early July through late September or mid-October, snow-dependent
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed for winter with the first heavy snowfall, typically mid-October; reopens in early summer as plowing allows
road.driveNote: From the White River Entrance, drive about 3 miles toward Sunrise to the Fryingpan Creek Bridge; trailhead parking is on the north side of the road just past the bridge.

climate.typical: Highly changeable alpine/glacier weather typical of Mount Rainier's east side; conditions can shift rapidly, and afternoon convective storms and high winds are possible in summer.

bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep), but still exposed to sudden weather changes and afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Route becomes more broken and icy as snow bridges deteriorate and the bergschrund opens further; road access also becomes weather-dependent.

access.landManager: National Park Service — Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: Climbing registration fee of $82 per person (required for travel above roughly 10,000 ft or onto glaciers); wilderness/backcountry camping permit $12 per person per night (summer season).
access.permit: Climbing registration plus a wilderness permit are required; reservations are made via Recreation.gov, with in-person registration at a Wilderness Information Center required within 24 hours of the climb during the summer season.
access.closures: Access via White River/Sunrise Road is seasonal and generally unavailable in winter (roughly mid-October to late June), independent of any climbing-specific closures.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine (predawn) start for the summit push, to cross the glaciers while snow and crevasse bridges are still firm.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Fryingpan Creek TH (3,800 ft) to Meany Crest/Snowfield camp (~7,350 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 4.5 miles via the Wonderland Trail through Summerland

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: High camp to Little Tahoma summit (11,138 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Fryingpan Glacier, Whitman notch (~9,100 ft), and Whitman Glacier
Descent, reversing the ascent route

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: High camp/Summerland to Fryingpan Creek TH
sectionBreakdown.note: Hike out

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mount Rainier NP dispatch (360) 569-2211, then prompt 0, during park operating hours; after-hours routed to Lewis County Sheriff (360) 740-1105, then prompt 0
emergency.rangerStation: White River Wilderness Information Center, Mount Rainier National Park
emergency.nearestHospital: St. Elizabeth Hospital, Enumclaw, WA (via SR 410)
emergency.notes: Climbers must register their itinerary; if overdue past the stated return time, park rangers automatically initiate a search.

routes.corrections: Multiple reputable sources (The Mountaineers, Mountain Project, and guidebook-derived route pages) consistently describe this route's NCCS commitment grade as Grade II+, not the Grade III given in the input — recorded here as 'commitment': 'II+'. No source found corroborates the input's alpine (French-system) grade 'PD+' for this specific route, so 'alpineGrade' is left null rather than repeating an unverified figure. The input's 'Class 4' is broadly consistent with sourced descriptions of a Class 3-4 summit scramble.

blurb: Liberty Cap (14,112 ft) is one of three named summits on the broad, glaciated crater rim of Mount Rainier, a large andesite stratovolcano in the Washington Cascades. It sits at the northwestern edge of the summit plateau, overlooking the Sunset Amphitheater, Willis Wall, and Liberty Ridge; with only about 138 ft of prominence it is not considered an independent peak, but it is the summit objective of the classic Liberty Ridge route, first climbed by Ome Daiber, Arnie Campbell, and Jim Borrow over September 28-October 1, 1935 — a line later enshrined in Steve Roper and Allen Steck's "Fifty Classic Climbs of North America."

routes.fa: Ome Daiber, Arnie Campbell, and Jim Borrow, September 28 - October 1, 1935 (first ascent of Mount Rainier by way of Liberty Ridge on Willis Wall)
objHaz: Serac fall from the active Liberty Cap ice-cliff complex overhanging the upper route (largely unavoidable)
Rockfall on the loose lower ridge, worsening once the sun hits it
Crevasse falls on the Carbon and Winthrop Glacier traverses
Avalanche hazard on the approach and lower route
Sudden high winds and severe weather at altitude
routes.face: North Face, between Willis Wall and Liberty Wall
routes.overview: Liberty Ridge is the prominent rock-and-ice ridge splitting the Willis Wall and Liberty Wall on Mount Rainier's remote, glaciated north face, climbing directly from the Carbon Glacier to Liberty Cap. It is one of Fifty Classic Climbs of North America and is widely regarded as the mountain's most serious standard route.
routes.beta: From a camp at Thumb Rock the route follows the crest of Liberty Ridge through mixed rock/snow terrain and the Black Pyramid, then a sustained steep-ice headwall (roughly 800+ ft in two blocks, up to 55-60 degrees) and a vertical ice bergschrund crux (about 25-30 ft) below the final summit slopes to Liberty Cap. Almost all parties descend via the Emmons-Winthrop route to Camp Schurman rather than reversing the ridge.
routes.approach: From White River Campground (~4,300-4,400 ft) hike the Glacier Basin Trail about 3.5 mi, cross the river, and climb to St. Elmo Pass (~7,500 ft); descend and cross the Winthrop Glacier (~7,300 ft), traverse below Curtis Ridge to a camp near 7,200-7,500 ft, then drop onto and ascend the Carbon Glacier to the base of Liberty Ridge and climb to a camp at Thumb Rock (~10,775-10,800 ft).
routes.descent: Emmons-Winthrop Glacier route to Camp Schurman, then down the Inter Glacier/Glacier Basin Trail to White River; no rappels required on a standard descent.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap, traverse the upper Winthrop Glacier beneath Columbia Crest, staying high (above roughly 13,600 ft) to avoid large crevasses, then join the standard Emmons-Winthrop boot pack down to Camp Schurman. From Camp Schurman, continue down the Emmons and Inter Glaciers to Glacier Basin and out the Glacier Basin Trail to White River Campground. Many parties spend a second night at Camp Schurman rather than walking all the way out.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-4 day plan: Day 1 - White River TH to Curtis Ridge camp (~7,200-7,500 ft); Day 2 - Curtis Ridge to Thumb Rock camp (~10,775-10,800 ft); Day 3 - Thumb Rock to the summit via Liberty Cap, descending to Camp Schurman; Day 4 (if needed) - Camp Schurman to White River TH. Very strong parties in perfect conditions have compressed this to 2 days.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once committed above Thumb Rock; downclimbing the upper ridge is generally not advised. Below the Black Pyramid/bergschrund, parties can descend the way they came, but above that point the safest bail is usually to continue to Liberty Cap and descend the standard Emmons-Winthrop route rather than reverse the technical ridge.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit push and honor it regardless of proximity to the top. Afternoon warming sharply increases rockfall, icefall, and serac hazard on this north-facing route, and deteriorating weather removes the option of a safe retreat down the ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through July is the narrow prime window, once the ridge has enough snow/ice cover to bury loose rock but before summer melt widens the bergschrund and opens crevasses. Route conditions are highly year-to-year dependent and it is rarely in good shape outside this window; by August the bergschrund often loses its snow bridge and parties must traverse hard climber's-right to end-run it via the Liberty Saddle.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the route above White River Campground; a satellite communicator (inReach/SPOT) is strongly recommended. NPS climbing rangers can be reached by radio in emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit (harness, crevasse-rescue gear, crampons, two ice tools per climber) plus a lean alpine ice rack: a handful of ice screws (roughly 3-6), 2-3 pickets, a few slings/draws, and a single 60m rope (about 7.7-8mm) shared by the rope team. Many parties add a mini-traxion or similar device per climber for efficient protection while simul-climbing the ice sections.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is light and ice-focused: a handful of screws for the bergschrund/headwall crux and steep ice bulges, plus pickets on the snow sections. Rock gear is generally not carried since the lower ridge is loose and typically scrambled with minimal protection.
gear: mini-traxion or similar (optional, for simul-climbing protection)
whatToBring: Stove and extra fuel for melting snow over 2-4 days
Glacier goggles/sunglasses and high-SPF sun protection
Extra insulation for high winds and possible storm days
proTips: Start the summit push by midnight-2am to be off the upper ice and past the bergschrund before afternoon warming.
Camp at Thumb Rock itself, not below it, to minimize exposure to rockfall/icefall from above.
Carry a satellite communicator; there is no cell coverage on the route.

Check current NPS climbing-ranger conditions reports before committing — ridge conditions vary drastically year to year and route difficulty/danger scale with how much snow/ice cover the loose lower rock.
watchOut: Serac fall from the Liberty Cap ice cliff is the route's primary objective hazard and cannot be fully mitigated.
Rockfall on the lower ridge's poor-quality rock increases sharply once the sun hits it.
Crevasse falls have occurred on the Winthrop Glacier traverse to and from Liberty Cap.
Once committed above the Black Pyramid, retreat down the ridge is very difficult.
knownHazards: Liberty Cap's active serac band overhangs much of the upper route and cannot be fully avoided.
Although fewer than 2% of Rainier summit climbers attempt Liberty Ridge, it accounts for roughly a quarter of the mountain's climbing deaths.
Loose, poor-quality rock on the lower ridge makes rockfall a hazard for the entire rope team.
Large, sometimes-hidden crevasses on the Carbon and Winthrop Glaciers have caused serious falls.

pitchDetail.notes: Loose rock and snow; poor rock quality, minimal protection typically placed.
Alternating snow and rock terrain, generally simul-climbed.

pitchDetail.pitch: Upper ice headwall (above the Black Pyramid)
pitchDetail.notes: Around 800+ ft of sustained steep ice in two blocks, often protected with running belays/screws.
Usually the technical crux of the route; commonly pitched out. By late season the snow bridge can disappear, forcing an end-run traverse to Liberty Saddle.
Final slopes leading to the Liberty Cap summit at 14,112 ft.

road.name: White River Road (Sunrise Road), off WA-410
road.status: Seasonal, open to vehicles in summer only
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens late May-late June and closes mid-October, weather/snowpack dependent; in shoulder season the road can close nightly and reopen mornings as conditions allow
road.driveNote: Paved road from the White River Entrance to White River Campground/trailhead within Mount Rainier National Park.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC issues avalanche forecasts only for terrain below 8,000 ft and only late November-mid April; there is no avalanche forecast for the upper mountain/technical route itself. Climbers rely on NPS climbing-ranger conditions updates and point mountain-weather forecasts.
climate.typical: Cold, heavily glaciated north-side alpine climate; brief stable-weather windows are common in late June-July before afternoon convection and glacial melt increase objective hazard. High winds and rapid weather changes are common above 12,000 ft.

bySeason.spring: Late spring (May-early June) can offer more consolidated snow/ice but colder temps, shorter daylight, and the route may not yet be fully formed.
bySeason.summer: Late June-July is prime season; by August, accelerated melt opens the bergschrund and crevasses and increases rockfall/icefall, forcing longer, more circuitous route-finding.
bySeason.fall: Rarely climbed after August; increasing storms, freeze-thaw instability, and road closures by late September-October end the season.
bySeason.winter: Essentially inaccessible; access road closed, with extreme cold, avalanche, and serac hazard — only rare winter ascents are attempted by expert parties.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: $82/person annual climbing cost-recovery fee plus a $6 non-refundable permit/reservation fee; $12/person/night wilderness camping fee (May 1-Sep 15)
access.permit: Climbing registration required (self-register via pay.gov Sept 15-May 21, or in person at a wilderness information station within 24 hours of the climb May 22-Sep 30); a wilderness/glacier camping permit is also required for overnight stays and travel above roughly 10,000 ft. Solo climbers need a separate NPS solo-climbing authorization (fewer than half of applicants approved).
access.passRequired: A park entrance pass/fee (e.g. America the Beautiful) covers vehicle entry but does not cover the separate climbing fee.
access.closures: White River/Sunrise Road is closed to vehicles roughly mid-October through late May/June depending on snowpack.

timing.recommendedStart: Midnight-2am alpine start for the summit day, to clear the upper ice and bergschrund before afternoon sun loosens rock and ice

sectionBreakdown.note: Fast-party time from a documented climb; most rope teams take considerably longer through the Black Pyramid, upper ice, and bergschrund.
