waypoints.name: White River Campground / Glacier Basin Trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Pierce County Sheriff's Dispatch: (253) 287-4455
emergency.rangerStation: White River Wilderness Information Center: (360) 569-6670; Mount Rainier climbing rangers: (360) 569-6641; park main line: (360) 569-2211
emergency.nearestHospital: Harborview Medical Center, Seattle (Level I trauma) via Airlift Northwest helicopter evacuation for serious injuries; MultiCare Good Samaritan Hospital, Puyallup, for ground-accessible cases
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage on most of the route; NPS climbing rangers monitor radio traffic. Register your climbing plan and carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevationFt (14,097 ft) differs from the figure widely cited by Wikipedia/USGS-derived sources (14,112 ft / 4,301 m); used 14,112 ft for highPointFt. Given iceGrade (AI3) was adjusted to AI3+ to match multiple sourced descriptions of sustained 55-60 degree ice above the Black Pyramid and a ~25-30 ft vertical bergschrund crux; several sources also describe the route's commitment/technical grade as 'IV' (occasionally cited up to V in poor conditions), consistent with the given 'Grade IV-V.'

blurb: Point Success (14,158 ft) is the second-highest of Mount Rainier's three named summits, sitting at the south edge of the summit ice cap atop the Success Cleaver ridge, within Mount Rainier National Park's andesite stratovolcano in the Cascade Range. It was the point first reached — and briefly mistaken for the true summit — by Hazard Stevens and P. B. Van Trump on August 17, 1870 during the first recorded ascent of Rainier, though their route climbed via Gibraltar Ledges, not the Success Cleaver line described below. Success Cleaver itself is a 3.3-mile rock ridge dividing the South Tahoma Glacier from the Success and Pyramid Glaciers, and offers one of the only ways up Rainier that avoids significant glacier travel for most of its length.

routes.face: Success Cleaver (SW ridge of Mount Rainier)
routes.overview: Success Cleaver is a long, remote line up Mount Rainier's southwest side, following the 3.3-mile rock ridge that separates the South Tahoma Glacier from the Success and Pyramid Glaciers to gain Point Success (14,158 ft) near the summit plateau. It is one of the only Rainier routes climbable without significant glacier travel for most of its length, finishing on steep snow/ice and a short rock band below Point Success.
routes.beta: From a base camp near Pyramid Peak, the route follows the crest and flanking gullies of Success Cleaver over class 3 rock and steep snow in early season, or loose, unstable scree once snow-free later in summer. Above roughly 10,700 ft, snow gullies lead up and right to merge with the Kautz Cleaver near 12,200-12,600 ft, from which a final short, difficult rock band (low class 5) or a steep snow/ice pitch (up to about 55 degrees) gains Point Success. Most parties take 2-4 days round trip given the long approach and over 11,500 ft of total elevation gain.
routes.approach: From the Longmire Wilderness Information Center (2,761 ft), follow the Wonderland Trail, climbing over the Rampart (~1,100 ft gain) and dropping about 300 ft to cross Kautz Creek, then continuing to Indian Henry's Hunting Ground (about 10 miles, ~4,100 ft gain from Longmire). From there take the Mirror Lakes trail toward Pyramid Peak and gain the Success Cleaver proper at the saddle north of Pyramid Peak (~6,500 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the Success Cleaver ridge and its flanking snow gullies back to the Pyramid Peak saddle and down the Wonderland Trail to Longmire.
routes.descentText: Descent generally reverses the ascent line down the cleaver crest and adjacent snow/scree gullies. Some parties instead continue over the summit and descend via the Disappointment Cleaver or Kautz Glacier routes to Camp Muir or Paradise if conditions and logistics allow, but this exits at a different trailhead and requires a car shuttle or road walk back to Longmire.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan is 2-4 days: Day 1, hike from Longmire via the Wonderland Trail to a camp near treeline below Pyramid Peak (~6,300-6,500 ft); Day 2, ascend Success Cleaver to a high camp around 9,400-10,700 ft on the cleaver crest; Day 3 (summit day), alpine start up the remaining cleaver and snow gullies to Point Success and the true summit, then descend; an extra day is often reserved as a weather/contingency buffer.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally back down the cleaver crest the way you came; there is no quick bail into a different drainage once established on the ridge, and downclimbing loose class 3-4 rock or soft snow gullies in deteriorating weather is the main retreat hazard.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, sustained elevation gain, and loose rock/scree that becomes more hazardous later in the day or in bad weather, set a turnaround time before the summit push and hold to it regardless of proximity to Point Success; deteriorating weather, active rockfall, or softening/unstable snow on the upper gullies are standard reasons to retreat.
routes.bestSeason: Most sources recommend an early-season attempt (roughly May-June) while snowfields still cover the steep, loose scree sections of the cleaver, giving more secure travel. By later summer the route becomes mostly bare rock with unpleasant, unstable scree traverses near the 14,000-ft level.
routes.detailedRack: Standard Rainier glacier/alpine rack: rope, harness with belay device and crevasse-rescue gear (prusiks/pulleys), crampons, and an ice axe; a second tool has been noted as useful on the steeper snow/ice sections (up to ~45-55 degrees) below Point Success. Snow pickets for the upper snowfields and a helmet for the loose rock sections are standard.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly snow pickets on the upper gullies and standard glacier-travel gear near the Kautz Cleaver junction; the class 3-4 rock sections are typically soloed with little to no fixed protection reported.
whatToBring: Second ice tool for steep snow/ice sections
Trekking poles for the long approach
Water treatment (limited water sources higher on the route)
Insect repellent for the lower forested approach
proTips: Climb early season (roughly May-June) while snow still covers the scree so travel is faster and more secure.
Established bivy sites exist on the cleaver around 8,100-8,300 ft, 9,400 ft, and 10,700 ft.
A second ice tool helps on the steeper (up to ~45-55 degree) snow/ice near the top.
Watch for recent point-release avalanche activity on loaded upper snow slopes before committing to traverses.
watchOut: Loose, unstable scree on the lower-to-mid cleaver once snow has melted out.
Heat and insects at lower elevations on the approach; bug activity drops off above about 7,000 ft.
Exposed knife-edge sections of the ridge crest.
Icefall/serac activity from the adjacent South Tahoma Glacier has been observed from the route.
knownHazards: Rockfall on the loose scree traverses, especially later in the season once snow cover is gone.
Avalanche danger on loaded snow slopes and traverses on the upper cleaver.
Icefall and serac collapse from the neighboring South Tahoma Glacier, documented by climbers on the route.
Crevasses where the route joins glacier terrain near the Kautz Cleaver/upper mountain.

pitchDetail.notes: Rock ridge crest and gullies; scree/choss when snow-free.
Steep chutes and rock bands; bivy sites near 8,100-8,300 ft, 9,400 ft and 10,700 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Upper chutes to Kautz Cleaver (10,700-12,600 ft)
pitchDetail.notes: Step-kicking up snow/ice gullies merging onto the Kautz Cleaver near 12,200-12,600 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Final headwall to Point Success (12,600-14,158 ft)
pitchDetail.grade: Low Class 5 rock or ~55 deg snow/ice
pitchDetail.notes: A short difficult rock band, or a steep snow/ice pitch (~15 ft at up to 55 degrees) bypasses cliffs below Point Success.

road.name: Longmire-Paradise Road (via Nisqually Entrance)
road.status: Open (per NPS road status, checked July 2026)
road.seasonalGate: Longmire-Paradise road can close nightly in winter (Nov 1 onward) depending on snow removal; tire chains required Nov 1-May 1 regardless of vehicle drivetrain.
road.driveNote: Access via the Nisqually (southwest) entrance to Longmire (2,761 ft), the trailhead for the Wonderland Trail approach to Success Cleaver.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier area / West Slopes South Cascades (NWS zone); NWAC covers regional avalanche conditions but has no dedicated Success Cleaver zone
climate.typical: Mount Rainier generates its own severe local weather and receives some of the heaviest annual snowfall totals in the US; conditions on the southwest (Success Cleaver) side are less monitored than the Paradise/Camp Muir side.

bySeason.spring: Cool and often stormy, but with the best snow coverage over the scree — generally the recommended season for this route.
bySeason.summer: Warmer and drier by August, but the cleaver becomes mostly bare, loose rock with unstable scree traverses.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency after early-to-mid September.
bySeason.winter: Not a standard season for this route; extreme cold, storms, and elevated avalanche hazard.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: Park entrance fee plus an $82/person climbing registration fee for travel on glaciers or above roughly 10,000 ft; a $12/person/night wilderness camping permit fee applies May 1-Sept 15.
access.permit: Climbing permit required for glacier travel or climbing above ~10,000 ft (self-registration via Pay.gov in winter, walk-up registration at wilderness stations in summer); a wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay, even for single-push climbers.
access.passRequired: NPS park entrance pass/fee plus the separate climbing registration permit.
access.closures: Not specific to this route, but note Carbon River Road and Mowich Lake Road currently have no public vehicle access from SR 165.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from high camp; one reported party left camp at ~10,700 ft at 2:30 a.m. and reached Point Success by about 7:00 a.m.

sectionBreakdown.note: Via the Wonderland Trail, crossing Kautz Creek and passing Indian Henry's Hunting Ground.

sectionBreakdown.section: Camp to high camp on Success Cleaver
sectionBreakdown.note: Scrambling/snow travel on the cleaver crest; multiple established bivy sites en route.
One trip report: departed 2:30 a.m., reached Point Success ~7:00 a.m.

waypoints.name: Longmire Wonderland Trailhead (current alternate access; Kautz Creek Trail washout-closed)

emergency.county: Pierce County, Washington (Mount Rainier NP headquarters and the Longmire/southwest side of the mountain)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for life-threatening emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NP Dispatch: 360-569-6600 (0700-2000); Puget Sound Interagency Coordinating Center: 425-783-6150 (24-hour); Longmire Wilderness Information Center for permits/trip planning.
emergency.notes: This is a remote, lightly-traveled route on the park's southwest side, away from the ranger presence concentrated on the Paradise/Camp Muir side; expect longer self-rescue times and response windows.

routes.corrections: Given elevation (14,147 ft) does not match reputable sources (Wikipedia, ListsOfJohn, PeakVisor, SummitPost), which consistently list Point Success at 14,158 ft; highPointFt corrected to 14,158 accordingly (a separate GNIS/topo-derived figure of 14,104 ft also appears in some indexes but is contradicted by the majority of current sources). The input's alpineGrade ("AD") and "Grade III-IV, Class 4" rating could not be independently confirmed by any source found in this research and are left null rather than repeated unverified; sources instead describe the route qualitatively as class 3 rock/steep snow lower down and low class 5 on a short final rock band. No source distinguishes a specific first-ascent party/year for the Success Cleaver line itself, so 'fa' is left null — the well-documented 1870 Stevens/Van Trump first ascent of Mount Rainier passed near/over Point Success but via the Gibraltar Ledges route, not Success Cleaver.

blurb: Colchuck Balanced Rock (8,240 ft, sometimes given as 8,239 ft) is a granodiorite spire in the Stuart Range of the Enchantments, within Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, rising above Colchuck Lake between Dragontail Peak and Jabberwocky Tower. Its steep, crack-split west face hosts several of the North Cascades' best moderate-to-hard multi-pitch alpine rock climbs, all finishing with an exposed boulder-problem move to stand on the summit's distinctive nested capstone. The peak was first climbed via its easiest line (Northwest Ridge, 5.6) in 1958 by Gene Prater and Thomas Quin.

routes.overview: The West Face is the striking, crack-laced prow of Colchuck Balanced Rock's summit block: roughly 850' of sustained corner and crack climbing up the peak's west aspect, capped by a short, wildly exposed boulder-problem finish to stand on the balanced summit block itself.
routes.beta: Widely regarded as one of the best moderate-to-hard alpine multi-pitch rock routes in the North Cascades. Eight to nine pitches of sustained 5.8-5.11 crack, corner, and roof climbing (crux 5.11+/12- through a roof and boulder problem) lead to a long, easier finishing pitch and a final V1 boulder move onto the summit block. Long days are typical: logged car-to-car times run about 14.5-15.5 hours.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (end of FR-7601 off Icicle Creek Road), hike the Colchuck Lake Trail roughly 4 miles and 2,280' of gain to Colchuck Lake (about 3-4 hours). From the lake's outlet/north end, either (1) cross the outlet and follow a climbers' path up a timbered hillside into the cirque below the West Face (about 1 hour), or (2) follow the trail around the lake and scramble up the gully above Jabberwocky Tower toward the notch (about 1.5-2 hours). The final scramble to the base is easy but notably loose; many parties rope up for it.
routes.descent: One roughly 30m rappel east from a slung horn near the top, then a scree/snow scramble rightward (clockwise around the peak) back down to the base of the climb.
routes.descentText: From the summit block, downclimb/scramble to a slung horn and make a single ~30m rappel to the east. From the rappel's end, scramble down sandy scree (or snow, depending on season) rightward/clockwise around the formation to regain the base of the route, then reverse the approach back to Colchuck Lake and the trailhead. No fixed multi-rappel line exists down the face itself.
routes.itinerary: Most parties do the route car-to-car in one very long day from the trailhead (documented totals of about 14.5-15.5 hours), starting well before dawn. Some instead backpack in and camp at Colchuck Lake (permit required in season) to shorten the summit-day push.
routes.bail: There are no fixed rappel anchors down the route line itself. Low on the route, retreat is by building gear anchors and rappelling/downclimbing back toward the base; above the mid-face roof pitches, continuing to the summit and using the single established rappel + scree descent is generally more practical than reversing the upper pitches.
routes.turnaround: Given documented car-to-car days of roughly 14.5-15.5 hours, set a firm turnaround point before starting the technical pitches so the descent scramble and long hike out are not done after dark or in worsening Cascades afternoon weather.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed in summer, after the FR-7601 access road (typically gated November-May) has opened and the approach gully has melted out; documented ascents span early June through early August. Snow can linger in the approach gully into June in higher-snow years.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack from small finger-size cams up to about #4 Camalot (3.5"), per Mountain Project's stated protection range, with doubles through the finger-to-hand sizes recommended given the sustained splitter and dihedral crack pitches (P2 thin-hands, P4 fingers/thin-hands, P6 hand crack); a set of nuts for the thinner corners is also useful.
routes.proNeeds: Protection to about 3.5 inches (a #4 Camalot); doubling up finger-to-hand-size cams is worthwhile for the sustained crack pitches.
gear: Single rack of cams, fingers to #4 Camalot (3.5")
whatToBring: Headlamp (long car-to-car days can finish after dark)
Extra water/electrolytes for a hot, sun-exposed face
Bug repellent (mosquitoes reported near the lake/descent)
proTips: A single 60m rope is sufficient for the route and the descent rappel; no need to carry a second line.
Simul-climbing the long, easier finishing pitch to the summit ledge speeds up the day.
Rope up for the final approach scramble to the base — it's easy but loose.
Budget a full alpine day: documented car-to-car times run 14.5-15.5 hours.
watchOut: The approach scramble/gully to the base is loose rock.
One of the mid-route hand cracks is reported to run wet.
The final summit boulder problem is short but highly exposed with real fall consequence.
The west-facing wall gets intense sun exposure on hot summer afternoons.
Mosquitoes can be heavy near Colchuck Lake in early-to-mid summer.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the approach gully and final scramble to the base of the climb.
No fixed rappel line down the route — descent depends on a single rappel plus route-finding down scree/snow.
Exposed, unprotected boulder-problem move required to reach the true summit.

pitchDetail.notes: ~20m; a few steep moves off a broken ramp and past a tree to a ledge.
~15m; thin-hands splitter, then left around a pillar.
~35m; stem a thin, slabby corner to a treed ledge.
~50m; corner (5.10b right / 5.8 left option) to a finger-and-thin-hand crack in a dihedral.
~15m; traverse left under the huge roof.
~20m; hand crack in a corner, past a roof, and a crux boulder problem to a ledge — the route's technical crux.
~30m; flakes and jugs into a flaring chimney, then a hand crack on a slab — called the region's hardest '5.8'.
60m+; move right along a ledge and up toward the summit; often simul-climbed.
~3m exposed boulder problem to stand on the balanced summit block.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Creek Road from US 2 in Leavenworth)
road.status: Gravel/dirt, rough and sometimes potholed; passable to Stuart Lake Trailhead in a standard car with care
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed to vehicles November-May
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road ~8.4-8.5 miles, turn onto FR-7601 (via Eightmile Road/FR-7600 junction), then continue ~3.7-4 miles of rough dirt road to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Stevens Pass / East Cascades zone (nearest official avalanche/weather zone; relevant mainly for early/late-season or winter conditions)
climate.typical: Warm, dry alpine summers with occasional afternoon thunderstorms typical of the East Cascades; the access road and high approach terrain are snow-affected outside the summer season.

bySeason.spring: FR-7601 road and approach gully often still snow-covered; road usually reopens by May.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; hot, sun-exposed face climbing with possible afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Cooler, shorter days; climbable into early fall until snow returns.
bySeason.winter: Road gated; the West Face has seen at least one documented winter ascent (Jan 2012) via skis/bootpack and mixed snow/rock conditions.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District; Alpine Lakes Wilderness / The Enchantments
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30 annual Recreation Pass at the trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issued day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead; overnight camping (e.g., at Colchuck Lake) requires an advance-reservation Enchantment Permit Area permit, required May 15-October 31
access.passRequired: NW Forest/Recreation Pass for parking
access.closures: FR-7601 access road is gated seasonally each winter, typically closed November-May

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start

sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 mi one-way, 2,280 ft gain (WTA)

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Colchuck Lake → base of West Face
sectionBreakdown.note: climbers' path/gully scramble, ~1-2 hrs depending on option taken (StephAbegg, Mountain Project)
documented total car-to-car times of 14.5 and 15.4 hours (StephAbegg trip logs)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency: (509) 667-6851; RiverCom 911 non-emergency dispatch: (509) 663-9911; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826 — 24/7 ER, (509) 548-5807
emergency.notes: Chelan County Sheriff's Office (with volunteer Chelan County Mountain Rescue) is the primary SAR responder for the Colchuck Lake/Enchantments area; higher-level trauma care is transported to Wenatchee, Seattle, or Spokane.

routes.fa: Tom Boley and Jack Lewis, 1980 (per Mountain Project; not independently corroborated by a second source)
routes.rappels: 1 rappel (~30m) from a slung horn near the summit, then scree/snow scramble back to the base
routes.season: Jun-Aug (early Jun to early Aug documented; climbable into Sep)
routes.corrections: Given data listed 6 pitches; two independent sources (Mountain Project and StephAbegg.com) both document 9 pitches (including the summit boulder problem), so pitches was corrected to 9. Given rockGrade was '5.11' (route name also carries 'Grade III, 5.11'); Mountain Project rates the route 5.11+ overall with the true crux pitch reported as 5.11+/5.12- by StephAbegg.com, so rockGrade was corrected to '5.11+'. routeFt (850') is sourced from StephAbegg's pitch-by-pitch topo; the AAC's first-winter-ascent writeup separately describes the route as ~300m (~984'), a close but not identical figure, likely reflecting different measurement/rounding conventions. Peak elevation is given as 8,239 ft; Wikipedia and Mountain Project list 8,240 ft — treated as the same figure within rounding, not corrected.

objHaz: Steep snow on the Aasgard Pass approach into midsummer
Route-finding on unmarked ledges near the crux
routes.overview: The standard, most-climbed line up Witches Tower: a mostly Class 3-4 scramble up the south face from the col southwest of the summit, with one short, somewhat run-out 5.5 step guarding the true summit block.
routes.beta: From Aasgard Pass, parties head south to the col below Witches Tower (camping here or near the pass), then scramble the sharp SW ridge and traverse onto the south face. A short 5.5 move is the technical crux; above it, exposed but easy scrambling — including a short slabby ledge and a step across a small gap/slot near the top — leads to the summit. It's commonly linked with Dragontail Peak and/or Little Annapurna as part of a bigger Enchantments day or two-day trip.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (end of FR 7601 off Icicle Creek Rd, Leavenworth), hike the Lake Stuart Trail 2.5 mi, then go left on the Colchuck Lake Trail 1.8 mi to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft). Continue around the south end of the lake and climb the steep Aasgard Pass trail (about 2,000 ft in under a mile) to Aasgard Pass (7,841 ft). From the pass, head south toward Witches Tower and ascend snow or Class 2 rock to the col just southwest of the peak, the start of the climbing.
routes.descent: Downclimb/scramble the south face (Class 2-3) back to the SW col, then reverse the approach over Aasgard Pass to Colchuck Lake and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb back to the notch atop the crux, then descend the south face on Class 2-3 terrain to regain the SW col. From there, retrace the approach: down past Aasgard Pass (steep snow/scree — several trip reports describe the pass as more nerve-wracking on the way down than the climbing itself), around Colchuck Lake, and out the Colchuck Lake/Lake Stuart Trail to the Stuart Lake Trailhead. No fixed rappel stations are reported; a rope can be used to belay or lower the short crux step if needed.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in one long day (~14 mi RT, ~5,400 ft gain) from the Stuart Lake Trailhead, or spread over two days with a camp near Aasgard Pass/upper Enchantment Basin, climbing Witches Tower together with Dragontail Peak and/or Little Annapurna.
routes.bail: Retreat below the crux is straightforward: downclimb back to the col and reverse the approach. Above the 5.5 step, downclimb it face-in, or belay/lower a hesitant party member from natural gear.
routes.turnaround: Because the long, loose Aasgard Pass descent is significantly more hazardous after dark or in bad weather, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight and margin to get back over the pass before conditions deteriorate — parties that linger on the summit have been caught descending the pass at night.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September), once Aasgard Pass has melted out enough for straightforward snow/rock travel. The pass can hold firm, steep snow into July in heavy-snow years and warrants an ice axe. The access road (FR 7601) is gated in winter, and the Enchantment Permit Area's overnight quota (May 15-Oct 31) makes the approach trail very crowded on summer weekends and during fall larch season.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the short roped section can be sparse and awkward to find; The Mountaineers specifically note it helps for the leader to have experience seeking out placements. A light rack of small-to-medium cams/nuts and a single rope are sufficient for the short 5.5 step.
gear: Light rack: small-to-medium cams and nuts
Ice axe for Aasgard Pass snow travel
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use parking fee
Free self-issued wilderness day permit from the trailhead box
Microspikes/crampons if early-season snow lingers on Aasgard Pass
Sun protection — the approach and tower are largely sun-exposed
Satellite communicator (cell coverage is unreliable in the basin)
proTips: Often tackled as an add-on to a Dragontail Peak or Little Annapurna day since the approach is shared
A rope and small rack let less-experienced scramblers protect the exposure at the crux even though the climbing itself is easy
Aasgard Pass frequently holds firm snow into July — bring an ice axe (and consider crampons) even in nominal 'summer' conditions
watchOut: The crux 5.5 step and adjoining ledges are exposed — a fall would be serious despite the low grade
Protection on the lower section can be hard to place; route-finding for gear matters as much as climbing ability
Aasgard Pass itself (ascending and descending) is steep, loose, and often snow-covered — many parties find it the crux of the whole day
knownHazards: Steep, loose Class 3-4 scrambling terrain with limited fixed protection
Steep snow/scree on the Aasgard Pass approach and descent, sometimes requiring an ice axe into midsummer
Rockfall potential from other parties on the tower or in the Aasgard Pass gully

pitchDetail.notes: From Aasgard Pass, hike/traverse south toward the tower to the col just southwest of the summit; a common camp spot.
From the col, ascend the sharp ridge about 100 ft, then traverse a few feet right onto the South Face.
A short, somewhat exposed step-up move — the technical crux of the route.
Climb a short face and scramble to the summit, jumping a small (~4 ft) slot near the top.

road.name: FR 7601 (via Icicle Creek Rd / Eightmile Rd)
road.status: Seasonal gravel forest road, open roughly May-October
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed to vehicles in winter, typically November to May
road.driveNote: About 8.4 mi up paved Icicle Creek Rd from Leavenworth, then roughly 3.7-4 mi on rough, steep, washboarded FR 7601 to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; high-clearance recommended, parking is very limited.

climate.typical: Dry east-slope Cascades alpine climate: sunny, hot, sun-exposed approach with cold nights; Aasgard Pass can hold firm, steep snow well into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Road (FR 7601) and upper trail are typically still snow-covered or gated; not a normal season for this climb.
bySeason.summer: Prime season; residual snow can linger on Aasgard Pass into July in heavy-snow years, the lower approach is hot and sun-exposed, and trail traffic is very high (day hikers plus Enchantment permit holders).
bySeason.fall: Good conditions into September/early October; the Colchuck Lake approach gets extremely crowded during larch season (late Sept-early Oct), with increasing chance of early-season snow at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Access road closed; this becomes a winter mountaineering objective with avalanche exposure on Aasgard Pass, well outside the scope of the standard scramble/climb.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (USFS) - Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5 per vehicle/day or $30/year day-use parking fee at the trailhead; a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass is also accepted
access.permit: Free, self-issued day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead; overnight camping within the core Enchantment Permit Area requires a reserved permit (lottery, plus limited walk-up availability) from May 15 to October 31
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent federal recreation pass for trailhead parking
access.closures: FR 7601 access road is gated and closed to vehicles in winter, typically November to May

timing.recommendedStart: An alpine (pre-dawn) start is recommended so the long Aasgard Pass ascent and descent aren't rushed against darkness or afternoon weather.

sectionBreakdown.note: 4.3 mi on maintained trail, gaining roughly 2,170 ft to the lake (5,570 ft).
About 0.75 mi but roughly 2,000 ft of gain on a steep, loose and/or snow-covered trail-gully; often the crux of the day.
Class 2 snow/rock traverse south toward the tower to the col below the summit.
Class 3-4 scrambling with one roped 5.5 step; short, well under a rope-length of technical climbing.

emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Leavenworth, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (24-hour Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Aasgard Pass is a recurring site of Chelan County Sheriff/volunteer SAR helicopter hoist rescues; cell coverage in the Enchantment Basin is unreliable, so carrying a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Given elevation (8,546 ft) differs from the Wikipedia/Peakbagger consensus of 8,566 ft; highPointFt corrected to 8,566 ft. The given technical grade/pitch count (Grade II, 5.4, 2 pitches) does not match how reputable sources describe the 'South Face'/'Southwest Corner' standard route: The Mountaineers' official route page (corroborated by a Wenatchee Outdoors guide and several trip reports) describes it as mostly Class 3-4 scrambling from the SW col, with a single exposed 5.5 step near the summit and a Class 2-3 downclimb — not a sustained 2-pitch 5.4 rock climb. That grade/pitch combination instead matches a separate, less-traveled route Mountain Project and other sources call the 'West Buttress' ('an easy two-pitch route up the backside of the tower, climbing to 5.4'). Fields here describe the actual South Face/Southwest Corner standard route (matching the given routeId/name and 'standard route' framing): rockGrade corrected to 5.5 (the crux move) and pitches corrected to 1 (a single roped step amid otherwise-unroped Class 3-4 scrambling); alpineGrade (PD) and commitment (Grade II) were left as given since no source directly contradicts them.

blurb: Witches Tower is a granite pinnacle in the Stuart Range (Alpine Lakes Wilderness), rising above Aasgard Pass at the west end of the Enchantments basin roughly 0.4 mile east-southeast of Dragontail Peak and ringed by remnants of the Snow Creek Glacier. Sound Stuart Range granite gives it an easy Class 3-4 scramble as well as a couple of short, low-grade roped lines to the summit, and it is most often climbed as an add-on to Dragontail Peak or Little Annapurna during a longer Enchantments trip.

routes.season: Jun-Oct (dry class 2 scramble); also climbed Apr-May as a snow scramble requiring ice axe/crampons
routes.overview: A class 2 scramble up Earl Peak's southwest ridge, reached via the Bean Creek Trail to Earl Pass (~6,200 ft) on the Bean-Standup Creek divide, then a faint boot path along the ridge crest to the 7,036 ft summit.
routes.beta: The standard route follows the Bean Creek Trail from the Beverly Turnpike Trailhead up the Bean Creek drainage to Earl Pass, then climbs the southwest ridge on intermittent tread to the summit. It is rated class 2 in dry summer conditions but is also a popular spring snow scramble that can require an ice axe and crampons on the steep, icy south-facing summit slopes.
routes.approach: From the Beverly Turnpike Trailhead, hike the Beverly Turnpike/Bean Creek Trail (#1391/1391A) about half a mile to a junction and take the right fork up the Bean Creek drainage. Bean Creek meadows are reached around 2.5 miles; the trail then bends east, crosses the creek, and climbs to Earl Pass (~6,200 ft) on the divide between Bean and Standup Creeks.
routes.descent: Descend the same southwest ridge scramble back to Earl Pass, then follow the Bean Creek Trail back to the Beverly Turnpike Trailhead.
routes.descentText: Reverse the southwest ridge scramble down to Earl Pass, then descend the Bean Creek Trail through Bean Creek meadows back to the trailhead. No rappels or technical descent gear are needed in summer conditions; in spring, glissading is sometimes possible on remaining snow above the pass.
routes.itinerary: Most parties climb Earl Peak as a long day hike from the Beverly Turnpike Trailhead (roughly 7.5-8+ miles round trip with 3,600-3,900 ft of gain, depending on source/variation): Bean Creek Trail to Bean Creek meadows, continue to Earl Pass, scramble the southwest ridge to the summit, and return the same way. Some parties camp at Bean Creek meadows or near the pass and make the summit push the next morning, especially for early-season snow ascents.
routes.bail: Bail by reversing the southwest ridge back to Earl Pass, then descending the Bean Creek Trail; there are no technical anchors or shortcuts, so retreat simply means down-climbing/hiking the ascent line.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time that gets you off the loose, exposed southwest ridge and below Earl Pass before afternoon thunderstorms (which do occur on the drier east-side Teanaway) or fading light; the upper ridge has no fixed anchors or quick bail options once committed.
routes.bestSeason: Most climbers go June through October, once winter snow has cleared the upper ridge and Bean Creek crossings have dropped to a manageable level. The route is also a popular April-May snow scramble, but then typically requires an ice axe and often crampons for the steep, icy summit slopes and can involve dangerous high water on Bean Creek during snowmelt.
gear: Ice axe and crampons (for early-season/spring snow ascents)
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
Map/compass or GPS - tread becomes faint above Bean Creek meadows and near Earl Pass
Sun protection for exposed ridge walking
Extra water - limited reliable sources above Bean Creek meadows
proTips: Many parties combine Earl Peak with neighboring Bean Peak via the connecting ridge (class 2-3 scrambling)
Best views are of the Stuart Range - Mount Stuart, Argonaut, Colchuck, and Dragontail - across Ingalls Creek
Spring ascents (April-May) typically require snow-travel gear; by mid/late summer the route is a dry class 2 scramble
watchOut: The final roughly 800 ft in about a half-mile above Earl Pass is steep, loose, and rocky
Trail tread is faint to nonexistent above Bean Creek meadows and can be easy to lose in poor visibility

Bean Creek Trail and Beverly Turnpike Trailhead access were closed by USFS order through at least Dec 31, 2026 due to Labor Mountain Fire (2025) damage - verify current closure status with the Cle Elum Ranger District before going
knownHazards: Loose rock and scree on the steep final push from Earl Pass to the summit
Early-season snow/ice on south-facing summit slopes, historically requiring ice axe and crampons on trip reports
High, potentially dangerous creek crossings on Bean Creek during spring snowmelt
Cliff bands requiring careful route-finding on the connecting ridge toward Bean Peak

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road to FS Road 9737 / 9737-112 (Beverly Turnpike Trailhead)
road.status: Paved to end of North Fork Teanaway Road, then gravel Forest Service road to trailhead; reported in good condition in recent (spring 2024) trip reports
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum, take Hwy 970 ~8.5 mi, turn onto Teanaway Road ~7.3 mi, then North Fork Teanaway Road ~5.8 mi to end of pavement, then FS Road 9737 ~3.7 mi to FS 9737-112, then ~1.5 mi north to the Beverly Turnpike Trailhead.

climate.typical: The Teanaway sits on the drier east side of the Cascade crest, receiving less precipitation than the western slopes, with generally drier conditions and occasional summer thunderstorms.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack lingers on the upper ridge and summit slopes into May-June; Bean Creek crossings can run high and dangerous with snowmelt.
bySeason.summer: Warm and generally dry - the standard season for a straightforward class 2 scramble.
bySeason.fall: Cool and mostly dry, with first snow possible by late October/November.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche-prone terrain; only visited by experienced winter mountaineers.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required for trailhead parking
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Bean Creek Trail #1391.1/1391.2 and the Beverly Turnpike Trail #1391 were closed by USFS order May 20 - Dec 31, 2026 due to Labor Mountain Fire (2025) damage; confirm current status with the Cle Elum Ranger District before heading out.

sectionBreakdown.note: ~50-minute final ascent reported in an April 2006 spring-snow trip report; not representative of full trailhead-to-summit time

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff's Office (non-emergency) 509-962-7525; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, 803 W 2nd St, Cle Elum, WA; 509-852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare Hospital, Ellensburg, WA (~35 mi from the trailhead)
emergency.notes: Remote trailhead with unreliable cell service; Cle Elum Ranger District and Kittitas County Search and Rescue (searchandrescue@co.kittitas.wa.us) are the relevant local contacts.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation was 7,039 ft; Wikipedia, WTA, and multiple trip reports consistently list Earl Peak at 7,036 ft (2,145 m) - used 7,036 ft as highPointFt. Approach stats also vary by source: WTA lists 7.5 mi round trip with 3,600 ft gain from the Beverly Turnpike/Bean Creek trailhead, while other trip reports (Wenatchee Outdoors, Have Tent Will Travel) cite 8-10 mi round trip with ~3,900 ft gain for slightly different route variations/trailheads.

blurb: Earl Peak (7,036 ft) is a summit in the Wenatchee Mountains of the Teanaway region, Cascade Range, on the edge of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness in Kittitas County, Washington. Composed of Jurassic-age rock, it sits about two miles southwest of Navaho Peak and is popular for straightforward class 2 scrambling and sweeping views across Ingalls Creek to Mount Stuart and the Stuart Range. No documented first-ascent record for the peak exists in standard references.

routes.overview: A straightforward, non-technical high point in the Teanaway region of the Wenatchee Mountains, gained via a long forested and subalpine trail to Navaho Pass followed by an easy scramble up the peak's south ridge/slopes to the summit.
routes.beta: The standard route follows the Stafford Creek Trail to Navaho Pass, then picks up the old County Line Trail heading east to intersect Navaho's south ridge; from there it's roughly a 700-foot Class 2 ridge scramble on a boot path to the summit. Reported round-trip stats vary by source from about 11-14 miles with 4,000-4,300 feet of gain depending on the exact line taken. A known shortcut leaves the main trail about 3 miles in, where two creek forks meet, and climbs directly to the saddle below the peak's southeast ridge, cutting roughly 2 miles off the round trip.
routes.approach: From the Stafford Creek Trailhead, follow Stafford Creek Trail #1359 uphill through forest along the creek's right bank. Around 3 miles the trail bends west and climbs through meadows and scattered trees below Navaho Peak, passes the Standup Trail junction, then switchbacks up rocky terrain to Navaho Pass. At the pass, pick up the old County Line Trail heading east/right and follow it along the ridge to Navaho's south ridge, then follow the boot path up loose scree to the summit.
routes.descent: Descend the same way: south ridge boot path back to Navaho Pass, then down the Stafford Creek Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route: pick your way back down the summit boot path (loose scree, short switchbacks) to where it meets the old County Line Trail, continue to Navaho Pass, then retrace the Stafford Creek Trail's switchbacks and forested tread back down to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties complete Navaho Peak as a long single day (about 8 hours car-to-car). It can also be split into an overnight by camping at one of the two backcountry sites near Navaho Saddle, hiking in on day one and tagging the summit before hiking out on day two.
routes.bail: There is no technical terrain on this route, so retreat at any point simply means turning around and following the same trail/boot path back down to Navaho Pass and the Stafford Creek Trail; no rappels or fixed retreat points are needed.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if lingering snow on the summit boot path (common into early July, especially on north-facing sections) cannot be crossed safely without an ice axe, or if afternoon thunderstorms build over the exposed south ridge and upper mountain.
routes.bestSeason: Snow can linger on the upper mountain into July, so most parties target August and September for the driest, most reliable footing, though the trail is generally hikeable from around June through October depending on snowpack (when not closed for other reasons).
whatToBring: extra water (sources near Navaho Saddle can dry up by late summer)
Northwest Forest Pass for the trailhead
proTips: A known shortcut leaves the main trail about 3 miles in, where two creek forks meet, and climbs directly to the saddle below the peak's southeast ridge, cutting roughly 2 miles off the round trip.
Fill water bottles at the Navaho Saddle campsites early in the trip since sources there can dry up by late summer.
Target an August or September ascent for the most snow-free footing on the summit boot path.
watchOut: Loose, unstable scree with short, tight switchbacks on the final push to the summit.
Snow patches can linger on the boot path into July, requiring an ice axe (and sometimes crampons) in early season.
The old County Line Trail beyond Navaho Pass is faint and easy to lose in poor visibility.
Afternoon thunderstorms are a real hazard on the exposed upper ridge.
knownHazards: Loose scree and short switchbacks on the final summit approach.
Lingering snow into early July on north-facing sections of the route.
Faint, unmaintained tread on the old County Line Trail past Navaho Pass.
Limited, seasonal water sources past Navaho Saddle.

road.name: FR 9737 & FR 9703 (North Fork Teanaway Road to Stafford Creek Road)
road.status: Gravel/dirt Forest Service road, generally passable to passenger cars in dry conditions
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum, take WA-970 east, turn onto Teanaway Road/North Fork Teanaway Road and follow it about 13 miles to pavement's end at Twentynine Pines Campground; continue on FR-9737 about 1.5 miles, then turn right onto FR-9703 (signed Stafford Creek) and drive about 2 miles to the trailhead.

climate.typical: Dry, warm summer days typical of the east-slope Cascades/Teanaway, with real potential for afternoon thunderstorms; snow lingers on shaded parts of the route into early July.

bySeason.spring: Trail typically still snow-covered and often inaccessible; avalanche and swollen creek-crossing hazards persist.
bySeason.summer: Prime season; expect some snow patches on the boot path into early-mid July, drier and more reliable by August.
bySeason.fall: Good, stable conditions into October, but water sources near Navaho Saddle can dry up.
bySeason.winter: Deep snow at elevation; the access road is not maintained for winter travel.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.fees: $5 daily or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass (or an America the Beautiful/interagency annual pass) required at the trailhead
access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass (day or annual) or interagency annual pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Stafford Creek Trail #1359 - the standard approach to Navaho Pass and Navaho Peak's South Slopes - is closed May 20, 2026 through Dec 31, 2026 due to fire and storm damage from the Labor Mountain Fire; confirm current status with the Cle Elum Ranger District before going.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, given the long car-to-car day

emergency.county: Kittitas County (trailhead and most of the approach); the summit sits on the Kittitas-Chelan county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 962-7525; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg, WA - (509) 962-9841, 24-hour ED, Level IV trauma
emergency.notes: Kittitas County Search and Rescue supports mountain rescues in this area.

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevation of 7,228 ft does not match multiple corroborating sources (Wikipedia, PeakVisor/Peakbagger, WTA, SummitPost, Wenatchee Outdoors), which consistently list Navaho Peak at 7,223 ft; highPointFt corrected to 7223. Round-trip distance/gain also vary noticeably by source (11-14 mi, 4,000-4,276 ft gain) depending on exact line and trailhead measured from; gainFt/distKm here follow WTA's figures as the most detailed single source.

blurb: Navaho Peak (7,223 ft) rises on the Kittitas–Chelan county line in the Wenatchee Mountains' Teanaway region, on the eastern edge of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. It is a non-technical Class 2 summit reached from Navaho Pass, and its broad, open top offers wide views of the Stuart Range, Mount Stuart, and the Teanaway drainage. Unlike many nearby granite peaks, Navaho is primarily a hiking/scrambling objective with no notable technical first-ascent history recorded in standard climbing references.

waypoints.name: Lake Ingalls / Longs Pass Trailhead (Esmeralda Basin, N. Fork Teanaway)
Sherpa Peak / Sherpa Balanced Rock summit

routes.fa: Dave Mahre, Gene Prater, Bill Prater, 1955
routes.rappels: Single fixed bolt anchor atop the Balanced Rock; one short rappel (~20 ft) back to the notch/ledge.
routes.overview: The Standard Route climbs the exposed, ~20-ft granite obelisk of the Sherpa Balanced Rock: class 3-4 ridge scrambling from the south basin reaches the base of the formation, a shoulder stand or thrown rope gains its overhanging east-side pedestal, and a short bolt-protected friction pitch (5.7) then leads to the summit block, which is rappelled from a single fixed bolt.
routes.beta: This is less a 'route up the peak' than the technical crux feature of Sherpa Peak: a free-standing granite pillar near the true summit. Parties typically combine it with the Class 3-4 ridge traverse to Sherpa's main summit. The crux move onto the pedestal has historically been done either by shoulder-standing a partner or by throwing/lassoing a rope over the pedestal's point; from there, bolt-protected friction slab gains the flat top, from which a single aging bolt is used to rappel back down.
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda Basin trailhead (~4,300 ft) or the Longs Pass trailhead off North Fork Teanaway Road (FS 9737), follow the Longs Pass Trail over Longs Pass (~6,200 ft) and down to Ingalls Creek (~4,800 ft). Cross the creek to the Ingalls Creek Trail and continue about a mile to the Beverly-Turnpike Trail junction (~4,700 ft), then leave the trail and climb talus and a gully on Sherpa's south side to the roughly 7,000 ft basin below the ridge crest east of the Balanced Rock.
routes.descent: Reverse the class 3-4 ridge scramble back to the notch and south basin, then descend the approach gully/talus back to Ingalls Creek and out over Longs Pass.
routes.descentText: From the Balanced Rock, rappel its single fixed bolt back to the ridge. Traverse/downclimb the class 3-4 ridge crest eastward back to the ~8,000 ft notch, then descend the broad gully/talus slope back into the ~7,000 ft basin. From there retrace the bushwhack/talus descent to the Beverly-Turnpike/Ingalls Creek Trail junction, follow Ingalls Creek Trail back to the Longs Pass Trail, and climb back over Longs Pass to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Long single-day round trip from the Esmeralda Basin or Longs Pass trailhead: pre-dawn start, approach via Longs Pass and Ingalls Creek to the south basin, class 3-4 ridge scramble to the Balanced Rock, technical pitch to the summit block and rappel, then reverse the full approach to the trailhead the same day.
routes.bail: Retreat is via the same class 3-4 ridge and gully back to the ~7,000 ft basin; the fixed rappel bolt atop the Balanced Rock is the only way down from the summit block itself, so the crux move is effectively committing until that bolt is reached.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and exposed, loose class 3-4 ridge terrain, turn around with enough margin to reverse the ridge and reach easier ground before dark or before afternoon storms build, which are common over the exposed Stuart Range crest in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September once the approach gullies and connecting ridge are snow-free; trip reports exist from as early as mid-June and as late as mid-September, with early-season parties reporting lingering snow in the notch and gullies.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack for the class 3-4 ridge and short technical sections: a handful of small-to-mid cams, a set of nuts/tricams, and slings. A 50-60m rope is used for a class 4 chimney with fixed rappel slings encountered en route, and for the final rappel off the single bolt atop the Balanced Rock.
routes.proNeeds: The Balanced Rock pitch itself is protected only by the fixed bolt near the top (per the original Beckey guide description); the preceding ridge scrambling is largely unprotected class 3-4 terrain where a light rack covers occasional harder moves and the fixed-line chimney section.
whatToBring: sturdy approach shoes or light boots for talus and scrambling
rock shoes optional for the summit-block friction moves
proTips: The crux is gaining the Balanced Rock's exposed east-side pedestal — parties use either a shoulder stand or a thrown/lassoed rope to get the first hold; put your strongest climber up first so they can assist the rest of the party.
Most parties combine the Balanced Rock with a traverse of Sherpa's true summit via the class 3-4 ridge just to its west.
A 60m rope has been reported as convenient for linking sections of the ridge and ropework near Sherpa; confirm your rope length covers the summit-block rappel.
watchOut: The rappel/aid bolt atop the Balanced Rock has been described by climbers as old and rusty — inspect it before committing your full weight.
Loose and sandy rock on ledges and benches along the ridge traverse demands care, especially with parties strung out below one another.
Afternoon thunderstorms build over the exposed Stuart Range crest in summer.
knownHazards: Sustained exposure on class 3-4 ridge terrain between the approach basin and the Balanced Rock.
A single, decades-old fixed bolt is the only rappel anchor atop the Balanced Rock.
Loose rock and a fixed-sling chimney section along the connecting ridge/approach.

pitchDetail.notes: Shoulder stand or thrown rope gains the exposed east-side pedestal of the Balanced Rock; bolt-protected friction climbing then leads to the flat summit block. Rappel from the single fixed bolt atop.

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road (FS 9737)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service road; passable to most passenger vehicles in summer, with washboard/rutted sections near the road ends
road.seasonalGate: Closed to wheeled vehicles roughly mid-November through early April (winter access is by ski, snowshoe, or snowmobile from the Teanaway Sno-Park)
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum, take WA-970 east about 7 miles to Teanaway Road, then follow North Fork Teanaway Road (FS 9737) north; pavement ends partway and the final miles are gravel to the Longs Pass or Esmeralda Basin trailheads.

climate.forecastZone: Stuart Range / East Slopes Central Cascades
climate.typical: Dry, warm alpine summers with afternoon thunderstorm potential; winters bring heavy snow that closes the access road

bySeason.spring: Lingering snow in approach gullies and on the connecting ridge into June
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season; warm and mostly dry with afternoon storm risk
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures with stable weather windows into September, early snow possible
bySeason.winter: Deep snowpack, access road gated, avalanche terrain on approach slopes

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Cle Elum Ranger District), Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for trailhead parking ($5/day or $30/year)
access.permit: No day-use climbing permit required; self-issue wilderness registration is typical at Alpine Lakes Wilderness trailheads for overnight use
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Access road (FS 9737) is gated to wheeled vehicles in winter, roughly mid-November to early April

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early alpine start given the long approach and afternoon storm risk

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Esmeralda Basin TH (~4,300 ft) to Longs Pass (~6,200 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Per a recorded September trip report; times vary with trailhead choice and fitness

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit); trailhead access is via Kittitas County (Teanaway)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff/Kittcom dispatch (509) 925-8534 (Teanaway/trailhead side); Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency (509) 667-6851 (RiverCom 911 dispatch area) — call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, 803 W. 2nd St, Cle Elum, WA, (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare (Ellensburg) or Cascade Medical Center (Leavenworth) for full emergency care; Cle Elum has urgent care only
emergency.notes: Kittitas County Search and Rescue supports backcountry incidents in the Teanaway/Stuart Range drainage; call 911 for any emergency.

routes.corrections: Sources vary slightly on Sherpa Peak's summit elevation: Wikipedia lists 8,630 ft, while WTA (8,600 ft) and a recorded trip report (8,605 ft) align closely with this catalog's 8,605 ft figure, so the given elevation was retained rather than changed.

blurb: Sherpa Peak is a granite spire in the Stuart Range of Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, a satellite summit of Mount Stuart along the Cascade crest near the Chelan/Kittitas county line. Its defining feature is the Sherpa Balanced Rock, a roughly 20-foot granite obelisk perched about 100 yards from the true summit, first climbed in 1955 by Dave Mahre, Gene Prater, and Bill Prater — the same party credited with the peak's first ascent. The formation and surrounding ridge are Cretaceous granodiorite of the Mount Stuart batholith, and topping the obelisk still requires a shoulder stand or thrown rope to gain its exposed east-side pedestal before a short, bolt-protected friction pitch to the summit block.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Tom Stewart, August 6, 1966 (first winter ascent: Bill Pilling and Steve Mascioli, March 3-5, 1984)
routes.rappels: 3 traversing rappels from the summit to Black Tooth Notch, then 3 more (60m rope, barely sufficient) into the Southwest Couloir, finishing on a loose 3rd-class gully
objHaz: crevassed Goode Glacier approach (worse late season)
rockfall/loose rock in the descent gully
routes.season: Jul-Sep (best after ~3rd week of July; earlier has more glacier/snow but easier moat crossing)
routes.face: Northeast Buttress (crest of the NE ridge)
routes.overview: A long, aesthetic buttress of solid gneiss climbing the crest of Mt. Goode's NE ridge from the Goode Glacier to the summit — regarded as one of the North Cascades' premier moderate alpine rock routes.
routes.beta: After crossing the Goode Glacier and negotiating the toe moat, climb 1,000+ ft of 3rd/4th-class buttress crest (often simulclimbed or soloed), then low-5th-class rock (mostly 5.4-5.5, with a short unprotectable 5.7 slab section near 8,000 ft usually bypassed right) for roughly 5 more pitches to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Bridge Creek Trailhead on Hwy 20 (2 mi east of Rainy Pass), follow the PCT south ~10 miles to the North Fork Bridge Creek Trail junction, cross Grizzly Creek and ford the North Fork, then climb timber/scree and slabs beside the stream draining the Goode Glacier to a bivy on the lateral moraine below the glacier.
routes.descent: Rappel from the summit to Black Tooth Notch, then continue rappelling into the Southwest Couloir and downclimb loose 3rd-class terrain to the Goode Glacier basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make three traversing rappels toward Black Tooth Notch; simulclimb/scramble a connecting ledge, then make three additional rappels down into the Southwest Couloir (a 60m rope is just long enough). Finish via a loose, 3rd-class gully back to the approach basin. Retrace the approach line back over the moraine and down to the North Fork Bridge Creek Trail and PCT.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike in via PCT/North Fork Bridge Creek to a bivy on the moraine below Goode Glacier (6+ hrs). Day 2: cross the glacier, climb the buttress to the summit (~8 hrs), descend via Black Tooth Notch/SW Couloir rappels back to camp. Day 3: hike out (parties commonly take the full descent-to-car push, ~10 hrs, in one day).
routes.bail: Below the crux slab section near 8,000 ft, downclimb the buttress crest back toward the glacier; above that, the Black Tooth Notch/SW Couloir rappel line is the standard retreat/descent option even mid-route if reached.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-rappel descent through Black Tooth Notch and the long glacier/approach return, parties should set a turnaround time that guarantees reaching the notch and starting the rappel sequence well before dark.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Bridge Creek/Goode Glacier basin; a satellite communicator/InReach is recommended given the remoteness and multi-day evacuation times cited in trip reports.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions are generally after the third week of July into September, once the highway and trail are snow-free and the glacier moat/bergschrund is more manageable, though access to the buttress toe gets harder as the season progresses and the moat opens further.
routes.detailedRack: A small alpine rack covers the route: a single set of cams (~0.4-2"), a set of nuts, a few tricams, and roughly 9 slings for building simul-climbing protection and rap anchors; a 60m rope is standard.
routes.proNeeds: Light, mostly small-to-mid cams and nuts for the low-5th-class crest; gear is used sparingly given long simulclimbing blocks.
gear: light alpine rack (small-mid cams, nuts, tricams)
whatToBring: glacier travel kit (if crossing Goode Glacier roped)
sun protection for long glacier/slab sections
proTips: Climb the direct slabs right of the waterfall on the approach to avoid the longer, brushier historic Beckey approach line.
Trail runners are reported as fine for the approach and descent; climbing shoes are optional on this route.
A snowpatch just north of the summit, down in the notch, is a reliable water source into August.
watchOut: Crossing the moat/bergschrund from the glacier onto the buttress toe is often the technical crux of the whole route.
The unprotectable 5.7 slab section near 8,000 ft should be bypassed right rather than climbed direct.
The long descent and hike-out expose parties to serious heat in the Stehekin-area valley bottom.
knownHazards: Glacier crevasse hazard crossing the Goode Glacier, worse in late season.
Loose rock in the final descent gully below the SW Couloir rappels.
Very long, remote approach with limited bail options once on the upper buttress.

pitchDetail.notes: Over 1,000 ft of scrambling on the buttress crest, often simulclimbed or soloed.
A short slab section on the crest is unprotectable; most parties deviate right onto easier 5th class.
Roughly 5 pitches of low-5th-class climbing to the summit, easing near the top.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Seasonal

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park Complex / Washington Pass-Rainy Pass corridor
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short, drier alpine climbing season roughly July-September; glaciated terrain retains snow/ice well into summer.

bySeason.spring: Highway and trail typically still snowbound; not viable for the rock route.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and early snow can end the season by late September.
bySeason.winter: Highway 20 closed; the route becomes a serious winter mountaineering/ice objective (first winter ascent 1984).

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade 5.6; the great majority of sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, The Mountaineers, Climber Kyle, Spokalpine) grade the route 5.5 (with some describing it as ~5.4 in dry conditions). Set to 5.5.

objHaz: icy moat/bergschrund crossing off the Goode Glacier
loose alpine rock typical of the range
routes.face: Northeast Face (true face right of/above the Northeast Buttress, linking toward Black Tooth Notch)
routes.overview: A steeper, more sustained line up Goode's true Northeast Face, distinct from (and harder than) the adjacent Northeast Buttress, involving mixed rock-and-ice terrain on the upper headwall.
routes.beta: Trip reports describe an 'upper northeast face' as the final headwall traversed/climbed between Black Tooth Notch and the true summit on Southwest Couloir ascents, and Megalodon Ridge's route description independently notes a short overhanging 5.10 pitch above the Goode Glacier in this same area — indicating this face holds harder, less-traveled mixed terrain than the standard buttress line.
routes.approach: Same as the Northeast Buttress: Bridge Creek Trailhead on Hwy 20, PCT south to North Fork Bridge Creek Trail, then up to a bivy near the Goode Glacier.
routes.descent: Descent is via Black Tooth Notch and the Southwest Couloir rappel sequence, as for the Northeast Buttress.
routes.turnaround: As with the Northeast Buttress, turn around with enough daylight margin to complete the Black Tooth Notch rappel sequence before dark.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Goode Glacier basin; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.bestSeason: Likely climbable in the same July-September window as the Northeast Buttress, with the ice/mixed sections most in condition earlier in the season before they melt out.
routes.proNeeds: A rack extending to protect 5.8 rock plus ice screws for the AI2 sections would be expected, though no source documents the exact rack for this specific line.
watchOut: This is a less-documented, harder variant than the standard Northeast Buttress — expect more route-finding uncertainty and looser rock.
knownHazards: Icy moat/bergschrund crossing from the Goode Glacier.
Mixed rock-ice terrain subject to melt-out conditions changing difficulty through the season.

road.status: Seasonal

bySeason.summer: Primary window, though ice features are most reliable early in the summer before melt-out.

routes.corrections: Could not independently verify this as a distinct, separately first-ascended route in any searchable online source (Mountain Project, SummitPost, AAC Publications, theCrag, cascadeclimbers). Trip reports do describe an 'upper northeast face' as a real feature climbed between Black Tooth Notch and the summit on Southwest Couloir ascents, and Megalodon Ridge's FA account references a hard (5.10) pitch on the Goode Glacier side of this same face, which is broadly consistent with a IV 5.8/AI2 face route existing here, but no source gives this line's own FA, pitch count, or length — those fields are left null rather than guessed.

routes.fa: Blake Herrington and Sol Wertkin, September 6, 2007
objHaz: icy moat near the final gendarmes/Black Tooth Notch
year-round snow/glacier ice at the saddle past the SE peak
loose alpine rock on lower sections
long, committing ridge with limited retreat
routes.face: East/Megalodon Ridge (long ridge rising from the North Fork of Bridge Creek to the SE Peak headwall)
routes.overview: A long, striking ridge line snaking nearly 6,000 ft up from the North Fork of Bridge Creek to Goode's SE Peak, mixing scrambling, running-belay ridge climbing up to 5.8, and a stout 5.10 headwall finish.
routes.beta: The route climbs a clean arete descending NE from the main ridge, simul-climbed to an 8,200-ft tower (one rappel), then continues along the crest with running belays through terrain up to 5.8. The SE Peak Headwall provides three long pitches around 5.9-5.10 (easier alternatives exist further left), with the crux an overhanging 5.10 pitch above the Goode Glacier near Black Tooth Notch.
routes.approach: From the Bridge Creek Trailhead on Hwy 20, follow the PCT and North Fork Bridge Creek Trail to the base of the ridge, which rises directly from the valley.
routes.descent: Descend via Black Tooth Notch/Southwest Couloir to Park Creek, as done on the first ascent, or via the standard SW Couloir rappel sequence back toward the Goode Glacier.
routes.descentText: The first-ascent party bivied on the summit before descending toward Park Creek; parties can alternatively use the Black Tooth Notch/Southwest Couloir rappel sequence shared with the Northeast Buttress descent.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day push: approach and bivy below the ridge, a long summit day of ~12 hours of simul-climbing (roughly 20 pitches' worth of terrain, though only 7-8 are actually belayed), summit bivy, then descent/hike-out.
routes.bail: Limited bail options once committed to the ridge crest; the safest retreat is generally to continue to the SE Peak and join the Southwest Couloir descent rather than reversing the ridge.
routes.turnaround: Given the ~12-hour first-ascent time and committing nature of the ridge, parties should plan a turnaround that keeps the Black Tooth Notch/SW Couloir descent achievable in daylight.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the North Fork Bridge Creek/Goode Glacier basin; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.bestSeason: Best in late summer (Aug-Sep) once the year-round snow/ice at the saddle past the SE Peak and the icy moat near the final gendarmes are most manageable.
routes.detailedRack: A single to double rack depending on how much simul-climbing is planned; small cams are reported as most useful given the crack/seam nature of the harder pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack biased toward small cams for the technical headwall pitches, supplemented by extra slings for simul-climbing protection along the long ridge crest.
gear: single-to-double alpine rack, small cams emphasized
proTips: The route can be done almost entirely by simul-climbing; only 7-8 of the ~20 pitches' worth of terrain need be individually belayed.
Bivy sites exist below the ridge after the SE Peak and on the summit.
watchOut: Year-round snow/glacier ice persists at the saddle just past the SE Peak.
The crux overhanging 5.10 pitch near Black Tooth Notch has an icy moat to negotiate.
This route sees very few ascents — expect route-finding uncertainty and no fixed protection.
knownHazards: Long, committing ridge with 4,000-5,000 ft of exposure on either side.
Persistent ice/snow at the SE Peak saddle.
Loose, 'distinctly alpine' rock on lower sections despite excellent gneiss higher up.

pitchDetail.notes: Clean arete descending NE from the main ridge, simul-climbed to an 8,200-ft tower, then one rappel.
Running-belay climbing along the crest toward the SE Peak.
Three long pitches; easier options exist further left.
Above the Goode Glacier near Black Tooth Notch, with an icy moat to negotiate.

routes.corrections: Given data listed grade V/5.9/TD. First-ascent sources (AAC Publications, Alpinist) and theCrag grade the route IV+ 5.10, and the AAC Publications entry lists the date as September 5-7, 2008, while Alpinist's contemporaneous newswire and Mountain Project both give September 6, 2007 (used here as the two independent, roughly contemporaneous sources agree). Mountain Project lists the route as Grade IV, YDS 5.9, 20 pitches, 3,000 ft — a notch easier than the AAC/Alpinist IV+ 5.10 rating; both are reported here as rockGrade 5.10 (majority/first-ascent-account grade) with the conflict noted.

routes.fa: Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickett, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, George MacGowan, July 5, 1936 (peak's original first ascent, via this line)
routes.rappels: Commonly 3 single-rope rappels to gain class-3 terrain in the couloir when descending; some descriptions note up to 6 total rappels combined with downclimbing depending on conditions
objHaz: extensive loose rock ('massive loose rocks') in the couloir
snow/ice patches on the couloir even in summer
cornice/bergschrund hazard near the notch between summits
ticks in the lower approach basin
routes.season: Jun-Sep (snow-climb conditions in early season, loose scrambling by late summer)
routes.face: Southwest Couloir, between Goode's main summit and the West Tower/SE Peak
routes.overview: The least technical of Goode's routes and the line of the peak's original 1936 first ascent: a steep gully between the main summit and West Tower, class 4 at its crux, reached via a long approach from either the North Fork Bridge Creek or Park Creek valleys.
routes.beta: From high camp, ascend to the saddle/notch and climb the steep, loose gully of the Southwest Couloir (class 4 at the top); a line of more solid darker rock on the left ('the black stairs') offers easier scrambling than the center of the gully.
routes.approach: Two approaches are used: (1) via Bridge Creek Trailhead on Hwy 20, PCT and North Fork Bridge Creek to a high camp below the SW side (~15 mi one-way to camp); or (2) via Park Creek Trailhead on Stehekin Valley Road (reached by boat/shuttle to Stehekin), up the Park Creek Trail ~4 miles to a climber's path (marked by a three-rock cairn) that follows the ridge north of the creek draining Goode's south side to a high camp near 7,200 ft on Goode Creek Bench.
routes.descent: Reverse the couloir: downclimb/rappel (up to 3 single-rope raps) to class-3 terrain, then continue down to the approach basin and out via the same trail used for the approach.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Southwest Couloir; where the couloir ends in cliffs, traverse left onto easier terrain and back into the basin below the gully. Typically 3 single-rope rappels gain class-3 ground, with further downclimbing through loose, insecure terrain; the 'black stairs' (a band of more solid rock on the left) offers an easier scrambling alternative to the center of the gully.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike in to high camp (Goode Creek Bench, ~7,200 ft, via Park Creek, or a comparable camp via Bridge Creek). Day 2: climb the Southwest Couloir to the summit (documented as ~3.7 hrs from camp) and descend (~3.1 hrs back to camp). Day 3: hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down the couloir is straightforward in its lower portion; above the crux class-4 section, downclimbing or a short rappel is the standard bail.
routes.turnaround: Loose rock and variable snow/ice in the couloir make afternoon conditions (rockfall, softening snow) worse — plan to be off the couloir before the heat of the day.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Bridge Creek or Park Creek backcountry; a satellite communicator is recommended.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable June through September; earlier in the season the couloir holds more consolidated snow (crampons/axe useful), while by late summer it reverts to a loose, dry scramble.
routes.proNeeds: A light rack, helmet, ice axe and crampons are commonly carried for the snow/ice sections and rockfall protection; the route is largely unprotected scrambling above camp.
gear: light rack/rope for the class-4 crux and rappels
whatToBring: bear canister (required in some NCNP zones)
tick protection for the lower approach
proTips: The 'black stairs' — a vein of darker, more solid rock on the left side of the couloir — offers easier, more secure scrambling than the loose center of the gully.
An alternative easier skinning/hiking line exists behind the southeast-facing ridge leading up to the saddle just before the Southwest Couloir.
watchOut: The couloir is heavily loaded with loose rock ('massive loose rocks') — helmet essential and parties below are at rockfall risk.
Pockets of thin ice can form on the couloir's solid granite steps after cold snaps, even in late season.
The lower approach basin (Park Creek side) is heavily infested with ticks in early summer.
knownHazards: Persistent loose rock throughout the couloir.
Seasonal snow/ice patches that can make footing insecure without crampons.
Long, remote approach with significant elevation gain/loss (~7,200 ft via Park Creek) before reaching the technical terrain.

climate.typical: Drier, more continental climate on the Stehekin (east) side than the Skagit (west) side of the crest; snow lingers in the couloir into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Couloir typically snow-filled; a spring/early-summer snow climb.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; couloir transitions from snow to loose rock as summer progresses.
bySeason.fall: Shortening season; early snow can return by late September.
bySeason.winter: Not a standard winter objective; road/boat access is limited.

emergency.rangerStation: Stehekin Ranger Station (NCNP/Lake Chelan NRA); North Cascades NP Park HQ (360) 854-7200
