blurb: Mount Goode (9,220 ft) is the highest peak in North Cascades National Park, rising nearly 7,000 feet above the Bridge Creek valley between the Skagit River and Lake Chelan. Built of solid alpine gneiss, it is prized among Cascades climbers for its remoteness (it's invisible from any road) and for the 1966 Fred Beckey/Tom Stewart Northeast Buttress, one of the range's classic moderate alpine rock routes. The peak was first summited July 5, 1936 by Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickett, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, and George MacGowan via the Southwest Couloir/Southeast Ridge.

waypoints.name: Colonial Creek Campground / Thunder Creek Trailhead

routes.fa: 1926 by Lage Wernstedt (peak FA; USFS surveyor mapping the North Cascades, believed to be via this side of the mountain)
routes.overview: Mount Logan's easiest and by far most-traveled line: a long backcountry approach through old-growth forest and alpine meadows to a high camp beneath the gentle Fremont Glacier, then a short glacier crossing and an exposed Class 2-4 ridge scramble to the true summit.
routes.beta: Considered the standard route on Logan. From a high camp near Park Creek Pass, climbers cross open heather, talus, and the low-angle Fremont Glacier to a notch in the summit ridge, then follow a mix of Class 2, 3, and 4 ledges and a short rock pitch to the summit; several parties choose to belay the more exposed steps.
routes.approach: Drive SR-20 to Colonial Creek Campground (~1,200 ft) and follow the Thunder Creek Trail roughly 18 miles south through old-growth forest to sandy meadows just north of Park Creek Pass, with a common camp around 6,350 ft. An alternate approach starts at High Bridge in Stehekin (reached via passenger ferry/shuttle up Lake Chelan) and follows the Stehekin River and Park Creek trails over Park Creek Pass to the same basin. From either camp, traverse ~2 miles north around a broad ridge to a high camp near 8,000 ft on the Fremont Glacier saddle.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the Class 2-4 summit ridge, re-cross the Fremont Glacier, and descend to the Park Creek Pass area camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ledge system down through the Class 3-4 sections to the low notch in the ridge, then descend the snow finger back onto the Fremont Glacier and cross it back to the saddle camp. From there, retrace whichever approach was used (Thunder Creek Trail or Park Creek Pass/Stehekin), noting that the Park Creek footlog crossing becomes markedly harder after dark.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: trailhead to Park Creek Pass-area camp (~6,350 ft). Day 2: camp to Fremont Glacier saddle high camp (~8,000 ft), or combine with Day 1 for a faster party. Day 3: alpine start, climb to the summit (~3-3.5 hrs from high camp) and descend to camp or lower on the same day. Day 4: hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The route is a simple there-and-back; retreat by reversing the line at any point. The only real commitment is the exposed Class 3-4 scrambling near the summit ridge, which should be downclimbed rather than pushed through in deteriorating weather.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets the party off the exposed summit ridge and back across the glacier before afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers, and allows the long trail descent (including creek crossings) to be finished before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, after the Thunder Creek/Park Creek Pass trail has melted out and stream crossings have dropped from peak runoff; the Fremont Glacier stays low-angle and relatively benign in a normal snow year through this window.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent throughout the Thunder Creek and Park Creek Pass drainages; per NPS guidance, do not rely solely on cell phones, satellite messengers, or PLBs for rescue coordination.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit for the Fremont Glacier crossing: rope, harness, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks/ascenders, pulley, pickets), ice axe, and crampons. The summit ridge is scrambling terrain (Class 2-4); a light rock rack is optional for parties who want to protect the more exposed steps rather than solo them.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel gear is the real requirement; rock protection needs on the summit ridge are minimal and route-dependent, mainly useful for belaying the short Class 4 step.
whatToBring: bear-resistant food storage (check current NPS zone requirements)
proTips: Camping at the ~8,000 ft Fremont Glacier saddle instead of at Park Creek Pass shortens summit day considerably.
Multiple boot-packed tracks braid through the meadows above Park Creek Pass in season and help with route-finding.
The Park Creek footlog crossing is far harder in near-darkness — time the return to cross it in daylight.
watchOut: Exposed, sometimes loose Class 3-4 moves on the final summit ridge where several parties choose to belay.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in this alpine terrain in summer.
Creek and footlog crossings on the approach become significantly harder in low light or high water.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Fremont Glacier require standard roped glacier-travel precautions.
Loose rock on the summit ridge and surrounding moraines creates rockfall risk to parties below.
The remote location (16+ miles from any trailhead) makes self-rescue capability essential.

Camp to high camp near 8,000 ft on the Fremont Glacier saddle, via heather and talus

routes.overview: A quieter, more strenuous alternative to the Fremont Glacier line, approaching Mount Logan's north side via Fisher Creek to a crevassed glacier and a short, exposed scramble to a summit that holds barely two people.
routes.beta: From a basin/lake camp north of the peak, the route crosses a connecting ridge, drops to the Banded Glacier, and climbs the glacier (with genuine crevasse hazard and at least one section steep enough that parties place protection) to the Banded-Douglas col, finishing with a short Class 3-4 scramble to the small summit.
routes.approach: From the Easy Pass Trailhead on SR-20, hike over Easy Pass and descend into the Fisher Creek drainage; alternatively, approach via the Thunder Creek Trail (~9 mi) and Fisher Creek Trail (~3 mi) to a log bridge, then climb steep brush and talus roughly 1,500 ft to an unnamed lake/basin north of Logan (~5,200 ft) used as base camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the Banded Glacier route back over the connecting ridge to camp.
routes.descentText: Downclimb from the summit to the Banded-Douglas col, recross the Banded Glacier (roped, watching for crevasses), regain the connecting ridge, and descend to the basin/lake camp before retracing the Fisher Creek approach.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: trailhead to basin/lake camp (11-13 mi, ~4,600 ft gain via the Easy Pass approach). Day 2: camp to summit and back via the Banded Glacier. Day 3: hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the route; the crevassed glacier and the connecting-ridge scree slopes are the main terrain to downclimb carefully rather than push through in poor conditions.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with enough time to recross the glacier and descend the scree slopes before weather or visibility deteriorate, given the remote, multi-day nature of the approach.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-summer (roughly July into August), after the Fisher Creek approach and glacier have consolidated but before late-season crevasse opening becomes severe.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable across the Fisher Creek/Easy Pass backcountry; do not rely solely on phone or satellite devices for rescue coordination.
routes.detailedRack: Rope, harness, and crevasse-rescue hardware for the Banded Glacier crossing; ice axe and crampons; a small rack of pickets or a screw or two for the one steeper glacier section some parties choose to protect.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal beyond glacier travel gear — mainly for the single steeper pitch on the glacier and for belaying the short summit scramble if desired.
proTips: The Easy Pass approach trades trail miles for more elevation gain on day one compared with the longer Thunder Creek approach.
In good, consolidated summer conditions some experienced parties get by with trail runners on the glacier, but rope and crevasse-rescue gear are still standard.
The summit is small and exposed — plan for only about two people at a time.
watchOut: Steep scree on both the connecting ridge and the final approach requires care.
One notably steep section of the Banded Glacier crossing may warrant a placed picket or screw.
Loose, precarious rock right at the small summit.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel on the Banded Glacier.
A long, remote approach (roughly 35 miles round trip) with limited bail options partway through.
Exposure on the final scramble to a very small summit.

pitchDetail.notes: Easy Pass or Thunder Creek + Fisher Creek Trail to a basin/lake camp (~5,200 ft) north of Logan
Ascend to a low point on the ridge, then descend to the Banded Glacier
Roped crossing to the Banded-Douglas col; crevasses present
Short scramble from the col to the small, exposed summit

routes.overview: The shortest line to Mount Logan's summit but the least traveled of the three standard routes: a Fisher Creek approach to a cliff-banded basin, a crux ramp onto the Douglas Glacier, and a shared final scramble to the summit block.
routes.beta: After descending from Easy Pass into the Fisher Creek basin below the glacier, a narrow ramp or gully — the only weakness through the surrounding cliff bands — gives access to the Douglas Glacier. Low-angle switchbacks lead up the glacier before a steeper (~1,000 ft) upper section requiring crampons to the Banded-Douglas col, where the route joins the Banded Glacier line for the final 3rd-4th class summit block.
routes.approach: Hike the Easy Pass Trail 3.7 miles to Easy Pass (~6,500 ft), descend to Fisher Creek, and follow its far side, contouring and losing elevation, to the basin below the Douglas Glacier; turn south (left) and travel cross-country into the basin.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back down the glacier and through the crux cliff-band ramp to Fisher Creek and over Easy Pass.
routes.descentText: From the col, downclimb the steeper upper glacier on firm, sometimes icy snow, cross the lower glacier via low-angle switchbacks, and downclimb the crux ramp through the cliff band back into the Fisher Creek basin before retracing the approach over Easy Pass.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Easy Pass Trailhead to base camp below the Douglas Glacier. Day 2: alpine start (as early as ~5:30 AM), climb the glacier and summit block, return to camp. Day 3: hike out via Easy Pass.
routes.bail: The crux cliff-band ramp is the main commitment point; below it, retreat to Fisher Creek is straightforward, but above it on the glacier, downclimbing the same ramp is the only reasonable way off.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before committing to the steep upper glacier ramp if conditions are icy or visibility is poor, since it is also the only descent option through the cliff bands.
routes.bestSeason: June through August, while the glacier ramp still holds consolidated snow for the crux section; conditions are noted to vary significantly year to year.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable throughout the Fisher Creek/Easy Pass backcountry; do not rely solely on phone or satellite devices for rescue coordination.
routes.detailedRack: Rope, harness, and crevasse-rescue hardware for the glacier; ice axe and crampons for the steep upper ramp and the crux gully onto the glacier; a light rack is optional for the shared Class 3-4 summit block.
routes.proNeeds: The technical need is glacier and steep-snow gear rather than rock protection; the crux is a steep snow/ice ramp, not a pitched rock climb.
whatToBring: sturdy approach footwear for Fisher Creek cross-country travel
proTips: The narrow ramp/gully through the cliff band below the glacier is the route's crux — scout it in good light before committing.
This is the shortest way up Logan, but the cliff-band crux and remoteness keep it the least-traveled of the standard routes.
watchOut: A large glide crack (20+ feet deep) has been observed near the glacier terminus.
Cliff bands surround the basin — stay on the known ramp/gully line rather than improvising.
Firm, steep snow on the upper glacier demands careful technique, especially on descent.
knownHazards: Cliff-band terrain funnels travel onto a single crux ramp with no easy alternative.
Crevasses on the Douglas Glacier, though reported as relatively sparse compared to Logan's other glaciers.
Remote, multi-day approach with limited rescue access.

pitchDetail.notes: Easy Pass Trail, 3.7 mi to Easy Pass (~6,500 ft)

routes.corrections: SummitPost describes this route as 'Grade II, Class 4,' which is a notch below the catalog's listed Grade III. Given the roughly 6,000 ft round-trip glacier climb, a distinct steep-snow/ice crux ramp, and multi-day remote approach reported across sources, Grade III is retained here as the more consistent rating, but the SummitPost II rating should be noted as a discrepancy.

blurb: Mount Logan (9,087 ft) is a remote, three-glacier peak in the Thunder Creek/Fisher Creek backcountry of North Cascades National Park, one of the highest non-volcanic summits in the range and among the most isolated Cascade peaks (16+ miles from any trailhead). The Fremont, Banded, and Douglas glaciers radiate off its southwest, north, and southeast flanks respectively, feeding a broken summit block of North Cascades metamorphic and granitic rock shaped by uplift and glaciation. U.S. Forest Service surveyor Lage Wernstedt made the first documented ascent in 1926 while triangulating the range for early Cascade mapping.

waypoints.name: High Bridge (Stehekin Valley Road shuttle trailhead)
Park Creek Pass (Thunder Creek / Park Creek Pass Trail)

routes.fa: Forrest Farr and Art Winder, 1933
routes.rappels: No fixed rappel stations; many parties rig a short hand-line/rappel on the summit step and the Class 3-4 traverse below it.
routes.overview: A long, remote scramble up Storm King's southwest ridge and upper slopes to a short, exposed Class 3-4 summit block, set deep in the Stehekin backcountry of North Cascades National Park.
routes.beta: From a high camp near Two Mile or Five/Six Mile Camp on the Park Creek Pass Trail, leave the trail around 4,100 ft at a large washout/avalanche path and climb steep timber and loose talus onto the nose of Storm King's southwest ridge (a rough climbers' path through ash, burnout and deadfall gains over 1,000 ft). Follow the loose ridge and ledges toward the summit pyramid, then make an exposed Class 3-4 traverse (commonly fixed-lined) below the final summit block, finishing with a short but exposed Class 4 step to the true summit. Descend by reversing the route.
routes.approach: From Stehekin Landing, take the seasonal shuttle bus (or hike) up the Stehekin Valley Road toward High Bridge, then follow the Park Creek Trail roughly 2.5 miles to Park Creek Campground. Continue on the Park Creek Pass Trail about 2 more miles to Two Mile Camp (3,400 ft), or a further 3 miles to Five Mile Camp (which overshoots the Storm King turnoff by about a mile). From the trail near 4,100 ft, cross a large washout/slide area, spot the nose of the southwest ridge above, and leave the trail near a small cairn to climb steep timber and talus onto the ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the Southwest Ridge route back to the Park Creek Pass Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the exposed Class 3-4 traverse (downclimbing or rappelling the short summit step), then descend the loose southwest ridge and talus back to timberline. Rejoin the climbers' path down through the burn/ash zone to the Park Creek Pass Trail near 4,100 ft and retrace the approach to camp and out via Park Creek/High Bridge.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Ferry/floatplane to Stehekin, shuttle toward High Bridge, hike in via Park Creek Trail to Two Mile or Five/Six Mile Camp. Day 2: Climb the Southwest Ridge to the summit and return to camp. Day 3: Hike out to High Bridge and shuttle back to Stehekin Landing.
routes.bail: The route can be reversed from essentially any point on the southwest ridge, descending the loose talus/climbers' path back to the Park Creek Pass Trail; there is no established alternate descent.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote approach and loose, exposed terrain near the summit, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to complete the full ridge and talus descent, with margin for the afternoon thunderstorms that are common in the North Cascades in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly July through the first significant snowfalls of autumn (into September/early October); snow and ice can linger on the upper ridge into early August, so July-August offers the most consistent, snow-free conditions and the most stable weather window for the exposed summit traverse.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the approach or climb; a satellite communicator (e.g. Garmin inReach) is recommended. If a cell connection is found, NPS Park Dispatch is (360) 873-4500 ext. 37, or call 911.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine kit rather than a technical rack: slings/webbing for a hand line on the exposed Class 3-4 traverse, a rope (30-60m) for a short belay or rappel near the summit, plus an ice axe and crampons for lingering snow/ice on the approach and ridge into early summer.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is used in practice — most parties sling horns/blocks to rig a hand line on the exposed traverse and belay or rappel the short summit step; a small rack of cams/nuts covers optional protection on the looser Class 3-4 sections.
whatToBring: Bear hang or canister (bear hang present at Two Mile Camp)
3+ days of food for the remote round trip
Map/compass or GPS for off-trail ridge route-finding
proTips: A short fixed hand line on the Class 3-4 traverse below the summit block speeds up parties and adds security.
Plan on a three-day round trip given the remote approach via Stehekin and the Park Creek Pass Trail.
Carry an ice axe and crampons even into August for lingering snow/ice near the summit ridge.
watchOut: Loose talus and exfoliating rock along the ridge — test holds carefully and watch for parties below.
The exposed Class 3-4 traverse below the summit pyramid is where most incidents occur; many parties fix a hand line here.
The off-trail line to gain the southwest ridge is easy to lose, especially through the ash and deadfall of a past burn zone.
knownHazards: The ridge and summit block are loose, exfoliating rock prone to rockfall, especially on ledges and in gullies.
Snow and ice can persist on the route into August; an ice axe and crampons are recommended even in mid-summer.
Off-trail route-finding through burned, ashy terrain with heavy deadfall makes the ridge approach easy to lose.
Storm King is one of the farthest Washington Bulger (100-highest) peaks from a road, so any injury or delay greatly extends evacuation time.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep timber and loose talus off the Park Creek Pass Trail (~4,100 ft) onto the nose of the southwest ridge, through ash/burnout and deadfall.
Loose ridge crest and ledges leading up toward the summit pyramid.
Exposed traverse below the summit block; most parties fix a hand line here.
Short but exposed final move to the true summit; a belay or fixed line is recommended.

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road (Stehekin Landing to High Bridge)
road.status: Stehekin has no highway access — reached only by passenger ferry, floatplane, or trail. A seasonal shuttle bus normally runs part of the Stehekin Valley Road toward High Bridge; the upper valley road beyond has long been closed to vehicles due to washouts, and a major December 2025 flood caused further, more severe damage to Stehekin Valley Road and Company Creek Road that was still under repair as of mid-2026.
road.seasonalGate: Shuttle typically operates roughly June-September; confirm current schedule, as 2025-26 flood repairs may affect service.
road.driveNote: Reach Stehekin via the Lady of the Lake passenger ferry from Chelan or Chelan Seaplanes, then take the NPS/concessioner shuttle bus toward High Bridge for the Park Creek/Storm King approach.

climate.typical: Cool, changeable mountain weather typical of the North Cascades interior; summers are the driest and most stable season, but rapid weather changes and afternoon thunderstorms can occur at any time.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack typically persists into June on the approach and ridge; not the standard season for this scramble.
bySeason.summer: July-August are usually the driest months with the most snow-free ridge conditions, though afternoon thunderstorms remain possible.
bySeason.fall: Climbable into September/early October until the first significant snowfalls; days shorten and weather grows less stable.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park / Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; backcountry camping requires a wilderness permit.
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight camping, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (or self-issue stations when closed); walk-up permits are not issued in Stehekin.
access.closures: Access to Stehekin and the upper valley has been significantly disrupted since a major December 2025 flood damaged Stehekin Valley Road and Company Creek Road; verify current NPS road, trail, and shuttle conditions before planning this climb.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from high camp (Two Mile, Five Mile, or Six Mile Camp) to reach the ridge and exposed summit traverse with a full day of daylight in hand.

sectionBreakdown.note: Via seasonal NPS/concessioner shuttle bus (~11 miles).

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245; NPS Park Dispatch: (360) 873-4500 ext. 37

emergency.notes: Storm King is one of the most remote peaks on Washington's 100-highest (Bulger) list; cell coverage is unreliable to absent on the approach and climb, so carry a satellite communicator. Rescue typically requires helicopter or multi-day ground SAR given the distance from road access, and access logistics have been further complicated since the December 2025 Stehekin flood.

routes.corrections: Elevation sources diverge: SummitPost, the Bulger 100-highest list, and peakery commonly cite ~8,520 ft (with an often-misquoted 8,515 ft triangulated subsummit), while LiDAR-based listsofjohn.com lists 8,564 ft, closely matching this app's listed 8,565 ft; treat 8,520 ft as the older, commonly-cited figure and ~8,564-8,565 ft as the more precise modern one. Some AI-search summaries conflated a 1936 Wolf Bauer party's first ascent of nearby Mount Goode with Storm King's first ascent; independent, repeated sourcing instead points to Forrest Farr and Art Winder, 1933 as Storm King's first-ascent party. Access has also been in flux: a major December 2025 flood further damaged Stehekin Valley Road/Company Creek Road beyond the pre-existing upper-valley washout closures, so approach logistics (shuttle mileage, camp locations) should be reconfirmed with current NPS trip-planning info before a trip.

blurb: Storm King is a remote, rarely visited summit in the North Cascades National Park backcountry near Stehekin, rising at the shared head of Park Creek, the North Fork of Bridge Creek, and Thunder Creek, with close neighbors Buckner Mountain, Mount Logan, and Mount Goode. Its upper mountain is loose, broken rock, giving it a reputation for rockfall-prone talus and ledges rather than clean climbing. First climbed in 1933 by Forrest Farr and Art Winder, it ranks among Washington's "Bulger" 100-highest peaks and is considered one of the most remote entries on that list, typically requiring a three-day round trip via boat or floatplane access to Stehekin.

routes.rappels: None typically needed on the standard scramble line
objHaz: Steep, sometimes icy snowfields on the Cache Col descent into the Kool-Aid Lake basin
Loose rock and talus on the peak's upper slopes
Route-finding difficulty around Point 7004 and near the false summit
routes.face: Northwest slope/gully above Kool-Aid Lake (the commonly scrambled line; a separate, distinct ski line called the 'South Couloir' is approached from the north via Cache Col/Art's Knoll)
routes.overview: A short, non-technical class 2-3 (locally a class 3 move or two) scramble to the summit of Hurry-up Peak, climbed almost exclusively as a side trip by parties camped at Kool-Aid Lake on the Ptarmigan Traverse rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.beta: From a camp at Kool-Aid Lake, scramble up the peak's slopes toward the summit ridge, working around cliff bands near Point 7004 via a climbers' path, then follow the exposed but moderate summit ridge to the true summit. Round trip from camp is roughly an hour to a few hours. Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide is the traditional reference for the standard gully line; some parties report a slightly different gully than the one described in the guide with equal success.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (end of the 23-mile Cascade River Road out of Marblemount), hike the maintained trail about 3.7 miles to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). Continue on a climbers' path across talus to Cache Col (~6,900 ft, on the Skagit-Chelan county line), then descend a steep snowfield — often requiring an ice axe, sometimes crampons in early season — into the Kool-Aid Lake basin (~6,800 ft), a common Ptarmigan Traverse camp roughly 5.5-6.8 hours and about 5.5 miles from the trailhead. Hurry-up Peak rises directly above camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent scramble back down to Kool-Aid Lake.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same class 2-3 slope/gully used on the ascent back toward the saddle above Kool-Aid Lake, then descend to camp. No rappels are typically required on the standard line.
routes.itinerary: Usually folded into a multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse: hike in from the Cascade Pass Trailhead over Cascade Pass and Cache Col to Kool-Aid Lake camp (roughly 6-7 hours), then scramble to the Hurry-up Peak summit and back before or after making camp (about 1-3 hours round trip), before continuing the traverse toward Yang Yang Lakes and beyond.
routes.bail: The scramble can be abandoned at nearly any point by reversing the ascent line back toward Kool-Aid Lake; the standard route has no technical commitment requiring rope work to retreat.
routes.turnaround: Turn around for deteriorating weather, whiteout, or if the snow/gully conditions above Kool-Aid Lake become icy or rockfall-prone; the exposed summit ridge and cliff bands around Point 7004 make route-finding difficult in poor visibility.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, once the Cache Col snowfield and approach trail are largely melted out; expect some firm, potentially icy snow near Cache Col into July. The peak has also been descended on skis in winter/spring conditions (a documented mid-March south-side couloir descent), but that is a distinct, more serious avalanche-terrain undertaking from the standard summer scramble.
routes.comms: North Cascades National Park advises that cell and satellite coverage is unreliable throughout its backcountry, including this area; carry a means of emergency communication but do not rely on it in place of self-sufficiency.
routes.detailedRack: This is an unroped class 2-3 (locally class 3-4) scramble; no technical rock rack is carried. Parties typically bring an ice axe and, depending on conditions, crampons for the snowfield below Cache Col and any firm snow/ice remaining on the peak's upper slope, plus a helmet for loose rock near the summit ridge.
whatToBring: Map and compass or GPS for route-finding around Point 7004
proTips: Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide documents the standard gully line up the peak's northwest side above Kool-Aid Lake; some parties take a slightly different gully than the one described with equal success.
An easy, worthwhile side-trip (as little as an hour round trip) for parties already camped at Kool-Aid Lake on the Ptarmigan Traverse.
The summit is a good viewpoint for Trapper Lake, Storm King Mountain, and Mount Goode.
watchOut: Point 7004 on the connecting ridge is difficult to pass directly; most parties detour around it via a climbers' 'goat path' rib rather than following the crest.
A short section of steep, sometimes icy snow (reported around 7,100 ft on one variant) has required crampons even in summer for some parties.
Loose rock and moderate exposure along the final summit ridge, and a false summit that adds route-finding interest.
knownHazards: Steep, sometimes icy snowfields on the Cache Col descent into the Kool-Aid Lake basin
Loose rock and talus on the peak's upper slopes
Route-finding difficulty around Point 7004 and near the false summit

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 10 miles, then rough gravel; the final few miles are narrow with steep drop-offs and limited turnaround/passing space.
road.seasonalGate: Closed in winter; a National Park boundary gate sits at about milepost 18 (vehicle length/width restrictions beyond this point).
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from SR-20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead. Typically opens to the trailhead by late June, sometimes not until early July after heavy-snow winters; high-clearance recommended for the final stretch.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North; NWS North Cascades Zone (WAZ301)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate on the range crest: wet, heavy-snowfall winters and relatively dry, mild summers; Pacific storms can leave deep, lingering snowpack on north-facing gullies and the Cache Col approach into July.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack remains; the peak has been descended on skis (a documented mid-March south-side couloir descent), but avalanche and icy conditions are a serious concern.
bySeason.summer: Prime scrambling season (roughly July-September) once the Cache Col snowfield and approach trail are largely melted out; some firm snow near Cache Col can persist into July.
bySeason.fall: Cooler, often clearer weather into September, but daylight shortens and early snow is possible.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow accumulation and a closed Cascade River Road make the approach a serious ski-mountaineering/avalanche-terrain undertaking.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service), on the shared boundary with Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, USFS)
access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee for a backcountry/wilderness permit via Recreation.gov; free for visitors under 16.
access.permit: An overnight wilderness permit is required year-round for camping at Kool-Aid Lake or elsewhere en route; about 60% of permits are reservable on Recreation.gov and 40% held for walk-up, picked up in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated and closed in winter; high-country wilderness camps along this corridor (including near Cascade Pass/Kool-Aid Lake) are not reservable for trips starting before July 1 due to snow.

sectionBreakdown.note: Via Cascade Pass and Cache Col; ends with a steep snowfield descent into the Kool-Aid Lake basin
Round-trip side-scramble, class 2-3 with a move or two of class 3; some parties do it in under an hour

emergency.county: Skagit County (peak sits on the Skagit-Chelan county line)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450; North Cascades NP Communications Center after-hours (360) 873-4590 ext. 37; 911 also routes to the appropriate agency but wilderness calls may be delayed.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA, (360) 854-7245 (permits/backcountry info); North Cascades National Park Headquarters, (360) 854-7200.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E. Kincaid St., Mount Vernon, WA, (360) 424-4111 (24-hour ER).
emergency.notes: North Cascades NP notes cell and satellite coverage are unreliable throughout its backcountry.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,845 ft) conflicts with Wikipedia, ListsOfJohn, and PeakVisor, which consistently cite 7,821 ft (2,384 m) for Hurry-up Peak; used 7,821 ft here. Grade is also more commonly reported by trip reports as Class 2-3 ('never getting much above a class 3 move or two') rather than Class 3-4, though one trip report notes a steep snow section requiring ice axe/crampons on a variant line. Multiple firsthand trip reports (trailcatjim.com, One Hike a Week) describe the standard scramble ascending the peak's northwest slope/gully from Kool-Aid Lake — sourced to Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide — rather than a distinct named 'South Ridge'; a separate 'South Couloir' ski-mountaineering line approached from Cache Col/Art's Knoll on the north side is documented independently by Cascade Crusades (March 2005).

blurb: Hurry-up Peak is a 7,821-foot summit of gneiss on the North Cascades crest, straddling the Skagit-Chelan county line at the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and Glacier Peak Wilderness. It sits at the northern end of the Ptarmigan Traverse just south of Cascade Pass, above Kool-Aid Lake, with the small S Glacier clinging to its east flank. It's most often climbed as a quick class 2-3 side-scramble by parties camped at Kool-Aid Lake rather than sought out as a destination in its own right.

routes.overview: A remote, off-trail Class 3 scramble up Trapper Mountain's south side, reached by a long cross-country approach from Cascade Pass over Alliteration Col and down to Trapper Lake, then up open slopes/ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: Trapper Mountain sees very few ascents because its standard route requires a long trail approach followed by sustained off-trail travel. Most parties approach from the Cascade River Road side via Cascade Pass, then travel cross-country east over a col (referenced in trip reports as 'Alliteration Col') and drop to Trapper Lake before climbing the peak's south-facing slopes/ridge to the summit; an alternate approach reaches Trapper Lake via a steep, unmaintained climber's path from the Upper Stehekin River Trail. The route itself is a scramble on talus, heather, and broken rock with only occasional Class 3 moves; the crux difficulty is routefinding and the sheer remoteness rather than technical climbing.
routes.approach: From SR-20 at Marblemount, drive the Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15) roughly 23 miles to its end at the Cascade Pass trailhead (road is paved for about the first third, then gravel/dirt, narrow and steep near the top). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail (about 3.7 mi, 1,700 ft gain) to Cascade Pass, then continue cross-country/off-trail east toward Trapper Lake (elev. ~4,170 ft) via the col above it, and ascend the south slopes to the summit. An alternate, equally remote approach reaches Trapper Lake from Stehekin via the Stehekin Valley Road to Harlequin Campground, then the Stehekin River Trail and a steep unmaintained climb to the lake.
routes.descent: Reverse the south-slopes ascent route back to Trapper Lake and out via Cascade Pass (or the Stehekin approach, if used).
routes.descentText: Descend the same broken south-facing terrain used on the ascent back down to Trapper Lake, then retrace the cross-country line back up and over the col to Cascade Pass, and finish on the maintained Cascade Pass Trail back to the trailhead. There is no maintained trail for the bulk of this descent, so keep track of the ascent line, especially in poor visibility.
routes.bail: Terrain below the upper scramble section is walkable talus/heather that can be down-climbed or descended at essentially any point back toward Trapper Lake; there is no fixed rappel or technical retreat required on the standard south-slopes line.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and return both involve many hours of trail and off-trail travel with no technical protection on the descent, set a turnaround time that guarantees clearing the cross-country sections (col crossing and Trapper Lake basin) before dark or before afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most favorable weather for climbing or viewing this peak; the Cascade River Road/Cascade Pass trailhead itself typically doesn't open until late June (sometimes not until early July in heavy-snow years), which effectively sets the start of the practical season from that side.
objHaz: afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers
stream/creek crossings on the Stehekin approach
knownHazards: The route is off-trail for most of its length beyond Cascade Pass, requiring solid map/compass or GPS navigation skills.
Trapper Lake and its surrounding slopes see occasional wet-slide avalanche activity in spring, per nearby ski trip reports (this applies mainly to snow-season travel, not the summer scramble).
watchOut: Very little beta exists for this specific route online; parties should be prepared for genuine routefinding rather than following a well-worn path.
The alternate Stehekin-side approach to Trapper Lake is explicitly described as a bushwhack requiring route-finding experience.
proTips: Because published trip reports for the standard South Slopes route are scarce, cross-reference a topo map/Beckey-style guide description with satellite imagery before committing to the cross-country sections.
The Cascade Pass trailhead access road typically doesn't open until late June/early July, so plan early-season attempts accordingly.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is used on the standard Class 3 route; a rope and rack are not typically needed for the south-slopes line in summer conditions.
gear: map and compass or GPS (off-trail navigation)
whatToBring: overnight backpacking gear (the approach is long enough that most parties camp near Cascade Pass or Trapper Lake)
North Cascades National Park backcountry permit if camping overnight
bear canister/food storage as required in the national park backcountry
layers for exposed alpine terrain and afternoon storms

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first third from Marblemount, then gravel/dirt with narrow, steep sections near the Cascade Pass trailhead; high-clearance recommended for the final miles.
road.seasonalGate: Closed to vehicles at milepost 20 (Eldorado) in winter; typically opens through to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June, sometimes not until early July in heavy-snow years.
road.driveNote: About 23 miles from SR-20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass trailhead at road's end.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Washington Cascades East
climate.typical: Cool, wet winters with heavy snowpack and warm, drier summers typical of the North Cascades crest; afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy residual snowpack, wet-slide avalanche hazard on steep slopes near the peak (noted around Trapper Lake/Hurry-Up on ski descents); road access to the Cascade Pass trailhead is typically still closed.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep); trailhead road open, but afternoon thunderstorms and lingering snow patches in shaded gullies are possible into July.
bySeason.fall: Stable weather windows possible into September/October but shortening days and increasing storm frequency; road may close for the season with early snow.
bySeason.winter: Road closed at MP20 (Eldorado); the peak becomes a remote ski-mountaineering/couloir objective (e.g., the North Couloir) rather than a scrambling destination.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness) and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Glacier Peak Wilderness) — the peak sits on the shared boundary.
access.permit: A National Park Service backcountry permit is required for overnight camping anywhere in the North Cascades National Park Complex; permits are obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount ($10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for advance reservations).
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated closed at milepost 20 (Eldorado) each winter and doesn't reopen to the Cascade Pass trailhead until late June/early July.

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit); the Cascade River Road approach lies in Skagit County
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA
emergency.notes: This is a remote wilderness/national park area with no cell coverage expected along most of the approach; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Search and rescue in North Cascades National Park is coordinated through the National Park Service; on the Glacier Peak Wilderness (national forest) side, coordination runs through the county sheriff.

routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected along the Cascade Pass approach, over the col, or at Trapper Lake; a satellite messenger/PLB is recommended.
routes.itinerary: Given the length of the approach (Cascade Pass trail plus substantial cross-country travel to Trapper Lake and back), most parties treat this as a 2-3 day trip: day 1 hike in and camp near Cascade Pass or Trapper Lake, day 2 climb the south slopes to the summit and return to camp, day 3 hike out.
routes.corrections: The peak's elevation is given in the seed data as 7,541 ft; Wikipedia (citing USGS-derived data) lists 7,530 ft. Both figures appear in circulation (peakbagger-style aggregators cite 7,541 ft) — noted here as an unresolved minor discrepancy rather than a confident correction.

blurb: Trapper Mountain is a 7,530 ft (per USGS/Wikipedia; some sources list 7,541 ft) summit in the North Cascades of Chelan County, sitting on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness) and the Glacier Peak Wilderness southeast of Cascade Pass. Like much of this part of the range, it is built from granitic and metamorphosed crystalline rock uplifted through Eocene volcanism and terrane accretion and later sculpted into craggy ridgelines by Pleistocene glaciation; its dramatic north side (an approximately 3,000-foot face compared by early climbers to the Grand Teton's) contrasts with the gentler south slopes used for the standard ascent. It was first climbed in 1949 by a Harvard-affiliated party of George Bell, Andy Griscom, Harry King, and W.V. Graham Matthews.

routes.fa: Clarence Fisher, Esther Buswell, Paul Hugdahl, Lars Loveseth, Winnie Spieseke, Harriet Taylor, 1927
objHaz: rockfall in the chimneys (popularity and loose rock combine)
icy Winnie's Slide in late season
crevasses on the White Salmon, Upper Curtis, and Sulphide glaciers
loose rock and multiple parties on the summit pyramid gully
routes.face: Shuksan Arm / Curtis Glacier basin, north side
routes.overview: A mixed glacier-and-rock line that climbs the chimney system on Shuksan Arm above Lake Ann, crosses the White Salmon, Upper Curtis, and Sulphide glaciers, then joins the standard summit pyramid finish shared with the Sulphide Glacier route.
routes.beta: The most-climbed route on the north side of the peak: about 1,000 ft of class 3-4 scrambling through the chimneys, glacier travel across three separate glaciers, a steep snow pitch at Winnie's Slide, then a class 3 gully to the summit. More involved than Sulphide Glacier but shorter in approach distance.
routes.approach: Drive I-5 to Bellingham, then SR-542 (Mount Baker Highway) past the ski area to the Lake Ann trailhead (about 1 mile before Artist Point). Hike the Lake Ann Trail south over Austin Pass (4,700 ft), cross a small creek at about 2 miles, and climb over a ridge to camp at Lake Ann.
routes.descent: Same route in reverse: down through the chimneys, or via the Sulphide Glacier for parties who ascended that line.
routes.descentText: From the summit pyramid, descend along the northwest edge of the Upper Curtis Glacier to about 7,050 ft, cross onto the White Salmon Glacier, descend a short steep slope, then traverse below Shuksan Arm to regain the top of the chimneys and downclimb to Lake Ann.
routes.itinerary: Typically 2-3 days: hike in and establish camp at Lake Ann (2-3 hrs), climb to the summit and return to camp (7-8 hrs), hike out on day 2 or 3.
routes.bail: Retreat down the chimneys to Lake Ann camp is possible from most points on route; the chimneys themselves are slow to reverse due to loose rock and crowding, so parties should start early to avoid being caught mid-route by weather or rockfall from above.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before starting that accounts for softening glacier snow bridges and rockfall risk in the chimneys later in the day; alpine starts are standard to be off the summit pyramid and back through the chimneys before afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August is prime — most stable weather and firmest snow. June and early July often carry lingering storms and heavier snow loading; by September crevasses are more open and the glaciers icier, sometimes requiring a second tool.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse rescue gear), ice axe and crampons for all members; a light rock rack is optional for the chimneys but most parties scramble unroped there.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection needs — the chimneys are typically scrambled, with glacier travel gear (pickets, screws) reserved for crevassed sections and Winnie's Slide.
gear: glacier travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse rescue gear)
whatToBring: overnight camping gear for Lake Ann camp
extra layers for exposed ridge sections
proTips: Start early to beat both rockfall and crowding in the chimneys
Rope up for the upper snowfield above the chimneys before crossing onto the glaciers
Winnie's Slide can firm to hard neve or ice by August — a second tool helps
watchOut: Rockfall triggered by parties above in the chimneys
Moats opening between snow and rock as the season progresses
Crowding on popular summer weekends increasing objective hazard
knownHazards: The chimneys funnel rockfall and see heavy traffic, creating a real risk of being hit by rock knocked loose above.
Winnie's Slide becomes hard ice late season and has been the site of falls.

pitchDetail.notes: ~1,000 ft of scrambling through the chimney system on Shuksan Arm
White Salmon, Upper Curtis, and Sulphide glaciers in sequence
Can be hard neve or ice late season
Loose rock gully to the true summit, shared with Sulphide Glacier route

road.name: SR-542 (Mount Baker Highway) to Lake Ann Trailhead
road.status: Paved highway, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Trailhead only reachable by vehicle once SR-542 is plowed through to Artist Point, typically not until early-mid summer
road.driveNote: Lake Ann trailhead parking is on the east side of SR-542, about 1 mile before Artist Point; lot fills early on summer weekends

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker area / West Slopes North Cascades
climate.typical: Maritime alpine climate with heavy winter snowpack and a short, often unsettled early-summer window before drier late-July/August weather

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack, road often not yet open to Artist Point
bySeason.summer: Best window July-Aug; June/early July can bring lingering storms
bySeason.fall: Drier but glaciers more crevassed and icy by September
bySeason.winter: Road closed near the trailhead; not a winter objective

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (approach) and North Cascades National Park (upper mountain)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Pass required for day-use parking at Lake Ann trailhead; NPS wilderness camping permit is $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for overnight climbing
access.permit: North Cascades National Park Wilderness/Climbing Permit required for overnight stays above the trailhead, obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or by advance reservation
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: SR-542 access to the trailhead is seasonal, dictated by snowpack and WSDOT plowing to Artist Point

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from Lake Ann camp to be off the summit pyramid and back through the chimneys before afternoon softening/rockfall

sectionBreakdown.note: Over Austin Pass
Via chimneys, three glaciers, and summit pyramid

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 360-676-6911 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, 10091 Mt. Baker Hwy, Glacier, WA — 360-599-2714; North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area is coordinated by the Whatcom County Sheriff's Office with Bellingham Mountain Rescue.

routes.corrections: Seed pitch count of 1 is not well supported for this route — sources describe it as sections of class 3-4 scrambling and glacier travel rather than discrete roped pitches, so pitches was left null. Some guide services grade the overall commitment as IV; Mountain Project lists it as Grade III, which is used here.

objHaz: afternoon whiteout/routefinding difficulty in poor visibility
routes.face: South side, via the Sulphide Glacier
routes.overview: The standard, easiest route on Mount Shuksan: a long forested and open-ridge approach to a broad, moderate glacier that leads directly to the base of the summit pyramid.
routes.beta: A 7-mile forest-and-ridge approach to a gentle glacier climb that ends with a roughly 500 ft snow (early season) or class 3 rock (late season) scramble up the summit pyramid. Standard glacier travel gear and a helmet are required; the crux is the exposed, loose summit block.
routes.approach: Drive SR-20 to Baker Lake Road (FS-11) and follow it about 23-24 miles to the Shannon Ridge trailhead at about 2,500 ft. Hike the old-road trail northeast about 2 miles to its end (~3,800 ft), then switchback up through open brush onto a forested north-south ridge; follow the ridge north to timberline (~4,600 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back down the Sulphide Glacier and Shannon Ridge trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class 3 gully (or snow in early season) back to the Sulphide Glacier, then retrace the glacier crossing and ridge descent to the Shannon Ridge trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically 2 days: hike in to a camp on or near the glacier (5-6 hrs), then climb to the summit and return (5-6 hrs).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward across most of the glacier back toward Shannon Ridge; the summit pyramid gully is the only committing section and can be downclimbed in worsening weather if conditions allow.
routes.turnaround: Because the summit block and glacier crossing depend on visibility for routefinding, set a turnaround time that keeps the party off the summit pyramid before afternoon cloud typically builds.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August offers the most stable snow and best crevasse bridging; by September the glacier is more broken and the summit gully mostly bare rock.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit — rope, harness, crevasse rescue gear, ice axe, crampons, and helmet for the summit gully.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — the summit pyramid is scrambled, with glacier gear reserved for crevasse crossings.
gear: glacier travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse rescue gear)
whatToBring: sun protection for extended glacier travel
proTips: A long right-hand curved line across the Sulphide Glacier avoids the worst crevassing
Trailhead registration exists for the Forest Service portion but not for the mandatory NPS wilderness permit — get that in advance
Good visibility is important for routefinding across the broad glacier
watchOut: Crevasses hidden under early-season snow
Loose rock in the summit gully with other parties above
Whiteout conditions on the broad, featureless glacier
knownHazards: The Sulphide Glacier is broad and can obscure crevasses under snow bridges, especially early season.
The final summit gully is loose class 3 rock with real rockfall exposure from parties above.

pitchDetail.notes: Old road then open ridge to timberline
Long curving line to the north side of the summit pyramid
Crux of the route; loose rock

road.name: Baker Lake Road (Forest Service Road 11) to Shannon Ridge Trailhead
road.status: Gravel forest road
road.seasonalGate: Road closed to all vehicle and foot traffic at Shannon Creek Bridge (~MP 23.8) starting around July 15 in some years for resource protection — check current FS alerts
road.driveNote: From SR-20 milepost 82 near Concrete, follow Baker Lake Highway (FS-11) about 24 miles, then FS-1152 and FS-1152014 to the trailhead

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker area / West Slopes North Cascades
climate.typical: Maritime alpine climate; south-facing approach sees more sun exposure than the north-side routes

bySeason.spring: Road access limited by snow and gate closures
bySeason.summer: Best window late July-Aug
bySeason.fall: Glacier more broken by September; still climbable into early October in good years
bySeason.winter: Not a winter objective; road inaccessible

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (approach) and North Cascades National Park (upper mountain/glacier)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Pass required for day-use at Shannon Ridge trailhead; NPS wilderness camping permit $10/person plus $6 reservation fee for overnight climbing
access.permit: North Cascades National Park Wilderness/Climbing Permit required for overnight stays, obtained at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center or by advance reservation; no trailhead self-registration covers the mandatory NPS permit
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Baker Lake Road (FS-11) has had seasonal closures at Shannon Creek Bridge in some years — verify current status before driving in

timing.recommendedStart: Early start from camp to cross the glacier and summit gully before afternoon cloud/softening

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 360-676-6911 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount; Mt. Baker Ranger District, Sedro-Woolley

routes.fa: Fasset, Hanft, Thompson
objHaz: large avalanches off the active Hanging Glacier threatening the approach valley
late-season schrund opening at the face base
variable snow (soft afternoon snow vs. firm morning conditions)
routes.face: North Face, between the White Salmon and Price Glacier cirques
routes.overview: A serious, moderately steep snow-and-ice line up the glaciated shoulder dividing the White Salmon cirque from the Price Glacier cirque — one of the more dramatic and seldom-done lines on the peak.
routes.beta: Sustained 40-50 degree snow and ice (steepening toward the summit shoulder), reached via a brushy approach into the White Salmon valley. Climbers wrap around the east side of the summit pyramid after gaining the North Shoulder col (~8,400 ft).
routes.approach: Take SR-542 to Glacier and get a permit at the ranger station, continue past the Nooksack River bridge to a hairpin corner at 3,500 ft near Mount Baker Ski Area; turn onto White Salmon Road (FR-3075) and follow it to its end above White Salmon Creek, then descend faint, flagged path to the creek and the glacier toe.
routes.descent: Descend via Fisher Chimneys and the Lake Ann Trail to Mount Baker Ski Area, or alternately descend the White Salmon Glacier back to the White Salmon Road cars.
routes.descentText: From the summit, cross to Fisher Chimneys terrain (Upper Curtis/White Salmon glaciers, Winnie's Slide, the chimneys) to reach Lake Ann and the ski area, or reverse the White Salmon Glacier if conditions favor it.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a long single push (car-to-car in roughly 14 hours in good conditions) or with a bivouac at the base of the face around 5,500 ft.
routes.bail: Below the North Shoulder col, retreat down the ascent line is generally possible; above that point, options narrow and continuing over the top to a known descent (Fisher Chimneys or White Salmon Glacier) may be safer than reversing steep terrain.
routes.turnaround: Given the avalanche exposure from the Hanging Glacier and softening snow, plan to be off the face and past the col well before the sun softens the upper snow — early starts are standard.
routes.bestSeason: Early-to-mid June is often cited as optimal, when cold, firm snow and ice give secure climbing before the schrund opens and snowpack deteriorates later in summer.
routes.detailedRack: A rack built around steep snow/ice protection: pickets, ice screws, and a rope for the team, plus crevasse rescue gear for the glaciated approach and descent.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets for the sustained 40-50 degree snow/ice; a second tool is recommended if the route has iced out.
whatToBring: bivy gear if not doing it car-to-car
extra layers for an exposed, high-commitment face
early-season snow gear (snowshoes optional for approach)
proTips: Climb it early in June before the bergschrund opens and while snow is still cold and firm
An early alpine start is essential to catch firm snow before afternoon softening
The North Shoulder col around 8,400 ft is the key decision point for continuing or bailing
watchOut: Avalanches shed by the active Hanging Glacier onto the approach and lower face
A late-season schrund that can block direct access to the face, requiring a left-leaning gully variation
Soft, unconsolidated snow making the steep sections insecure
knownHazards: The Hanging Glacier above the route periodically sheds large avalanches that can reach the approach valley and lower face.
Variable snow condition is the defining hazard — soft snow on this route has been described by climbers as dangerous.

bySeason.fall: Generally too melted out and icy without additional tools/screws

routes.corrections: Given grade 'Grade IV, AI3' partly conflicts with route-guide sources that describe the North Face as AI2-3 and Grade II-III; both gradings appear in circulation depending on conditions and source, so the seed's D/AI3/IV commitment was kept but readers should expect the route to sometimes be described as easier (Grade II-III).

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jack Schwabland, Bill Granston, 1945
objHaz: seracs and slough chutes above the bergschrund
rockfall down the gully between Nooksack Tower and the main lobe of the glacier
objective danger from Nooksack Tower's east face looming over the route
routes.face: Nooksack Cirque headwall, between Nooksack Tower and the main summit
routes.overview: One of the Cascades' most historic ice routes: a steep, glaciated line up the impressive Nooksack Cirque headwall of Mount Shuksan, long regarded as one of the hardest alpine ice climbs in the contiguous United States and included in 'Fifty Classic Climbs of North America'.
routes.beta: After crossing beneath Nooksack Tower at roughly 6,500 ft, the route climbs steep snow and alpine ice to a bergschrund near 7,800 ft, where climbers choose rock on the left, an ice line up the middle, or mixed ground on the right; above, steep snow/ice leads to easy gullies (4th or low 5th class) on the south side of the summit pyramid.
routes.approach: Drive SR-542 to Forest Road 32 (Hannegan Pass Road), then FR-34 to its end at the Nooksack Cirque trailhead (2,150 ft). Hike the Nooksack Cirque trail through brush toward the cirque, then traverse to a ridge on the south side of Price Lake and ascend rock slabs, or continue onto the glacier to bivouac below Nooksack Tower.
routes.descent: Descend via the Fisher Chimneys route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, join the Fisher Chimneys descent line down through the Upper Curtis/White Salmon glaciers, Winnie's Slide, and the chimneys to Lake Ann.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip: approach and bivouac below Nooksack Tower, climb the glacier and headwall to the summit, then descend via Fisher Chimneys.
routes.bail: Below the bergschrund, retreat down the glacier toward Nooksack Cirque is possible; above it, options are limited and continuing to the summit for a Fisher Chimneys descent is often the safer choice.
routes.turnaround: Given serac and slough hazard above the bergschrund, keep exposure under the schrund's overhanging ice to a minimum and turn around rather than linger below it in warming conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Conditions vary greatly by year: early season heavy snowpack can bridge crevasses more simply, while by mid-to-late August the walls are barer and the route reveals why it was once considered unclimbable.
routes.detailedRack: A snow/ice-oriented rack: pickets, ice screws, and a small rock rack for the bergschrund's rock or mixed options; nearly all gear typically gets used given the sustained, varied terrain.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws, pickets, and a small rock rack for the bergschrund crux, where the line can go on rock, ice, or mixed ground depending on conditions.
whatToBring: bivy gear for the base-of-route camp below Nooksack Tower
extra water/fuel for a remote multi-day approach
route photos/topo for the bergschrund decision point
proTips: Avoid lingering under the seracs near the bergschrund — move through quickly
Conditions vary hugely year to year; check recent trip reports before committing
Nearly a full rack of pickets, screws, and rock gear gets used on this route
watchOut: Overhead seracs at the bergschrund
Slough and rockfall funneling down the gully between Nooksack Tower and the glacier's main lobe
Remote, committing position with a long descent via Fisher Chimneys
knownHazards: Seracs above the bergschrund periodically release ice and debris onto the route.
The gully between Nooksack Tower and the main glacier lobe is a rockfall funnel.

pitchDetail.notes: From beneath Nooksack Tower (~6,500 ft) up to the bergschrund
At ~7,800 ft; three line choices depending on conditions
Above the schrund to the south-side summit gullies
South side of the summit pyramid

bySeason.spring: Heavy snow loading, high avalanche hazard
bySeason.summer: Main season, though conditions shift quickly through Jul-Aug
bySeason.fall: Route becomes barer and more mixed by September

objHaz: avalanches from the Hanging Glacier on the shared North Face approach
complex routefinding on a seldom-traveled line
routes.face: Upper mountain, reached from the North Face/Hanging Glacier side of the summit pyramid
routes.overview: A seldom-done finish to the summit pyramid, reached via the North Face and Hanging Glacier approach rather than climbed as a stand-alone base-to-summit line; guide sources describe it as requiring additional ice climbing and complex alpine rock climbing beyond the standard North Face finish.
routes.beta: Typically combined with the North Face approach: climbers gain the Hanging Glacier area, then choose the Northeast Ridge over the more common finishes (the 'normal' summit pyramid line or the Southeast Ridge) for a harder, more technical way to the top involving 4th-class-to-moderate-5th-class rock and additional ice.
routes.approach: Shares the North Face approach: SR-542 to White Salmon Road (FR-3075), descending to White Salmon Creek and up onto the glacier system toward the Hanging Glacier and the base of the summit pyramid.
routes.descent: Fisher Chimneys and the Lake Ann Trail, or the White Salmon Glacier, matching the standard North Face descent options.
routes.itinerary: Usually built into a multi-day North Face itinerary, with the Northeast Ridge substituted for the standard finish on summit day.
routes.bail: Because it is reached high on the mountain via the North Face/Hanging Glacier, retreat generally means reversing back onto the glacier system rather than descending the ridge itself.
routes.turnaround: As a variation finish on an already-committing North Face day, turnaround decisions should be made with the same margin as the North Face itself — before afternoon softening and with enough daylight for the harder finish and full descent.
routes.bestSeason: Same narrow early-summer window as the North Face it's typically climbed from — cold, stable snow conditions matter more than for the standard routes.
routes.detailedRack: A rack similar to the North Face's, supplemented with a light rock rack for the moderate 5th-class rock sections of the ridge finish.
routes.proNeeds: Ice gear (screws/pickets) for the glacier approach plus protection for 4th-to-low-5th-class rock on the ridge itself.
whatToBring: full North Face kit plus a rack for the harder rock finish
bivy gear for a multi-day itinerary
proTips: Only worth pursuing by parties already comfortable on the North Face — it adds technical ground rather than replacing any of that route's difficulty
Combine with a North Face ascent rather than treating it as an independent line
watchOut: Avalanche exposure from the Hanging Glacier shared with the North Face approach
Loose rock on a rarely-traveled ridge
Complex routefinding with little beta available
knownHazards: Shares the North Face's Hanging Glacier avalanche exposure on approach.
Being seldom climbed, route conditions and hazards are less well documented than the standard lines.

objHaz: large serac overhanging the crux choke point
avalanches from the active Hanging Glacier
icy patches on the crux bulge
routes.face: North side, on the Hanging Glacier between Crystal Glacier and Upper Curtis Glacier
routes.overview: A line on and around the Hanging Glacier itself — a small, steep icefall on Shuksan's north slopes connected to the Crystal and Upper Curtis glaciers — used both as a technical climbing/ski line and as a link between the North Face and the upper mountain.
routes.beta: Low-angle terrain on the upper glacier steepens to a fall-line choke point: a steep, narrow section over a rock bulge (roughly 50 ft) below an overhanging serac, where the route forks into east and west variants. Reached from Upper Curtis Glacier via the White Salmon drainage and Winnie's Slide, or from the North Face side.
routes.approach: Via the White Salmon drainage and Winnie's Slide to Upper Curtis Glacier, then down/across into the Hanging Glacier basin; alternately reached from the North Face's North Shoulder col.
routes.descent: Reverse the route back to Upper Curtis Glacier, or continue over the mountain via Fisher Chimneys.
routes.descentText: Parties minimize time under the overhanging serac at the crux, moving through quickly to the saddle where the east and west variants split before regaining Upper Curtis Glacier.
routes.itinerary: Often combined with a North Face or Northwest Couloir objective as a single technical day/multi-day outing rather than climbed as a stand-alone route.
routes.bail: Retreat toward Upper Curtis Glacier is the standard option below the serac; parties should not linger at the crux given overhead ice hazard.
routes.turnaround: Given the overhanging serac, time-under-hazard should be minimized regardless of a fixed clock — move through the crux quickly rather than waiting out conditions there.
routes.bestSeason: Early summer (June into July) when the glacier is more snow-covered and crevasses/bergschrunds are better bridged; the icefall and serac hazard are present in some form all season.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel and steep-snow/ice gear: rope, pickets, ice screws, and two tools for the crux bulge.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws/pickets for the glacier and crux bulge; protection options are limited directly beneath the serac, so speed substitutes for placed pro there.
whatToBring: radios for team communication near the serac hazard
poles or a second axe for stability on the icy crux
proTips: Move quickly through the crux beneath the overhanging serac rather than stopping to assess
The east and west variants split at a saddle partway down — know both before committing
Best combined with a North Face or couloir objective rather than as a stand-alone goal
watchOut: The overhanging serac above the crux bulge, which has been observed actively sloughing
Icy conditions on the narrow crux section
General Hanging Glacier avalanche activity affecting nearby routes and approaches
knownHazards: A large serac directly overhangs the route's crux and has been observed releasing slough during descents.
The glacier is active and produces avalanches that also threaten the adjacent North Face approach.
