routes.corrections: No source documents a stand-alone 'Hanging Glacier' base-to-summit route with its own first ascent or fixed grade; available sources (trip reports, guide pages) describe the Hanging Glacier as a technical glacier/icefall feature used as a link between the North Face, Upper Curtis Glacier, and Northwest Couloir objectives. Independent-route fields (fa, approach distance, gain) were left null accordingly.

blurb: Mount Shuksan (9,131 ft) is a heavily glaciated massif in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, rising immediately east of Mount Baker and about 12 miles south of the Canadian border. Built of Shuksan greenschist — oceanic basalt metamorphosed roughly 120 million years ago — it is one of the most photographed peaks in the Cascades, its north side hung with the Curtis, Hanging, Crystal, Price, and White Salmon glaciers. The first ascent is usually credited to Asahel Curtis and W. Montelius Price on September 7, 1906, though an 1897 ascent by Joseph Morowits was later claimed in a disputed account.

routes.overview: The original 1946 line: an 800-ft north-facing snow/ice couloir to a notch below the summit, followed by roughly 1,200 ft of loose, exposed 4th-class-to-5.4 climbing along the north arete/ridge to the top. Often called the East Ridge, Beckey-Schmidtke, or NE/North Face route interchangeably in trip reports — it is the same standard line.
routes.beta: A serious grade IV alpine outing that is more about commitment and route-finding than pure difficulty: a long, semi-technical approach (river ford, brush, moraine, glacier) to a high bivy, then a steep snow/ice gully followed by loose, marginal rock to the summit, with a slow, rappel-heavy descent back down the same line. Mountain Project's dedicated route page lists it as 1,000 ft over 10 pitches, YDS 5.4 (AI1-2/steep snow in the gully); Wikipedia and SummitPost describe the crux pitch as roughly 5.4-5.5.
routes.approach: Drive SR 542 east from Glacier, WA about 12.5 miles, turn onto Hannegan Pass Road (FS-32), go about 1.4 miles to the FS-34 (Nooksack Cirque Road) junction, then about 1 mile to the Ruth Creek/Nooksack Cirque trailhead. Ford (or log-cross) Ruth Creek, which has no bridge, then follow the Nooksack Cirque trail/old roadbed along the North Fork Nooksack River. Beyond the National Park boundary the route leaves maintained trail for steep forest and a climber's path up to the moraine above Price Lake, then follows the moraine and a ridge crest between the Price and East Nooksack Glaciers to a bivy spot at about 5,900 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb/rappel the north arete back into the couloir, then downclimb or rappel the snow/ice gully to the glacier.
routes.descentText: Descent is via the same north couloir and ridge used on the ascent and is reported to take longer than the climb. Parties typically make around ten rappels (mostly on a 60m rope) down the couloir/rock, including at least one rappel from a deadman picket anchor in the upper gully; some parties report only a handful of raps from natural anchors (horns, blocks, occasional fixed pins/knifeblades) with belayed downclimbing between stations, so a 70m or two ropes and spare tat are worth carrying. Stay to climber's right in the gully on both ascent and descent, since rockfall funnels to the left side.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 3-day round trip: Day 1, drive and hike/ford in to the ~5,900 ft high camp on the ridge between the Price and East Nooksack Glaciers; Day 2, alpine start, climb the couloir and ridge to the summit and descend back to camp; Day 3, hike out. Some strong parties compress this to 2 days.
routes.bail: Retreat down-climbs or rappels the same north couloir/ridge line back to the glacier — there is no easier alternate descent or walk-off; carry extra cord/webbing since fixed rappel anchors along the route are thin (tied-off horns, blocks, and occasional old pins).
routes.turnaround: Because the descent reliably takes as long as or longer than the climb and involves loose rock and a steep snow/ice gully, set a firm turnaround time from high camp and be willing to retreat rather than push for the summit late in the day — parties have been caught short on daylight even near the summer solstice.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in an early-to-mid-summer window (roughly July-August) when the couloir still holds consolidated snow rather than bare ice; many parties time the trip near the summer solstice to maximize daylight for the long approach, climb, and descent. Spring attempts are discouraged by avalanche hazard and short days; by fall the couloir typically consolidates to bare ice, raising the difficulty and rockfall/ice-fall risk.
routes.comms: No official cell-coverage data specific to the summit; North Cascades National Park notes cell service is unreliable across the park's rugged terrain even at higher elevations, so a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended for this remote objective.
routes.fa: July 5, 1946 — Fred Beckey and Clifford Schmidtke
routes.rappels: ~10 rappels (mostly 60m), including at least one from a deadman/picket anchor in the couloir
routes.face: North Face/North Couloir to the North Arete
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack: ice axe, crampons, and 2-3 snow/ice pickets for the couloir; glacier travel gear (rope, crevasse-rescue kit) for the approach; a modest rock rack of small-to-mid cams and stoppers for the loose 4th-class-to-5.4 arete pitches; two 60m ropes (or one 70m) for the rappel-heavy descent, plus spare cord/webbing since existing rap anchors are thin natural or fixed-tat stations.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse and route-finding dependent — small-to-mid cams and stoppers on the rock, pickets in the snow/ice couloir, and a helmet at all times given constant rockfall potential from parties above or natural release.
whatToBring: sun protection for long solstice-season days
Northwest Forest Pass and/or NPS backcountry permit paperwork
proTips: Climb near the summer solstice for maximum daylight given the long approach, technical climbing, and slow descent.
Go in an early-to-mid-summer window while the couloir is still snow, not fall ice — conditions get harder and more rockfall-prone as the season dries out.
The ~5,900 ft bivy site on the ridge between the Price and East Nooksack Glaciers has flat rock for about three bivy sacks; bring a stove.
watchOut: Everything that falls in the upper couloir funnels to climber's left — stay right on both ascent and descent.
Ruth Creek has no bridge and its crossing conditions change through the day; a log crossable in the morning can be underwater by afternoon.
The descent is slower than the climb and has caught parties out after dark even close to the solstice.
knownHazards: The unbridged Ruth Creek/North Fork Nooksack crossing can be dangerous or impassable in high water, especially early season.
The route climbs loose, marginal-quality greenschist throughout the upper section, making rockfall a constant hazard for climbers and belayers.
The approach and lower route cross a crevassed glacier and bergschrund, requiring roped glacier travel and rescue skills.
The 800-ft north-facing couloir can turn to bare ice by late season and has turned back parties due to unstable conditions or high temperatures loosening rock and ice.

pitchDetail.notes: Cross the bergschrund at roughly 6,500 ft and climb the north-facing snow/ice couloir, staying to the right side where rockfall is less concentrated; often an unroped snow climb early season, more often roped/iced-up by late summer.
Exit the couloir, traverse west into a hidden rock trough, then follow the loose north arete over sustained 4th-class and short low-5th-class terrain to the summit.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (FS-32) to Nooksack Cirque Road (FS-34)
road.status: Open and driveable in summer season per current Forest Service info; no closures reported
road.seasonalGate: No formal gate reported, but the road is snow-covered/unusable in winter and early spring and Road 32 crosses avalanche terrain above the FS-34 junction
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive SR 542 east ~12.5 miles, turn onto FS-32, continue ~1.4 miles to the FS-34 junction, then ~1 mile to the Ruth Creek/Nooksack Cirque trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Mt Baker zone
climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate with a heavy winter snowpack and a comparatively short, drier summer weather window

bySeason.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack; avalanche hazard on the approach and in the couloir discourages spring attempts
bySeason.summer: Best window (Jul-Aug); Pacific high-pressure spells bring clearer, drier weather, though the north couloir still holds snow/ice
bySeason.fall: The couloir typically consolidates to bare ice, raising difficulty and rockfall/icefall risk; days grow noticeably shorter
bySeason.winter: Not a viable climbing season — deep snow, high avalanche danger, and minimal daylight

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (upper mountain/glaciers) and Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mount Baker Wilderness (approach trail and trailhead)
access.fees: No day-use parking fee currently posted at the Ruth Creek/Nooksack Cirque trailhead per WTA; some older references note a Northwest Forest Pass requirement — check current signage
access.permit: Free NPS Backcountry Use Permit required for overnight camping (e.g., the ~5,900 ft high camp), obtained in person at the Wilderness Information Center or Glacier Public Service Center
access.passRequired: Possible Northwest Forest Pass at trailhead; NPS backcountry permit for overnight camping
access.closures: Ruth Creek has no bridge; ford or log-crossing conditions vary by time of day and season and can be impassable in high water

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from high camp, given the long technical descent

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Ruth Creek TH (~2,550 ft) to ridge camp (~5,900 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Includes the unbridged Ruth Creek ford, steep forest, and moraine travel; commonly done as its own day.
Glacier travel along the ridge between the Price and East Nooksack Glaciers.
~800 ft snow/ice couloir followed by ~1,200 ft of 4th-class-to-5.4 ridge climbing.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatch: (360) 384-5390 (per NPS North Cascades Climb Safely page); 911 also reaches dispatch but may route to a distant center
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, (360) 599-2714 (passes/general info); North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, (360) 854-7245 (backcountry permits)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA — Level II Trauma Center, 24-hr ER, (360) 734-5400
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable throughout North Cascades National Park's rugged terrain per NPS; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Whatcom County SAR (with Bellingham Mountain Rescue and NPS) coordinates rescues in this area.

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade 5.6 and 8 pitches. Mountain Project's dedicated route page (mountainproject.com/route/109359586/beckey-schmidtke), Wikipedia, and SummitPost all describe the hardest pitch as roughly 5.4-5.5 (MP lists YDS 5.4) and Mountain Project lists 10 pitches over 1,000 ft, not 8 — corrected accordingly. The given alpineGrade 'D' could not be confirmed by any source found and was left null rather than repeated unverified.

blurb: Nooksack Tower (8,285 ft) is a steep, narrow rock-and-ice spire on the Mount Shuksan massif in North Cascades National Park, Washington, standing about 0.75 mile northeast of Shuksan's main summit and ringed by the Price, East Nooksack, and West Nooksack Glaciers. Built of loose greenschist (metamorphosed seafloor basalt) on slopes of 50 degrees or steeper, it is widely regarded as one of the most difficult and rarely climbed summits in the Cascade Range. It was first climbed on July 5, 1946 by Fred Beckey and Clifford Schmidtke, who kicked steps up an 800-foot ice couloir on the north face and finished via fourth-class climbing along the north arete.

routes.rappels: ~3 rappels on descent (through the Great Chimney and the adjoining snow gully/upper trough)
objHaz: multi-rappel retreat with limited bail options
routes.overview: The Southeast Face route follows a long, complex traverse from High Pass across Mount Larrabee's west flank to a notch on the Larrabee-American Border ridge, then climbs slabs, ledges, and a striking ~150-foot vertical chimney (5.3-5.4) to reach the summit ridge and the true summit.
routes.beta: This is the standard, 'easiest' way up American Border Peak: mostly 3rd-4th class scrambling bracketing two short pitches of roped climbing (up to 5.4) through a distinctive chimney on the southeast face. It is deceptive — short approach mileage and a moderate grade belie a long, exposed cross-country traverse, persistently loose rock, and a multi-rappel descent that demands real alpine climbing skills (handline/rappel proficiency), not just basic scrambling.
routes.approach: From the Twin Lakes Trailhead (end of FR 3065 off Mount Baker Highway/SR 542), take the trail north past Twin Lakes to Low Pass, then continue to High Pass. From High Pass, traverse north across Mount Larrabee's west-facing slopes (talus, scree, heather) to a notch on the ridge connecting Mount Larrabee and American Border Peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb/rappel back through the chimney and gully system to the ridge notch (commonly ~3 rappels), then re-traverse below Mount Larrabee back to High Pass and out via Low Pass to Twin Lakes.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the summit ridge back to the top of the technical section. Parties commonly make three rappels to retreat: through the upper trough/gully, through the Great Chimney itself (including a tight rock 'cannonhole' squeeze that can be difficult for larger climbers), and through the adjoining snow gully (which may have its own snow-choked cannonhole in early season). From the base, re-cross the exposed traverse ledge and reverse the long cross-country traverse below Mount Larrabee's west flank back to High Pass, then descend via Low Pass to the Twin Lakes trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a very long single day, or as a 2-3 day trip with a camp near High Pass / Gargett Mine basin (~5,400 ft) to break up the long approach traverse, technical climbing, and long descent.
routes.bail: Retreat is via the same chimney/gully system — there is no easier alternate way down once past the ridge notch. If forced to bail below the chimney, simply reverse the traverse back to High Pass; above the chimney, expect to rappel back down through it to escape.
routes.turnaround: Because retreat requires multiple rappels back through the chimney/gully system plus a long, exposed cross-country traverse, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight for the full multi-rappel descent and the return traverse — parties that linger near the summit have ended up downclimbing loose terrain after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is roughly July through September, after the Twin Lakes access road and approach slopes have melted out. The entry gully and chimney can still hold snow/ice into early summer, so many parties carry an ice axe for the approach and gully in June-July. Twin Lakes Road (FR 3065) is typically snowbound into early summer and can be rough or washed out later in the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected on the approach or on the peak, consistent with the wider North Cascades backcountry; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergency contact.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rock rack with a few small-to-medium chocks/cams covers the two short 5.3-5.4 pitches through the chimney's chockstone constrictions. Carry two ropes (parties have used two 25m ropes, or a single rope doubled) so you can make full-length rappels back through the chimney and gully system on descent; bring a few slings/rap rings for anchors.
routes.proNeeds: A light rack of small-to-medium nuts and cams protects the chimney's two roped pitches; placements are somewhat sparse around the chockstone sections, so extra small-to-mid-size pieces are worth carrying.
gear: Rappel anchor gear: slings, rap rings
whatToBring: Ice axe if snow lingers in the gully
Extra headlamp battery in case of a late descent
proTips: The route is more involved than its short mileage and moderate grade suggest — treat it as a full alpine day, not a basic scramble.
Carry two ropes so you can make full-length rappels back through the chimney and gully rather than getting stuck mid-rope.
The chimney's chockstone squeeze ('cannonhole') is tight — larger climbers may need to remove their pack to pass through.
watchOut: Rock quality on American Border Peak is notably poor and loose; helmets are essential and rockfall onto climbers below is a real risk.
Some parties have found the chimney crux harder and harder to protect than the guidebook's 5.4 rating, describing 5.8-equivalent moves in spots — don't rely on the grade alone.
The summit ridge and approach traverse involve sustained exposure on loose, unstable rock.
knownHazards: Loose, 'rotten' rock throughout the route, especially in the chimney and on the summit ridge, creates ongoing rockfall risk to the party and anyone below.
A long, exposed cross-country traverse below Mount Larrabee's west flank is required both to reach and leave the technical climbing, adding significant time and fall exposure to the day.
Snow/ice can linger in the entry gully into early summer, adding an extra hazard before the rock climbing even begins.

pitchDetail.notes: From High Pass, traverse north across slabs, ledges, and gullies below Mt. Larrabee to a notch on the Larrabee-American Border ridge.
Rope up to enter the Great Chimney via 5.3 moves off an exposed traverse ledge, then easier chockstone steps up the chimney interior.
Continue up smooth 5.4 face moves past a large chockstone to a tight squeeze/'cannonhole' near the top of the chimney.
Above the chimney, work up and left through a loose trough to the summit ridge, then follow it (with one exposed step-across move) to the summit.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065)
road.status: Rough, unpaved forest road; passenger-car accessible to the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake trailhead at mile 4.5, high-clearance recommended for the final ~2 miles to Twin Lakes
road.seasonalGate: Snowbound and impassable in winter/spring; typically opens to Twin Lakes by mid-to-late summer
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive east on SR 542 (Mount Baker Highway) ~12 miles to the DOT Shuksan maintenance facility and turn onto FR 3065. Mining truck traffic may be present in summer; check with the Mt. Baker Ranger District for current conditions.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate: wet, cloudy winters with heavy, wet snow and significant avalanche danger; drier summers under Pacific high pressure provide the main climbing window.

bySeason.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack persists into early summer; access road often still snowbound.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); expect some residual snow/ice in the entry gully into early summer.
bySeason.fall: Shorter weather windows; first snow can arrive by October.
bySeason.winter: Road and approach buried in snow; avalanche terrain, not a climbing objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker Ranger District (Mount Baker Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use fee (or $30 annual) at the Twin Lakes/Yellow Aster Butte trailhead
access.permit: No permit required for day climbs; wilderness group-size limits apply for overnight camping
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Pass accepted in lieu of the day-use fee
access.closures: FR 3065 (Twin Lakes Road) is closed by snow in winter/spring and subject to washouts; check Mt. Baker Ranger District alerts before driving

timing.recommendedStart: Predawn/alpine start recommended given the long approach traverse and multi-rappel descent

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Twin Lakes TH -> Gargett Basin (~5,400 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Low Pass, High Pass, Gargett Mine; timing from one reported ascent

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Gargett Basin -> South AmBo Saddle (~6,840 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Cross-country traverse of Mt. Larrabee's west flank; timing from one reported ascent
Includes roped chimney climbing; summit reached ~8.4 hrs after leaving camp in one reported ascent
3 rappels plus a nighttime return traverse in one reported ascent

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); Whatcom County Sheriff's Office coordinates Whatcom County Search & Rescue non-emergency
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center / Mt. Baker Ranger District - (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage on approach or peak; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Whatcom County Search and Rescue is dispatched via 911.

routes.corrections: Catalog listed 3 pitches; The Mountaineers' route description cites two short roped pitches (up to 5.4) bracketed by extensive 3rd/4th-class scrambling, so pitches was corrected to 2. Some trip reports (e.g., a Sept. 2012 account) found the chimney crux considerably harder to protect than 5.4, describing 5.8-equivalent moves in spots, though the consensus guidebook/route-page grade remains 5.4. Also, a Sept. 21, 1952 first ascent by Dwight Baker, Fred Beckey, and John Dudra sometimes cited for 'American Border Peak' actually refers to the separate Northeast Face route, not this Southeast Face line; the peak's overall first ascent was Sept. 14, 1930 (Dalgleish, Fyles, Henderson, Fraser), but it is not confirmed that party climbed this exact chimney pitch, so no route-level 'fa' is asserted.

blurb: American Border Peak (7,998 ft) rises in the Nooksack Mountains of the North Cascades, straddling the Washington–British Columbia border just north of Mount Larrabee and immediately south of its taller sister summit, Canadian Border Peak. Composed of notably loose, "rotten" rock, it sits within the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest) and offers steep, complex alpine terrain that belies its modest elevation. The peak was first climbed on September 14, 1930, by Alec Dalgleish, Tom Fyles, Stan Henderson, and R. A. Fraser.

objHaz: Loose, rotten phyllite/greenstone rock in the summit gully and ridge
Rockfall onto climbers below in the gully
Persistent steep snowfields between Low Pass and High Pass into midsummer
Avalanche terrain on the approach in early season
Cornices on the north side of the summit ridge
Black bear activity along the High Pass approach
routes.overview: The South Ridge is Mount Larrabee's standard, non-technical line: a long trail approach over Low and High Passes followed by an off-trail scramble up loose talus and a steep summit gully to gain the ridge crest, then a short ridge walk to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Twin Lakes trailhead, follow the maintained High Pass Trail over Low Pass and High Pass. Leave the trail near 6,400 ft and traverse/descend into the basin below the peak's south side, then climb boulder fields, scree, and dirt to the southwest spur at roughly 6,900 ft. A rocky face there holds two narrow gully channels about 30 ft apart — take the right-hand one, following it from a narrow slot into a more open gully of broken rock to reach the ridge crest. From there, follow the ridge, favoring the west side, to the summit. The rock throughout is notoriously loose and reddish, so helmets and small party sizes are strongly advised.
routes.approach: Drive Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) to Forest Road 3065 (Twin Lakes Road) and follow it to the Twin Lakes Trailhead; the final roughly 2 miles are a rough, unmaintained native-surface road recommended for high-clearance/4WD vehicles (if impassable, park at the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake trailhead and walk the road). From Twin Lakes, hike the High Pass Trail north over Low Pass and High Pass (about 2.3 miles), then leave the trail to descend into the basin below Larrabee's south side.
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the ascent route: downclimb the ridge and gully back to the southwest spur and basin, then regain the High Pass Trail for the walk out over High Pass and Low Pass to Twin Lakes.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ridge crest (favoring the west side) back down to where it was gained near 6,900 ft, then downclimb the loose gully — the right-hand of the two channels — back to broken rock and scree below the southwest spur. Descend the boulder fields and scree into the basin, then regain the maintained trail near High Pass for the hike out over Low Pass to the Twin Lakes trailhead. Move carefully and stay close together in the gully to minimize rockfall risk onto teammates below.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a long single day from the Twin Lakes trailhead: hike to High Pass, drop into the basin, climb the SW spur and summit gully/ridge, then reverse the route to the car. Some parties camp at Twin Lakes or in the basin below High Pass to shorten the summit day or wait out weather.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward: reverse the ridge and gully back to the basin below the southwest spur, then regain the maintained High Pass Trail, which offers a quick, low-angle walk back to Twin Lakes over High Pass and Low Pass.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting that accounts for the loose gully and ridge being far more hazardous to downclimb in the dark, in a storm, or as afternoon sun loosens rock and destabilizes lingering snow; descending the gully while other parties are below adds risk, so timing to avoid crowding matters as much as the clock.
routes.bestSeason: Most parties climb the South Ridge from July through September, after the steep snow approach below High Pass has melted out somewhat but before fall storms arrive. Early-season ascents (June) can find firmer snow that speeds travel through the boulder fields, but require an ice axe and comfort on steep, avalanche-prone slopes between Low Pass and High Pass; the gully near the summit can also hold snow into midsummer.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed rock rack is used on this scramble; the technical gear that matters is snow gear — an ice axe (and crampons or microspikes in early season) for the steep snowfields between Low Pass and High Pass and any lingering snow in the summit gully.
routes.proNeeds: None — this is unroped class 3-4 scrambling terrain; protection is not typically placed.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass for trailhead parking
Trekking poles for the scree/talus sections
Map/GPS for off-trail route-finding beyond High Pass
proTips: Keep parties small (6 or fewer) and stay tight together in the summit gully — the rock is notoriously loose and rockfall onto climbers below is the primary hazard.
Of the two narrow gully channels in the rocky face near 6,900 ft, take the right-hand one to reach the ridge crest.
Early-season snow (into July) can make the boulder fields and gully faster and more stable than the dry, loose talus found later in summer — carry an ice axe accordingly.
watchOut: Extremely loose, rotten rock throughout the upper mountain — helmets are essential.
Persistent steep snowfields on the approach above Low Pass/High Pass can linger into August, requiring ice axe and crampon skills.
Cornices can overhang the north side of the summit ridge into summer.
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose, chossy rock in the summit gully, especially with parties above or below.
Avalanche and steep-snow terrain between Low Pass and High Pass into early summer.
Black bear activity is common along the High Pass approach; store food securely.
Exposed scrambling with fall potential on the class 3-4 ridge crest and gully.

pitchDetail.notes: Maintained High Pass Trail via Low Pass then High Pass, roughly 2.3 miles one-way; can hold snow/avalanche debris into summer.

pitchDetail.pitch: High Pass basin to SW spur
pitchDetail.notes: Leave the trail near 6,400 ft, traverse/descend about 100-200 ft into the basin, then ascend boulder fields, scree, and dirt to the southwest spur near 6,900 ft.
Two narrow gully channels about 30 ft apart split the rocky face; take the right-hand one, ascending narrow then open gully through loose, broken rock to the ridge crest.
Follow the ridge crest, favoring the west side, to the summit.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (NF Road 3065)
road.status: Rough native-surface road; the final approximately 2 miles are unmaintained for passenger vehicles
road.driveNote: High-clearance/4WD recommended for the last 2 miles beyond the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake trailhead; that stretch is a remnant of an old mining-era road and can be muddy/slippery when wet.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Mt. Baker area (West Slopes North zone)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy North Cascades winters and a short climbing season; snow can linger on north- and shaded approach slopes into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack and avalanche hazard on the Low Pass/High Pass approach slopes; not the typical scramble season.
bySeason.summer: July-September is the primary climbing window, though steep snowfields on the approach can persist into July in heavy-snow years.
bySeason.fall: Stable weather windows common into September-October with fall color, but shortening days.
bySeason.winter: Access road typically snowed-in/closed; not a winter objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker Ranger District (Mt. Baker Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use or $30 annual pass at Twin Lakes Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (digital passes and Scan & Pay also honored); Washington Discover Pass is NOT accepted
access.closures: Mt. Baker Wilderness regulations apply: group size limited to 12, no campfires; the access road may be gated or impassable when snowed-in or washed out — check with the Glacier Public Service Center.

timing.recommendedStart: An early/alpine start is recommended to cross the High Pass snowfields while firm and to be off the loose summit gully before afternoon rockfall/thunderstorm risk increases

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Twin Lakes TH -> Low Pass -> High Pass
sectionBreakdown.note: Maintained trail, about 2.3 miles one-way; can hold snow/avalanche debris into summer.

Class 3-4 gully (right-hand of two channels) to the ridge crest, then ridge crest favoring the west side to the summit.

routes.corrections: The peak's documented first ascent (James J. McArthur's party, Sept 11, 1908, per Beckey/Wikipedia) is a PEAK first ascent, not confirmed to specifically be this South Ridge line, so 'fa' is left null rather than assumed. Mountain Project's area page lists a similar loose 4th-class line as the 'Southwest Route' (with an odd 'I 2, M 1b' rating that looks like a data artifact rather than a real mixed-climbing grade) — this may be the same feature described elsewhere as the 'South Ridge'/standard scramble, but the naming is not confirmed identical across sources, so it was not merged in. The given alpineGrade 'PD' and 'Grade II, Class 3-4' are consistent with the sourced route description (loose class 3-4 gully and ridge scrambling) and were not changed.

blurb: Mount Larrabee is a 7,865-foot summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, rising in the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest) just 1.4 miles south of the Canada-US border alongside American Border Peak. It is built of reddish, iron-oxidized interbedded phyllite and greenstone that gives it a rust-colored profile and notoriously loose, rotten rock. Originally named Red Mountain, it was renamed in 1951 for Bellingham's Charles F. Larrabee; the peak's first recorded ascent was by James J. McArthur's party on September 11, 1908.

waypoints.name: Tomyhoi Lake / Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead

objHaz: crevasses and unstable ice on the Tomyhoi Glacier
route-finding on the ridge boot path
routes.overview: The Southeast Ridge (Tomyhoi Arm) is Tomyhoi Peak's standard route: a long approach through Yellow Aster Meadows to gain the crest of the peak's broad southeast ridge, followed by benches above the Tomyhoi Glacier, a hard-snow/moat crux below the summit block, and a final Class 3-4 scramble on glacier-polished rock to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Tomyhoi/Yellow Aster Butte trailhead, hike the maintained trail through Yellow Aster Meadows, then leave the trail to gain the crest of the Tomyhoi Arm ridge. A boot path follows the ridge along benches above the retreating Tomyhoi Glacier to bypass rock gendarmes. Below the summit block, parties cross hard snow/ice around a small moat (the technical crux, often the site of a hip belay or hand-line), then finish on a Class 3-4 scramble over glacier-polished rock, past a false summit, to the true 7,439 ft summit.
routes.approach: Drive Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) east from Glacier about 12 miles to Twin Lakes Road (Forest Road 3065, near the Shuksan DOT maintenance yard); turn north and drive roughly 4.5-4.7 miles of gravel road to the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead (~3,600 ft). Hike the Yellow Aster Butte/Gold Run Pass trail to about 5,900 ft, bear onto the Yellow Aster Meadows trail, and cross the high basin of tarns toward the base of the Tomyhoi Arm ridge.
routes.descent: Descend by the same Southeast Ridge/Tomyhoi Arm route back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the Class 3-4 rock scramble down through the polished-rock band, recross the snow/moat section below the summit block, and follow the ridge-crest boot path back down to Yellow Aster Meadows, then the maintained trail to the trailhead. No rappels are typically needed; downclimb the loose upper sections carefully and glissade stable snow where present.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a single long day (roughly 10-14 hours round trip depending on conditions and pace); can be split into two days with a camp near Yellow Aster Meadows or the Tomyhoi Lake basin to shorten summit day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward back down the ascent ridge and boot path to Yellow Aster Meadows and the maintained trail; there is no established alternate descent line off the peak.
routes.turnaround: Because the return follows the same exposed ridge, scramble terrain, and moat crossing, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to reverse those sections before dark, and build in margin for fast-moving afternoon weather on the long, unshaded ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September. Earlier-season (June) ascents typically involve more continuous snow/ice near the summit moat and may call for an ice axe throughout; late-season (Aug-Sep) trips see a drier, more exposed rock scramble as the Tomyhoi Glacier's remnant recedes further each year.
routes.detailedRack: No traditional rock rack is needed for the standard scramble line. Carry an ice axe (crampons optional depending on conditions) for the snow/moat crossing below the summit, and a helmet for rockfall and loose terrain. Some parties bring a short (30m) rope to set a hip or hand belay across the Class 3.5 moat section.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is placed on this scramble; the moat/snow crux is generally protected with an ice-axe self-belay or a short hand-line rather than traditional rock gear.
gear: optional 30m rope for the moat crux
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee for the trailhead
extra layers for ridge wind and weather
ample water (limited sources high on the ridge)
proTips: Camping at Yellow Aster Meadows shortens the summit day if you split the trip into two days.
Later-season trips (Aug-Sep) generally avoid the steepest snow at the moat crossing but expose more bare, loose rock; June trips have more snow coverage but a firmer moat crossing.
The boot path along the Tomyhoi Arm ridge can be faint in places above the meadows -- route-find carefully and be ready to backtrack around gendarmes.
watchOut: Glacier-polished rock on the summit scramble is slippery, especially when wet.
The hard-snow/moat crossing just below the summit block is the route's technical crux and has caught descending parties running out of daylight.
The ridge is long and fully exposed to wind and afternoon thunderstorms -- watch weather closely and turn around early if conditions build.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock along the ridge and summit scramble.
Crevasses and unstable ice on the shrinking Tomyhoi Glacier, worsened during warm spells.
Sustained Class 3-4 exposed scrambling with real fall consequences.
A hard-snow or ice moat crossing directly below the summit block, widely described as the route's crux.

pitchDetail.notes: ~3 miles of maintained trail through forest and subalpine meadows to roughly 5,600-5,900 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Meadows to base of Tomyhoi Arm ridge
pitchDetail.notes: Cross-country travel over benches and a col (~6,266 ft per one trip report) to gain the ridge-crest boot path.

pitchDetail.pitch: Ridge crest to base of summit block
pitchDetail.notes: Follow the boot path along the Tomyhoi Arm, traversing benches above the Tomyhoi Glacier to bypass rock gendarmes.
Crux: hard snow or ice around a small moat below the summit block; ice axe (and sometimes a hand-line or belay) recommended.
Glacier-polished rock scramble via easy ledges past a false summit to the true 7,439 ft summit.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Road 3065)
road.status: Gravel, narrow with few pullouts, not maintained for passenger cars beyond the trailhead; possible mining-truck traffic in summer
road.driveNote: From Glacier, drive Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) east about 12 miles to Twin Lakes Road (FR 3065) near the Shuksan DOT maintenance yard; turn north and drive 4.5-4.7 miles of gravel road to the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead (~3,600 ft). High-clearance vehicles can continue about 2 more miles to Twin Lakes; 4WD is helpful but not required to that point.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate: wet, snowy winters and a shorter, drier, milder summer window; the ridge and summit are exposed to wind.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack lingers on the approach and ridge into June; avalanche and cornice hazard are possible on steeper slopes.
bySeason.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Aug); snow patches persist near the summit moat and gradually give way to bare rock/ice by late summer.
bySeason.fall: Cooler, often stable weather into September with the smallest snow/ice extent at the moat crossing, but shorter daylight and earlier storm systems.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and high avalanche danger make the route a winter mountaineering/ski objective rather than a standard scramble.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start (roughly 4-6 a.m.) given the long round-trip distance and exposed scramble terrain

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (7,439) matches Wikipedia and other authoritative sources; a few trip reports (Stephabegg, trailcatjim) list the summit at 7,435 ft, likely an older/rounded figure. Round-trip mileage and elevation gain vary noticeably by source: WTA reports 13.1 mi / 4,700 ft to a 7,294 ft high point that falls short of the true summit (i.e., the maintained-trail portion only), while Stephabegg reports 13.5 mi / 5,600 ft to the true 7,439 ft summit and trailcatjim reports 14 mi / 4,800 ft. This enrichment uses Stephabegg's summit-to-summit figures as the primary gain/loss/distance values. The peak's first ascent is disputed: commonly credited to Lage Wernstedt (1927), but some sources suggest Ben Thompson or Clarence Fisher may have made an early-1930s first ascent instead, so no route-specific 'fa' is asserted.

blurb: Tomyhoi Peak (7,439 ft) rises in the Skagit/Nooksack section of the North Cascades, about one mile south of the Canada-US border within the Mount Baker Wilderness of the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. Its glacier-polished summit block is bordered on the north flank by the shrinking Tomyhoi Glacier and offers close-up views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, Mount Larrabee, and the Border Peaks. The peak was likely first climbed in 1927 by forest surveyor Lage Wernstedt, though some records credit an early-1930s ascent by Ben Thompson or Clarence Fisher instead.

routes.overview: The Southwest Ridge is Mount Sefrit's standard route: a long approach from the Nooksack Cirque trail leads to open meadows and a snow/talus gully on the peak's south-southwest side, gaining the ridge crest for a short, sometimes-airy Class 3-4 scramble to the summit.
routes.beta: Most parties approach from the Nooksack Cirque trailhead, fording the North Fork Nooksack/Ruth Creek at the start, then following an old roadbed and climbers' path through steep, brushy forest to open meadows near 5,800 ft. From there the route trends toward an obvious gully/ramp on the peak's south-southwest side, which in early-to-mid season holds continuous snow requiring ice axe and crampons; late-season parties instead find loose talus and short Class 3-4 rock. The final scramble up the Southwest Ridge to the 7,191 ft summit is airy in spots but can generally be kept to Class 3 with careful route-finding, though it can feel like Class 4 off the best line.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA drive Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) east about 12.5 miles to Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32); follow it roughly a mile to the Nooksack Cirque Road (FS 34) junction and continue about a mile to the Nooksack Cirque trailhead. Ford Ruth Creek/the North Fork Nooksack right at the trailhead (no bridge - wade or find a log), then follow the old roadbed about 2 miles, continue through second growth to the Mt. Baker Wilderness boundary, and through old growth to the North Fork Nooksack riverbank. Leave the maintained trail here and climb steep, brushy, sparsely-flagged forest toward treeline near 4,600 ft, emerging into meadows around 5,800 ft below the southwest side of the peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the Southwest Ridge/gully to the meadows and back through the forest to the Nooksack Cirque trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the ridge crest back to the head of the ascent gully/ramp and descend it (facing in on any firm snow or the Class 3-4 rock) to the 5,800 ft meadows. Continue down through the steep, brushy forest along the ascent line to the North Fork Nooksack riverbank, then follow the trail/old roadbed back to the Ruth Creek/river crossing and the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a long single day from the trailhead (roughly 6 hours to the summit, give or take an hour); some parties camp in the meadows near 5,800 ft to split the approach and summit push over two days.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally straightforward back down the ascent gully and forest line in good visibility; in whiteout or unstable snow, avoid dropping into unfamiliar gullies off the ridge because of cliff bands, and stay on the ascent track.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets you back across Ruth Creek/the North Fork Nooksack before afternoon snowmelt raises the water level, and off the corniced summit ridge before afternoon warming increases avalanche and rockfall hazard.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in the Ruth Creek/Nooksack Cirque drainage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
routes.bestSeason: June through September offers the most favorable weather. A late-spring window (May-June) can provide a continuous snow ramp up the approach gully but carries higher avalanche risk, while late-summer/fall visits find the creek crossing lowest but more loose talus underfoot.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack (a few slings plus a couple of small-to-medium cams or nuts) can protect the short Class 4 rock steps near the summit for less confident scramblers, though most parties climb the ridge unroped.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal - this is primarily an unroped scramble, with a rope/light rack useful only for confidence on brief exposed Class 3-4 moves near the top.
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the Ruth Creek/river ford
Bug repellent (mosquitoes in the lower forest)
Sun protection for open meadow/snow travel
proTips: Time the Ruth Creek/North Fork Nooksack crossing for early morning when water is lowest; afternoon snowmelt can submerge log crossings.
Late spring through mid-summer, a continuous snow ramp still covers the approach gully, easing travel over what is loose talus later in the season.
The ridge feels airy near the top; careful route-finding keeps most of it to Class 3 rather than the harder Class 4 sometimes hit off the best line.
watchOut: Ruth Creek/North Fork Nooksack ford can become uncrossable by afternoon or after storms.
Corniced, potentially avalanche-prone summit ridge, especially in winter and early season.
Steep, brushy, poorly-flagged forest section below treeline where route-finding is easy to lose.
Loose rock on the short Class 3-4 steps near the summit.
knownHazards: Unbridged creek/river crossing at the trailhead with fluctuating flow.
Avalanche danger on the approach gully and corniced summit ridge in winter and early season.
Loose gabbro/greenschist rock on the summit block.
Dense brush (slide alder, devil's club) in the lower forest approach.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32) to Nooksack Cirque Road (FS 34)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service roads; subject to seasonal washout/flood damage
road.seasonalGate: Closed in winter; check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts for current opening
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA drive SR 542 east about 12.5 miles to Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32); follow it about a mile to the FS 34 junction, then FS 34 about a mile to the Nooksack Cirque trailhead (no facilities).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (Mount Baker zone)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy Pacific-driven precipitation, especially as winter snowfall; July-September brings the most stable, driest weather for climbing.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack on the approach gully; avalanche danger persists into early summer.
bySeason.summer: Best conditions June/July-September; residual snow on the approach lingers into July, giving way to talus, while the corniced ridge crest can persist into summer.
bySeason.fall: Drier for the creek crossing but shorter days and possible early season snow by October.
bySeason.winter: Access roads closed; heavy snowfall and high avalanche hazard make this a serious, treacherous winter objective.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service - Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mount Baker Ranger District (Mount Baker Wilderness)
access.fees: No climbing fee; a Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful pass) is requested for trailhead parking
access.permit: No climbing permit required for day trips; an NPS backcountry permit is needed only if camping within the adjoining North Cascades National Park
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass (Washington Discover Pass not valid)
access.closures: FS Roads 32/34 are typically gated/closed in winter; the Ruth Creek/North Fork Nooksack ford can be impassable in high water

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine/pre-dawn start recommended given the long approach and to cross Ruth Creek before afternoon snowmelt raises water levels

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Nooksack Cirque TH to old roadbed
sectionBreakdown.note: Unbridged Ruth Creek/river ford right at the start; log crossing depends on water level

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Old roadbed to North Fork Nooksack riverbank
sectionBreakdown.note: ~2 mi roadbed, 1 mi second growth to Wilderness boundary, ~0.5 mi old growth
Steep, brushy climbers' path with sparse flagging; treeline near 4,600 ft
Open meadow travel to the summit gully/ramp, then Class 3-4 rock/snow up the Southwest Ridge

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 emergency; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatches Whatcom County Search & Rescue, on-call 24/7
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, Mt. Baker Ranger District - (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA - (360) 734-5400 (Level II trauma center, only hospital in Whatcom County)
emergency.notes: Remote alpine terrain with no reliable cell coverage in the Ruth Creek/Nooksack Cirque drainage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.

blurb: Mount Sefrit (7,191 ft) is the high point of Nooksack Ridge in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, about three miles north of Mount Shuksan within the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest). The peak is built of gabbro and greenschist uplifted as the North Cascades formed roughly 50 million years ago, and its summit gives close views of Mount Shuksan's north face, Mount Baker, and the Picket Range. It was first climbed in 1930 by Jim Irving and Brick Spouse.

routes.fa: 1916 (party not documented in available sources; peak-level first ascent, attributed to this standard line)
routes.rappels: None - descend via the ascent (glacier) route
routes.overview: A moderate, largely non-technical glacier climb up Ruth Mountain's broad north slopes via the small Ruth Glacier, reached from Hannegan Pass; widely regarded as an approachable introduction to North Cascades glacier mountaineering.
routes.beta: From Hannegan Pass, a climbers' path angles east and south past a small pond and around rock outcrops before reaching the toe of the Ruth Glacier on the peak's north side. The route then winds between crevasses up moderate (Class 2-3) snow/glacier slopes to the summit ridge and register. In a typical dry summer the glacier is well-tracked and the crevasse hazard is manageable, but early- and late-season parties can encounter open crevasses, softening snow bridges, and a short, steep, washed-out section of trail above high camp.
routes.approach: Drive Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) east from Bellingham through the town of Glacier, then follow Forest Road 32 (Hannegan Pass Road) to its end at Hannegan Trailhead/Campground (~3,100 ft). Hike the Hannegan Pass Trail roughly 4 miles and ~2,000 ft of gain, through forest and open avalanche-chute terrain along Ruth Creek, to Hannegan Pass (~5,066-5,100 ft, sources vary slightly). From the pass, follow the unmaintained climbers' trail east and south, passing a small pond and traversing around cliff bands and a steep, washed-out slope, to the base of the Ruth Glacier.
routes.descent: Descend the same south-slopes/Ruth Glacier line back to Hannegan Pass, then out the maintained trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ascent line down the glacier, staying alert for the same crevasses passed on the way up (softer afternoon snow can obscure or weaken snow bridges). Regain the climbers' trail at the base of the glacier, re-cross the exposed washed-out slope and cliff-band traverse, and descend to Hannegan Pass, then follow the maintained Hannegan Pass Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a long day (car-to-car) in a dry, stable summer window, or as a 2-day trip: Day 1 - hike ~4 miles/2,000 ft to Hannegan Pass or a high camp near the base of the Ruth Glacier, with a glacier-travel/crevasse-rescue refresher; Day 2 - pre-dawn start, climb roughly 2 miles/2,000 ft up the glacier to the summit (about 3+ hours), then descend to camp and out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The route has no technical retreat problems - descend the glacier and climbers' trail the way you came to Hannegan Pass, then the maintained trail to the trailhead. In poor visibility, use your ascent track/GPS to stay off crevassed terrain.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting that accounts for softening snow and deteriorating weather/visibility on the glacier, plus the exposed washed-out trail section above camp; abandon the summit push rather than descend the glacier or that slope in the dark or in a whiteout.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August is the classic window, when the Hannegan Pass Trail is snow-free and the glacier is more consolidated with fewer open crevasses. Earlier in the season (June) the approach carries lingering snow and avalanche exposure on the pass trail and gully sections; by late September/October crevasses on the Ruth Glacier are more open and early-season snow can already be falling.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable through most of the approach and route in this part of the North Cascades backcountry; reception is sometimes available from ridgelines/high points (per NPS Climb Safely guidance).
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed. Bring a standard glacier-travel kit: ice axe, crampons, a rope for the team (set up for glacier travel/crevasse rescue), harness, a few slings/prusiks and a pulley for crevasse rescue, plus a picket or two if traveling roped through the more crevassed sections.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are glacier-travel oriented rather than rock or ice protection: a rope for the team, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks/ascenders, pulley, one or two pickets), and a helmet for rockfall on the cliff-band traverse below the glacier.
proTips: Many parties climb the Ruth Glacier unroped in dry, well-tracked August conditions, but hidden crevasses have caught climbers off guard even then - rope up if you're unsure of conditions.
Camp with water access at Hannegan Pass/Hannegan Camp, or push on to the alpine 'Ruth Arm' area for a shorter summit day.
Get the required backcountry permit in person at the Glacier Public Service Center if you plan to camp overnight once inside North Cascades National Park.
Start pre-dawn to cross the glacier while the snow is still firm and to beat afternoon thunderstorms.
watchOut: A steep, washed-out section of the climbers' trail above camp requires using tree roots as handholds and has real fall exposure.
Crevasses flank the boot track on the Ruth Glacier and can be obscured by early- or late-season snow bridges.
The lower trail is brushy and buggy in early-to-mid summer - expect mosquitoes.
Weather and visibility change quickly at this elevation; the cliff-band traverse below the glacier is no place to be in a whiteout.
knownHazards: Open and hidden crevasses on the Ruth Glacier, worse in early and late season.
Avalanche terrain along the Hannegan Pass Road/Trail corridor in winter and early spring.
Rockfall potential on the cliff-band traverse between Hannegan Pass and the glacier.
Steep, exposed, washed-out trail tread above high camp.
Afternoon thunderstorms and rapidly forming whiteout conditions common to North Cascades summers.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: Gravel forest road, rough and potholed in sections; high-clearance recommended. Has experienced washout/flood damage near the trailhead in recent years, at times limiting access for larger vehicles.
road.seasonalGate: Not gated at Hannegan Trailhead itself, but generally impassable/unplowed under winter snow; sections beyond the Goat Mountain trailhead cross avalanche terrain.
road.driveNote: From Bellingham, take Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) east through the town of Glacier, then follow FR 32 (Hannegan Pass Road) to its end at Hannegan Trailhead/Campground.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (Mount Baker area)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced North Cascades climate with a heavy winter snowpack and a drier, more stable summer window.

bySeason.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack with avalanche hazard on the pass trail and approach slopes.
bySeason.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Aug); glacier most consolidated but still crevassed; afternoon thunderstorm risk.
bySeason.fall: Increasing crevasse exposure as snow bridges melt out; early-season snow/storms possible by late September-October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain; road and route are generally not maintained or safely accessible.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mt. Baker Ranger District) for the trailhead/approach; North Cascades National Park Service Complex for the upper mountain and summit.
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30/year day-use fee at Hannegan Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass accepted).
access.permit: Free backcountry/wilderness permit required for overnight camping once within North Cascades National Park boundary; obtain in person at the Glacier Public Service Center.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful interagency day-use pass for trailhead parking.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (alpine start) to cross the glacier on firm snow before it softens.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Hannegan Trailhead (~3,100 ft) to Hannegan Pass (~5,066-5,100 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: 4 miles of maintained trail, ~2,000 ft gain.

sectionBreakdown.section: Hannegan Pass to base of Ruth Glacier
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Hannegan Pass to toe of Ruth Glacier
sectionBreakdown.note: Unmaintained climbers' trail past a pond and cliff bands, including a steep washed-out slope.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Base of Ruth Glacier to summit (7,115 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Roped or unroped (conditions-dependent) glacier travel between crevasses on moderate slopes.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-384-5390 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center (joint USFS/NPS), 360-599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center / Haggen Family Emergency and Trauma Center, Bellingham, WA (Level II Trauma Center)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable through most of the approach and route; reception is sometimes available from ridgelines/high points (per NPS Climb Safely guidance).

routes.corrections: Added a peak-level first-ascent year (1916, party not documented) from Wikipedia/USGS-derived sources; not previously in the given data. Approach elevation-gain figures vary slightly across guide sources (roughly 4,100-4,200 ft total trailhead-to-summit); reconciled to ~4,100 ft. Hannegan Pass elevation is cited variously as 5,056, 5,066, and 5,100 ft depending on source. Given grade ('Grade II, Class 2-3', PD) and elevation (7,115 ft) are corroborated by multiple sources and unchanged.

blurb: Ruth Mountain (7,115 ft) is a glaciated summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, a remnant of the ancient Hannegan Caldera, two miles south of Hannegan Pass on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest). The small Ruth Glacier caps its north side, and the peak is a popular introductory glacier climb, first ascended in 1916. Summit views take in Mount Shuksan, the Nooksack Cirque, Icy Peak, and the Picket Range.

routes.rappels: One rappel (natural sling/horn anchor, roughly a 30m line) off a boulder near the top of the summit gully gets past the steepest step; the rest of the descent is down-climbed/walked.
objHaz: Rockfall on Icy Peak's upper slopes, worse in warm afternoon conditions
Loose, slabby rock in the summit gully
Unbridged Ruth Creek ford at the Nooksack Cirque trailhead, with fast-changing water levels
Avalanche terrain along the approach road/basin in winter and early spring
Black flies and mosquitoes on the lower approach in summer
routes.overview: A remote southwest-side line up the Icy Glacier, approached from the Nooksack Cirque: climbers wind between crevasses toward the rock horn southwest of the summit, cross a notch, then finish up a short, exposed Class 3-4 gully to the twin summit blocks.
routes.beta: From the Nooksack Cirque Trailhead, follow the primitive river-bed route up the North Fork Nooksack to the base of Nooksack Cirque, then climb moraine debris onto the western arm of Icy Peak - considered the hardest route-finding of the trip. From there, ascend the Icy Glacier, weaving between crevasses to a point southwest of the peak's northwest rock horn. Around 6,500 ft, step through a notch in the ridge coming west off the summit and continue roughly 500 more feet to the base of the summit gully. The gully - worn clean by traffic - gives Class 3-4 scrambling, exposed with some slabby and loose sections, to the summit block. Helmets are recommended/required by local club programs running this peak.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, drive Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) east about 12.5 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center, turn left on Hannegan Pass Road (FR 32), go just over 1 mile to the junction with Nooksack Cirque Road (FR 34), then follow FR 34 about 1 mile to the Nooksack Cirque Trailhead. Ford Ruth Creek at the trailhead (no bridge; a log crossing may or may not be available). Follow the Nooksack Cirque Trail - maintained for about 4 miles, then primitive/riverbed travel with brush and gravel bars - roughly 9 miles one-way to the base of the cirque, then climb moraine and heather/broken-rock ridge onto Icy's southwest side to reach the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent gully and glacier; rappel the short crux step off the horn/boulder sling anchor near the gully top, then downclimb/walk the glacier and moraine back to the cirque and trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the Class 3-4 gully to the sling/horn rappel anchor and make a single rappel (roughly 30m) past the steepest section. Continue down through the notch and back onto the Icy Glacier, re-crossing crevassed terrain on the same line used for the ascent. Descend the moraine and heather/rock ridge back into Nooksack Cirque and retrace the riverbed/trail to the trailhead, re-fording Ruth Creek.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Nooksack Cirque Trailhead, ford Ruth Creek, and hike/bushwhack roughly 9 miles up the cirque to a base camp near the moraine (e.g., a large boulder used historically as a camp). Day 2: Pre-dawn start up the moraine and Icy Glacier, through the ~6,500 ft notch, and up the summit gully; descend the same way to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally back down the same glacier and gully line used on the ascent; there are no documented fixed bail anchors beyond the summit-gully rappel horn, so parties should be prepared to downclimb or self-equip a rappel lower on the route if needed.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time based on remaining daylight, weather, and how the glacier and gully are softening through the day - loose rock and crevasse bridges both become less trustworthy in the afternoon sun, and the long cross-country approach leaves little margin for a late start or slow return.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September gives the most reliable weather and firmer glacier travel; go early enough in summer for continuous snow cover over the moraine and gully approach, but note Ruth Creek at the trailhead can be high and dangerous to ford in early-season high water. Fall brings lower water levels and easier cirque travel but less snow coverage on the approach.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit (crampons, ice axe, rope, crevasse-rescue gear) for the Icy Glacier crossing, plus a light rock rack/runners and a 30m (or longer) rope for the single rappel off the summit-gully horn anchor. A helmet is recommended for the rockfall-prone upper mountain and gully.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is needed - the crux is a short, exposed Class 3-4 scramble typically protected by handholds/spotting rather than placed gear; carry a few slings/runners to back up or replace the existing horn-sling rappel anchor at the top of the gully.
gear: A few slings/runners for the summit-gully rap anchor
whatToBring: Sturdy footwear or sandals for the unbridged Ruth Creek ford
Bug protection (black flies and mosquitoes common on the lower approach)
Extra water for the long riverbed approach
Approach shoes or light boots suitable for both scrambling and glacier travel
proTips: The moraine route-finding onto Icy's western arm above Nooksack Cirque is considered the hardest navigation on the route - expect no established trail and steep, loose sections.
Time the trip for continuous snow cover if possible; it makes the moraine and glacier travel faster and safer than bare loose talus.
Check Ruth Creek's water level before committing - it can be unfordable in the morning-vs-afternoon swing during high runoff.
A firm overnight refreeze significantly reduces crevasse-fall risk on the Icy Glacier for parties considering lighter glacier gear.
watchOut: Ruth Creek at the trailhead has no bridge and can rise to unfordable levels quickly, especially in early season.
Rockfall increases on Icy Peak's upper slopes as the day warms.
Route-finding through the moraine/heather ridge above the cirque is steep and unmarked.
Crevasses open up on the Icy Glacier later in the season.
knownHazards: Glacier travel across the Icy Glacier exposes parties to crevasse fall, especially as the season progresses and snow bridges weaken.
The summit gully's rock is described as slabby and loose in places despite being 'worn clean' by traffic.
The Nooksack Cirque approach crosses avalanche terrain on the access road near Goat Mountain Trailhead in winter/spring.
The unbridged Ruth Creek crossing at the trailhead poses a drowning/injury hazard in high water.

pitchDetail.notes: Riverbed and brush travel up Nooksack Cirque, then moraine debris onto Icy's western arm - the route's hardest navigation.
Wind between crevasses to a point southwest of the peak's northwest rock horn.
At roughly 6,500 ft, step through a notch in the ridge coming west off the summit and continue about 500 more feet toward the base of the summit gully.
Exposed scramble up a gully worn clean by traffic, with some slabby, loose rock, to the twin summit blocks.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (FR 32) to Nooksack Cirque Road (FR 34)
road.status: Open gravel road; recent reports describe it as passable to standard vehicles with some potholes/washboard
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA drive SR 542 east about 12.5 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center, turn left onto Hannegan Pass Road (FR 32), continue just over 1 mile to the junction with Nooksack Cirque Road (FR 34), then follow FR 34 about 1 mile to the Nooksack Cirque Trailhead at road's end.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Mount Baker zone
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy orographic precipitation; wet, heavy maritime snowpack drives avalanche danger on the approach in winter/spring.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable maritime snowpack lingers; Ruth Creek at the trailhead can run high and be dangerous to ford.
bySeason.summer: July through September offers the most reliable weather and firmest glacier travel; black flies and mosquitoes are common on the lower approach.
bySeason.fall: Lower water levels ease the Ruth Creek ford and cirque travel, though snow coverage on the moraine/glacier approach diminishes.
bySeason.winter: Rarely climbed; only one documented winter ascent via the Nooksack Cirque route (1988).

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mt. Baker Wilderness) for the approach; North Cascades National Park for the upper mountain and summit
access.fees: No fee/parking pass required at the Nooksack Cirque Trailhead itself
access.permit: A North Cascades National Park backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay once within the Park boundary (available via the Glacier Public Service Center or recreation.gov)
access.closures: No standing closures reported; Ruth Creek at the trailhead has no bridge and may be unfordable in high water, especially early season

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start from a cirque camp to catch a firm glacier refreeze

sectionBreakdown.note: Roughly 3 miles and 3,500 ft of gain from a cirque-area camp, per a June trip report
Descent via the same gully/glacier line

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office (911; coordinates volunteer Whatcom County Search and Rescue units for backcountry incidents)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park - Glacier Public Service Center, (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA - (360) 734-5400 (Level II trauma center; only hospital in Whatcom County)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is not documented for this remote cirque approach; parties should carry a satellite communicator. The route crosses between Forest Service and National Park jurisdiction, so both agencies may be involved in a rescue.

routes.corrections: Icy Peak has two summits about 11 feet apart in elevation; the Southeast summit is the true high point (7,073 ft) per Beckey's guide, while the Northwest summit is the one most parties reach because it is easier to access - relevant when confirming which summit a given trip report describes.

blurb: Icy Peak (7,073 ft) is a Skagit Range summit in the North Cascades of Whatcom County, Washington, standing within North Cascades National Park at the head of Nooksack Cirque and linked by high ridges to Seahpo Peak and Mount Shuksan. It is a remnant of the ancient Hannegan Caldera volcano, with small glaciers - including the Icy Glacier - cladding its flanks and an icefall occupying its northeast cirque. Rarely climbed compared with its neighbors, the peak has twin summits roughly 11 feet apart in elevation and is typically reached by a glacier approach followed by a short, exposed Class 3-4 rock scramble to the summit block.

objHaz: loose rock and exposure in the summit gully
lingering snow/avalanche terrain into early summer (40-50 deg)
off-trail route-finding through brush and basin terrain
routes.overview: The standard scramble follows the maintained Goat Mountain Trail up through forest and alpine meadows to a ridge-crest viewpoint, then leaves the trail to drop into a basin and climb an easy but exposed Class 2 gully to the narrow, rocky East Peak summit.
routes.beta: Most parties hike the Goat Mountain Trail (#673) roughly 4-4.5 miles and 3,000+ ft to the 5,400-5,600 ft ridge-crest viewpoint, then leave the trail, lose about 300 ft into a basin, and regain terrain toward the East Peak. The final push climbs a Class 2 gully that narrows and becomes exposed on both sides near the top; parties are advised to stay inside the gully. Steep heather and residual snow below the summit call for an ice axe/poles well into early summer. The West Peak (a shorter, easier trail-plus-scramble objective) is a separate, lower summit reached via the same trailhead.
routes.approach: From Glacier, drive SR 542 east about 12.5 miles, turn left (north) onto Hannegan Pass Road (FS Road 32), and continue 2.1 miles to the Goat Mountain Trailhead (2,470 ft; two parking areas, vault toilet). Follow Trail #673 through a series of switchbacks in old-growth forest recovering from an old fire, then along a side-hill ridge into alpine meadows around 2.7 miles, reaching the ridge-crest viewpoint near 5,400-5,600 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the Class 2 summit gully back into the basin, regain the ridge-crest trail, and follow the maintained trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the East Peak summit, carefully downclimb the narrow rocky spine and Class 2 gully back into the basin below, staying within the gully where the route narrows and the ground drops away on either side. Regain the roughly 300 ft back up to the ridge-crest viewpoint (~5,600 ft), then follow the maintained Goat Mountain Trail back down through meadows and forest switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed car-to-car in a single long day: an early trailhead start, roughly 5 hours to the summit, and roughly 3 hours back down (about 8-9 hours total). Parties wanting a more relaxed pace sometimes camp in the upper basin/meadow area and summit the next morning.
routes.bail: There is no shortcut off the peak once past the basin; retreat is back down the same gully/ridge line into the basin and out via the maintained trail. Turn back before committing to the exposed upper gully if weather, snow conditions, or daylight are marginal.
routes.turnaround: Given the long round-trip distance and elevation gain plus the exposed, narrow summit terrain, set a turnaround time before starting and stick to it — heather and any remaining snow become more hazardous to descend later in the day, especially if afternoon weather moves in.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring through summer is the usual window. Snow can linger on the upper basin and final gully into July, so an ice axe and the willingness to self-arrest are recommended even for a 'dry season' summer attempt; by late summer the heather slopes are dry but still steep and slick underfoot.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection or rope is used on the standard line; the only 'gear' most parties carry for security is an ice axe (and sometimes poles) for the steep heather and any residual snow below the summit.
whatToBring: Recreation/day-use pass for the trailhead ($5/vehicle or annual pass; Northwest Forest Pass and Interagency passes honored)
self-issue wilderness permit from the trailhead box
water treatment (no potable water at trailhead)
layers for exposed ridge and summit weather
proTips: Carry an ice axe and know how to self-arrest — the final gully and heather hold snow into July and are treacherous when wet or icy.
Stay inside the Class 2 gully near the summit; the terrain narrows and drops off steeply on either side of it.
The East Peak is a notably longer, harder day than the West Peak despite being only about 170 ft higher — budget extra time and daylight for the basin detour.
watchOut: Steep, slippery heather on the final approach to the summit; an ice axe or poles are recommended even in dry conditions.
Dropping roughly 300 ft into a basin then regaining it adds significant extra elevation gain and loss beyond the maintained trail.
The summit itself is a narrow rocky spine that drops off on all sides.
knownHazards: Snow slopes in the 40-50 degree range can persist into early summer on the approach to the East Peak, presenting slip-and-fall and avalanche hazard.
Loose rock and exposure on the final scramble gully to the East Peak summit.
Black bears are common in the area; standard food-storage precautions apply.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Service Road 32)
road.status: Gravel forest road, generally passable to the trailhead in season
road.seasonalGate: May be snowed-in or gated in winter/early spring; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving
road.driveNote: From Glacier, drive SR 542 east 12.5 miles, turn left onto FS Road 32, and continue 2.1 miles to the Goat Mountain Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (Mount Baker area)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascades weather with rapid changes possible even in summer; snow can linger on north-facing basin terrain into July.

bySeason.spring: Trail is often snow-covered above the meadows; ice axe/traction typically required for the upper mountain.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (roughly June-September); heather slopes are usually dry but still steep, and some snow can linger into early summer.
bySeason.fall: Generally dry and clear but shorter daylight; early snow possible by late season.
bySeason.winter: Full winter mountaineering/avalanche conditions; not the standard scramble season.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness)
access.fees: $5 per vehicle day-use fee at the trailhead (or annual Recreation Pass)
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit available at the trailhead; maximum party size of 12
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or other accepted Interagency Recreation Pass (Washington State Discover Pass is not accepted at this Forest Service site)

timing.recommendedStart: Early/pre-dawn start recommended given the long car-to-car day

sectionBreakdown.note: Maintained trail through forest and alpine meadows, about 4-4.5 miles
Off-trail: drop ~300 ft into a basin, then climb the Class 2 summit gully
