routes.corrections: Route elevation/high point: multiple sources (Wikipedia, listsofjohn.com-derived figures, trip reports) put the East Peak — the true, higher summit — at 6,891 ft, essentially matching the catalog's 6,892 ft (normal survey rounding). Wikipedia's main infobox for 'Goat Mountain' separately lists 6,844 ft, which appears to be an older/rounded or differently-referenced figure; the subsidiary West Peak is lower, at about 6,721-6,725 ft. No source specifically names a 'South Ridge' line — trip reports describe the standard scramble via the trail, a basin, and a Class 2 gully/west-facing terrain to the East Peak, or via the West Ridge/SW face to the West Peak; treat the route name as the generic standard-scramble line to the higher (East) summit.

blurb: Goat Mountain is a double-summited peak in the North Cascades' Nooksack/Skagit Range, sitting in the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest) about three miles south of the Canadian border between Ruth Creek and the Nooksack River. The mountain is composed of Darrington Phyllite and carries remnants of the small Swamp Creek Glacier between its West Peak (~6,721-6,725 ft) and higher East Peak (~6,891 ft); it is a popular non-technical scramble with views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, and Mount Sefrit. Frank Calkins and George Otis Smith made the first recorded ascent of the mountain in 1904.

waypoints.name: Middle Fork Nooksack gated bridge/logging-road access

routes.overview: The classic and most-climbed line on South Twin: a long, mostly non-technical scramble up the west-facing ridge from Orsino Creek Basin, with a short exposed low-5th-class section near the top that most parties bypass on gullies to the south.
routes.beta: From the Orsino Creek Basin, ascend steep dirt/scree/talus to a notch near 5,200 ft where the ridge proper begins. Class 2-3 scrambling follows the crest (sometimes on the solid right/south side, sometimes on looser rock to the left) to a high notch about 400 ft below the summit. From there, staying directly on the crest hits a couple of low-5th-class spots best protected with a rope; most parties instead traverse south around a seasonal snowfield into broad gullies (Class 2-3) that lead to the summit.
routes.approach: Approach via the Middle Fork Nooksack Road out of Acme/Mosquito Lake Road to a gated bridge on private timberland (Dailey Prairie/Dailey Quarry Road). From the gate, bike (strongly recommended) several miles of logging road to Dailey Prairie, then continue toward Orsino Creek, following a brushy old road/climbers' path through timber, across a red talus slope, past the ruins of an old cabin, and into Orsino Creek Basin below the west ridge.
routes.descent: Descend the same way (West Ridge).
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent line back down the ridge to the high notch, then down through the talus/scree basin. Below the notch, some parties find it easier to drop into the scree slope beneath the ridge and re-ascend slightly to the lower notch rather than retracing every step of the crest, since route-finding through the upper notch area can be tedious and mildly technical both ways. If descending off the north side is considered instead, expect a loose dirt gully followed by a hard snow slope requiring an ice axe.
routes.bail: The ridge can be abandoned at most points by dropping into the talus/scree basin on either side and returning to the Orsino Creek approach path; the crux terrain near the high notch is the main place where retreat requires care (down-climb or short rappel rather than continuing over exposed low-5th terrain).
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote approach, set a firm turnaround time based on your pace on the logging-road/basin approach so the ridge and descent aren't rushed in fading light or deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September once the upper talus and ridge notch have shed most seasonal snow; the approach slopes and notch area can hold snow well into summer in an average year, which adds difficulty and fall hazard on the scramble terrain.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack is sufficient: a handful of small-to-mid cams, a few nuts, slings, and a 30-60m rope carried mainly as a safety net for the short low-5th-class section near the high notch. Most parties climb the majority of the route unroped in sturdy boots or approach shoes.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear needed overall; protection is only for the brief technical step(s) below the summit, not sustained climbing.
gear: sturdy approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: mountain bike for the logging-road approach
detailed map/GPS track (the logging-road network has many confusing unmarked spurs)
extra water — Orsino Creek is the only reliable source
sun protection for exposed talus and ridge sections
proTips: Bring a bike for the logging-road miles — most parties ride the majority of the roughly 12-mile approach to save hours of walking.
Call the private-land gate operator ahead of time to confirm the road/gate is open before committing to the long drive.
Fill water at Orsino Creek in both directions; it's the only dependable source on the route.
watchOut: The final ~400 ft below the summit is exposed low-5th-class terrain if the ridge crest is followed directly.
Upper talus and the ridge notch can hold snow well into summer, adding fall hazard.
Descent route-finding through the notch area is tedious and has led parties to detour onto the scree slope below.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the ridge's north (climber's left) side.
Very long, remote approach on unmarked private logging roads — navigation error is a real risk.

Access gate on private timberland is sometimes closed to the public; a June 2026 multi-day search-and-rescue operation for a missing scrambler was centered on this peak and the nearby Sisters Glacier, underscoring the area's remoteness.

road.name: Dailey Prairie Road / Dailey Quarry Road (Middle Fork Nooksack logging roads)
road.status: Gated, private industrial timberland (historically Weyerhaeuser, later Hampton Family Forests/Longview Fibre-associated ownership); rough road with many unmarked spur junctions
road.driveNote: Cross the Middle Fork Nooksack River bridge/gate off Mosquito Lake Road near Acme; the road continues many miles into the range. A mountain bike is strongly recommended for the lower miles to cut hours off the approach.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (Mount Baker area)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy Pacific-storm precipitation and orographic snowfall; conditions are wettest and snowiest outside summer.

bySeason.spring: Substantial snowpack lingers on approach slopes and the ridge into early summer.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep); talus and ridge mostly snow-free by mid-to-late summer in an average year.
bySeason.fall: Snow-free but shortening daylight and earlier storms narrow the window into October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche-prone conditions; not a standard climbing season for this route.

access.landManager: Private industrial timberland (approach) adjoining Mount Baker Wilderness/Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest near the summit
access.permit: The north (Dailey Prairie) approach crosses gated private timberland; public keys are no longer issued for this gate. Call ahead (Olivine Corp., (360) 733-3332) to check gate status before you go — enter at your own risk if it happens to be open.
access.closures: The Dailey Prairie gate has effectively been closed to public access in recent years due to dumping/vandalism concerns; access is inconsistent and should be confirmed in advance.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start given the long approach

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Gate to Orsino Creek Basin approach
sectionBreakdown.note: ~1.5 mi by vehicle plus ~7 mi biking logging roads (per one recorded trip)

Hiking plus biking out

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: (360) 676-6911 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, 810 State Route 20, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284, (360) 854-2553 (nearest USFS station; approach itself is on private timberland, not NF land)

emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search & Rescue Council coordinates backcountry rescues in this range; the approach is remote with unreliable navigation on private logging roads, which has complicated past search operations near this peak.

routes.corrections: Multiple independent sources (Wikipedia citing USGS, Mountain Project, Peakbagger) list South Twin Sister's summit elevation as roughly 7,000-7,040 ft (Wikipedia: 7,004+ ft), not 6,935 ft as given in the input data.

routes.overview: A longer, more sustained ridge line up South Twin's north side, featuring enjoyable low-5th-class rock climbing on the lower ridge near a feature known as Arrowtip Tower before joining scramble terrain higher up.
routes.beta: Gain the ridge crest from the saddle/basin area below (either scrambling steep rock or, depending on season, climbing steep snow). From several possible gain points, work eastward at or near the crest, dropping slightly onto the south side where obstacles bar the way. Around 6,200 ft a 15-ft step must be down-climbed (a bypass on the south side may exist). Beyond it, cairned, exposed ledges lead across the south side of the ridge. The last ~400 vertical ft are the steepest and most exposed on the route (perhaps low 5th class); staying on the crest here is best done roped, or it can be avoided by traversing around a seasonal snowfield onto the south side and scrambling up gullies to the summit.
routes.approach: Approached via the same Dailey Prairie logging-road network as the West Ridge, then into the basin toward the North Twin/South Twin saddle area to gain the base of the north ridge (lower ridge sections offer optional low-5th rock pitches, notably near Arrowtip Tower).
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the West Ridge / south gullies rather than reversing the North Ridge.
routes.descentText: Traverse from the summit toward the high notch shared with the West Ridge and descend via the south-side gullies and West Ridge talus back to Orsino Creek Basin, avoiding down-climbing the North Ridge's exposed sections.
routes.bail: Retreat is easiest from below the 6,200-ft step or below the final 400-ft headwall by dropping onto the south-side ledges/snowfield rather than continuing up the crest.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux terrain is high on the route and descent is typically via a different line, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to negotiate both the summit block and the West Ridge descent.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep; the ridge is reported to hold snow into late season in an average year, which affects the character (and difficulty) of the lower gain points onto the crest.
routes.detailedRack: A small rack of cams and nuts plus a rope is worthwhile for the low-5th rock near Arrowtip Tower and for the exposed final headwall if staying on the crest; many parties otherwise treat it as an exposed scramble.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is optional/spot-use on the two technical sections (lower rock step, summit headwall) rather than continuous.
gear: ice axe (early/late season snow on the crest)
whatToBring: mountain bike for the logging-road approach
map/GPS for route-finding on the ridge
proTips: The lower ridge below Arrowtip Tower gives genuinely fun low-5th rock climbing for parties who want a rope out.
Descending the West Ridge/south gullies instead of reversing the North Ridge is the standard, faster option.
watchOut: A 15-ft step near 6,200 ft requires a down-climb (or a possible south-side bypass).
The final ~400 vertical ft are the steepest, most exposed terrain on the route — rope up if staying on the crest.
Exposed, cairned ledge traverses on the south side below the summit.
knownHazards: Loose/exposed rock on ledge traverses.
Seasonal snow or ice on the ridge crest, especially early season.
Long, remote approach shared with the West Ridge on unmarked private logging roads.

bySeason.spring: Ridge and gain points typically still snow-covered.

bySeason.fall: Snow-free but a narrowing weather window.
bySeason.winter: Not a standard season for this route.

access.closures: North (Dailey Prairie) gate access is inconsistent for the public; confirm before relying on it.

routes.face: Orsino Creek Basin / south-facing summit gullies
routes.overview: The easiest, fully non-technical way up South Twin Sister: a Class 2-3 talus/scree and gully scramble from Orsino Creek Basin that avoids the low-5th-class terrain of the West and North Ridges by traversing to south-facing summit gullies.
routes.beta: From Dailey Prairie, follow the old forest-service road into the basin between the Twin Sisters, then a climbers' trail through timber, bearing right to a talus slope (often snow-covered into summer). Climb the talus toward the west ridge, and once at a high notch about 400 ft below the summit, traverse south to reach broad gullies that lead directly to the top (Class 2-3 throughout), avoiding the ridge-crest's technical sections entirely.
routes.approach: Same Middle Fork Nooksack / Dailey Prairie logging-road approach as the West Ridge: gated bridge, several miles of biking, then a climbers' path through timber and talus into Orsino Creek Basin.
routes.descent: Descend the same scramble route.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent gullies and talus back to the notch and down through Orsino Creek Basin to the logging-road approach; snow-covered talus can make glissading a fast option in early/mid season where safe.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at nearly any point on this route by descending the talus/scree back into the basin.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and afternoon convective weather common in the Cascades, turn around with enough margin to be off the exposed upper talus and gullies before storms build.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, once the talus and gullies below the notch have mostly melted out; snow can linger into summer in an average year and adds a fall hazard on the steep dirt/scree sections.
routes.proNeeds: None — this is a non-technical scramble; no rope or rack is typically carried.
whatToBring: mountain bike for the logging-road approach
ice axe if snow lingers on the talus/gullies
extra water — Orsino Creek is the only reliable source
map/GPS for the confusing logging-road network
proTips: This line is the way to avoid the technical sections of both ridge routes while still reaching the true summit.
Start low on the talus rather than trying to gain the crest early — it's an easier line.
watchOut: Talus and scree below the notch are loose and tiring, especially descending on tired legs.
Snow can persist on the talus and in the summit gullies into summer, requiring an ice axe and careful footing.
knownHazards: Loose rock underfoot on extended talus/scree sections.
Long, remote approach on unmarked private logging roads.
Rockfall risk from parties above on the same gully system.

routes.corrections: Sourced summit elevation (~7,000-7,040 ft per Wikipedia/USGS, Mountain Project, Peakbagger) conflicts with the 6,935 ft given in the input data.

blurb: South Twin Sister is the high point of the Twin Sisters Range, a jagged, glaciated sub-range of the North Cascades about 10 miles southwest of Mount Baker in Whatcom County. Unlike most of the Cascades, the range is built of dunite, an unusual reddish-orange, olivine-rich ultramafic rock prized by scramblers for its exceptionally solid, high-friction ("sandpaper") texture. The peak was first climbed August 1, 1891 by J.M. Edson, E.A. Hegg, and P.J. Parris; today access is complicated by miles of gated private industrial timberland surrounding the range.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevationFt is 6510; multiple corroborating sources (Wikipedia/NRHP nomination #87001188, WTA, National Historic Lookout Register) consistently give the summit/lookout elevation as 6,521 ft, used here as highPointFt. No route-specific first ascent is documented — the historic 1858 first ascent of the peak by Henry Custer's boundary survey party predates the modern Trail #685 line built for lookout access in the 1930s, so 'fa' is left null per the peak-vs-route FA distinction.
routes.overview: A short, steep 3.4-mile round-trip trail (Trail #685) climbing from the Twin Lakes basin over a heather-and-blueberry shoulder to the historic Winchester Mountain fire lookout, widely cited as one of the best view-to-effort ratios in the North Cascades.
routes.beta: From the trailhead between the two Twin Lakes, the trail climbs moderately steeply through blueberry and heather slopes, meeting the High Pass Trail (#676) junction at about a quarter to half mile (stay left/straight for Winchester). It continues in tight switchbacks through fireweed and blueberries, crests a saddle/pass near 1.3 miles, then follows the final ridge to the restored 1935 lookout at the 6,521-ft summit. A short rock-cut section near the top is exposed and requires care, especially with children or dogs.
routes.approach: From I-5 exit 255 (Bellingham), drive Mt. Baker Highway SR 542 east about 33 miles to the Glacier Public Service Center, then continue east roughly 12-13.5 miles to the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance shed. Turn left (north) onto Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065) and follow it about 7 miles to its end between the two Twin Lakes, where the signed Winchester Mountain trailhead begins.
routes.descent: Descend the same Trail #685 back to the Twin Lakes trailhead.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route: down the summit ridge, back through the rock-cut section, down the switchbacks through blueberry/heather slopes, past the High Pass Trail junction, and back to the Twin Lakes trailhead. No technical descent gear is needed outside of early-season snow, when a lingering steep snowfield near the summit can require an ice axe and caution well into July.
routes.bail: The trail can be abandoned at any point back toward Twin Lakes; the only notable hazard sections to retreat carefully through are the exposed rock-cut near the summit and any lingering snowfield below it.
routes.turnaround: Because a steep, icy snowfield below the summit can persist into July and the final approach includes an exposed rock-cut, turn back if snow conditions exceed your party's comfort/ability with an ice axe, if weather closes in on the open ridge, or if you won't reach the lookout with enough daylight/margin to return to the trailhead and negotiate the access road before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly August through September, when the steep snowfield below the summit has usually melted out; parties going earlier in the season (into July) should expect to cross steep, icy snow and carry an ice axe. The lookout itself is locked to interior public use from November through May each year.
routes.detailedRack: None required — this is a Class 1-2 maintained trail hike to a fire lookout with no technical climbing.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is needed; the route is a hiking trail. An ice axe (and the skill to use it) is recommended for the lingering snowfield below the summit in early season (into July).
proTips: Park at the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake trailhead (4.5 miles up Twin Lakes Road) and walk the final ~2.5 miles of road if you lack a high-clearance vehicle; the last stretch to Twin Lakes is rough, rocky, and not maintained for passenger cars.

The lookout is open first-come, first-served for day use or an overnight stay (two bunks) when unlocked (roughly June through October); pack out everything and treat it as the volunteer-maintained historic structure it is.
Fall visits bring good blueberry picking and foliage color along the switchbacks.
watchOut: A steep, icy snowfield just below the summit routinely persists into July and has caused falls; cross it only with an ice axe and the skill to self-arrest, or turn back.
A short but exposed rock-cut section near the summit requires caution, especially for children or leashed dogs.
Twin Lakes Road (FR 3065) is narrow, rocky, and pothole-prone beyond the Yellow Aster Butte trailhead; high-clearance and sometimes 4WD is needed for the final ~2.5 miles.
knownHazards: Lingering steep snow/ice below the summit into early-mid summer.
Exposed rock-cut trail section near the top with real fall consequences.
Rough, high-clearance-only access road that can strand low-clearance vehicles.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065)
road.status: Paved/graded and generally passable to sedans for the first 4.5 miles to the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake trailhead; the final ~2.5 miles to Twin Lakes are narrow, rocky, and pothole-prone, not maintained for passenger vehicles — high-clearance is recommended and 4WD can be needed depending on conditions.
road.seasonalGate: Typically snowbound and impassable from fall through early summer; usually opens by mid-summer once snow clears.
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, go east on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) roughly 12-13.5 miles to the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance facility, then turn left onto Twin Lakes Road (FR 3065) and follow it about 7 miles to its end between the Twin Lakes. If the final stretch is impassable, park at Yellow Aster Butte trailhead (mile 4.5) and walk the extra ~2-2.5 miles each way.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC "West Slopes North" zone (covers the Mount Baker Highway/backcountry corridor).
climate.typical: Wet, snowy Cascade climate; the summit ridge is exposed to wind and fast-moving weather typical of the Mount Baker area.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack, road to Twin Lakes typically still closed/impassable.
bySeason.summer: Prime hiking window (roughly July-Sept); a steep snowfield below the summit can linger into July.
bySeason.fall: Good blueberry/foliage color into September/October; first snows and shortening season by October.
bySeason.winter: Lookout locked to interior public access November-May; access road typically snowbound and impassable.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker Ranger District (Glacier Public Service Center).
access.fees: Day-use fee at Twin Lakes trailhead ($5/vehicle/day or $30 annual, per USFS); Washington State Discover Pass is NOT accepted here.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency (America the Beautiful) Pass, or other USFS-honored digital/recreation pass.
access.closures: Winchester Mountain Lookout interior is locked to public use from November through May annually; Twin Lakes Road is typically impassable outside of summer/early fall due to snow.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 676-6911; 911 for emergencies.
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District / Glacier Public Service Center: (360) 854-2553.
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA (roughly 45-60 minutes' drive from the Mt. Baker Highway corridor to the trailhead area).
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent along Twin Lakes Road and on the mountain itself; the nearest reliable help is via the Glacier Public Service Center or 911 once back in cell range on SR 542.

blurb: Winchester Mountain (6,521 ft) rises above Twin Lakes in the Mount Baker Wilderness of the North Cascades, on the Nooksack Ridge crest between Tomyhoi and Gold Run passes northeast of Mount Baker. The peak was named in the early 1900s for prospector Jack Post's rifle and was first climbed (as recorded) by Henry Custer's 1858 US-Canada boundary survey party; its summit carries a restored 1935 L-4 cab fire lookout, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1987 and maintained today by Mount Baker Club volunteers. The mountain and surrounding basin are metamorphic/volcanic North Cascades terrain, popular for wide views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, the Pickets, Mount Larrabee, and the Border Peaks.

objHaz: crevassed glacier travel on the approach (Le Conte, South Cascade and other glaciers)
remote, multi-day commitment with no quick bail option
rapid weather changes and whiteout at Lizard Col/Sentinel Pass
loose/blocky terrain on the class 3 scramble
routes.face: South side, rising above Lizard Col / White Rock Lakes
routes.overview: A class 3 scramble up Lizard Mountain's south side from the Lizard Col/White Rock Lakes basin, done as a short side-trip mid-way through the multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse rather than as a stand-alone day climb.
routes.beta: Lizard Mountain has no trailhead of its own — parties reach it only by first completing several days of the Ptarmigan Traverse's off-trail, glaciated high route, then scrambling from a camp near Lizard Col. The summit push itself is straightforward (class 3) once you're on site; the real crux of the route is the multi-day glacier approach to get there.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (end of Cascade River Road), follow the Ptarmigan Traverse's off-trail route south: over Cache Col to Kool-Aid Lake, over Spider-Formidable Col past Yang Yang Lakes, across the Le Conte Glacier to Lizard Col, then down to camp at White Rock Lakes. Lizard Mountain's south side rises directly above this camp and is typically climbed as a half-day scramble from there.
routes.descent: Reverse the class 3 south-side scramble back down to the Lizard Col/White Rock Lakes camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same south-facing class 3 terrain back into the Lizard Col/White Rock Lakes basin. There's no separate descent line. From camp, parties either continue the Ptarmigan Traverse toward Dome Peak and exit via Cub Lake, Itswoot Ridge, and the Bachelor Creek/Downey Creek Trail, or reverse the whole route back over the Le Conte Glacier, Spider-Formidable Col, and Cache Col to the Cascade Pass Trailhead. As of 2026 the historic Downey Creek exit is not reachable by vehicle because the Suiattle River Road is washed out, so most parties should plan an out-and-back via Cascade Pass.
routes.itinerary: Typical 5-7 day Ptarmigan Traverse itinerary that includes Lizard Mountain: Day 1 Cascade Pass TH to Kool-Aid Lake (~6 hrs) via Cache Col; Day 2 to Yang Yang Lakes via Spider-Formidable Col; Day 3 to White Rock Lakes via the Le Conte Glacier and Lizard Col, side-scrambling Lizard Mountain from camp; Days 4+ continue toward Dome Peak/Cub Lake or reverse the route back out to Cascade Pass (the historic Downey Creek exit is currently closed).
routes.bail: There is no quick bail option once committed past Cache Col — retreat means reversing the glaciated route back to the Cascade Pass Trailhead. Do not count on reaching the Downey Creek/Suiattle River Road exit, which is currently closed by flood damage.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround for the glacier crossings to and from Lizard Col: deteriorating weather or whiteout on the Le Conte or South Cascade Glaciers sharply raises crevasse-fall and navigation risk, and this is remote terrain where a late retreat can force an unplanned bivouac.
routes.bestSeason: Prime timing is roughly late July through September, after the Cascade Pass trail and passes have cleared enough snow for travel (the Cascade Pass parking lot typically doesn't open until late June or July) but before fall storms set in. Earlier trips have more continuous snow covering crevasses (easier travel but snow-bridge risk); later trips expose more bare glacier ice and open crevasses.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of this remote, glaciated section of the Glacier Peak Wilderness. NPS advises against relying on cell or satellite phones alone and recommends a two-way satellite messenger (e.g., a PLB or inReach) for this route.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the class 3 scramble itself. The real gear need is standard summer glacier-travel kit for the approach: a rope per team, harness, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusik cords, pulley/ascender), ice axe, and crampons for crossing the Le Conte, South Cascade, and other glaciers between Cascade Pass and Lizard Col.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are almost entirely about the glacier approach rather than the summit scramble: a rope for each glacier-travel team, a full crevasse-rescue kit, and a helmet for rockfall/icefall exposure along the traverse.
proTips: Base at White Rock Lakes camp near Lizard Col and use a layover/rest day to scramble Lizard Mountain unladen rather than carrying full packs up the peak.
Aluminum crampons are adequate for most of the Ptarmigan Traverse's summer glacier crossings, but expect firmer, icier snow on the early-melting section of the Le Conte Glacier near Lizard Col.
Pick up the mandatory NPS bear canister (free loaner) at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount before starting — food hanging isn't feasible on this above-treeline traverse.
watchOut: Crevasse hazard crossing the Le Conte and South Cascade Glaciers on the approach to Lizard Col — rope up and carry crevasse-rescue gear.
Weather can change rapidly and obscure visibility right at Lizard Col/Sentinel Pass.

The Suiattle River Road (FS-26) exit via Downey Creek Trailhead is closed at milepost 4 by flood damage (in effect April 2, 2026 - January 1, 2028) — plan to retrace the route back out via Cascade Pass rather than counting on that exit.
knownHazards: Off-trail glacier travel across multiple glaciers en route (including Cache, Middle Cascade, Le Conte, South Cascade and Chickamin on the full traverse) with real crevasse-fall risk.
Remote, multi-day commitment deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness with no quick bail-out; full glacier-travel and self-rescue skills are required.
Cell phone coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the route; NPS recommends carrying a two-way satellite messenger.

road.name: Cascade River Road (to Cascade Pass Trailhead)
road.status: Open seasonally; as of Jan 2026 open to cars to about milepost 21 (with the gravel section reported in good condition), though the very end can hold snow/ice into early summer
road.seasonalGate: Snowed in over winter; the Cascade Pass parking lot itself typically doesn't open until late June or July
road.driveNote: Paved from Marblemount then gravel, with potholes and a narrow, steep final stretch — high-clearance recommended. Note: the historic southern exit road for this traverse, the Suiattle River Road (FS-26) to Downey Creek Trailhead, is closed at milepost 4 by flood damage through at least January 2028, so plan an out-and-back via Cascade Pass.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park backcountry / Mount Baker-Snoqualmie & Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest crest
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate along the Cascade crest: Pacific storms bring heavy winter snowfall and can produce rapid weather changes even in summer; extensive glacier travel means high UV and snow-glare exposure.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack still covers approach trails and passes; avalanche and snow-bridge hazard is high.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep) once Cascade River Road and Cascade Pass trail have cleared of snow, though glaciers still carry real crevasse hazard.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and increasing storm frequency after mid-September; more exposed bare ice and open crevasses late season.
bySeason.winter: Road and trailhead are snowed in; the route is effectively inaccessible.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex / Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for trailhead parking ($5/day or $30/year); NPS backcountry camping permit $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.permit: NPS wilderness/backcountry permit required for any overnight camping along the traverse, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking) plus an NPS wilderness camping permit
access.closures: Suiattle River Road (FS-26) — the traditional southern/Downey Creek exit for this traverse — is closed at milepost 4 due to flood damage, in effect April 2, 2026 through January 1, 2028; the Downey Creek and Green Mountain trailheads are inaccessible by vehicle during that time.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning departure from Cascade Pass Trailhead on Day 1; the Lizard Mountain summit push is a half-day side trip from White Rock Lakes camp, typically on a layover day

sectionBreakdown.note: via Cache Col

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Kool-Aid Lake to Yang Yang Lakes
sectionBreakdown.note: via Spider-Formidable Col

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Yang Yang Lakes to White Rock Lakes
sectionBreakdown.note: via the Le Conte Glacier and Lizard Col; side-scramble Lizard Mountain from camp

emergency.county: Skagit County (near the Chelan County line)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450; 911 in this area may route to a distant dispatch center
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245; NPS Communications Center (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, after-hours (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (over 40 miles from Marblemount)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable at best even from high points in this remote glaciated terrain; NPS recommends a two-way satellite messenger (PLB/inReach) rather than relying on phone service, and warns that rescue response may take hours to days given the remoteness.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation is 7,399 ft. Reputable modern sources vary 7,399-7,420 ft for this rarely-surveyed summit (PeakVisor/USGS DEM-derived: 7,408 ft / 2,258 m; ListsOfJohn page title: 7,420 ft; some older sources/derived conversions show ~7,306 ft). The spread is within normal survey/DEM variance for a minor peak; 7,408 ft (PeakVisor, USGS-derived) is used here as highPointFt rather than treating any single figure as definitively authoritative.
whatToBring: sun protection for extensive glacier travel (glare/UV)
satellite communicator (e.g. Garmin inReach or PLB)
Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking

blurb: Lizard Mountain (informally "The Lizard") is a minor summit on the crest of the North Cascades in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, sitting just west of much higher Sentinel Peak above the Le Conte and South Cascade Glaciers. It has no independent trailhead of its own and is reached only as a side-scramble from the classic multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse between Cascade Pass and the Suiattle River drainage, typically tagged from a camp at White Rock Lakes near Lizard Col.

routes.comms: This is deep wilderness with no maintained trail; expect no cell coverage for essentially the entire approach and climb, consistent with the rest of the Glacier Peak Wilderness / Stehekin backcountry. A satellite communicator or PLB is strongly advised.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Chelan Ranger District), within the Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required to park at National Forest trailheads in the area ($5/day or $30/year)
access.permit: Free backcountry camping permit required for overnight stays inside North Cascades National Park; Glacier Peak Wilderness (National Forest) portions require only trailhead self-registration, no permit
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; RiverCom 911 (Chelan/Douglas County dispatch) non-emergency line (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — (509) 682-4900, 428 W. Woodin Ave, Chelan, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health Hospital, 110 S Apple Blossom Dr, Chelan, WA (24-hour ER)
emergency.notes: Extremely remote terrain with no maintained trail or cell coverage; self-rescue capability and a satellite communicator are essential, as SAR response in this part of the Glacier Peak Wilderness/Ptarmigan Crest can take a day or more.

routes.corrections: Given data lists 'Standard Scramble', Class 3, alpineGrade 'F', but no guidebook, trip report, or peak-database route description could be found for Rimrock Ridge specifically — Peakbagger logs only one ascent/attempt and ListsOfJohn lists zero reported ascents, with no route notes on either site, and it has no Mountain Project or SummitPost page. The Class 3/F rating could not be independently verified, so alpineGrade/rockGrade and other technical fields are left null rather than confirmed or contradicted. Elevation (7254.5 ft per Peakbagger LIDAR data, 7260 ft per ListsOfJohn) is consistent with the given 7255 ft. Coordinates 48.375725, -120.978245 (Peakbagger) and 48.3755, -120.9783 (ListsOfJohn) agree.
routes.overview: Rimrock Ridge has no published route description. It is an obscure, off-trail summit near Old Guard Peak in the Ptarmigan Crest of the Glacier Peak Wilderness, with essentially no recorded climbing history.
routes.beta: No guidebook or online route beta exists specifically for Rimrock Ridge. It lies about 2.3 miles from Old Guard Peak in the same remote LeConte Glacier / Ptarmigan Traverse massif, so an ascent would likely involve terrain similar to nearby Old Guard and Sentinel Peaks: long, unmaintained approaches from Stehekin (via Agnes Creek or Flat Creek) or a cross-country detour off the Ptarmigan Traverse, followed by off-trail alpine scrambling to the summit.
routes.approach: There is no maintained trail to Rimrock Ridge. The nearest documented approaches in this massif start from Stehekin (reached by boat, floatplane, or trail up Lake Chelan) via the Stehekin River Trail, then a multi-day bushwhack up Agnes Creek or Flat Creek to gain the ridge system near Old Guard Peak and the LeConte Glacier; alternatively, parties on the Ptarmigan Traverse (starting from the Cascade River Road / Cascade Pass trailhead near Marblemount) pass through the same general area.
routes.turnaround: No party-specific timing data exists for this peak. As on the surrounding Ptarmigan Traverse terrain, plan turnaround around weather, off-trail route-finding time, and the long unmaintained approach rather than a fixed clock time — afternoon thunderstorms and sudden whiteout conditions are common on this section of the North Cascades crest in summer.
routes.bestSeason: The broader Ptarmigan Crest / Ptarmigan Traverse area is normally climbed mid-July through mid-September, once seasonal snow has receded from approach trails and cols. No season-specific reports exist for Rimrock Ridge itself, but expect snow travel on approach in early season and glacier-adjacent hazards near the LeConte Glacier persisting into fall.
whatToBring: Sturdy boots suited to bushwhacking and talus
proTips: No trip reports or GPS tracks are published for Rimrock Ridge — carry full off-trail navigation skills and don't count on beta.
Parties near Old Guard/Sentinel report multi-day bushwhacks through slide alder and devil's club with few good campsites; treat the approach itself as the crux of any Rimrock Ridge attempt.
watchOut: No established route or trip reports exist for Rimrock Ridge; approach and route-finding are entirely off-trail.
Approaches in this massif involve long, dense bushwhacks (slide alder, devil's club) typical of unmaintained Stehekin-area drainages.
knownHazards: The peak lies in extremely remote, seldom-visited terrain — Peakbagger records only one logged ascent/attempt and ListsOfJohn lists none, so full self-rescue capability is essential.
Nearby LeConte Glacier travel (used to reach adjacent Old Guard and Sentinel Peaks) involves standard glacier hazards (crevasses, bergschrunds) if that access line is used.

road.name: Access via Stehekin Valley Road (Stehekin, boat/floatplane/trail access only) or Cascade River Road (from Marblemount, for Ptarmigan Traverse approaches)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service/NPS roads; no through-road reaches Rimrock Ridge itself
road.driveNote: Cascade River Road is paved then gravel for its final miles to the Cascade Pass trailhead and has periodically washed out short of the end; Stehekin has no outside road access and is reached only by Lake Chelan boat, floatplane, or trail.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Seattle forecast office covers North Cascades zone forecasts for this area
climate.typical: East-side Cascades crest climate: generally drier and sunnier than the west slope, but still subject to rapid weather changes, afternoon thunderstorms, and lingering snow at this elevation into summer.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack persists on approach terrain and the nearby LeConte Glacier; travel is glacier/snow travel rather than scrambling.
bySeason.summer: Mid-July through mid-September is the usual climbing window in this area once snow recedes from approach routes.
bySeason.fall: Increasing storm frequency and early snow; days shorten quickly.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; effectively inaccessible without ski mountaineering skills.

blurb: Rimrock Ridge is a remote, largely undocumented 7,255-foot summit in the Ptarmigan Crest area of the North Cascades, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness portion of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest in Chelan County. It sits about 2.3 miles from its line parent, Old Guard Peak, in the same rugged, glacier-carved massif crossed by the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. Reached only by long off-trail approaches, it has no recorded first ascent and shows just a single logged ascent/attempt on Peakbagger (none on ListsOfJohn), making it one of the least-visited named summits in the range.

routes.fa: Curtis I. James, Barrie James, and Joe Leuthold (Mazamas), 1937 — the peak's first ascent; this easiest line is widely regarded as the original route
objHaz: icefall in the glacier's icefall zone
rockfall on the waterfall slabs and upper ridge
moat/bergschrund gap between snow and rock near the top of the glacier
routes.overview: The standard and easiest route on Bonanza Peak: a long glacier climb up the broad Mary Green Glacier on the peak's east side, finishing with a short, exposed scramble on class 3-4 (briefly low-5th class) rock to the summit.
routes.beta: From Holden Pass, scramble up slabs to the glacier's northern edge, rope up, and ascend bearing west along the north (climber's right) edge, then traverse southwest along the base of the northeast ridge to the glacier's upper corner. Climb the steep upper snow finger on climber's right to its highest point, exit left onto rock past a possible moat/overhanging snow lip, then climb class 3-4 rock right of a gully for several rope-lengths, traverse left into another gully, and scramble to a notch in the upper northeast ridge just north of the summit.
routes.approach: From Chelan or Field's Point Landing, take the Lady of the Lake ferry to Lucerne, then the Holden Village shuttle bus (~11 mi on FS Rd 8301) to Holden Village. Follow the Railroad Creek Trail about 1 mile to the Holden Lake Trail junction, turn right and follow it about 4 miles to Holden Lake (5,278 ft), then continue north/northwest to camp at Holden Pass (6,400 ft) or on slabs above.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the summit rock scramble back to the glacier and descend Mary Green Glacier to Holden Pass.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class 3-4 rock back through the notch and gully system to the top of the glacier, then descend the Mary Green Glacier along the same line used on ascent, staying wary of the moat/bergschrund and crevasses, back to Holden Pass and the Holden Lake trail.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-day trip: Day 1 — ferry/bus to Holden Village and hike to camp at Holden Lake or Holden Pass; Day 2 — alpine start, climb the glacier and summit scramble, return to camp; Day 3 — hike out and catch the return ferry/bus to Chelan.
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the ascent line down the glacier to Holden Pass; the main technical constraints on a bail are the moat/bergschrund crossing near the top of the glacier and crevassed terrain, which may require a rappel or careful downclimbing.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting that accounts for the long approach, glacier travel, and the exposed summit scramble, and stick to it — afternoon thunderstorms and softening snow/increasing rockfall on the waterfall slabs and glacier are recurring hazards later in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Early-to-mid July generally offers the best balance of an easily-crossed bergschrund and a mostly snow-free rock scramble; as summer progresses the glacier becomes more broken up and crevassed, making the glacier-to-rock transition harder, while a lingering/deep snowpack in early season can complicate travel on the approach slabs.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Railroad Creek drainage or on the peak; Holden Village has limited landline/wifi service. A satellite communicator is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: rope, harness, crevasse-rescue gear (pickets/prusiks), ice axe and crampons for all party members. A few slings and small-to-medium cams/nuts can be useful for the short class 4 rock step near the summit, though many parties climb it unprotected.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel protection (pickets, screws for crevasse rescue) is the primary need; rock protection is optional given the short, moderate nature of the summit scramble.
whatToBring: overnight camping gear for a 2-3 day trip
proTips: Camp at Holden Pass rather than Holden Lake to save roughly 1,000 ft of extra elevation gain on summit day
Register at the Hike Haus in Holden Village before heading out
Coordinate ferry/bus schedules in advance since Holden Village has no through road
watchOut: Rockfall and icefall near the base of the glacier below the icefall zone
A moat or overhanging snow lip can form where the glacier meets the rock late in the season
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the North Cascades in summer
knownHazards: Active crevasses and seracs on the Mary Green Glacier
Loose rock on parts of the summit ridge
Waterfall slabs on the approach can be slick and are exposed to rockfall/afternoon exposure

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble from Holden Pass up slabs to the glacier's north edge; stay alert for rockfall/icefall from above.
Rope up; ascend along the north (climber's right) edge, then traverse southwest along the base of the NE ridge to the glacier's upper corner.
Steep snow to its highest point; a moat or overhanging snow lip may guard the exit onto rock.
Rock right of a gully for several rope-lengths, then traverse left into another gully and scramble to a notch on the NE ridge just north of the summit.

road.name: Lucerne dock to Holden Village (Forest Service Road 8301)
road.status: No public through road; access is by ferry then a village shuttle bus only
road.seasonalGate: Lady of the Lake ferry runs a fuller schedule roughly May-October, with a reduced winter schedule
road.driveNote: Take the Lady of the Lake ferry from Chelan or Field's Point Landing to Lucerne dock, then the Holden Village bus about 11 miles on FS Road 8301 to Holden Village; there is no private-vehicle access to the trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Cascades / Lake Chelan area
climate.typical: Warm, relatively dry summers with afternoon thunderstorm potential; heavy winter snowpack that can linger into early summer on the approach slabs and glacier.

bySeason.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack; glacier travel conditions but avalanche and cornice hazard, generally not attempted for the standard rock-scramble finish
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window; best conditions typically early-to-mid July before the glacier becomes more broken up
bySeason.fall: Route can still go into September but glacier crevasses are more exposed and snow bridges less reliable
bySeason.winter: Very rarely climbed; steep slopes, cornices on the summit ridge, and difficult access make winter ascents hazardous and uncommon

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass typically required at the trailhead
access.permit: No special climbing permit; parties are asked to self-register at the Hike Haus in Holden Village before a summit attempt
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful (National Park) Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start (roughly 2-4 AM) from a high camp at Holden Pass on summit day

sectionBreakdown.note: Railroad Creek Trail 1 mi, then Holden Lake Trail ~4 mi

sectionBreakdown.section: Holden Lake to Holden Pass camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Continue north/northwest to camp near 6,400 ft
Glacier travel plus class 3-4 summit scramble, round trip

emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health Hospital, 110 S Apple Blossom Dr, Chelan, WA — (509) 682-3300
emergency.notes: No road access to the trailhead; a rescue requires helicopter or a multi-hour boat/vehicle evacuation via Lucerne. Cell coverage is minimal to nonexistent in the Railroad Creek drainage.

routes.corrections: Given rockGrade of 5.6 is not supported by sources: Mountain Project rates the route 4th class (YDS)/Alpine Grade III, and the Mountaineers/SummitPost/multiple trip reports describe the summit scramble as class 3-4 with at most a short low-5th-class step — no source documents sustained 5.6 climbing, so rockGrade is left null rather than asserted. The peak's 1937 first-ascent party (James, James, Leuthold) is well documented by Wikipedia/AAC, but no source explicitly confirms they climbed this exact glacier line rather than a variant, so the route 'fa' attribution carries that caveat. WTA lists the summit elevation as 9,511 ft vs. 9,516 ft from Wikipedia/USGS-derived sources; 9,516 ft was used as the more commonly cited figure.

routes.approach: Shares the same Lake Chelan ferry / Holden Village / Railroad Creek approach used for the Mary Green Glacier route; the closest documented technical line on this aspect (the Northeast Buttress) was accessed from climber's right of the Mary Green Glacier at about 8,350 ft, roughly 3 hours from Holden Lake.
routes.turnaround: Set and respect a turnaround time given the sustained, exposed nature of technical terrain on this aspect and the long, complex approach/retreat.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Railroad Creek drainage or on the peak; a satellite communicator is recommended.
knownHazards: Bonanza's rock is described by climbers as loose in places on its ridges and faces, consistent across multiple documented routes on the massif
Glacier travel (crevasses, icefall) on the approach glaciers flanking this aspect of the peak

emergency.notes: No road access to the trailhead; a rescue requires helicopter or a multi-hour boat/vehicle evacuation via Lucerne. Cell coverage is minimal to nonexistent in this drainage.

routes.corrections: No reputable source documents a route literally named 'Northeast Face / Company Glacier' on Bonanza Peak. The only well-documented technical line on the peak's northeast aspect near the main summit is the Northeast Buttress, first climbed to the main summit by Kurt Buchwald, Peter Avolio, and Martin Volken on Aug 21-22, 2004 as part of the first three-summit ridge traverse (graded V 5.7/5.8 for the full traverse) — approached via climber's right of the Mary Green Glacier, not Company Glacier, which lies further north/around the corner. Because it is unclear whether the catalog's 'Northeast Face / Company Glacier' route is meant to be this same buttress line, a distinct undocumented line, or a composite/approximate label, most route-specific technical fields (fa, pitches, routeFt, rappels, detailed beta, timing) are left null rather than asserted. The rockGrade of 5.7 is retained because it matches the '5.7 range' climbing reported on the Northeast Buttress by the AAJ; the given alpineGrade (D) and iceGrade (AI2) could not be independently confirmed by any source found and should be treated as unverified.

blurb: Bonanza Peak rises in the North Cascades within the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Chelan Ranger District), Chelan County. At 9,516 ft it is the highest non-volcanic summit in Washington and in the Cascade Range as a whole, with 3,711 ft of prominence; three active glaciers (Mary Green, Company, and Isella) flank its main, west, and southwest summits. The peak is remote and roadless — access is by ferry across Lake Chelan to Lucerne, then bus to Holden Village. The first ascent was made in 1937 by Curtis I. James, Barrie James, and Joe Leuthold of the Mazamas.

waypoints.name: Ridge saddle/notch below Seven Fingered Jack

routes.fa: Oscar Pennington & Hermann Ulrichs, 1932 (first recorded ascent of the peak; the historic line is generally consistent with today's standard SE Face/Leroy Basin route, but the exact line of the 1932 ascent is not separately documented)
routes.season: Jul-Sep (snow/ice can linger into midsummer on the upper face)
routes.face: Southeast Face, approached via Leroy Basin and the remnant glacier/snowfield below the ridge north of the summit
routes.overview: A long, remote scramble up the highest peak in the Entiat Mountains: approach via Phelps Creek to Leroy Basin, cross a saddle and a small remnant glacier below the ridge, then climb loose class 3-4 (briefly class 5) terrain up the southeast face to the summit.
routes.beta: The standard route follows the Phelps Creek Trail to Leroy Basin, then goes cross-country north across a ridge saddle, descends roughly 700-1,000 ft to cross a small glacier/snowfield beneath Fernow's north/east side (informally called Gloomy Glacier), then climbs loose gullies and ridge terrain to a saddle south of the summit before a short, exposed scramble to the top. It is generally rated Class 3-4 with brief class 5 moves reported near the summit, and is one of the longer, more remote scrambles among Washington's highest peaks. Alternate approaches exist via the Entiat River/Cottonwood trailhead (less avalanche exposure, favored in winter) and via the Holden Village ferry and Copper Creek/east ridge.
routes.approach: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead (~3,500 ft, end of FR 6211), follow the Phelps Creek Trail roughly 3.5 miles to the Leroy Creek climbers' path, then climb the steep, loose Leroy Creek trail about 1.5-1.9 miles and ~2,000 ft to camp and water in Leroy Basin (~6,100 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb to the saddle south of the summit, re-cross the small glacier/snowfield, regain the ridge saddle above Leroy Basin, then descend back into the basin and out via the Leroy Creek and Phelps Creek trails.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same class 3-4 terrain used on the ascent to the saddle south of the top. Re-cross the small glacier/snowfield below the ridge and regain roughly 700-1,000 ft back up to the ridge saddle above Leroy Basin - several trip reports flag this re-ascent as the sting in the tail on an already long day. From the saddle, descend heather and scree back into Leroy Basin and follow the Leroy Creek and Phelps Creek trails out to the trailhead. The loose talus/scree makes the descent nearly as slow as the ascent, and multiple reports note slips and near-misses on the way down, especially after dark.
routes.itinerary: Most parties do it as a very long (12-20 hour) single-day car-to-car push from the Phelps Creek Trailhead with a pre-dawn start. Others backpack in ~3.5-5 miles to camp in Leroy Basin (6,100 ft) on day one and make a lighter summit day via the SE Face on day two, often combined with nearby Mount Maude and/or Seven Fingered Jack from the same basin camp.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ascent line back to the saddle/notch and down into Leroy Basin. There is no shortcut bail once past the basin, since the route crosses a remnant glacier and a ridge saddle rather than following a single continuous gully or ridge back to camp.
routes.turnaround: Because the return crosses back over the ridge saddle and remnant glacier with roughly 700-1,000 ft of re-ascent before the descent to camp even begins, set a turnaround time that accounts for that climb back out, not just the summit-to-basin descent, and build in margin for the loose, slow terrain in both directions.
routes.bestSeason: Peak season is roughly July through September, once the approach trail and Leroy Basin are snow-free. Even then, the small glacier/snowfield below the ridge and shaded gullies on the SE Face often hold snow and ice into midsummer, so many summer parties still carry an ice axe and crampons. Winter and spring ascents are typically done via the longer Entiat River/Cottonwood approach specifically to avoid avalanche terrain encountered on the Phelps Creek side.
routes.detailedRack: Beyond a standard scrambling kit (ice axe, crampons, helmet), many parties carry a short rope (30-60m) with a light rack of slings and a few small pieces of protection for the brief class 4-5 exposed moves near the summit block; a picket or two is useful for firm snow on the glacier/snowfield traverse below the ridge, especially early season.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly natural/scrambling protection; a small rack of slings and a few small cams or nuts cover the short exposed class 4-5 section near the top. Snow pickets are more useful than rock gear for most parties, for the glacier/snowfield crossing below the ridge.
whatToBring: headlamp (very long car-to-car days are common)
water filter (limited water above Leroy Basin)
extra layers for exposed ridge/glacier crossing
proTips: Get an early alpine start (3:00-4:30 am) - most parties report 12-20 hour car-to-car days.

The route drops roughly 700-1,000 ft off the ridge saddle into the basin below the glacier/snowfield, which must be re-climbed on the way out; budget time and energy for that regain, not just the descent.
Snow and ice can linger on the southeast face and the glacier/snowfield crossing into midsummer; carry an ice axe and crampons even for a July/August attempt.
The final summit block has several plausible lines, some involving brief class 4-5 moves with real exposure - scout for the easiest line before committing.
watchOut: Extensive loose talus and scree above Leroy Basin and near the summit create rockfall and ankle-injury hazard, especially in a group.
The small remnant glacier/snowfield (informally 'Gloomy Glacier') below the ridge can hold crevasses and icy 30-40 degree slopes even late season.
Sandy, exposed ledges on the upper mountain are slick and consequential, particularly on descent.
Route-finding is nontrivial with multiple gully/ridge variations and sparse cairns.
knownHazards: Crevasses/icy slopes on the remnant glacier traversed between the ridge saddle and Fernow's north/east side.
Rockfall from loose, exfoliating gneiss on the upper southeast face and summit block.
Long, remote approach (roughly 11 miles one-way) with limited reliable water above Leroy Basin.
Rough, high-clearance-only access roads add significant drive time and can strand vehicles if road conditions are underestimated.

pitchDetail.notes: About 3.5 miles of maintained, mostly flat Phelps Creek Trail through forest.
Steep, dusty/loose climbers' path gaining about 2,000 ft in 1.5-1.9 miles to camp/water at ~6,100 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Basin to ridge saddle and glacier crossing
pitchDetail.notes: Cross-country over heather/scree/talus to a saddle, then a 700-1,000 ft descent and traverse of a small remnant glacier/snowfield below the ridge.
Loose gullies and ridge terrain lead to multiple route options above ~8,800 ft; several trip reports note short, exposed class 4-5 moves near the top.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) to Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: Rough dirt/gravel; high clearance recommended, especially the final ~2 miles of FR 6211
road.seasonalGate: Not gated on a fixed seasonal schedule, but has been subject to storm-damage closures beyond Atkinson Campground in recent years
road.driveNote: From Hwy 2 near Coles Corner, take Hwy 207 and Chiwawa Loop Road to Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200), then follow FR 6211 to its end at the Phelps Creek Trailhead - about 23 miles of driving with pavement ending roughly 11 miles in. Check current Wenatchee River Ranger District road status before driving in.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Slopes / Stevens Pass area
climate.typical: Continental east-slope Cascades climate: colder, snowier winters and drier, sunnier summers than the west-side Cascades. North- and east-facing terrain in the Entiat Mountains holds snow well into summer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack typically persists on the approach and route into June; avalanche exposure is a real concern on the Phelps Creek side.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); the small glacier/snowfield below the ridge and shaded gullies on the SE Face often still hold snow and ice into midsummer.
bySeason.fall: Often stable weather windows into September, sometimes October, but with shortening daylight and possible early-season snow.
bySeason.winter: Full winter mountaineering conditions; recorded winter ascents used the longer Entiat River/Cottonwood approach via snowmobile and ski to avoid avalanche terrain.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District; route lies within the Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required at the Phelps Creek Trailhead
access.permit: Free, self-issued wilderness permit required at the trailhead within Glacier Peak Wilderness; no quota or reservation needed for day/overnight climbing use
access.passRequired: None
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) has experienced storm-damage closures beyond Atkinson Campground in recent seasons - verify current road status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before driving in.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start (roughly 3:00-4:30 am) reported by multiple parties

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Phelps Creek TH -> Leroy Creek cutoff
sectionBreakdown.note: Flat forested trail, ~3.5 miles
Steep climbers' trail, ~2,000 ft gain in 1.5-1.9 miles
Saddle, glacier/snowfield crossing, and loose class 3-4 terrain; varies widely by conditions and party (multiple trip reports show 4-7 hrs)
Full reversal including the ~700-1,000 ft re-ascent out of the glacier basin; several parties finish after dark

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); RiverCom regional dispatch 509-663-9911 (non-emergency), Chelan County Sheriff's Office administers SAR
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth, WA - (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (24-hour Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach; expect little to no cell coverage for much of the route.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (9,246 ft) is lower than the elevation cited by most reputable sources (Wikipedia, WTA, Peakery, aggregated Peakbagger data), which consistently give 9,249 ft; highPointFt above is set to 9,249 ft accordingly. alpineGrade/rockGrade/iceGrade/commitment/pitches/routeFt/rappels were left null - no source assigns formal technical grades or a measured route length to this scramble route (sources describe it in Yosemite Decimal class terms, i.e. Class 2-4 with brief Class 5 moves near the summit, rather than a fixed alpine/rock grade).

blurb: Mount Fernow (9,249 ft) is the highest summit in the Entiat Mountains, a narrow, glacier-carved subrange of the North Cascades in Chelan County, Washington, rising within the Glacier Peak Wilderness near the headwaters of the Entiat River. Like its neighbors Mount Maude and Seven Fingered Jack, it is built largely of the metamorphic Swakane Gneiss intruded by Late Cretaceous granodiorite, which weathers into the loose, exfoliating rock typical of the range. Hermann Ulrichs and Oscar Pennington made the first recorded ascent in 1932, during a season that also saw Ulrichs climb nearby Mount Maude and Seven Fingered Jack.

routes.season: Jul-Sep (snow-free window); early June or Oct possible with an ice axe for lingering snow
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September when the approach trail and upper mountain are largely snow-free. It can go in June with an ice axe for lingering snow patches on the upper ridge, and into early October in stable weather, but the unmaintained climber's path above Leroy Basin becomes harder to follow if fresh snow falls.
routes.rappels: None — non-technical walk-down descent, same as ascent
routes.overview: The standard, non-technical route up Mount Maude: a long talus-and-scree scramble up the broad south ridge from Leroy Creek Basin, with no technical climbing but sustained route-finding on an unmaintained climber's path.
routes.beta: From the Leroy Creek Basin camp (~6,100 ft), the climber's path heads southerly/southeasterly, contouring toward Ice Lakes. At the gap overlooking Ice Lakes, climbers can choose the south ridge or south face to gain Maude's broad summit ridge, then follow easy talus to the top. Class 2 scrambling throughout with confident route-finding required; some parties combine it with neighboring Seven Fingered Jack via the connecting ridge.
routes.approach: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft), hike the Phelps Creek Trail about 3.5 miles to the Leroy Creek crossing (~4,120-4,140 ft). Cross on a log or by rock-hopping (difficult in early season) and pick up the unmaintained climber's path up the north side of Leroy Creek. It climbs steeply before leveling into the upper Leroy Creek Basin meadow (~6,100 ft), a good camp with water. From the basin, follow the climber's trail south/southeast, eventually meeting Maude's south ridge around 7,700 ft and following it to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route down the south ridge/climber's path to Leroy Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: Down-climb the same broad talus south ridge and climber's path back to the 6,100 ft Leroy Creek Basin meadow, then retrace the path down along Leroy Creek to the Phelps Creek Trail and out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 2-day trip: Day 1 — hike in to Leroy Creek Basin camp (~6,100 ft); Day 2 — summit via the south ridge and return to the trailhead (or exit on Day 3). Strong parties can do it car-to-car in a single long day from the west (Phelps Creek) side.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point — simply reverse course down the talus/scree slopes and climber's path to Leroy Creek Basin; no technical gear or rope is needed to bail.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting that accounts for the long approach and sustained route-finding on the unmaintained climber's path; afternoon thunderstorms and reduced visibility on the open upper ridge are the main reasons to commit to it.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage past the trailhead area in this remote Glacier Peak Wilderness drainage; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack needed. Trekking poles help on the talus; an ice axe (and microspikes/light crampons) is worth carrying in early season or after fresh snow on the upper ridge.
gear: microspikes or light crampons for lingering snow
whatToBring: extra water (dry basin and ridge sections)
layers for wind exposure on the open ridge
bear-resistant food storage for camping in Leroy Basin
proTips: Camp at the 6,100 ft meadow in Leroy Creek Basin to break the climb into a manageable two-day trip.
Many parties combine Mount Maude with neighboring Seven Fingered Jack via the connecting ridge for an efficient two-summit outing.
The Leroy Creek crossing can be a real obstacle in early season high water — scout for a log crossing.
watchOut: The climber's path above Leroy Basin is unmaintained and easy to lose in poor visibility.
Loose talus and scree on the final approach to the summit.
Afternoon thunderstorms on the exposed upper ridge.
knownHazards: Unstable talus/scree underfoot near the summit.
Route-finding difficulty on the unmaintained climber's trail, especially in early season or fog.
Difficult early-season crossing of Leroy Creek.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) to Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: FR 6200 CLOSED beyond Atkinson Flat Campground to all vehicles as of a Forest Service order effective May 20, 2026 (through Dec 31, 2027 unless rescinded), due to Dec 2025 storm/flood damage; no reopening estimate as of this writing. This blocks normal vehicle access to Phelps Creek Trailhead — confirm current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before driving in.
road.seasonalGate: Even outside of storm closures, the upper Chiwawa River Road/Phelps Creek Road is typically snow-gated into late spring.
road.driveNote: From US-2 near Coles Corner, take WA-207 toward Lake Wenatchee, then Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) about 22 miles, then right on FR 6211 for roughly 2.3-2.5 rough miles to the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft); high-clearance recommended.

climate.typical: East-Cascades continental climate: drier and more temperature-extreme than the west-side Cascades due to the Cascade crest rain shadow, with a shorter high-elevation snow-free window than west-side peaks.

bySeason.spring: Deep lingering snowpack on the approach trail and upper ridge; access road often still snow-gated.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; talus slopes mostly snow-free by July, warm and generally stable weather with occasional afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Cool, often stable weather windows into September/October; increasing chance of early-season snow at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Road access typically snowed in; the route becomes a serious winter mountaineering undertaking.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (west/Phelps Creek approach); Entiat Ranger District (east/Entiat River approach). Route is within Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.fees: No day-use fee found for Phelps Creek Trailhead itself.
access.permit: Free self-issued wilderness permit required at the Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary (~2.6 miles in on Phelps Creek Trail).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass may be required at some nearby trailheads (e.g., the east-side Entiat River Trailhead); not confirmed as required at Phelps Creek Trailhead.
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) closed beyond Atkinson Flat Campground to vehicles as of 2026 storm damage — see 'road' field. Verify current status before travel.

timing.recommendedStart: Early/pre-dawn start recommended given the long approach and full summit day.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom (Chelan/Douglas Co. dispatch) non-emergency 509-663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth, WA — 509-548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (critical access hospital); higher-level trauma care at Confluence Health, Wenatchee, WA
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness drainage with little to no cell coverage past the trailhead; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.

routes.corrections: The peak's overall first ascent (Aug 19, 1932, Hermann F. Ulrichs & John Burnett, per Wikipedia) is a mountain-summit FA, not confirmed to be specifically via this South Slopes line, so no route-specific 'fa' is given. One source vaguely references a 1970 south-slopes ascent, but this does not read as a credible first-ascent claim and was not used.

routes.season: May-Jul (best while a continuous snow/neve line links the Entiat Glacier to the summit; melts out by mid-to-late July)
routes.bestSeason: Best in late spring to early summer (roughly May through early July) while a continuous runnel of snow/neve links the Entiat Glacier to the summit. By mid-to-late July the snow typically melts out, exposing the face's loose, poor-quality rock and turning the route into an unpleasant, hazardous scramble.
routes.rappels: None — walk-off descent via the south-southwest ridge (South Slopes route)
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Don Gordon (Claunch), John Rupley, Herb Staley — June 16, 1957
routes.overview: A moderate alpine ice/snow face route climbing the roughly 1,750-foot North Face of Mount Maude, following a continuous snow/ice line from the Entiat Glacier to the summit ridge, steepening from about 35-45 degrees at the base to as much as 60-65 degrees near the top.
routes.beta: Climb snow and loose class 3 rock from Entiat Meadows/the Entiat Glacier basin to the base of the North Face proper. Before mid-July the face typically holds a continuous snow line from the glacier to near the summit; the lower face runs 35-45 degree snow between two prominent rock buttresses, steepening progressively and reaching 55-65 degrees just below the summit ridge, with possible short sections of hard alpine ice where the face is steepest. A short class 2 scramble finishes the last ~100 vertical feet to the summit. The rock on this face is notably poor quality and loose, so the route sticks to the snow/ice line rather than the flanking rock.
routes.approach: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft), hike the Phelps Creek Trail about 3.5-3.7 miles, cross Leroy Creek (~4,120-4,140 ft), and follow the unmaintained climber's path up the north side of Leroy Creek to the meadow camp at about 6,100 ft in upper Leroy Creek Basin. From camp, climb to the col between Seven Fingered Jack and Mount Maude, traverse the shoulder, and descend roughly 400 ft to the (South) Entiat Glacier at about 7,300 ft, then make a descending traverse northwestward across the glacier beneath a prominent icefall to the base of the North Face — rope up, since the glacier has multiple areas of wide, open crevasses. An alternate, longer approach comes from the east via the Entiat River Trail (#1400) and Ice Creek Trail to Ice Lakes/Entiat Meadows and the base of the Entiat Glacier.
routes.descent: Descend the standard south-southwest ridge (South Slopes route) back to Leroy Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the broad talus south-southwest ridge/South Slopes route down to the 6,100 ft Leroy Creek Basin meadow, then follow the climber's path and Phelps Creek Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip: Day 1 — hike to Leroy Creek Basin camp; Day 2 — pre-dawn start over the col, across the Entiat Glacier, up the North Face, and down the South Slopes to camp (one trip report logged about 13 hours car-to-car for a similar linkup); Day 3 — hike out.
routes.bail: While on the face, retreat by down-climbing the snow/ice line (belayed/protected with pickets or ice screws as needed) back toward the glacier; once past the col onto the south-southwest ridge, easier talus/scree offers a straightforward walk-off bail to Leroy Creek Basin.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting: the biggest hazards — rockfall off the loose face and softening/sluffing snow — both increase through the day, and a large cornice can form on the summit ridge, so climbing the face early and being off it before the sun hits it hard is the priority.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Leroy Creek Basin, on the Entiat Glacier, or on the North Face; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A single 30-60m rope, several snow pickets, and a handful of ice screws (roughly four) cover the route; screw sizes/quantity should flex with conditions since the face varies between soft neve and short sections of harder alpine ice depending on the year and time of season. Crevasse-rescue gear is required for the Entiat Glacier traverse to the base of the face.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is almost entirely pickets on the moderate lower snow, with ice screws for the steeper, harder ice near the top; natural rock protection is unreliable given the loose, poor-quality rock forming the face and flanking buttresses.
proTips: Climb before mid-July for a continuous snow/neve line rather than exposed loose rock and patchy ice.
Start early (alpine start) to cross the Entiat Glacier and be off the face before afternoon rockfall and snow softening.
Rope up for the Entiat Glacier traverse — it has multiple areas of wide, open crevasses.
watchOut: A large cornice can form on the summit ridge and make the right side of the route dangerous until it releases.
Rockfall is the biggest objective danger on this face, especially as the season warms.
Open crevasses on the Entiat Glacier traverse to the base of the face.
Ice conditions vary year to year — be ready for short sections of hard alpine ice near the top.
knownHazards: Loose, poor-quality rock on the face and flanking buttresses, prone to rockfall.
Crevasse hazard while traversing the Entiat Glacier to reach the base.
Cornice on the summit ridge that can collapse.
Sustained 45-65 degree snow/ice exposure with limited protection quality.

pitchDetail.notes: Descending traverse across the (South) Entiat Glacier beneath an icefall to the base of the face; multiple areas of wide, open crevasses.
Broad snow/ice runnel between two prominent rock buttresses; often soloed or simul-climbed in good neve conditions.

pitchDetail.grade: Up to 55-65° snow/ice (AI2-3 in harder conditions)
pitchDetail.notes: Steepens sharply near the top; possible short sections of hard alpine ice; watch for a cornice on the summit ridge.
Short scramble from the top of the face to the true summit.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) to Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: FR 6200 CLOSED beyond Atkinson Flat Campground to all vehicles as of a Forest Service order effective May 20, 2026 (through Dec 31, 2027 unless rescinded), due to Dec 2025 storm/flood damage; no reopening estimate as of this writing. Confirm current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before driving in. (The alternate east-side Entiat River Road approach is a separate, much longer road/trail.)
road.seasonalGate: The upper Chiwawa/Phelps Creek Road is typically snow-gated into late spring even in normal years.
road.driveNote: From US-2 near Coles Corner, take WA-207 toward Lake Wenatchee, then Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) about 22 miles, then right on FR 6211 for roughly 2.3-2.5 rough miles to the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft); high-clearance recommended.

climate.typical: East-Cascades continental climate in the Cascade crest's rain shadow; the face holds a narrower, earlier-season snow/ice window than west-side alpine faces, typically melting out by mid-to-late July.

bySeason.spring: Building/consolidating snowpack on the face; avalanche hazard on the glacier and lower face approach can be significant.
bySeason.summer: Snow/ice line present into roughly late June-early July, then rapidly deteriorates to loose rock and scattered ice/snow patches.
bySeason.fall: Route generally out of condition — bare, loose rock with little snow/ice cover.
bySeason.winter: Full winter alpine conditions; serious avalanche and access concerns, rarely climbed.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (west/Phelps Creek approach); Entiat Ranger District (east/Entiat River approach). Route is within Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.fees: No day-use fee found for Phelps Creek Trailhead itself.
access.permit: Free self-issued wilderness permit required at the Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary (~2.6 miles in on Phelps Creek Trail).
