access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass may be required at some nearby trailheads (e.g., the east-side Entiat River Trailhead); not confirmed as required at Phelps Creek Trailhead.
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) closed beyond Atkinson Flat Campground to vehicles as of 2026 storm damage — see 'road' field. Verify current status before travel.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine (pre-dawn) start to cross the Entiat Glacier and climb the face before afternoon rockfall and snow softening set in.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom (Chelan/Douglas Co. dispatch) non-emergency 509-663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth, WA — 509-548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (critical access hospital); higher-level trauma care at Confluence Health, Wenatchee, WA
emergency.notes: Remote glaciated terrain with no reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB, and rope up for all glacier travel.

routes.corrections: Given alpineGrade 'II-III' corrected to 'III' — Mountain Project and Mountaineers.org both list this as a Grade III route. Given iceGrade 'AI2-3' partially conflicts with Mountain Project's single 'AI2' rating; multiple trip reports describe conditions ranging from moderate 35-45° snow up to short sections of harder alpine ice (front-pointing, screw placements) near the top depending on year/season, which is consistent with the broader AI2-3 range, so it was retained. 'fa' listed here is for this North Face line specifically (Beckey/Gordon/Rupley/Staley, June 16, 1957), distinct from the peak's 1932 overall first ascent.

blurb: Mount Maude (9,082 ft) rises at the headwaters of the Entiat River in the Entiat Mountains, a remote subrange of the North Cascades within the Glacier Peak Wilderness, Chelan County. It is built largely of Cretaceous orthogneiss and tonalite, with talus-covered south flanks and a small glacier tucked beneath its steep north face. Hermann F. Ulrichs and John Burnett made the peak's first recorded ascent on August 19, 1932; the mountain is one of three 9,000-foot summits ringing the Entiat headwaters cirque, alongside Seven Fingered Jack and Fernow.

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical scramble line on Seven Fingered Jack: broad talus, scree, and (early season) snow slopes rising from Leroy Basin to the summit rocks on the mountain's southwest side.
routes.beta: From Leroy Basin (about 6,100 ft), climbers ascend either a rock gully or a gentler ridge to the right of it onto the lower southwest slope; the two lines converge in a flat area where the broad, rocky southwest slope begins. From there, trend up and slightly left, picking the best line among several options up loose granite to the summit rocks. Some parties instead take an obvious snow couloir/chute out of the basin toward the saddle between Seven Fingered Jack and Mount Maude, which can bypass the looser lower slopes in early season. The rock on the upper mountain is notably loose ("chossy"); above roughly 8,000 ft there is no established trail, only a faint, cairned climbers' path.
routes.approach: Start at the Phelps Creek Trailhead (elev. ~3,500 ft), reached via Coles Corner, WA-207, Chiwawa Loop Road, and Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200 (~22 miles, paved to gravel), then FR 6211 (Phelps Creek Road) for about 2.3–2.5 miles to road's end. Hike the Phelps Creek Trail about 3.5 miles to the Leroy Creek crossing (~4,140 ft; the trail enters Glacier Peak Wilderness at 2.6 miles). Immediately after the crossing, turn right (northeast) onto the steep, unmaintained Leroy Creek climbers' path and follow it roughly 1.5 miles up the north side of the creek to a basin camp near 6,100 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to Leroy Basin.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the southwest slopes back toward Leroy Basin, retracing (or varying slightly from) the ascent line; the loose granite demands care on the way down, and lingering snow in the couloir/chute can allow glissading or plunge-stepping in early season. From the basin, follow the climbers' path back down along Leroy Creek to the Phelps Creek Trail and out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2–3 day trip: Day 1, hike in to Leroy Basin (~6,100 ft) and camp; Day 2, climb the southwest slopes to the summit (optionally continuing to Mount Maude via the connecting col/traverse) and return to camp; Day 3 (if used), hike out. Strong parties have also completed it car-to-car in a long single day.
routes.bail: Retreat is a reversal of the ascent line: downclimb the southwest slopes/couloir back to the Leroy Basin camp. There are no fixed rappel anchors reported on this standard route, so bail options in deteriorating weather or footing are limited to careful downclimbing and, if present, glissading firm snow in the couloir.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and extended time spent on loose, exposed talus and scree, set a conservative turnaround time before starting up the southwest slopes so the party is back below the ridge and off unstable ground before afternoon thunderstorms, fatigue, or softening snow become a factor.
routes.bestSeason: The standard scramble season runs roughly June through September, once low-elevation snow has cleared the Phelps Creek Trail approach but before fall storms set in; some sources extend the climbing window into October. Snow can linger into early summer on the upper southwest slopes and in the couloir/chute variant, which can actually make for an easier, more stable line than the loose dry talus later in the season. Winter and early-spring ascents have been documented (including on skis/splitboard) but require full avalanche and glacier-travel judgment.
watchOut: Loose, chossy granite on the upper southwest slopes and near the summit spires
Lingering steep snow in the couloir/chute above Leroy Basin can require an ice axe and crampons well into early summer
Avalanche hazard on the southwest slopes/couloir during winter and spring ascents
No maintained trail or fixed route-finding aids above roughly 8,000 ft
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock on the upper mountain
Steep snow/couloir sections requiring ice axe and crampons in early season
Avalanche terrain on the southwest slopes and connecting chute in winter/spring
Route-finding difficulty above Leroy Basin
whatToBring: Free self-issued wilderness permit (available at the trailhead, required past the 2.6-mile Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary)
Overnight camping gear if using the Leroy Basin camp
Sun protection for the exposed talus/scree slopes
Extra water -- the basin and upper scree sections can be dry
Map/compass or GPS for route-finding above Leroy Basin, where there is no maintained trail
proTips: An early-season snow couloir/chute out of Leroy Basin toward the Maude col can bypass the looser lower southwest slopes and reach the same upper ridge (per The Mountaineers)
The Leroy Creek climbers' path is steep, unmaintained, and easy to miss -- it branches right immediately after the Leroy Creek crossing, about 3.5 miles up the Phelps Creek Trail
The rock on the upper mountain is notably loose -- test holds and be mindful of parties below you

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) and Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: As of mid-2026, Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) is closed to vehicles beyond Atkinson Flat Campground under a USFS storm-damage closure order (effective May 20, 2026 through Dec 31, 2027, pending repair); Phelps Creek Trailhead is listed among the recreation sites affected -- check current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts before driving.
road.seasonalGate: The upper road is typically snow-covered/inaccessible by vehicle in winter, reopening as snow melts out in late spring in a normal (non-closure) year.
road.driveNote: From Coles Corner: WA-207 north a few miles to Chiwawa Loop Road, then Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) roughly 22 miles (paved to gravel/dirt), then right on Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211) about 2.3-2.5 miles to the trailhead; the road is rough and may not suit low-clearance vehicles.

climate.typical: Best climbing months are generally cited as June through September/October; lingering snow is common into July on north- and shaded-slope terrain and in the summit couloirs.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack with avalanche hazard on the southwest slopes/couloir; some backcountry ski/splitboard descents are made in spring corn-snow conditions.
bySeason.summer: Primary scramble season; loose, dry talus and scree predominate once snow clears, with afternoon thunderstorm risk typical of Cascades summers.
bySeason.fall: Good stable-weather window into September, sometimes October, before winter storms arrive; nearby Leroy/Spider basins are known for fall larch color.
bySeason.winter: Full winter mountaineering/ski-mountaineering conditions; road access to the trailhead is typically snowed in, requiring a much longer ski, snowshoe, or snowmobile approach.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: No fee at Phelps Creek Trailhead; no Northwest Forest Pass required per USFS.
access.permit: Free, self-issued wilderness permit required at the trailhead once past the 2.6-mile Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary.
access.passRequired: None required at this trailhead.
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) closed beyond Atkinson Flat Campground under an active USFS storm-damage closure order affecting access to Phelps Creek Trailhead; verify current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before a trip.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Phelps Creek Trailhead (~3,500 ft) to Leroy Creek crossing (~4,140 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~3.5 miles on the maintained Phelps Creek Trail

sectionBreakdown.section: Leroy Creek climbers' path to basin camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Leroy Creek crossing to Leroy Basin (~6,100 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~1.5 miles on a steep, unmaintained climbers' trail

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Leroy Basin (~6,100 ft) to summit (~9,078-9,100 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~3,000 ft via the southwest slopes over talus, scree, and (early season) snow, Class 2-3 scrambling

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 (Chelan County emergency dispatch): 911, or 509-663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), Leavenworth: 509-548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA -- 24-hour Emergency Department
emergency.notes: Access to the Phelps Creek Trailhead is currently constrained by an active USFS storm-damage road closure on Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) beyond Atkinson Flat Campground; confirm road status before a trip.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation varies by source: Peakbagger 9,100 ft, listsofjohn.com 9,080 ft, Peakware 9,077 ft (closest to the given 9,078 ft), USGS GNIS 9,022 ft, PeakVisor 9,088 ft (2,770 m); no single authoritative figure exists, so the given 9,078 ft was retained. Wikipedia attributes the peak's overall first ascent to Richard Alt in 1932, but this could not be corroborated by a second independent source and is not confirmed to be specifically via this Southwest Slopes line, so no route-level 'fa' was set (per the rule that a peak FA is not automatically a route FA). The maxAngle (45°) and one-way distKm (10.5 km, derived from a sourced 13-mile round trip) are approximate figures drawn from trip-report descriptions rather than a surveyed profile.

blurb: Seven Fingered Jack is a craggy, serrated granite peak (roughly 9,078–9,100 ft depending on source) at the north end of the Entiat Mountains, a Cascade Range sub-range in Washington's Glacier Peak Wilderness, forming the middle summit of the "Entiat Cirque" trio alongside Mount Maude and Mount Fernow. Once mapped as the "Entiat Needles" for its jagged spires, it was renamed by USGS topographer and Wenatchee National Forest supervisor Albert H. Sylvester, and its slopes drain into both the Entiat and Wenatchee river basins near the Entiat Glacier. Wikipedia credits the first ascent to Richard Alt in 1932, though that specific claim could not be independently corroborated by a second source.

routes.fa: Franklin Bennet, Edgar Courtwright, Toivo Hagman — August 1937 (this is Copper Peak's overall first ascent; period accounts don't specify the exact line, though it is presumed to have followed close to today's standard Copper Basin / Northeast Ridge approach, the only documented line up the peak).
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 scrambling with one short, exposed 4th-class step near the summit (Wikipedia lists the peak's 'easiest route' as YDS class 4-5)
objHaz: persistent snowfield requiring crampons/ice axe into midsummer
avalanche potential on steep snow early season
routes.season: Jul-Sep (the east-face snowfield below the ridge often still requires crampons/ice axe into July)
routes.face: Northeast Ridge, approached across an east-face snowfield above Copper Basin
routes.overview: The standard line up Copper Peak climbs from a Copper Basin camp across a lingering east-face snowfield, then follows the Northeast Ridge through several similar-looking notches to a summit ridge that is mostly class 3 scrambling with one short, exposed class 4 section.
routes.beta: Copper Peak is most often climbed out of a Copper Basin camp above Holden Village, frequently paired with neighboring Mount Fernow (the two are connected by a rugged, 'contrived' ridge traverse with significant elevation loss/regain that is not generally recommended as a link-up). The route is a long, remote approach — boat, shuttle, and trail — followed by moderate but exposed scrambling rather than sustained technical climbing; the crux is routefinding among several ridge notches and a short 4th-class step near the top. A Mountaineers trip report combining Copper Peak and Mount Fernow in one outing logged roughly 9.7 miles round trip and just under 6,000 ft of cumulative gain by GPS, though that figure spans both summits, not Copper Peak alone, so it is not used here as a route-specific stat.
routes.approach: From Holden Village (3,228 ft, reached via the Lady of the Lake passenger ferry from Chelan to Lucerne Landing and then Holden's shuttle bus up FS Road 8301), follow the Railroad Creek Trail about half a mile, cross Railroad Creek, and pick up the Copper Creek Trail past the old mine tailings. Follow it roughly 3 miles up through a burn zone into Copper Basin (about 5,600 ft), a good camping area below the peak's east face.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the Northeast Ridge's class 3-4 terrain back through the same notch, re-cross the east-face snowfield (crampons), and descend talus and slabs back to Copper Basin and the Copper Creek Trail to Holden Village.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the ridge back toward the notch used on the way up — trip reports note this notch can require an airy, exposed class 3+ down-climbing move — then continue down the Northeast Ridge's class 3 terrain to the top of the east-face snowfield. Re-cross the snowfield on its far-right side (crampons on) to regain talus and slabs leading back to the Copper Basin camp, then follow the Copper Creek and Railroad Creek trails back to Holden Village.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip: Day 1 — ferry from Chelan to Lucerne Landing, shuttle to Holden Village, hike via Railroad Creek and Copper Creek trails to a basecamp in Copper Basin (~5,600 ft). Day 2 — climb the snowfield and Northeast Ridge to the summit and return to camp, then hike out to Holden Village (some parties combine the descent and hike-out into one long day).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward for most of the route by reversing the ridge scramble and snowfield traverse back to Copper Basin; the crux notches near the summit are the main spot where down-climbing is more involved than the way up, and some parties choose to back off there in poor conditions or with a large/inexperienced group.
routes.turnaround: Because the route involves picking the correct one of several similar-looking notches and an exposed 4th-class step, set a firm turnaround time before starting the ridge section so the down-climb and the snowfield/creek crossings on the way back to camp aren't rushed into the afternoon or after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Most ascents happen in mid-to-late summer (roughly July through September) once the long approach trail is clear of early-season snow, though the east-face snowfield just below the ridge is reported to still require crampons and an ice axe well into July. Access is further constrained by Holden Village's boat-and-shuttle logistics, which typically only run in the warmer months.
routes.detailedRack: Most parties climb this as an unroped scramble, carrying an ice axe and crampons for the snowfield below the ridge. A light rack (a few slings, a 30m rope) is worth having for the short, exposed class 4 moves near the summit notches, where some parties prefer to belay or rappel rather than solo them.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection exists on the route; natural gear (slings over horns, occasional small cams or nuts placed in cracks on the ridge's gneissic rock) can back up the short 4th-class sections for parties who don't want to solo them.
gear: 30m rope (optional, for the short class-4 notches)
light rack of slings/small cams for the exposed step
whatToBring: overnight/camping gear for a Copper Basin basecamp (typical 2-day trip)
food storage appropriate for Glacier Peak Wilderness bear activity
sun protection for exposed snowfield travel
map/compass/GPS for the ridge's similar-looking notches
proTips: Stay on the far-right side of the summit snowfield rather than climbing it directly — its top is guarded by cliff bands that are avoided by scrambling class 3 terrain on the right instead.
A basecamp in Copper Basin (~5,600 ft) breaks the long approach into a manageable two-day trip and gives easy access to both Copper Peak and Mount Fernow.
Carry an ice axe and crampons even in mid-summer — the east-face snowfield below the ridge has been reported to linger into July.
watchOut: Route-find carefully among the notches on the upper ridge; picking the wrong one leads to harder climbing (up to low 5th class) than the standard line.

FS Road 8301, the only access route between Lucerne Landing and Holden Village, has been washed out by landslides since December 2025, and Holden Village is closed to visitors through at least summer 2026 — verify current access status before planning a trip.
Loose rock is common on the ridge scrambling; parties should be mindful of rockfall exposure to climbers below.
knownHazards: The route crosses an east-face snowfield below the Northeast Ridge that has been reported to still require crampons and an ice axe into July.
Several notches along the upper ridge look similar; the wrong choice can lead to unexpectedly exposed 4th/low-5th-class terrain.
The connecting ridge to neighboring Mount Fernow involves significant elevation loss and regain over rugged, exposed terrain and is generally not recommended as a link-up.

Historic mine tailings and ongoing groundwater remediation work exist near Railroad Creek/Holden Village, and some side trails near the old Holden Mine site are subject to a standing conditional Forest Service closure order.

pitchDetail.notes: Holden Village to Railroad Creek Trail to Copper Creek Trail through a burn zone, reaching a camp in Copper Basin (~5,600 ft).
Talus and slabs above camp lead to the base of the east-face snowfield; crampons typically go on here.
Traverse the far-right side of the snowfield rather than climbing it directly — its top is guarded by cliff bands.
Scramble from the snowfield's right edge up to the crest of the Northeast Ridge.
Mostly class 3 with routefinding among several notches; one short, exposed class 4 step near the top.

road.name: FS Road 8301 (Lucerne Landing to Holden Village)
road.status: Closed/impassable as of 2026 — washed out by December 2025 storms and landslides; Holden Village is closed to visitors through at least summer 2026 while repairs (estimated around $20M) are underway.
road.driveNote: Not open to public vehicle traffic in any season under normal operations — standard access is by passenger ferry (Lady of the Lake) from Chelan to Lucerne Landing, then Holden Village's shuttle bus up FS 8301; that shuttle service is currently suspended during the closure.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades continental climate: colder winters and drier summers than the west-side Cascades, with snow persisting on north/east aspects and in the summit snowfield well into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack lingers on the approach trail and summit snowfield; avalanche hazard on steep slopes.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (roughly July-September) once the Copper Creek/Copper Basin trail and snowfield are more consolidated, though crampons are often still useful into July.
bySeason.fall: Cooler, more stable weather is typical, but access depends on Holden Village's boat-and-shuttle season, which is itself subject to the current road closure.
bySeason.winter: Very limited access; Holden Village and the approach trails become snowbound and this is not a winter objective under normal conditions.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan Ranger District, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No specific wilderness trailhead fee found; Holden Village's Lucerne-to-village shuttle bus and the Lady of the Lake ferry from Chelan are separate paid services.
access.permit: Self-issue wilderness permit typical at Glacier Peak Wilderness trailheads; no separate climbing permit found for Copper Peak.
access.closures: FS Road 8301 (Lucerne Landing to Holden Village) is washed out by December 2025 storm/landslide damage; Holden Village and this approach are closed to visitors through at least summer 2026, with reopening uncertain (possibly not until late 2027). Separately, a standing Forest Order (06-17-02-11-755) allows conditional closure — 'when posted' — of trails near the historic Holden Mine site, including the Copper Creek Trail, due to mine remediation work.

timing.recommendedStart: An early start from the Copper Basin camp is standard practice for scrambling routes with snow travel and route-finding among similar notches, to cross the snowfield while it's firmer and leave margin for the descent.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Holden Village to Copper Basin camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Reported in published trip accounts as roughly 'a few hours' of trail hiking with overnight gear.
Snowfield crossing, ridge scrambling, and notch routefinding; no consistent time figure is reported across sources.
One trip account describes reaching the trailhead by evening after a long same-day round trip from camp.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom regional dispatch at (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — (509) 682-4900
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health Hospital, Chelan, WA — (509) 682-3300 (nearest); Confluence Health/Central Washington Hospital, Wenatchee, WA is the nearest larger regional trauma center.
emergency.notes: Technical mountain rescue support in this area is typically coordinated through Chelan County Mountain Rescue alongside the Chelan County Sheriff's Office. Cell coverage is unreliable in the Railroad Creek/Copper Basin drainage; a satellite communicator is recommended given the remote, boat/shuttle-only access, which is itself currently disrupted by the FS Road 8301 closure.

routes.corrections: Source material (Wikipedia, Mountaineers.org trip listings, a Steven Song trip report, and Country Highpoints) consistently describes Copper Peak's standard/easiest line as ascending from a Copper Basin camp across an east-face snowfield to the Northeast Ridge — not a distinct 'South Route' as given. Difficulty is also consistently reported as sustained class 3 with a short, exposed class 4 step (Wikipedia's summary even lists the peak's easiest route as YDS class 4-5), rather than the given 'Class 2-3'. rockGrade above has been set to reflect this; since no separate south-side route is documented in any source found, this entry treats the given route as the standard Copper Basin/Northeast Ridge line.

blurb: Copper Peak (8,965 ft) is a gneissic summit in the Entiat Mountains, a North Cascades subrange, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness in Chelan County, Washington, about 80 miles northeast of Seattle. It sits just 0.88 miles north of its taller neighbor Mount Fernow as part of a rugged, connected massif that also includes Seven Fingered Jack, and its name refers to an abandoned copper mine at its northeast base. Franklin Bennet, Edgar Courtwright, and Toivo Hagman made the first recorded ascent in August 1937.

routes.overview: The standard route climbs Clark Mountain via a long backcountry approach through Boulder Basin and over ~6,300-ft Boulder Pass to the Walrus (Clark) Glacier on the peak's east side, then follows a moderate snow chute through scattered crevasses to a short Class 3 rock scramble on the summit block.
routes.beta: Most parties camp at Boulder Basin and climb Clark as a two-day (sometimes three-day) trip. From camp, drop over Boulder Pass to a bench on Clark's eastern arm, gain the Walrus Glacier, and ascend a moderately steep chute on the glacier's left (south) margin, stepping over narrow crevasses and crossing at least one snow-bridge, to a short summit scramble. A glacier-free alternative (the 'Southeast Route') avoids the glacier via a gully out of the south basin but is not the line documented here.
routes.approach: From Coles Corner on US-2, take Hwy 207 toward Lake Wenatchee; at ~MP 10.6 turn onto White River Road (FS 6400) and drive ~10.2 miles to its end at the White River Trailhead (2,300 ft). Hike the White River Trail (#1507) about 4 miles, then turn right (east) onto the Boulder Creek/Boulder Pass Trail. A few miles up, ford Boulder Creek (no bridge, splits into three shallow but cold channels) and continue to camp at Boulder Basin, roughly 5.5-6 hours from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: descend the glacier chute, cross back over Boulder Pass, and return via the Boulder Creek/White River Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the Class 3 scramble to the glacier, then descend the same chute on the glacier's south margin used on ascent, re-crossing the snow-bridge/crevasse sections. Regain the bench below Boulder Pass, climb back over the pass, and descend to Boulder Basin camp; from there retrace the Boulder Creek and White River Trails to the trailhead.
routes.bail: This is a non-technical route with no fixed rappel stations, so retreat at any point is simply reversing the ascent line back down the glacier chute to Boulder Pass and out the Boulder Creek Trail. The main complication for a bail is timing the unbridged Boulder Creek ford, which rises later in the day with snowmelt.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before committing to the glacier chute — softening snow bridges over crevasses later in the day, deteriorating visibility/flat light (both reported as common on this glacier), and a rising afternoon flow at the unbridged Boulder Creek ford all argue for an early start and a conservative decision to retreat if conditions or weather sour.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, once seasonal snow has consolidated enough to bridge the Walrus Glacier's crevasses but before the glacier opens into bare, more heavily crevassed ice later in the season; trip reports document glacier travel with fresh snow (6-12 in) as late as June slowing progress, and mosquitoes are noted in late June at lower elevations.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage is reported in Boulder Basin or on Clark Mountain itself; the remoteness of the Glacier Peak Wilderness and the multi-day nature of the approach make a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) advisable — Chelan County Sheriff's Office/SAR has responded to inReach-triggered rescues in this general area.
routes.face: Walrus (Clark) Glacier, east flank of the peak
objHaz: unbridged, multi-channel ford of Boulder Creek
loose rock on the summit scramble
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the Boulder Creek ford
Food storage appropriate for Glacier Peak Wilderness
Map/compass or GPS for route-finding on the glacier and pass
Camping gear for an overnight at Boulder Basin
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed; this is a glacier scramble rather than a roped rock climb. Bring a standard glacier-travel kit — ice axe, crampons, a rope for the team, and a basic crevasse-rescue rack (a few ice screws, prusik cords or mechanical ascenders, a pulley, and slings/carabiners) for the Walrus Glacier crossing, where narrow crevasses and at least one snow-bridge are typically encountered.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier-travel and crevasse-rescue gear rather than rock protection: a rope, crevasse-rescue hardware, ice axe, and crampons for the glacier chute. No fixed or removable rock protection is typically placed on the Class 3 scrambling sections above the glacier.
proTips: Time the Boulder Creek ford for the morning when flows are lowest; by afternoon on a warm day the three braided channels can run noticeably higher and colder to cross.
The Walrus Glacier route's crux chute stays on the left (south) margin of the glacier — staying off the open glacier ice keeps crevasses more visible.
Fresh snow can obscure crevasses on the glacier; parties report needing to probe and rope up conservatively after new snowfall even in early summer.
watchOut: Boulder Creek has no bridge; the three-channel ford can be knee-deep and cold, especially on the return in the afternoon.
Fresh snow (6-12 inches) on the glacier is reported to slow progress significantly and can hide crevasses.
Flat light and low cloud are common on the glacier and complicate route-finding through the crevasse field.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Walrus (Clark) Glacier require roped glacier travel and at least one snow-bridge crossing on the standard line.
The approach includes an unbridged, multi-channel ford of Boulder Creek.
Loose rock is present on the short summit scramble above the glacier.

pitchDetail.notes: White River Trail to the Boulder Creek Trail junction, about 4 miles, mostly forest.
Climb from Boulder Basin camp northeast to roughly 6,300-ft Boulder Pass, then descend steep snow on the far side to a ~6,700-ft bench on Clark's eastern arm.
Ascend a moderately steep chute on the glacier's left (south) margin, stepping over narrow crevasses and crossing a snow-bridge over one larger crevasse.
Short scramble over the summit rocks above the upper glacier snowfield to the true summit.

road.name: White River Road (Forest Service Road 6400)
road.status: Gravel forest road to a road-end trailhead; has a history of storm/flood damage and periodic closures (a June 2023 flash flood closed it, and it was again listed among Wenatchee River Ranger District roads affected by a December 2025 storm-damage closure order) — check current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Closed by snow in winter/spring; typically opens for the climbing season by early-mid summer depending on snowpack and any storm repairs.
road.driveNote: From Coles Corner on US-2, take Hwy 207 toward Lake Wenatchee; at about milepost 10.6 turn right onto White River Road and drive roughly 10.2 miles to the road-end trailhead.

climate.typical: East-side Cascades crest climate: warmer and drier than the west-slope North Cascades but still subject to fast-moving mountain weather; glacier terrain holds snow well into summer.

bySeason.spring: Continuous snow cover on the approach and glacier; avalanche and stream-crossing hazard both elevated.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; snow bridges over glacier crevasses are most reliable in late June-July, becoming more broken later in summer.
bySeason.fall: Route remains climbable into September in good years; earlier season closures of the access road or new snow can end the season abruptly.
bySeason.winter: Road and trail access effectively closed by snow; not a winter objective via this approach.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District); route lies within the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or Interagency/America the Beautiful pass) required to park at the White River Trailhead.
access.permit: Free, self-issued wilderness permit at the White River Trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass
access.closures: White River Road (FS 6400) has experienced repeated flood- and storm-related closures (e.g., a June 2023 flash flood and a winter-2025 storm-damage closure order affecting Wenatchee River Ranger District roads) — verify current road status with the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest before driving.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: White River Trailhead to Boulder Basin
sectionBreakdown.note: Via White River Trail then Boulder Creek/Boulder Pass Trail, including the unbridged Boulder Creek ford; ~3,000 ft gain.
Over Boulder Pass, down to the eastern-arm bench, up the Walrus Glacier chute, and the summit scramble.
Reverse of the summit push.

emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Leavenworth, WA)
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA — 24-hour Emergency Department (critical access hospital).
emergency.notes: Remote, multi-day approach with no reliable cell coverage; Chelan County Sheriff's Office (Special Operations SAR Unit) together with volunteer groups such as Chelan County Mountain Rescue and Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue respond to incidents in the Glacier Peak Wilderness. A satellite messenger is strongly recommended.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,587 ft) differs from USGS/Wikipedia's 8,602 ft (some peakbagging sites list yet another figure, ~8,582 ft); highPointFt above uses the USGS/Wikipedia figure. No reputable source documents a distinct named 'West Ridge' line on Clark Mountain — the well-documented standard non-technical route is universally called the 'Walrus Glacier' (or 'Clark Glacier') route, approached via Boulder Basin and Boulder Pass on the peak's south/west side and ascending the glacier on the east flank; a separate glacier-free 'Southeast Route'/gully line also exists via the south basin. The data above describes the Walrus Glacier line, which best matches the route's PD alpine grade and Class 2-3 scramble grade already on file.

blurb: Clark Mountain (8,602 ft per USGS/Wikipedia, though some peakbagging sites list 8,582 ft) is the highest summit in the Dakobed Range, a cluster of glaciated 8,000-foot peaks trending southeast from Glacier Peak in Washington's North Cascades, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. The North Cascades crystalline rock of the range carries the Richardson Glacier on the peak's northeast flank and the Walrus (Clark) Glacier on its east side, feeding a long, remote approach through Boulder Basin. It is climbed almost exclusively as a non-technical glacier scramble and is regarded by regional guides as one of the most scenic moderate glacier climbs in the eastern Cascades.

objHaz: lingering snowfields and avalanche terrain into early summer
exposed Class 3 moves near the summit
routes.overview: The standard Class 3 scramble climbs Buck Mountain's south-facing slopes and ridge system, most commonly approached from the King Lake basin below the peak's east ridge, finishing with exposed Class 2-3 scrambling on loose, slabby schist to the saddle between the south and middle summits and on to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Trinity Trailhead, follow the Buck Creek Trail (#1513) about 1.5 miles past the Chiwawa River Trail junction (3,200 ft), then leave the trail and climb forested, brushy, and open slopes southwestward to King Lake (5,940 ft), which has good camping. From the lake, climb directly south to a notch on Buck's east ridge, cross to the south side of the ridge, contour around the next ESE ridge onto the low-angle southeast slopes, and bear WNW to the saddle between the south and middle summits before scrambling up rubbly, slabby schist to the middle (highest) or north summit. An alternative, longer approach reaches the peak's west/southwest slopes via Buck Creek Pass and High Pass.
routes.approach: Drive to the Trinity Trailhead at the end of Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200), elevation ~2,800 ft. Hike the Buck Creek Trail (#1513) roughly 3 miles to the Chiwawa River Trail junction area, then leave the maintained trail and climb steep forested/brushy slopes southwest to King Lake (5,940 ft) to establish camp before the summit push.
routes.descent: Descend the same south/southeast slopes back toward King Lake or the high camp.
routes.descentText: Most parties reverse the ascent line, downclimbing the southeast slopes back to the saddle and King Lake basin. A documented alternate descent (used from the west-slope approach) drops to an ~8,100-ft saddle south of a false summit, crosses loose talus into the basin southeast of Buck, and finds a small notch near 7,140 ft on the rib dropping off the east ridge, leading down to the lower east ridge (~6,400 ft) and a steep, bushy descent east to Buck Creek.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trinity Trailhead to King Lake (or Buck Creek Pass/High Pass basin) camp. Day 2: climb Buck Mountain via the south/southeast slopes and return to camp; Day 2 or 3: hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this unroped scramble route — descend the same slopes back toward King Lake or the Buck Creek Pass/High Pass basin; no technical/rope descent is required on the standard line.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit push from camp and honor it — afternoon thunderstorms, the long remote approach, and loose schist on the exposed Class 3 terrain make a late or rushed descent significantly more hazardous.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly July through September, after high snowfields on the approach and summit slopes have melted out; snow can linger into July in the basins below the summit ridge. The route has also seen winter/early-spring ski-mountaineering ascents under stable avalanche conditions, but that requires additional glacier/avalanche skills.
routes.proNeeds: This is an unroped Class 2-3 scramble with no fixed protection or established rappel anchors; parties uncomfortable with the exposed schist moves near the summit sometimes carry a rope and a few slings for a hip-belay or short rappel, but it is not standard gear for this line.
gear: ice axe (early/late season or snow lingering on the approach)
whatToBring: map/compass or GPS (route-finding above the trail is tricky)
proTips: Camp at King Lake (5,940 ft) or in the upper basin near Buck Creek Pass/High Pass to break the long approach into two days.
A free, self-issue wilderness permit is required at the Trinity Trailhead in addition to a parking pass.
watchOut: Route-finding above King Lake is not obvious — the traverse around the ESE ridge to the south/middle summit saddle has caught parties off-guard.
Brushy, pathless slopes between the maintained trail and the upper basins add significant time and effort.
Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) is currently closed north of Atkinson Flats Campground due to 2025 storm damage, blocking the normal drive to the Trinity Trailhead as of mid-2026.
knownHazards: Loose, slabby schist near the summit ridge can shift underfoot on the exposed Class 3 terrain.
Buck Creek must be crossed (log or ford) near roughly 4,000 ft on some approach variants; high water can make this difficult.
Lingering snowfields and avalanche-prone slopes below the summit persist into early-mid summer in some years.

pitchDetail.notes: Trinity Trailhead via Buck Creek Trail #1513 to the Chiwawa River Trail junction area (~3,200 ft), then off-trail southwest to King Lake (5,940 ft)
From King Lake, climb south to a notch on Buck's east ridge and cross to its south side
Contour around the ESE ridge onto low-angle southeast slopes, bearing WNW toward the saddle between the south and middle summits
Exposed Class 2-3 scrambling up loose, slabby schist to the middle (highest) or north summit

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (Forest Road 6200/62)
road.status: Closed (storm damage) from Atkinson Flats Campground north to Trinity, effective 2026-05-20 through 2027-12-31 per USFS closure order, with no reopening estimate as of mid-2026
road.driveNote: Normally ~23 miles from Chiwawa Loop Road to the Trinity Trailhead; the final ~12 miles are rough gravel/dirt that may not suit low-clearance vehicles. Currently impassable/closed north of Atkinson Flats due to December 2025 storm damage.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use or $30/year at Trinity Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass honored)
access.permit: Free, self-issue wilderness permit required at the Trinity Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) closed from Atkinson Flats Campground north to Trinity Trailhead due to Dec 2025 storm damage; closure order effective 2026-05-20 through 2027-12-31 with no reopening estimate as of mid-2026, blocking the standard approach to this route.

sectionBreakdown.section: Trinity Trailhead to Buck Creek Pass (via Buck Creek Trail #1513)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~9.6 miles, ~3,100 ft gain on maintained trail (USFS figures for the full trail to the pass; King Lake approach leaves the trail sooner)
Off-trail Class 2-3 scrambling; ~4 hours round trip from a high camp per trip reports

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff via Rivercom Dispatch (non-emergency): 509-663-9911; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District: 509-548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (24/7 emergency department): 509-548-5807
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness climb with only a handful of ascents per year; cell coverage in the approach valleys and basins is unreliable per general USFS wilderness guidance for the area, so a satellite communicator is recommended.

routes.corrections: Sources disagree on Buck Mountain's exact summit elevation: older USGS 15-minute maps and most trip reports cite 8,528 ft; Wikipedia lists 8,534 ft; listsofjohn.com's LIDAR-derived figure of 8,539 ft (matching the given peak data) is likely the most accurate current value; one field-survey estimate (trailcatjim.com) suggests the true middle-summit elevation may be closer to 8,560-8,573 ft. No change is made to the given elevationFt since it already matches the best-sourced (LIDAR) figure. Separately, Wikipedia records the peak's first ascent as 1948 by Carl Suback and Ray Rigg, but the specific line they climbed is not documented, so this is not attributed as this route's 'fa' (a peak FA is not necessarily this route's FA).

blurb: Buck Mountain (8,539 ft per modern LIDAR survey) is a remote summit on Chiwawa Ridge in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, Chelan County, roughly 10 miles east of Glacier Peak in Washington's north-central Cascades. Built largely of schist and metamorphic rock typical of the range, with small glaciers and snowfields on its upper flanks, it is the second-highest point on Chiwawa Ridge after 8,760-ft Fortress Mountain and sees only a handful of ascents per year owing to its long wilderness approach. The peak was first climbed in 1948 by Carl Suback and Ray Rigg.

routes.overview: The West Ridge (also described as the Southwest Gullies–West Ridge) is the only well-documented route on Martin Peak: an alpine scramble that gains the ridge crest above Holden Pass and follows it east to a short, exposed Class 4 gully just below the summit.
routes.beta: From a high camp at Holden Pass (6,400 ft) — or via avalanche chutes and snowfields from Holden Lake (5,278 ft) to a cliff band near 7,000 ft — climb east to gain Martin's west ridge. Scramble the ridge crest easterly on Class 3-4 rock; easier ground lies on the south (climber's right) side rather than directly on the crest. A notch near 7,400 ft leads through ribs and a broad, loose, beige-marble gully back to the crest. About 100 ft below the 8,509-ft summit, a short, exposed Class 4 gully forms the route's crux.
routes.approach: Reach Holden Village (~3,200 ft) via the Lady of the Lake ferry from Chelan or Field's Point Landing to Lucerne, then the Forest Service shuttle bus (~11 mi on FS Road 8301) up the Railroad Creek valley. From Holden, follow the Railroad Creek Trail about 1 mile to its junction with the Holden Lake Trail (#1251) at 3,600 ft, then climb 4 more miles to Holden Lake (5,278 ft, ~5 mi one-way from Holden). From the head of the lake, continue north-northwest, gaining roughly 1,100 ft more, to camp at Holden Pass (6,400 ft) — the shared high camp for Martin and neighboring Bonanza Peak.
routes.descent: Downclimb the ascent line, staying on the south (climber's right) side of the ridge crest to avoid the more exposed Class 4 terrain directly on the crest; a fixed rappel anchor near the summit gully is available for parties who prefer not to downclimb the crux.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ascent gully and ridge, favoring easier ground below (south of) the crest rather than climbing the crest itself. Expect roughly 3-3.5 hours back to high camp. A fixed rappel anchor exists near the crux gully just below the summit for parties who prefer to rappel the short, exposed Class 4 step rather than downclimb it. Route-finding cairns exist in places but are not continuous, so note landmarks on the way up.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-day trip: Day 1 — ferry and bus to Holden Village, then hike ~5 mi to Holden Lake or continue on to Holden Pass camp (6,400 ft). Day 2 — climb Martin Peak (often combined with Bonanza Peak) round-trip from camp, roughly 8-9 hours camp-to-camp. Day 3 — hike out to Holden Village and catch the return shuttle/ferry.
routes.bail: Retreat by downclimbing the ridge/gully line back toward Holden Pass; the crux gully just below the summit has a fixed rappel anchor as an alternative to downclimbing it.
routes.turnaround: Because the summit gully and upper ridge are exposed, loose Class 4 terrain, turn back if the rock is wet or snow-covered, if the group is not comfortable downclimbing or rappelling the crux, or if afternoon thunderstorms are building — both rockfall and lightning exposure increase the longer a party lingers on the upper mountain.
routes.bestSeason: Most ascents happen in July and August, after snow clears the approach gullies; the east-slope Cascades location gives Martin a drier, earlier-melting season than peaks on the west side of the crest. Snow can still linger in the gullies into early July, and fresh snow is possible by mid-September.
routes.rappels: Not usually required (route is downclimbed); one optional rappel at a fixed anchor near the summit crux gully for parties who prefer not to downclimb it — rope length not documented.
objHaz: loose/choss rock on the ridge and summit gully
routes.detailedRack: A single 30-60m rope with a light rack — a handful of small-to-mid cams/nuts and a few slings — is enough to protect the roped Class 3-4 sections and the exposed Class 4 crux gully below the summit. Many strong scrambling parties climb the route unroped in dry conditions; a helmet is recommended given the loose rock.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal and mostly limited to the crux gully and short ridge steps; a few small-to-mid cams, nuts, and slings for natural gear (horns, chockstones, occasional trees) cover most parties' needs.
whatToBring: Sturdy approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
Multi-day camping gear for the Holden Pass/Holden Lake high camp
proTips: Combine Martin with neighboring Bonanza Peak from the same Holden Pass camp to make the long boat-and-bus approach worthwhile.
Stay on the south side (climber's right) of the west ridge crest — it offers noticeably easier terrain than climbing directly on the crest.
A rope and light rack let you protect or rappel the short Class 4 crux gully below the summit rather than downclimbing it exposed.
watchOut: The final summit gully is exposed Class 4 with loose rock — a fall here would be serious; many parties rope up for this section.
Route-finding on the ridge and gullies is easy to lose in the choss; cairns exist but are not continuous.
Afternoon thunderstorms and rockfall risk increase later in the day, especially on the exposed upper ridge.
knownHazards: Loose, chossy rock throughout the upper ridge and summit gully has caused reported rockfall incidents.
The approach gullies below the west ridge can hold snow into early summer, raising fall and wet-slide hazard.
The trailhead is reachable only by boat and Forest Service shuttle bus with no road access, making self-rescue and evacuation slow.

pitchDetail.notes: Holden Pass to the base of the west ridge; talus/scramble to gain the ridge crest.
Ridge crest scramble easterly; easier terrain lies on climber's right (south side) of the crest, through ribs and a loose, beige-marble gully.
About 100 ft below the summit: a steep, exposed summit gully — the route's crux. A fixed rappel anchor is present nearby.

road.name: FS Road 8301 (Lucerne Landing to Holden Village)
road.status: Holden Village and its approach corridor are closed to guests and hikers in 2026 due to flood damage, with no summer/peak season operations.
road.driveNote: No public road reaches Holden Village. Normal access is by Lady of the Lake ferry from Chelan/Field's Point Landing to Lucerne Landing, then an ~11-mile Forest Service shuttle bus on FS Road 8301 up the Railroad Creek valley (~40 minutes).

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades climate — drier and sunnier than the west-side Cascades, with earlier snow-melt and a longer climbing season.

bySeason.spring: Snow lingers in the approach gullies and on the ridge into June; avalanche and wet-snow hazard.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Aug); warm, mostly dry days with afternoon thunderstorm potential.
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures and possible early snow by mid-September; larches turn color in the surrounding basins.
bySeason.winter: Deep snowpack; Holden Village access runs on a limited winter boat schedule only (no summer shuttle) — not a realistic winter climbing objective.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan Ranger District
access.fees: No specific climbing fee found; access requires a paid round-trip Lady of the Lake ferry ticket (Chelan or Field's Point ↔ Lucerne) plus the Holden Village shuttle bus — confirm current fares at ladyofthelake.com.
access.permit: No route-specific climbing permit found; standard Glacier Peak Wilderness travel rules apply (free self-issue wilderness permits are typical at Okanogan-Wenatchee NF trailheads).
access.closures: Holden Village and the Lucerne-to-Holden approach corridor (FS Road 8301) are closed to guests and hikers in 2026 due to flood damage, with no summer/peak season Holden Village operations (targeted reopening winter 2026-27) — check holdenvillage.org before planning a trip. A standing Forest Order (#755, in effect since 2011) also restricts several named trails in the adjacent Holden Mine area 'when posted closed.'

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Holden Lake camp (5,278 ft) to ~7,000 ft cliff band
sectionBreakdown.note: Via avalanche chutes and upper snowfields; one documented account

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: ~7,000 ft cliff band to 8,509 ft summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Class 3-4 rock past the 7,400 ft west-ridge notch to the final exposed gully
Stays below the ridge crest to avoid Class 4 terrain on the crest itself

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); RiverCom 911 non-emergency (509) 663-9911 — dispatches the Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search & Rescue unit
emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, 428 W. Woodin Ave, Chelan, WA 98816, (509) 682-4900
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital, Chelan, WA
emergency.notes: The approach is boat- and shuttle-bus-only with no road access; expect no or unreliable cell coverage in the Railroad Creek/Holden area — a satellite communicator is strongly advised, and self-rescue/evacuation will be slow.

routes.corrections: The catalog lists this route as 'Southeast Slopes,' Class 2-3, alpineGrade F. No reputable source documents a route by that name on this specific Martin Peak (8,509 ft, Chelan County/Stehekin Mountains, immediately east of Bonanza Peak near Holden Village) — a 'Southeast Slope' descent line does appear in trip reports, but for a different, unrelated Martin Peak (8,377 ft, Methow Mountains/Sawtooth Range near Winthrop, accessed via Crater Creek and Martin Lake), which was excluded from this research. Every source found for the Holden-area Martin Peak (Wikipedia, Mountaineers.org route beta, and multiple trip reports) describes the only documented line as the West Ridge (aka Southwest Gullies–West Ridge) from Holden Pass/Holden Lake, rated Class 3-4 with a short, exposed Class 4 crux gully near the summit — harder than the catalog's Class 2-3/F. Fields above reflect this documented West Ridge route; alpineGrade has been set to PD- and rockGrade to 'Class 3-4' to match sourced difficulty. A peak first ascent (Ida Zacher Darr, July 1936) is documented, but no source ties it to a specific route line, so 'fa' was left null rather than assumed to be this route.

blurb: Martin Peak (8,509 ft) rises in the Stehekin Mountains subrange of the North Cascades, in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Chelan County, immediately east of Bonanza Peak — Washington's highest non-volcanic summit — above Railroad Creek and Holden Village. Composed largely of schist and marble, it is the second-highest summit in its group but draws only a handful of parties a year, overshadowed by its far more famous neighbor. Ida Zacher Darr is credited with the peak's first recorded ascent in July 1936; today it is most often combined with Bonanza Peak as a multi-day trip staged from a high camp at Holden Pass.

routes.overview: A remote, non-technical scramble up Luahna's east-facing slopes and south ridge, reached via a long cross-country approach through Boulder, Clark, and Luahna Basins from the White River valley; Class 2 ledges lead to the ridge crest, with a short, exposed Class 3+ step needed near the summit.
routes.beta: The standard line climbs scree, talus, and broken ledges on Luahna's east/south-facing slopes to gain the south ridge, then follows the ridge crest toward the summit. Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide describes it as a 2nd-class line, but most parties encounter a short Class 3-3+ traverse or step to bypass a gap in the ridge below the true summit. The route is glacier-free but crosses persistent snow and loose rock well into summer, and is commonly combined with adjacent Clark Mountain in a single long day or overnight trip.
routes.approach: From US-2 at Coles Corner, drive WA-207 (Lake Wenatchee Highway) about 10.5 miles, then continue on FS Road 6400 (White River Road) roughly 10 more miles to its end at White River Trailhead (2,300 ft). Hike White River Trail #1507 about 4 miles to the Boulder Creek/Boulder Pass Trail #1562 junction, then follow Boulder Creek Trail up through forest and brush to the open basin below Boulder Pass (roughly 5,000-5,800 ft). Leave the maintained trail and pick up a faint, unmaintained sheepherder's path that contours through a brushy first cirque into open, park-like Clark Basin, then continues into Luahna Basin below the peak's east slopes. From upper Luahna Basin, ascend scree and talus to the south ridge at roughly 7,500 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the ridge and ledges to Luahna Basin, then retrace the sheepherder's path back through Clark Basin to Boulder Pass Trail and White River Trail.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the Class 3+ step and ledges back to the south ridge, then descend scree/talus into upper Luahna Basin. Retrace the faint sheepherder's path west through Luahna Basin and Clark Basin -- trail-finding is the crux here, especially through the brushier first cirque -- to rejoin Boulder Pass Trail #1562, then White River Trail #1507 back to the trailhead. Parties combining Luahna with Clark Mountain typically descend Clark's south spur/gully first before traversing to Luahna, then reverse the whole line on the way out.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike in from White River Trailhead via White River and Boulder Creek trails to a camp at or above Boulder Basin (roughly 5,000-5,800 ft). Day 2: Travel cross-country through Clark Basin into Luahna Basin, climb the east slopes and south ridge to the summit, then return to camp -- a long day with significant elevation gain/loss and route-finding. Day 3: Hike out to the White River Trailhead.
routes.bail: The route is a straightforward reversal below the ridge -- retreat down the same scree/ledge line into Luahna Basin at the first sign of deteriorating weather, whiteout, or unstable snow. Above the ridge, the Class 3+ step is the main commitment point; if it is holding ice or fresh snow, back off rather than press for the summit.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit push and stick to it -- the return through Clark and Luahna Basins relies on a faint, easily-lost path, and afternoon thunderstorms and reduced visibility are common on this exposed ridge. Losing daylight or visibility while still above Luahna Basin significantly raises the risk of an unplanned bivy.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, after seasonal snow has mostly cleared the basins and ledges but before early-season storms return. Trip reports describe firmer, easier travel in early autumn (late Sept-Oct), but with a higher chance of fresh snow/ice on the ridge requiring crampons.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected once off Highway 207 into the Boulder Creek/Luahna Basin backcountry; carry a satellite messenger or PLB for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard scramble; carry an ice axe and crampons whenever snow lingers on the approach slopes, ledges, or ridge crest (common into midsummer and again in early fall).
routes.proNeeds: None on the standard line -- it is climbed unroped. A rope, a few slings, and a light rack may be prudent for parties uncomfortable with the exposed Class 3+ traverse near the summit, or if approaching via the alternate, glaciated East Ridge/Richardson Glacier variant instead.
gear: sturdy mountaineering boots or approach shoes
whatToBring: overnight/bivy gear for the standard 2-3 day itinerary
extra layers for exposed ridge weather
proTips: Most parties combine Luahna with nearby Clark Mountain in one long outing since both share the same Boulder Basin approach.
The old sheepherder's path through the cirques between Boulder Basin and Luahna Basin is faint and easy to lose -- track your route on the way in.
Beckey's guide rates the line 2nd class, but plan on some 3rd-class scrambling to bypass a gap in the ridge below the true summit.
watchOut: Loose, chossy rock on the southwest slopes and ridge crest.
A short, exposed Class 3+ traverse on the north side of the ridge below the summit.
Lingering snow/ice on ledges and the ridge well into summer.
Afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving weather common on the exposed upper ridge.
knownHazards: Extended unmaintained/off-trail travel with real route-finding difficulty through Clark and Luahna Basins.
Class 3-4 exposure near the summit ridge.
Wet-slide/avalanche terrain on approach slopes when early-season snow lingers.
Remote location far from help -- self-rescue capability is essential.

road.name: White River Road (FS Road 6400)
road.status: Open as of the Wenatchee River Ranger District's June 2026 storm-damage closure order, which does not list White River Road among the closed routes (unlike nearby Chiwawa River, Little Wenatchee, and several other district roads closed into 2027 for Dec 2025 storm damage).
road.seasonalGate: Typically inaccessible in winter/early spring due to snow; check current Okanogan-Wenatchee NF conditions before a shoulder-season trip.
road.driveNote: From US-2 at Coles Corner, take WA-207 (Lake Wenatchee Hwy) about 10.5 miles, then continue on FS Rd 6400 for about 10 miles to White River Trailhead (2,300 ft).

climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and springs; drier, warmer summers with afternoon convective storms common in the high Cascades.

bySeason.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack on approach slopes; avalanche hazard likely into June.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep); lingering snow patches on shaded ledges into July.
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures, shorter days, and an increasing chance of fresh snow/ice on the ridge by October.
bySeason.winter: Road access typically snowed in; a winter ascent (first documented January 2021) requires deep-winter mountaineering skills and avalanche-terrain avoidance.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30/year day-use fee at White River Trailhead (or a valid Northwest Forest Pass/Interagency Pass)
access.permit: Free, self-issued wilderness permit available at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (day-use fee applies)
access.closures: White River Road (FS 6400) itself was not on the district's June 2026 storm-damage closure list, but check current Okanogan-Wenatchee NF alerts before driving -- several nearby district roads remain closed into 2027 due to December 2025 storm damage.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start from high camp for the summit push, given the long, route-finding-heavy traverse through Clark and Luahna Basins.

sectionBreakdown.section: White River TH to Boulder Basin camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: White River TH (2,300 ft) to Boulder Basin (~5,000-5,800 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Via White River Trail #1507 and Boulder Creek Trail #1562.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Boulder Basin camp to Luahna summit (8,450 ft) via Clark/Luahna Basins and the south ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Per Jim Brisbine's trip report; cross-country with significant route-finding.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: (509) 667-6851 non-emergency (RiverCom 911 dispatches Chelan County Sheriff's Office); call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826 -- (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA -- (509) 548-5815 (Level 5 trauma ER)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach with no reliable cell service; carry a satellite communicator. Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue is the primary rescue resource for this area.

routes.corrections: The provided peak elevation (8,450 ft) matches ListsOfJohn; Wikipedia (citing USGS-derived data) lists the true summit at 8,445 ft with a separate 8,369 ft northwest subpeak -- the given 8,450 ft was kept, noting this ~5 ft discrepancy. Sources describe this standard non-glaciated line variously as the 'South Ridge,' 'Southwest Slope-Southeast Ridge,' or a 2nd-class 'East Ridge' (per Beckey); it is distinct from the glaciated Pilz Glacier route approached from the Napeequa Valley side. No source confirms the August 13, 1911 first-ascent party used this exact line, so a route-level FA was left null even though it is very likely the historic line of ascent.

blurb: Luahna Peak is a double-summited peak in the Dakobed Range within the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, Chelan County, Washington -- the second-highest peak in the range after nearby Clark Mountain, about 7 miles southeast of Glacier Peak. Composed of schist accreted roughly 50 million years ago and later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, it is ringed by the Richardson, Pilz, and Butterfly glaciers above the White River and Napeequa River drainages. The peak was first climbed on August 13, 1911 by Mazamas members Rodney Glisan, H.H. Prouty, Winthrop Stone, and C.W. Whittlesey.

waypoints.name: Dumbell Mountain summit (adjacent peak; route passes near the connecting col)

routes.face: West Ridge (finish); approach gully faces N/NE
objHaz: loose rock in the approach gully
lingering snow/ice on the Dumbell-Greenwood col
avalanche debris and steep snow patches into midsummer
routes.overview: A Class 2-3 scramble up Greenwood's west ridge from the Dumbell-Greenwood col, reached via a loose approach gully and an airy ledge traverse around Dumbell's east face; almost always combined with a climb of adjacent Dumbell Mountain in the same outing.
routes.beta: From a camp or day-start in upper Phelps Basin, climb a large, usually wet gully trending northeast from about 5,600 ft to a loose, class 2+ notch near 7,900 ft on Dumbell's southeast ridge. From the notch, follow a narrow, exposed ledge that wraps around Dumbell's east face (can be difficult or impassable when snow-covered) to the Dumbell-Greenwood col at about 7,740 ft, descending a short snowfield to reach it. From the col, a straightforward Class 2 talus scramble of roughly 700 feet up Greenwood's west ridge leads to the summit; the higher of Greenwood's two summit bumps is the southern one on the north-south summit ridge, marked by a cairn and a register placed in 2009.
routes.approach: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft), hike the Phelps Creek Trail about 4.5 miles to Spider Meadow, then continue past the signed Spider Gap/Upper Phelps Creek Trail junction roughly another half mile to a flat basin in upper Phelps Creek at about 5,400 ft (roughly 6.5 miles and 1,900 ft one-way to this point). This basin, near treeline around 5,800 ft, is the usual base for the climb.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the west ridge talus to the Dumbell-Greenwood col, re-ascend and cross the exposed ledge back around Dumbell's east face, descend the approach gully to upper Phelps Basin, and hike out the Phelps Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Class 2 talus of the west ridge back to the 7,740-foot Dumbell-Greenwood col, climbing back up the short snowfield/slope on the far side. Re-cross the narrow, exposed ledge around Dumbell's east face to the 7,900-foot notch, then downclimb the loose gully (staying near the slabs to skirt the flowing creek) back to upper Phelps Basin and the trail. There is no shorter alternate descent off this side of the mountain given the surrounding cliff bands.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-day trip: Day 1, hike from the Phelps Creek Trailhead through Spider Meadow to a camp in upper Phelps Basin (5,400-5,800 ft, ~6.5 miles). Day 2, climb the gully to the notch, traverse the ledge to the Dumbell-Greenwood col, and scramble Greenwood's west ridge to the summit, typically combined with an ascent of adjacent Dumbell before returning to camp and hiking out. Strong parties do the entire outing car-to-car in one long day (about 18 miles round trip with roughly 5,800 ft of total gain for both peaks, per trip reports).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward but long: reverse the route back down the ledge and gully to upper Phelps Basin at any point below the col. There is no shortcut off the ledge or gully due to the cliff bands on either side.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets your party off the exposed ledge traverse and loose gully before afternoon thunderstorms build or light fades — both sections are considerably more hazardous to downclimb in poor visibility or a storm, and the hike back to the trailhead from upper Phelps Basin is still several hours.
routes.proNeeds: No rope or fixed protection is typically used on this scramble route; carry an ice axe and crampons for the north-facing approach gully and the snow slope at the Dumbell-Greenwood col, which can hold snow into July.
proTips: Most parties combine Greenwood with adjacent Dumbell Mountain (8,416 ft) in the same outing, saving a second long approach.
A climbers' path exists partway up the approach gully from upper Phelps Basin toward the 7,900-foot notch.
Camping near treeline (~5,800 ft) in upper Phelps Basin shortens the summit push for both peaks and makes a two-day trip comfortable.
watchOut: The airy ledge traverse below the Dumbell-Greenwood col is narrow and exposed; it can be difficult or impassable when snow-covered.
The final approach gully to the notch is loose Class 2+ terrain with rockfall potential, and it almost always has water running down it.
Snow persists on the col traverse into July in many years, requiring an ice axe and possibly crampons.
knownHazards: The exposed ledge traverse around Dumbell's east face is narrow, airy, and can be nearly impassable when snow-covered.
The gully leading to the 7,900-foot notch is loose and unstable, especially in its final 100 feet.
Snow and small crevassed patches on the descent to the Dumbell-Greenwood col may require crampons and an ice axe into early summer.
Avalanche debris and lingering steep snow patches are visible on the peak's flanks well into midsummer in some years.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) to Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: Rough native-surface Forest Service roads; a high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially the last two-plus miles
road.driveNote: About 22 miles on FR 6200 from the Chiwawa Loop/Highway 207 area, then 2.3 miles on FR 6211 to the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft). Sections of FR 6200 beyond Atkinson Flats Campground have been closed by storm damage in recent seasons — verify current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before driving.

climate.typical: A continental-influenced East Cascades climate: snowy winters close road access, and the reliable snow-free window for the approach and route is roughly July through September.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack, elevated avalanche danger, and difficult seasonal creek crossings on the Phelps Creek Trail generally make spring attempts impractical.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep); the trail and route are typically snow-free by mid-to-late July in an average year, though the north-facing approach gully and the Dumbell-Greenwood col can retain snow into July.
bySeason.fall: Good conditions can persist into September with cooler temperatures and fewer bugs; watch for early-season snow and shrinking daylight.
bySeason.winter: Access roads (FR 6200/6211) are typically snowed in or gated; this becomes a winter mountaineering objective only for well-equipped, experienced parties.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District), Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.permit: A free, self-issue wilderness permit is required upon entering Glacier Peak Wilderness, about 2.6 miles up the Phelps Creek Trail.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) has been closed beyond Atkinson Flats Campground due to storm damage in recent seasons; check current Forest Service alerts before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start if attempting the whole route car-to-car in one day; otherwise camp in upper Phelps Basin and start early on summit day to beat afternoon thunderstorms.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft) to Spider Meadow
sectionBreakdown.note: About 4.5 miles via the Phelps Creek Trail

sectionBreakdown.section: Spider Meadow to upper Phelps Basin
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Spider Meadow to flat basin at 5,400 ft
sectionBreakdown.note: About half a mile past the Spider Gap/Upper Phelps Creek trail junction

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Upper Phelps Basin to Dumbell Mtn summit (8,416 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: 3-4 hours per multiple trip reports

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Dumbell's south ridge to Greenwood summit (8,413 ft) and return
sectionBreakdown.note: Allow roughly 2 extra hours round trip via the ledge traverse and col

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead to both summits and back to trailhead
sectionBreakdown.note: About 18 miles round trip with ~5,800 ft total gain, per a car-to-car trip report climbing both Dumbell and Greenwood together

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or Chelan County/RiverCom dispatch at 509-663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA (24-hour ER), (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach with no confirmed cell coverage; Chelan County Mountain Rescue, a volunteer SAR group, responds to callouts within Glacier Peak Wilderness.

routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is prime, once seasonal creek crossings on the Phelps Creek Trail have dropped and snow has receded from the approach gully and the Dumbell-Greenwood col; snow can linger on the north-facing gully and col well into July in many years, so early-season parties should carry an ice axe and crampons.
routes.corrections: Sources disagree on Greenwood Mountain's summit elevation: ListsOfJohn.com (a recognized peakbagger authority) lists 8,413 ft, matching this app's existing data, while Wikipedia's Dumbell Mountain article lists Greenwood as an unofficial 8,408 ft 'northeast subpeak' of Dumbell, and other sources (Mountaineers.org, LemkeClimbs) cite 8,415 ft. No change made to elevationFt given the ListsOfJohn match; flagging the discrepancy for awareness.

blurb: Greenwood Mountain is a subpeak of the double-summit Dumbell Mountain massif in the Entiat Mountains, deep within the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Chelan County's North Cascades. It sits within the Cloudy Pass batholith, formed from gneissic hornblende quartz diorite roughly 20 million years ago, and is almost always climbed in tandem with adjacent Dumbell (first ascended in 1932 by Richard Alt and George Fahey) via a long approach through Spider Meadow and upper Phelps Basin. Sources disagree slightly on its exact summit elevation (roughly 8,408-8,415 ft depending on source), a reflection of differing surveys of this remote, rarely visited peak.

routes.overview: The standard scramble route on South Spectacle Butte follows the Southwest Ridge from the larch basin between the two buttes, climbing Class 2-3 rock and talus with a short, exposed Class 3-4 crux around a rotten second spire near 7,700 ft before easier ledges lead to the summit block.
routes.beta: Most parties approach via Phelps Creek Trail to Leroy Basin, cross the Leroy High Route to Ice Lakes, camp at Lower or Upper Ice Lake, then descend and traverse into the basin between North and South Spectacle Butte before climbing the Southwest Ridge to the south summit. Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide describes the line as a straightforward Class 3 ridge scramble, but several trip reports note the ridge crest is broken by loose gendarmes that force route-finding onto ledges and a short chossy chute/dike passage that some parties rate closer to Class 4.
routes.approach: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead (~3,500 ft, end of Phelps Creek Road off Chiwawa River Road), hike the Phelps Creek Trail roughly 3.5 miles to the Leroy Creek turnoff, then climb the steep, unmaintained Leroy Creek climbers' trail into Leroy Basin (camping ~5,400 ft). From Leroy Basin follow the Leroy High Route east toward the Maude-Seven Fingered Jack col, then take the trail branch south (~1.5 miles) over a shoulder below Mount Maude and drop to Ice Lakes. From Lower Ice Lake, descend the Ice Creek drainage to about 6,400-6,500 ft, then cut left (east) across talus, erosion gullies, and heather slopes into the larch basin between the two buttes at the base of the southwest ridge (~6,800-6,900 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the same Southwest Ridge climbing route back to the basin between the buttes, then reverse the traverse to Ice Lakes and the Leroy High Route/Phelps Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ledge system down the southeast side of the summit block back onto the southwest ridge crest, downclimbing or bypassing the loose second-spire gendarme on the same line used for the ascent. Continue down the ridge to about 6,800 ft, then cross back west through the larch basin, erosion gullies, and talus to Ice Creek and up to Lower Ice Lake. From Ice Lakes, retrace the Leroy High Route to Leroy Basin and descend the Leroy Creek trail to the Phelps Creek Trail and trailhead.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at almost any point on the southwest ridge by reversing the ascent line back into the basin between the buttes; there is no fixed rappel infrastructure, so retreat is by downclimbing the same terrain.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent retraces exposed, loose Class 3-4 ridge terrain and a long cross-country approach, parties should set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to reverse the ridge and reach camp or the trailhead before dark, and turn back early if weather, rockfall risk from other parties, or route-finding delays compound.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, after seasonal snow has cleared the approach basins and before early-season snowfall; trip reports cluster in late August and September, when Ice Lakes and the ridge are snow-free but water sources above Ice Lakes can be limited.
whatToBring: Bear canister or hang line (bear activity reported in basin)
Overnight camping gear for Leroy Basin or Ice Lakes base camp
Water treatment (limited sources above Ice Lakes)
Sun protection for exposed talus slopes
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is placed; this is an unroped scramble on loose rock, though some parties carry a short rope/harness for the exposed traverse near the second spire as a precaution.
objHaz: Exposed Class 3-4 traverse near second spire
knownHazards: A rotten second spire near 7,700 ft on the ridge crest forces an exposed, consequential Class 3 (some rate Class 4) traverse around a gully headwall.
Loose talus, erosion gullies, and hardpan-dirt chutes below the ridge are prone to slipping underfoot.
Multiple gendarmes on the ridge crest require route-finding to bypass, typically on the south (climber's right) side.
Bears have been reported in the Ice Lakes/basin area.
watchOut: The gully/ditch crossing below the ridge has loose clay and scree that has stopped some parties.
The crux traverse near the second spire is short but exposed and consequential; do not underestimate it based on Beckey's brief 'benign' route note.
Water is scarce above Ice Lakes, especially late season.
proTips: Camp at Lower Ice Lake to shorten the summit day versus a Leroy Basin-only camp.
Stay on the south (climber's right) side of ridge obstacles/gendarmes, as the north side of the ridge is cliffy.
Expect roughly 4.5-5 hours from an Ice Lakes camp to the summit and 2.5-3 hours back down.
A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for the rough Chiwawa River Road/Phelps Creek Road approach to the trailhead.
