pitchDetail.notes: Talus and broken rock up the southwest ridge crest, bypassing minor gendarmes on climber's right.
Crux: exposed traverse around a rotten gully headwall; one documented line descends a short chossy chute, traverses on down-sloping rock, then ascends a Class 3 dike.
Leave the ridge crest and follow ledges/ribs on the southeast side of the summit block to the top.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road / Phelps Creek Road
road.status: Rough gravel/dirt, not suitable for low-clearance vehicles
road.driveNote: About 22 miles up Chiwawa River Road (paved then gravel after ~11 miles) plus roughly 2.3 miles of rocky Phelps Creek Road to the Phelps Creek Trailhead at ~3,500 ft.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center / Central Cascades East forecast zone (summer climbing conditions; no winter avalanche route data found)

bySeason.summer: Warm, dry days typical July-September with afternoon thunderstorm risk; wildfire smoke can affect visibility in late summer.
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures and possible early snow by late September/October.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at trailhead entry into Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

sectionBreakdown.section: Phelps Creek Trail to Leroy Creek turnoff
sectionBreakdown.note: Well-maintained trail, minimal elevation gain

sectionBreakdown.section: Leroy Creek trail to Leroy Basin camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Leroy Creek junction to Leroy Basin (~5,400 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, unmaintained but well-trodden trail, ~1,200 ft/mile

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Ice Lakes/Leroy Basin camp to South Spectacle Butte summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Leroy High Route, Ice Lakes traverse, and southwest ridge

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: South Spectacle Butte summit to camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Reverse of ascent route

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch handles Chelan County Mountain Rescue call-outs
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550 (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest)
emergency.nearestHospital: Central Washington Hospital, 1201 S Miller St, Wenatchee, WA, (509) 662-1511 (approx. 1.5-2 hr drive from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Chelan County Mountain Rescue is the volunteer SAR resource for the Entiat Mountains/Glacier Peak Wilderness area; cell coverage is unreliable in the Phelps Creek/Ice Lakes basin.

routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage reported in the Phelps Creek valley, Leroy Basin, or Ice Lakes basin; a satellite communicator is advisable for this remote, multi-day approach.
routes.fa: South Butte: Rowland Tabor and Dwight Crowder, August 20, 1953 (peak first ascent; not confirmed as specific to this southwest ridge line)
routes.corrections: Peak elevationFt of 8400 given in source data is a rounding of the surveyed South Butte elevation of 8,392 ft (confirmed via Wikipedia/Peakbagger); highPointFt set to 8392.

blurb: Spectacle Buttes are a pair of gneissic summits in the Entiat Mountains subrange of the North Cascades, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness on the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest in Chelan County. The higher, pyramid-shaped South Butte (8,392 ft) and the lower North Butte (8,080+ ft) are separated by a deep saddle near 7,280 ft and sit about 1.9 miles east-northeast of Mount Maude. The south peak was first climbed by Rowland Tabor and Dwight Crowder on August 20, 1953; both summits are reached by long cross-country approaches from the Phelps Creek / Leroy Basin / Ice Lakes trail network rather than by maintained trail.

routes.overview: A remote, off-trail scramble up Chalangin's south side: gain the Chalangin–Luahna col/ridge from the Boulder Basin or Napeequa side, then follow class-3 terrain to the summit.
routes.beta: Chalangin has no maintained trail and only a handful of documented ascents, most since it was added to the Washington Top 100 list. Climbers use three approach corridors: (1) White River Trail to Boulder Creek/Boulder Basin, over or near Clark Mountain and Luahna Peak, then a short ridge traverse to Chalangin — the most above-treeline travel, and the natural choice when combining with Clark and Luahna in one trip; (2) White River Trail to Thunder Creek, then a bushwhack up Thunder/Chalangin Basin onto the west face and the Chalangin–Luahna col — shortest distance and least elevation gain of the three, with the least time above treeline and avalanche exposure; (3) Chiwawa River Road to Little Giant Trailhead, over Little Giant Pass into the Napeequa Valley, then up the east side past the Pilz Glacier margin — the longest option but mostly on trail. From the col/ridge, parties report the final push to the summit stays class 3 with some exposure ('every time there was a difficult-looking section, there was always a reasonable way down, never exceeding class 3'), though one trip log describes it as 'loose class 4 or low 5th class' depending on line choice, and another notes '4th class moves' on an icy rock step early in the season. Loose rock is consistent across all reports.
routes.approach: Most parties start at the White River Trailhead (end of White River Road/FR 6400, ~2,300 ft): from Coles Corner on US-2 take WA-207 (Lake Wenatchee Hwy) 10.5 mi, then White River Road ~10 mi to the road-end trailhead. Follow White River Trail roughly 4 miles to the Boulder Creek Trail junction (for the Clark/Luahna ridge line) or continue toward Thunder Creek and bushwhack up Thunder/Chalangin Basin (for the west-face line) to snowline near 3,500 ft and a camp/basin around 5,100–6,200 ft below the Chalangin–Luahna col. The alternative east-side approach starts at the Little Giant Trailhead on Chiwawa River Road, fords the Chiwawa River, crosses Little Giant Pass into the Napeequa Valley, and climbs northwest through larch basin toward the Pilz Glacier margin.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line to the col/ridge, then descend the same approach corridor used going up.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class-3 ridge/face back to the Chalangin–Luahna col (or ridge point used on the ascent), taking care on loose rock and any exposed steps. From there, retrace the chosen approach: back down Thunder/Chalangin Basin to White River Trail, back over Boulder Basin/Boulder Creek to White River Trail, or back over Little Giant Pass to the Chiwawa River. Parties have found the descent easier than expected because a walkable line ('never exceeding class 3') was found around each apparently harder step, but downclimbing loose terrain still warrants care and, on early-season snow, an ice axe/crampons for the col approach.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a 2–3 day backpack: Day 1 hike in to a basin camp (Chalangin/Thunder Basin ~5,100 ft, or Boulder Basin ~5,800 ft, or a Napeequa Valley camp near Butterfly Butte ~6,200 ft); Day 2 climb to the col/ridge and scramble to the summit (often combined with Clark Mountain and/or Luahna Peak on the Boulder Basin approach), then return to camp or partway out; Day 3 hike out. Strong parties have also done the West Face/Thunder Creek line car-to-car in a long single day (one report: summited about 8 hours after a 12:45 AM start).
routes.bail: Retreat down the same ascent corridor to the col/ridge and back into the basin you camped in; there is no shorter bail option given the remote, off-trail terrain. On the Boulder Basin/Clark-Luahna line, drop back into Boulder Basin toward the White River Trail; on the Thunder Creek line, descend into Thunder/Chalangin Basin; on the Little Giant line, return over Little Giant Pass to the Chiwawa River.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting given the long, mostly off-trail approach and remote location — the round trip commonly runs a very long day or multi-day trip, terrain is loose and exposed near the summit, and any of the approach basins can hold lingering snow and avalanche-prone slopes well into early summer.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through September is the working window; late May/early June can offer firm, snow-covered approach slopes with the summit block melted out to rock, but requires crampons/ice axe or a whippet for icy traverses and carries avalanche risk on the col and basin slopes. Mid-summer through September gives snow-free scrambling but more loose rock and exposed slide-alder bushwhacking lower down; an October ascent has also been reported in dry, stable conditions. Winter/early-season attempts have been turned back by wind-loaded avalanche slopes near the col.
routes.comms: No reputable source documents cell coverage for this peak; the surrounding Glacier Peak Wilderness basins and ridges are remote and coverage should be assumed unreliable to nonexistent — carry a satellite communicator/PLB.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock/ice gear is required for the standard class-3 scramble; parties instead carry approach/glacier-travel gear appropriate to the season: ice axe, crampons (or a whippet for firm/icy traverses) for the col and basin slopes in late spring/early summer, snowshoes for lingering snowpack, and a helmet for the loose final ridge/face.
routes.proNeeds: Not a roped/protected climb on the standard line; no rack is carried. Parties on the Little Giant/east-side approach note a crevassed Pilz Glacier margin nearby and recommend roping up or careful route-finding if glacier travel is required.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
Avalanche safety gear if traveling on snow-covered approach slopes
proTips: Combine Chalangin with Clark Mountain and Luahna Peak in one trip via the Boulder Basin approach if you're out for all three DaKobed-area summits.
The Thunder Creek/west-face line has the least distance, least elevation gain, and least time above treeline/avalanche terrain of the three approach options.
Late May/early June can let you travel on firm snow most of the way with the summit melted to rock, but expect an icy overnight-freeze crust that calls for crampons and a whippet on steep traverses.
On the final scramble, a walkable path around apparent 4th-class-looking steps has consistently been found by parties who took time to route-find, keeping the climb at class 3.
watchOut: Loose, chossy rock on the final ridge/face to the summit.
Avalanche-prone slopes near the Chalangin–Luahna col and below the summit in spring/early-season/winter conditions — at least one party turned back here due to wind-loaded slabs.
Thigh-deep, swift creek crossings (Thunder Creek, Chiwawa River) especially during spring snowmelt.
Significant off-trail travel and bushwhacking (slide alder) below treeline on all three approach corridors.
Crevassed terrain near the Pilz Glacier margin on the Little Giant/east-side approach.
knownHazards: Loose rock (choss) on the summit ridge/face has caught multiple parties off guard.
Avalanche terrain exists on the approach slopes and near the col well into early summer.
Deep/fast creek and river fords are required on at least two of the three approach options.
The Pilz Glacier is crevassed near the east-side route.

road.name: White River Road / FR 6400
road.status: Generally maintained to the road-end trailhead
road.driveNote: From Coles Corner on US-2, take WA-207 (Lake Wenatchee Hwy) 10.5 mi, then White River Road/FR 6400 about 10 mi to the trailhead at road's end (~2,300 ft). Note: the alternate Little Giant Trailhead is reached via Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200), which has had storm-damage closures beyond Atkinson Campground — check Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest current-conditions postings before relying on that access.

climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate with a heavy winter snowpack; upper basins and the summit ridge are typically snow-free from roughly late June through September.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June; avalanche hazard on approach slopes and near the col, with an icy overnight freeze-crust common on firm spring mornings.
bySeason.summer: Best window (Jul–Sep); trail and upper slopes generally snow-free, loose rock and slide-alder bushwhacking lower down, afternoon thunderstorms possible.
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures with dry, stable conditions reported into early October in some years; shortening daylight.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow, road closures, and avalanche terrain make this a non-objective in winter; at least one winter/November attempt was turned back by wind-loaded slabs.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Lake Wenatchee/Wenatchee River Ranger District), within the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or interagency/America the Beautiful pass) required for trailhead parking; day passes available at the trailhead.
access.permit: Free, self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead (Glacier Peak Wilderness); no quota or reservation.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) has been closed beyond Atkinson Campground due to storm damage, affecting Little Giant Trailhead access; check Okanogan-Wenatchee NF current-conditions postings before a trip on that approach.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/midnight start recommended for a single-push day via the White River Trail (one reported ascent started 12:45 AM and summited by 8:45 AM); most parties instead backpack in over 2–3 days.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 dispatch; non-emergency (509) 663-9911; Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue.
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District (Lake Wenatchee area), Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — (509) 548-2550.
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24-hour Emergency Department, (509) 548-5815.
emergency.notes: Remote, off-trail wilderness terrain with essentially no cell coverage; Chelan County Mountain Rescue (CCMR), a volunteer technical rescue team, supports the Sheriff's SAR unit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness area.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (8,371 ft) is consistent with listsofjohn/peakbagger/countryhighpoints survey data (8,370.7 ft surveyed, commonly rounded to 8,370–8,371 ft); no correction needed. Older USGS-derived figures cited in some trip reports (e.g. '8,320+/8,366 ft', '8,369 ft') reflect pre-resurvey estimates superseded by the 8,370.7 ft survey used to add the peak to the WA Top 100 list.

blurb: Chalangin Peak (8,371 ft; surveyed 8,370.7 ft) is the northwest sub-summit of Luahna Peak in the Dakobed Range of the North Cascades, rising above the head of the Napeequa River about half a mile northwest of Luahna's true summit, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan County). It shares Luahna's schist bedrock and sits along a ridge running west toward Tenpeak Mountain above the Honeycomb/Pilz Glacier. Long overlooked, it has drawn only a handful of documented ascents, mostly since updated survey data added it to the Washington Top 100 peaks list; no separate first ascent is documented for the summit or any of its lines.

routes.overview: A long, remote scramble up Buckskin Mountain's south-facing rib/face from the Entiat Meadows basin at the head of the Entiat River valley, gaining the summit ridge on talus and scree rather than technical rock.
routes.beta: The standard line climbs a rib on the south side of the peak directly above Entiat Meadows — trip reports describe it as the 'least brush, straight shot' option among several parallel ribs/gullies on the south face. Parties gain a broad south/southwest slope of scree, talus, and heather to the summit ridge; in early season this same terrain holds firm snow and the final ridge can be corniced with gendarmes, turning the outing into a real mountaineering objective rather than a summer scramble.
routes.approach: From the town of Entiat on US-97, drive the Entiat River Road (County Rd 19, becoming FR-51 then FR-5100, pavement ends near Cottonwood Campground around mile 34) about 38 miles to the Entiat River Trailhead (day-use only, no overnight camping at the trailhead). Hike the Entiat River Trail #1400 up-valley through 2015 Wolverine Fire burn terrain (deadfall, dust, some river fords) roughly 14-15 miles to the Entiat Meadows/Ice Creek area at the head of the valley, which serves as the base for the climb.
routes.descent: Descend the same south rib/face used on ascent back to Entiat Meadows, then reverse the Entiat River Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Down-climb or scramble the ascent rib back to the open slopes above Entiat Meadows, avoiding the corniced east ridge and any steep snow gullies that may be softened by afternoon sun; regain the valley trail at Entiat Meadows and hike out the Entiat River Trail.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a 2-3 day backpack: hike in ~14-15 miles to a camp near Entiat Meadows/Ice Creek on day one, climb Buckskin via the south rib on day two, and hike out on day two or three. At least one recorded ascent instead used a mountain bike on the approach road/trail to complete trailhead-to-summit-to-trailhead (about 42 miles round trip with roughly 6,500 ft of cumulative elevation gain) in a single very long day.
routes.bail: This is an unroped scramble with no fixed protection — retreat by reversing the ascent rib back down to Entiat Meadows; do not descend unfamiliar gullies or the corniced east ridge toward the Fernow-Buckskin saddle.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before committing to the upper south face or summit ridge if thunderstorms are building, if the south-facing snow/scree is softening badly in afternoon sun (rockfall and wet-slide risk), or if the party is not comfortable with exposed Class 3 scrambling above cliffs — the descent and hike out are long and remote.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September gives the most reliable conditions, once the approach trail and the south face have melted out to talus and scree. The route can still hold significant snow and a corniced final ridge into June, at which point an ice axe, crampons, and glacier-travel-level judgment are warranted; the Entiat River Road/trailhead gate can also restrict early-season access.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in the Entiat River valley or on the peak; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended given the length and remoteness of the approach.
objHaz: avalanche and wet-slide potential on the south face in early season
corniced summit ridge with gendarmes (early season)
lightning exposure on the open summit ridge
deadfall and dusty terrain in the 2015 Wolverine Fire burn
river fords along the Entiat River Trail
knownHazards: The 2015 Wolverine Fire burned much of the lower Entiat River Trail, leaving deadfall, dust, and reduced shade for miles of the approach.
The south face used for the climb is an avalanche path and holds a large open snow slope well into early summer.
In spring/early season the summit ridge near 7,200 ft (the Fernow-Buckskin saddle area) can carry large cornices and rock gendarmes requiring careful routefinding.
The approach involves multiple fords of the Entiat River and side creeks that can run high during snowmelt.
watchOut: Loose, unstable talus and scree on the upper south face — rockfall risk to parties below.
Corniced, gendarme-studded summit ridge in late spring/early summer conditions.
Long, exposed, and remote approach with no cell coverage — injuries or weather changes are serious commitments.
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer on this open, high alpine terrain.
proTips: Camp at or near Entiat Meadows to shorten summit day and take advantage of the flattest, most established base in the upper valley.
Go after the south face has melted to scree/talus (typically by late July) to avoid the steep snow and corniced ridge of an early-season ascent.
Some parties mountain-bike the lower trail/road when the Pope Creek gate is closed to cut hours off a long approach.
Pick the rib with the least brush directly above Entiat Meadows rather than adjacent gullies, which tend to hold more brush and deadfall.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed for the standard summer condition — this is an unroped Class 2-3 scree/talus scramble. In early season (typically before mid-to-late July), carry an ice axe and crampons for the snow-covered south face and the corniced final ridge.
routes.proNeeds: None for the summer scramble condition; no fixed protection exists on this route. Early-season ascents crossing the corniced east ridge/gendarmes near the Fernow-Buckskin saddle are a genuine mountaineering undertaking and should be treated with full glacier-travel judgment.
whatToBring: insect repellent (mosquitoes are heavy in the burn/meadow terrain)
water treatment (Entiat River and side creeks)
map and compass or GPS (extended off-trail travel above Entiat Meadows)
bivy/camping gear for a multi-day approach

road.name: Entiat River Road (County Rd 19 / FR-51 / FR-5100)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 34 miles from Entiat, then gravel to the trailhead near mile 38; the road has a history of washout/mudslide closures (closed 2014-2017) and can be gated early in the season.
road.seasonalGate: A gate near Pope Creek can remain closed into late spring, adding roughly 6 miles of road walking or biking each way to reach the trailhead.
road.driveNote: From the town of Entiat on US-97, follow County Rd 19, which becomes FR-51 and then FR-5100, about 38 miles up the Entiat Valley to the Entiat River Trailhead.

climate.typical: High, dry east-Cascades alpine climate; snow can linger on the south face into early summer, and summer afternoons bring a real thunderstorm risk on the open summit ridge.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack on the approach and climbing terrain, avalanche-prone south face, and the access road/gate often still closed.
bySeason.summer: Best window from roughly late July on; trail mostly snow-free and lower valley hot and dusty through the 2015 burn, upper mountain exposed scree/talus.
bySeason.fall: Often stable, cool weather into September; early snow is possible and the road may still be open.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible — heavy snow, serious avalanche terrain, and the Entiat River Road closed.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Entiat Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass required for trailhead parking.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / federal recreation pass
access.closures: Entiat River Trailhead is day-use only — no overnight camping at the trailhead itself; the access road has a history of seasonal gate closures and washout-driven closures.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Entiat River Trailhead to Entiat Meadows/Ice Creek area
sectionBreakdown.note: ~14-15 miles of trail through 2015 Wolverine Fire burn terrain with deadfall and river fords

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Meadows camp to summit via south rib
sectionBreakdown.note: Off-trail Class 2-3 scree/talus scramble; early-season parties encounter steep snow and a corniced final ridge
Reverse of ascent line

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 (Chelan County 911 dispatch); Entiat non-emergency line (509) 667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Entiat Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Central Washington Hospital, Wenatchee, WA (roughly 40-45 miles from the Entiat trailhead)
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Entiat River valley or on the peak; the Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue unit is the primary SAR resource for this area.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 8,127 ft is broadly consistent with, but not exactly matched by, sourced figures: topozone/mountainzone list 8,061 ft, two independent trip reports (onehikeaweek.com and countryhighpoints.com) converge on 8,124 ft, and listsofjohn/Peakbagger list 8,129 ft. No single authoritative survey elevation could be confirmed; treat 8,124-8,129 ft as the most likely true range.

blurb: Buckskin Mountain is a summit in the Entiat Mountains subrange of Washington's North Cascades, rising in the Glacier Peak Wilderness at the head of the Entiat River valley in Chelan County. It sits in a remote, seldom-visited cluster of peaks around Entiat Meadows alongside Mount Fernow, Mount Maude, and Seven Fingered Jack, and is reached only by a long trail approach up the Entiat River followed by off-trail scrambling; the Entiat Mountains were largely named by USFS supervisor Albert H. Sylvester in the early 1900s.

objHaz: permanent snowfield and moat near 7,000 ft (Spider Gap approach)
routes.face: South/southwest slopes above the Lyman Lakes basin
routes.overview: The East Route is a cross-country scramble line of open meadow, talus, and scree up North Star Mountain's south/southwest-facing slopes from the Lyman Lakes basin, finishing on Class 3 rock near the summit; it is the peak's standard and easiest way up.
routes.beta: From a camp at Lyman Lakes, leave the trail and hike cross-country across open meadow, then up talus and scree (Class 2) on the south side of the mountain, with the final push to the summit on looser, steeper Class 2-3 rock. The peak is commonly combined with adjacent Cloudy Peak (about 0.9 mi away) from the same basin camp.
routes.approach: Two long wilderness approaches reach Lyman Lakes, the base for this route. Via Phelps Creek: drive the rough Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) about 22 miles from Lake Wenatchee, then Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211) 2.3 miles to the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft); hike the Phelps Creek Trail through Spider Meadow, up Spider Glacier to Spider Gap (7,100 ft, ice axe recommended), then down to Lyman Lakes. Via Holden Village/Lucerne: ride the Lady of the Lake boat up Lake Chelan to Lucerne, bus to Holden Village, then hike the Railroad Creek and Cloudy Pass trails to Lyman Lakes (~16 mi one-way). From Lyman Lakes it is roughly 2 miles cross-country to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to Lyman Lakes basin.
routes.descentText: Descend the same talus/scree slopes used on ascent back to Lyman Lakes. Take extra care on the upper Class 3 rock, which a 2002 trip report describes as slick when lichen is wet; loose talus lower down is easiest to descend by retracing the ascent line rather than seeking a shortcut.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-4 day backpacking trip: hike in and camp at Spider Meadow or Lyman Lakes on day one, day-scramble North Star Mountain (often paired with Cloudy Peak) on day two, and hike out over the remaining day(s).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this non-technical scramble: simply reverse course down the talus/scree to Lyman Lakes basin. No fixed rappel or technical bail option is needed.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on the slowest party member's pace and current weather, and commit to it — afternoon thunderstorms and fast-changing conditions are common in this high, remote wilderness terrain.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the most reliable window, once seasonal snow has cleared the Spider Gap crossing and the Lyman Lakes basin talus slopes. A persistent snowfield and shallow moat can linger near 7,000 ft on the Spider Gap approach into August. Larch color draws hikers into early October, but days are shorter and weather less stable by then.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected anywhere along either approach or on the peak; the route lies deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness far from cell infrastructure.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard scramble. Carry an ice axe (and consider crampons in early season) for the Spider Gap snowfield/glacier crossing if approaching via Phelps Creek, plus a helmet for the loose talus and the Class 3 summit block.
routes.proNeeds: None — this is an unroped scramble. The main terrain concern is the permanent snowfield/moat near 7,000 ft on the Spider Gap approach, which some parties treat with an ice axe for self-arrest.
whatToBring: Bear-resistant food canister (recommended in Glacier Peak Wilderness)
Extra layers for exposed, weather-prone ridge terrain
Water treatment for streams/lakes along the approach
Sun protection for long talus slopes
proTips: Carry a helmet for the talus and scree sections near the summit; trip reports note significant loose rock.
In mid-to-late summer a permanent snowfield with a shallow moat lingers around 7,000 ft on the Spider Gap approach — assess it carefully before committing.
Combine with Cloudy Peak (0.9 mi away) for an efficient two-summit day from a Lyman Lakes camp.
watchOut: The final scramble section is exposed Class 3 rock that becomes slick when lichen-covered rock is wet.
Weather in the Glacier Peak Wilderness can deteriorate quickly at this elevation; a 2002 trip report describes rain forcing an early descent from near the summit.
The Spider Gap crossing involves glacier/permanent-snowfield travel with moat hazard; an ice axe is recommended.
knownHazards: Loose talus and scree on the upper slopes.
A permanent snowfield and moat near 7,000 ft on the Spider Gap approach persists into mid-to-late summer.
Phelps Creek crossings can be difficult at high water, especially early season.
The route is in a remote wilderness setting with no reliable cell coverage.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) / Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: Rough, narrow gravel forest road; may not suit low-clearance vehicles
road.driveNote: About 22 miles on Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) from Lake Wenatchee, then 2.3 miles on Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211) to the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft).

climate.forecastZone: NWS Western Chelan County (WAZ048)
climate.typical: Cool, unsettled mountain weather typical of the eastern North Cascades; snow lingers on north-facing terrain and at Spider Gap into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack still covers the approaches through June; stream crossings and avalanche hazard are elevated.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window, especially late July-September; afternoon thunderstorms are common.
bySeason.fall: Stable weather and larch color into early October, but shorter days and earlier snow are possible.
bySeason.winter: Access roads are gated or snowed in; not a realistic winter objective.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — Wenatchee River Ranger District (Phelps Creek side) and Chelan Ranger District (Lyman Lakes/Railroad Creek side); Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No parking pass or entry fee required at the Phelps Creek Trailhead.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for entry into Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.closures: Holden Village (the Lucerne/Railroad Creek approach) is closed to vehicles and hikers due to storm damage through summer 2026; Stehekin remains open. This closes off the shorter Holden-side approach to Lyman Lakes for the time being.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Phelps Creek TH (3,500 ft) to Lyman Lakes (~5,600 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Spider Meadow, Spider Glacier, and Spider Gap (7,100 ft); ice axe recommended for the snow/glacier crossing.

sectionBreakdown.section: Alternate approach via Holden Village (currently closed)
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Lucerne boat dock to Lyman Lakes
sectionBreakdown.note: ~16 mi via Railroad Creek and Cloudy Pass trails; Holden Village access closed to vehicles/hikers due to storm damage through summer 2026.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Lyman Lakes to North Star Mountain summit
sectionBreakdown.note: ~2 mi one-way cross-country over meadow and talus/scree, Class 2-3 near the top.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 or RiverCom Dispatch (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth (509) 548-2550; Chelan Ranger District, Chelan (509) 682-4900
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA — 24-hour ED, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach (16+ miles one-way depending on route); cell service is unreliable to absent throughout — carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: Given elevation was 8,067 ft; multiple independent sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn, PeakVisor, WTA) consistently list North Star Mountain at 8,096 ft. highPointFt corrected to 8,096 accordingly.

blurb: North Star Mountain is an 8,096-foot summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Washington's North Cascades, in Chelan County near Lyman Lake, with Cloudy Peak its nearest neighbor (0.9 mi southwest) and Bonanza Peak the nearest higher summit (1.9 mi northeast). The peak is built of granodiorite from the Cloudy Pass batholith, an intrusion emplaced roughly 20 million years ago in the early Miocene, and its easiest route is a non-technical talus/scree scramble. It carries a notable naming quirk: the USGS's first 1904 topographic survey of the area accidentally swapped the names North Star Mountain and Bonanza Peak.

routes.overview: A long, remote southwest-facing scramble up Class 2-3 terrain to the summit of Mount Berge, a double-summited granite peak deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness on Chiwawa Ridge.
routes.beta: From the basin on Berge's southwest side, work up through meadows and heather benches toward the upper mountain, then pick a line of Class 2-3 rock and steep heather/talus up the southwest-facing slopes to the summit ridge. Berge's north and west faces are cliffy, so parties must stay on the southwest side rather than trying to go straight up from High Pass; guidebook description notes a descending traverse near 5,400 ft into meadows from which several scrambling lines reach the top, and near a snowfield around 7,000 ft climbers use a snow finger/moat and steep heather to reach a saddle around 7,650 ft below the final summit block.
routes.approach: From the Trinity Trailhead (2,800 ft) follow Buck Creek Trail #1513 up Buck Creek for 9.6 miles and about 3,100 ft of gain to Buck Creek Pass (~5,900 ft). Continue on trail roughly 3 more miles and about 1,400 ft of gain to High Pass, then leave the trail and descend/traverse south into the basin on Berge's southwest side to reach the base of the scramble.
routes.descent: Descend the same southwest slopes back to the basin, then retrace the approach over High Pass and Buck Creek Pass to the Trinity Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ascent line down the southwest-facing rock, heather, and talus to the basin below, taking care on any lingering snow in the moat/gully near the saddle. From the basin, climb back up to High Pass, then descend the trail over Buck Creek Pass and down Buck Creek Trail #1513 to the Trinity Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a 2-3 day backpack: Day 1 hike in via Buck Creek Trail (~9.6 mi, +3,100 ft) to Buck Creek Pass, then continue toward High Pass (~3 mi, +1,400 ft) and camp in the basin on Berge's southwest side; Day 2 scramble the Southwest Route to the summit and return to camp; Day 3 hike out to the Trinity Trailhead.
routes.bail: The southwest slopes have no fixed rappel stations; retreat by reversing the ascent line back into the basin. Below treeline, Buck Creek Trail provides a straightforward bail to the Trinity Trailhead from any point.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and climb combine for a very long day (or multi-day trip) with long return mileage and exposure to afternoon mountain thunderstorms, set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit scramble and be willing to retreat if weather deteriorates or the trip is running behind schedule.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, after the approach snow finger/gully on the southwest side has melted out and the Chiwawa River fords have dropped to a safer level; earlier-season (June) attempts should expect lingering snow/ice on the approach slopes and avalanche-prone terrain on Buck Creek Trail's upper switchbacks.
objHaz: loose rock on upper scramble terrain
steep snow/ice in the approach gully and moat below the summit saddle (early season)
cliffy north and west faces — do not attempt a direct line from High Pass
avalanche-prone slopes on the approach in spring/early summer
cold, high-water crossings of the Chiwawa River during snowmelt
routes.detailedRack: Class 2-3 scrambling terrain; most parties carry no technical rock rack. An ice axe (and possibly crampons) is recommended in early season for the snow finger/gully below the roughly 7,650 ft saddle.
whatToBring: backpacking gear for a 2-3 day approach
map of the Glacier Peak Wilderness / Buck Creek Pass-High Pass area
proTips: Time the trip for mid-to-late summer so the Chiwawa River fords and the approach snow finger are easier and safer.
Camp in the basin near High Pass or on Berge's southwest side to shorten summit day.
Approach the summit only from the southwest — Berge's north and west sides are cliffed out and are not a viable line.
watchOut: Berge's north and west faces are cliffed out; don't try to shortcut directly from High Pass.
The final gully/moat below the summit saddle can hold snow and ice well into summer.
The Chiwawa River crossing on the approach can run waist-deep and hazardous during peak snowmelt.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the upper scramble terrain.
Avalanche-prone slopes on the approach in spring/early summer.
High, cold river crossings of the Chiwawa River during snowmelt season.
Blowdown has been reported on sections of Buck Creek Trail in some years.
Very remote location with long evacuation times if injured.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road / Forest Road 6200 (via Chiwawa Loop Road from WA-207)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 11 miles from Coles Corner/WA-207, then rough native-surface gravel and dirt for the final ~12 miles to Trinity Trailhead
road.driveNote: High-clearance vehicle recommended for the final 12 miles (sharp rocks, deep ruts); allow extra time — about 23 miles/1-1.5 hours from Coles Corner near Leavenworth.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Central Chelan County zone (WAZ047)
climate.typical: Dry, sunny east-Cascades summers with afternoon thunderstorm potential; the high basins near Buck Creek Pass/High Pass hold snow into July even in an average year.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack lingers in the high basins and approach gullies into June; avalanche terrain on Buck Creek Trail's upper switchbacks and on Berge's approach slopes.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul-Sep) once the approach snow finger/gully melts out and the Chiwawa River fords drop to a safer level.
bySeason.fall: Often good, stable weather into September/early October, with shortening days and the possibility of early snow at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Road access beyond the lower valley is typically snowed in; a remote, largely inaccessible winter approach.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District), Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30/year day-use fee at Trinity Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency/America the Beautiful Pass, or Digital Day Pass also accepted)
access.permit: Free, self-issued wilderness permit required at the Trinity Trailhead for entry into Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency (America the Beautiful) Pass / Digital Day Pass
access.closures: No route-specific closure found at time of research; standing forestwide fire restrictions can apply above 4,000 ft (stoves only) in dry years — check current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts and road conditions before going, since the final ~12 miles of Chiwawa River Road are rough, high-clearance-only gravel.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trinity Trailhead (2,800 ft) to Buck Creek Pass (~5,900 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: 9.6 mi, ~3,100 ft gain on Buck Creek Trail #1513

sectionBreakdown.section: Buck Creek Pass to High Pass
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Buck Creek Pass to High Pass
sectionBreakdown.note: ~3 mi, ~1,400 ft gain

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: High Pass to Mount Berge summit (7,948 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Off-trail: descend into the basin on Berge's southwest side, then Class 2-3 scramble up southwest-facing slopes to the summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 (multi-jurisdictional dispatch for Chelan & Douglas counties), via Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District (Leavenworth), (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (24-hour Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Very remote approach (roughly 9.6-13+ trail miles from the trailhead to the base of the climb); expect a long ground evacuation and likely need for helicopter extraction if injured deep in the Buck Creek Pass/High Pass basin.

routes.corrections: Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and ListsOfJohn list Mount Berge's commonly cited (south) summit at 7,951 ft. A private surveyor-level measurement (countryhighpoints.com) argues the mountain's north summit — an unofficial survey point of 7,948 ft, matching the elevation given in this dataset — is actually about 20-34 ft taller and is the true high point, though it requires a harder 4th-class finish versus the south summit's 3rd class. No first-ascent record for the peak or this route was found in available sources, so 'fa' is left null.

blurb: Mount Berge is a striking double-summited granodiorite peak on Chiwawa Ridge in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Washington's North Cascades, in Chelan County near Buck Creek Pass and Napeequa Peak. It was named for climber Richard "Dick" Berge, who died at age 23 while climbing Baring Mountain with Fred Beckey in 1952. The peak's north and west faces are steep and cliffy, so its easiest line is a Class 2-3 scramble reached from the southwest side.

waypoints.name: Key col (saddle toward Dark Peak/Bonanza massif)

routes.overview: The South Route is the presumed line up Mount Lyall's south side above the Lyman Lake/Cloudy Pass basin. No published trip report, guidebook entry, or completed-ascent account was located for this peak; Peakbagger and ListsOfJohn both list zero logged ascents of Mount Lyall.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan Ranger District — Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No wilderness entry fee; a Northwest Forest Pass may be required at some connecting trailheads on the Okanogan-Wenatchee NF
access.permit: Free, self-issued wilderness permit at the trailhead (required); maximum group size 12 people/stock combined
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass may apply at some trailheads
access.closures: The normal west-side approach to Holden Village via Forest Service Road 8301 has been closed since a December 2025 storm caused flooding/landslides at 11+ sites on the road; Holden Village is closed to guests through at least summer 2026, and the USFS closure order runs through 12/31/2027 absent repairs. An alternate approach exists via the Stehekin/Company Creek or Agnes Creek-PCT corridor on the east side, reached by boat/shuttle from Chelan to Stehekin, which remained open as of the closure reporting.

road.name: Forest Service Road 8301 (Lucerne–Holden Village road)
road.status: Closed (landslide/flood damage at multiple sites, since Dec 2025)
road.driveNote: Not reachable by private vehicle in any season — normal access is the Lady of the Lake ferry from Chelan or Field's Point to Lucerne Landing, then an ~11-mile bus/shuttle on FR 8301 to Holden Village. That shuttle access has been suspended since a Dec 2025 storm damaged the road at 11+ locations (est. $20M in repairs); Holden Village remains closed to guests through at least summer 2026 with a USFS closure order extending to 12/31/2027 absent earlier repair. The Stehekin ferry/shuttle on the east side of the range was unaffected and offers an alternate approach via Company Creek or Agnes Creek.

bySeason.summer: The adjoining Lyman Lake basin, about 8 trail miles south of Holden and immediately south of Mount Lyall, is typically snow-free only from roughly July through September; snow can persist above ~5,600-5,800 ft into early July in an average year.

routes.bestSeason: No route-specific season data exists for Mount Lyall, but the adjacent Lyman Lake/Cloudy Pass basin it overlooks is best hiked June through September and is typically not fully snow-free until July, per USFS/WTA trail data for that basin.
routes.comms: No cell service should be expected in this section of the Glacier Peak Wilderness; the area is reached only by boat/shuttle via Lake Chelan.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff's Office — dispatch (509) 667-6851, non-emergency (509) 667-6860; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, 428 W. Woodin Ave, Chelan, WA 98816, (509) 682-4900
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health Hospital (24-hr ER), 110 S Apple Blossom Dr, Chelan, WA 98816, (509) 682-3300
emergency.notes: Extremely remote wilderness approach with boat/shuttle-only access via Lake Chelan; expect no cell coverage and long search-and-rescue response times.

routes.corrections: The peak elevation given (7,911') is essentially consistent with the current LiDAR-surveyed figure of 7,912' (ListsOfJohn); older USGS topo maps and Peakbagger's map-derived figure list 7,892'. No other conflicts found; the route's technical grade (Class 2-3 / alpine F) could not be independently confirmed or refuted — no trip report or guidebook description of an ascent was located.

blurb: Mount Lyall (7,912' per modern LiDAR survey, 7,892' on older USGS topo maps) is a remote summit of the Bonanza Massif in the Central North Cascades, rising within the Glacier Peak Wilderness on the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest in Chelan County, Washington. It sits near Bonanza Peak (its line parent) and Dark Peak (its nearest higher neighbor, to the SSW), overlooking the Lyman Lake/Cloudy Pass basin south of Holden Village. It is very rarely climbed — both Peakbagger and ListsOfJohn list zero logged ascents — and no guidebook or trip-report description of a completed climb was found in available sources.

routes.overview: A long wilderness approach to Cloudy Pass leads to an easy scramble up Cloudy Peak's southwest slopes and ridge, finishing with a short, exposed class 3-4 move sequence to gain the true summit block.
routes.beta: From Cloudy Pass, leave the trail and head roughly northeast/north following intermittent social trails and open slopes to gain the southwest ridge, then follow the ridge/slopes up to the base of the summit block. To finish, walk around the block's east side and continue to its north (back) side, where a short series of class 3-4 moves (minimal exposure, sometimes roped by cautious parties) gains the true summit.
routes.approach: Reach Cloudy Pass from either the Trinity Trailhead (via Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200 and the Buck Creek Trail over Buck Creek Pass) or the Phelps Creek Trailhead (via the Phelps Creek Trail, Spider Meadow, and either Spider Gap or the Lyman Lakes trail past Hart Lake). Both are multi-day backpacking approaches through the Glacier Peak Wilderness; from Lower Lyman Lake it is roughly 1.8 miles of open meadow to Cloudy Pass, from which the peak is gained cross-country.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back down the southwest slopes to Cloudy Pass.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the class 3-4 summit block moves the way you came, then descend the southwest ridge/slopes back toward Cloudy Pass, picking up the social trail/open slopes used on the ascent. From Cloudy Pass, retrace either the Buck Creek Pass (Trinity Trailhead) or Spider Gap/Lyman Lakes (Phelps Creek Trailhead) trail back to the car.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at almost any point on the southwest slopes/ridge by simply reversing back to Cloudy Pass and the approach trail; the only committing section is the short class 3-4 summit block, which can be downclimbed the way it was ascended.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach is long and the summit block scramble is exposed, set a firm turnaround time before starting the final push so you are off the block and back on lower-angle terrain well before afternoon thunderstorms or fatigue become a factor.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-summer into early fall (roughly July through September) once seasonal snow has melted off the southwest slopes and approach trails; early-season attempts can encounter lingering snow on the pass approaches and a snow-covered, more consequential summit block.
routes.comms: Remote wilderness terrain with unreliable cell coverage; do not count on a phone signal at Cloudy Pass or on the peak itself.
objHaz: loose rock on the summit block
exposure on class 3-4 summit block moves
lingering early-season snow on approach slopes
knownHazards: The summit block requires several class 3-4 moves; while exposure is generally described as minimal, some parties choose to rope up for this section.
Approach trails cross open talus/scree and meadow slopes where early-season snow can obscure the route and add fall-line hazard.
Long, remote approach (multi-day backpack) means self-rescue and route-finding skills matter more than the technical difficulty of the peak itself.
watchOut: Don't mistake the class 3-4 summit block for harder terrain than it is, but don't underestimate the exposure either — several trip reports note it's the only real technical crux on an otherwise walk-up peak.
Social trails above Cloudy Pass are informal and can be hard to follow; use general ridge/slope terrain judgment rather than relying on a defined tread.
Afternoon thunderstorms are a recognized summer hazard in the North Cascades high country — plan an early start and early summit.
proTips: Many parties combine Cloudy Peak with the classic Spider Gap-Buck Creek Pass loop, tagging the summit as a side trip from a base camp at Cloudy Pass, Hart Lake, or Lower Lyman Lake.
The Trinity and Phelps Creek trailheads are only about 2.5 miles apart by road/trail, so a car shuttle or loop combining both is a popular option for the broader area.
A free Glacier Peak Wilderness permit (self-issued at the trailhead) and a parking pass (Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency, or Digital Day Pass) are required at both trailheads.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is used on the standard route; the class 3-4 summit block is typically soloed, though a party uneasy with the exposure could belay it with a short length of rope and a sling/cordelette over a horn if one is available.
whatToBring: backpacking/camping gear for the multi-day approach (tent, sleeping bag, stove)
Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Pass for trailhead parking
layers for exposed alpine terrain and afternoon weather
ice axe/microspikes if attempting in early season with residual snow

road.name: Chiwawa River Road / Forest Road 6200 (Trinity Trailhead); Phelps Creek Road / FR 6211 (Phelps Creek Trailhead)
road.status: Rough gravel/dirt for the final ~12 miles to Trinity; suitable for higher-clearance vehicles. As of June 2026 there was an active Forest Service alert for storm-damaged-road closures on the Wenatchee River Ranger District affecting Chiwawa River Road beyond Atkinson Campground — check current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts before driving in.
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, take US-2 west 15 miles to Coles Corner, then WA-207 north 4.4 miles, right on Chiwawa Loop Road, then left onto Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200 and continue roughly 23 miles to Trinity Trailhead (2,800 ft); Phelps Creek Trailhead is reached via FR 6200 for about 22 miles then FR 6211 for 2.3 miles.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Central Cascades / Glacier Peak Wilderness
climate.typical: Continental-influenced alpine climate with warm, relatively dry summers and cold, snowy winters; the high country typically holds snow well into June or later.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack on approach trails and slopes into June; not recommended for the summit block due to snow/ice.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (July-September) with the most reliable snow-free access to the southwest slopes and summit block, though afternoon thunderstorms are common.
bySeason.fall: Stable weather windows in September can be excellent, but days shorten and early snow is possible by October.
bySeason.winter: Approach roads and trails are typically snowed in and impractical; not a winter objective.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District), Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: $5/day or $30/year day-use fee at trailheads (Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Pass, or Digital Day Pass accepted)
access.permit: Free, self-issued Glacier Peak Wilderness permit required at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass / Digital Day Pass
access.closures: Check current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts; the Chiwawa River Road corridor has had storm-damage closures in recent seasons.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start from camp (Cloudy Pass, Hart Lake, or Lower Lyman Lake) to allow time for the cross-country ridge scramble and summit block before afternoon weather develops.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 dispatch: (509) 663-9911; Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (509) 667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (closest); Central Washington Hospital / Confluence Health, Wenatchee, WA (regional trauma center)
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area is coordinated by the Chelan County Sheriff's Office, often with volunteer Chelan County Mountain Rescue; cell coverage is unreliable in the Glacier Peak Wilderness backcountry.

routes.corrections: Given grade of 'Class 2' likely undersells the route: Wikipedia lists the peak elevation as 7,915 ft (vs. 7,912 ft given), and multiple independent trip reports/guides (peakery-aggregated source, Lemke Climbs) consistently describe the final summit block as requiring several class 3-4 moves, so the route difficulty should be understood as Class 3-4 at the crux rather than a straightforward Class 2 walk-up. The peak's 1921 first ascent by The Mountaineers is documented for the peak generally; it is not confirmed which specific line they climbed, so no route-level 'fa' is asserted.

blurb: Cloudy Peak (7,915 ft) rises in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Chelan County, just north of Cloudy Pass, within the North Cascades' Cloudy Pass batholith. Its granitic rock intruded roughly 20 million years ago during the Miocene, and the peak is popular as a moderate, view-rich scramble reached via long backpacking approaches from the Trinity or Phelps Creek trailheads. The Mountaineers climbing club recorded the first ascent of the peak in 1921 during their annual summer outing to the area.

waypoints.name: High Bridge (Stehekin Valley Road / PCT access)
Swamp Creek Camp (base camp for Needle Peak)

sources: https://carto.nationalmap.gov/arcgis/rest/services/geonames/MapServer/5/query?where=gaz_name=%27Needle+Peak%27+AND+state_alpha=%27WA%27 (USGS GNIS official summit point, gaz_id 1523602, Chelan County WA)
routes.overview: The only documented line up Needle Peak follows the peak's long north ridge, gained via a steep brushy slope out of the Swamp Creek drainage, and finishes with an exposed, narrowing Class 3 gully through loose stacked blocks to the summit.
routes.beta: Needle Peak sees very few ascents. The standard (and only reported) line leaves the unmaintained Swamp Creek Trail around 3,200 ft, climbs a steep brushy slope to gain the peak's long north ridge near 7,300–7,400 ft, then route-finds through west-facing gullies and krummholz along the ridge before a narrow, choss-filled Class 3 gully leads through stacked boulders to the summit. The line is referenced in Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide and described in a 2023 trip report.
routes.approach: From High Bridge (the shuttle turnaround/trailhead where the Stehekin Valley Road meets the PCT, ~11 miles up-valley from Stehekin), follow the Agnes Creek Trail (PCT) south past Five Mile Camp (~2,200 ft) to Swamp Creek Camp (~2,800 ft), about 8 miles in. Just past the creek crossing, pick up the signed but unmaintained Swamp Creek Trail, which switchbacks roughly 800 ft over 1–1.5 miles before fading into alder brush; continue up-valley, pushing through brush, to around 3,200 ft below Needle Peak's north ridge, then climb the steep brushy slope to gain the ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the summit gully and ridge traverse back to the top of the brushy slope, then descend to Swamp Creek Camp.
routes.descentText: There is no reported technical descent or rappel route — climbers downclimb the Class 3 summit gully, retrace the north-ridge traverse through the west gullies/krummholz, then descend the steep brushy slope back to roughly 3,200 ft to rejoin the abandoned Swamp Creek Trail and return to Swamp Creek Camp on the Agnes Creek Trail (PCT).
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the ascent line — downclimb the Class 3 gully and ridge back to the brushy descent slope and down to the Swamp Creek drainage; no fixed anchors or rappel stations are reported on this route.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, unmaintained approach and loose, exposed summit gully, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to reverse the brushy descent slope and regain the Swamp Creek Trail and Agnes Creek Trail before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best attempted in later summer into early fall (roughly August–September) once the Swamp Creek brush and approach slopes are snow-free; the one documented modern ascent was completed in early September.
whatToBring: sturdy gloves and long sleeves for brush/krummholz
trekking poles for the brushy approach
map/compass/GPS — trail becomes unmaintained and faint
proTips: The Swamp Creek Trail is unmaintained and fades quickly above its first ~800 ft of switchbacks — budget extra time for brush-bashing and route-finding.
The crux is finding a workable line through the west-facing gullies and krummholz along the 7,300–7,400 ft north-ridge traverse; scout options before committing to a gully.
watchOut: Loose, stacked choss in the final ~200 ft below the summit — test holds carefully.
Wasp nests have been reported along the brushy approach in late summer.
This is a long, remote, trail-less objective — an injury here means a very long evacuation.
knownHazards: Loose/chossy rock near the summit block
Dense brush and krummholz on the approach slopes and ridge traverse
Route-finding difficulty on the unmaintained Swamp Creek Trail and upper ridge
Exposure on the Class 3 summit gully

pitchDetail.notes: Agnes Creek Trail (PCT) from High Bridge to Swamp Creek Camp (~2,800 ft)
Unmaintained trail switchbacks ~800 ft over 1-1.5 mi before fading into alder brush
Steep brush from ~3,200 ft up to Needle Peak's long north ridge near 7,300-7,400 ft
Route-find through west-facing gullies and krummholz along the ridge crest
Narrow gully through loose, stacked boulders/choss for the final ~200 ft to the summit

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road
road.status: Open only to High Bridge (~11 mi from Stehekin); washed out/impassable beyond toward Cottonwood Camp
road.driveNote: Stehekin has no outside road access — reached only by passenger ferry or float plane up Lake Chelan. From Stehekin, a seasonal shuttle bus runs the Stehekin Valley Road to High Bridge, the trailhead for the Agnes Creek Trail/PCT used to approach Needle Peak.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Lake Chelan National Recreation Area) near the boundary with Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest's Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: NPS backcountry permit: $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for overnight stays
access.permit: A wilderness backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay in the North Cascades NP Complex/Lake Chelan NRA; obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or by advance reservation
access.closures: Stehekin Valley Road washed out beyond High Bridge toward Cottonwood Camp; no vehicle access into the park from the Stehekin side past High Bridge

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or Chelan County Sheriff / RiverCom non-emergency dispatch (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health (Lake Chelan Community Hospital), 110 S Apple Blossom Dr, Chelan, WA
emergency.notes: Extremely remote objective — the only outside access to the trailhead is by ferry/float plane to Stehekin, then ~8+ trail miles. Cell coverage is unreliable to absent in the Agnes Creek/Swamp Creek drainage.

routes.corrections: This appears to be the only documented climbing line on Needle Peak (per Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide and available trip reports); it ascends via the peak's north ridge from the Swamp Creek drainage, not a south-side line — no separate 'South Route' description was found in available sources, so the aspect/name given may not match the documented route. Peak elevation is given elsewhere as 7,897 ft by Peakbagger/ListsOfJohn (vs. 7,896 ft in the input) and as low as ~7,795 ft in one older USGS-derived source (TopoZone); treated here as normal cross-source survey variance rather than a hard correction.

blurb: Needle Peak is a slender 7,896–7,897-ft spire in the North Cascades, rising directly west of Mount Lyall above the Swamp Creek and Agnes Creek drainages south of Stehekin, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness / Lake Chelan National Recreation Area. It is linked to Dark Peak on the south by the jagged, largely unclimbed Anonymity Towers ridge. The peak is very rarely climbed given its long, unmaintained approach and loose, brushy terrain typical of this part of the range.

waypoints.name: Massie Peak (listed as "Massie Ridge" by ListsOfJohn)

objHaz: scarce water above the river crossing
routes.overview: A remote, off-trail scramble to a seldom-visited 7,534' summit in the Chiwawa River drainage, reached via Massie Lake and cross-country ridge travel rather than any maintained trail.
routes.beta: From the Trinity/Buck Creek Trailhead, follow the Chiwawa River Trail north about 3 miles to the unsigned, unmaintained 'Massie Lake' way-trail (an old sheepherder driveway marked by axe/burn blazes on trees) and climb steeply to Massie Lake. From the lake, gain the ridge above and continue cross-country toward the summit, bypassing a false summit before reaching the true top. The line is difficult to follow, brushy, and has claimed parties onto unwanted 4th-class terrain when off-route; a Aug-2024 trip report logged 14.75 miles round trip, about 5,400' of gain, and 7 hours car-to-car via a non-standard east-ridge/south-ridge variant, noting the descent line is easy to lose.
routes.approach: Drive Chiwawa River Road (Forest Road 6200) to Trinity/Buck Creek Trailhead (2,800'). Hike the Chiwawa River Trail #1550 north roughly 3 miles, watching on the left for the unsigned Massie Lake route. Cross the Chiwawa River (ford it directly rather than using a nearby log jam, which lands you on the wrong side of the old trail) and climb the steep, unmaintained sheep-drive path — marked by burn blazes for about 90% of its length — to Massie Lake.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent line back to the ridge above Massie Lake, down to the lake, and back to the Chiwawa River crossing and trail junction to return to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descend from the summit ridge back toward the notch/ridge above Massie Lake, then down the steep, brushy slope to the lake. From the lake, most parties retrace their ascent route to the Chiwawa River crossing rather than search for a distinct 'trail' down, since the old sheep-drive path is very hard to relocate from above; expect loose, vegetated ('veggie belay') downclimbing in spots. Cross the Chiwawa River and follow the Chiwawa River Trail back to Trinity Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties should plan for a long single day or, given the difficulty of the approach, an overnight camp at Massie Lake with a day trip to the summit and back.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ascent line back toward Massie Lake and down to the Chiwawa River crossing, then out the Chiwawa River Trail to Trinity Trailhead. No technical rope work is typically required to bail, but parties who wander off the standard line have encountered short 4th-class cliff bands that require careful, deliberate downclimbing.
routes.turnaround: Given the difficult, unmaintained approach and real risk of getting cliffed-out or losing the route in poor visibility or failing light, set a firm turnaround time based on your pace back to Massie Lake and the trailhead, and turn back if you have not located the lake or the ridge above it well before your planned midpoint.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in mid-summer to early fall (roughly July through September), after brush and the Chiwawa River crossing have eased; snow can linger into early summer on the upper slopes, and the long access road (FR 6200) is only usable once it is clear of winter damage/snow.
gear: map, compass, and/or GPS (route-finding is difficult)
whatToBring: extra water (little to none above the Chiwawa River crossing)
gaiters or brush pants for bushwhacking
overnight gear if camping at Massie Lake
satellite communicator/PLB (remote area, limited cell coverage)
proTips: Ford the Chiwawa River directly rather than using a nearby log jam — the log jam lands you on the wrong side to pick up the old sheep-drive trail.
Look for hatchet/burn blazes on trees to stay on the unsigned Massie Lake route; they mark roughly 90% of the trail.
Fill water bottles at the Chiwawa River crossing — there is little to no reliable water from there to the summit.
watchOut: The route above Massie Lake is unmaintained and easy to lose; parties who strayed off-route have ended up on unwanted 4th-class cliff bands.
A false summit sits below the true top on the summit ridge.
The area is prone to fast-moving, unexpected rain.
knownHazards: Steep, brushy, unmaintained terrain with significant route-finding difficulty from the Chiwawa River crossing to Massie Lake and beyond.
Exposed 4th-class rock and loose, vegetated ('veggie belay') terrain reported by parties who strayed off the standard line.
A cold, potentially high-volume crossing of the Chiwawa River, especially early season.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (Forest Road 6200)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 11 miles, then rough gravel/dirt for the final ~12 miles to Trinity Trailhead; low-clearance vehicles are not recommended
road.driveNote: From Coles Corner on US-2, take WA-207 north 4.4 miles, right on Chiwawa Loop Road, then left onto Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200 for about 23 miles to Trinity Trailhead.

climate.typical: Dry, warm summer days are typical, but the area is prone to fast-moving, unexpected rain showers; winter brings heavy snow that closes the access road.

bySeason.spring: Road access is limited by lingering snow into early summer, and the Chiwawa River crossing runs high and cold with snowmelt.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (roughly Jul-Sep); hot, dry days but sudden thunderstorms/rain are common.
bySeason.fall: Cooler temperatures and turning larches on the upper slopes in late September/October, but early snow is possible.
bySeason.winter: Forest Road 6200 and the trail are snowed in and effectively inaccessible.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District — Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No standing use fee beyond the trailhead parking pass
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at Trinity Trailhead for entry into Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/Interagency Pass required to park at Trinity Trailhead
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) has experienced storm-damage closures — check current Forest Service alerts before driving in; the final ~12 miles are rough dirt/gravel.

timing.recommendedStart: Early (pre-dawn/first light) given the long approach and difficult route-finding

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (509) 667-6851, 401 Washington St, Wenatchee, WA 98801; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-5815 (Level 5 trauma ER)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness with little to no cell coverage on most of the approach; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. The access road is long and rough, adding significant time to any evacuation.

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 7,533' does not match independent sources: Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn, Peakery, and an Aug-2024 NWHikers trip report all give 7,534'. ListsOfJohn separately catalogs this same summit (48.1363°N, 120.9123°W, Chelan County, Suiattle Pass quad, Glacier Peak Wilderness) under the name 'Massie Ridge' rather than 'Massie Peak.' The 'West Route' name/line in the given data could not be independently verified against any source found — the only detailed trip report located (NWHikers, Aug 2024) ascended from Massie Lake onto the peak's east ridge, then traversed south beneath cliffs to the south ridge and true summit past a false summit; a commenter on that report indicates the standard/easier line from the lake avoids the 4th-class cliff bands that reporter encountered off-route.

blurb: Massie Peak (7,534') is a remote, rarely climbed summit in the Entiat Mountains subrange of the Glacier Peak Wilderness, rising above Massie Lake in the upper Chiwawa River drainage of Washington's Cascade Range. There is no maintained trail to the summit — the standard approach follows an old, unsigned sheepherder's driveway trail to Massie Lake before climbers pick a cross-country line up steep, brushy slopes and a ridge crest to the top. The peak sees very few recorded ascents; a 2024 party found a summit register placed by a well-known Cascades peakbagger, underscoring how seldom it is visited.

objHaz: Rough, potholed final 2.3 miles of access road
Open talus-slope crossing partway up the switchbacks
Lingering snow on upper switchbacks/basin into early-mid July
Exposed, unshaded ridge/summit walk (lightning and wind exposure)
No reliable water above the ~6,100 ft basin
routes.season: Jul-Oct (best for larches late Sep-mid Oct)
routes.overview: A steep, switchbacking climber's trail that gains the open larch-covered ridge of Carne Mountain from Phelps Creek, finishing along a gentle ridgewalk to the summit with 360-degree North Cascades views.
routes.beta: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead, follow the wide Phelps Creek Trail for about a quarter mile, then bear right and steeply uphill onto the narrower, unmaintained-feel Carne Mountain Trail (#1508). It climbs via moderate-to-steep switchbacks through forest and across an open talus slope for roughly 2.5-2.9 miles to a small alpine basin (~6,100 ft) with campsites and a stream. From there the trail continues to a low pass/junction with the Old Gib Trail around mile 3, then climbs to a saddle (~6,800 ft) with views of Mount Maude, Clark Mountain, and Glacier Peak, before a final quarter mile along the ridge to the 7,085 ft summit.
routes.approach: From Coles Corner on US-2, take WA-207 toward Lake Wenatchee, then Chiwawa Loop Road, County Highway 22, and Chiwawa River Road (Forest Road 62/6200) north for about 22.3 miles (pavement ends around mile 11), then turn right onto Phelps Creek Trailhead Road (Road 6211) for the final 2.3 rough, potholed miles to the Phelps Creek Trailhead (~3,500-3,540 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the same trail back to the Phelps Creek Trailhead.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route: from the summit, follow the ridge back to the ~6,800 ft saddle, drop past the Old Gib Trail junction and through the ~6,100 ft basin, then descend the switchbacks and talus crossing back to the Phelps Creek Trail junction and out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Standard single-day round trip (~7.3-7.4 miles RT, ~3,600 ft gain); no permit reservations needed for a day trip.
routes.bail: The trail can be abandoned at any point along the switchbacks or at the ~6,100 ft basin and descended the way you came; there is no faster alternate exit.
routes.turnaround: Because the upper trail is fully exposed along an open ridge with no tree cover, set a firm turnaround time before starting and retreat promptly if thunderstorms or worsening weather develop.
routes.bestSeason: Snow can linger on the upper switchbacks and basin into early-to-mid July; wildflowers peak in July; the trail is best known as a premier Washington larch hike, with the western larches turning gold from late September into mid-October.
whatToBring: Layers for wind/weather on the open summit ridge
Microspikes/traction if hiking before mid-July snow melts out
Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass for trailhead parking
proTips: Most Phelps Creek Trail traffic continues to Spider Meadows, so the Carne Mountain cutoff is comparatively quiet.
Fall is the signature season here for the golden western larch display along the ridge.
watchOut: The Phelps Creek Trailhead access road (Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200) has had closures beyond Atkinson Campground due to storm damage — check current Forest Service road status before driving in.
The trailhead parking lot fills quickly on summer and fall weekends.
knownHazards: An open talus slope must be crossed partway up the switchbacks.
Snow can persist on the upper trail and basin well into early summer, requiring traction and route-finding skill.
The final ridge to the summit is fully exposed to wind and lightning.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200/62) to Phelps Creek Trailhead Road (FR 6211)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 11 miles then gravel; FR 6200 has had closures beyond Atkinson Campground due to storm damage — verify current status before driving
road.driveNote: The final 2.3 miles on Road 6211 to the trailhead are significantly rough and potholed; high-clearance vehicles are more comfortable though not strictly required in dry conditions.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Central Cascades and East Slopes North Cascades (NWS/NWAC forecast zones)
climate.typical: Dry, warm summers with afternoon thunderstorm potential on the exposed upper ridge; snow can linger into July at elevation

bySeason.spring: Trail largely snow-covered into June; not a recommended season
bySeason.summer: Prime hiking window from July once snow melts out of the basin and switchbacks; wildflowers peak in July
bySeason.fall: Best-known season for the trail's western larch display, late September through mid-October
bySeason.winter: Road access typically snowed in/gated; not a maintained winter route

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (sources differ on Wenatchee River Ranger District vs. Entiat Ranger District administration; USFS's own trail page lists Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead required for entry into Glacier Peak Wilderness; no advance reservation needed for day use
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) has been closed beyond Atkinson Campground to vehicles due to storm damage — check Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts before the drive

sectionBreakdown.section: Phelps Creek Trailhead to Carne Mountain Trail junction
sectionBreakdown.note: Wide, gentle trail before bearing right onto the steep Carne Mountain Trail

sectionBreakdown.section: Switchbacks and talus crossing to basin
sectionBreakdown.note: Sustained moderate-to-steep climbing through forest and an open talus slope
Past the Old Gib Trail junction, up to the ~6,800 ft saddle, then a final quarter mile along the ridge to the 7,085 ft summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 Dispatch (all Chelan County police/fire/EMS): 509-663-9911; Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 509-667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-5815 (24-hour ED)
emergency.notes: Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue (CCVSAR) supports backcountry rescues in this area; cell coverage in the Chiwawa/Phelps Creek drainage is not documented by any source checked and should be assumed unreliable.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 7,091 ft does not match sourced figures: Wikipedia lists 7,080 ft, WTA lists the true (higher) summit at 7,085 ft, and other aggregators show a range from ~7,079-7,100 ft; this route uses the WTA-sourced 7,085 ft high point. Land-manager ranger district is listed inconsistently across official USFS pages (Wenatchee River vs. Entiat Ranger District) — noted rather than resolved. Elevation gain is cited as 3,600 ft by WTA/AllTrails-type sources but only 3,000 ft on the official USFS trail page; 3,600 ft was used as the more commonly corroborated figure. Round-trip distance is cited as 7.3-7.4 miles (WTA/USFS, 3.7 mi one way) versus 8 miles by another source; the 3.7 mi one-way (USFS trail #1508) figure was used for distKm.

blurb: Carne Mountain (7,080–7,085 ft depending on source) is a double-summit peak in the Entiat Mountains, a sub-range of the North Cascades in Chelan County, on the edge of the Glacier Peak Wilderness. It has no technical climbing routes — its popularity rests on a steep switchbacking trail to an open, larch-covered ridge with panoramic views of Glacier Peak, Mount Maude, Fortress Mountain, and Seven-Fingered Jack. A fire lookout once stood on its south summit, and the mountain was named by USFS surveyor Albert H. Sylvester for English clergyman W. Stanley Carnes.

routes.fa: 27 August 1930 by Forest Farr, Art Winder and Laurence Byington (peak/route first ascent, via the south and east faces to the main summit)
routes.rappels: About 13 rappels (double-rope) down the face; parties often tandem the descent with other groups on the peak to save time
objHaz: glacier travel / crevasses on the Chimney & Overcoat Glaciers
loose rock and rockfall in the chimney pitches
moat/bergschrund crossing at the base of the climb, worst early-mid season
wet, slippery rock even in summer given the peak's shaded, glaciated aspect
routes.season: Jun-Sep, with July-early Sept most reliable once the moat has opened enough to cross
routes.overview: The standard technical line to Chimney Rock's main summit climbs the East Face Direct above the Chimney/Lemah Glaciers: roughly nine pitches of low-fifth-class corners, blocks, and chimneys (crux 5.6 in a right-facing dihedral) leading to a final chimney onto the summit.
