routes.beta: From a high camp near the Chimney/Lemah Glacier, cross the glacier to the base of the face and negotiate the moat (often via a snow bridge), then climb a mix of low-5th-class corner/block pitches and two chimney sections (one with an awkward overhang, one unprotectable class-4 slab) to the true summit; the whole line sees roughly one ascent party per year, so expect route-finding and no fixed gear beyond occasional rap anchors.
routes.approach: From the Pete Lake Trailhead (2,800 ft) on FS Road 4616 off Salmon la Sac Rd/FS-46, hike the Pete Lake Trail past Pete Lake and onto the Lemah Meadow Trail to the PCT junction; head north on the PCT about half a mile to the second switchback, then leave the trail heading northwest through open forest, cross the North Fork Lemah Creek on a log, and follow a cairned/flagged climbers' path (increasingly brushy above ~3,700 ft) up steep slopes to a high camp near the base of the Chimney/Overcoat Glaciers.
routes.descent: Rappel the East Face (~13 double-rope rappels) back to the glacier and reverse the approach to camp/trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/rappel back through the upper chimney and slab pitches, then continue down the lower low-5th-class pitches on a long series of anchors (about 13 rappels total using two ropes); many parties share anchors and tandem-rappel with other groups climbing the same day to speed up the descent before re-crossing the glacier moat and hiking back to camp and out via the Pete Lake Trail.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-day trip: Day 1 — hike from Pete Lake Trailhead via Pete Lake and the PCT to a high camp below the Chimney/Lemah Glaciers (roughly a half-day, ~6 hours for a loaded pack); Day 2 — cross the glacier, climb the East Face Direct to the summit, rappel the route, and return to camp, then hike out (or split the hike-out into a Day 3).
routes.bail: Retreat is by rappelling the anchors on the route back to the glacier at essentially any point on the face; below the glacier, reverse the approach gully/climbers' path back toward camp and the Pete Lake Trail.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent is a long multi-rappel affair (~13 raps) followed by a glacier and gully descent, start early enough on summit day to be off the technical terrain well before dark, and build in a hard turnaround time that accounts for the full rappel descent plus the hike back to camp — Cascade summer afternoons can also bring thunderstorms, another reason not to linger high on the face.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through early September: Wikipedia and SummitPost both note the peak is only easily attempted in early-to-mid summer given its elevation and small glaciers, and the moat/bergschrund at the base of the climb needs to open enough to cross safely, which typically isn't reliable until mid-summer.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of nuts/stoppers and cams sized for the crack and dihedral pitches, plus extra slings for the chimney sections; carry two ropes since the descent is a series of double-rope rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine trad rack; the crux dihedral and crack pitches take gear well, but some of the class-4 slab and chimney terrain is sparsely protectable, so be comfortable running it out on easier ground.
gear: Microspikes or light crampons for glacier/moat crossing
Glacier travel gear for the Chimney/Overcoat Glacier crossing
whatToBring: Bivy/camping gear for the standard 2-day approach-and-climb itinerary
Extra slings/anchor material for shared rappel stations
Map/compass or GPS — the peak sees only about one ascent party a year and route-finding beta is thin
proTips: Parties often share rappel anchors and tandem-rappel with other groups on the peak the same day to speed up the roughly 13-rappel descent.
Carry printed topo/beta since the summit register shows only about one ascent party per year and the line is not well-trodden.
Plan for a moat crossing at the base of the climb — a collapsed snow bridge is sometimes the only way across early/mid-season.
watchOut: Loose blocks in the upper chimney pitches — helmet mandatory.
The glacier moat/bergschrund can be a serious, sometimes unprotected obstacle depending on season.
Even in summer, shaded rock on this glaciated peak can be wet and slippery.
knownHazards: Crevasse hazard while crossing the Chimney and Overcoat Glaciers on approach.
Rockfall potential in the approach gully and within the chimney pitches on route.
Very light traffic (roughly one summit party per year) means self-rescue capability and full self-sufficiency are essential.

pitchDetail.notes: Right-facing dihedral with crack climbing; this is the technical crux of the route.
Scrambling broken by short 5.4 block-and-face moves.
Three long (roughly 40-50m) pitches of easier fifth-class climbing.
Chimney pitch with an awkward overhang.
Largely unprotectable class-4 slab — climb carefully.
Low fifth-class slab leading to a final short chimney onto the summit; guidebooks typically describe the route as 9 pitches, though parties sometimes break it into 10.

road.name: Salmon la Sac Rd -> FS-46 -> FS-4616 to Pete Lake Trailhead
road.status: Paved (Salmon la Sac Rd) transitioning to gravel Forest Roads (FS-46, FS-4616); generally passable to passenger cars in summer
road.seasonalGate: No permanent gate, but the trailhead is only reliably snow-free roughly early June through late October
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum: WA-903 N ~7.6 mi, continue on Salmon la Sac Rd ~9.2 mi, left on FS-46 ~4.7 mi, right on FS-4616 ~0.5 mi to the Pete Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades / Snoqualmie Pass area (check NWAC mountain weather forecasts)
climate.typical: Cool, often snowy well into June at this elevation; warm, mostly dry summers July-September with occasional afternoon thunderstorms; snow returns by October.

bySeason.spring: Deep lingering snowpack; glacier crossings and moat conditions are more hazardous and route access is difficult.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; moat/bergschrund typically open enough to cross by mid-summer, warm and mostly dry with some thunderstorm risk.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows; early snow can return by October, closing out the season.
bySeason.winter: Not a climbing season for this route; heavy snow and avalanche terrain on the approach.

access.closures: Trailhead facilities are seasonal, generally open early June through late October; road/trail access is snow-limited outside that window

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start on summit day to allow time for the glacier crossing, technical climbing, and the long multi-rappel descent before dark

sectionBreakdown.note: Documented time for a loaded-pack approach day via Pete Lake and the Lemah/Chimney basin (from a recorded ascent); times vary with pack weight and conditions.
Glacier crossing plus technical face climbing to the top, per a documented ascent.
Rappel descent and glacier/gully return to camp, per a documented ascent; add additional time to hike out to the trailhead.

routes.corrections: The app names this route 'West Face / South Summit' but no reputable source documents a distinct West Face line to the South Peak on this Chimney Rock (WA, 7,727 ft). The only well-documented technical routes on the peak are the East Face (5.3, Grade III, 3 pitches on Mountain Project) and the East Face Direct (5.6, Grade II per The Mountaineers, ~9 pitches per multiple trip reports) via the Chimney/Lemah Glaciers to the main summit; the Direct's 5.6 grade matches the app's given grade, so the route-specific facts here (pitches, rappels, gear, timing) are sourced to the East Face Direct rather than an actual west-facing line. A SummitPost page titled 'West Face (Standard Route)' exists but belongs to a different Chimney Rock in Idaho's Selkirk Range and was excluded from this research. The 1930 first ascent (Farr/Winder/Byington) is confirmed as the peak's overall first ascent via the south and east faces to the main summit, not a west-face line specifically.

blurb: Chimney Rock (7,727 ft) is a steep, craggy granite peak on the Cascade crest in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, about 10 miles north-northeast of Snoqualmie Pass on the divide between the Snoqualmie and Yakima drainages. It has three summits — a main/central spire (7,727 ft), a northeast "north peak" (7,634 ft), and a minor south peak (7,440 ft) — mantled by the small Chimney and Overcoat Glaciers, and every route to the top involves some Class 5 climbing. It was one of the last major Snoqualmie-region peaks to be climbed, first summited on 27 August 1930 by Forest Farr, Art Winder, and Laurence Byington via the south and east faces, a decade before the second ascent by Fred Beckey and Jim Crooks in 1940.

routes.rockGrade: 4th class (Class 3-4 scrambling; brief damp rock steps)
routes.overview: The East Route (a.k.a. the Lemah Glacier route) is the standard and most direct line up Lemah Mountain's Main Peak (Lemah Three), climbing a long shaded snow finger and the small Lemah Glacier on the peak's southeast side before finishing on Class 3-4 rock to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From a camp near Lemah Creek, the route climbs a shaded snow finger to a bench around 4,800-5,000 ft, crosses damp Class 2-3 rock beside a small waterfall (~5,100 ft), then ascends broken, low-angle rock and snow toward the Lemah Glacier in the gap between Lemah Two and the Main Peak. A short, often wet rock chimney breaches the moat below the summit rocks, followed by Class 2-3 ledges and a final short Class 3 scramble along the summit ridge to the top.
routes.approach: Start at the Pete Lake Trailhead (2,800 ft) reached via Cooper Lake Road/FR-46 and FR-4616. Hike the Pete Lake Trail 4.4 miles to Pete Lake, continue 1.3 miles to a junction (stay right; do not cross the creek), then 0.8 miles to the PCT. Head left (south) on the PCT about 0.8 miles to Lemah Creek (~3,200 ft) - the old footbridge here is destroyed but is not needed for the standard line. Leave the PCT at the creek and follow a faint, intermittently flagged climbers' path on the creek's north side, gaining 400-500 ft through moderate brush over about half a mile to a large wet meadow, then cross the meadow (creek on your left) and push through a dense alder thicket before reaching dry streambeds that lead to the snow finger.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the exit chimney and glacier, re-cross the bench and snow finger, then retrace the meadow/bushwhack section back to the PCT and Pete Lake Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: There is no established rappel line on the East Route; parties downclimb the Class 3-4 exit chimney and ledges, re-cross the Lemah Glacier (roped if used on the ascent), and descend the snow finger before dropping back through the meadow and creekside bushwhack to the PCT and out via Pete Lake. Snow on the finger and gully typically softens through the day, so many parties aim to be off the steepest snow before it becomes unstable and post-holed in the afternoon.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a long single day (multiple trip reports cite roughly 8-10 hours trailhead-to-summit one-way, making a very long round trip) or split into two days with a camp at the Lemah Creek crossing (~3,200 ft) or the upper meadow, allowing an earlier start on the snow finger and glacier while snow is still firm.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ascent line to the snow finger and Lemah Creek is the only practical option; there is no established rappel route, so parties commit to downclimbing the chimney and glacier terrain in reverse rather than bailing laterally.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the exit chimney is running heavy with water or verglassed, if the Lemah Glacier crossing looks broken up with hidden crevasses beyond the party's comfort or gear, or if the remaining bushwhack/creek crossing back to the trailhead cannot be completed before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed primarily July through September, once the approach trail and meadow are snow-free but before the small Lemah Glacier opens up too many crevasses late in the season. The shaded snow finger and gully hold firm neve well into summer, so many parties carry and use crampons and an ice axe even in July and August; conditions soften notably by afternoon.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed protection is placed on the standard line - this is a snow/scramble route rather than a technical rock climb. Bring a light glacier-travel kit (rope, harness, slings, basic crevasse-rescue gear) for the short Lemah Glacier crossing, plus a general mountaineering axe and crampons for the snow finger and summit gully.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal traditional protection is needed on the standard line; most parties travel unroped on the snow finger and rock sections and rope up only for the short, lightly crevassed Lemah Glacier crossing, especially later in the season.
gear: Rope for the glacier crossing (recommended, especially late season)
whatToBring: Helmet (loose/wet rock near the exit chimney)
Water treatment (Lemah Creek and snowmelt sources)
Overnight camping gear if splitting the trip into two days
proTips: Camp at the Lemah Creek crossing (~3,200 ft) or in the upper meadow to split the roughly 20-mile, 5,000-ft round trip into two days.
Start early enough to be on the snow finger and glacier while the snow is still firm, before afternoon softening sets in.
Carry a rope for the upper glacier crossing, particularly in late summer when crevasses open up between Lemah Two and the Main Peak.
watchOut: Firm morning neve on the snow finger can make an ice axe and crampons necessary even in mid-summer.
Route-finding through the meadow and alder thickets is easy to lose, especially in low visibility.
Afternoon softening of the gully snow increases post-holing risk and slows the return trip.
knownHazards: The footbridge over Lemah Creek has been destroyed; the standard route stays on the creek's north side and does not require crossing it.
The exit chimney above the Lemah Glacier typically has water running down it and can be slick; parties move onto ledges to the right when it gets too wet.
The upper Lemah Glacier shows crevasse breaks, especially later in the season, and roping up is recommended there.
The snow finger and summit gully hold continuously steep snow (35-45 degrees) that can be firm/icy in the morning and unstable once it softens in the afternoon.
Heavy bushwhacking and dense slide-alder thickets in the Lemah Creek meadow slow travel and can obscure footing.

pitchDetail.notes: Pete Lake Trailhead (2,800 ft) to Pete Lake, 4.4 mi.
Pete Lake to PCT junction via Lemah Creek, ~2.9 mi; left on PCT ~0.8 mi to Lemah Creek (~3,200 ft); footbridge here is destroyed but not needed.
Leave PCT at Lemah Creek; follow faint, flagged climbers' path on the creek's north side, gaining 400-500 ft over ~0.5 mi to a wet meadow.
Cross the meadow (creek on left), push through a dense alder thicket, then follow dry streambeds toward the snow finger.
Ascend the shaded snow finger (firm neve likely; crampons recommended); exit between about 4,800-5,000 ft toward a small waterfall (~5,100 ft).
Damp rock right of the waterfall, then a broad bench with cliffs on the right, gaining the ridge over mixed snow and rock.

pitchDetail.grade: Easy snow / glacier, YDS 4th class overall
pitchDetail.notes: Cross the small Lemah Glacier between Lemah Two and the Main Peak; minor crevasses, more pronounced late season - rope up if carrying one.
Exit the snow via an often-wet rock chimney (work right onto ledges when too wet), then Class 2-3 ledges to a notch in the summit ridge.
Short, easy final scramble along the summit ridge to the top of the Main Peak (Lemah Three).

road.name: Cooper Lake Road / Forest Road 46, then FR-4616
road.status: Gravel/paved forest road, generally passable to passenger vehicles in season
road.seasonalGate: Closed to wheeled vehicles in winter (open to snowmobile use); trailhead season is roughly early June through late October
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum: WA-903 north ~7.6 mi, then Salmon La Sac Road ~9.2 mi, left on FR-46 for 4.7 mi, right on FR-4616 for 0.5 mi, then Road 113 for 1 mi to the Pete Lake Trailhead.

climate.typical: Alpine Lakes Wilderness crest climate: cool, wet winters with heavy snowpack and warmer, mostly dry summers. The shaded Lemah snow finger and small glacier tend to hold firm neve well into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack; the access road may still be gated and the approach trail largely snow-covered; high avalanche exposure on the snow finger/gully.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); the snow finger and glacier are typically firm in the morning and soften by afternoon; the meadow bushwhack is usually snow-free by mid-to-late July in normal years.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and possible early-season snow by October; the access road may be gated again late in the month.
bySeason.winter: Trailhead road closed to vehicles; deep snowpack makes the approach and route essentially inaccessible without ski touring and involves much greater avalanche exposure.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District; route lies within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness

access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (or pay the day-use fee)
access.closures: Pete Lake Trailhead/access road season is roughly early June-late October; FR-46 corridor is closed to wheeled vehicles in winter

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Pete Lake Trailhead (2,800 ft) to Lemah Creek camp (~3,200 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~7.3 mi via the Pete Lake Trail and PCT; washed-out footbridge at Lemah Creek is not needed
Bushwhack, snow finger, bench, Lemah Glacier, exit chimney, and summit ridge scramble
Multiple trip reports cite roughly 8-10 hours trailhead-to-summit one-way; often split into a 2-day trip with a camp at Lemah Creek or the upper meadow given the ~20-mile, 5,000-ft round trip

routes.corrections: Input carried rockGrade '5.0'; Mountain Project's route page for this line (listed as 'Lemah Glacier') grades it YDS 4th class overall (Grade II / 'Easy Snow'), and the detailed SummitPost route description tops out at Class 3-4 scrambling with no roped rock climbing - set rockGrade to '4th class' rather than 5.0. Could not independently source the alpine grade 'PD+' from any reputable route-specific page, so alpineGrade is left null rather than carried forward unverified. The Main Peak's first ascent (July 7, 1923, Wallace Burr and party, per Wikipedia) is a PEAK first ascent; no source confirms it was made via this specific East Route/Lemah Glacier line, so the route-level 'fa' is left null per the peak-vs-route FA distinction. Peak elevation is reported variably across sources: 7,512 ft (SummitPost, Wikipedia, Mountain-Forecast) vs. 7,519-7,520 ft (Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn); this is within normal survey/LiDAR variance of the given 7,519 ft, so no change was made to the peak elevation field.

blurb: Lemah Mountain is a serrated, five-summited peak on the Snoqualmie Crest in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Washington Cascades, about 7.7 miles south of Snoqualmie Pass on the King-Kittitas county line. Built of granite and metamorphic rock typical of the Cascade Volcanic Arc, its highest point, the Main Peak (Lemah Three), holds a small remnant glacier on its east side and was first climbed on July 7, 1923 by Wallace Burr and party.

routes.fa: 1925 (Mountaineers club outing; specific climbers not documented in available sources; this SE-face/south-side line is described as the mountain's easiest and most-traveled route)
routes.rappels: None established; descent is by downclimbing the ascent line (no rappel line documented)
objHaz: residual snowfields and moats below the south ridge
complex route-finding across slabby traverses and gullies
long, remote approach with limited bailout options
routes.face: Southeast Face / Southwest Gully (south ridge finish)
routes.overview: A remote Class 3-4 scramble up Summit Chief's southeast basin and south ridge, finishing via a loose southwest gully/face to the summit; reached only after a long backcountry approach through the Alpine Lakes Wilderness.
routes.beta: From a high camp near Vista Lakes or Summit Chief Lake, parties traverse off-trail north across Vista Ridge to Summit Chief Pass and into the peak's southeast basin. Snowfields below the southeast face are climbed via a snow finger to gain the south ridge (the first, left-most finger dead-ends at a notch, requiring a rightward traverse to the upper snowfield corner). From the ridge notch, Class 2-3 (locally Class 4) scrambling on loose, convoluted sandstone/siltstone slabs, gullies and a short exposed ramp leads to the summit.
routes.approach: Start at Pete Lake Trailhead (2,800 ft) at the end of Forest Road 4616/113 off Cooper Lake/Salmon la Sac Road. Follow Pete Lake Trail 4.5 miles to Pete Lake, then continue via a Lemah Meadow bypass and the Pacific Crest Trail roughly 8.5 more miles to Vista Lakes (camp near 5,600 ft) or on to Summit Chief Lake. From camp, leave the trail and traverse north across Vista Ridge to Summit Chief Pass to reach the base of the route.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the southwest gully/southeast face, re-cross the snowfields back to the south ridge notch, and retrace the off-trail traverse to the trail system.
routes.descentText: There is no rappel line or alternate descent documented for this route. Downclimb the same loose gully and face used on ascent, taking care on the exposed ramp and slab traverses, then descend the snow finger back into the southeast basin. From there, re-cross Vista Ridge to Summit Chief Pass and pick up the Pacific Crest Trail / Pete Lake Trail back to the trailhead. Some parties extend the trip with a loop past Escondido Point and Escondido Lake instead of retracing the exact approach.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a 2-3 day backpack: Day 1, hike in (~13 miles) to a camp at Vista Lakes or Summit Chief Lake; Day 2, alpine start for the off-trail traverse, snowfield/south-ridge climb, and Class 3-4 scramble to the summit and back to camp (roughly 5.7 hours camp-to-summit per one trip report); Day 3, hike out. Strong day-hiking parties have done it car-to-car in 8-12 hours, though a multi-day approach is recommended given the length and route-finding demands.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same ascent line; there is no shorter bail option once above the snowfields on the south ridge other than downclimbing the southwest gully/southeast face back into the basin.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote approach and loose, route-finding-intensive upper mountain, set a firm turnaround time that gets the party off the snowfields and south ridge before afternoon thunderstorms build or overnight-firm snow softens into the afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Documented ascents cluster from early July through August (per trip reports and logged climbs), when the approach trails are snow-free but residual snowfields on the southeast face are still consolidated enough to aid the climb; by late season those snowfields can melt out and expose looser rock and moats.
routes.detailedRack: Not a roped rock climb for most parties, but the exposed, loose Class 3-4 moves near the south-ridge notch and the crux ramp are commonly protected with a light rack and a short rope for less-experienced scramblers; many parties climb it entirely unroped with a helmet.
routes.proNeeds: A 30m rope with a small rack of slings/light gear is enough to belay the short crux sections; an ice axe (and traction in early season) is needed for the approach snowfields.
gear: approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: map and compass or GPS (route-finding across slabs and basins is easy to get wrong)
extra water (long dry stretches away from camp)
overnight/backpacking gear for the standard 2-3 day itinerary
microspikes or similar traction in early season
proTips: Break the long approach into two days by camping at Vista Lakes or Summit Chief Lake rather than attempting a single push.
Start early to cross the approach snowfields while they're still firm.
On the crux snow finger, don't commit down into the dead-end notch — traverse right to the upper corner of the snowfield to exit onto the ramp that leads to the higher south-ridge notch.
watchOut: The upper mountain's sandstone/siltstone is notoriously loose and crumbly — test every hold and watch for parties above/below.
Snow fingers below the southeast face can hide moats and require careful step-kicking.
The slabby traverses and gully system are easy to route-find incorrectly, especially in poor visibility.
knownHazards: Loose, crumbly rock throughout the upper mountain creates ongoing rockfall risk, particularly with multiple parties on route.
Residual snowfields on the approach to the south ridge can be firm early and require an ice axe to safely ascend/descend.
The approach is long and remote (roughly 13 miles one-way to the base of the climb), increasing exposure to weather changes and fatigue-related risk with few bailout options.

pitchDetail.notes: Pete Lake Trailhead to Pete Lake, 4.5 miles.
Pete Lake to Vista Lakes via a Lemah Meadow bypass and the Pacific Crest Trail, roughly 8.5 miles.
Camp to Summit Chief Pass: off-trail traverse north across Vista Ridge into the peak's southeast basin.
Ascend the snowfield/snow finger below the southeast face to gain a notch on the south ridge; the left-most finger dead-ends, requiring a rightward traverse to the upper snowfield corner.
South ridge notch to summit via loose southwest gully/face: steep dirt and scree, a left-slanting ramp, and exposed slab moves on unstable rock to the summit.

road.name: Forest Road 46 to Forest Road 4616 to Road 113 (off Salmon la Sac Road, WA-903 north of Cle Elum)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service road to the Pete Lake Trailhead
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum: WA-903/Salmon la Sac Road north about 16.8 miles to FR-46, left 4.7 miles, right on FR-4616 about 0.5-1 mile to the Pete Lake Trailhead (2,800 ft).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Cascade-crest climate with a defined wet/dry season; the approach and route are best in the July-September dry window, per documented ascent dates.

bySeason.spring: Deep lingering snowpack on approach trails and the route; avalanche hazard on steep terrain, not a standard climbing season.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; approach trails snow-free by July with residual snowfields persisting on the southeast face into mid-summer.
bySeason.fall: Generally dry with shorter days; early-season snow can arrive by October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow accumulation; a winter/ski-mountaineering objective only, not the scramble route described here.
