emergency.notes: The route lies deep in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, roughly 13 miles from the trailhead to the base of the climb, so expect an extended response time for any rescue.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,466 ft) differs slightly from the commonly cited figures: Wikipedia and Peakbagger list 7,467 ft, while several trip reports round to 7,464 ft; the difference is trivial survey rounding, not a substantive conflict. The route is documented under several overlapping names across sources (Wikipedia's 'Southeast Face', trip reports' 'Southeast Face-Southwest Face', and this catalog's 'South Route') describing the same standard easiest line up the mountain's south side; they are treated here as one route. Distance/elevation-gain figures for the full trip vary by source (e.g., a documented loop trip that also tags nearby Escondido Point logged 30 miles / 7,700 ft round trip, while a plain out-and-back day-hike account cites roughly 25 miles / 5,000 ft) — the higher figures used here (gainFt/lossFt) reflect the loop-with-side-trip version and may overstate a direct out-and-back ascent.

blurb: Summit Chief Mountain (7,467 ft) is the high point of a remote, deeply glacier-carved massif on the Cascade Crest in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, about nine miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass on the King-Kittitas county line. Unlike the granite typical of the surrounding Cascades, its upper mountain is loose sandstone and siltstone, producing convoluted slab traverses, gullies and notches rather than clean rock. The peak was first climbed in 1925 during a Mountaineers club outing that also explored and named the adjacent Little Big Chief Mountain, though the specific climbers are not documented in available sources.

routes.rappels: 2 full-length (60m) rappels off the descent gully back onto the Overcoat Glacier
objHaz: Loose rock at the base of the south arete
routes.face: South arete of the summit block, reached via the Chimney Rock–Overcoat col
routes.overview: The Southeast Route gains Overcoat Peak's summit block via its south arete. Climbers cross/circle the Overcoat Glacier from a high camp to the Chimney Rock–Overcoat col, then climb the south ridge and an exposed ledge on its west side to the summit.
routes.beta: From camp near the Overcoat Glacier, travel across or around the glacier eastward to the notch/col between Chimney Rock and Overcoat Peak. At the col, shed glacier gear and step directly onto the south ridge, which is loose for the first ~20 feet before improving. A ledge traverses the west side of the ridge — exposed, strong Class 3 with sections of Class 4 (touching low 5th class) — leading to the summit block. This line is only reasonable in snow-free late-summer conditions; an alternate, more technical variation climbs a steep snow/ice ramp on the northeast face of the summit block when conditions allow.
routes.approach: From North Bend, drive the Middle Fork Snoqualmie Rd (FR 56) about 12 miles to the Taylor River Rd junction, then FR 5620 about 5 miles to Dingford Creek Trailhead (~1,500 ft; last stretch is rough, potholed gravel). From the trailhead, bike or hike the old road/Dutch Miller Gap Trail corridor several miles, ford the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River, then ascend steep, mostly off-trail forest and talus to a high camp near the Overcoat Glacier/Overcoat Lake. There is no maintained trail for the upper approach — expect bushwhacking, waterfalls, and route-finding through the drainage.
routes.descent: Descend east off the summit through a gully and heather ledges, downclimb a dihedral (prominent ramp) to an area of dripping water, then make two full-length (60m) rappels back onto the Overcoat Glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit, drop east into a gully system and follow heather ledges down. Downclimb a prominent dihedral/ramp feature until reaching wet rock near a seep, then rig two consecutive full-length rappels (60m rope) to regain the surface of the Overcoat Glacier, from which the col and camp are reached in reverse of the ascent.
routes.itinerary: Typically done over 2-3 days: Day 1 - drive, bike/hike the approach, ford the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River, and bushwhack to high camp near the Overcoat Glacier; Day 2 - alpine start, cross to the Chimney Rock–Overcoat col, climb the south arete to the summit, and descend via the east gully and two rappels back to camp; Day 3 - hike/bike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down-climbs the same south arete back to the Chimney Rock–Overcoat col and onto the glacier; below the col there are no fixed anchors, so a party should carry rope and a small rack in case a rappel is needed to reverse any committing section.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, mostly off-trail approach, a late-season river ford, and an exposed, loose scramble near the top, set a firm turnaround time on the summit push that guarantees daylight (or at least good visibility) for the descent gully, the rappels, and the return bushwhack to camp.
routes.bestSeason: The approach and the south arete are only reasonable in late-summer, snow-free conditions — roughly July through September. Snow on the glacier and col earlier in the season adds difficulty and moat hazard to the rock scramble, and the Middle Fork Snoqualmie ford is safest at the lower water levels of late summer.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack of small-to-mid cams and nuts plus a 60m rope covers the short technical sections on the south arete and both rappel anchors on the descent; a helmet is essential given the loose rock encountered right off the col.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection is placed on the class 4-5.0 scrambling itself; the rack is mainly used to back up exposed moves on the west-side ledge traverse and to build the two rappel anchors on descent.
whatToBring: Mountain bike (optional, speeds the gravel road approach)
River-crossing/camp shoes for the Middle Fork ford
Bug protection (mosquitoes reported in the basin in summer)
Overnight/bivy gear for a 2-3 day trip
Map/compass or GPS for the trail-less upper approach
proTips: Many parties bike the gravel FR 56/FR 5620 road from the gate to save hours of walking on the approach.
Expect the rock right at the Chimney Rock–Overcoat col to be loose for the first ~20 feet before the south arete firms up.
Leave a bike cached near the start of the bushwhack to speed the long forested exit.
watchOut: The ledge traversing the west side of the south ridge is quite exposed — a fall there would be serious.
The Middle Fork Snoqualmie River ford can be cold, swift, and thigh-to-waist deep, especially early season or after rain.
The off-trail bushwhack to camp is easy to lose track of in poor visibility; waterfalls and cliff bands force careful route-finding in the drainage.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Overcoat Glacier require standard glacier travel precautions and a rope.
Rock quality near the base of the south arete, just above the col, is notably looser than the rest of the route.
Dense alder and steep, trail-less forest on the approach to camp.
Heavy-flowing waterfalls and cliff bands in the approach drainage.

pitchDetail.notes: Cross or circle the Overcoat Glacier from high camp to the notch/col between Chimney Rock and Overcoat Peak; rope and crampons typically used.
Shed glacier gear at the col and step directly onto the south ridge; rock is loose for roughly the first 20 feet.
A ledge traverses the west side of the south ridge with real exposure; rock is generally sound but looser than elsewhere on the route — test holds carefully.
Final short scramble/climbing moves to the true summit.

road.name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Rd (FR 56) / FR 5620
road.status: Gravel, rough and potholed on the final stretch to Dingford Creek Trailhead; passable to most vehicles at low speed, higher clearance recommended
road.seasonalGate: Road is closed to vehicles at Dingford Creek Trailhead itself; no separate winter gate documented beyond normal seasonal snow/debris closure
road.driveNote: From North Bend, follow the Middle Fork Snoqualmie Rd about 12 miles to the Taylor River Rd junction, then FR 5620 about 5 miles to Dingford Creek Trailhead (~1,500 ft).

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades / Snoqualmie Pass region (NWAC)
climate.typical: Wet, heavy-snow winters and springs typical of the west-slope Central Cascades; drier but still changeable weather June-September, which is the realistic climbing window for this route.

bySeason.spring: Deep lingering snowpack, avalanche hazard on approach slopes, and high, cold river fords from snowmelt.
bySeason.summer: Best window (Jul-Sep); snow can still linger on the glacier and col into early July.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and the possibility of early-season snow by October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and difficult access; not a standard winter objective for this route.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: None currently required at Dingford Creek Trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue Wilderness Permit required for day and overnight use, May 15-Oct 31
access.passRequired: No Northwest Forest Pass currently required at Dingford Creek Trailhead per the USFS trailhead listing

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from high camp to allow time for the glacier crossing, the exposed scramble, and the gully/rappel descent before dark

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Dingford Creek TH toward Dutch Miller Gap Trail
sectionBreakdown.note: Rough gravel/dirt road, commonly biked; time from one trip report

sectionBreakdown.section: Trail, ford, and bushwhack to camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Dutch Miller Gap Trail area to high camp near Overcoat Glacier
sectionBreakdown.note: Ford the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River then ascend steep, mostly trail-less forest/talus; duration varies significantly by party and conditions

emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office - (425) 888-1421, 902 SE North Bend Way, North Bend, WA 98045
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital (24/7 Emergency Dept.) - 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA 98065
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness objective with a long, mostly off-trail approach; expect no reliable cell coverage in the Middle Fork Snoqualmie drainage. King County Sheriff's Office coordinates backcountry search and rescue (King County SAR units, including Seattle Mountain Rescue and Explorer SAR) for this area.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists a separate route on this peak called 'East Face' (Trad, roughly 4th class with 1 pitch of mixed/alpine 1b climbing, GPS ~47.51388,-121.29218, cited elevation 7,416 ft) rather than 'Southeast Route.' Its coordinates and elevation are close to the summit and its described character (short 4th-class mixed pitch) overlaps with descriptions of the standard line, but sources do not confirm whether it is the same line as this app's 'Southeast Route' or a distinct variation; treat the two names as possibly-overlapping but not confirmed identical.

blurb: Overcoat Peak (7,432 ft) rises in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Mount Baker–Snoqualmie National Forest, immediately northwest of Chimney Rock in the remote upper Middle Fork Snoqualmie River drainage. Built of granite and carrying the small Overcoat Glacier on its east slopes, it is a rarely-visited peak with a long, mostly trail-less approach. USFS surveyor Albert H. Sylvester and John Charlton made the first ascent in July 1897, and Sylvester named the mountain after leaving his overcoat on the summit.

routes.overview: A long wilderness approach up the Lemah Creek valley followed by a scramble up cliff bands to the col between Lemah Two and Lemah Mountain's Main Peak, then a steep snow-and-rock chute to Lemah Two's summit.
routes.beta: From the Lemah Creek meadow (~3,600 ft), traverse toward the base of the peaks and climb a series of Class 2-3 cliff bands to the col between Lemah Two and the Main Peak (Lemah Three). From the col, a snow slope and chute lead up toward Lemah Two's upper slopes and summit ridge; the chute itself -- a mix of steep snow/ice and Class 3-4 rock, often with water running down it -- is the technical crux. Crampons and an ice axe are commonly used even in summer.
routes.approach: From the Cooper River/Pete Lake trailhead, follow the Cooper River Trail about 4 miles to Pete Lake, continue roughly 1.2 miles to the PCT, then head north on the PCT about 1 mile to the Lemah Creek crossing. Pick up the climber's trail on the north side of the creek and follow it past a series of waterfalls and swimming holes to the meadow where Lemah Creek breaks into shallow ponds around 3,600 feet.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb (or rappel) the crux chute and cliff bands back to the col and Lemah Creek meadow, then retrace the approach trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descend by downclimbing the same Class 3-4 chute and cliff-band terrain used on ascent back to the Lemah Creek meadow, then follow the climber's trail and maintained trail system (Lemah Creek, PCT, Pete Lake, Cooper River Trail) back to the trailhead. Some parties rappel the steepest, wettest section of the crux chute rather than downclimb it, particularly if it is holding ice or running water.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as an overnight trip: hike in roughly 8 miles to a camp at Spectacle Lake, Glacier Lake, Chikamin Lake, or the Lemah Creek meadow on day one, then make an early summit push and return to camp or out to the trailhead on day two. Strong parties have done it car-to-car in a single long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible at essentially any point on the cliff bands or col below the summit by reversing the ascent line back down to the Lemah Creek meadow; there are no fixed bail anchors, so retreat means downclimbing (or rappelling) the same terrain just climbed.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on your party's pace, snow conditions, and forecast -- the crux chute and cliff bands become more hazardous to downclimb once afternoon sun softens snow or exposes wet rock, and this is a long day with a long walk out.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed mid-summer into early fall (roughly July-September) once the approach trail and Lemah Creek crossing are snow-free, though the summit chute and upper slopes often hold snow or ice well into summer, so crampons and an ice axe are commonly carried even in July and August.
routes.comms: This is a deep, remote section of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness; expect no reliable cell coverage in the Lemah Creek valley or on the peak. A satellite communicator or PLB is recommended for emergencies.
routes.face: East side, via the Lemah Two-Main Peak col
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is required for the standard line -- most parties carry an ice axe and crampons for the snow slope/chute below the col, plus a helmet for the loose cliff-band terrain. A light rack (a few slings/a short rope) is sometimes carried in case a party wants to rappel the crux chute rather than downclimb it.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is used; this is an unroped scramble/snow climb for most parties rather than a pitched rock route. Ice axe and crampons substitute for rock protection on the summit snow/ice chute.
gear: Short rope (optional, for rappelling the crux chute)
whatToBring: Free self-issue wilderness permit (fill out at trailhead)
Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
Bug protection (mosquitoes in the lower valley)
Water treatment for Lemah Creek/tarns along the approach
Sturdy footwear for the climber's trail past waterfalls
proTips: Camp at Spectacle, Glacier, or Chikamin Lake to split the long approach across two days.
Use the climber's trail on the north side of Lemah Creek to bypass brush, passing waterfalls and swimming holes along the way.
Bring crampons and an ice axe even in mid-summer -- the chute below the Lemah Two/Main col often holds snow or ice into August.
Plan an alpine start given the long round-trip mileage and elevation gain.
watchOut: The summit chute is the technical crux and can involve wet rock alongside snow/ice -- some parties rappel it rather than downclimb.
Loose, chossy rock on the cliff bands and ridge, with rockfall risk from parties above.
Moats can open around early-season snowfields, complicating the approach onto the snow/glacier terrain.
Afternoon sun softens snow, making the descent through the chute more hazardous later in the day.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock ("vertical jungles of choss") on the cliff bands
Crevasse and moat hazard on the Lemah Glacier/snow slopes, especially early season
Steep mixed snow, ice, and rock in the summit chute
Stream crossings and slick rock near waterfalls on the Lemah Creek approach

pitchDetail.notes: ~4 miles, mostly flat forest trail along the Cooper River.
~1.2 miles.
~1 mile north on the PCT.

pitchDetail.pitch: Lemah Creek climber's trail to meadow
pitchDetail.notes: Climber's path on the north side of the creek past waterfalls and swimming holes to the ~3,600 ft meadow.
Cliff bands lead up to the col between Lemah Two and the Main Peak.
Snow slope and chute (crux) to Lemah Two's summit ridge and top.

road.name: Forest Roads 46 and 4616 (Cooper Lake Road)
road.status: Gravel/paved forest road, generally passable to standard vehicles once open for the season
road.seasonalGate: Closed to wheeled vehicles in winter (open to snowmobiles); reopens once snow clears, typically by late spring
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 80 (Roslyn/Salmon la Sac), follow SR-903 through Roslyn past Cle Elum Lake about 16.8 miles to FR-46, then 4.7 miles on FR-46, then right on FR-4616 about 1 mile to the Cooper River/Pete Lake trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC avalanche forecast, Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Wet, mild winters with heavy snowpack give way to drier, warmer summers; the Alpine Lakes Wilderness sees significant orographic and Puget Sound convergence-zone precipitation off the Cascade crest.

bySeason.spring: Deep, lingering snowpack into June with elevated avalanche and moat hazard on the approach and lower slopes.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (July-September), though the summit chute and shaded slopes can still hold snow or ice into midsummer.
bySeason.fall: Cools quickly after summer; early snow is possible by October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow with limited road access to the trailhead; not a typical winter objective.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass, America the Beautiful Pass, or day-use fee required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free, self-issue wilderness permit available at the trailhead; no trailhead quota for this area (unlike the separate Enchantments permit zone)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: Year-round campfire restrictions apply above 5,000 ft elevation and near many wilderness lakes.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start recommended given the long round-trip distance, elevation gain, and exposure on the descent.

sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 miles of flat forest trail.

sectionBreakdown.section: Pete Lake to Lemah Creek crossing
sectionBreakdown.note: ~2.2 miles via the PCT junction.
Climber's trail past waterfalls and swimming holes.
Cliff bands to the col, then the crux snow/rock chute to the top.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittcom (Kittitas County 911 dispatch); non-emergency line 509-925-8534, call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, 803 W. 2nd St, Cle Elum, WA, (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg, WA (nearest full-service hospital); KVH Urgent Care in Cle Elum for non-emergency care
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach with limited to no cell coverage for much of the route; nearest full hospital is roughly 1.5-2 hours' drive from the trailhead area.

routes.corrections: Provided elevationFt (7,339 ft) is closely supported by listsofjohn.com's figure of 7,342 ft for Lemah Two (used for highPointFt); note that older Wikipedia data lists this summit at 7,280 ft. No specific first-ascent record distinct from Lemah Mountain's other towers (Main Peak first climbed 1923 by a Wallace Burr-led party; Lemah One in 1925) was found for Lemah Two specifically, so 'fa' is left null rather than guessed.

blurb: Lemah Two is one of five distinct rock towers strung along Lemah Mountain's ridge on the Snoqualmie Crest, in Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness roughly 7-8 miles northwest of Snoqualmie Pass, straddling the King/Kittitas county line. The peaks are built of rugged, often loose Cascade granite and gneiss ("vertical jungles of choss" in one climber's account) rising above the Lemah Creek valley and Lemah Glacier basin. The highest tower, Main Peak (7,512 ft), was first climbed in 1923 by a party led by Wallace Burr; the other summits, including Lemah One (1925) and Lemah Two, were climbed in subsequent decades as each finger got its own independent route.

waypoints.name: Dingford Creek Trailhead (Dutch Miller Gap Trail access)

objHaz: Exposed, semi-technical notch on the northeast ridge that trip reports rate Class 4-5, vertical to slightly overhanging on its north side
Loose rock typical of a seldom-climbed North Cascades granite peak
Cliff bands below the summit that can trap a direct descent line
Lingering snow in gullies into August in some years
Long, remote approach that limits daylight margin for the summit day
routes.face: Named 'Northeast Face' by Mountain Project; a detailed 2014 trip report describes the line as East Slope-Northeast Ridge-East Face-Southeast Face
routes.overview: The peak's easiest/standard line climbs class 3-4 scrambling terrain on Little Big Chief's northeast side: up the east slope onto the northeast ridge, across an exposed ridge notch, then finishing on the east and southeast faces to a small, precarious summit block. Mountain Project separately catalogs this general line as the 'Northeast Face' (4th class); Wikipedia calls the easiest route 'Northeast Face, Class 3-4.'
routes.beta: This is a rarely climbed, route-finding-intensive scramble rather than a well-trodden trail. A 2014 trip report (Jim Brisbine, trailcatjim.com) climbing from a camp at Williams Lake found the crux to be a wildly exposed notch on the northeast ridge, then Class 4-5 moves on a steep southwest face near the true summit; the party descended the 'easier' Class 3 east face but still ran into cliff bands that forced a traverse back onto the ascent line. A separate account (referenced via SummitPost) similarly describes exposed Class 3 moves on the east face and a short Class 3 section gaining the north ridge, with Beckey's guide noting a short rock notch some parties rate as low fifth class.
routes.approach: Most parties reach the peak from the west side of the range. One documented option: East Fork Foss River Trailhead (1,640 ft) up the Necklace Valley Trail, over La Bohn Lakes country to a camp at Williams Lake (~5,500 ft), then east toward Dutch Miller Gap to gain the peak's east-side slopes. An alternate west-side approach follows the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Trail from the Dingford Creek Trailhead (1,400 ft, reached via the rough FR 56) to the Dutch Miller Gap Trail. A third option approaches from the south via Pete Lake and Waptus Lake. All approaches are long (multi-hour to multi-day) and the peak itself is well off-trail.
routes.descent: Most descents reverse the broken Class 3-4 terrain used on ascent (the 'easier' east face) rather than attempting a direct gully.
routes.descentText: A 2014 trip report found that a direct couloir descent below the summit cliffed out and required traversing back onto the east-facing ascent slope to avoid the bands; the exposed ridge notch may require a short downclimb or careful reversal. Expect real route-finding on the way down, and budget for finishing in the dark if the summit push starts late — one party's return to their trailhead was in headlamp.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan is a 2-3 day trip: backpack in via Necklace Valley/Foss River Trail (or the Middle Fork Snoqualmie/Dutch Miller Gap Trail) to a camp near Williams Lake or in the Dutch Miller Gap area, make a long summit day to Little Big Chief and back to camp, then hike out. One party completed the Williams Lake-to-summit round trip as a single roughly 5.5-hour push from an established camp.
routes.bail: There is no established rappel/walk-off bail line reported for this route; retreat means reversing the ridge and notch section back onto the east-slope descent line. Turn around before committing to the exposed notch if conditions, party ability, or time are marginal.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote approach and the North Cascades' typical afternoon-thunderstorm and weather-change risk in summer, set a firm turnaround time before starting the final ridge/notch scramble so the exposed sections and descent are not attempted in the dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September once seasonal snow clears from the approach basins and summit gullies; parties climbing in August have still found lingering snow patches in the gully system, so an ice axe can be useful into midsummer.
proTips: The crux notch on the northeast ridge is reported as vertical to slightly overhanging on its north side — scout alternatives before committing to a line through it.

Treat this as a route-finding problem as much as a technical one; the line is loose, complex, and rarely traveled, so the nominal Class 3 rating undersells the difficulty reported by parties who have actually climbed it.
Start early — at least one party finished their descent by headlamp after a late-afternoon summit push.
watchOut: A direct descent gully below the summit has cliffed out on at least one attempt; be ready to traverse back onto the ascent line rather than committing to an unscouted way down.
Snow can linger in the gully system into August in some years.
knownHazards: Exposed, semi-technical (Class 4-5 per multiple trip reports) notch on the northeast ridge.
Loose rock typical of seldom-climbed North Cascades granite scrambles.
Remote setting with a long approach that limits daylight margin for a safe retreat.

road.name: Foss River Road (FR 68) to Necklace Valley Trailhead, or Middle Fork Snoqualmie Road (FR 56) to Dingford Creek Trailhead
road.status: Foss River Road is paved/gravel and generally maintained to the trailhead; FR 56 beyond pavement is rough, with four-wheel drive recommended, and the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF has reported road damage/closures in the North Bend/Middle Fork area — check current alerts before driving.
road.driveNote: Necklace Valley TH: from Skykomish on US-2, turn onto Foss River Road and continue about 4 miles to the trailhead (1,640 ft). Dingford Creek TH (for the Dutch Miller Gap Trail): about 18 miles up the Middle Fork Snoqualmie Road (FR 56) from North Bend.

climate.forecastZone: NWS 'Cascades of Southern King County' / 'Cascades of Snohomish and Northern King Counties' zone forecasts; NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone for avalanche/winter mountain weather
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and drier summers typical of the Washington Cascades crest; summer freezing levels can climb well above 10,000 ft on clear days, but afternoon thunderstorms and rapid weather changes are common at this latitude.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack lingers into June, especially on north- and east-facing gullies; approach trails can be snow-covered at elevation.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window, July-September, though shaded gullies can hold snow patches into August.
bySeason.fall: Short weather windows before snow returns; daylight shortens quickly.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard make the peak essentially a ski/snowshoe mountaineering objective, not a scrambling one.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Skykomish Ranger District), Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Recreation Pass or day-use fee required at developed trailheads such as Necklace Valley ($5/day or $30 annual if not covered by a Northwest Forest/Interagency Pass)
access.permit: Free self-issue Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit required at the trailhead for day and overnight use, May 15-October 31
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass (or pay the day-use fee) at Necklace Valley Trailhead
access.closures: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Road (FR 56) has had rough and storm-damaged sections; check current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before relying on Dingford Creek/Dutch Miller Gap trailhead access.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early morning start recommended given the long approach and remote, route-finding-heavy summit day

sectionBreakdown.section: Williams Lake camp to Little Big Chief summit and back
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Williams Lake (~5,500 ft) to summit (7,225 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: One trip report logged roughly 5.5 hours round-trip from a Williams Lake camp to the summit and back, including the ridge notch crux and route-finding on descent.

emergency.county: King County / Kittitas County (the summit sits on the county line)
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area is typically coordinated among King County Sheriff SAR, Kittitas County Search & Rescue, and Seattle Mountain Rescue given the peak's position on the county line within Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Cell coverage is not documented for this specific summit; expect no reliable service in the approach valleys.

routes.corrections: Elevation: the given 7,227 ft is not matched by primary sources — Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and PeakVisor all list 7,225 ft (per the Big Snow Mountain USGS quad); highPointFt corrected to 7,225. Route identity/grade: no source uses the name 'West Route.' Mountain Project's area page lists only two named routes on this peak — 'Falcon Route' (IV 5.9, northwest face, FA Jeff Hansell & Martin Volken, Sept 10, 2001) and 'Northeast Face' (4th class); Wikipedia calls the easiest line 'Northeast Face, Class 3-4.' A detailed 2014 trip report (trailcatjim.com) and a SummitPost trip account both describe the standard scramble as harder than a plain Class 3 — a genuinely exposed Class 4-5 notch on the northeast ridge and Class 4-5 moves near the summit tower. Because I could not confirm this catalog route corresponds exactly one-to-one with Mountain Project's 'Northeast Face' line (vs. simply being 'the west-approached standard route'), I left 'fa,' 'alpineGrade,' 'pitches,' and 'routeFt' unset rather than assert unverified specifics, while still reporting the aspect/face and difficulty character that multiple independent trip reports agree on.

blurb: Little Big Chief Mountain (7,225 ft) is a granite summit on the Cascade Range crest within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, sitting immediately southwest of Dutch Miller Gap on the King-Kittitas county line about ten miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass. It was first climbed in August 1939 by Fred Beckey, Wayne Swift, Joe Barto, and Campbell Brooks, and was named for pioneering Mountaineers climber Lorenz A. Nelson during the club's 1925 ascent of neighboring Summit Chief Mountain. The peak sees few visitors — approaches via Necklace Valley, the Middle Fork Snoqualmie/Dutch Miller Gap trail, or Pete Lake are long and seldom-traveled, and both the standard scramble line and the technical Falcon Route (5.9) on the northwest face have a reputation among Cascades climbers for loose, complex terrain.

routes.fa: C.G. Morrison and I.J. Kohler, May 30, 1915 (first recorded ascent of the peak; the historical record does not specify the exact line, but this is presumed to be via the peak's easiest — southeast — side)
objHaz: steep residual snow finger below the summit block (early-mid season)
biting insects near the summit in late summer
routes.face: Southeast slopes / south ridge (summit horn's east-ridge notch is a harder, non-standard descent variant)
routes.overview: A long Alpine Lakes Wilderness approach past a string of alpine lakes leads to an easy but sustained Class 2-3 scramble up Chikamin Peak's southeast slopes and south ridge, finishing with a short exposed gully/chimney to the summit block.
routes.beta: From the Mineral Creek Trailhead, follow the Mineral Creek Trail and past the Park Lakes to the Pacific Crest Trail. Head north on the PCT along the west side of Chikamin Ridge, then leave the trail around 5,500 ft to climb southeast-facing slabs, heather benches, and a broadening gully onto the crest of Chikamin Ridge. Follow the ridge/gully system to the summit horn, finishing with a short, well-protected Class 3 chimney (about 10 ft) and roughly 75 more feet of easy scrambling to the top. An alternate approach from Glacier Lake/Chikamin Lake (via Mineral Creek and a Spectacle Point saddle) reaches the same upper mountain from the east.
routes.approach: From I-90, exit at Cle Elum and follow SR 903 north about 17 miles (through Roslyn, along Cle Elum Lake, to Salmon la Sac), then turn onto Forest Road 46 (Cooper Lake Road) for about 10 miles to the signed Mineral Creek Trailhead (~2,375 ft). Hike the Mineral Creek Trail roughly 5.4 miles and 2,570 ft of gain — crossing the Kachess River near the start and passing through old-growth forest and brushy avalanche paths — to the Pacific Crest Trail junction near the Park Lakes.
routes.descent: Reverse the southeast slopes/south ridge line used on the ascent, downclimbing the summit chimney/gully back to Chikamin Ridge, then the PCT and Mineral Creek Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: The standard descent reverses the ascent gully and short summit chimney back down to Chikamin Ridge, then follows the PCT south and drops back down the Mineral Creek Trail to the trailhead. At least one documented party has instead descended via the upper east ridge to a roughly 6,600-ft notch and then down lower east-ridge cliffs toward Chikamin Lake — this variant involves down-climbing loose Class 4 rock and is not the recommended way off for most parties.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done either as a single very long day from the Mineral Creek Trailhead (parties report roughly 12 hours car-to-car), or as a 2-day trip: hike in and camp at the Park Lakes, Glacier Lake, or Chikamin Lake basin on day one, then climb the peak (roughly 5-6 hours round trip from a high camp) and hike out on day two.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the ascent line back down to Chikamin Ridge and the PCT; there are no fixed rappel anchors or bail infrastructure on route, so any retreat is by downclimbing the same terrain.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, multi-hour approach before any technical terrain begins, set a firm turnaround time that leaves a comfortable daylight margin for the full descent, and bail well before deteriorating weather, wet rock, or lingering snow make the summit chimney and ridge scrambling unsafe.
routes.bestSeason: The route is typically snow-free and in prime scrambling condition from around early-to-mid July through September, with real Cascade summer weather (persistent high pressure) usually settling in by July; it can remain feasible into October in dry years. Earlier in the season, or after a heavy winter, a steep snow finger below the summit block can persist and require an ice axe and solid snow-travel skills.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through most of this stretch of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness; Kittitas County Search and Rescue has noted relying on satellite communication for rescues in this area due to the lack of phone or radio signal.
routes.detailedRack: No roped protection is used on the standard scramble line; some parties carry a short hand-line ("panic rope") to belay themselves or teammates through the crux summit chimney or on any loose downclimbing.
routes.proNeeds: None required for the standard Class 2-3 line — it is unroped scrambling. A short hand-line for the summit chimney/any loose sections is a common precaution rather than a necessity.
whatToBring: bug protection (biting insects reported near the summit in August)
extra water — few reliable sources high on the route
layers for the exposed ridge and summit
proTips: Early/mid-season, traveling the summit snow finger with an ice axe is often faster and more enjoyable than the loose talus alternative beside it.
From a Glacier Lake camp, aim for the largest pond above the creek and stay on its right (east) side, where a boot path leads up to the gully and white slab section below the ridge.
Many parties combine Chikamin Peak with nearby Lemah Thumb via the same Glacier Lake/Chikamin Lake basin approach.
watchOut: Loose, blocky rock on the upper mountain, especially on the harder east-ridge descent variant some parties have used.
Topo maps can be misleading about which gully/ridge feature is which near the summit; route-finding errors above the PCT are common.
Wet, damp rock slabs where the route leaves the PCT become slick and are a fall hazard.
knownHazards: A steep residual snow finger below the summit block can persist into summer and calls for an ice axe and self-arrest ability.
The final summit chimney and adjacent scrambling are exposed Class 3 (occasionally Class 4), and some parties have used a hand-line for security.
The approach is a long, remote multi-mile trip through the Alpine Lakes Wilderness with little to no cell coverage.

pitchDetail.notes: Mineral Creek Trail from the trailhead (~2,375 ft) to the PCT junction near the Park Lakes: ~5.4 mi, ~2,570 ft gain, log/rock-hop crossing of the Kachess River near the start.
Northbound on the Pacific Crest Trail along the west side of Chikamin Ridge to roughly 5,500 ft, where the route leaves the trail.
Off-trail slabs, heather benches, and a broadening gully leading up onto the crest of Chikamin Ridge.
A short (~10 ft), well-protected chimney/slot followed by ~75 ft of easy scrambling to the summit.

road.name: Forest Road 46 / Cooper Lake Road (via Salmon la Sac Road, SR 903)
road.status: Generally good, well-maintained gravel road passable to passenger cars, with some potholes reported in the final couple of miles before the trailhead.
road.driveNote: From I-90 at Cle Elum, take SR 903 north about 17 miles through Roslyn to Salmon la Sac, then Forest Road 46 (Cooper Lake Road) about 10 miles to the signed Mineral Creek Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone (Alpine Lakes Wilderness, central WA Cascades)
climate.typical: Cool, wet winters with heavy snowpack transition to a short, mostly dry summer window; stable Pacific high-pressure weather typically dominates by July.

bySeason.spring: Deep, lingering snowpack on the approach and route, with elevated avalanche hazard on steep slopes and in basins.
bySeason.summer: Prime scrambling season; most of the route is snow-free by mid-to-late July, with occasional afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Can remain climbable into October in dry years, but earlier snow and shorter daylight raise the stakes on the long approach.
bySeason.winter: Access road and approach trail are typically snow-covered; the objective becomes a winter mountaineering/ski undertaking rather than a scramble.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness, jointly managed with Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required at the trailhead ($5 day-use or $30 annual).
access.permit: Free, self-issue Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit required for all visitors May 15 through October 31, available at the trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass, plus the free self-issue wilderness permit.

timing.recommendedStart: An alpine/pre-dawn start is recommended for a single-day car-to-car push given the length of the approach.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead (2,375 ft) to PCT junction near Park Lakes
sectionBreakdown.note: ~5.4 mi, ~2,570 ft gain; log/rock-hop crossing of the Kachess River near the start.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Park Lakes to ~5,500 ft below Chikamin Ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Northbound on the PCT along the west side of Chikamin Ridge.
Off-trail scrambling on slabs, a gully, and the summit chimney.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff via KITTCOM — 911 for emergencies; (509) 925-8534 non-emergency dispatch.
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, 803 W. 2nd St, Cle Elum, WA; (509) 852-1100.
emergency.nearestHospital: Cle Elum Community Hospital (formerly Roslyn-Cle Elum Hospital), Cle Elum, WA, with higher-level care available at Kittitas Valley Healthcare in Ellensburg, WA.
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness terrain with little to no cell coverage; Kittitas County Search and Rescue (an all-volunteer group formed in 1991) is the primary responding agency and has used satellite communicators on rescues in this area.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 6,992 ft is off slightly from the figure supported by multiple independent sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn) of 6,994 ft; this route's highPointFt uses the corrected 6,994 ft.

blurb: Chikamin Peak (6,994 ft) is the highest point on Chikamin Ridge in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Cascades, about six miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass, on land split between the Okanogan-Wenatchee and Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forests. The peak sits above a cluster of alpine lakes (Chikamin, Glacier, and the Park Lakes) along the Pacific Crest Trail corridor, in rugged, glacially-carved terrain typical of the range. It was first climbed May 30, 1915 by C.G. Morrison and I.J. Kohler; the name "Chikamin" comes from the Chinook Jargon word for "money" or "metal," officially adopted in 1916.

objHaz: Loose and blocky rock on the upper gully/slab and ridge sections
Class 3-4 exposure skirting cliff bands below the summit ridge
Persistent snow patches in the gullies into midsummer
Avalanche terrain if attempted in winter/spring
Steep bushwhacking and faint tread between the road and Hardscrabble Lakes
Long, remote, low-traffic route with little chance of encountering other parties
routes.overview: The standard south-side line climbs from the gated Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road up to Hardscrabble Lakes, then ascends steep gully and granite-slab terrain north/northwest to gain Big Snow Mountain's broad east-northeast ridge, which is followed to the summit.
routes.beta: Regarded as the 'preferred' approach to this seldom-visited scramble peak, the Hardscrabble Lakes line avoids the worst brush of the alternate Myrtle Lake/Snowflake Lake route on the NW side but still demands real route-finding: a steep, sometimes-faint boot path climbs from the old road to Lower and Upper Hardscrabble Lakes, after which climbers pick a way up broken gullies, granite slabs, and heather benches to the ridge crest. Most parties treat the roughly 7-mile road-walk or bike beyond the Dingford Creek gate as part of the day, making this a long outing even though the technical difficulty rarely exceeds class 2-3 (occasional class 3-4 moves have been reported skirting upper cliff bands).
routes.approach: From North Bend, drive the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road (FR 56) to its gate at the Dingford Creek Trailhead (roughly 18 miles from North Bend, rough gravel in sections). Continue on foot or by bike along the closed road about 7 miles to a boot path for Hardscrabble Lakes, which begins roughly half a mile below the old Hardscrabble Horse Camp. Climb the steep, sometimes hard-to-follow path to Lower Hardscrabble Lake (~4,000 ft), continue past Upper Hardscrabble Lake, and leave the trail there to begin the scramble.
routes.descent: Descend the ascent route in reverse.
routes.descentText: There is no separate descent line; parties down-climb the same gullies, slabs, and ridge used on the ascent back to Hardscrabble Lakes, then retrace the boot path and road-walk/bike back to the Dingford Creek Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done car-to-car in a single long day; some parties bike the closed-road section to save time, and an overnight camp at Hardscrabble or Myrtle/Big Snow Lakes can convert the trip into a more relaxed two-day outing.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward but long: reverse the ascent gully/ridge line back to Hardscrabble Lakes and the old road. No rappel or shortcut bail points are documented on this route.
routes.turnaround: Given the long road approach and unmarked, route-finding-heavy scramble terrain, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to reverse the gully/slab sections and complete the multi-mile road-walk/bike out before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in mid-to-late summer, roughly July through September, once snow has cleared from the upper gullies and slabs; snow patches can linger into July, and the route crosses avalanche terrain that makes winter/spring ascents hazardous.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed protection or roped climbing is used on the standard line; a helmet is the main protective item given loose rock on the upper mountain. Parties crossing lingering snow patches in the gullies may want an ice axe and, in early season, light traction.
whatToBring: Plenty of water (little reliable water above Hardscrabble Lakes)
Self-issue wilderness permit, filled out at the trailhead
proTips: Biking the closed road beyond the Dingford Creek gate saves significant time and energy versus walking it.
The boot path between Lower and Upper Hardscrabble Lakes is faint in places, so expect some route-finding.
The upper granite slabs are low-angle but can be slick when wet or lichen-covered.
watchOut: The route has no maintained trail above Hardscrabble Lakes and sees few parties, so route-finding mistakes are easy to make.
Loose, blocky rock is present on the upper gullies and ridge.
Snow can linger in the couloirs into summer, adding fall hazard on firm morning snow.
knownHazards: Class 3-4 scrambling moves have been reported skirting cliff bands below the summit ridge.
Steep brush and boulder fields make the Hardscrabble Lakes trail slow and occasionally hard to follow.
The gully sections cross avalanche terrain, making winter/spring travel hazardous.

pitchDetail.notes: Dingford Creek Trailhead gate to the Hardscrabble Lakes boot path start, via ~7 miles of closed FR 56 (bike-friendly).
Steep, sometimes-faint boot path to Lower and Upper Hardscrabble Lakes (~4,000 ft).
Gully and granite-slab terrain north/northwest from Hardscrabble Lakes, skirting cliff bands, to gain the east/northeast ridge.
Follow the broad ridge over talus and slab to the summit.

road.name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road (Forest Road 56)
road.status: Open to (high-clearance recommended) vehicles as far as the Dingford Creek Trailhead gate; closed to motor vehicles beyond that point.
road.seasonalGate: The gate at the Dingford Creek Trailhead is a standing vehicle closure, not a seasonal one; the road has undergone multi-year paving and washout repair.
road.driveNote: From North Bend, follow FR 56 roughly 18 miles to the Dingford Creek Trailhead gate; expect a rough, sometimes washboarded gravel/paved surface with river-rock sections.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone / NWS West Slopes of the Central Cascades
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and drier summers typical of the west-slope Central Cascades; the route's gullies and upper slabs can hold snow into midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack lingers in the approach gullies; avalanche terrain makes this a poor season without avalanche skills and gear.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); still expect isolated snow patches on north-facing sections into July.
bySeason.fall: Good scrambling conditions into September/October with shorter daylight hours and higher chance of early-season snow.
bySeason.winter: Heavy Cascade snowpack and avalanche hazard make this a winter mountaineering/ski objective for experienced parties only.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District - Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent interagency pass) required for trailhead parking.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for day and overnight use, May 15-Oct 31.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road is gated at the Dingford Creek Trailhead - no motor vehicle access beyond the gate; dispersed camping along FR 56/5640 is prohibited through Dec 31, 2026.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Dingford Creek gate to Hardscrabble Lakes boot path
sectionBreakdown.note: ~7 miles of closed road; a bike is commonly used to cut this time.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Boot path start to Hardscrabble Lakes and back
sectionBreakdown.note: Reported as ~9.8 mi round trip with ~4,100 ft gain from the Hardscrabble trailhead to the lakes.
Gully/slab scrambling with route-finding; reported as the technical crux of the day.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (routes to King County Sheriff's Office, which coordinates SAR)
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest - Skykomish Ranger District: (360) 677-2414; Snoqualmie Ranger District: (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA

routes.corrections: Peak elevation was given as 6,682 ft; USGS/GNIS, Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and listsofjohn.com consistently list 6,680 ft - corrected to 6,680 ft. No source uses the literal name 'South Route.' The Class 2-3 grade in the given data most closely matches the standard approach from the south via the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road and Hardscrabble Lakes, ascending the peak's east/northeast ridge - distinct from the alternate Dingford Creek-Myrtle Lake-Snowflake Lake approach on the NW side (which some sources call the 'North Slope' route). Mileage and elevation-gain figures vary noticeably by source (12-16 mi round trip, 4,000-5,300 ft gain) depending on whether the ~7-mile road-walk/bike beyond the Dingford Creek gate is counted; figures used here follow Peakbagger's commonly cited 16 mi / 5,300 ft for the full car-to-car outing.

blurb: Big Snow Mountain (6,680 ft) is a remote, trail-less summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Middle Fork Snoqualmie drainage, about 7 miles north of Snoqualmie Pass in King County. Its upper slopes are known among Cascades scramblers for extensive granite slabs, talus, and heather benches reached only by cross-country travel from Hardscrabble or Myrtle/Big Snow Lakes. A Mountaineers party made the first recorded ascent in 1917, and the peak still sees relatively few visitors given its long, route-finding-intensive approaches.

waypoints.name: East Fork Foss River / Necklace Valley Trailhead

objHaz: Unstable 19th-century lateral moraine below the summit
Early-season snow/ice in the La Bohn Gap area (ice axe may be needed)
Fog/whiteout can obscure cairns and route-finding through boulder fields
East Fork Foss River footlog crossing early in the approach
routes.face: Southwest/west slopes above La Bohn Lakes
routes.overview: An off-trail talus scramble up La Bohn Peak's southwest slopes from the La Bohn Lakes basin, crossing a broad talus shoulder to a false summit before a short, airy ridge walk to the true summit.
routes.beta: From a camp at La Bohn Lakes or La Bohn Gap, climbers ascend talus on the west/southwest side of the peak. Trip reports describe crossing to a false summit just short of the true top, with roughly the final 1/8 mile being a walk along the summit ridge. The line is entirely off-trail Class 2-3 talus with no technical climbing required in summer conditions.
routes.approach: Start at the East Fork Foss River (Necklace Valley) trailhead (1,650 ft) off Foss River Road (FR 68) near Skykomish. Follow the Necklace Valley Trail roughly 5 miles of easy forest walking, then a steep, rocky ~2,400 ft climb over about 3 miles into the Necklace Valley lake basin (Jade, Emerald, Opal Lakes). Continue up-valley past Necklace Basin and ascend the La Bohn Creek drainage on a boot path to Lower and Upper La Bohn Lake; from the upper lakes it is roughly 950 ft of gain in about 0.5 mile over boulders and boot path to La Bohn Gap, from which the southwest slopes of the peak are climbed.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back down the southwest talus slopes to La Bohn Lakes/La Bohn Gap.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same talus slopes used on ascent. Some parties descend via the large lateral moraine beneath the peak, which multiple trip reports describe as loose and tedious footing; staying on established boot paths where visible reduces rockfall risk to parties below. In poor visibility, cairns across the boulder fields near La Bohn Gap can be hard to follow, so note terrain features on the way up.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a 2-day trip: Day 1 hike from the East Fork Foss trailhead to a camp at La Bohn Lakes (roughly 7+ hours with overnight pack, per trip reports). Day 2: climb the southwest slopes to the summit and return to camp, then hike out (some parties combine the summit with Mt. Hinman, which shares the same approach).
routes.bail: There is no technical descent to rig; retreat simply means reversing the talus slopes back toward La Bohn Gap/La Bohn Lakes at any point.
routes.turnaround: Given the length of the approach (20+ miles round trip from the trailhead) and off-trail talus terrain, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to redescend the valley trail before dark, and be prepared to bail from the summit slopes if fog or weather moves in and cairns become hard to follow.
routes.bestSeason: Most parties climb July through September, after the Necklace Valley trail and La Bohn Gap area have lost most of their snow; early-season ascents can involve a snow-filled gap/couloir near La Bohn Gap that some reports note requires an ice axe.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is used on this Class 2-3 talus scramble; carry an ice axe (and know how to self-arrest) if climbing early season while snow lingers near La Bohn Gap.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Pass, or Trail Park Pass (day-use fee)
Free self-issued Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit (available at trailhead, required May 15-Oct 31)
Map and compass/GPS for off-trail sections
Ample water (little reliable water above the lakes)
Warm layers for the exposed summit ridge
proTips: Break up the long approach with a camp at La Bohn Lakes rather than attempting a single very long day from the trailhead.
Use the west-side talus and skirt the false summit rather than tackling it head-on, per multiple trip reports.
Carry an ice axe on early-season trips in case snow remains in the La Bohn Gap approach.
watchOut: Fog/low cloud is common on the exposed talus benches near La Bohn Gap and can make cairns hard to follow.
The lateral moraine below the peak is loose, unstable underfoot, and tedious to descend.
The approach is long and strenuous (20+ miles round trip with several thousand feet of gain) — factor this into turnaround planning.
knownHazards: Extended off-trail travel over talus and boulder fields with route-finding challenges, especially in poor visibility.
Loose rock on the moraine and talus slopes beneath the summit.
Possible lingering snow/ice in the La Bohn Gap area into early summer.

road.name: Foss River Road (Forest Road 68)
road.status: Paved/gravel, generally open to the trailhead; periodic construction-related delays reported in recent years
road.driveNote: From US Highway 2 near Skykomish, follow Foss River Road (FR 68) about 4.1 miles south to the Necklace Valley/East Fork Foss trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Stevens Pass zone (nearest regional avalanche/mountain forecast reference)
climate.typical: Typically snow-free and passable July through September; snow can linger into early summer around La Bohn Gap.

bySeason.spring: Trail and upper basin largely snow-covered; approach requires snow travel skills and possibly an ice axe.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep); talus slopes generally snow-free for scrambling.
bySeason.fall: Shortening daylight and a chance of early snowfall by October; otherwise similar to late summer conditions.
bySeason.winter: Heavy Cascade snowpack makes the approach a multi-day snow/glacier-travel undertaking; not a standard season for this route.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (206) 296-3311
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288, (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett (nearest full-service hospital; roughly 1.5-2 hour drive)
emergency.notes: Remote, multi-hour approach (7+ hours one-way to La Bohn Lakes); cell coverage is unreliable to absent in the Necklace Valley/La Bohn Lakes basin.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (6,587 ft) does not match reputable sources: Wikipedia, Peakbagger, PeakVisor, and ListsOfJohn all list La Bohn Peak at 6,585 ft (2,007 m). Corrected highPointFt to 6,585 ft; consider updating the peak's elevationFt to match.

blurb: La Bohn Peak is a 6,585-foot summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Cascades, rising above La Bohn Lakes at the head of Necklace Valley in King County, Washington, about 1.7 miles west of higher neighbor Mount Hinman. Its granite slopes and surrounding lake basins were sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation; runoff from the south side feeds the Snoqualmie River while the north side drains to the Foss River. No documented first-ascent party exists for the peak, which is reached almost exclusively via the long Necklace Valley approach from the East Fork Foss River trailhead.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Robert Craig, and William Ford (1940)
routes.rappels: 2-3 rappels (a single 60m/half rope works) from near the summit down the East Ridge notch/gully back into the basin; the lowest anchor is reported to be tucked back in brush and easy to miss
objHaz: reported runout above the nominal 5.6 crux
routes.overview: A 6-pitch, roughly 550-ft low-5th-class rock climb up the striking west arete of Mount Thomson to a narrow, exposed summit; widely regarded as one of the best moderate alpine-rock outings near Snoqualmie Pass.
routes.beta: From the alpine basin south of the peak, contour to the toe of the West Ridge and rope up. The line follows the crest for roughly six pitches (low 5th class, crux around 5.6) through a chimney, an exposed ramp below some intimidating roofs, dirty tree ledges, and a hanging slab, before a Class 3-4 slab traverse leads to a false summit; from there drop to a notch and climb one final pitch to the true summit. Protection is generally mediocre and the route-finding wanders, so expect some exposure and loose rock along the way.
routes.approach: From I-90 take Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass to the PCT North (Pacific Crest Trail 2000) trailhead. Hike the PCT north past Commonwealth Basin and the Kendall Katwalk to Ridge Lake, then continue about 0.4 mile beyond the lake to a steep climbers' path that leaves the PCT and climbs to Bumblebee Pass (~5,400 ft). Descend the north side into the basin below Mount Thomson's south/west aspect, then contour northwest/west toward the base of the West Ridge (~5,960 ft) to rope up.
routes.descent: Descend the East Ridge: scramble down from the true summit, cross back over or around the false summit, and make 2-3 rappels through a notch/gully to regain the basin, then reverse the approach over Bumblebee Pass to the PCT.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, downclimb/scramble toward the East Ridge and make a series of rappels (parties report anywhere from one longer double-rope rappel to three shorter ones) to get back down into easier terrain; the final anchor has been described as hard to spot, tucked into brush. Once off the technical ground, Class 3 scrambling on slabs and talus leads back to the basin beneath the south face, from which the approach route is reversed over Bumblebee Pass to the PCT and out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a long single day car-to-car (roughly 13-14 hours total for the round trip, including the ~5-6 mile, ~2,900-3,600 ft approach each way), but many parties instead backpack in and camp at Ridge Lake or in the basin below the peak, splitting the trip into an approach/camp day and a summit day; that approach typically takes ~4-5 hours to camp, with another ~8-9 hours for the round trip to the summit and back to camp.
routes.turnaround: Because the route is a long approach followed by a fully committing multi-pitch ridge with limited fast retreat, set a hard turnaround time based on your pace on the approach; the Cascades are prone to afternoon thunderstorms in summer, and being caught on the exposed ridge or during the rappel descent in a storm is a serious hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Most reliable July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared from the approach basin and ridge; the route can still hold snow into June in a heavy year, and many recorded ascents are done in August and September. Afternoon thunderstorms are a recurring summer hazard, so an early start is standard practice.
routes.detailedRack: A light traditional rack suffices: a full set of nuts plus cams from finger-size up to about #2-3 (roughly 3 inches), with extra slings/runners for the many mediocre, wandering placements along the ridge crest. A single 60m rope and helmets are standard.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is described by multiple parties as generally mediocre and sparse in places; a doubled set of small-to-medium cams and nuts with generous extra slings covers most of the route, but expect some runout between placements.
whatToBring: extra water (limited reliable sources on the ridge)
wind layer for the exposed crest
bivy/camp gear if overnighting at Ridge Lake or the basin
Northwest Forest Pass or self-issued wilderness permit
proTips: Rope up at the toe of the West Ridge rather than lower in the basin, since the initial scrambling to reach it is loose and unroped.
Start early - the route is popular in summer and belays can back up behind other parties.
Bring extra slings; the mediocre, wandering protection eats gear faster than the grade suggests.
Carry a topo or a recent trip report - the line wanders across several ledge systems and it's easy to stray off-route.
watchOut: Loose blocks and dirty ledges show up on several pitches, especially early in the season.
The lowest rappel anchor on the East Ridge descent is tucked into brush and easy to walk past.
Some parties have found an unexpectedly stiff, runout move above the nominal 5.6 crux (reported as feeling closer to 5.8).
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Washington Cascades in July and August.
knownHazards: Loose rock and generally mediocre, sparse protection are the primary objective hazards on the ridge.
Route-finding is nontrivial: a false summit partway along the ridge has led parties off-route.
The East Ridge descent involves scrambling plus rappels whose anchors can be difficult to locate.

pitchDetail.notes: Exposed step out over the gully on the left side, continue up to a right-trending chimney feature, then to trees on the left and a ledge.
Wander up an exposed ramp to the right, then back left and up toward unlikely-looking roofs - the physical crux of the route.
Up a short wall (a fixed/stuck cam has been reported near the top), then dirty tree ledges and steps to a belay at the base of a hanging slab.
Float up the top of the slab, climb over a bush/tree, then right and up through steeper, exposed 5.5 moves.
Class 3-4 traverse of a large slab toward the false summit.
From the false summit, drop into a notch, then climb one final roped pitch of ledges and cracks to the true summit.

road.driveNote: From I-90 take Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass, turn north, and take the first right into the Pacific Crest Trail 2000 (PCT North) trailhead parking lot.

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with wet, heavy winter snow and avalanche hazard on the approach terrain; summers are notably drier, and July-September is the most reliable window for this rock route.

bySeason.spring: Snow typically still covers the upper approach and basin into June
bySeason.summer: July-August are the driest, warmest months and the primary climbing season, with periodic afternoon thunderstorms
bySeason.fall: September can still offer good conditions but the window narrows with the first fall storms
bySeason.winter: Heavy wet snow with significant avalanche hazard on the approach; the route is out of condition

routes.corrections: Peak elevation is 6,554 ft per USGS/Wikipedia (given data listed 6,550 ft). Route pitch count and length were unset in the given data; Mountain Project and multiple trip reports confirm 6 pitches, ~550 ft, NCCS Grade II. First ascent of the West Ridge (Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Robert Craig, William Ford, 1940) was unset in the given data and has been added; this is distinct from the peak's overall first ascent (Joe Hazard and B. French, 1917, via the easier East Ridge).

blurb: Mount Thomson (6,554 ft) is a sharp, pyramidal summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Cascades, about 4-5 miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass on Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest land. Built of Keechelus andesite, its precipitous, Matterhorn-like profile is a well-known landmark from the Pacific Crest Trail and neighboring summits. The peak was first climbed in 1917 by Joe Hazard and B. French (via the easier East Ridge); its technical West Ridge was first climbed in 1940 by Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Robert Craig, and William Ford.

objHaz: River ford of the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River
Dense brush and downed timber below treeline
Loose talus and scree on the upper mountain
Steep snow and a moat in the gully (early-to-mid season)
Difficult route-finding on an unmaintained, seldom-traveled line
Remote setting with slow potential rescue access
routes.face: North side, via the northeast gully
routes.overview: A remote, unmaintained Class 3 scramble up Burnt Boot Peak's north side, crossing the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River and climbing brush, talus, and a snow/scree gully to the small, sharp summit.
routes.beta: This is the peak's standard (easiest) route, reached from the Dutch Miller Gap Trail. After a long approach and a cold river ford, parties climb through brush and talus on the peak's north side to about 5,400 ft, then ascend a gully (reported as a 'northeast gully') that holds a steep snow ramp and a moat into mid-summer, before boulder-hopping the final talus to the summit. Trip reports describe it as seldom visited, with real route-finding and bushwhacking involved; a neighboring northwest gully is reported to involve Class 4 terrain and should be avoided.
routes.approach: From North Bend, drive Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road (Forest Road 56, rough/high-clearance) roughly 17-18 miles to the Dutch Miller Gap/Dingford Creek Trailhead. Hike the Dutch Miller Gap Trail east; parties typically break the approach at Hardscrabble Horse Camp (about 7.5 miles and roughly 1,400 ft of gain from the trailhead, on a trail closed to bikes past this point). From near the horse camp, leave the trail, ford the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River (reported thigh-deep and cold), then work up through brush and talus on the peak's north side toward the base of the summit gully.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the gully and talus back to the river ford, re-cross the Middle Fork Snoqualmie, and return via the Dutch Miller Gap Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descend the same northeast gully and talus slopes used on the ascent, taking care on loose rock and any lingering snow/moat sections. Re-ford the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River (conditions can change through the day with snowmelt), then follow the brush/talus line back to the Dutch Miller Gap Trail and out to Hardscrabble Horse Camp and the Dingford Creek Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-3 day backpack given the length of the approach: Day 1 hike in to Hardscrabble Horse Camp (or nearby); Day 2 climb Burnt Boot Peak round-trip from camp (river ford, gully, summit, return to camp); Day 3 hike out. Some parties combine it with nearby Big Snow Mountain.
routes.bail: Retreat is essentially the ascent line in reverse - there is no shortcut once in the gully. If conditions deteriorate, downclimb the gully/talus back toward the river ford and return to the Dutch Miller Gap Trail.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote approach and required river ford, turn around if the ford looks unsafely high or fast, if the gully holds hard snow/ice without proper gear, or if your pace will put the river crossing or brushy sections after dark.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most favorable weather; sources note significant snow can linger in the summit gully (with a moat) into late July, and the maritime Cascade snowpack keeps avalanche and unstable-snow hazard high outside summer.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed on the standard Class 3 line; it is a scramble, not a roped climb.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed or removable rock protection is typically placed on the standard route. Carry an ice axe and helmet for the snow gully and loose rock; a short section of steep snow/moat may warrant extra caution or a rope for less experienced parties in early season.
whatToBring: Spare footwear or water shoes for the river ford
Map, compass, or GPS - route is unmaintained and brushy
Extra layers and food for a long, remote day
proTips: Split the long approach over two days by camping at Hardscrabble Horse Camp
Ford the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River earlier in the day when flows are lower
Stay in the northeast gully - the northwest gully has reported Class 4 sections
Expect a snow moat in the gully around 6,000-6,200 ft into mid/late summer
watchOut: The Middle Fork Snoqualmie River ford can be thigh-deep, cold, and hazardous during snowmelt or after rain
Steep snow and a moat can guard the gully well into July
Loose talus and scree on the upper mountain
This is an extremely seldom-traveled route - expect real route-finding difficulty and brush
knownHazards: Cold, thigh-deep ford of the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River
Steep snow and a moat in the northeast gully, especially early-to-mid season
Loose rock and talus on the approach and gully
Dense brush and downed timber below treeline

road.name: Forest Road 56 (Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road)
road.status: Rough; high-clearance/4WD recommended for the final miles to the trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Road is closed and gated at the Dutch Miller Gap/Dingford Creek Trailhead (end of the drivable road)
road.driveNote: From North Bend, I-90 exit 34, then Middle Fork Road/FR 56 roughly 17-18 miles to the Dutch Miller Gap/Dingford Creek Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: Nearest NWAC zones are Snoqualmie Pass and West Slopes Central (Skagit River to south of I-90)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascade climate with heavy winter snowfall; the drier July-September window is best for climbing

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; high avalanche danger and dangerous, high-volume river fords from snowmelt
bySeason.summer: Best conditions July-September; snow can linger in the gully into July, talus mostly bare by August
bySeason.fall: Generally stable but with shortening days; early-season snow possible by October
bySeason.winter: Heavy, wet maritime snowfall with high avalanche hazard; road access likely snowed in

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Sources conflict: one Forest Service trail page states a Recreation Pass is required at the Dutch Miller Gap Trailhead, while the Forest Service's own Dingford Creek Trailhead site page states no fee is required. Carrying a Northwest Forest Pass is a reasonable precaution.

access.closures: FR 56 is gated at the trailhead; the trail is closed to motorized vehicles, and bicycles are not allowed beyond Hardscrabble Horse Camp

timing.recommendedStart: Early/alpine start given the long approach and to time the river ford before afternoon snowmelt raises flows

routes.corrections: Peak elevation is given here as 6,483 ft, but Wikipedia lists 6,540 ft (1,993 m, USGS Big Snow Mountain quad) and a 1970s American Alpine Club report refers to the peak as 'P 6480' - sources disagree by roughly 50-60 ft, likely reflecting different survey/rounding eras; this route entry uses 6,540 ft (Wikipedia) for highPointFt. Separately, sources disagree on the year of the technical North Ridge route's first ascent (Wikipedia: June 1971; AAC: 1972) by the same party (Williamson, Oas, Bucher) - that 5th-class North Ridge is a distinct, harder line from this Class 3 scramble route and its FA is not attributed here.

blurb: Burnt Boot Peak is a 6,483–6,540 ft (sources vary) summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Cascade Range, about 6.5 miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass in King County and managed by the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. It rises above the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River, one mile east of the Cascade crest, built of granite and known as one of the range's most seldom-visited peaks due to a required river ford and brushy, unmaintained approach. The peak was first climbed in August 1963 by Phil Weiser and Clarke Stockwell; a separate, harder technical line up its North Ridge (5th-class granite) was first climbed by Don Williamson, Tom Oas, and Bill Bucher in the early 1970s.

objHaz: lingering snow in gullies into early summer
exposure near the cliffy ridge-crest points
routes.overview: A non-technical scramble up the southwest-facing slopes and gully systems of Four Brothers to the ridge crest and summit, staying off the cliffy, technical northeast side of the formation that faces Chikamin Pass and Glacier Lake.
routes.beta: Four Brothers is a cluster of four rocky points on Chikamin Ridge. Reports on the adjacent, better-documented Chikamin Peak route describe the ridge-crest side of Four Brothers as presenting 'very difficult (and technical) obstacles,' and parties headed for Chikamin Peak deliberately detour around it via steep grassy slopes and gully systems well south of Chikamin Pass. The Southwest Route on Four Brothers itself follows this gentler southwest aspect rather than the ridge crest.
routes.approach: Two approaches are documented for this area. (1) From the PCT North Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass (I-90 Exit 52), hike the Pacific Crest Trail north past the Commonwealth Basin junction (~2.5 mi) and Kendall Katwalk to the Ridge Lake/Gravel Lake saddle (~7.6 mi, ~5,195 ft), then leave the trail to gain Chikamin Ridge and traverse toward Four Brothers. (2) Via the Gold Creek Trail (#1314) from the Gold Creek Pond trailhead (I-90 Exit 54) to Alaska Lake (~5 mi one-way) and a gully up to the ridge crest from the southwest/Gold Creek side — note this approach is currently affected by a seasonal Gold Creek area closure (see access).
routes.descent: Reverse the southwest slope/gully line used on ascent.
routes.descentText: Descend the same southwest-facing gullies and slopes climbed on the way up, staying off the cliffy northeast ridge-crest side of the Four Brothers formation. In early season, glissading may be possible in snow-filled gullies; in low-snow conditions expect loose talus and route-finding among several similar-looking gully options.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same southwest gully/slope system used for the ascent rather than attempting to cross toward the ridge-crest side of the formation, which guidebook sources describe as cliffy and technical.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-mile trail approach before the off-trail scrambling even begins, set a turnaround based on remaining daylight to reverse both the ridge scrambling and the long trail hike out, and turn back promptly if afternoon thunderstorms build over the exposed ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Because of the long trail approach and persistent high-elevation snow, this area is typically only a reasonable day-climb objective from early-to-mid July through October; snow can linger in the ridge gullies into early summer in heavy-snowpack years.
proTips: Parties aiming for Chikamin Peak are advised to go at least a third of a mile past Chikamin Pass on the PCT before leaving the trail, to reach gully systems that bypass the cliffy Four Brothers formation entirely — useful context for staying off the technical ridge-crest side of Four Brothers itself.

From Park Lakes (east-side approach via Mineral Creek), ascend the saddle southwest of the upper lake, then follow heather and slab slopes to Chikamin Ridge just northwest of its saddle with Four Brothers.
watchOut: The ridge-crest side of Four Brothers, facing Chikamin Pass and Glacier Lake, is made of four cliffy points described in guidebook sources as presenting 'very difficult (and technical) obstacles' — this is not the scrambling line.
Multiple similar-looking gully systems drop off the ridge; confirm you're descending the same line you ascended.
knownHazards: Loose rock and talus in the approach gullies.
Snow can linger in shaded, ridge-facing gullies into early summer.
The northeast/ridge-crest aspect of the formation is technical, cliffy terrain distinct from the southwest scrambling line.

road.name: I-90 to Snoqualmie Pass, PCT North Trailhead (Exit 52); alternate Forest Road 4832 / Gold Creek Road 142 to Gold Creek Trailhead (Exit 54)
road.status: Paved highway to both trailheads; final approach to the Gold Creek Trailhead includes a short stretch of unimproved forest road
road.seasonalGate: The Gold Creek Trail/area (alternate southwest-side approach via Alaska Lake) is closed seasonally April 1–November 30 (through at least 2027-2028) for a stream restoration project; the PCT Snoqualmie Pass trailhead is the reliable summer approach
road.driveNote: PCT North Trailhead: I-90 Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass. Gold Creek Trailhead: I-90 Exit 54, then north on FR 4832 (~1 mi) and left on Gold Creek Road 142 (~0.3 mi) to the Gold Creek Pond parking lot.

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC avalanche/weather zone)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate; Pacific weather fronts drive heavy orographic precipitation and wet, heavy snow on west-facing slopes, with generally cloudy winters and clearer summers.

bySeason.spring: Snowpack lingers into June/July on shaded, ridge-facing gullies; approach trails are often still snow-covered.
bySeason.summer: Primary scrambling season, generally drier from July through September, with possible afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Often stable weather into October before winter snow returns.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and high avalanche danger; not a scrambling objective in this season (the Glacier Lake Couloir off Four Brothers has been skied/ridden in winter).

access.closures: Gold Creek Trail/area (the southwest-side approach via Alaska Lake) is closed seasonally April 1–November 30 through at least 2027/2028 for a stream restoration project

timing.recommendedStart: An early/alpine start is advisable given the multi-mile trail approach before any ridge scrambling begins
