routes.corrections: Wikipedia and PeakVisor both give Four Brothers' elevation as 6,485 ft (not 6,462 ft as in the existing app data) with coordinates ~47.4656°N, 121.3050°W. Wikipedia's infobox also lists the peak's easiest route as 'Class 2 Southwest slope,' one class easier than the Class 3 in the existing data — though SummitPost/related sources describe the Four Brothers ridge-crest formation as cliffy and technical on its northeast side (which the standard route avoids), so a Class 3 rating is plausible for parts of the southwest gully system depending on the exact line and season/snow conditions.

blurb: Four Brothers is a 6,485-foot summit on Chikamin Ridge in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Washington's central Cascades, about 4 miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass and just southeast of its taller parent, Chikamin Peak. The name refers to four rocky points along the ridge crest, named in the 1890s for prospector brothers John, Tom, Vic, and Lawrie Denny, who had a cabin at the mountain's base. The ridge-crest side of the formation (facing Chikamin Pass and Glacier Lake) is notably cliffy and technical, while its southwest-facing slopes offer the peak's standard scrambling line.

objHaz: Loose rock and scree on the ridge
routes.overview: The South Route climbs Iron Cap's south ridge to the summit, a harder alternative to the standard class 2-3 North Ridge. Parties approach via the West Fork Foss Trail and Chetwoot Lake, then leave the trail to gain the south ridge and follow it north over talus and blocky class 3 terrain to the 6,347-foot summit.
routes.beta: A long backcountry approach (6.6+ trail miles, ~3,400 ft gain to Chetwoot Lake) followed by off-trail cross-country travel and unroped class 3 scrambling on the south ridge. Solid granite in places but loose talus and scree lower down; most parties treat it as a 2-3 day trip with a camp at Big Heart or Chetwoot Lake.
routes.approach: From the West Fork Foss Lakes Trailhead (end of FS Road 6835, elev. ~1,640 ft), follow Trail #1064 about 6.6 miles and roughly 3,400+ ft of gain past Trout, Copper, Little Heart, and Big Heart Lakes to Chetwoot Lake (~4,900 ft) on Iron Cap's west side. Leave the maintained trail here and work cross-country south/southeast toward Iron Cap's south ridge, then follow the ridge crest northward to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the south ridge and cross-country line back down to Chetwoot Lake, then descend Trail #1064 to the West Fork Foss trailhead.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the south ridge's class 3 terrain with care — loose rock is more hazardous descending than ascending. Once back at the ridge base, drop west to regain the Chetwoot Lake basin and pick up Trail #1064 for the maintained hike-out; watch for the correct drainage in fog, as several similar talus gullies exist in this basin.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-3 day backpack: Day 1, hike in from the West Fork Foss trailhead to a camp at Big Heart or Chetwoot Lake (~6.6 mi, ~3,400 ft gain); Day 2, climb the south ridge to the summit and return to camp; Day 3, hike out to the trailhead. Strong parties have also done it as a very long single day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at nearly any point on the south ridge or the cross-country approach by simply reversing the ascent line back to Chetwoot Lake and the maintained trail — no fixed rappels or technical retreat gear are needed.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and off-trail, unmarked ridge terrain, set a firm turnaround time before setting out and stick to it — losing daylight or getting caught by weather on the loose, exposed class 3 ridge or during cross-country travel back to the trail is the main risk on this route.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most favorable conditions; snow typically lingers on the approach and upper ridge into early summer, and the trail and off-trail sections are generally most manageable once snowfields have melted out by mid-to-late July.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is needed — this is an unroped class 3 scramble on talus and blocky rock; a helmet is a reasonable precaution against rockfall on the ridge.
gear: Sturdy boots or trail runners with good traction
Trekking poles for the long approach
Helmet (optional, for loose rock on the ridge)
Map/compass or GPS for off-trail navigation
whatToBring: Free self-issue wilderness permit (available at trailhead)
Overnight backpacking gear if camping at Big Heart or Chetwoot Lake
Water treatment (no potable water at trailhead)
proTips: Fill out the free self-issue Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit at the trailhead before starting
Break the long approach into two days with a camp at Big Heart or Chetwoot Lake
Iron Cap Lake, just below the north side, makes a scenic side trip if time allows
watchOut: Loose rock and talus on the south ridge, especially while descending
Cross-country navigation between Chetwoot Lake and the south ridge is easy to get wrong in poor visibility
The approach alone is 6.6+ miles with 3,400+ ft of gain, making this a very full day if attempted car-to-car
knownHazards: Exposed class 3 scrambling with loose rock on the south ridge
Steep talus and scree slopes above Chetwoot Lake
No maintained trail beyond Chetwoot Lake, raising navigation-error risk in fog or whiteout

road.name: Foss River Road (FS 68) and FS Road 6835
road.status: Paved for about the first 1.1 miles from US-2, then gravel; occasional potholes reported
road.driveNote: From US-2 near Skykomish, go south on Foss River Road (FS 68) about 4.7 miles, then left onto FS Road 6835 for 2.0 miles to the West Fork Foss Lakes Trailhead (about 6.7 miles total from US-2). Maps sometimes show FS 68 continuing to the trailhead incorrectly — watch for the FS 6835 turn, which may be poorly signed.

climate.typical: Wet, temperate Pacific Northwest mountain climate typical of the west-slope Cascades; snow can persist on the approach trail and upper ridge into early summer.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack typically covers the trail and approach into June; not a scramble objective without mountaineering skills and gear.
bySeason.summer: July through September is the primary season; the trail and ridge are generally snow-free by mid-to-late July in an average year.
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures and shortening days; early season snow is possible by October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain; access road conditions deteriorate and the route is a winter mountaineering undertaking, not a scramble.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District — Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5 per vehicle/day or $30 annual day-use fee at the West Fork Foss Lakes Trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass and Interagency Recreation Passes are honored
access.permit: Free self-issue Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit required at the trailhead (available May 15-Oct 31)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass, or pay the day-use fee

timing.recommendedStart: An early or pre-dawn start is recommended given the long approach and off-trail scrambling required to reach and return from the summit.

emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe (Emergency Dept.), 14701 179th Ave SE, Monroe, WA 98272
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach with no expected cell coverage beyond US-2; the self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead records basic trip/contact info for rangers

routes.corrections: Peak data listed elevationFt as 6,349 ft; multiple sources (Wikipedia, ListsOfJohn, PeakVisor) consistently give 6,347 ft — corrected highPointFt to 6,347. gainFt/lossFt for this route are derived from officially sourced trailhead elevation (1,640 ft, USFS) and summit elevation (6,347 ft, Wikipedia/PeakVisor) plus the USFS-sourced trail elevation-loss figure (473 ft one-way on Trail #1064), not a single source's stated total route gain — treat as an approximate net figure rather than a directly cited stat. No source was found describing a route explicitly named 'South Route,' but multiple trip reports (WTA, NWHikers) independently describe a harder scramble line up Iron Cap's south ridge as an alternative to the easier, standard North Ridge (class 2-3) — the given name/grade is consistent with that South Ridge line and was kept.

blurb: Iron Cap Mountain (6,347 ft) rises in the western Alpine Lakes Wilderness of King County, Washington, about two miles west-northwest of Dutch Miller Gap and two miles west of the Cascade crest, on land managed by the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. It is a granite peak ringed by cirque lakes (Iron Cap and Chetwoot Lakes among them) with over 2,500 feet of relief above the Middle Fork Snoqualmie valley in under a mile. Wikipedia credits the first ascent to a 1925 Mountaineers party led by Joe Hazard; the class 2-3 North Ridge is the standard/easiest line, with a harder South Ridge scramble as an alternative.

routes.rappels: Most parties rope up and rappel the exposed summit block (60m rope recommended to reach the ground in one pull); an additional rappel/anchor station also exists lower on the east ridge for parties wanting extra protection on descent.
routes.fa: Charles Hazelhurst and Thomas Acheson, May 1915 (first ascent of the peak via its original, and for decades only, line — approached from the west via the future PCT corridor). A separate, more technical West Face line was first climbed in September 2001.
routes.overview: The historic line up Huckleberry Mountain follows the Pacific Crest Trail roughly 10 miles north from Snoqualmie Pass, then leaves the trail to scramble the peak's ridge/gully system to a small, exposed summit block guarded by class 3-4 terrain with a short low-5th-class finish.
routes.beta: There is no true walk-up to Huckleberry's summit. The mountain's original (and for decades only) route climbs class 3-4 terrain along the ridge crest with a short, exposed low-5th-class step to top out the summit block; two later technical lines — a 5.7 South Face and a 5.6 West Face first climbed in 2001 — are more sustained rock routes. Nearly all parties, including strong scramblers, bring a rope and light rack to protect and rappel the final block.
routes.approach: From the Pacific Crest Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass (I-90 Exit 52, ~3,000 ft), follow the PCT north (the Commonwealth Basin cutoff saves about a mile over staying on the PCT the whole way) up switchbacks to Kendall Katwalk (~5,400 ft, about 6 miles in, ~2,700 ft gain), then continue past Ridge Lake, Gravel Lake, and Alaska Pass through Huckleberry Flats to the base of the peak, roughly 10 miles from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line, rappelling the exposed summit block (60m rope) before scrambling back down to the PCT and hiking out the way you came.
routes.descentText: Most parties downclimb or rappel back the way they climbed. A 60-meter rope is recommended to get the summit-block rappel done in one pull; a second rappel/anchor station lower on the east ridge gives parties the option to protect more of the descent. From the base of the peak, retrace the PCT south through Huckleberry Flats, past Gravel and Ridge Lakes and back over Kendall Katwalk to the Snoqualmie Pass trailhead.
routes.itinerary: A long, committing single-day car-to-car push from Snoqualmie Pass runs in the 20+ mile round-trip range; many parties instead backpack in and camp near Ridge Lake or Huckleberry Flats to split the approach over two days and climb the peak on day two before hiking out.
routes.bail: Retreat is easy anywhere along the PCT approach. Above the Katwalk, the simplest bail off the peak itself is to downclimb or rappel back down the same ridge/gully rather than push onto unfamiliar terrain, then rejoin the PCT for the walk out.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on how much daylight remains for both the exposed summit block and the long PCT return; afternoon thunderstorms are common on the Cascade Crest in summer, and the technical section is a bad place to be caught by weather or darkness.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is most commonly cited as prime condition, once seasonal snow has cleared the approach gullies and the rock is dry; the broader window is roughly June through October depending on snowpack, but even in good conditions this is described as a stiff outing.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack is typical: a handful of small-to-medium stoppers, one or two small cams, and several slings for horn/block anchors, plus a 60-meter rope to protect and rappel the summit block. A helmet is recommended given loose rock in the approach gullies.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are modest — small stoppers and cams protect the short technical step to the top of the summit block, and slings back up horns or blocks for the rappel anchor.
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS — route-finding through Huckleberry Flats is easy to lose
Extra water — no reliable water source past Ridge/Gravel Lakes
Sun protection for the long, exposed PCT approach
proTips: Bring a 60m rope even if you plan to free-scramble up — nearly every party rappels the summit block on the way down.
The Commonwealth Basin shortcut off the PCT saves about a mile compared to staying on the PCT the whole way.
Start very early — the PCT trailhead lot fills fast on summer weekends and the round trip is long.
watchOut: The approach gullies below the summit block hold loose, blocky andesite even though the upper rock is generally solid.
The final summit block is exposed low-5th-class climbing, not a simple scramble — underprepared parties should turn around rather than solo it.

At least seven Washington peaks share the name Huckleberry Mountain; confirm you're headed for the Alpine Lakes Wilderness summit between Mount Thomson and Chikamin Peak, not the Suiattle River, Stevens County, or Klickitat County peaks of the same name.
knownHazards: Loose rock in the approach gullies and on ledges below the summit block.
Exposed, largely unroped scrambling terrain leading to a low-5th-class summit step.
Lingering snow in shaded basins into early summer can require an ice axe.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common on the Cascade Crest in late summer.

road.name: I-90 to Snoqualmie Pass PCT Trailhead (Exit 52)
road.status: Paved and open year-round to the trailhead; the trail itself is typically snow-free roughly June through October
road.driveNote: The trailhead lot sits right off I-90 Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass and fills early on summer weekends; overflow parking is limited.

climate.forecastZone: Washington Cascades East Slopes Central / Snoqualmie Pass area (Cascade Crest)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with a heavy winter snowpack; summers are mild and mostly dry with occasional afternoon convective storms

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack typically lingers into June on approach gullies; snow-travel gear needed
bySeason.summer: Prime season, dry rock July-September, but afternoon thunderstorms are possible on the Crest
bySeason.fall: Cool, often stable weather into early-mid October before snow returns; days shorten quickly
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain; not a winter objective

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day at the PCT Trailhead, or use a Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass in lieu of the fee
access.permit: Free self-issue Wilderness Permit required at the trailhead, May 15-Oct 31, for entry into Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency (America the Beautiful) Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Very early (pre-dawn/first light) given trailhead-lot crowding and a 20-plus-mile round trip

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: PCT Trailhead (~3,000 ft) to Kendall Katwalk (~5,400 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 6 miles, ~2,700 ft gain on maintained PCT tread

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Kendall Katwalk to below Huckleberry Mountain
sectionBreakdown.note: About 4 more miles past Ridge Lake, Gravel Lake, and Alaska Pass to reach the base of the peak, roughly 10 miles from the trailhead
Class 3-4 scrambling to an exposed, low-5th-class summit block; most parties rope up and rappel the block on descent

emergency.county: Straddles the King/Kittitas county line on the Cascade Crest; the trailhead and most of the approach lie in Kittitas County
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff non-emergency (509) 962-7525 (King County Sheriff also has jurisdiction on the west side of the Crest); call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office — (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — (425) 831-2300, 24-hour ER
emergency.notes: Remote Cascade Crest terrain — cell coverage along the PCT approach and near the summit is inconsistent; do not count on a phone signal to call for help.

routes.corrections: The catalog lists this route as 'West Route, Class 2-3,' but no source found supports an easy class 2-3 line to Huckleberry Mountain's true summit. Multiple independent sources (Mountain Project, Wikipedia, WTA/Mountaineers-adjacent trip reports, and several detailed trip logs) agree the summit is guarded by exposed class 3-4 scrambling finishing on a low-5th-class step on every documented named line: East Ridge ('Easy 5th class, 1+'), South Face (5.7), and West Face (5.6, FA September 2001). The peak's only route for its first ~90 years (FA Charles Hazelhurst and Thomas Acheson, May 1915) is approached from the west via the PCT but climbs the ridge/gully system on the peak's east side — there is no separately documented route literally named 'West Route,' so precise technical fields (exact rockGrade, pitch count, face/aspect) are left null rather than guessed, since it's unclear which of the three named lines this catalog entry is meant to represent. On elevation, Mountain Project's area page lists 5,878 ft for this peak, which conflicts with the 6,320-6,340 ft cited by Wikipedia and other sources; the higher figure (matching the catalog's given 6,327 ft) is far better corroborated and was kept. Also note at least seven Washington peaks are named Huckleberry Mountain — this is the Alpine Lakes Wilderness summit between Mount Thomson and Chikamin Peak near Snoqualmie Pass, distinct from the Suiattle River/Darrington peak that Washington Trails Association's 'Huckleberry Mountain' hike page (14 mi RT, 5,900 ft high point) actually describes.

blurb: Huckleberry Mountain (6,320+ ft) is a small but striking andesite peak on the Cascade Crest in Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, about 5 miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass between Mount Thomson and Chikamin Peak. It was first climbed in May 1915 by Charles Hazelhurst and Thomas Acheson via what remained the mountain's only known route for nearly 90 years, until a technical west-face line was added in September 2001. Despite its modest elevation, the summit is capped by an exposed, low-fifth-class rock block that most parties rope up and rappel, making it one of the more committing scrambles reached from the Pacific Crest Trail near Snoqualmie Pass.

routes.overview: A short, steep, mostly-boot-path scramble up Snoqualmie Mountain's southwest slopes and south ridge (via Cave Ridge) from the Alpental/Snow Lake trailhead; loose and eroded underfoot but non-technical when dry, with growing exposure near the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From the Snow Lake trailhead at Alpental, find an unsigned boot path just off an old dirt road. The path climbs a steep, loose creek bed through forest for about a mile to a junction (right forks to Guye Peak/Cave Ridge; stay left/straight for the summit). Continue past a roughly 50-ft waterfall to an open meadow between Snoqualmie Mountain and Cave Ridge, then switchback up open southwest-facing slopes to the summit. Most parties treat it as a there-and-back half-day trip; it is popular as both a snow-free scramble and, with an ice axe/crampons, a winter/spring snow climb.
routes.approach: From I-90 eastbound or westbound, take Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass/Alpental). Follow Alpental Road north about 1.5-2 miles to the large parking area at the Alpental ski area/Snow Lake trailhead (a Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee is required). Start on the signed Snow Lake Trail, then peel off onto the unsigned Snoqualmie Mountain boot path a short distance up (roughly 20-50 ft right of the official trailhead, on an old blocked dirt road).
routes.descent: Descend the same boot-path/scramble route back to Alpental.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent line: down the open southwest slopes/south ridge to the meadow, back across the creek near the waterfall, down through the steep loose creek-bed/root section in the trees, and out to the Snow Lake trailhead parking lot. There is no established alternate descent or rappel route; in winter/spring conditions parties sometimes glissade lower snow sections where safe.
routes.itinerary: Single-day round trip from the Alpental/Snow Lake trailhead; most parties complete it in about 4-5 hours car-to-car.
routes.bail: The route is a there-and-back boot path with no committing terrain — retreat is possible from anywhere along the line back down to the Alpental parking lot; the main navigational hazard is losing the unmarked path in the trees or straying onto the Guye Peak/Cave Ridge fork.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before committing to the exposed summit ridge if the boot path or slopes are snow- or ice-covered without proper gear (ice axe/crampons and the skill to use them), if thunderstorms are forecast or building, or if visibility drops enough to risk losing the unmarked path.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a snow-free scramble roughly July through September/October once the boot path and upper slopes clear of snow; it is also a popular winter and spring snow climb/ski descent (e.g., the adjacent Phantom Slope and Slot Couloir lines) from roughly December through April, but that requires avalanche awareness, an ice axe, and crampons.
gear: Ice axe and crampons (required for snow-covered conditions, common into early summer and again in winter/spring)
whatToBring: Map/GPS or downloaded route track (the boot path is unmarked and easy to lose)
Wind layer for the open summit ridge
proTips: The route follows an unmaintained, unsigned climbers' boot path, not an official trail — expect to lose and re-find it, especially in the lower forested creek-bed section.
At about one mile a junction opens up on rocky ground: the right fork goes to Guye Peak/Cave Ridge, so bear left/straight for the Snoqualmie Mountain summit.
Most of the loose rock on the well-traveled sections has been kicked off by heavy scramble traffic, but stay alert for rockfall dislodged by parties above you.
Trekking poles help a lot on the steep, loose, root-and-scree lower section.
watchOut: Loose scree, roots, and eroded tread on the steep creek-bed section below the waterfall junction.
Growing exposure on the final summit ridge requires careful, sure footing.
In winter/spring, cornices and wind-loaded slabs form along the upper ridge and ski slopes (e.g., Phantom Slope), with avalanche danger on slopes reported around 30-40 degrees.
The unsigned boot path and the Guye Peak/Cave Ridge junction are easy to misread in poor visibility.
knownHazards: Steep, unmaintained boot path with loose scree, roots, and unstable footing in the lower forest section.
Rockfall risk from parties climbing above on the scree/talus sections.
Exposed summit ridge terrain near the top of the route.
Seasonal avalanche and cornice hazard on the upper mountain outside the summer window; the ski area's avalanche control work on adjacent slopes is a sign of the terrain's avalanche potential.

road.name: Alpental Road
road.status: Paved and maintained year-round to serve the Alpental ski area and Snow Lake trailhead.
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass/West Summit), turn north onto Alpental Road and continue roughly 1.5-2 miles to the large gravel/paved parking lot at the Snow Lake trailhead/Alpental ski area. I-90 itself can require chains/traction tires or see temporary closures during winter storms over Snoqualmie Pass.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and milder, drier summers typical of the Cascade-crest climate at Snoqualmie Pass; snow can linger on the upper mountain into early summer.

bySeason.spring: Snowpack persists on upper slopes; wind slab and wet-loose avalanche danger common, especially on north- and east-facing terrain near the summit ridge.
bySeason.summer: The main snow-free scrambling window, typically July through September, once the boot path and upper slopes have melted out.
bySeason.fall: Cooler and often drier through September-October, but early-season snow can return to the upper mountain by October or November.
bySeason.winter: Heavy Cascade snowfall and high avalanche potential; the mountain's slopes (e.g., Phantom Slope, Slot Couloir) are popular backcountry ski and snow-climb objectives, and the adjacent Alpental ski area actively controls avalanches nearby.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District; the upper route enters the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, whose boundary crosses the summit.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or $5 day-use fee) required to park at the Snow Lake/Alpental trailhead.
access.permit: Free, self-issue day-use wilderness permit required at the trailhead for Alpine Lakes Wilderness travel from May 15 through October 31.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful interagency pass (or day-use fee).

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, both to beat trailhead parking congestion (this is a popular Snoqualmie Pass objective) and to be off the exposed summit ridge before afternoon thunderstorms.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Snow Lake trailhead to unsigned boot path
sectionBreakdown.note: Boot path starts a short distance off the official Snow Lake trailhead/old dirt road.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Boot path to Guye Peak/Cave Ridge junction (~1 mi)
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, loose creek-bed and root scramble through trees; bear left/straight at the junction, not right toward Guye Peak.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Junction to open meadow below the ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Crosses a creek near a roughly 50-ft waterfall before opening onto rocky ground between Snoqualmie Mountain and Cave Ridge.
Switchbacks up open southwest-facing slopes/south ridge with increasing exposure near the top.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line: (206) 296-3311; call 911 for emergencies.
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office: (425) 888-1421, 902 SE North Bend Way, Bldg 1, North Bend, WA 98045.
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — 24/7 emergency department (Level 5 trauma / Level 2 cardiac / Level 3 stroke center, with helicopter transport available for higher-level trauma).
emergency.notes: King County Search and Rescue (volunteer teams dispatched via King County Sheriff's Office) covers this area. No verified cell-coverage data was found for the route itself; do not assume reliable service once off I-90.

routes.fa: Albert H. Sylvester and a USGS survey party, 1897 or 1898 — first recorded ascent of the peak; sources do not explicitly confirm the exact line climbed, but it was almost certainly this easiest standard south-ridge/southwest-slopes approach.
routes.face: Southwest Slopes / South Ridge via Cave Ridge
routes.corrections: Peak data lists elevationFt 6,283, but the definitive September 2023 survey (countryhighpoints.com, Eric Gilbertson) puts the trail-accessed west/main summit reached by this route at 6,278.4 ft (matches SummitPost and Willhiteweb's 6,278 ft), while a harder, unmarked 3rd-class east summit spire about 550 ft away is the true high point at 6,281.4 ft; Wikipedia's infobox gives 6,270 ft. Approach distance/gain figures also vary noticeably by source and likely reflect different trailhead/endpoint definitions: WTA reports 3.8 mi round trip with 3,105 ft gain (used here); Willhiteweb and SummitPost report roughly 5 mi round trip with about 3,200 ft gain; Gaia GPS reports 3.0 mi round trip with 3,069 ft gain.

blurb: Snoqualmie Mountain rises directly above Snoqualmie Pass and the Alpental ski area in the central Cascades of King County, Washington, with the Alpine Lakes Wilderness boundary crossing its summit. It sits within a thick sequence of Cascade-crest volcanic and sedimentary rock (the Denny and Naches formations) intruded by the Miocene Snoqualmie granodiorite batholith. Albert H. Sylvester and a USGS survey party made the first recorded ascent in 1897 or 1898, and the peak is now one of the most popular short scrambles near Seattle, with a 2023 precision survey confirming a harder, unmarked eastern rock spire is about 3 feet higher than the traditional, trail-accessed west summit.

routes.fa: Sidney V. Bryant, 1914 (first ascent of Kaleetan Peak; believed to be via this south-ridge line, the peak's standard/easiest route)
objHaz: loose rock and talus on the upper ridge/summit block
unbridged creek crossing (high water in early season)
lingering snow near Melakwa Pass into summer
routes.season: Jun-Oct (snow can linger near Melakwa Pass into early summer and return by fall)
routes.overview: A roughly 12-mile round-trip, Class 3 scramble that climbs Kaleetan Peak's south side, following the crest of the south ridge from Melakwa Pass to a short, blocky summit tower.
routes.beta: From the Denny Creek Trailhead, hike the Denny Creek Trail past Keekwulee and Snowshoe Falls to Melakwa Lake, cross talus toward Melakwa Pass (the saddle between Kaleetan and Chair Peak), then follow the scramble trail up the crest of Kaleetan's south ridge to the summit block. The route is mostly a well-worn boot path with a stretch of Class 2-3 scrambling and one short rock-chimney crux near 5,100 ft.
routes.approach: Drive to the Denny Creek Trailhead off I-90 Exit 47 (Denny Creek Road/FR 58, then FR 5830). Follow Denny Creek Trail 1014 roughly 4 miles, passing Keekwulee Falls and Snowshoe Falls, to Melakwa Lake at about 4,500-4,600 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the south-ridge scramble route back to Melakwa Lake and the Denny Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the south ridge scramble trail, staying high on the ridge crest rather than dropping into the basin on the east side. Downclimb the short eroded rock chimney near 5,100 ft carefully (2-3 moves onto a steep, loose section), then continue the boot path back to Melakwa Pass, Melakwa Lake, and the Denny Creek Trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a single long day: early start from the trailhead, ~4 miles to Melakwa Lake, continue to Melakwa Pass and up the south ridge to the summit, then reverse the route. Camping at Melakwa Lake (self-issue wilderness permit required) can split the trip into two days.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at almost any point by retracing the ridge/scramble trail back down to Melakwa Lake and the maintained Denny Creek Trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting and stick to it; the descent involves down-climbing loose, exposed terrain (including the summit chimney) that is markedly harder and slower in poor visibility, rain, or fading light.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly June through October once seasonal snow has cleared from the upper ridge and Melakwa Pass; snow patches can persist into early summer and traction devices are often useful into July, with new snow possible again by October.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable at the trailhead and along the route; WTA advises saving driving directions offline before departure.
routes.detailedRack: No rock rack is needed - this is an unroped Class 3 scramble. A helmet is recommended for the summit chimney and any loose rock below it, and many parties carry an ice axe and/or microspikes for lingering snow near Melakwa Pass early in the season.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or Sno-Park Permit for the trailhead
self-issue wilderness permit (required May 15-Oct 31 in Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
proTips: Stay high on the ridge crest on the way down rather than dropping into the basin on the east side.
Start early - the shared upper Denny Creek/Franklin Falls parking lot fills very early on summer weekends.
Expect snow patches near Melakwa Pass well into summer; carry traction in early season.
watchOut: A short (~5 ft) eroded rock chimney near 5,100 ft requires 2-3 careful downclimbing moves onto a steep, loose trail below it.
Denny Creek is forded without a bridge partway up the approach trail; high water from spring snowmelt can make this crossing dangerous or impassable.
The upper Denny Creek Trailhead parking lot is shared with the popular Franklin Falls hike and is routinely full in summer.
knownHazards: Loose rock and talus on the upper ridge and summit block.
Unbridged Denny Creek crossing subject to dangerous high water in early season.
Lingering snow and potential for slips on the approach to Melakwa Pass into early summer.

road.name: Denny Creek Road (Forest Road 58) to FR 5830
road.status: Paved to gravel; open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closed to motorized traffic and unplowed in winter; the road is gated/bermed near the top in the snow season
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 47, cross the overpass, turn right at the T, then left onto Denny Creek Road (FR 58); continue about 2.5-2.8 miles past Denny Creek Campground and turn left onto the paved spur (FR 5830) to the trailhead at road's end. NF 5800 is one-way approaching the turn, and the trailhead lot is shared with Franklin Falls and fills early.

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade climate: wet, snowy winters and drier but still changeable summers; check the NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone forecast and mountain weather before going in shoulder seasons.

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack lingers on the upper trail and ridge; expect route-finding over snow and possible avalanche concern into May-June.
bySeason.summer: Prime scrambling season, though snow patches can persist near Melakwa Pass into July.
bySeason.fall: Generally dry and clear but shortening days; new snow possible by October.
bySeason.winter: Road access is closed/unplowed and the route becomes a serious winter mountaineering undertaking with avalanche hazard.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District), Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or Sno-Park Permit required to park at the Denny Creek Trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for day and overnight use, May 15-Oct 31
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Sno-Park Permit
access.closures: Denny Creek Road (FR 58) is closed to motorized traffic and unplowed in winter

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start recommended to allow a full day and to secure trailhead parking

sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 miles via Denny Creek Trail 1014, passing Keekwulee and Snowshoe Falls
Talus crossing to the saddle between Kaleetan and Chair Peak
South ridge scramble, Class 2-3, with a short chimney crux near 5,100 ft

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: 206-296-3311 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office - 902 SE North Bend Way, North Bend, WA - (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA - 24-hour ER, (425) 831-2300
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable at the trailhead and on route; King County Sheriff's Office coordinates search and rescue for this area under RCW 38.52.

routes.corrections: Peak elevation varies slightly by source: USGS/Wikipedia give 6,259 ft (matches the provided 6,260 ft within normal rounding); WTA's hike page lists 6,224 ft. Round-trip stats also vary: WTA and Mountaineers sources describe ~12 miles round trip with roughly 4,500-5,000 ft of gain/loss, while a GaiaGPS-tracked variant shows only 10.5 miles and 3,621 ft gain, likely reflecting a different trailhead measurement or truncated track - the WTA figures were used as the more authoritative trail-association source. Note this South Ridge route is distinct from Kaleetan's other common scramble line, 'White Ledges' (steeper, east-face, includes a Class 4 move); White Ledges-specific details were intentionally excluded here to avoid conflating the two routes.

blurb: Kaleetan Peak is a 6,259-foot granite summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Washington's Central Cascades, about four miles northwest of Snoqualmie Pass and immediately west of Chair Peak across Melakwa Pass. Its craggy, glacier-carved profile rises above Melakwa Lake and is a popular non-technical scrambling objective reached via the Denny Creek Trail. The name derives from the Chinook Jargon word for "arrow" (officially adopted in 1916), and Sidney V. Bryant made the first recorded ascent in 1914, almost certainly via the peak's easiest line, the south ridge.

routes.rappels: 1-2 double-rope (up to 60m) rappels from tree/rock anchors atop the buttress, alternating with downclimbing, back to the base; some parties exit with a single 60m rope through a cornice gap into the south-facing gully
objHaz: avalanche-prone approach and descent gully (wet-slough/roller-ball release in warm conditions)
overhead hazard in the descent gully
routes.season: Jun-Sep as a rock/scramble route; Dec-Mar as a mixed snow/ice climb
routes.overview: A classic moderate line up Chair Peak's prominent northeast rock rib, climbed as an exposed Class 3-4 rock scramble in summer and as a popular Grade II-III mixed snow/ice route (with an obvious ice bulge and short gully pitches) once winter conditions set in.
routes.beta: Roughly 400-600 ft of climbing over 4 short pitches: an S-shaped gully/rib to tree anchors, an easier snow section, a short steep ice/mixed bulge, and a final snow pitch to the summit block, which many parties unrope to scramble.
routes.approach: Park at the upper Alpental lot (3,100 ft). Follow the valley trail past Source Lake toward the basin below Chair Peak's east face (~4,200 ft), then climb a short snow gully (~300 ft) to gain the crest that forms the Northeast Buttress.
routes.descent: Scramble the rocky summit terrain back to a cluster of trees atop the buttress, then rappel (double-rope or single-rope via the cornice-gap variant) and downclimb back to the base of the route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble to the trees at the top of the buttress. A double-rope (60m) rappel plus a bit of downclimbing to the head of the gully, followed by another rappel, brings parties to the base; alternatively, a single 60m rope can be threaded through a gap in the summit cornice to rappel directly into the south-facing gully, then descend/glissade to Source Lake. Watch for wet-slide activity and rockfall in the gully during warm afternoons.
routes.bail: The route can be downclimbed or rappelled from natural anchors on most of its pitches; a single 60m rope is generally enough to retreat via the south-facing gully rappel.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on daylight and current avalanche/rockfall conditions in the approach and descent gullies, and be prepared to retreat rather than push through deteriorating snow or overhead hazard from other parties.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable nearly year-round in different styles: a summer/fall rock scramble in dry conditions (roughly June-September) versus a winter/spring mixed ice climb (December-March) when the gullies and bulge are filled with consolidated snow and ice; many parties prefer weekdays to avoid crowding and rockfall from parties above.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack: ice screws (a handful of short-to-mid length), 2-4 pickets, a small rack of nuts/cams (roughly 0.3-1" range) and a few slings for tree/rock anchors; two tools and crampons for winter ascents.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is a mix of ice screws and pickets on the snow/ice sections and small-to-medium rock gear plus fixed tree/rock anchors on the rock pitches; anchors can be sparse on the ice bulge pitch.
gear: single 60m rope (tag line useful for rappels)
whatToBring: approach shoes or mountain boots depending on season
sunglasses/sun protection for glacier-like snow approach
proTips: Climb midweek if possible; weekend traffic causes queuing and increases rockfall/icefall exposure from parties above
Start pre-dawn to cross the avalanche-prone approach and descent gullies before the sun softens the snow
A single 60m rope with a tag line keeps rappels efficient and reduces time spent in overhead-hazard zones
watchOut: Party-caused rockfall onto climbers below is a real hazard on this popular route
The descent gully has produced loose wet avalanches and roller-ball release during warm, sunny conditions
Cornices can form at the summit exit in winter
knownHazards: The slopes above Source Lake below Chair Peak have a documented history of avalanche accidents in high-hazard conditions
Overhead hazard from other climbing parties is significant given the route's popularity

pitchDetail.grade: Class 3-4 rock in summer; up to M2-M4 mixed in winter
pitchDetail.notes: S-shaped gully/rib on the right side of the buttress; ends at tree anchors
Typically easy snow climbing leading toward the ice bulge
Short, steep ice/mixed bulge; anchor placements can be sparse
Leads to the summit saddle; many parties unrope to scramble the final short block

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade weather: wet, snowy winters and drier, milder summers; conditions on the route change quickly and rockfall/avalanche hazard is strongly tied to recent freeze-thaw and storm cycles.

bySeason.spring: Variable freeze-thaw snowpack; good window for consolidated mixed climbing but wet-slide hazard increases with warm afternoons.
bySeason.summer: Generally dry rock conditions by July-September, though early-season parties may still find snow in the approach gully.
bySeason.fall: Rock conditions persist into September before early-season snow returns.
bySeason.winter: Primary ice/mixed season (roughly Dec-Mar); best after a hard freeze has consolidated new snow and bonded the ice.

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade as a flat 5.6; Mountain Project's aggregated route page lists the crux rock closer to 5.4 with mixed AI2-3/M1-2 sections in winter conditions - both figures are retained/blended here since guidebook (Beckey/Mountaineers) sources use 5.6 while online aggregator data skews lower. Sources also disagree on the alpine commitment grade (Grade II per several trip reports vs Grade III per Mountain Project), so 'commitment' was left null rather than guessed. Peak elevation is commonly cited as 6,238 ft (USGS/Wikipedia/Peakbagger) vs. 6,240 ft in the provided seed data - a negligible rounding difference.

routes.fa: Kit Lewis, Charlie Hampson, Rob Harris, Greg Jacobson (January 1975)
routes.rappels: Typically a single 60m rappel (occasionally two) through the summit gully/notch back into the basin; some late-day/crowded ascents have required additional downclimbing when rope length fell short
objHaz: bergschrund/moat opening near the route base, especially late season
overhead rockfall/icefall from parties above on busy days
routes.overview: A moderate, sustained alpine ice/mixed line up the center of Chair Peak's north face above the Source Lake/Snow Lake basin - one of the most popular early-winter moderate ice climbs near Seattle.
routes.beta: Four pitches of steep snow and ice (up to 70 degrees) broken by tree and rock belays, finishing at a small col just below the summit block; conditions are best after a freeze has consolidated the snowpack into good neve/ice.
routes.approach: Park at the upper Alpental lot (3,100 ft) and hike up the valley past Source Lake toward the basin below Chair Peak's north/east side (~4,200 ft). Continue along the base of the peak, traversing out to mid-face, where the North Face route begins (rather than climbing the short gully used to reach the Northeast Buttress).
routes.descent: Downclimb/scramble from the summit to a notch, then make one (occasionally two) rappels through the descent gully back to the basin, watching for cornice and overhead hazard.
routes.descentText: From the top of the North Face, most parties join the standard Chair Peak descent: scramble/downclimb from the summit block to the col/notch, then rappel through the gully (a single 60m rope is often sufficient) back down to the basin below the face, taking care around the summit cornice and any wet-slide activity in the gully during warmer conditions.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible by downclimbing the moderate-angle snow/ice pitches or rappelling from tree and rock anchors along the line; the bergschrund near the base can complicate a low retreat late in the season.
routes.turnaround: Establish a turnaround time based on daylight, party size/queuing at popular pinch points, and current avalanche hazard in the approach and descent gullies; this route sees heavy weekend traffic that can significantly slow ascents.
routes.bestSeason: A December-March objective; best climbed after a hard freeze-thaw cycle has consolidated the snowpack into solid neve and well-bonded ice, and ideally attempted below-freezing rather than during or immediately after a storm.
routes.detailedRack: Two ice tools and crampons, a rack of ice screws, several pickets, a few pitons, and a light selection of small rock protection (nuts) for mixed sections and anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets provide the bulk of protection on the snow/ice pitches; small rock gear and occasional pitons back up belays at tree and rock stances.
whatToBring: extra insulating layers for the exposed, shaded north-facing line
sun/wind protection for the summit ridge
proTips: Go early in the week or early in the day to avoid the queuing and overhead hazard common on this popular route
Time the climb after a freeze-thaw cycle for the most solid, well-bonded ice and snow
A single 60m rope is usually enough for the standard descent, saving weight on the approach
watchOut: Overhead hazard from other parties is significant on weekends given the route's popularity
The bergschrund/moat near the base can become a difficult, committing crossing later in the season
Cornice at the summit ridge
knownHazards: The approach and descent gullies below Chair Peak have a history of avalanche activity in high-hazard conditions
Loose wet-snow sloughing and rockfall from the upper face can occur during warm, sunny periods

pitchDetail.notes: Steep snow and ice from the base to a shoulder belay where the angle eases
A full rope-length of typically steep snow to an exposed tree belay on the face
Roughly a rope-length using mainly tree anchors along the way
Leads to a small col about 60 ft below the summit block, where a cornice may be encountered

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade weather; the north-facing aspect holds snow and ice longer than the sunnier east/southeast sides of the peak, which is why it climbs best in the core winter months.

bySeason.spring: Ice quality degrades as temperatures warm; wet-slide hazard increases.
bySeason.summer: Not a viable ice route once the face melts out.
bySeason.fall: Early season snow/ice can build enough for climbable conditions late in the season.
bySeason.winter: Primary season (Dec-Mar); best after a hard freeze has consolidated new snow into solid neve/ice.

routes.corrections: Given data listed iceGrade as AI3 and alpineGrade/overall grade as III; Mountain Project's route page lists the technical ice grade as AI2 (M1-2) at an overall Grade III, while Mountaineers.org and several trip reports describe it simply as a 'Grade II' ice climb - sources conflict on the roman-numeral commitment grade (II vs III), so 'commitment' was left null. routeFt (800 ft) and the January 1975 FA party (Kit Lewis, Charlie Hampson, Rob Harris, Greg Jacobson) came from Mountain Project and were not present in the seed data.

blurb: Chair Peak (6,238 ft) is a steep granodiorite summit of the Snoqualmie Batholith in the Central Cascades near Snoqualmie Pass, sitting inside the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest) and rising roughly 2,200 ft above Snow Lake in just half a mile. First climbed in 1913 by Hec Abel and L.F. Curtis, its compact rock and seasonal ice/mixed lines above Source and Snow Lakes have made it one of the most popular moderate alpine objectives close to Seattle.

routes.rockGrade: 5.0 (Easy 5th, mostly Class 4 with a few low-5th moves)
routes.rappels: 1-2 raps (30m rope) or downclimb most of the ridge
routes.overview: A short, classic entry-level alpine ridge climb: mostly Class 3-4 scrambling up blocky granodiorite with one Class 4/5.0 slabby crux near the top, finishing on an exposed summit ridge.
routes.beta: The West Ridge is a compact, sub-1,000-foot ridge climb rated Easy 5th/Grade II. Climbers gain the ridge crest from a notch, then move over generally sound blocky rock, mostly Class 3-4 scrambling with a crux slabby depression rated Class 4/5.0 guarding the final approach to the summit. It's frequently used as a first alpine rock climb, though route-finding, some cliffing, and loose talus mean it should not be treated as a casual scramble.
routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 52, take the first left (Alpental Road) then the first right into the Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) parking area. Follow the PCT north about 2.5 miles, then descend into Commonwealth Basin and contour to Lundin Basin (~4,200 ft) below the peak's south side to reach the base of the west ridge. A late-season alternative starts from the Alpental lot via the unmarked Cave Ridge Trail (just east of the Snow Lake trailhead), over Cave Ridge, then an ascending traverse to Lundin Peak.
routes.descent: Downclimb or rappel the west ridge, or descend via the east/southeast ridge with a car shuttle; in early season, glissade the south-facing snow couloir/chute back into Lundin Basin.
routes.descentText: Most parties reverse the west ridge, downclimbing or making one to two short rappels (30m rope) off the blocky crest back to the approach notch. An alternative is to continue over the summit and descend the southeast ridge/south couloir into Lundin Basin (one short rappel may be needed off the upper southeast ridge), which works well when the couloir still holds snow for glissading or plunge-stepping. A car shuttle is required if descending a different ridge than the ascent.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward along the ascent ridge at nearly any point by downclimbing back to the entry notch; the route has abundant trees for slinging rappel anchors on the lower ridge.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux and summit ridge are near the top of a short route with an already-substantial approach, turn around with enough daylight margin to reverse any exposed downclimbing before dark, and account for extra time if the descent snow couloir has softened or melted out.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September; many parties prefer early season (June-July) when residual snow covers the approach brush and talus and allows a couloir descent, while late season exposes more loose rock on the approach and descent chute.
routes.detailedRack: A small alpine rack suffices: roughly six or seven pieces (small-to-medium cams/stoppers), several slings/runners (there are many trees to sling for anchors), a 30m rope, rappel device, and a helmet. Rock shoes are unnecessary.
routes.proNeeds: Light trad rack for the short Class 4/5.0 sections and tree/gear anchors for the rappel; the route is short enough that protection needs are minimal compared to the routefinding and loose-rock hazard.
whatToBring: approach shoes suited to talus and scrambling
ice axe and traction for early-season snow couloir
navigation tools for the unmarked/climber's-trail approach
proTips: Many climbers prefer early-season conditions when snow covers the brush and boulder-hopping on the approach, and allows a glissade descent
The Cave Ridge approach from Alpental is a good late-season alternative once the direct couloir has melted out
There are plenty of trees on the lower ridge for building rappel/sling anchors
watchOut: Apparently stable talus on the descent has proven unreliable in past accidents
Unroped scrambling on exposed sections has caused fatal falls on this peak; consider roping up if uncertain
Cliffing and route-finding difficulty exist below the ridge proper, especially in early season under snow
knownHazards: At least two documented fatal falls have occurred on Lundin Peak, including a 1980 accident where a climber fell about 600 ft while descending, illustrating how deceptively unstable the talus can be
A 1997 accident also resulted in a fatality near the base of the peak
The route is short but exposed; several sections carry real consequences if unroped

pitchDetail.notes: Blocky granodiorite ridge crest gained from the approach notch; large blocks and horns
Slabby depression near the top of the ridge, roughly 120 ft section; the technical crux of the route
Heather ledges and rock outcrops leading to the summit

road.driveNote: Exit 52 off I-90 near Snoqualmie Pass; turn left for the PCT-North trailhead or right into the Alpental/PCT-South gravel lot depending on approach chosen.

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass / West Slopes Central Cascades (NWAC)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters (average >450 in of snow) and warm, drier summers typical of the Cascade crest near Snoqualmie Pass; conditions and freezing levels should be checked before any snow-couloir approach or descent.

bySeason.spring: Heavy residual snowpack in the couloir and basin; avalanche hazard possible into early summer
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep) once snow has receded from the ridge but may still linger in the descent couloir
bySeason.fall: Generally dry rock but shortening daylight and possible early snow
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain; not a winter rock-climbing objective

timing.recommendedStart: Early (pre-dawn/dawn) given a long approach and exposed summit ridge

sectionBreakdown.note: Via PCT north ~2.5 mi then descent/contour into the basin

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Lundin Basin to summit via west ridge

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office (dispatches King County Search & Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest / Snoqualmie Ranger District
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA (24-hour ER, Level 5 trauma center)

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 6,045 ft appears slightly off; multiple reputable sources (Wikipedia/USGS-derived, Peakbagger, GNIS) consistently list Lundin Peak at 6,057 ft (topozone/other secondary sources show 6,059 ft as a rounding variant). Route length (routeFt) of ~3,300 ft comes from Mountain Project and refers to the ridge climb's total route length, not vertical gain. Approach distance/elevation figures (distKm, gainFt, lossFt, timing) are drawn from a single detailed trip report (trailcatjim.com) for a South Couloir-West Ridge combination and are reasonable estimates for the standard West Ridge approach, but were not independently corroborated by a second numeric source, so treat as approximate.

blurb: Lundin Peak (6,057 ft) is a granodiorite summit on the ridge between Snoqualmie Mountain and Red Mountain, three miles north of Snoqualmie Pass in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Originally called "Snoqualmie Little Sister," it was renamed in 1931 to honor Mount Baker-Snoqualmie district forest ranger John Lundin. Its short, exposed alpine ridges are popular entry-level rock/scramble objectives out of the Seattle area, but the loose talus and exposed downclimbing have been the site of at least two fatal falls.

routes.overview: The standard scramble line up Bryant Peak, approached from Hemlock Pass and climbing talus and a short, loose rock step to the summit block.
routes.beta: From Hemlock Pass the route follows the ridgeline toward Bryant Peak, angling right behind trees to a brushy notch, then climbs roughly 170 ft to a meadow at the base of a talus slope that covers about three-quarters of the peak's base. The final scramble goes up rock on the left (or, when snow-free, around the exposed north side) to the summit; the rock is loose and helmets are recommended.
routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 47 (North Bend), turn north and follow signs to Denny Creek Road (Forest Road 58); continue about 2.5 miles past Denny Creek Campground, then a short distance further to the Denny Creek Trailhead (large paved lots). Hike the Denny Creek Trail (No. 1014) roughly 4 miles, passing Keekwulee and Snowshoe Falls and entering the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, to Hemlock Pass, then leave the trail to gain the ridge toward Bryant Peak.
routes.descent: Descend the same Southeast Slopes/talus line back to Hemlock Pass and return via the Denny Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route: down-climb the loose summit rock back to the talus meadow, descend the talus and the brushy notch back to the ridge near Hemlock Pass, then rejoin and follow the Denny Creek Trail back to the trailhead. Loose rock makes the descent through the summit block the most hazard-prone section; move carefully and avoid kicking rock onto others below.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this route: reverse course down the talus to Hemlock Pass and the Denny Creek Trail at essentially any point below the final summit rock step.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with enough daylight and margin to reverse the loose, exposed summit scramble before deteriorating weather, wet rock, or fatigue increase fall and rockfall risk.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September when the summit rock and talus are snow-free; the route is also done as a winter/spring scramble with an ice axe, microspikes, or snowshoes when snow-covered, which can actually ease exposure on the upper mountain but adds avalanche exposure on the approach slopes.
routes.comms: No confirmed cell coverage data found for this specific route; the broader Snoqualmie Pass/I-90 corridor has patchy but sometimes usable cell signal on ridges and summits, unreliable in valleys and basins.
knownHazards: The final summit scramble is on notably loose rock with some exposure; helmets are recommended.
Waterfalls and wet, steep sections of the Denny Creek approach trail can be slick.
When snow-covered, approach slopes and the notch below the summit talus can present avalanche hazard.
watchOut: Loose rock throughout the upper talus and summit block — test holds and avoid climbing directly below other parties.
Route-finding through the brushy notch below the summit talus field can be tricky; look for a bearing of roughly 33 degrees off the ridge crest near Hemlock Pass.
Without snow cover, the only way to the true summit is an exposed traverse around the north side of the summit block.
proTips: A grippy approach shoe or light mountaineering boot helps on both the talus and the loose summit rock.
Combine with nearby Hemlock Peak for a bigger day, as many parties do, since both are reached from the same Hemlock Pass ridge.
Snow cover (spring/early summer) can actually make the low summit block easier and less exposed than the bare-rock condition, per multiple trip reports.
whatToBring: trekking poles for talus and stream crossings
ice axe and traction (microspikes/snowshoes) for spring/winter snow conditions

road.name: Forest Road 58 (Denny Creek Road)
road.status: Open, paved to trailhead
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 47, turn north, then left onto Denny Creek Road (FR 58); follow roughly 2.5 miles past Denny Creek Campground to the paved trailhead access and parking lots.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District); route lies within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Day-use fee ($5/day or $30 annual) or a Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass required at Denny Creek Trailhead

climate.typical: Wet, marine-influenced Cascade weather; heavy winter snowpack, drier and more stable July-September

bySeason.spring: Lingering deep snowpack on approach slopes and the notch below the summit talus; avalanche hazard requires care
bySeason.summer: Warm, mostly dry conditions July-September; best window for the scramble with the least snow and hazard
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures, increasing chance of early snow at elevation by October
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow, done as a winter/backcountry ski or ice objective rather than a standard scramble; avalanche terrain on approach
