routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (5,790) is close to but not identical to sourced values: Wikipedia lists 5,801 ft while ListsOfJohn (LiDAR-based) lists 5,791 ft for this King County/Snoqualmie Pass Bryant Peak (note: a separate, unrelated 6,401-ft 'Bryant Peak' also exists elsewhere in Washington per ListsOfJohn — not this peak). Approach/round-trip distance and gain vary by source depending on whether Hemlock Peak is combined: Bryant Peak alone is commonly cited as ~9 mi round trip / 3,600 ft gain; combined with Hemlock Peak, ~9.5–10.8 mi / 3,900+ ft gain. The 1926 first-ascent party is documented for the peak overall (Wikipedia), not confirmed as specific to this Southeast Slopes line, so 'fa' was left null per the route-vs-peak FA distinction.

blurb: Bryant Peak (5,791–5,801 ft, sources vary) sits on the ridge between Denny Mountain and Chair Peak in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Central-West Cascades, just northwest of Snoqualmie Pass and the Alpental ski area. Composed of andesite, it was originally named Hemlock Peak and was renamed in 1925 to honor Sidney V. Bryant of The Mountaineers, with the first recorded ascent in 1926 by Anderson, Best, Maxwell, Shorrock, West, and Wunderling. Though a modest summit, it offers a surprisingly alpine-flavored scramble with loose rock and some exposure near the top, plus steeper ice/mixed lines on its north side popular with backcountry skiers and winter climbers.

objHaz: avalanche/wet-snow slides on the ski slopes and Edelweiss Bowl
exposed, down-sloping rock ledge near the summit (cable-protected)
loose rock on the summit block
whiteout/featureless terrain on the open ski runs
routes.face: East flank, via the Alpental ski runs (Lower/Upper International)
routes.overview: The standard route climbs Denny Mountain's east-facing slopes above the Alpental Ski Area, following the Lower and then Upper International ski runs to their top, then finishes with a short, exposed scramble - aided by a fixed steel cable across a rock ledge - to the summit horn.
routes.beta: From the Alpental base area, ascend barren lower slopes to snowline, then follow the Lower International run and the steep, double-black-diamond Upper International run. From the top of Upper International, a wide ramp leads to a clump of alpine fir below the summit block; a steel cable protects a down-sloping, exposed rock-ledge traverse, followed by a short Class 2-3 scramble to the summit, which carries a radio tower and marker pole. Many parties descend a counterclockwise loop through Edelweiss Bowl and Midway Saddle, glissading Debbie's Gold back to the base.
routes.approach: From I-90 eastbound take Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass West Summit/Alpental), cross under the freeway, and follow the Alpental access road (Forest Road 9040) about 1.25-1.4 miles to the large Alpental ski area parking lots (approx. 3,100 ft). The route starts directly from the base lodge area.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent slopes, or loop counterclockwise via Edelweiss Bowl and Midway Saddle and glissade down the Debbie's Gold ski run back to the Alpental base.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties either downclimb/reverse the cable-protected ledge and re-descend the International runs, or continue the loop north over Edelweiss Bowl and Midway Saddle before dropping into the Debbie's Gold ski run for a glissade back to the base lodge. Watch avalanche conditions in Edelweiss Bowl, and be aware the route crosses operating ski-area terrain that may be closed to uphill/backcountry travel while the resort is running.
routes.itinerary: Single-day car-to-car trip: roughly 2.5-3 hours to the summit and 1-1.5 hours back down, best started early in spring while the snow is still firm.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward down the open ski-slope terrain at almost any point below the summit horn; the only committing section is the fixed-cable ledge traverse just under the top - downclimb it carefully or wait it out rather than pushing through in poor conditions.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time based on the current avalanche forecast and how the snow is softening through the day, and avoid the exposed cable/ledge section near the summit if it is icy, wet, or visibility drops - a slip there is consequential.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring (roughly April-June) is considered the sweet spot: after Alpental closes for the ski season but before the lower slopes melt out, giving continuous snow travel on firm corn snow. Once the snow is gone, the lower slopes reportedly revert to loose scree/brush, and the route overlaps operating ski runs (closed to backcountry travel) during the winter season while the resort is open.
routes.detailedRack: Not a roped climb for most parties; an ice axe and crampons are the primary tools for the snow slopes, and some parties carry a short sling/handline as backup on the cable-protected ledge below the summit.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection beyond the resort-installed steel cable on the final ledge; a rope is not typically used but could be carried for extra security on that section.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
map/compass or GPS track (the open ski-run terrain is featureless in fog/whiteout)
avalanche gear (beacon/probe/shovel) if traveling on snow with avalanche hazard
proTips: Time the trip for spring corn snow after Alpental closes for the season but before the lower slopes melt out, for fast, firm travel.
Use the fixed steel cable on the exposed ledge below the summit - it's there for a reason.
The Debbie's Gold glissade on descent is popular but scout it first for exposed rocks.
watchOut: The final rock ledge/cable traverse below the summit is exposed and can be slick or down-sloping.
Wet, heavy Cascade snow on these slopes creates high avalanche danger, especially in Edelweiss Bowl.
The route crosses operating Alpental ski-area terrain, which may be closed to uphill/backcountry travel while the resort is running.
knownHazards: Avalanche danger on the ski slopes and Edelweiss Bowl, particularly with new or wind-loaded snow.
Exposed, down-sloping rock ledge near the summit protected only by a fixed cable.
Loose rock on the summit block.

road.name: Alpental Road / Forest Road 9040
road.status: Paved access road to the ski area parking lots; generally open year-round
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 52 (signed Snoqualmie Pass/Alpental), turn toward Alpental, cross under the freeway, and follow the road about 1.25-1.4 miles to the parking lots at the base of the ski area.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate: Pacific storms are forced upward by the Cascade crest, producing heavy winter precipitation; snow here is characteristically wet and heavy, driving high avalanche danger.

bySeason.spring: Prime scrambling window (Apr-Jun) once Alpental closes for the season; snow still bridges the lower slopes but afternoon warming raises wet-slide/avalanche danger.
bySeason.summer: By mid-to-late summer snow typically melts off the lower slopes, exposing looser scree/talus underneath.
bySeason.fall: Early-season snow can return by October, recoating the upper mountain while the access road stays open.
bySeason.winter: Deep, wet Cascade snowpack with high avalanche danger; the route overlaps operating Alpental ski runs, which are closed to backcountry travel while the resort is open.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service - Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required to park at the Alpental trailhead lot
access.closures: Route crosses operating Alpental ski-area terrain; backcountry/uphill travel on those slopes is generally restricted while the resort is open for the ski season (roughly Nov-Apr).

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, especially in spring, to catch the snow before it softens

sectionBreakdown.note: via Lower/Upper International ski runs and the cable-protected summit ledge
loop via Edelweiss Bowl/Midway Saddle, glissading Debbie's Gold

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (206) 296-3311 (dial 911 for an active emergency; KCSO coordinates backcountry search and rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office - 902 SE North Bend Way, Bldg 1, North Bend, WA 98045, (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA, (425) 831-2300 - 24-hour emergency department, roughly 30-35 minutes' drive from the Alpental trailhead

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevation of 5,614 ft is higher than the elevation most commonly cited by USGS/Wikipedia (5,520+ ft NGVD29); a GPS reading of 5,608 ft has also been reported by climbers, and an Alpental trail map lists 5,530 ft. highPointFt here uses the more widely cited ~5,520 ft figure rather than the given 5,614 ft. Separately, the standard route's summit block is consistently described (Mountaineers, trip reports) as finishing with an exposed, cable-protected Class 2-3 ledge scramble rather than a uniform Class 2 walk-up. Distance/gain also vary noticeably by source and exact line taken: a Mountaineers program page cites 6 mi/2,500 ft round trip, a WTA-logged trip report recorded 4.7 mi/2,635 ft, and a detailed tracked trip report logged about 2.8 mi one-way with ~2,500 ft gain via the International ski runs (the figures used here). Despite the route being labeled 'North Slopes,' published descriptions (including Wikipedia) place the ski-area ascent line on the mountain's east-facing flank, not a north aspect - aspect is left null here due to that inconsistency rather than guessed.

blurb: Denny Mountain is a jagged, roughly mile-long NNW-trending ridge on the south side of Snoqualmie Pass in the central Cascades, with the Alpental ski area occupying its eastern flank and I-90 running along its southern base. It is named for Seattle founder Arthur A. Denny, who helped survey and build the original 1860s Snoqualmie Pass wagon road through the area. Geologically it lies within Cascade-arc volcanic and sedimentary rock — Permian volcanics, limestone and chert of the (informally named) Denny Formation, unconformably overlain by Oligocene-Miocene andesitic/dacitic tuff and breccia of the Denny Mountain Formation — and is popular as a straightforward snow/scree scramble up the ski area's slopes.

routes.fa: Lloyd Anderson & Herman Wunderling, 1928 (South Face first ascent; the peak's overall first ascent, via an easier scramble line, is credited to Charles Hazelhurst & C.G. Morrison, 1916)
routes.rappels: Most parties reverse the class 3-4 scramble to Pineapple Pass rather than rappel; if rapping, 2 raps (60m rope) down the east gully from bolted chain/ring anchors
objHaz: crowding and rockfall from parties above
exposure on the narrow catwalk traverse
routes.overview: A 4-pitch, mostly 5.4 line up blocky andesite on the south wall of The Tooth above Pineapple Pass — the most-climbed moderate alpine rock route near Seattle, first ascended in 1928.
routes.beta: Four short pitches (5.3-5.4 with a 4th-class pitch 3) climb juggy ledges and flakes; an airy, roughly four-inch-wide 'catwalk' ledge traverse is the route's signature feature. Rock is generally solid with some loose, dirty sections; several belays and rappel anchors are bolted.
routes.approach: From the Alpental parking area (3,100 ft, Exit 52 off I-90), hike the Snow Lake Trail about 2 miles to a talus slope above Source Lake, then traverse above the lake beneath a cliff band. Beyond the cliff band, climb a steep slope and cross a stream into the basin under The Tooth's east face, ascend to the second notch south of the South Face, drop slightly north, then climb back up to Pineapple Pass — the saddle between The Tooth and the pinnacle to its south. A short class 3-4 scramble around the south side leads to the base of the South Face.
routes.descent: Reverse the class 3-4 scramble back to Pineapple Pass (preferred/safer); alternatively rappel the east gully (2 raps, 60m rope) from bolted anchors.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, most parties down-climb/scramble the class 3-4 notch back to Pineapple Pass and retrace the approach through the basin above Source Lake to the Snow Lake Trail. Some parties instead rappel the east gully from Pineapple Pass: two bolted chain-and-ring stations allow two rappels with a 60m rope (an intermediate anchor tucked under a small roof roughly halfway down), but the gully carries notable rockfall hazard and is slower for larger parties, so reversing the scramble is generally considered the faster, safer option.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single long day: drive to Alpental, approach via the Snow Lake Trail/Source Lake basin to Pineapple Pass (roughly 2-3 hr), climb the 4-pitch route to the summit (trailhead-to-summit commonly cited as 5-6 hr total), then descend by reversing the scramble or rapping the east gully.
routes.bail: The route is only 4 short, moderate pitches, so retreat from any belay is straightforward — downclimb or build a gear anchor and get back to the notch/Pineapple Pass rather than continuing up.
routes.turnaround: The approach and route are popular and can back up with parties; build in extra time for traffic on route and turn around if afternoon thunderstorms are forecast or if there isn't enough daylight/margin left to reverse the scramble descent.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September once approach snowfields have consolidated or melted out; it is one of the most popular moderate alpine rock outings near Seattle and can be crowded on summer weekends and into fall.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack suffices: a set of nuts and cams up to about 3 inches, a handful of slings/draws. Fixed rappel/belay anchors (rings, cordelette, slings) are in place at several stations as of recent reports.
routes.proNeeds: Well protected throughout with cracks, flakes, and slung blocks, supplemented by bolted belay/rap anchors at several stances; a single light rack is adequate.
whatToBring: Extra water for the long summer approach
Light insulating layer for the exposed pass
Northwest Forest Pass / day-use fee for parking
proTips: Start early — the route is extremely popular and can back up with parties on summer weekends
A single light rack (nuts + cams to 3in) is enough; don't overload for a 4-pitch route
Reversing the class 3-4 scramble back to Pineapple Pass is usually faster and safer than rappelling the loose east gully
watchOut: Loose, dirty rock in sections despite generally solid andesite
Rockfall hazard in the east gully descent, especially with parties above
Crowding on route can cause long belay waits and raise rockfall risk
knownHazards: Rockfall in the descent gully from parties above
Exposed, narrow 'catwalk' traverse ledge on the upper route
Loose rock mixed with sound climbing on parts of the face

pitchDetail.notes: ~100 ft. From Pineapple Pass, head up and trend right through the main groove/line of weakness; belay at a slung block.
~100 ft. Climb right to a ledge and follow ledges back and forth, ending near trees with rap tat on the left.
~70 ft. Class 4 scrambling with several reasonable lines ('choose your own adventure').
~50 ft. Three finishing options of varying difficulty lead to the summit block.

road.name: Alpental Road
road.status: Paved, maintained year-round to the Alpental ski area parking lot
road.driveNote: From I-90 take Exit 52, turn northwest onto Alpental Road, and follow it about 1.5 miles to the large gravel/paved parking area (3,100 ft).

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime Cascades climate — wet, snowy winters and milder, drier summers, right at the crest near Snoqualmie Pass.

bySeason.spring: Lingering snow on the approach basin into June; the route itself is usually still snowy/wet.
bySeason.summer: Prime rock season, July-September, but frequently crowded on weekends.
bySeason.fall: Can stay in condition into September/October depending on weather, with rapidly shortening days.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard on the approach; the South Face itself is not a winter objective (see the Northeast Slabs for a winter line).

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: $5 day-use parking fee at the Alpental/Snow Lake trailhead if no pass is displayed
access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass, America the Beautiful interagency pass, or the day-use fee
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency pass

sectionBreakdown.note: Commonly cited as 5-6 hr trailhead-to-summit; does not include descent.

emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, North Bend, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, Snoqualmie, WA
emergency.notes: King County Sheriff's Office coordinates search and rescue (including the volunteer Ski Patrol Rescue Team, SPART) for the Alpental/Source Lake basin; Snoqualmie Pass Fire & Rescue provides local EMS.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation: Wikipedia/Peakbagger give 5,606 ft vs. the 5,605 ft supplied. FA name discrepancy: Mountain Project credits 'C.L. Anderson & Herman Wunderling (1928)' while Wikipedia credits 'Lloyd Anderson and Herman Wunderling (1928)' for the South Face — both agree on Wunderling and 1928; kept as Lloyd Anderson (likely the Mountaineers member/REI co-founder) per Wikipedia. Note this is distinct from the peak's overall first ascent (an easier line), dated 1916 to Charles Hazelhurst & C.G. Morrison per secondary sources.

routes.fa: Jim Nelson & Paul Stevenson, 1982 (as the 'Northeast Slabs' — see corrections on naming)
routes.iceGrade: WI3 (moderate), mixed snow/ice — highly conditions dependent
routes.rappels: Descend via the South Face's rappel stations rather than reversing the route (2 raps, 60m rope, from bolted chain anchors at Pineapple Pass)
objHaz: cornices/wind slab near the summit ridge
routes.overview: A moderate winter/spring mixed line up the slabby northeast side of The Tooth, gaining the north ridge via snow and thin ice before finishing to the summit — a popular winter alternative to the summer South Face.
routes.beta: Roughly 5 pitches of easy 5th-class rock mixed with moderate ice/snow. The crux second pitch is a long, largely unprotectable ascending traverse (up to ~30 m) across a 50-60° snow/ice-covered slab that requires adequate coverage to be feasible; the route then follows the north ridge, with some line variations, to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Alpental parking lot, follow the Source Lake trail/ski tracks, then head up the valley on the true left (south) side of the creek near Source Lake, ascending a snow finger into the basin below the peak's northeast side to reach the base of the route.
routes.descent: Standard rappel down the South Face route.
routes.descentText: Parties typically descend by rappelling the South Face's bolted stations rather than reversing the winter line; expect intermixed down-climbing between rappels depending on snow coverage. See the South Face route's descent notes for anchor details.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once past the unprotectable P2 traverse; if snow/ice coverage looks marginal or conditions deteriorate, back off before committing to that pitch rather than partway across it.
routes.turnaround: Given the avalanche-prone approach and an essentially unprotectable crux traverse, set a firm turnaround point and be willing to retreat if coverage, weather, or avalanche conditions are marginal.
routes.bestSeason: A winter/spring mixed objective that needs enough snow and ice bonded to the slabs to protect and climb safely, so conditions vary significantly year to year — check recent trip reports and the NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast before attempting.
routes.detailedRack: Alpine rock rack plus 10cm ice screws, pickets, and a knife-blade piton for thin/mixed placements (per historical route notes); ice tools and crampons required.
routes.proNeeds: Pitch 2's long horizontal traverse is essentially unprotectable; screws and pickets are marginal elsewhere in thin ice, so protection quality depends heavily on current conditions.
proTips: Confirm current snow/ice coverage via recent trip reports before committing — the crux traverse needs adequate bonding to be feasible
Check the NWAC Snoqualmie Pass avalanche forecast before the approach
watchOut: Pitch 2's ~30 m traverse on 50-60° snow/ice is essentially unprotectable and depends on coverage — retreat is difficult once committed
Route conditions vary significantly year to year
knownHazards: Unprotectable crux traverse pitch on steep snow/ice
Thin ice with long runouts reported in some seasons
Avalanche hazard on approach slopes

pitchDetail.notes: Climbs the entry gully to start the route.
Up to a 30 m, largely unprotectable ascending traverse on a 50-60° snow/ice-covered slab to gain the ridge; feasibility depends on adequate snow/ice coverage.
Ridge pitches, passing a rock prominence on the right.
Traverses a snow- and ice-covered slab to a belay ledge before the short finish to the summit.

road.name: Alpental Road
road.status: Paved, maintained year-round to the Alpental ski area parking lot
road.driveNote: From I-90 take Exit 52, turn northwest onto Alpental Road to the parking area (3,100 ft); winter access can require chains/traction tires per WSDOT pass conditions.

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime Cascades climate — wet, snowy winters right at the crest near Snoqualmie Pass, producing the significant and highly variable avalanche hazard that governs this route's conditions.

bySeason.spring: Consolidating snowpack can offer good coverage on the slabs, but stability and daily warming/wet-slide risk both increase.
bySeason.summer: Route is generally out of condition once snow/ice melts off the slabs.
bySeason.fall: Early-season snow can be thin and poorly bonded.
bySeason.winter: Primary season for the route; heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard on the approach must be actively managed.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: $5 day-use parking fee at the Alpental/Snow Lake trailhead if no pass is displayed
access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass, America the Beautiful interagency pass, or the day-use fee
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency pass

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start recommended for stable snow conditions and to manage avalanche exposure on the approach

emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, North Bend, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, Snoqualmie, WA
emergency.notes: King County Sheriff's Office coordinates search and rescue (including the volunteer Ski Patrol Rescue Team, SPART) for the Alpental/Source Lake basin; Snoqualmie Pass Fire & Rescue provides local EMS.

routes.corrections: No source checked (Mountain Project, Mountaineers.org, SummitPost, thecrag) documents a route named 'Catscratch Couloir' on The Tooth. The winter/mixed line matching the supplied description (NE-facing, alpine ice/mixed, conditions dependent) is universally documented as the 'Northeast Slabs' (Mountain Project route page), first climbed by Jim Nelson & Paul Stevenson in 1982 — all data above is sourced to that route; verify the 'Catscratch' naming locally before publishing. Pitch count also varies slightly by source: Mountain Project's route page states 5 pitches (used here), while some secondary trip-report narratives describe up to 6 rope-lengths depending on rope stretch/anchor choice.

blurb: The Tooth is a 5,606-foot andesite pillar in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), rising above Source Lake just northwest of Snoqualmie Pass and the Alpental ski area. Its blocky South Face is one of the most popular moderate alpine rock climbs near Seattle, first climbed in 1928 (peak's overall first ascent, via an easier line, dates to 1916). A winter/spring mixed line up the peak's northeast slabs offers a snow-and-ice alternative when the summer rock route is out of condition.

routes.overview: A remote, off-trail scramble up Mount Price's north side: leave the Dingford Creek Trail near Goat Creek, climb cross-country through brush and below cliff bands to the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake, then follow the connecting ridge south to the summit.
routes.beta: Sources describe three commonly used lines up Mount Price: this North Route (Dingford Creek/Goat Creek drainage), a route via Hester Lake on the northeast side, and a southeast-slope route. The North Route follows the Dingford Creek Trail about 2.1 miles (4-5 creek crossings), then leaves the trail near Goat Creek (~2,570 ft), crosses Dingford Creek, and climbs cross-country on a bearing of roughly 130°, staying below cliff bands on an ascending traverse to the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake, then follows the ridge south to the 5,587-5,592 ft summit. It is class 2-3 routefinding terrain with a short section of moss-covered boulder scrambling to gain the saddle.
routes.approach: From Seattle/I-90, take exit 34 (Edgewick Rd), go north to the Middle Fork Snoqualmie Road (FR 56), and continue about 12.5 miles past the Taylor River Road junction, then follow FR 5620 about 5 miles to the Dingford Creek Trailhead (1,400 ft). Hike the Dingford Creek Trail roughly 2.1 miles, crossing the creek 4-5 times, to about 2,570 ft near Goat Creek, then leave the trail and climb cross-country toward the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: return to the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake and descend through the brush/talus slopes back to the Dingford Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, follow the ridge back north to the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake, then descend the same ascending-traverse line below the cliff bands, dropping to the unnamed lake and Dingford Creek crossing near Goat Creek (~2,570 ft), and finish on the Dingford Creek Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties complete the North Route as a long single day (car-to-car) from the Dingford Creek Trailhead given the roughly 10-mile round trip and heavy brush low on the route; some climbers doing it with lingering snow or combining it with Hester Lake break the trip into two days.
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the ascent line back to the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake and down through the drainage to the Dingford Creek Trail; there is no shortcut once past the brush and cliff-band terrain.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent retraces brushy, routefinding-dependent terrain with minor cliff bands and creek crossings, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to get back below the brush and across Dingford Creek before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed when a lingering spring snowpack still covers the dense brush and devil's club below about 4,000 ft, which speeds travel considerably; by mid-to-late summer the brush has grown in and the approach becomes noticeably more tedious, though the peak is sometimes climbed late season when higher alpine objectives are still snow-bound.
gear: Map/compass or GPS for off-trail routefinding
whatToBring: Long sleeves/pants for brush (devil's club, slide alder)
proTips: Time the climb for a lingering spring snowpack to bury the devil's club and brush below about 4,000 ft, which makes travel much faster.
On the related Price Creek gully approach, exiting the gully to climber's right below about 3,200 ft and ascending the slopes west of the creek avoids the gully's narrowest, most technical section.
watchOut: Dense brush and devil's club low on the route make routefinding and pace unpredictable.
Minor cliff bands below the saddle require a careful ascending traverse to stay beneath them.
Multiple unbridged crossings of Dingford Creek on the trail approach can be difficult in high water.
knownHazards: Devil's club and slide-alder brush between the trail and roughly 4,000 ft.
Loose rock and talus on the upper mountain and summit ridge.
Cliff bands on the approach to the saddle northwest of Little Hester Lake that require careful routefinding to avoid.
routes.face: North side (Dingford Creek / Goat Creek drainage)

road.name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Road (FR 56) / FR 5620
road.status: Gravel forest roads; rough, high-clearance recommended, especially on FR 5620
road.driveNote: From I-90 exit 34 (Edgewick Rd), follow SE Middle Fork Rd (FR 56) about 12.5 miles past the Taylor River Road junction, then FR 5620 about 5 miles to the Dingford Creek Trailhead.

climate.typical: Wet, mild western Cascades maritime climate; snow can linger into early summer at higher elevations on the route.

access.landManager: U.S. Forest Service, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District); route lies within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: No fee currently charged at the Dingford Creek Trailhead per USFS
access.permit: Standard Alpine Lakes Wilderness travel regulations apply; no special day-use permit required per the USFS trailhead listing
access.passRequired: USFS states no pass currently required at Dingford Creek Trailhead; some third-party sources list a Northwest Forest Pass, so check current trailhead signage

sectionBreakdown.section: Dingford Creek Trail to Goat Creek
sectionBreakdown.note: ~2.1 miles, 4-5 creek crossings

sectionBreakdown.section: Cross-country to saddle NW of Little Hester Lake
sectionBreakdown.note: Off-trail; dense brush/devil's club below ~4,000 ft, ascending traverse below cliff bands
Follow the connecting ridge southerly

emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office, 902 SE North Bend Way, Bldg 1, North Bend, WA 98045 — (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — (425) 831-2300 (24-hour ED)
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area is coordinated by the King County Sheriff's Office; call 911 in an emergency.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 5,592 ft is very close to but not identical across sources: Peakbagger/PeakVisor list Mount Price at 1,703 m / 5,587 ft, while a topozone.com listing shows a slightly different summit-point elevation near 5,535-5,587 ft depending on datum/summit point used. Treat 5,587-5,592 ft as the accepted range rather than a hard conflict.

blurb: Mount Price (about 5,587–5,592 ft) is a mostly forested summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Washington Cascades, rising above the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River near North Bend. It is the highest peak in the area bounded by the Snoqualmie River to the south and the Dingford Creek drainage to the west, north, and northeast, with steep southern slopes climbing roughly 4,000 vertical feet above the valley floor. Unlike many nearby summits it has no maintained trail to the top — the standard lines are off-trail routefinding scrambles through brush, talus, and minor cliff bands out of the Dingford Creek Trailhead.

routes.overview: The classic, non-technical-approach line up Mount Index's Main Peak: a long scramble from Lake Serene up the east ridge and Hourglass Gully to the summit, historically approached from the north side of the lake.
routes.beta: Follows the Lake Serene trail to the lake, skirts around it (usually via the west/right side over talus and boulders), then climbs a steep forested ridge and snow/rock gully (Hourglass Gully) to the summit ridge. Involves sustained brush, class 4 to low 5th-class rock, and typically one steep snow or rock step near 3,500 ft rated up to 5.6.
routes.approach: From US-2 at milepost 35.2, turn south on Mount Index Road and reach the Lake Serene/Bridal Veil Falls trailhead in about 0.3 mi. Hike the maintained Lake Serene trail 4.1 mi and about 2,000 ft of gain to the north end of the lake, then traverse talus/boulders around the west side to its south end before beginning the technical climbing.
routes.descent: Same route, with a rappel (often 1-3 raps) through the steep rock/tree step near 3,500 ft and a downclimb of the gully.
routes.descentText: Descend the summit ridge and re-enter Hourglass Gully, glissading or boot-skiing snow where present; at the steep rock/brush step on the ridge near 3,500 ft, most parties rappel (reports describe one to three rappels using slings/trees) rather than downclimb, then continue down through avalanche debris and thick brush back to the lake and out the maintained trail.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally by reversing the ascent line; below the crux rock/brush step, downclimbing or a single rappel back to the ridge/talus allows a return to the lake basin and the maintained trail.
routes.turnaround: Given the long round trip and technical brush/rock/snow sections that are far slower to descend in the dark or in worsening weather, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and current party pace, and commit to it regardless of proximity to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in late spring/early summer (roughly May-June) when snow still covers the notoriously thick brush band (3,000-3,600 ft) and fills the gully for easier travel; by mid-to-late summer the brush is exposed and the gully requires low 5th-class rock and can be dry.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be assumed once off Highway 2 and into the Lake Serene basin; this is a remote, steep alpine setting with limited signal.
whatToBring: Extra water (little to no water on route)
routes.detailedRack: A single light alpine rack (small set of cams/nuts) plus pickets or an ice axe for early-season snow; carry tat/slings for the rappel(s) off the steep rock/brush step, and a 30-60m rope is typical.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are modest overall (mostly class 4 with a short low-5th step); the main use of gear is for the rappel anchors on the steep east-ridge step rather than sustained free-climbing protection.
proTips: Go early season (May-June) so snow covers the brutal brush band between 3,000-3,600 ft and fills Hourglass Gully
Start with headlamps to bank daylight for the technical middle section
Fill water at Lake Serene — there is little to none higher on route
watchOut: Extremely thick, soul-crushing brush between roughly 3,000 and 3,600 ft when out of snow
Loose rock throughout, especially overhead in the gully in early season
Long, committing round trip (15+ hours reported by multiple parties)
knownHazards: Rockfall and icefall in Hourglass Gully, worse in early season with more overhead snow/ice
Steep, exposed brush/tree/rock step on the east ridge near 3,500 ft, often wet or icy
Avalanche debris and cracking lake ice encountered on winter/spring ascents

road.name: Mount Index Road
road.status: Paved US-2 to a short gravel/dirt spur
road.driveNote: Turn south off US-2 at milepost 35.2 onto Mount Index Road, then right again 0.3 mi to the Lake Serene/Bridal Veil Falls trailhead parking lot.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes Central
climate.typical: Wet, temperate Cascades climate with a distinct wet season Oct-Apr and a drier window Jun-Sep.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Skykomish Ranger District)
access.fees: Day-use fee at the trailhead
access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency/America the Beautiful pass required at the trailhead parking lot
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (SAR coordinated via Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue, SCVSAR)
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett (Level II trauma center serving Snohomish County)

waypoints.name: Lake Serene (approach lake, water source, then talus to base of buttress)

routes.overview: A roughly 2,000-ft big-wall line up the North Norwegian Buttress, the prominent granodiorite wall on Mount Index's north side clearly visible from US-2, historically climbed as a multi-day aid/free line.
routes.beta: Approach via Lake Serene to the base of the wall, then climb sustained, often loose rock on the buttress; the original (Doorish) line and later variations (e.g. Jötnar, VI 5.9 A3+, 16 pitches, 2,000 ft, established 2020 by Sam Boyce, Lani Chapko, and Kyle Willis) ascend the buttress in multi-day pushes with fixed lines and portaledge camps; the wall was later freed as the route Bluebell (21 pitches, 5.13-).
routes.approach: Hike the maintained trail to Lake Serene (3.6 mi, roughly 1.5-2 hrs), then follow a short unofficial path around the right (north) side of the lake, descend granite slabs to the talus, refill water at the lake, and continue up talus to the base of the wall (about 30 minutes from the lake).
gear: Portaledge and fixed lines (for multi-day ascents)
whatToBring: Multi-day food/water (wall is dry; haul water from the lake)
Portaledge for bivies on longer pushes
proTips: Fill all water at Lake Serene — the wall itself is dry
This has never been a popular wall despite its size and roadside visibility; expect loose rock and route-finding challenges typical of an unpopular big Cascades face
watchOut: Sustained loose ('chossy') rock reported on multiple ascents
Seasonal water streaks/waterfalls on parts of the wall depending on conditions
Long, committing multi-day nature of most ascents to date
knownHazards: Rockfall on a large, infrequently-traveled alpine big wall
Significant dehydration risk noted by a 2020 ascent party who turned back before continuing to Middle Index

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes Central
climate.typical: Wet, temperate Cascades climate; wall best attempted in the drier summer window (Jun-Aug).

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Skykomish Ranger District)
access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass required at the Lake Serene trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (SAR via SCVSAR)
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett (Level II trauma center serving Snohomish County)

routes.corrections: The given grade (V, 5.9) matches the general difficulty of the original/moderate lines on this buttress reported by sources (e.g., the Doorish original route era); note that specific documented lines on this buttress vary widely in difficulty and commitment — Jötnar is VI 5.9 A3+ (16 pitches, 2,000 ft) and the freed Bluebell is 5.13- (21 pitches), so the seed grade likely represents the original/aid-era line rather than the harder modern free variations.

routes.overview: A technical line up the steep northeast side of Mount Index's North Peak, in the same general area as the peak's historic 1929 first-ascent line up its northeast corner.
routes.beta: Sources confirm a 1929 first ascent of the North Peak via its steep northeast corner (L. Chute, G. Tepley and a companion, per American Alpine Club records), and describe the standard North Face Route on the North Peak (Grade III, 5.6-5.7) as beginning from a broken, bushy spur on the northeast side before angling onto NNE-facing terrain. However, no reputable source available online independently documents a distinctly named, IV/5.8 'Northeast Buttress' route on Mount Index separate from these — most named Northeast Buttress climbs found in research are on other Cascades peaks (Chair Peak, Mount Goode, Mount Slesse). Route-specific technical details (FA, pitch count, length, pitch-by-pitch beta) could not be independently confirmed and are left null rather than guessed; general approach/access/hazard information shared by all Mount Index technical routes is provided below.
routes.approach: All technical routes on the North Peak share the Lake Serene approach: hike the maintained Lake Serene trail (about 4.1 mi, ~2,000 ft gain) to the lake, then traverse around it toward the base of the North Peak's northeast-facing terrain.
watchOut: Loose, sometimes rotten rock reported broadly across Mount Index's less-traveled faces
Thick brush low on approach ridges
Serious route-finding difficulty on North Peak lines, repeatedly flagged as the main challenge in trip reports
knownHazards: Rockfall on steep, loose alpine terrain
Long, remote, committing routes with no easy bail in the upper mountain

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (SAR via SCVSAR)

routes.corrections: No independent reputable online source could confirm a distinctly named 'Northeast Buttress' route (IV, 5.8) on Mount Index separate from the peak's historic northeast-corner/North Face line on the North Peak; several route-specific fields are left null pending a print-guidebook source (e.g., Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide) that could not be accessed online.

blurb: Mount Index is a 5,991-ft rock massif of three summits (North, Middle, Main) in the central Cascades of Snohomish/King counties, rising abruptly above the Skykomish River and US Highway 2 near the town of Index. Its steep, often loose granodiorite/gneiss walls include the nearly 2,000-ft Norwegian Buttresses on the north side, among the biggest and least-traveled big-wall faces in the Lower 48. The Main Peak's first recorded ascent was October 29, 1911 by H. B. Hinman, Ernest Martin, Lee Pickett, and George E. Wright, who found a flagpole already on the summit, evidence of an earlier, undocumented climb; the North Peak was first climbed in 1929 via its steep northeast corner.

Mount Herman (officially renamed "Mount Hermann" by the U.S. Board on Geographic Names in 1988) is a 6,240-foot summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, Whatcom County, sitting in the Mount Baker Wilderness immediately west of the Mt. Baker Ski Area at the end of Mount Baker Highway (SR 542). The mountain is built from Cascade volcanic-arc terranes and was heavily sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation; it is named for Binger Hermann (1843-1926), a former head of the U.S. General Land Office. It is a popular, moderate objective climbed both as a summer class 2-3 scramble via Herman Saddle and as a winter/spring ski-mountaineering tour.

routes.overview: A moderate hike-and-scramble up Mount Herman's south ridge, reached via the Bagley Lakes Trail and Herman Saddle just outside the Mt. Baker Ski Area; mostly on trail and open slopes with a short class 3 section near the top.
routes.beta: From the Heather Meadows/Bagley Lakes trailhead, follow the maintained Bagley Lakes Trail up toward Herman Saddle, then leave the trail near the last switchback to gain the ridge between Mazama Dome and Mount Herman. From there the route generally follows the south/southwest ridge to the summit over a mix of dirt, talus, and short class 3 rock steps; in early season the ridge and gullies hold snow and are often climbed with an ice axe and crampons, or skied/split-boarded as a ski-mountaineering tour. It is also frequently combined with a loop past Chain Lakes.
routes.approach: Drive Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) east from Bellingham/Glacier to the Heather Meadows Visitor Center parking area (about 55 miles from Bellingham, elevation roughly 4,400 ft). Start on the Bagley Lakes Trail past the lakes and climb toward Herman Saddle.
routes.descent: Reverse the south ridge/shoulder line back down to Herman Saddle and the Bagley Lakes Trail.
routes.descentText: Descend the same south/southwest ridge used on ascent. Some parties avoid the steeper west gully on the way down by following the broader south shoulder instead, which involves a short section of steep snow and down-scrambling before regaining easier ground; in winter this same shoulder is a common ski/snowboard descent line back toward Herman Saddle and Bagley Lakes.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single long day: trailhead to Herman Saddle, ridge to the summit, then reverse the route back to the car; can be paired with a Chain Lakes loop or Table Mountain circuit for a bigger day.
routes.bail: The route is short and low-commitment - retreat is straightforward at any point back down the ridge to Herman Saddle and onto the maintained Bagley Lakes Trail.
routes.turnaround: Because the upper ridge and gullies hold snow into summer and can develop cornices and breakable crust, set a turnaround time before starting and be willing to back off if snow conditions, visibility, or afternoon warming make the ridge or gully unstable.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a dry scramble from roughly July through September, when the south ridge is mostly snow-free class 2-3 terrain; it is also a well-known winter and spring ski-mountaineering/snowshoe objective via Herman Saddle, though that requires avalanche awareness and, often, an ice axe and crampons for the ridge itself.
objHaz: loose/exposed rock on the upper ridge and short class 3 steps
lingering steep snow and possible cornices near Herman Saddle in winter/spring
breakable crust and rapid afternoon warming on south-facing snow
a moat/gap between snow and rock near the summit col in late-season conditions
knownHazards: The ridge and adjoining gullies hold snow well into summer, requiring an ice axe/crampons and creating cornice and moat hazard near Herman Saddle and the summit col.
The top of the west gully variant can be melted out with loose, down-sloping rock late in the season.
As a winter ski/snowshoe tour via Herman Saddle, the route crosses avalanche-prone slopes and should be assessed with current Northwest Avalanche Center (Mount Baker zone) conditions.
watchOut: Loose rock on the short class 3 sections near the summit.
Cornices and wind-loaded snow near Herman Saddle after storms.
Breakable crust and softening south-facing snow later in the day.
Route-finding off-trail between the last Bagley Lakes Trail switchback and the summit ridge, especially in poor visibility.
proTips: Leave the Bagley Lakes Trail at the last switchback below Herman Saddle to gain the ridge between Mazama Dome and Mount Herman.
The broader south shoulder is a gentler alternative to the steeper west gully, especially useful as a descent line.
Check current Bagley Lakes/Herman Saddle avalanche and snow conditions before an early-season or winter attempt.
Combine with Chain Lakes or Table Mountain for a longer day from the same Heather Meadows trailhead.
gear: ice axe and crampons (early season, spring, or winter ascents)
helmet recommended for loose rock on the upper ridge
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee for the Heather Meadows trailhead
map/GPS - the route is unmarked above the Bagley Lakes Trail
layers for the exposed, often windy ridge

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (SR 542)
road.status: paved; maintained/plowed year-round to Heather Meadows (around milepost 54)
road.seasonalGate: The final ~2.7 miles beyond Heather Meadows to Artist Point is gated and closed to vehicles roughly mid-October through early-to-mid June, depending on snowpack.
road.driveNote: Drive SR 542 east from Bellingham/Glacier to the Heather Meadows Visitor Center parking area; this stretch of highway is kept open in winter even when the final leg to Artist Point is closed.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker zone (Northwest Avalanche Center)
climate.typical: Wet, cloudy, and cool for much of the year with very heavy snowfall in winter - the adjacent Mt. Baker Ski Area holds the world record for single-season snowfall (1,140 inches, 1998-99).

bySeason.spring: Deep, lingering snowpack into June with avalanche hazard on steeper slopes near Herman Saddle; a popular ski-mountaineering season.
bySeason.summer: Driest and warmest window (Jul-Sep); the standard south ridge is typically mostly snow-free.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and increasing chance of early-season snow; the road to Artist Point closes for the season around mid-October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and avalanche terrain; Mount Herman is a well-known winter ski/snowshoe tour via Herman Saddle.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker Ranger District (Mount Baker Wilderness)
access.fees: Day-use fee applies at the Heather Meadows trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent interagency pass) required for parking at Heather Meadows.
access.closures: Vehicle access beyond Heather Meadows to Artist Point closes seasonally (roughly mid-October to early/mid-June); Heather Meadows itself is reachable via the plowed highway most of the year, conditions permitting.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office - 911 for emergencies; non-emergency dispatch (360) 676-6911. Search and rescue is coordinated through the Whatcom County Sheriff's Office with Whatcom County Search & Rescue volunteers.
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center (Mt. Baker Ranger District), 10091 Mt. Baker Hwy, Glacier, WA 98244, (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA - Whatcom County's only hospital, with a Level II trauma/emergency center.

routes.corrections: Given elevation (6,285 ft) conflicts with sourced elevation: Wikipedia and PeakVisor both list Mount Herman/Hermann at 6,240 ft (also confirmed by search snippets referencing the same figure); used 6,240 ft as highPointFt. The peak's official USBGN name since 1988 is 'Mount Hermann,' though 'Mount Herman' remains the common usage matching this catalog entry. Could not independently verify the given alpineGrade 'F' from any route-specific source (this is a class 2-3 hike/scramble, not typically assigned a French alpine grade), so it was left null rather than repeated unsourced.

blurb: Yellow Aster Butte (6,241 ft) is a summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, within the Mount Baker Wilderness of Whatcom County, about three miles south of the Canadian border. Its bedrock is the "Yellow Aster Gneiss" — roughly 400-million-year-old metamorphic rock exposed alongside the younger, mixed volcanic-sedimentary Chilliwack River terrane — among the oldest exposed rock in the Pacific Northwest. It is reached by a highly popular alpine-meadow trail known for summer wildflowers and fall color, with summit views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, Tomyhoi Peak, and the Border Peaks; no technical first-ascent record is documented for this hike/scramble summit.

objHaz: exposure on the narrow summit ridge (steep drop-offs both sides)
loose or wet rock on the final scramble
lingering snow/ice on the shaded ridge into early summer
mountain goats in the area (FS-posted alert)
lightning exposure on open ridge and meadows
biting insects in the meadow sections
routes.face: North ridge (final scramble section to the true summit)
routes.overview: A very popular North Cascades trail hike that climbs through avalanche-path switchbacks and alpine meadows to a broad false-summit viewpoint (6,178 ft), then finishes with a short, exposed Class 2 scramble along a narrow ridge to Yellow Aster Butte's true summit (6,241 ft).
routes.beta: From the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead, follow the shared Tomyhoi Lake Trail through steep switchbacks up an old avalanche path, then split left onto the Yellow Aster Butte Trail. The trail flattens and traverses the south flank of the butte through meadows and past tarns before climbing to the false summit, a broad viewpoint most day hikers treat as the destination. Reaching the true summit requires continuing along the increasingly narrow north ridge with light Class 2 scrambling and real exposure on both sides in spots; conditions and perceived difficulty vary a lot with snow cover.
routes.approach: From the Glacier Public Service Center on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542), drive east about 12 miles and turn left (north) onto Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065), at the east end of the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance facility. Follow FR 3065 about 4.5 miles to the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead (~3,700 ft); the road is unpaved, narrow, and potholed with limited pullouts but is generally passable to sedans driven carefully to this point.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route: reverse the ridge scramble back to the false summit, then follow the maintained trail back down through the meadow traverse and switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, carefully reverse the ridge scramble back to the false summit viewpoint, taking extra care on any wet, icy, or snow-covered rock given the exposure on both sides of the ridge. From the false summit, rejoin the maintained trail and retrace the traverse around the south flank of the butte, back through the meadow and tarn area, then descend the switchbacks through the old avalanche path to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Standard outing is a long day hike: trailhead to false summit (~4 mi, ~2,500 ft gain), continue via the ridge scramble to the true summit (~0.4 mi further), then return the same way — typically 5.5-6+ hours round trip for the full summit. Some parties turn it into an overnight by camping at the tarns near the 4-mile mark (camping is restricted to designated sites at/beyond the tarns).
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at any point along the maintained trail with no consequence; the false summit (6,178 ft) is a natural bailout that gives a full panoramic view without any of the ridge's exposure if the final scramble looks unsafe (wet rock, snow, thunderstorms, or party comfort level).
routes.turnaround: Because the final ridge scramble has real exposure on both sides, turn around before committing to it in poor visibility, on wet or snow/ice-covered rock, or if thunderstorms are building — the maintained trail and false summit are a safe, satisfying fallback.
routes.bestSeason: Best from late July through early October; snow can linger on the shaded north ridge into July even after the lower trail melts out. Early August brings peak wildflower blooms in the meadows, while late September into early October brings huckleberry and larch fall color. The road and trail are generally not accessible in winter/spring due to snow.
routes.proNeeds: No rope or rack is used on this route — it's an unroped Class 2 trail-scramble. Trekking poles help on the steep lower switchbacks, and in early season (June-July) an ice axe and/or microspikes are useful for lingering snow patches on the shaded summit ridge.
gear: microspikes or ice axe (early-season snow on the ridge)
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass for trailhead parking
extra layers for the exposed summit ridge
system to pack out human waste (required — no cathole digging permitted on this trail)
proTips: Arrive by 8 AM in season — the small trailhead lot fills quickly on weekends
The false summit alone is a worthwhile, non-exposed goal if you don't want the ridge scramble
Camp only at the designated tarns (~4 miles in) if backpacking — camping elsewhere within a mile of the trail is prohibited
Check Twin Lakes Road/FR 3065 conditions with the Glacier Public Service Center before driving, since it can be washed out or snow-blocked
watchOut: The ridge to the true summit narrows with steep drop-offs on both sides — not recommended in wet or icy conditions
Snow can linger on the ridge well into summer, changing the difficulty significantly from dry-rock conditions
Mountain goats and black bears are regularly present — store food securely and keep distance from goats
No potable water is available at the trailhead or along most of the route
knownHazards: Exposed, narrow ridge scramble to the true summit with steep terrain on both sides
Lingering snow/ice on the shaded north ridge into early summer can turn an easy Class 2 scramble into a much more serious undertaking
Afternoon thunderstorms are a lightning hazard on the open ridge and meadows
Loose rock in places on the final scramble

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065)
road.status: Unpaved, narrow, and potholed with limited pullouts; passable to standard passenger vehicles with careful, slow driving as far as the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead (4.5 mi from SR 542). Beyond the trailhead the road narrows further and is not maintained for passenger cars — high-clearance recommended for the additional 2 miles to Twin Lakes.
road.seasonalGate: Not gated, but typically snow-blocked and impassable from late fall through late spring/early summer; check current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving.
road.driveNote: Turn onto FR 3065 about 12 miles east of the Glacier Public Service Center on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542), at the east end of the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance facility; the trailhead is 4.5 miles up FR 3065 at roughly 3,700 ft.

climate.typical: Best hiking window is roughly late July through early October; the trail and access road are typically snowed in outside that window, and snow can linger on the shaded summit ridge into July.

bySeason.spring: Access road and trail typically still snow-covered and not passable to the trailhead into early summer.
bySeason.summer: Prime season; peak wildflower bloom in the meadows around early August, warm days, but afternoon thunderstorm risk on the open ridge.
bySeason.fall: Late September into early October brings huckleberry and larch color; cooler temperatures and shortening daylight.
bySeason.winter: Road and trail are snowbound and not accessible to vehicles.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mount Baker Ranger District; Mount Baker Wilderness (wilderness regulations apply above the trailhead)
access.fees: Day-use fee of $5/vehicle/day or $30/year at the trailhead
access.permit: No wilderness entry permit required for day use; visitors must pack out all human waste rather than burying it, due to fragile alpine soils
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/Interagency Recreation Pass (Washington State Discover Pass is NOT accepted at this Forest Service fee site)
access.closures: No camping within one mile of the Yellow Aster Butte Trail except at the designated tarns (~4 mi in); no campfires; hiker-only trail

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning — both for the small trailhead parking lot and to be off the exposed summit ridge before any afternoon thunderstorms

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead (~3,700 ft) to Yellow Aster Butte Trail split
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep switchbacks up an old avalanche path, ~1,400 ft gain in about 1.5-1.7 mi, shared with the Tomyhoi Lake Trail

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trail junction to base of the summit ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Trail flattens and traverses the south flank of the butte through meadows and past tarns, roughly 2 miles

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Ridge base to false summit viewpoint (6,178 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~0.3 mi of maintained trail; most day hikers stop here

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: False summit to true summit (6,241 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~0.4 mi Class 2 scramble along a narrowing, exposed ridge

waypoints.name: Tomyhoi Lake / Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office (911 for emergencies); backcountry search-and-rescue response is coordinated through Whatcom County Search & Rescue, including Bellingham Mountain Rescue
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, Mount Baker Ranger District — (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA (roughly a 1-hour drive from the trailhead)
emergency.notes: No potable water at the trailhead; mountain goats and black bears are regularly present in the area per Forest Service alerts.

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevation of 6,257 ft is not supported by any source found; Wikipedia, ListsOfJohn, and multiple trail-data sites consistently list Yellow Aster Butte's true summit at 6,241 ft, which is used here as highPointFt. Note also that most 'official' trail stats (Forest Service, WTA) describe the maintained-trail high point at the false summit (6,178 ft, ~4 mi one-way); reaching the true summit (6,241 ft) requires an additional ~0.4 mi of Class 2 ridge scrambling beyond the end of maintained tread.

blurb: Table Mountain is a 5,742-foot summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, in Whatcom County's Mount Baker Wilderness, west of Mount Shuksan and northeast of Mount Baker. It formed from an unusually thick andesite lava flow with columnar jointing, likely once constrained by glacial ice, and is reached by a short, steep trail from Artist Point — the highest point reachable by car in Whatcom County. The plateau summit offers panoramic views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, and the Chain Lakes basin.

routes.overview: A short, steep out-and-back trail scramble from Artist Point that switchbacks up Table Mountain's exposed southeast cliff face onto a broad volcanic plateau, with sweeping views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, and the Chain Lakes basin.
routes.beta: From the Artist Point parking lot (about 5,075 ft), the Table Mountain Trail climbs a series of tight, exposed switchbacks cut into the mountain's southeast-facing rock face for roughly 0.6-1 mile to gain the broad summit plateau near 5,700 ft. Beyond the switchbacks the trail is gentle, crossing the plateau to tarns with Shuksan views around the 1.3-mile mark; most hikers turn around there. The trail continues west and eventually drops to link with the Chain Lakes Trail system. The route is entirely above treeline, narrow and cliff-edge in its lower half, and not recommended for those uneasy with exposure, small children, or dogs.
routes.approach: Drive SR 542 (Mount Baker Highway) east from Glacier about 24 miles to its terminus at the Artist Point parking lot. The trail begins at the northwest corner of the lot, opposite the restrooms.
routes.descent: Descend the same switchbacked trail back to Artist Point.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent: from the plateau, head back east to the top of the switchbacks and down-climb the exposed, cliff-cut trail carefully, especially if snow, ice, or loose gravel is present. Some parties instead continue west across the plateau and loop down via the Chain Lakes Trail to Bagley Lakes, returning to Artist Point via the Wild Goose Trail, which significantly extends the outing.
routes.bail: There is no technical commitment on this trail — simply turn around and descend the way you came at any point.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before starting the switchbacks if thunderstorms are building or snow/ice coats the exposed traverse, and turn back at any point if a member of the party is uncomfortable with the airy, cliff-edge tread — there is no easier alternative once committed to the narrowest section.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-July through September, after SR 542 opens for the season (typically mid-to-late June, varying with snowpack) and the trail has melted out; the road usually closes for winter by mid-to-late October. Snow and ice can linger on the shaded switchbacks into July.
gear: Trekking poles (helpful on the steep switchbacks)
Map or GPS (boot paths braid on the plateau)
whatToBring: Sun protection (route is entirely above treeline)
Microspikes/traction if snow or ice is present on the switchbacks
proTips: Arrive early or hike on a weekday — the Artist Point lot fills quickly on summer weekends
Continue west across the plateau to link up with the Chain Lakes Trail for a longer loop
Morning light is best for Mount Shuksan views from the plateau
watchOut: The lower switchbacks are cut into an exposed rock face with real fall consequences; not for those afraid of heights
Loose gravel and rock on the switchbacks — avoid kicking rocks onto hikers below
Snow/ice can linger on the exposed traverse well into early summer
knownHazards: Steep, narrow, cliff-edge switchbacks with significant exposure
Rockfall potential on and below the trail
Lingering snow/ice on the shaded switchbacks into early summer
Afternoon thunderstorms with no shelter above treeline

road.name: Mount Baker Highway / SR 542
road.status: Paved state highway, seasonal
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens mid-to-late June and closes mid-to-late October, varying year to year with snowpack
road.driveNote: From Glacier, drive SR 542 east about 24 miles to its terminus at the Artist Point parking lot (elev. ~5,075-5,100 ft), the highest point reachable by car in Whatcom County. Not plowed in winter.

climate.forecastZone: Mt Baker zone (NWAC)
climate.typical: Cool, wet, and heavily snowed-in much of the year, with a short drier window from July through September when the trail is typically snow-free.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack lingers; the road to Artist Point is typically still closed into June.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep); warm days, cool nights, and afternoon thunderstorm risk above treeline.
bySeason.fall: Snow returns by October and the road closes for the season, usually by mid-to-late October.
bySeason.winter: Road closed to Artist Point; heavy snowfall and avalanche terrain cover the trail corridor.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness), Glacier Public Service Center
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required to park at Artist Point; the Washington State Discover Pass is not valid here.
access.permit: No hiking permit required; standard wilderness group-size rules apply.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: No camping in the Table Mountain/Artist Point area May 15-Nov 14; SR 542 to Artist Point is closed to vehicles in winter (roughly mid-October to mid-June).

timing.recommendedStart: Morning start recommended to beat crowds, secure Artist Point parking, and avoid afternoon thunderstorms.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Artist Point trailhead to top of switchbacks
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, exposed switchbacks cut into the southeast cliff face
Gentle walking across the summit plateau with Baker/Shuksan views

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line 360-676-6911
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search & Rescue, a volunteer council coordinated by the Whatcom County Sheriff's Office, covers missions along the Mount Baker Highway corridor including Artist Point and Table Mountain.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (5,744 ft) is close to but not identical to Wikipedia's cited 5,742 ft for Table Mountain (Whatcom County) — both are consistent within normal USGS rounding, no change made to the peak record. The maintained Table Mountain Trail from Artist Point tops out around 5,700 ft (WTA) at a plateau/tarn area; reaching the true topographic summit (5,742 ft) involves additional unmaintained terrain beyond where most hikers turn around, so highPointFt here reflects the trail's documented high point rather than the absolute summit. No reputable source was found assigning a formal alpine grade to this hiking trail, so alpineGrade was left null rather than confirming the given 'F' value.

blurb: Luna Peak (8,311 ft) is the highest summit in the Picket Range, an exceptionally rugged and remote subrange of the North Cascades within North Cascades National Park. Formed as part of the Cascades' ~50-million-year-old terrane assemblage and heavily sculpted by glaciation (the McAllister Glacier persists on its north slopes), it rises more than 6,500 ft above McMillan Creek to the south. First climbed in September 1938 by Bill Cox and Will F. Thompson, it remains one of the least-visited major North Cascades summits owing to its long, brushy, multi-day approach.

routes.fa: Bill Cox and Will F. Thompson, September 1938 (peak first ascent; the original party's line is generally identified with this southeast-slopes-to-south-ridge approach)
routes.face: Southeast-facing slopes/snowfields from the Access Creek basin up to Luna Pass, then the south (southwest) ridge to the summit
routes.overview: The standard line climbs broad southeast-facing slopes and snowfields out of the Access Creek basin up to Luna Pass, then follows the peak's south/southwest ridge over a subsidiary false summit to the true summit, finishing with an exposed but only moderately technical (Class 3, occasionally Class 4) ridge crossing.
routes.beta: Luna Peak has no sustained technical climbing; the route is a long, remote scramble reached via a multi-day approach through Big Beaver and Access Creek. From a high camp near the Access Creek headwaters (~3,650-4,100 ft), parties climb scree/talus and a dirt-and-boulder gully to a col near 5,800-6,300 ft, traverse southeast-facing heather, talus, and snow slopes to Luna Pass (~7,200 ft), then follow the broad south ridge over a false summit to the true summit. The false-to-true-summit crossing is the crux: roughly 500 ft of loose, exposed Class 3-4 ridge, which some parties protect with a running belay or short rope; following ledges on the ridge's east side avoids most Class 4 moves and keeps the crossing closer to Class 3.
routes.approach: From the Ross Dam Trailhead (SR 20, milepost 134), descend to Ross Lake and take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi (advance reservation required) up-lake to the Big Beaver Trail area, avoiding several miles of lakeshore hiking. Follow Big Beaver Trail roughly 10 miles to Luna Camp, continue about 1.5 miles farther to a cairn marking the bushwhack turnoff, ford Big Beaver Creek, and follow increasingly brushy terrain (with an intermittent boot path in recent years) up the Access Creek drainage 3+ miles to a high camp near its headwaters.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the south ridge to Luna Pass, then back down the southeast-facing slopes/snowfields to the Access Creek basin and out the same approach.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend by reversing their ascent line - down the south ridge to Luna Pass, then down the southeast-facing snow/scree slopes back into the Access Creek basin to high camp. At least one party has instead descended a steeper gully off the peak's NE shoulder involving loose downclimbing, pack-lowering, and rappelling; this is not the standard descent and is considerably more hazardous and time-consuming.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-4 day trip: Day 1 - water taxi across Ross Lake and hike Big Beaver Trail to Luna Camp (~10 mi); Day 2 - continue to the Access Creek crossing and bushwhack up-drainage to a high camp near its headwaters; Day 3 - summit day via the col, Luna Pass, and south ridge, returning to high camp; Day 4 - hike back out to the Ross Dam Trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward but slow anywhere below Luna Pass - simply reverse the ascent slopes back into the Access Creek basin. Above Luna Pass, the exposed ridge traverse between the false and true summits is the main commitment point; parties uncomfortable with the loose Class 3-4 terrain should turn back at the false summit rather than press on in poor weather or failing light.
routes.turnaround: Given the long multi-day approach and the loose, exposed ridge crossing near the summit, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather rather than pushing to complete the traverse in deteriorating conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September), once seasonal snow has diminished on the Access Creek approach slopes; documented trip reports span mid-July through late August. Earlier-season attempts encounter more snow on the approach slopes (an ice axe is useful), while fall attempts have less daylight for the long approach and bushwhack.
gear: light rack (slings, a few small-to-mid pieces)
whatToBring: map/compass or GPS for the Access Creek route-finding
extra food buffer for weather or route-finding delays
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices: a handful of slings/runners and a few small-to-mid cams or nuts for occasional protection on the loose Class 3-4 ridge crossing near the summit, plus a 30-35m rope used more for running belays and short lowers/rappels than sustained pitched climbing.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal and concentrated on the short, exposed ridge section between the false and true summits; many parties climb it unroped, while others fix a short rope or running belay across the loose, exposed ledges.
proTips: The Access Creek approach is generally easier and less brushy than the alternative Luna Creek approach, and is the line most modern parties use.
A faint boot path has developed along Access Creek's bank in recent years, easing what was once pure bushwhacking.
Reserve the Ross Lake Resort water taxi well in advance - it saves significant lakeshore hiking to reach Big Beaver Trail.
The false-summit-to-true-summit ridge can be kept mostly Class 3 (instead of full Class 4) by following ledges on the east side of the ridge rather than the crest directly.
watchOut: The ridge between the false and true summit is loose, exposed, and is repeatedly flagged as the crux by trip reports and guidebooks.
Upper scree/talus gully sections on the approach can be loaded with unstable, loose rock underfoot.
Ground-nesting bees and heavy insect pressure have been reported along the brushy summer approach.
knownHazards: Loose, exposed Class 3-4 rock on the ridge traverse between the false and true summits.
Extended off-trail travel with route-finding challenges and dense brush (devil's club, slide alder) in the Access Creek drainage.
Creek fords (Big Beaver Creek) that can be more difficult during high water.
Residual snowfields on the approach slopes into early summer.

pitchDetail.notes: High camp near Access Creek headwaters (~3,650-4,100 ft) up scree/talus and a dirt-filled gully to a col near 5,800-6,300 ft.
Heather, talus, and snowfield traverse across southeast-facing slopes to Luna Pass (~7,200 ft).
Broad ridge scramble from Luna Pass to the false summit.

~500 ft of loose, exposed ridge from the false summit to the true summit; one party reported a ~15 ft Class 4 chimney off the false summit followed by narrow ledges. Following east-side ledges avoids most Class 4 moves.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20) to Ross Dam Trailhead
road.status: Paved and normally open in summer season to the Ross Dam Trailhead (milepost 134)
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes for winter between the Ross Dam Trailhead gate (MP 134) and the Silver Star gate (MP 171); in heavier-snow years the west closure point can move further out to the Newhalem gate (MP 120), cutting off road access to the trailhead itself.
road.driveNote: Trailhead parking is directly off SR 20 at milepost 134; reaching Big Beaver Trail additionally requires either a long lakeshore hike or a reserved Ross Lake Resort water taxi across Ross Lake.

climate.typical: Cool, wet, and highly variable mountain weather typical of the North Cascades; the Pickets sit on the wetter west side of the range crest and hold a heavy winter snowpack with a comparatively short summer weather window.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists on the approach slopes and Access Creek drainage into early summer; travel can be easier with continuous snow cover but wet-slide/avalanche hazard remains on steeper terrain.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul-Sep); most snow has melted from the approach slopes by mid-to-late summer, though patches remain into July; warm, buggy conditions in the lower valleys.
bySeason.fall: Shorter days and increasing chance of early-season snow or storms; still climbable into September in good weather.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible - SR 20 is gated for the season and the approach crosses avalanche-prone, snowbound terrain.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Ross Lake National Recreation Area / North Cascades National Park)
access.fees: No day-use entrance fee; a backcountry camping permit fee applies in summer season (roughly $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee; no charge for youth 15 and under, and no charge outside the mid-May-early October fee season)
access.permit: A backcountry permit is required for all overnight stays in the park's wilderness; obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or via advance reservation.
access.closures: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) is gated and closed for the winter season, typically early December into spring, cutting off road access to the Ross Dam Trailhead.

timing.recommendedStart: Early-morning departure from high camp is recommended to allow a full day for the ridge traverse and a return to camp well before dark.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Access Creek headwaters camp (~3,650-4,100 ft) to col (~5,800-6,300 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Scree/talus and a dirt-filled gully.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Col to Luna Pass / south ridge base
sectionBreakdown.note: Traverse across heather, talus, and snowfields on southeast-facing slopes.
Class 2-3 ridge scramble.
~500 ft of loose, exposed Class 3-4 ridge; some parties use a running belay.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 676-6650; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III trauma center/ER) is the nearest full-service hospital serving this area; smaller clinics exist closer in Concrete and Darrington.
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness location reached only by trail and off-trail travel beyond the Ross Dam Trailhead; response times are long and a satellite communicator is strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Elevation: the supplied data lists 8,290 ft; the most current, cross-confirmed sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Mountain-Forecast.com's 2,533 m) give 8,311 ft, which this entry uses. WTA cites an older 8,285 ft figure. Route naming: published route literature (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide as referenced by trip reports, Wikipedia, SummitPost, Mountain Project) most commonly labels the standard line the 'Southwest Ridge' or 'South Ridge' rather than 'Southeast Slopes'; 'Southeast Slopes' most closely corresponds to the southeast-facing approach slopes/snowfields climbed from the Access Creek basin up to Luna Pass, used by most modern parties before finishing via the south ridge - this entry describes that same standard line. Approach distance/gain also vary noticeably by source: WTA's 36.5 mi round trip / 8,009 ft gain is used here, but other accounts describe round-trip totals as high as ~48 miles depending on the bushwhack line and camp placement.

blurb: Mount Fury sits at the geographic heart of the Northern Picket Range in North Cascades National Park, one of the most remote and rugged massifs in the Lower 48. It has twin summits of near-equal height carved from metamorphic gneiss and hung with several small glaciers; the officially-named (and slightly harder) summit is the West Peak, with the East Peak a closely linked satellite reached along the same approach. First climbed in 1938, the mountain later became a proving ground for extreme alpine ridge climbing, most famously Wayne Wallace's 2006 solo first ascent of the mile-long Mongo Ridge on the South Buttress.

routes.fa: Fred and Helmy Beckey, 1940 (East Peak specifically; the broader Mount Fury massif/West Peak was first climbed in 1938 by the Ptarmigan Climbing Club)
objHaz: sudden fog/whiteout on the summit ridge
devil's club and slide alder bushwhacking on approach
routes.season: mid-Jul to late-Aug (stable Sept possible in good years)
