routes.face: Southeast Glacier / Northeast Ridge from Luna-Fury Col
routes.overview: The standard route on East Fury: a long approach through Access Creek to Luna-Fury Col, then a rock ridge traverse to the base of the Southeast Glacier, which is climbed on 30-50 degree snow/ice past a bergschrund to a summit snow arete.
routes.beta: From the Luna-Fury col, traverse the northeast ridge on ledgy, sometimes wet Class 3 rock past a small rock knob, descend a narrow ramp to a snow saddle, cross a short step of low 5th-class rock, then descend a heather chute and exposed rocky-rib traverse to reach the base of the Southeast Glacier. Ascend the glacier (crevasses present) on an S-shaped line, crossing a bergschrund around 7,900 ft, with the final summit snowfield steepening to near 50 degrees before a narrow snow arete leads to the top.
routes.approach: From the Ross Dam trailhead (Hwy 20, milepost 134) take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi (reservation required) to the Big Beaver Trail landing, then hike the maintained Big Beaver Trail roughly 11-12 miles to Luna Camp. Leave the trail and bushwhack/scramble up Access Creek (dense brush, log/creek crossings) to Access Basin (~4,300 ft), then climb a steep scree/heather chute to Access Col (~6,100 ft) and traverse heather and talus slopes to Luna-Fury Col (~7,200-7,300 ft), the standard high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back down the Southeast Glacier, rocky-rib traverse, and northeast ridge to Luna-Fury Col.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the snow arete and Southeast Glacier on the ascent line (roped travel recommended given crevasse hazard), re-cross the bergschrund, and regain the rocky-rib traverse and northeast ridge back to Luna-Fury Col. From the col, retrace the approach: down through Access Col and Access Basin, then the Access Creek bushwhack back to the Big Beaver Trail and out to the water taxi landing.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: water taxi + hike Big Beaver Trail to Luna Camp/Access Creek approach, camp in Access Basin (~4,300 ft). Day 2: climb to Access Col and traverse to Luna-Fury Col, establish high camp (~7,200-7,300 ft); optionally tag Luna Peak. Day 3: alpine start (commonly ~4:30 am) for the summit push via the northeast ridge and Southeast Glacier (roughly 5-8 hours up depending on conditions), return to camp same day. Day 4: descend fully back to the trailhead/water-taxi landing.
routes.bail: The northeast ridge traverse and rocky-rib section below the glacier offer natural retreat points back to Luna-Fury Col in deteriorating weather; once established on the Southeast Glacier, retreat is by reversing the same snow/ice line rather than a shortcut.
routes.turnaround: Because the summit day mixes glacier travel, exposed rock traverses, and a multi-day remote approach, set a firm turnaround time before starting and commit to it regardless of proximity to the summit — deteriorating weather or fog can make the ridge traverse and glacier descent significantly harder to navigate in the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best conditions are typically mid-July through late August, once the approach brush has dried out and the glacier is more consolidated; some years offer a stable weather window into September, but early-season snow can make Access Creek and the col crossings more hazardous.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected anywhere on the approach or route; this is one of the most remote climbs in Washington and satellite communication (satellite messenger/phone) is strongly advised for emergencies.
routes.proNeeds: A light rack is useful for the brief low-5th-class rock steps on the ridge, plus a couple of snow pickets or flukes for glacier protection and belays around the bergschrund.
gear: 30m rope (or two shorter ropes for team glacier travel)
whatToBring: bivy/camping gear for a 3-4 day trip
gaiters and brush-resistant clothing for bushwhacking
map/compass or GPS with the route loaded
proTips: Descending the ridge's rock knob is done via a narrow ramp on its left (west) side, not the right side some older guidebook descriptions suggest.
Reserve the Ross Lake Resort water taxi well in advance; the trip's start and end times are built around its schedule.
Fog can roll in quickly on the northeast ridge traverse — note landmarks on the way in for the return.
watchOut: Crevasses and a bergschrund on the Southeast Glacier require roped travel and solid snow/ice protection skills.
The rocky-rib traverse below the glacier is exposed and has been called the 'diciest' part of the route by parties who have climbed it.
Access Creek bushwhacking involves devil's club, slide alder, and biting insects that can slow travel significantly.
knownHazards: Glacier crevasses and a bergschrund near 7,900 ft on the summit snowfield.
Loose rock on the northeast ridge scramble sections.
Steep snow (up to ~50 degrees) near the top requiring solid self-arrest and cramponing skills.
Extremely long, remote approach with no rescue access beyond what climbers can self-arrange or trigger via satellite communicator.

pitchDetail.notes: Ledgy, sometimes wet rock; pass below a rock knob above the col.
Descend a steep, narrow ramp on the west side to a snow saddle (correcting older guidebook beta that suggests the east/right side).
A short, sound step with a vertical crack; usually simulclimbed or scrambled.
Considered the most exposed, least popular section of the route.
S-shaped line with crevasses; bergschrund crossing near 7,900 ft; steepens toward the summit snowfield.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Ross Dam Trailhead, milepost 134
road.status: Paved, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closes for winter (typically early Dec-mid/late Feb, weather dependent) at gates near Ross Dam TH (MP134) and points east; opening/closing dates vary year to year with snow and slide conditions.
road.driveNote: Park at the Ross Dam/Happy Panda trailhead pullout on SR 20 and hike about 1 mile down to Ross Lake to meet the Ross Lake Resort water taxi (reservation required).

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Washington Cascades backcountry (no dedicated NWAC zone; this is a summer non-avalanche-season objective)
climate.typical: Cool nights and highs in the 60s-70s F at elevation during the summer climbing window; weather can change quickly with little warning in the Pickets.

bySeason.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack and high creek flows make the Access Creek approach and col crossings hazardous; not the standard season.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (mid-Jul-Aug); glacier more consolidated, brush drier, longer daylight for the long summit day.
bySeason.fall: A stable September window can work in good years but shortening days and early-season snow are risks.
bySeason.winter: Road access closed; not a winter objective.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: Backcountry permit fee: $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee (youth 15 and under free); park itself has no entrance fee.
access.permit: Overnight backcountry permit required year-round from the NPS Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount); reservable in advance via Recreation.gov or picked up as a walk-up.
access.closures: SR 20 gate at Ross Dam TH (MP134) closes seasonally in winter, cutting off the standard approach until the road reopens (typically mid-to-late winter/early spring, weather dependent).

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start around 4:00-4:30 am from Luna-Fury Col for the summit push

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Ross Dam TH / water taxi landing -> Access Basin (~4,300 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Includes ~11-12 trail miles plus Access Creek bushwhack.

sectionBreakdown.section: Access Basin to Luna-Fury Col (Day 2 AM)
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Access Basin -> Access Col (6,100 ft) -> Luna-Fury Col
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep scree/heather; some parties use crampons on the heather slopes.
Includes ridge traverse and Southeast Glacier climb; heavy fog reported on some ascents.
Full reversal of the approach.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff non-emergency line 360-676-6911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245 / (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA (open 24 hrs); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA is a further alternative.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage should be expected on the approach or route; a satellite messenger is the practical way to trigger a rescue from this area of the Pickets.

routes.corrections: The route is on the East Peak specifically. A recent (Oct 2022) theodolite survey by Gilbertson et al. measured East Fury at 8,356 +/-8 ft, making it slightly higher than West Fury (8,305 ft) and the highest point in the Picket Range/Northern Pickets area — higher than the 8,326 ft given and higher than the commonly cited 8,280-8,325 ft figures from older sources; USGS quad maps only bracket the summit within an 8,280-8,320 ft contour. Multiple independent web sources attribute the East Peak's first ascent specifically to Fred and Helmy Beckey in 1940, distinct from the main Mount Fury massif's 1938 first ascent by the Ptarmigan Climbing Club (which the given '1938' FA year most likely reflects) — flagged with moderate rather than high confidence since a primary AAC/guidebook source could not be directly retrieved to fully confirm.

routes.fa: Wayne Wallace, solo, August 24-28, 2006
routes.rappels: 12-13 rappels (including several double-length/60m raps)
objHaz: no reliable bivy sites on the ridge proper
sudden weather changes exposed on a knife-edge ridge
routes.face: South Buttress (Mongo Ridge) of West Peak
routes.overview: A mile-long, 4,000-foot alpine rock ridge on the South Buttress of Fury's West Peak, broken by a series of towering gendarmes and knife-edge sections that must each be climbed and rappelled in turn to make progress — one of the longest and most remote ridge climbs in the Lower 48.
routes.beta: After first climbing East Fury and descending to the ridge, the route follows the crest of the South Buttress over roughly a dozen gendarmes, with the crux around the third major tower (5.10a) and further difficulty at the exposed 'Rooster Comb' knife-edge traverse near the top; the final ~500 feet to the West Peak summit eases to Class 4. In the modern standard approach, the bottom four narrow towers require summiting and rappelling each in turn, with long double-rope rappels between them.
routes.approach: Climb the Southeast Glacier standard route to the summit of East Fury, then descend north/northwest to gain the start of Mongo Ridge (the South Buttress of West Fury) — avoiding the historical alternative of a direct, brush-heavy approach up Access Creek to the toe of the ridge.
routes.descent: From the West Peak summit, traverse back to East Fury and descend via the Southeast Glacier / Access Creek approach route.
routes.descentText: Summit West Fury, then reverse across to East Fury's summit and descend the Southeast Glacier standard route back to Luna-Fury Col, Access Col, Access Basin, and out via the Big Beaver Trail to the water taxi landing.
routes.itinerary: A multi-day undertaking: typically one day approaching to East Fury's summit/base of the ridge, one to two days climbing the ridge itself with an open bivy (no good ledges on route), and a final day traversing to West Fury's summit and descending back over East Fury to the trailhead. Wallace's solo FA took roughly 13 hours of technical climbing on the ridge itself within a longer multi-day push; a two-person team in 2021 completed the ridge itself camp-to-camp in about 23 hours from East Fury's summit.
routes.bail: Retreat from most points on the ridge means reversing via rappel back down the tower/gendarme just climbed — there is no easy lateral escape once established on the crest, so a bail decision is best made early, before committing above the lower towers.
routes.turnaround: Given the total commitment of climbing over each gendarme (up-and-over rather than around), set a hard decision point before starting a new tower late in the day, since retreating from the crest typically means the same rappels used to get there.
routes.bestSeason: July-August, when the route is driest and the approach glaciers/snowfields are most stable; this is such a remote, all-around undertaking that a multi-day stable weather window is essential.
routes.comms: No cell coverage on the ridge or approach; satellite communication is the only realistic way to call for help from this line.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack from small cams (TCU/00-0 size) through about a 2" cam, doubled in the 0.3-0.75 range, a small set of nuts, a single 60m lead rope plus a 60m pull cord for the long double-rope rappels, roughly ten single and three double alpine draws plus a quad, a light technical ice tool (e.g. Petzl Gully) for any residual snow/ice, steel horizontal front-point crampons, and about 50 ft of 5mm cord for building/backing up rappel anchors (expect no fixed gear).
routes.proNeeds: No fixed anchors exist on the ridge; parties build natural or cord-and-nut rappel anchors at each gendarme, and protection is trad throughout with an emphasis on small-to-mid cams for the crack systems on the towers.
gear: single rack small cams to 2 in
whatToBring: open-bivy sleeping gear (sit pad + bag; no good ledges on route)
sun protection for long ridge days
approach shoes/boots suited to both glacier and technical rock
proTips: There are no good bivouac sites on the ridge itself — plan for an open bivy with just a sit pad and bag if the forecast is stable.
Climbing East Fury first and descending to the ridge start avoids a much longer, brush-heavy direct approach up Access Creek.
Move roped-together/simul-climb where terrain allows to keep pace on a route with 25 pitches and a dozen-plus rappels.
watchOut: Each of the lower gendarmes must be climbed and then rappelled to continue along the crest, which is slow and can eat an entire day for relatively little ridge distance gained.
Loose rock is present on some of the towers.
The 'Rooster Comb' knife-edge traverse near the top is highly exposed on both sides.
knownHazards: Extreme remoteness — self-rescue is the default expectation if something goes wrong.
No reliable bivy ledges force open bivouacs exposed to weather.
Long, technical ridge with limited fast retreat options once committed above the lower towers.

pitchDetail.notes: Roughly 400 ft, often free-soloed by strong parties to save time.
Each tower is summited then rappelled to continue; slow going.
The technical crux of the route; a nearby 5.11 option was avoided on the FA.
Knife-edge ridge traverse near the top of the route.
Named rock tower near the upper ridge, considered one of the most inaccessible features in the Lower 48.
Last ~500 ft to the West Peak summit.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Ross Dam Trailhead, milepost 134
road.status: Paved, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closes for winter (typically early Dec-mid/late Feb, weather dependent) at gates near Ross Dam TH (MP134).
road.driveNote: Same access as the Southeast Glaciers route: park at Ross Dam TH and take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi to the Big Beaver Trail landing.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Washington Cascades backcountry (summer objective, outside NWAC's winter avalanche season)
climate.typical: Warm, dry summer days are needed to keep the rock free of snow/ice and the multi-day open bivies tolerable.

bySeason.spring: Not viable — heavy snow load on the ridge and approach.
bySeason.summer: Primary and essentially only season (Jul-Aug).
bySeason.fall: Possible in a stable September but daylight and weather risk increase.
bySeason.winter: Not a winter objective; road access closed.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: Backcountry permit fee: $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee.
access.permit: Overnight backcountry permit required from the NPS Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount).
access.closures: SR 20 gate at Ross Dam TH (MP134) closes seasonally in winter.

timing.recommendedStart: Early-morning starts on each ridge day to maximize daylight given the slow tower-by-tower pace

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Base of Mongo Ridge -> West Fury summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Wayne Wallace's solo FA climbing time on the ridge itself, within a longer multi-day push that also summited East Fury twice.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: East Fury summit (base camp) -> West Fury summit
sectionBreakdown.note: One 2021 two-person team's camp-to-camp time for the ridge itself.

waypoints.name: West Peak of Mount Fury (Mongo Ridge / South Buttress)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff non-emergency line 360-676-6911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245 / (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA (open 24 hrs); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA is a further alternative.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage on route; a satellite messenger is the practical way to trigger a rescue given the multi-day commitment and lack of fast retreat options.

routes.corrections: Mongo Ridge is the South Buttress of Mount Fury's WEST Peak, not the East Peak — multiple independent sources (Mountain Project, AAC Publications, NW Mountaineering Journal, Wayne Wallace's own trip account) consistently place the route and its 25-pitch, 4,000-ft south buttress on West Fury (8,305 ft per the 2022 theodolite survey), which climbers reach by first summiting East Fury and descending to the ridge. It is being enriched here under the East Peak's route ID as given, but the technical climbing itself takes place on the neighboring West Peak. Grade sources consistently give the rock crux as 5.10a (VI 5.10), slightly harder than the 5.9 given; FA year 2006 is confirmed (climbed Aug 24-28, 2006), though one secondary source (Alpinist) mis-cites it as 2007 (the year the AAJ report was published).

blurb: Mount Fury's West Peak (about 8,305 ft per a 2022 theodolite survey) is the more remote and technically serious of Fury's twin summits, deep in the Northern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park — a sub-range known for shattered granodiorite spires, hanging glaciers, and notoriously brushy, multi-day approaches. It was first climbed in 1958 by five Seattle American Alpine Club members (Vic Josendal, Maury Muzzy, Phil Sharpe, Duke Watson, and Warren Spickard), who reached it from Whatcom Pass over the Challenger Glacier and climbed loose class 4-5 chimneys to the top. West Fury is now grouped among Washington's unofficial "Difficult Ten" hardest peaks for the combination of an extreme approach and loose, exposed climbing on even its easiest line.

routes.fa: 1958 - Vic Josendal, Maury Muzzy, Phil Sharpe, Duke Watson, Warren Spickard (Seattle AAC climbers) - first ascent of Mount Fury's West Peak
objHaz: Crevassed glacier travel (Whatcom Icefall / Challenger Glacier)
Loose, chossy rock on the summit chimneys
Long, remote approach with real route-finding
Serac/icefall exposure crossing the Whatcom Icefall
routes.face: North/northwest side of the West Peak, above the Challenger Glacier and Perfect Pass
routes.overview: The original 1958 first-ascent line onto Mount Fury's West Peak, approached from Whatcom Pass and the Challenger Glacier on the peak's north side rather than from Luna Col to the south. It climbs two steep, loose class 4-5 chimneys directly below the summit block to a short final scramble on top.
routes.beta: First climbed via a long approach over Whatcom Pass and across the badly crevassed Challenger/Whatcom Icefall to a base camp near Perfect Pass, the route ascends class 4-5 chimneys directly beneath the west summit before an easy scramble to the top. Most present-day parties instead reach West Fury from the south via Access Creek and Luna Col, traversing three towers from East Fury; this historic Whatcom Pass line sees very little modern traffic.
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Pass Trailhead (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF, off SR 542), hike over Hannegan Pass and down to the Chilliwack River, then up to Whatcom Pass (about 18 miles one-way in the original account). Cross the badly crevassed Whatcom Icefall beneath Whatcom Peak to reach the cliffs above the Challenger Glacier, then descend to Perfect Pass for a base camp on the north side of the Fury massif.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent chimneys back toward Perfect Pass and the Challenger Glacier/Whatcom Icefall.
routes.bail: Retreat is by down-climbing/reversing the chimneys to the Challenger Glacier and Perfect Pass; there is no shorter bail option on this side of the peak given the glaciated, off-trail approach.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, glaciated, off-trail nature of the approach, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather so the icefall crossing and chimneys can be reversed in good light and stable snow conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-summer into early fall (roughly July through September), when the Whatcom Icefall and approach snowfields are most stable and stream crossings are lower; the first ascent itself was made in mid-August.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in this part of North Cascades National Park is unreliable at best, and NPS describes satellite phone coverage here as "rather spotty"; do not rely on cell phones as an evacuation plan, and carry a PLB or satellite communicator as a supplement to self-sufficiency.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rock rack (small-to-medium cams and nuts, slings) for the class 4-5 chimney sections, plus full glacier travel gear for the Whatcom Icefall/Challenger Glacier crossing.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs on the chimneys are modest — mostly natural gear and slings on loose class 4-5 rock — but full crevasse-rescue and glacier travel gear is mandatory for the icefall crossing.
proTips: The Whatcom Pass/Challenger Glacier approach used on the first ascent avoids the Access Creek bushwhack but adds significant glacier travel and mileage; most modern parties instead go via Luna Col from the south.
West Fury sees very few ascents (fewer than 20 recorded as of the mid-2010s), so route-finding on the upper chimneys requires real judgment rather than a beaten path.
watchOut: The Whatcom Icefall is described in the first-ascent account as 'badly-crevassed' with sections of tottering seracs — travel roped and early before the sun softens snow bridges.
The class 4-5 chimneys beneath the summit are on loose rock; the first-ascent party specifically called them 'unpromising.'
knownHazards: Crevasse-fall hazard crossing the Whatcom Icefall/Challenger Glacier
Rockfall and loose holds in the summit chimneys
Extreme remoteness — any injury or rescue is a major undertaking given the multi-day, glaciated approach

pitchDetail.notes: Hannegan Pass Trail to Whatcom Pass (~18 mi one-way per the original account), then across the crevassed Whatcom Icefall to the cliffs above the Challenger Glacier, descending to a Perfect Pass base camp.
Two steep, loose, 'unpromising' shallow chimneys directly beneath and about 1,000 ft below the west summit; the first-ascent party split to scout alternate lines.
Short, easy scramble from the top of the chimneys to the true summit.

road.name: Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) to Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Service Road 32)
road.status: Paved to Glacier, WA, then gravel FS Road 32 for 5.3 miles to the trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Usage period is summer-fall; stock access is officially restricted to July 1-Oct 31, and the road/trail is generally not fully snow-free until early-to-mid summer.
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive SR 542 east about 12.5 miles, turn onto Hannegan Pass Road #32, and follow it 5.3 miles to the trailhead. As of recent reports, storm damage prevents vehicle access beyond mile 5.2, with only limited parking available until repairs are completed.

climate.forecastZone: NOAA North Cascades Zone forecast; Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) for snowpack/avalanche conditions
climate.typical: Highly variable alpine weather with strong elevation and microclimate effects; conditions can differ sharply from valley forecasts and even professional forecasters are frequently wrong here.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack and avalanche hazard; the glacier approach is largely snow-covered
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); most stable conditions and lowest stream flows
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows, with possible early snow at elevation from September on
bySeason.winter: Route effectively inaccessible; SR-20 and high trailheads close under snow

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (approach trail) / North Cascades National Park - NPS (climbing zone)
access.fees: No NPS entrance fee for North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Free backcountry/wilderness permit required from NPS for any overnight stay once inside North Cascades National Park
access.passRequired: A valid Northwest Forest Pass (Recreation Pass) must be displayed at the Hannegan Pass Trailhead (USFS land); Washington's Discover Pass is not accepted there
access.closures: Storm damage currently limits vehicle access on FS Road 32 to about mile 5.2, adding walking distance to the trailhead

emergency.county: Whatcom County (peak and northern approach); Skagit County (park administrative dispatch)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff: 360-384-5390; Skagit County Sheriff: 360-336-9450
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA (backcountry permits); NPS dispatch (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, after-hours Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (roughly 90 minutes from the park per NPS)
emergency.notes: Cell and satellite phone coverage in this part of the Picket Range is unreliable; do not depend on cell service for rescue coordination.

routes.corrections: The given FA year of 1938 is not supported by sources. The AAJ 1959 report "New Climbs in the Northern Pickets" documents the first ascent of Mount Fury's West Peak in 1958 by Vic Josendal, Maury Muzzy, Phil Sharpe, Duke Watson, and Warren Spickard, approaching via Whatcom Pass and the Challenger Glacier from the north/northwest side (consistent with the 'Northwest Route' naming) rather than from Luna Col. Note that most modern ascents of West Fury instead approach via Access Creek and Luna Col, traversing three towers from the East Fury side -- a different, more commonly used line than the historic Whatcom Pass/chimneys route described here. The given elevation (8,303 ft) is close to but not identical to the 2022 theodolite survey figure of 8,305 ft +/- 10 ft; within margin of error, not treated as a correction.

blurb: Mount Challenger (8,236 ft per WTA/ListsOfJohn LIDAR data) anchors the northern end of the Picket Range deep inside North Cascades National Park, one of the most rugged and remote mountain wildernesses in the Lower 48 and not visible from any road. Its summit rocks are metamorphic biotite gneiss of the Custer Gneiss unit, and the peak is flanked on its north side by the large, heavily crevassed Challenger Glacier. It was first climbed on September 7, 1936 by Phil Dickert, Jack Hossack, and George MacGowan, whose account "The Challenge of Challenger" appeared in The Mountaineer annual that year.

routes.fa: Phil Dickert, Jack Hossack & George MacGowan — September 7, 1936 (first ascent of the peak via this line)
routes.rappels: One short rappel off the summit block/horn (a 30m rope is sufficient); parties on the Whatcom Peak approach also report a rappel descending wet class-4 terrain near Perfect Pass
objHaz: Bergschrund/moat that can become impassable by late season
Loose, chossy rock on the exposed summit block
Aggressive wasps/hornets on the brushy lower approach
Whiteout/routefinding risk in fast-moving Pacific weather
routes.face: North/northwest slopes (Challenger Glacier side of the massif)
routes.overview: The standard route up Mount Challenger: ascend the broad, crevassed Challenger Glacier on the peak's north side to a saddle below the summit, then finish with a short, exposed class 3-4 scramble and one 5.4-5.5 rock move to the small summit block.
routes.beta: From a high camp at Perfect Pass, the Wiley Ridge bench, or Whatcom Pass, cross the Challenger Glacier (commonly around 6,800-7,200 ft depending on crevasse conditions), pick a line through open crevasses to the bergschrund, and climb steepening snow to a broad saddle/snow arête below the summit rocks. From there, class 3 scrambling on loose rock leads to a short crux — a steep dihedral/bulge rated about 5.4-5.5, sometimes protected by an old fixed piton — followed by breathtakingly exposed scrambling to the small summit block. A single rappel is typically used to retreat from the top.
routes.approach: Most parties reach the mountain via one of three long approaches, all requiring 1-2 days of travel before the climb: (1) Hannegan Pass Trailhead -> Hannegan Pass -> down the Chilliwack River Trail -> ford the Chilliwack River -> up Easy Ridge Trail to ~5,160 ft -> traverse Easy Peak's north ridge, bypassing the dangerous gorge known as 'the Impasse,' to Perfect Pass camp; (2) Hannegan Pass Trailhead -> Chilliwack River -> Whatcom Pass -> traverse below/around Whatcom Peak via the Whatcom Glacier to Perfect Pass; or (3) Ross Lake water taxi to Big Beaver Landing -> Big Beaver Trail to Beaver Pass -> off-trail travel via Wiley Ridge (significant bushwhacking) to a high camp near 7,100 ft on the Wiley Ridge bench.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb/rappel off the summit block, retrace the snow arête, and re-cross the Challenger Glacier via the same crevasse-avoiding line back to high camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties make a single rappel to get off the exposed summit horn/block, then downclimb the class 3-4 rock and snow arête back to the saddle above the glacier. Re-cross the Challenger Glacier using the outbound line to avoid known crevasses and the bergschrund, then return to high camp (Perfect Pass, Whatcom Pass, or the Wiley Ridge bench) before retracing the multi-day approach out.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip is 3-4 days: Day 1, hike in from the trailhead to a high camp near Perfect Pass or Whatcom Pass (roughly 8-9 hours of travel); Day 2, alpine start for the glacier crossing and summit push (reported summit-day round trips range from about 9.5 hours camp-to-camp on the Wiley Ridge approach to 11 hours from Whatcom Pass); Day 3-4, hike back out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward below the glacier by simply reversing the approach trail. Once on the glacier or above the bergschrund, the only reasonable retreat is to reverse the ascent line (downclimb/rappel) back across the glacier to camp — there is no quick bailout from the upper mountain given the remoteness and lack of trail access.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, complex multi-day approach and the crevassed glacier crossing, set a firm turnaround time based on weather, visibility, and snow-bridge conditions rather than the clock alone, and be willing to turn back well before the summit if the bergschrund crossing looks compromised or weather is closing in — self-rescue options in the Pickets are extremely limited.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September, when snow bridges over the crevasses and bergschrund are most consolidated; conditions and the state of the bergschrund/moat crossing vary significantly year to year and can render the standard finish difficult or impassable by late summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Picket Range interior; parties should plan on being unreachable by phone for the multi-day approach and carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier kit is the priority: harness, crevasse-rescue gear (prusiks/pulley/cordelette), crampons, and an ice axe (or two tools if snow is firm early season). For the short summit pitch, carry a handful of small-to-medium cams and a few nuts/alpine draws to back up or replace the old fixed piton reported at the crux.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal rock protection is needed — a few alpine draws and small cams/nuts back up the single 5.4-5.5 summit move (a fixed piton has been reported there). The real gear demand is glacier travel and crevasse-rescue equipment.
gear: Small rack (a few nuts and cams)
whatToBring: Gaiters for river fords and brush
Bug protection (aggressive wasps/hornets reported on the lower approach)
Bear canister (required in some NCNP backcountry zones)
Water treatment for the multi-day approach
Approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
proTips: Camp at Perfect Pass or the Wiley Ridge bench to shorten summit day and get close-up views of the glacier crossing
Start the glacier crossing pre-dawn while snow bridges are firmest
The Easy Ridge approach is reported by multiple parties as faster and less brushy than the Whatcom Pass alternative
A 30m rope has proven sufficient for the single summit rappel
watchOut: Wasps/hornets and other stinging insects on the brushy lower Wiley Ridge/Chilliwack approach have stung multiple parties badly
The bergschrund and moat crossing below the summit ridge can become impassable by late summer in low-snow years
Rock on the summit block is loose and choss-prone — move carefully on the exposed final scramble
'The Impasse,' a gorge on the Easy Ridge approach, must be bypassed on higher ground, not crossed directly
knownHazards: The Challenger Glacier is heavily crevassed with icefalls in its lower reaches, requiring careful routefinding
Bergschrund/moat conditions vary significantly by year and can block the standard finish
The summit scramble is narrow and exposed on choss-prone rock
The approach is extremely remote with very limited bailout or rescue options once committed

pitchDetail.notes: Cross the Challenger Glacier roughly around 6,800-7,200 ft, weaving between open crevasses toward the bergschrund
Pass the bergschrund (bridge/moat conditions vary by year) and climb steepening snow to a broad saddle or snow arête below the summit rocks

pitchDetail.grade: Class 3-4 scrambling to a short 5.4-5.5 bulge
pitchDetail.notes: Short, exposed rock finish to the small summit block; an old fixed piton has been reported at the crux; some parties belay the last move, others simul-scramble

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32) / Ruth Creek Road
road.status: Generally open and drivable to the Hannegan Trailhead in summer season; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts for storm damage before driving
road.seasonalGate: Not gated on a fixed date but becomes snowed-in and impassable in winter; used as a winter snow-play/ski area when closed to vehicles
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) east about 12.5-13 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center, turn onto Hannegan Pass Road #32, and follow it about 5.3 miles to the Hannegan Trailhead (~3,100 ft) at road's end; a Northwest Forest Pass is required for trailhead parking

climate.forecastZone: NWAC 'West Slopes North' zone covers winter avalanche forecasting for this area (no dedicated summer alpine forecast zone)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy orographic precipitation off the Pacific; North Cascades National Park averages over 100 inches of precipitation a year, and the Pickets are known for fast-moving, unpredictable weather that can close in with little warning

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; approach trails typically remain snow-covered into June
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window June-September; still subject to sudden Pacific frontal systems, low cloud, and rain even in midsummer
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early-season snow by late September
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall, avalanche terrain on the approach road/trail, and roads typically snowed in and impassable

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (route/upper mountain); Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Hannegan Trailhead access)
access.fees: NPS backcountry permit: $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee (youth 15 and under free); a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass is needed for trailhead parking
access.permit: Overnight backcountry permit required year-round; reserve in advance via Recreation.gov or obtain a walk-up permit in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, or the seasonal Glacier Public Service Center
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking) plus an NPS wilderness camping permit
access.closures: Check current NPS North Cascades and Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF conditions/alerts pages for trail washouts, bridge closures, or fire restrictions before the approach

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn departure from high camp to cross the glacier and reach the bergschrund while the snow is still firm

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Hannegan Trailhead -> Perfect Pass / Whatcom Pass high camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Reported roughly 8-9 hours via the Easy Ridge or Whatcom Pass approaches, split across one or more days depending on party
Camp-to-summit time via the Wiley Ridge bench/Challenger Glacier approach (trailcatjim.com trip report)
Same trip report; a separate Whatcom Pass-based report cites an 11-hour round trip camp-to-camp on summit day (jeffreyjhebert.com)
Reported descent-day time from a Whatcom Pass camp back to the Hannegan Trailhead (jeffreyjhebert.com)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Emergencies: 911. Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 778-6600. Whatcom County Search & Rescue is dispatched through the Sheriff's Office.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245. Seasonal Glacier Public Service Center (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF): (360) 599-2714.
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (Level II Trauma Center), 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA — (360) 734-5400
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Picket Range interior; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. NPS backcountry rangers coordinate search-and-rescue with Whatcom County SAR for incidents in this area.

routes.corrections: Given rockGrade of 5.4 differs from Mountain Project's listed grade of 5.5 for the summit pitch; used 5.5 per MP as the more specific technical source. Peak/route summit elevation shows real source variance: Wikipedia and Mountain Project cite an older figure of 8,207 ft, while WTA (8,236 ft) and ListsOfJohn's higher-resolution LIDAR-based data (8,239 ft) closely match the given 8,238 ft; highPointFt uses 8,236 ft as the best-supported modern figure, consistent with the input data within normal survey variance. Mountain Project's listed route grade also includes an NCCS commitment grade of 'II' (distinct from the given French/alpine grade 'PD+', which was kept as-is since no source contradicted it).

blurb: Poltergeist Pinnacle (8,198 ft) is a subsidiary rock tower in the Northern Pickets of North Cascades National Park, the southernmost of a line of subsummits between Mount Challenger and Crooked Thumb Peak. Its east face is a striking shield of solid granite crosscut by hundreds of dark dikes, and the peak was named to match the "Ghost/Phantom" theme of its neighbors. It saw its first ascent on July 3-4, 2004, when Dan Aylward and Forrest Murphy put up a new route on the East Face after a multi-day approach via Hannegan Pass, Easy Ridge, and the Challenger Glacier.

routes.fa: Dan Aylward & Forrest Murphy, July 3-4, 2004
objHaz: Possible loose rock in gully sections
Rapid weather changes typical of the Pickets
routes.overview: A four-pitch alpine rock route up the distinctive dike-streaked East Face shield of Poltergeist Pinnacle, a subsidiary tower of Mount Challenger in the Northern Pickets, finishing with a long low-fifth-class ridge crest to the summit.
routes.beta: Gain the rock via a dihedral just left of the main shield feature, then traverse right across parallel dikes on sustained, largely unprotectable 5.9 face climbing to reach the edge of the face (P1, crux). Continue up clean corners with a small roof (P2, 5.8) and a ledge/gully system that breaks through the lower face (P3, 5.8) to reach the ridge crest, then follow the crest — mostly low fifth class with a short 5.7 section — for roughly 800 feet to the true summit (P4).
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Pass Trailhead (end of Forest Road 32, off Mt. Baker Highway SR 542), hike the Hannegan Pass Trail (about 4 miles, 2,000 ft gain) to Hannegan Pass. Continue over Easy Ridge toward the Chilliwack River drainage and up to Perfect Pass below Mount Challenger's west side — a multi-day cross-country approach. From Perfect Pass, cross the Challenger Glacier roughly 3 miles to reach the base of Poltergeist Pinnacle, the southernmost rock tower south of Challenger's main summit.
routes.descent: Simul-climb or belay northward along the connecting ridge toward Mount Challenger's main summit, then descend via Challenger's standard glacier route.
routes.descentText: From the summit of Poltergeist Pinnacle, reverse along the ridge crest (simul-climbing or short-roping as needed) north toward Mount Challenger's true summit. Join Challenger's standard ascent route and descend via the established glacier trail back toward Perfect Pass, watching for crevasse hazard on the Challenger Glacier.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a 3-4 day trip: Day 1 hike the Hannegan Pass Trail to a camp near Easy Ridge or the Chilliwack River; Day 2 continue over Easy Ridge to Perfect Pass; Day 3 cross the Challenger Glacier to the base of the pinnacle, climb the East Face, and return to camp; Day 4 hike out via Hannegan Pass.
routes.bail: Retreat from low on the route is difficult given the runout crux pitch; parties should generally commit to topping out onto the ridge crest and descending via the connecting ridge toward Mount Challenger's standard route rather than attempting to reverse the face.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach alone (Hannegan Pass, Easy Ridge, Perfect Pass, Challenger Glacier) takes a full day or more, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight, weather, and the multi-day nature of the outing rather than attempting a car-to-car push.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in mid-to-late summer (roughly July through September) once the Challenger Glacier approach and Easy Ridge snow have consolidated or melted out; the first ascent was done July 3-4, 2004. Earlier-season attempts involve more snow/glacier travel and higher crevasse risk.
routes.comms: No verified cell coverage data for this location; this is a remote section of the Northern Pickets with no reliable cell service, so carry a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine trad rack: a set of nuts and cams from finger to hand size, a few slings/draws for the corner and gully pitches, plus full glacier travel gear (crampons, axe, crevasse-rescue kit, rope) for the Challenger Glacier approach. Rock is generally sound for gear anchors along the route.
routes.proNeeds: Pitch 1's crux traverse across the dikes is largely unprotectable for about 40 feet (rated R); cracks partway up the pitch allow some gear. The corner and gully pitches (P2-P3) protect well with a standard rack. The upper ridge (P4) is mostly low fifth class and is often simul-climbed with occasional gear placements.
whatToBring: Bivy/camping gear for the multi-day approach
Extra layers for the exposed ridge finish
proTips: Approach via Hannegan Pass and Easy Ridge to Perfect Pass, then cross the Challenger Glacier — plan on at least 2 days of approach before attempting the route.
Rope up and carry crevasse-rescue gear for the glacier crossing to the base of the pinnacle.
The route can be linked with an ascent of Mount Challenger's main summit by continuing the ridge north after topping out.
watchOut: Pitch 1's crux section is unprotectable for roughly 40 feet — a fall there could be serious (rated R).
This is a remote, rarely-repeated line deep in the Northern Pickets; retreat and rescue are difficult.
Challenger Glacier crossing carries real crevasse hazard both approaching and descending.
knownHazards: Runout (R) crux face-climbing pitch with about 40 feet of unprotectable climbing.
Crevassed Challenger Glacier must be crossed on both approach and descent.
Very remote location with a multi-day approach, making retreat and rescue slow and difficult.
Rapidly changing alpine weather typical of the Northern Pickets.

pitchDetail.notes: Gain the rock via a dihedral left of the shield, then traverse right across parallel dikes; face climbing to 5.9 with about 40 feet unprotectable (crux); cracks partway up allow some gear. ~180 ft.
Clean, easily-protected corner system with a small roof at mid-height. ~200 ft.
Traverse/ledge and rock gully breaking through the lower face to reach the ridge crest. ~200 ft.
Long ridge-crest section to the true summit, mostly low fifth class; often simul-climbed. ~800 ft.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: Open with limited access for larger vehicles due to flood damage (per recent USFS alerts) — check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed by snow in winter/early spring; opens as snowpack allows, usually by early summer
road.driveNote: From Glacier, drive east on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) 12.5 miles to Hannegan Pass Road #32, then follow FR 32 for 5.3 miles to the trailhead at road's end.

climate.forecastZone: NWS/NWAC Mount Baker / Northern Cascades zone (Whatcom County backcountry)
climate.typical: Wet, heavily glaciated alpine climate typical of the Northern Pickets, with a short, often unsettled summer climbing window.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists; high avalanche and crevasse hazard, access road/trail may still be snowed in.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing window (Jul-Sep); glacier travel still required, afternoon thunderstorms possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and building instability; early-season snow can return by late September/October.
bySeason.winter: Approach road and trail typically snowed in; not a realistic winter objective.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (National Park Service) for the peak/glacier; Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest for the Hannegan Pass approach trail and road.
access.fees: No day-use fee for climbing; a backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay.
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required from North Cascades National Park, available at the Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount) or via Recreation.gov.
access.closures: Check current NPS and USFS alerts for trail, road, or area closures (e.g., flood/washout damage on FR 32) before approaching.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn from a high camp (e.g., near Perfect Pass or on the Challenger Glacier) to allow a full day for the glacier crossing, route, and return.

sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 mi, ~2,000 ft gain on a maintained trail.

sectionBreakdown.note: Cross-country ridge route; typically a full day of travel.
