routes.corrections: No independently-documented 'North Route' was found for Poltergeist Pinnacle across Mountain Project, the AAC Publications/American Alpine Journal 2005 report, Kurt Hicks' trip-report blog, or CascadeClimbers.com. Every source describes a single technical route on this peak, the 'East Face' (III/IV, 5.9, R/X), first climbed July 3-4, 2004 by Dan Aylward and Forrest Murphy after approaching via Hannegan Pass, Easy Ridge, Perfect Pass, and the Challenger Glacier — that route's data is filled above under the given routeId. Given rockGrade '5.7' is corrected to '5.9' (5.7 only describes the final ridge-crest pitch, not the route's crux/overall rating). Given alpineGrade 'D' does not match any sourced grade for this route and was cleared to null; commitment is set to 'IV' per the AAC/AAJ report, though Kurt Hicks' listing gives 'III' for the same route (grading systems for this line are not fully consistent across sources). Mountain Project's structured metadata lists 6 pitches/1,200 ft, while the narrative description (also sourced via Mountain Project/AAJ) breaks the route into 4 labeled sections (P1-P4, the last a long simul-climbed ridge); pitches is set to 4 to match pitchDetail, with routeFt kept at the sourced 1,200 ft total. Given FA year (2004) matches sources and is retained, with full party names and exact date added.

blurb: Mount Terror is the highest peak of the Southern Picket Range in the Stephen Mather Wilderness of North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA — a serrated summit of granite/gneiss forged from accreted oceanic-continental terrane rock and heavily sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, dropping over 2,500 ft to the McMillan Creek Cirque on its north side. Its easier flanks were first climbed in 1932 by William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg of The Mountaineers; the far more serious North Face wasn't climbed until Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, Dave Hiser, and Mike Swayne forced the North Buttress line in July 1961. It remains one of the most remote and committing objectives in the Cascades, reached only via multi-day approaches through the Southern Pickets.

routes.overview: The original 1961 line up Mount Terror's dramatic North Face, following the North Buttress just right of the crest from the McMillan Creek Cirique side up roughly 2,500 ft of mostly 3rd-4th class terrain with a 5.7 crux chimney near the top.
routes.fa: Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, Dave Hiser, Mike Swayne (July 8-11, 1961) — North Buttress/North Face
routes.face: North Face (above McMillan Creek Cirque), North Buttress
objHaz: rockfall / sudden loose rock (documented serious accident)
crevasses and moats crossing the Mustard/Terror Glacier
The Barrier crossing (loose ledges or steep choss/moats)
wet, heavy maritime snow and high avalanche danger into early summer
very long, complex retreat if injured or benighted
routes.beta: Climb above the initial snowfield and make a long, ascending 3rd-class traverse rightward as the face steepens into dihedrals and cracks. Aim for the west (right) crest of the buttress about two-thirds up where the rock darkens, staying on the crest only briefly (no harder than 5.7), then move back left onto the face toward a chimney above an awkward belay — the technical crux is near the top of this chimney; some parties report a harder 5.8 traverse variant there.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Trailhead (SR-20 near Newhalem), follow the climbers' path through Stump Hollow to a high camp near Terror Basin (~6,000 ft, roughly 6.5-9.5 hrs from the trailhead). Cross The Barrier (a lower crossing on loose ledges at the base of the Terror Glacier, or a higher crossing over moats/choss below Degenhardt) into Crescent Creek Basin. From that camp, traverse the Ottohorn-Himmel col (~1 hr) and descend its north side (Class 3-4) onto the Mustard/Terror Glacier, then scramble on solid rock to the snowfield at the base of the North Face — even fast parties need 2-3 hours from Crescent Creek Basin to the base of the route.
routes.descent: Most modern parties descend the West Ridge gully back toward Crescent Creek Basin (short scrambling and rappels on fixed anchors); the 1961 first-ascent party instead made four steep rappels down the east corner of the face.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble the West Ridge to a low notch between Terror and The Rake, where a short section of easy climbing on established rap/belay anchors (~25m spacing) leads to easier terrain; from the notch either downclimb or rappel the snow couloir back toward Crescent Creek Basin. Carry a single rope for the anchors along the West Ridge.
routes.bail: Retreat by rappelling the route on natural/gear anchors, or by traversing off toward the West Ridge notch once within reach; the multi-day, glaciated approach (Barrier, Ottohorn-Himmel col, Mustard Glacier) makes any low-face bail slow and committing.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour glacier and col crossing required just to reach the base, and the exposed, loose upper chimney, set a firm turnaround time that guarantees re-crossing the Ottohorn-Himmel col and Barrier terrain in daylight and before afternoon snow softening.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August offers the most consolidated conditions, once seasonal snow patches have mostly melted off the approach and glacier; July can still hold more snow (and more bugs), while September is climbable but comes with shorter daylight and higher chance of early-season snow.
routes.comms: Cell service is largely absent in the Terror Basin/Crescent Creek cirques, but has been reported reachable from near the summit — a call from the summit area was critical in a 2009 rescue after a rockfall accident on this face.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack (cams and stoppers to ~3in, several slings/draws) covers the technical ground; add ice axe, crampons, and glacier travel gear (rope, pickets/screws as conditions dictate) for the Ottohorn-Himmel col and Mustard/Terror Glacier crossing.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is moderate for the grade — mid-size cams and stoppers protect the cracks and dihedrals on the traverse, with natural gear/slung horns useful near the crux chimney; the rock is described as generally solid but with isolated loose blocks.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for the multi-day approach
Blue bags for human waste (required above treeline)
proTips: Stay right on the buttress crest only briefly — the sustained 5.7 ground is short; drifting left too early adds unnecessary difficulty
A doubled thinner rope can be cut by rockfall on a single strand — consider rope choice and redundancy given documented rockfall on this face
Four-person teams give redundancy for self-rescue if someone is injured this deep in the Pickets
watchOut: Rock that looks solid can pull loose without warning — a party-tested hazard on the low-fifth-class ledges about a third of the way up
Late-season col/glacier crossings (Ottohorn-Himmel, Mustard Glacier) get looser and more exposed as snow recedes
The Barrier crossing is committing in both directions and harder to reverse quickly
knownHazards: A 2009 accident: a leader fell ~60 ft when a large 'solid-looking' block tore loose on the low-fifth-class ledges, sustaining serious injuries; the party self-rescued using cell service reached at the summit
Mount Terror has a long history of natural rockfall and icefall off its faces

pitchDetail.notes: Above the initial snowfield, long simulclimbable/3rd-class ground traversing rightward
Ascending traverse through dihedrals and cracks as the face steepens
Brief section directly on the west (right) crest ~2/3 up where rock darkens; crux ground, do not linger here
Move back left onto the face to a chimney above an awkward belay — historically the technical crux near the top of the face

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Paved and generally open year-round to Newhalem/Goodell Creek (MP 119)
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally over Washington Pass, well east of Goodell Creek (roughly late Nov-Apr in a typical year; 2026 reopening has run later than usual due to repair work) — this does not affect access to the Goodell Creek Trailhead from the west
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive SR-20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem, then follow the Goodell Creek Campground road to the trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Picket Range (mountain-forecast.com 'Mount Terror')
climate.typical: Maritime alpine climate — wet, heavy snowpack keeps avalanche danger elevated into early summer; conditions are notably wetter and less predictable than the drier east-side Cascades.

bySeason.spring: Approach still largely snow-covered; unstable wet-snow avalanche hazard
bySeason.summer: Primary season; by late August most seasonal snow patches on the approach are gone, though July can still be buggy and snowier
bySeason.fall: Climbable into September but with shortening daylight and rising chance of early snow
bySeason.winter: Not a realistic climbing season — deep snowpack and difficult access

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No park entrance fee
access.permit: Free overnight wilderness/backcountry permit required for camping in the Crescent Creek cross-country zone (via Recreation.gov reservation or in person at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center)
access.passRequired: None for day use; overnight camping requires the NPS backcountry permit
access.closures: Pack out solid human waste with blue bags when camping on rock/snow/ice; strict food-storage rules (bear activity documented at Pickets bivy sites)

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn departure from Crescent Creek Basin camp to cross the Ottohorn-Himmel col and glacier while snow is still firm

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek Trailhead (600 ft) to Terror Basin camp (~5,800-6,000 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Stump Hollow; can run up to ~9.5 hrs depending on party and brush conditions

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Terror/Crescent Creek Basin to base of North Face
sectionBreakdown.note: Includes crossing The Barrier, the Ottohorn-Himmel col, and the Mustard/Terror Glacier; even fast parties need 2-3 hrs

emergency.county: Whatcom County (access via SR-20 also crosses Skagit County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 (after-hours backup dispatch for the park)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111; United General/PeaceHealth, Sedro-Woolley — (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: NPS North Cascades business-hours line (360) 856-5700 ext. 515; after-hours Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37. Cell coverage in the Southern Pickets cirques is minimal; a signal was found near the summit during a 2009 rescue.

routes.corrections: Given fa '1932' is the peak's overall first ascent (Degenhardt & Strandberg via an easier flank), not this technical North Face/North Buttress line — the North Face's actual first ascent was Bell, Cooper, Hiser, and Swayne, July 1961 (AAC Publications). Multiple sources (AAC, route-description synthesis) grade this original North Buttress line 'III 5.7', not the given 'Grade IV' — commitment corrected to III. Summit elevation is most commonly cited as 8,151 ft (USGS/Wikipedia, NGVD29 datum) versus the given 8,154 ft; figures vary slightly by source/datum. Note the North Face also hosts a distinct, harder line (Stoddard Buttress, John Stoddard solo, July 1985, 5.8+) via the left/east rib — not the same route as this entry.

routes.overview: The least-traveled way up Mount Terror: granite slabs lead to the col/saddle between Terror and Mount Degenhardt, from which a short but steep ridge climbs directly to the summit. Mostly climbed by parties enchaining the Southern Pickets traverse rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.face: Southeast Face / South Ridge (Terror-Degenhardt col side)
objHaz: loose/wet slabby rock low on the approach slabs
glacier and col travel required just to reach Crescent Creek Basin
extreme remoteness — very long retreat if injured
routes.beta: From Crescent Creek Basin, ascend broad granite slabs up toward the saddle between Mount Terror and Mount Degenhardt, then follow the short, steep summit ridge directly to the top — described by multiple sources as offering 'challenges beyond that of a normal alpine rock climb' despite its modest 5.6 rating, due to looseness and exposure on the upper ridge.
routes.approach: Same multi-day approach as the North Face routes: Goodell Creek Trailhead to a high camp via Stump Hollow/Terror Basin, crossing The Barrier into Crescent Creek Basin. From that camp, ascend the slabs on Terror's south/southeast side toward the Terror-Degenhardt col rather than heading toward the Ottohorn-Himmel col.
routes.descent: Descend via the West Ridge gully back into Crescent Creek Basin — the standard descent used for all of Terror's summit routes.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble the West Ridge past the false summit to the low notch between Terror and The Rake, using the fixed rap/belay anchors spaced roughly 25m apart, then downclimb or rappel the snow couloir back to Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.bail: Retreat back down the slabs toward Crescent Creek Basin is generally straightforward below the col; above the col, downclimb or rappel the short ridge rather than pushing for the summit in deteriorating weather.
routes.turnaround: Because the return to Crescent Creek Basin and the trailhead is a multi-hour, multi-day undertaking through glaciated and slabby terrain, set a turnaround that leaves ample daylight to get back below the col and across any snow/glacier terrain before conditions soften.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August, once the approach slabs and glacier crossings have shed most seasonal snow; conditions and route-finding are more straightforward in this window than in early season.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is minimal in Crescent Creek Basin and on the approach; as with the North Face, service has occasionally been found near the summit.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rock rack (a handful of cams and stoppers) suffices for the low-5th-class ridge; glacier travel gear is needed for the approach to Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are modest given the grade — small-to-mid cams and stoppers protect the steep summit ridge; the slabs below are mostly unprotected low-angle scrambling.
gear: Rope for the summit ridge and descent
whatToBring: Bivy gear for the multi-day approach
Sun protection for the slab approach
proTips: This route is rarely climbed on its own — most ascents happen as part of a Southern Pickets enchainment/traverse, so plan logistics accordingly
The slabs can be slick when wet; a dry weather window matters more here than the grade suggests
watchOut: Looseness and exposure on the short summit ridge are more serious than the moderate 5.6 rating implies
Route-finding on the slabs toward the Terror-Degenhardt col is not well cairned or documented
knownHazards: General Southern Pickets objective hazards apply: remoteness, glacier travel, and unstable weather with little chance of rescue

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Paved and generally open year-round to Newhalem/Goodell Creek (MP 119)
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally over Washington Pass, well east of Goodell Creek; the Newhalem/Goodell Creek access itself stays open
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive SR-20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem, then follow the Goodell Creek Campground road to the trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Picket Range (mountain-forecast.com 'Mount Terror')
climate.typical: Maritime alpine climate — wet, heavy snowpack keeps avalanche danger elevated into early summer.

bySeason.spring: Approach still largely snow-covered
bySeason.summer: Primary season; conditions best by late August
bySeason.fall: Climbable into September with shortening daylight
bySeason.winter: Not a realistic climbing season

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No park entrance fee
access.permit: Free overnight wilderness/backcountry permit required for the Crescent Creek cross-country zone (Recreation.gov or Marblemount Wilderness Information Center)
access.passRequired: None for day use; overnight camping requires the NPS backcountry permit
access.closures: Pack out solid human waste with blue bags; strict food-storage rules apply

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from Crescent Creek Basin to make the most of a dry weather window on the slabs

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek Trailhead (600 ft) to Terror Basin camp (~5,800-6,000 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Stump Hollow

emergency.county: Whatcom County (access via SR-20 also crosses Skagit County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 (after-hours backup dispatch)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111; United General/PeaceHealth, Sedro-Woolley — (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: NPS after-hours Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37. Cell coverage in the Southern Pickets is minimal.

routes.corrections: No independent source could confirm a first-ascent party/date specific to the Southeast Face/South Ridge line, consistent with it being described as the least-climbed named route on the peak; fa left null rather than guessed. Summit elevation most commonly cited as 8,151 ft (USGS/Wikipedia) vs. the given 8,154 ft.

blurb: Crooked Thumb Peak (8,124–8,129 ft depending on source) is a summit in the Northern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA, sitting on the connecting ridge about half a mile south of Mount Challenger and half a mile north of Phantom Peak. Composed of North Cascades gneiss, it is one of the range's remote, glacier-flanked alpine summits, typically climbed as part of a Northern Pickets traverse or a dedicated Challenger-area trip. Fred and Helmy Beckey made the first ascent in 1940 during their extensive that-summer exploration of the Pickets, which also yielded first ascents of Phantom Peak, Mount Challenger, Luna Peak, and several other range summits.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey & Helmy Beckey, 1940
objHaz: avalanche/wet-snow hazard on approach couloirs and slopes
moats where snow pulls away from rock
afternoon thunderstorms and fast-changing maritime weather
routes.face: South ridge to west face (route gains the ridge from the Phantom Peak col, then climbs the west face)
routes.overview: The South Route gains Crooked Thumb's connecting ridge from the Phantom Peak col to the south, then follows a line of steep, solid gneiss on the west face/south ridge to the summit — the general line the Beckey brothers used on the 1940 first ascent and the same west-face variant later climbed by Roper as noted in Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide.
routes.beta: Crooked Thumb is most often climbed as one link in a longer Northern Pickets itinerary (Access Creek or Whatcom Pass approach), not as a stand-alone objective. From the south, parties reach the peak along the connecting ridge from Phantom Peak, then scramble/climb exposed class 4-5 rock on the west face to the summit fin; trip reports describe the rock on this side as solid and featured with incut holds, in contrast to looser terrain elsewhere on the peak. A north-side alternative exists via the Crooked Thumb Glacier and a northwest snow couloir from the West Challenger Notch, but that line involves a harder rock crux (reported up to 5.8+ or 5.2/A1) and is a distinct route from the South Route.
routes.approach: Standard access is a multi-day approach into the Northern Pickets: from Ross Dam Trailhead (North Cascades Highway/SR-20, milepost 134) via the Big Beaver Trail and a cross-country push up Access Creek to gain the ridge crest south of the peak, then traverse north over/around Ghost Peak and Phantom Peak to reach the Crooked Thumb col. An alternate northern approach uses the Hannegan Pass/Whatcom Pass/Perfect Pass trail system and the Challenger Glacier to reach the peak from the West Challenger Notch, but that approach serves the north-side (northwest couloir) line rather than the South Route.
routes.descent: Reverse the south ridge/west face to the Phantom col and continue the ridge traverse, or continue north over the connecting ridge toward Mount Challenger if traversing.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend by downclimbing/rappelling the same west-face terrain back to the col with Phantom Peak, then continue along the ridge either back south the way they came or, if continuing a traverse, north toward Mount Challenger. Trip reports note the ridge climbs are generally harder from the south side and the descents to the north side of intervening notches are usually easier.
routes.bail: Retreat down the climbed line to the Phantom Peak col; from there the ridge can be down-climbed/traversed back toward Access Creek and Big Beaver Trail to bail out to Ross Dam, or, if closer to the north end, toward Whatcom Pass and the Hannegan Pass trailhead.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before committing to the summit push given the multi-day, no-trail approach and the long, exposed ridge terrain; deteriorating weather or late starts should trigger retreat well before the summit given how far this peak is from any trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September once seasonal snow on the approach couloirs/glacier has consolidated and creek crossings/bushwhacking on the Access Creek approach are more manageable; earlier-season attempts encounter more avalanche-prone wet snow and larger moats/bergschrunds per trip reports.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Northern Pickets backcountry; North Cascades National Park is largely a cell-service dead zone away from its extreme edges. A satellite messenger (e.g., Garmin inReach) or satellite phone is strongly recommended for emergency communication.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine rock rack for the class 4-5 ledges and short technical steps, plus glacier travel gear (rope, crevasse-rescue kit, pickets/flukes) for the approach glaciers/snowfields.
whatToBring: Bear canister (required in North Cascades NP backcountry)
Bivy/camping gear for a multi-day approach
Navigation (map/compass/GPS) — route-finding is complex and largely off-trail
proTips: The west face has solid, featured gneiss with incut holds, described by one party as reminiscent of Teton golden granite, in contrast to looser rock elsewhere on the peak.
Summit register traffic suggests only a party or two per year climbs Crooked Thumb, so expect no beta on the ground and plan for real route-finding.
watchOut: Loose class 4-5 ledges around rocky pinnacles on the north side are notably looser than the west-face line.
Moats and bergschrunds can be significant obstacles late in the season as snow melts back from the rock.
This is a serious, remote objective — a rescue would take a long time to reach this part of the Pickets.
knownHazards: Rockfall potential on loose, exposed ledges.
Avalanche and wet-snow hazard on approach couloirs and glacier terrain, especially early season.
Crevasse hazard while roped travel is required on the Crooked Thumb and Challenger Glaciers.
Extended, trail-less approach with significant bushwhacking along Access Creek.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134)
road.status: Paved highway, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter near Ross Dam/Diablo (closure points shift between roughly MP 120 and MP 134 depending on conditions) typically early December through April or May; closures have extended into June in storm-damage years.
road.driveNote: Ross Dam Trailhead sits directly off SR-20 at milepost 134; an alternate northern approach uses Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32) off SR-542 (Mount Baker Highway) to the Hannegan Trailhead.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate typical of the west-side North Cascades: heavy Pacific precipitation, with wet, heavy snow at elevation increasing avalanche hazard, especially outside the summer climbing window.

bySeason.spring: Significant lingering snowpack and higher avalanche/wet-snow hazard on approach couloirs and glaciers.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (roughly July-September) as approach snow consolidates, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency into September-October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and high avalanche danger; SR-20 access to Ross Dam Trailhead is closed for the season.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (National Park Service)
access.fees: No specific per-visit trail fee found; national park entrance fees may apply.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness camping permit is required for any overnight stay in North Cascades National Park; summer-season permits cost $10/person plus a non-refundable $6 reservation fee (youth 15 and under free).
access.closures: SR-20 access to the Ross Dam Trailhead is closed in winter (roughly December-April/May, variable by year and storm damage).

emergency.county: Whatcom County (park headquarters/dispatch based in Sedro-Woolley, Skagit County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: North Cascades NP Communications Center (360) 856-5700 ext. 515 during business hours; after-hours (360) 873-4590 ext. 37; if unreachable, Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA (also Mt. Baker Ranger District / Glacier Public Service Center for the Hannegan Pass side approach).
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (nearest full-service hospital for the Mt. Baker Highway/Hannegan side approach).
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in this part of the backcountry; carry a satellite communicator. Rescue in the Northern Pickets is a major undertaking given the remoteness — Mount Rainier, North Cascades, and Olympic NPs have historically shared a contracted short-haul helicopter for the summer season.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,121 ft) is close to but not identical to sourced figures, which range 8,123-8,129 ft across Wikipedia, listsofjohn.com, and mountainzone.com (peakbagger.com blocked WebFetch with a 403); used 8,124 ft (listsofjohn) as the route highPointFt. Could not independently verify the given alpineGrade of "D" against any source, so left it null rather than repeat an unconfirmed value. No source specifically names a "South Route" or documents a 5.6 rating for it by that name; the description here is a synthesis of the closest documented line (south-ridge/west-face approach from the Phantom Peak col, class 4-5 per Beckey's guide and trip reports) and the given grade/FA were left as provided since nothing contradicts them, only that no source independently confirms the exact 5.6 number.

blurb: Mount Degenhardt (commonly cited as 8,000+ ft; other surveys place it 8,102-8,107 ft) is a remote summit in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, sitting at the north end of "the Barrier," the connecting ridge between the Terror Creek and Crescent Creek drainages. Like its Picket Range neighbors it is built of loose, glacially scoured granitic/metamorphic rock — locally noted as some of the chossiest rock in the Pickets — with the small Terror/Degenhardt Glacier draped across its southeast slopes. It was first climbed in 1931 by Seattle Mountaineers climbers William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg, whose 1931-32 reconnaissance of the Southern Pickets produced first ascents of most of the range's summits, including the neighboring peak later named Mount Terror in their honor.

routes.fa: William Degenhardt & Herbert Strandberg, 1931 (Seattle Mountaineers) — this is the peak's original first-ascent line; no separate first-ascent record exists for a distinct "Southwest Route" as such
routes.rappels: Parties commonly make one short rappel (or a careful downclimb) off the blocky, loose summit tower; exact rappel setup varies by conditions and party.
objHaz: Loose rock / choss on the upper mountain
Crevassed glacier crossing below the SW side (Terror/Degenhardt Glacier)
Ambiguous route-finding near the summit block
Afternoon thunderstorms common in the North Cascades in summer
Long, complex retreat if injured or benighted
routes.face: Southwest side of the peak, above Crescent (Creek) Basin
routes.overview: The Southwest Route follows Mount Degenhardt's original 1931 first-ascent line: a long alpine approach out of Crescent Basin onto the peak's southwest side over snow/glacier terrain, finishing with a short, loose pitch of low-5th-class rock (roughly 5.6) to the summit block.
routes.beta: This is far more an approach problem than a technical climb. From a camp in Terror Basin or Crescent (Creek) Basin, parties cross snowfields/glacier ice below Degenhardt's southwest flank and pick a way up broken, often wet and loose rock to the base of the summit tower, then climb a short 5.6-ish pitch (accounts describe it as "low 5th class" and chossy) to the top. Several trip reports note the line above the glacier is ambiguous and that parties who traverse too low end up on harder, looser terrain than necessary — route-finding judgment matters more than raw difficulty here. Degenhardt is frequently combined with Mount Terror, Inspiration Peak, Pyramid Peak, or the McMillan Spires on multi-day Southern Pickets trips.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Trailhead (~600 ft) off SR-20 near Newhalem, hike the old roadbed of the Goodell Creek Trail roughly 4-4.5 miles to the Terror Creek climbers' trail (marked by a rock arrow, post, and large cairn). Cross Terror Creek — typically on a downed log — then climb a steep, brushy boot path up through cliff bands onto the crest of the Barrier, breaking treeline around 5,000-5,200 ft and reaching the crest near a 6,200-6,400 ft saddle (described in trip reports as "serious Class 3" scrambling in spots, on loose ground). From the Barrier crest, descend/traverse into Crescent (Creek) Basin below Degenhardt's southwest side, crossing snowfields and glacier ice to reach the base of the final scramble/climb.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb or make a short rappel off the summit block, descend the SW-side snow/glacier back into Crescent Basin, then re-climb the Barrier and descend to Terror Creek and the Goodell Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb (or rig a short rappel on natural gear/existing anchors) off the loose summit tower back to the snow/glacier below the SW side. Re-cross the glacier and snowfields into Crescent (Creek) Basin, then climb back over the Barrier crest (the same 6,200-6,400 ft saddle used on approach) before descending the steep, brushy climbers' path to Terror Creek and following the Goodell Creek Trail roadbed back to the trailhead. Multiple trip reports emphasize staying on the established boot path across the Barrier's forested, cliff-broken slope rather than improvising a line.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan is 3 days: Day 1 — hike in from Goodell Creek Trailhead, cross Terror Creek, climb over the Barrier, and camp in Terror Basin, Crescent Creek Basin, or at a saddle camp (e.g., ~6,400 ft); Day 2 — climb Mount Degenhardt (often combined with a neighboring Southern Pickets summit); Day 3 — hike out via the same approach. Strong parties occasionally compress this to 2 days.
routes.bail: There is no shortcut retreat from Crescent/Terror Basin — an injured or turned-back party must reverse the full route, including re-climbing the Barrier, to reach the Goodell Creek Trail. In poor visibility on the glacier/snow crossings below the SW side, GPS tracking or wanding the outbound line is advisable given the featureless terrain.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour, route-finding-dependent approach over the Barrier and across glacier/snow terrain, and the loose rock on the final pitch, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to re-cross the Barrier and Terror Creek before dark; wet rock from afternoon thunderstorms or fog on the summit block meaningfully raises both the difficulty and the objective hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is most commonly reported, once seasonal snow on the Barrier traverse and Crescent Basin has consolidated or mostly melted out; some parties climb in late June in low-snow years. Degenhardt is remote enough that most ascents happen as part of longer, multi-summit Southern Pickets trips rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this part of the Picket Range backcountry; NPS notes cell/satellite phones and PLBs are useful but should not be solely relied upon, and rescue response in this terrain may take hours to days.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack is typical for the summit pitch(es): small-to-mid cams and a few nuts (roughly finger- to hand-size, e.g. 0.3-2 in equivalents), a few slings/draws, and a single rope (30-60m) sufficient for a short rappel off the summit block. Full glacier travel gear is needed for the SW-side glacier/snow crossing.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly for the final loose 5.6-ish pitch off the glacier — small cams and nuts on natural rock features; anchors for the summit rappel are typically improvised (natural gear or slung horns) rather than fixed hardware.
gear: Slings/anchor material for a summit rappel
whatToBring: Topo map/GPS with the Goodell Creek-Terror Creek-Barrier-Crescent Basin approach loaded
Bivy/camping gear for a 2-3 day trip
Bear canister or food-hang line (backcountry food storage)
Extra layers — North Cascades weather changes quickly
proTips: Stay on the established Terror Creek climbers' trail (marked by a rock arrow/cairn) rather than bushwhacking the brushy, cliff-broken slope below the Barrier.
Don't traverse too low below the summit block on the SW/S side — multiple parties report ending up on harder, looser terrain than necessary by cutting the corner.
Consider combining Degenhardt with a neighboring Southern Pickets summit (Terror, Inspiration, Pyramid, or the McMillan Spires) given the size of the approach commitment.
watchOut: The rock on the final pitch and summit block is loose and 'chossy' — test holds and be wary of rockfall onto party members below.
The Barrier crossing has sections of serious Class 3 scrambling on loose, exposed ground.
The SW-side glacier/snowfield below the summit can be crevassed; rope up as conditions warrant.
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout the upper mountain, especially near the summit tower.
Crevasse hazard on the small glacier below the SW side of the peak.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the North Cascades in summer and turn the final scramble greasy and more dangerous.
The approach crosses steep, brushy, cliff-broken terrain above Terror Creek where a fall could be serious.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Goodell Creek Road, near Newhalem
road.status: Open (as of the 2026 season, SR-20 fully reopened June 14, 2026 after an extended closure for washout/rockslide repairs between Colonial Creek and Porcupine Creek)
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 typically closes for the season in late November/early December and reopens in April or May depending on snowpack and avalanche conditions; Goodell Creek itself, near Newhalem, is on the low-elevation west side and is normally accessible whenever SR-20 is open.
road.driveNote: From Sedro-Woolley/Marblemount, drive SR-20 east to the Goodell Creek turnoff just west of Newhalem (near milepost 119); check WSDOT's real-time SR-20 map before traveling given the highway's history of closures/repairs in this corridor.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park backcountry / Skagit County high country (NWS zone forecasts and NPS trip-planning weather apply; no dedicated NWAC avalanche zone in summer season)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate: substantial precipitation, rapid weather changes, and frequent afternoon convective buildup in summer.

bySeason.spring: Heavy residual snowpack on the Barrier and Crescent Basin approach well into June; glacier travel gear essential.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep); still subject to afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving weather typical of the North Cascades.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early snow by late September/October.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible; SR-20 is gated and the approach is a serious winter mountaineering undertaking.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No route-specific fee; standard NPS wilderness permit is free.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for all overnight stays; available via Recreation.gov advance reservation (for a share of sites) or first-come walk-up at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: No park entrance fee/pass required for backcountry travel; a wilderness camping permit is mandatory for overnight trips.
access.closures: Watch for NPS wilderness-area or wildlife closures and WSDOT SR-20 corridor closures/repairs, which have periodically affected access in recent seasons; verify current status before a trip.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start from camp to allow a full margin for the Barrier crossing, glacier travel, and the loose summit pitch, plus the return crossing before dark.

sectionBreakdown.section: Goodell Creek Trailhead to Terror Creek climbers' trail
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: 600 ft TH to Terror Creek crossing
sectionBreakdown.note: ~4-4.5 mi of old roadbed, roughly 1+ hour to the marked climbers' trail turnoff.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Terror Creek crossing to ~6,200-6,400 ft saddle
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, brushy climbers' path through cliff-broken forest; described as serious Class 3 in spots.

sectionBreakdown.section: Barrier crest to Crescent Basin / SW-side base
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Barrier saddle to base of Degenhardt's SW side
sectionBreakdown.note: Snowfield/glacier traverse into Crescent (Creek) Basin.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: High camp (~6,000-6,400 ft) to summit (~8,100 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: One reported timing for a variant summit-block line; expect substantial variability by route-finding and conditions.
Includes downclimb/rappel off the summit block.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit area) / Skagit County (approach and trailhead)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-336-9450; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-384-5390; 911 also works but may route to a distant dispatch center.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — 360-854-7245 (permits); Park Dispatch (best number if you have cell signal in-park for an emergency): 360-873-4500 ext. 37.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E. Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — 360-424-4111 (Level III Emergency Department); Skagit Regional Health's Sedro-Woolley-area facility (formerly United General) is closer to the trailhead for urgent (non-trauma) care.
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage in the Picket Range backcountry. NPS emphasizes self-sufficiency: rescue response can take hours to days depending on weather and terrain. A wilderness/backcountry permit is required and helps rangers know your itinerary if you are overdue.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,102 ft) is within the range reported by different sources: Wikipedia/NPS-style sources round to "8,000+ ft," Mountain Project's Mt Degenhardt area page lists 7,812 ft, and listsofjohn.com lists 8,107 ft — likely reflecting different DEM/survey datasets rather than a real discrepancy in the peak's height; I kept the given 8,102 ft rather than override it, since no single source clearly supersedes the others. Multiple modern trip reports (AAC's "Walking the Fence," onehikeaweek, Dr. Dirtbag) describe the summit scramble as Class 3/4 via ridge/notch lines rather than a clean 5.6 pitch, and one Steph Abegg trip report documents a separately-named "Degenhardt South Route" (low-5th-class, ~5.7-ish, from a different aspect) rather than a "Southwest Route" by that exact name — I found no source using the exact route name "Southwest Route," but the given grade (III, 5.6) and FA year (1931) are consistent with the documented original/easiest line up the peak's south/southwest side, so I did not overwrite the given route identity, only supplemented it with corroborating beta.

blurb: West McMillan Spire (roughly 8,000-8,040 ft depending on source) is the higher of the twin McMillan Spires in the remote Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, built of biotite gneiss and standing above the Terror Glacier. The Southern Pickets were largely opened up by teenage brothers Fred and Helmy Beckey, who made the first ascent of West McMillan Spire in 1940 during their pioneering forays into the range. Reached only via a long, unmaintained approach and glacier crossing, it remains one of the most sought-after and committing objectives in the Pickets.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, 1940 (brothers were 16 and 14 at the time; part of their pioneering forays into the Southern Pickets)
objHaz: loose rock / choss on the upper ridge and gully
unmaintained, brushy approach trail with numerous blowdowns
short but exposed Class 3 step below the summit block
remote location with no easy bail once above Terror Basin
routes.face: West Ridge, above the Terror Glacier
routes.overview: A long, remote alpine scramble up the west side of West McMillan Spire in the Southern Picket Range, climbing mixed snow, scree and low-class rock along the peak's west ridge to a short, exposed Class 3 summit block; the standard and most-climbed line in the Southern Pickets.
routes.beta: From a camp at Terror Basin, cross benches and a high glacial-lake outlet (~5,550 ft) onto rock and snow leading to the Terror Glacier below the spire. Climb toward the base of the west ridge, ascend a steep snow/scree gully that transitions to heather and more scree, and finish with a short, exposed Class 3 scramble to the summit block. Reported difficulty ranges from sustained Class 2-3 to a brief harder step near the top; some parties belay a short section on slabby rock for security. A separate, harder 'Southwest Ridge' variation (5.8-) exists nearby.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive SR-20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem and turn onto Newhalem Creek/Goodell Creek Road just east of Goodell Creek Campground. The Goodell Creek climbers' trail starts behind a large boulder at the campground (~600 ft), follows an old roadbed for roughly 3.5-4.5 miles, then turns steeply uphill with no switchbacks at a cairn/rock arrow, climbing through forest onto a ridge around 3,200 ft. Continue above treeline through a notch near Glee Peak, traverse broad benches, and drop to the outlet of a high glacial lake before reaching the established camp at Terror Basin.
routes.descent: Descend the same west-ridge line back to the Terror Glacier and Terror Basin, downclimbing or glissading snow where safe and favoring skier's-right on the looser upper gully.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ridge scramble to the top of the snow/scree gully. Downclimb the gully staying skier's right where the rock is more solid and less loose; snow sections can often be boot-skied or glissaded when soft enough, but late-season the gully may show bare, polished ice requiring care or a short rappel/downclimb near the moat. Re-cross the Terror Glacier back toward camp, watching for crevasses and softened snow bridges in the afternoon. Continue down through the notch and benches to Terror Basin camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike from Goodell Creek trailhead to Terror Basin camp (strenuous, several hours with a loaded pack). Day 2: alpine-start summit push via the west ridge and back to camp. Day 3: hike out to the trailhead. Some strong parties complete it car-to-car in a single very long day, but most climb it as a 2-3 day trip.
routes.bail: Retreat below the summit block is a straightforward reversal of the ridge and gully, though loose rock makes downclimbing slow. Once committed above Terror Basin there is no shorter escape route off this side of the Pickets -- retreat follows the ascent line back through the notch to Terror Basin and out the Goodell Creek trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before leaving camp and stick to it; the descent involves loose rock and glacier travel that become significantly more hazardous in deteriorating weather, low light, or softened afternoon snow, and the approach trail is difficult to navigate in the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Most parties climb in July through September. Early-season attempts (June) typically require more snow/glacier travel skill and gear as the Terror Glacier and approach snowfields are more continuous; by late season the glacier is more broken and the gully rock more exposed and choss-prone, though snow bridges become less reliable.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier-travel kit (harness, crevasse-rescue gear, prusiks) and a rope are standard given the Terror Glacier crossing. Some parties carry a small rack of nuts/light cams to protect short rock steps or belay the exposed summit-block move, though many competent scramblers climb it unroped once conditions are dry.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal and route-finding/conditions dependent -- a few small nuts or cams cover the short technical steps; the main protection concern is glacier travel gear for the Terror Glacier crossing.
whatToBring: sturdy mountaineering or stiff hiking boots
trekking poles for the old-roadbed section
bug protection (approach can be buggy in summer)
water treatment (limited water sources on the upper approach)
overnight camping gear for Terror Basin
map/GPS -- the unmaintained trail and open benches are easy to lose track of
proTips: Camp at Terror Basin to split the long approach over two days; a rested start makes the summit push meaningfully safer.
Watch for the cairn/rock arrow around mile 4-4.5 of Goodell Creek trail where the route leaves the old roadbed and turns steeply uphill.
Trekking poles help on the lower roadbed but become a liability on the steep, root-choked upper trail.
Many parties combine this peak with nearby Mount Terror and/or Mount Degenhardt from the same Terror Basin camp.
watchOut: Snow bridges on the Terror Glacier become less reliable by late summer -- rope up and probe.
The descent gully's moat/bergschrund can be awkward to negotiate late season.
The unmaintained climbers' trail is easy to lose, especially in the dark or in fog.
The Pickets are highly weather-exposed with no quick bail; deteriorating weather should trigger an early retreat.
knownHazards: The unmaintained Goodell Creek approach trail is brushy with numerous blowdowns that can significantly slow travel.
Terror Glacier is crevassed and requires glacier-travel skills and gear, particularly early season.
The upper ridge and gully hold loose rock (choss) hazardous to parties below.
A short, exposed Class 3 step guards the true summit and has caught out parties on wet or icy rock.

pitchDetail.notes: Terror Basin camp to the base of the Terror Glacier via benches and a high-lake outlet (~5,550 ft).
Cross the (crevassed) Terror Glacier toward the base of West McMillan's west ridge; rope for glacier travel.
Steep snow gully transitioning to scree and heather, then more scree, gaining the west ridge crest.
Mostly easy ridge scrambling with a choice of solid rock steps or looser talus.
Short, exposed step to the true summit; some parties protect this section.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Goodell Creek Road
road.status: Open year-round to Newhalem/Goodell Creek
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally between Newhalem and Mazama (roughly late Nov-late Apr/May); the closure gate sits at Newhalem itself, so Goodell Creek Campground and trailhead on the west side remain accessible year-round.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, follow SR-20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem; Goodell Creek Road/Campground is signed on the north side of the highway.

climate.typical: Maritime North Cascades climate: wet, snowy winters/springs and drier but still unsettled summers. The Southern Pickets hold heavy snowpack into midsummer, so route conditions (glacier cover, snow vs. bare rock) shift significantly through the season.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack persists; glacier and steep snow travel skills required, avalanche hazard on approach slopes.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); still typically snow-covered into July on the glacier and gully.
bySeason.fall: Drier rock and more exposed choss by late season; shorter days and increasing chance of early-season storms/snow.
bySeason.winter: Not a viable climbing season -- deep snow and avalanche hazard make the approach unreasonable even though the road stays open to Newhalem.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No day-use fee; overnight backcountry camping requires a paid wilderness permit ($10/person plus a $6 reservation fee, per WTA).
access.permit: Backcountry/wilderness permit required for overnight stays, obtained from the NPS Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or via Recreation.gov reservation.
access.passRequired: None for day-use parking at the Goodell Creek trailhead (NPS land, not a USFS trailhead).

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from Terror Basin camp to cross the glacier and descend before afternoon snow softens and rockfall/glacier hazard increases.

sectionBreakdown.note: Commonly 6-9+ hours with an overnight pack on the steep, unmaintained trail; one recorded GPS track shows ~7.4 mi and ~5,835 ft gain to camp.
Round trip from camp commonly reported as 8-12 hours depending on conditions and party; fast/light parties have done the full car-to-summit push in as little as ~5.5 hours.
Roughly 6-8 hours descending the same approach trail.

waypoints.name: Goodell Creek Campground / climbers' trail start

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 360-676-6911; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley (360-856-6021); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (360-424-4111) for higher-level care
emergency.notes: Extremely remote terrain with little to no expected cell coverage; an NPS backcountry permit itinerary and a filed trip plan with a home contact are the primary way rescuers would learn a party is overdue.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation varies by source: Wikipedia/USGS-derived listings give 8,004 ft, listsofjohn.com gives 8,041 ft, and Mountain Project rounds to 8,000 ft; the given 8,038 ft falls within this spread and was left unchanged. Mountain Project's community route page rates its 'West Ridge' line as roughly 4th class / NCCS Grade I (23 logged ascents) and separately lists a harder 'Southwest Ridge' at 5.8-; no source independently confirms a straight 5.6 rating or Grade III commitment for the combined 'West Ridge / Southwest Approach' name used in the given data, though the long multi-day approach and glacier travel are consistent with the Grade III commitment level used in guidebook-style route descriptions. The FA is correctly attributed to the Beckey brothers in 1940, and this west-ridge line appears to be essentially their original route.

blurb: The Pyramid is a remote summit in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, standing between Mount Degenhardt and Inspiration Peak and separated from Mount Terror's basin by a sharp connecting ridge called "The Barricade." Like the rest of the Pickets, it is built of granitic and gneissic bedrock and was first climbed in 1951, decades after the range's jagged skyline had already drawn attention from early Cascades explorers. It sees few ascents each year owing to the multi-day off-trail approach required to reach it.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine line up the south side of The Pyramid, gained from a high col on "The Barrier"/"The Barricade" ridge system between Crescent Creek Basin and Terror Basin; most modern parties describe the final push to the summit as scrambling on the ridge above the col rather than sustained pitched climbing.
routes.beta: The Pyramid is a secondary objective in the Southern Pickets, usually tagged onto a longer trip based out of Crescent Creek Basin or Terror Basin alongside Mount Degenhardt, Inspiration Peak, and Mount Terror. Guidebook sources (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide) list a technical South Route from the 1951 first ascent, while several recent trip reports describe reaching the summit by 3rd-class scrambling from the col above the Barrier/Barricade crossing — the easiest way to the top may not follow the original 1951 line exactly.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek trailhead off State Route 20 near Newhalem, follow the old road bed and climbers' path up Goodell Creek into the Southern Pickets, then continue off-trail to Terror Basin (via Terror Creek) or over into Crescent Creek Basin. From a high camp below Mount Terror or in Crescent Creek Basin, cross "The Barrier"/"The Barricade" ridge system (high or low crossing) to reach the col beneath The Pyramid and Mount Degenhardt, then ascend south-facing slopes/ridge to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to the col and down to Crescent Creek Basin or Terror Basin.
routes.descentText: Descend the same south-side terrain used on the ascent back to the col between The Pyramid and Mount Degenhardt, then downclimb/scramble back into Crescent Creek Basin (or re-cross the Barrier/Barricade back toward Terror Basin, depending on approach used). No fixed rappel stations are documented; parties should be prepared to downclimb or set gear rappels on loose rock if needed.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally back down the ascent slopes to the col; there is no easier alternate descent documented, so parties should be prepared to reverse technical terrain in poor conditions.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day off-trail approach and lack of any quick bail-out, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to re-cross the Barrier/Barricade and return to camp before dark, and account for the difficulty of downclimbing this terrain if conditions deteriorate.
routes.bestSeason: The Southern Pickets are typically climbable July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared from the approach slopes; August is statistically the driest month in this part of the North Cascades and is considered prime time, though early-July parties have also climbed here using leftover snowfields to speed travel.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Southern Pickets is unreliable; the National Park Service notes reception is possible at some higher points but should never be relied on as an evacuation plan — expect no signal in the basins and cols.
routes.face: South side, approached via the Barrier/Barricade col between The Pyramid and Mount Degenhardt
proTips: Cross the Barrier/Barricade from east to west (Terror Basin to Crescent Creek Basin) if possible so you can see the cliff you need to surmount, rather than routefinding it blindly from below.
The Pyramid is often combined with Mount Degenhardt from the same high col, since both are reached from the same crossing point.
Expect classic "Pickets Class 3" — exposed, loose scrambling that becomes far more serious when wet.
watchOut: Loose, blocky rock typical of the Pickets, especially on ledges and gullies.
The Barrier/Barricade crossing itself is a crux of the trip and has caught out parties trying to find the right line.
Wet rock turns easy scrambling terrain here into a genuinely hazardous proposition.
knownHazards: Extended off-trail, brushy, and exposed approach with significant routefinding.
Loose rock on the summit slopes and connecting ridges.
No easy bail-out once committed above the Barrier/Barricade col.
Remote location means any rescue may take many hours to days to arrive.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park Complex
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay; obtained through the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or via recreation.gov.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Goodell Creek Road/trailhead
road.status: Goodell Creek Road and trailhead are reached directly off SR 20 near Newhalem (about 13 miles east of Marblemount); beyond the road end, access into the Southern Pickets is via an unmaintained climbers' path and off-trail travel.
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 itself is open year-round at this elevation (the seasonal closure is farther east near Washington Pass), but snow lingers on the approach slopes into the Pickets well into summer.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced Cascades climate with a short dry-and-stable window in mid-to-late summer; the Pickets are known for holding weather and generating their own localized cloud and precipitation.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack still covers approach slopes and cols; travel is glacier/snow travel oriented.
bySeason.summer: July-August is the primary climbing window as snow recedes from approach terrain; August is typically the driest.
bySeason.fall: Early September can still be climbable but new snow can arrive with little warning as the season turns.
bySeason.winter: Not a viable climbing season; deep snow and avalanche hazard make the approach impractical.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (Southern Pickets area), immediately adjacent to Skagit County near the Goodell Creek access
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff dispatch: 360-384-5390; Skagit County Sheriff dispatch: 360-336-9450 (general emergency: 911, though wilderness calls may route to a distant dispatch center)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA (24-hour ER)
emergency.notes: North Cascades National Park and the local County Sheriff's Office are jointly responsible for backcountry rescues, with volunteer Mountain Rescue units assisting; response can take hours to days given the remoteness and terrain.

routes.corrections: Sourcing conflict on elevation: the peak data given lists 8,031 ft, but Wikipedia's Picket Range peak table lists The Pyramid at 7,960 ft, and multiple trip reports (Steph Abegg, The Mountaineers) describe the summit as roughly 7,920 ft. The first-ascent year of 1951 is corroborated by Wikipedia's Picket Range table. I could not independently confirm the specific route name "South Route" (III, 5.6, PD+); available online sources describe The Pyramid's easiest line as a 3rd-class scramble via the col on the Barrier/Barricade ridge (sometimes called a "West Ridge" approach), while a separate, harder East Ridge/Face line is documented elsewhere at II 5.8 — it is plausible the 1951 first-ascent party took a harder direct line than the scramble route later parties use, but I could not verify this in an online source (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, the standard reference for Picket Range route names/grades, was not accessible online).
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light mountaineering boots suited to scrambling
Standard alpine rock rack for the technical sections (per guidebook route description)
Bivy/overnight gear for the multi-day approach
Map and compass/GPS for the Barrier/Barricade crossing routefinding

blurb: Phantom Peak (8,016 ft) is a remote summit in the Northern Pickets of North Cascades National Park's Picket Range, rising above Luna Cirque between Crooked Thumb Peak and Mount Fury. Composed of solid granitic rock interspersed with steep snow and small hanging glaciers, it is one of the range's lower-profile but still committing objectives. It was first climbed in 1940 by teenage brothers Fred and Helmy Beckey during their pioneering exploration of the Pickets.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, 1940
objHaz: exposed unroped scrambling on summit ridge
routes.face: Southwest Buttress / Southwest Glacier-South Couloir-Southwest Ridge
routes.overview: The South (Southwest) Route climbs Phantom Peak's southwest side via a hanging pocket glacier, a bergschrund crossing, a moderately steep snow finger, and Class 3-4 rock scrambling along the summit ridge to a small false summit and then the true summit.
routes.beta: From a high camp near the Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine, descend and traverse to Phantom Peak's broad southwest buttress, climb a snowfield to a roughly 6,100-foot saddle, then ascend the pocket glacier tucked between cliffs on the peak's southwest side. Cross the bergschrund (reported wide but shallow, with slabby-rock crampon moves), then zig-zag up a moderately steep (40°+) snow finger protected with pickets/flukes. Angle left onto a broken rock slope (Class 2-3) to a dip in the summit ridge, then follow a long, exposed Class 3-4 traverse along the solid granitic ridge crest, past a small false summit (parties have used a hand line to protect the crossing), to the true summit.
routes.approach: Start at the Hannegan Trailhead (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF, end of Hannegan Pass Road/FR 32). Hike the Hannegan Pass Trail over Hannegan Pass and down the Chilliwack River Trail, then climb to Whatcom Pass and continue east, traversing below Whatcom Peak and Mount Challenger's glacier toward Crooked Thumb Peak, to a camp on the Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine below Phantom Peak's southwest side. A logged multi-day trip covered this approach in about 3 days and roughly 8,700 ft of cumulative gain from trailhead to high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down-climb the Class 3-4 summit ridge and false-summit hand line, descend the snow finger and glacier, and re-cross the bergschrund back to the southwest buttress base.
routes.descentText: Parties descend by reversing the ascent line rather than rappelling: back down the exposed granite ridge crest (using a hand line to re-cross the false-summit notch if one was placed on the way up), down-climb the broken Class 2-3 rock slope, then down-climb/glissade the snow finger (40°+) back across the bergschrund and pocket glacier to the southwest buttress, and traverse back to the moraine camp. One trip report logged roughly 5.7 hours from summit back to high camp.
routes.itinerary: A typical trip is a multi-day backpack: Day 1 Hannegan TH to a camp near Copper Creek/Boundary Camp (~2,000 ft gain); Day 2 continue over Whatcom Pass toward the Whatcom Arm area (~3,000 ft gain); Day 3 traverse to a camp on the Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine below Phantom Peak (~3,700 ft gain); Day 4 climb Phantom Peak's Southwest Route (about 7 hours up, 6 hours down to camp); remaining days retrace the approach back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Below the false-summit hand line and upper ridge, retreat is by reversing the same terrain (down-climb the ridge, then the snow finger); there is no shorter bail option once established on the southwest buttress/glacier, so deteriorating weather or softening snow should prompt turning around before committing to the bergschrund crossing.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting the snow finger and summit-ridge traverse: the bergschrund crossing and exposed Class 3-4 ridge are far more hazardous to reverse in the dark, in poor visibility, or once morning-firm snow has softened, so budget a conservative round-trip window from high camp and stick to it.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in a narrow early-to-mid summer window (roughly late June through August) while the snow finger and glacier crossing are still consolidated enough to protect with pickets/flukes but before the bergschrund opens up too far; a successful ascent was documented in late June 2016. The deep, remote approach means conditions are rarely dialed-in for long.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit (crampons, ice axe, harness, glacier rope) plus several snow pickets or flukes for the bergschrund and snow-finger section; one recorded ascent used two lightweight 6.9mm ropes (40m + 30m, 70m total) for simul-climbing/glacier travel, plus a hand line/sling for the false-summit crossing on the upper ridge.
routes.proNeeds: Snow protection (pickets/flukes) is the primary gear need on the glacier and snow-finger section; the Class 3-4 ridge above is typically climbed unroped or protected with a simple hand line at the exposed false-summit notch, since the loose, broken rock offers limited natural gear placements.
whatToBring: bear-resistant food canister (required seasonally in parts of the NOCA backcountry)
sturdy boots for the long multi-day approach
map/compass or GPS for complex Luna Cirque/Whatcom Pass navigation
extra rope/sling for a hand line at the false summit
proTips: The route-finding across the southwest buttress and pocket glacier is as much the crux as any single move; study trip-report photos and expect to problem-solve terrain on the fly.
A small false summit sits a few hundred feet before the true summit on the ridge crest; don't mistake it for the top, and consider a hand line to protect the crossing.
The summit register has historically been signed very infrequently, reflecting how rarely this peak is visited even by Pickets standards.
watchOut: The bergschrund on the pocket glacier has been reported wide (though not deep) and requires careful crampon work on slabby rock to cross.
The snow finger above the bergschrund reaches 40+ degrees and should be assessed for firmness before committing.
The upper ridge is loose, blocky granite with real exposure on both sides; a slip or rockfall both carry serious consequences.
knownHazards: Crevasse and bergschrund hazard on the approach glacier and pocket glacier below the summit ridge.
Loose, blocky rock on the Class 3-4 summit ridge increases rockfall risk to parties below.
Remote location deep in the Picket Range means any rescue would be a multi-day, helicopter-dependent effort.

pitchDetail.notes: Descend/traverse from the Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine camp to Phantom Peak's southwest buttress, then climb a snowfield to a roughly 6,100 ft saddle.
Cross the pocket glacier nestled between cliffs on the peak's southwest side to the bergschrund.
Cross the bergschrund (wide but shallow; slabby-rock crampon moves) and zig-zag up a moderately steep snow finger, protected with pickets/flukes.
Angle left onto a broken rock slope, scrambling up to a dip in the summit ridge.
Long, exposed traverse along the solid granitic summit ridge crest to a small false summit; a hand line has been used to protect the crossing to the true summit.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: Paved/gravel Forest Service road to a road-end trailhead; day-use fee area requiring a Northwest Forest Pass or interagency pass
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA drive east on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) about 12.5 miles, turn onto Hannegan Pass Road (FR 32), and follow it 5.3 miles to the road-end trailhead; the road has had limited access for larger vehicles due to flood damage.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Mount Baker-Snoqualmie backcountry
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced alpine climate typical of the North Cascades: heavy winter snowpack, a short prime climbing window, and rapid weather changes even in summer.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack still covers approach trails and passes into June; access to Hannegan Pass/Whatcom Pass opens progressively as snow melts.
bySeason.summer: The main climbing window (roughly late June-August); expect residual snow on the glacier/bergschrund crossing and be ready for afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and the first fall storms/snow typically end the season by September-October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain make the approach roads and trails effectively closed to climbing access.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (peak/climb) and Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (approach trailhead/road)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day (or interagency/Northwest Forest Pass) at the Hannegan Trailhead; NPS backcountry permit fee of $10/person plus a $6 reservation fee for overnight camping (youth 15 and under free)
access.permit: Overnight backcountry permit required, reservable on Recreation.gov with a mandatory orientation video, or by advance email to noca_wilderness@nps.gov for walk-up permits
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency (America the Beautiful) pass for trailhead parking; Washington Discover Pass is not valid at this US Forest Service fee site
access.closures: Bear-resistant food canisters are required in some North Cascades NP cross-country/alpine zones from June 1-November 15 where trees suitable for hanging food aren't available; check current NPS food-storage requirements for the Whatcom Pass/Pickets zone before the trip.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Camp 1 to Whatcom Pass/Whatcom Arm area
sectionBreakdown.note: +3,000 ft gain, Day 2

sectionBreakdown.note: +3,700 ft gain, Day 3

sectionBreakdown.note: Southwest buttress/glacier/ridge route
Reverse of ascent route

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 360-676-6911 (911 for emergencies); Whatcom County Search & Rescue dispatched through the Sheriff's Office
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA - (360) 854-7245 / (360) 854-7200; Glacier Public Service Center (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF) - (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: Extremely remote alpine terrain deep in the Picket Range; any rescue would likely require a multi-day ground effort or helicopter, and cell coverage in Luna Cirque/Whatcom Pass is unreliable.

routes.corrections: Wikipedia (citing GNIS) lists the easiest route as the 'Southwest Route, Class 4' and gives the peak's elevation as '8,000+ ft'; this matches the given route (renamed 'South Route' here) and its 8,016 ft figure closely, so no numeric correction was made, but note the route is more precisely described in trip reports as combining a southwest glacier, a bergschrund/snow-finger crux, and a southwest ridge scramble rather than a single simple 'south' line. A distinct, harder technical route on the peak's West Ridge (IV 5.7+, first climbed later) also exists but is not the route covered here.

blurb: Ghost Peak is a small, sheer granite horn in the Northern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, wedged between Crooked Thumb Peak and Phantom Peak and marked by a huge, steep east face. Its rock is noted as some of the more solid granite in the notoriously choss-prone Pickets, likened to the golden granite of the Tetons. It was first climbed July 17, 1970 by Joan Firey, Carla Firey, David Knudson, and Peter Renz during an 11-day traverse of the Goodell Creek drainage, via the south face to the south arête and onto the west face.

routes.fa: Carla Firey, Joan Firey, David Knudson, Peter Renz — July 17, 1970
routes.face: South Face / South Arête, finishing on the West Face
objHaz: Loose or 'choss'-prone rock is common on neighboring Pickets summits, though Ghost's own rock is comparatively solid
Crevasse and moat hazard crossing the Whatcom and Challenger Glaciers on approach, worse later in summer
Extreme remoteness deep in the Northern Pickets with no quick retreat option
Route-finding difficulty on the multi-day cross-country approach
routes.overview: A short, easy-to-moderate 4th/low-5th-class rock scramble up the south and west sides of a small granite horn between Crooked Thumb and Phantom Peaks, almost always climbed as a side trip from a Crooked Thumb Glacier high camp or as part of a longer Northern Pickets enchainment rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.beta: The 1970 first-ascent party gained the notch south of the summit from the west side, then stayed on the south and west faces, following 'obvious weaknesses' over easy-to-moderate fifth-class pitches to the top. Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide separately notes an 'exposed class 4' line on the west face reported by climber Steve Roper. Modern parties (e.g. a 2005 nine-summit Northern Pickets traverse) have simul-climbed over Ghost Peak en route between Phantom and Crooked Thumb; rock quality here is reported as notably better than on adjacent Pickets summits.
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Trailhead (end of Ruth Creek/Hannegan Pass Road, FS-32), follow the Hannegan Pass, Chilliwack River, and Brush Creek trails to Whatcom Pass, then travel cross-country over the Whatcom and Challenger Glaciers via Perfect Pass to a high camp on the Crooked Thumb Glacier's lateral moraine — the standard base from which Ghost Peak's south side is reached, roughly 2-3 days of travel one-way.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down the west/south faces to the connecting notch; no fixed rappel anchors are established on the peak.
routes.descentText: Descend by down-climbing/reversing the south-west face line back to the notch south of the summit, then descend to the Crooked Thumb Glacier camp. New bolted anchors are prohibited under North Cascades National Park's clean-climbing wilderness policy, so any rappels must rely on natural anchors, slung horns, or removable protection.
routes.itinerary: Typical 4-6 day round trip: Day 1 - Hannegan Trailhead to a camp along the Chilliwack River Trail; Day 2 - continue via the cablecar crossing and Brush Creek Trail to a camp near Whatcom Pass; Day 3 - cross the Whatcom and Challenger Glaciers via Perfect Pass to a high camp on the Crooked Thumb Glacier moraine (base for Ghost, Phantom, and Crooked Thumb); Day 4 - climb Ghost Peak's south side and return to camp; Days 5-6 - reverse the approach back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the climbing line back to the south notch and down to the glacier camp; given the peak's position deep in the Northern Pickets there is no faster bail option.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach is multi-day and fully glaciated with no quick egress, set a turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to re-cross the Whatcom/Challenger Glaciers back to camp, and do not linger on the exposed south/west faces if weather moves in.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early September, once the approach trail is snow-free and the Whatcom/Challenger Glacier crossing has consolidated; early August is statistically the driest window in the North Cascades, though glaciers become more broken and crevassed as summer progresses.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the interior of the Picket Range; a satellite communicator or messenger is strongly advised given the multi-day remoteness from the nearest trailhead or road.
routes.detailedRack: Only a light rock rack is needed for the class 4/low-5th climbing itself (small-to-medium cams, a few nuts, and slings for natural anchors); the more consequential kit is full glacier-travel gear for the Whatcom and Challenger Glacier crossings on approach.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection is needed on the peak's easy fifth-class terrain; prioritize glacier rescue gear (rope, prusiks/mechanical ascenders, pickets) over a large rock rack.
gear: Light rock rack (small cams and nuts)
whatToBring: Bear canister (required for overnight camping in North Cascades NP backcountry)
Multi-day bivy/camping gear for the approach
Sun protection for extended glacier travel
proTips: Climb Ghost as a short add-on from a Crooked Thumb Glacier camp rather than a dedicated objective, since reaching its base alone requires the full multi-day Northern Pickets approach.
Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide records an 'exposed class 4' west-face line (per Steve Roper) as a variation on the 1970 first-ascent south-face/south-arête line.
watchOut: Rock quality varies sharply across the Northern Pickets; Ghost's granite is comparatively solid but neighboring terrain is often loose and rotten
Crevasses and moats on the Whatcom/Challenger Glacier approach become more pronounced later in the season
The approach is long, remote, and route-finding intensive with essentially no fast retreat
knownHazards: Multi-day glaciated approach with real crevasse exposure on the Whatcom and Challenger Glaciers
Very remote location deep in the Northern Pickets, far from any road, implying long search-and-rescue response times
Reports of loose/rotten ('choss') rock are common on nearby Pickets summits even though Ghost itself is regarded as solid

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Service Road 32) off Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542)
road.status: Open to standard passenger vehicles; limited access for larger vehicles/trailers due to past flood damage near the trailhead
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive about 12.5 miles east on SR 542, then turn onto Hannegan Pass Road (FS-32) and continue 5.3 miles to the Hannegan Trailhead (approx. 3,100 ft).

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park backcountry / Northern Pickets (nearest point forecasts are for Mount Challenger and Whatcom Peak)
climate.typical: Cool, wet, and highly changeable crest weather typical of the North Cascades; glaciers and snowfields dominate the approach terrain into mid-summer

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack and avalanche hazard; approach trail and glacier crossings generally still snowed in
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window; early August is statistically the driest/most stable period, though glaciers open up with more exposed crevasses as summer progresses
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early snow at elevation after September
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible for standard climbing due to heavy snow and avalanche terrain

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park, Stephen Mather Wilderness); trailhead access via Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass required for trailhead parking (about $5/day or $30/year); no separate climbing fee
access.permit: Free NPS wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight camping, available from the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or the Glacier Public Service Center
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (day use at trailhead); NPS backcountry permit (overnight)
access.closures: New fixed-anchor (bolt) placement is prohibited under NPS's mandatory clean-climbing moratorium, which applies to the Stephen Mather Wilderness covering 99% of the park

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff non-emergency dispatch: 360-676-6911 (911 for emergencies)
