routes.corrections: Elevation is inconsistent across sources: Mountain Project's Ghost Peak area page lists 7,032 ft, while Wikipedia and secondary sources describe it only as '8,000+ ft' — closer to the catalog's given 7,993 ft. The MP figure looks like a likely data error, since Ghost sits immediately between Crooked Thumb Peak (8,124 ft) and Phantom Peak (8,045 ft) as a connecting horn, which is hard to reconcile with a summit 1,000 ft lower. Separately, the primary 1970 first-ascent account (AAC) describes 'easy to moderate fifth-class pitches,' slightly harder than the catalog's listed Class 4 rating — Beckey's guide records a distinct 'exposed class 4' west-face variation (per Steve Roper) that may be the basis for the Class 4 grade.

blurb: East McMillan Spire (~7,989–7,992 ft, sources vary slightly) is the lower, eastern summit of the McMillan Spires, a twin-summited peak in the Southern Pickets of North Cascades National Park, separated from the higher West McMillan Spire (8,004 ft) by only about 0.14 mile of connecting ridge. Like the rest of the Pickets, it rises in resistant biotite gneiss/granite that gives the range its jagged, glacier-carved profile above the McMillan Creek and Terror Glacier cirques. Fred and Helmy Beckey's exploratory 1940 Picket Range trips produced the first ascent of the McMillan Spires' main (west) summit via its West Ridge; the range's remote approach and steep faces have since drawn much harder modern lines, including a 2,400-foot, 29-pitch V 5.10- route on East McMillan Spire's northeast buttress.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, 1940
objHaz: Loose/rotten rock typical of the Pickets
Crevassed travel on the McMillan Creek/Terror Glacier remnants
Steep snow/ice gully (up to ~40°) below the West Ridge col
Exposed low-5th-class ridge scrambling near the summit
Rockfall off surrounding faces and gullies
Very remote — no quick evacuation if injured
routes.face: Southwest side, reached via the shared West Ridge approach and the col with West McMillan Spire
routes.overview: A moderate alpine outing that follows the same West Ridge approach used for West McMillan Spire — a snow/scree gully to the roughly 7,200-ft col between the two spires — then continues east along a low-fifth-class ridge and a short southwest-facing rock section to reach the true (east) summit of the McMillan Spires.
routes.beta: Most parties climb East McMillan Spire as an add-on to West McMillan Spire via the shared Terror Basin/West Ridge approach, then traverse east along the connecting ridge crest (roughly 5.6) to tag the lower, eastern summit.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek trailhead near Newhalem on SR 20, follow the Goodell Creek trail and climbers' path up through old-growth forest and brush, then ascend steep heather and rocky benches to Terror Basin camp (~5,800 ft). From camp, cross snow/talus benches, pass the outlet of the basin's glacial tarn, and climb slabs and snow onto the Terror Glacier remnant below the McMillan Spires.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb/traverse the connecting ridge back to the West Ridge col, descend the snow/scree gully onto the glacier, and retrace the approach to Terror Basin and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the East summit, downclimb the connecting ridge back toward West McMillan Spire's West Ridge and the ~7,200-ft col. From the col, face in and downclimb the steep snow/ice gully (up to ~40° near the top) back onto the Terror Glacier remnant, then descend talus and heather benches to Terror Basin camp before reversing the Goodell Creek approach to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typical 3-day plan: Day 1 — hike in to Terror Basin camp (~5,800 ft); Day 2 — climb West McMillan Spire via the West Ridge, traverse the connecting ridge to East McMillan Spire's summit, and return to camp; Day 3 — hike out via Goodell Creek.
routes.bail: Retreat is essentially a reversal of the ridge traverse and the West Ridge snow/ice gully back onto the glacier; there is no shorter escape route given the peak's remote, glacier-guarded position.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and exposed glacier/gully terrain, set a firm turnaround time before starting the ridge traverse so the gully descent and multi-mile hike out aren't done after dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed mid/late July through September, once approach brush has dried out and the Terror Glacier snow gully is soft enough to kick steps without excessive icy, refrozen conditions. Earlier-season (June) trips are wetter and snowier with more avalanche/glissade hazard; later-season (September) trips find the glacier more broken and crevassed.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack — small set of nuts/cams to about 2 inches, a few slings — plus ice axe and crampons for the glacier and snow gully, and a rope for the low-5th-class ridge traverse and any short rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Gear needs are modest: mostly for the short 5.6 sections on the connecting ridge and summit block, since the bulk of the route is snow/glacier travel and 3rd-4th class scrambling.
whatToBring: bivy/overnight gear for the multi-day approach
map/compass or GPS for glacier navigation
extra layers for the exposed ridge
proTips: Most parties tag West McMillan Spire first via the West Ridge, then continue the ridge traverse east to the lower East summit rather than approaching East McMillan Spire in isolation.
Firm early-morning snow makes the steep gully below the West Ridge col much safer than soft afternoon conditions.
watchOut: The connecting ridge between the two McMillan summits has sustained exposure at low 5th class — not a place for beginners.
The Terror Glacier remnant grows more broken/crevassed later in the season.
Loose rock is common throughout the Pickets; a helmet is recommended.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the McMillan Creek/Terror Glacier remnants.
Rockfall from surrounding faces and gullies.
Long, remote approach with limited bail options.

road.name: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Newhalem, then Newhalem Creek/Goodell Creek Road
road.status: Newhalem is reachable year-round via SR 20 from the west (Sedro-Woolley/Marblemount side)
road.seasonalGate: The SR 20 gate east of Diablo/Ross Lake closes roughly late November to late April with snowpack, but this does not affect access to Newhalem/Goodell Creek
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive SR 20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem; find Newhalem Creek Road on the north side just east of Goodell Campground, follow it to a T-junction, turn left, then take the next right uphill toward the group campground/Goodell Creek trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park backcountry, Skagit County — no NWAC avalanche coverage in the summer climbing season; use NWS point/zone forecasts
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate: wet, heavy-snow winters and a relatively short, moderate summer climbing window

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; lower-elevation brush still buried
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms possible
bySeason.fall: Shortening days; early snow possible by late September/October
bySeason.winter: Heavy maritime snowfall, avalanche terrain, and closures at higher elevations

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; backcountry camping permit costs $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay, obtained through the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or via recreation.gov
access.closures: Goodell Creek Campground itself has been subject to bear-activity closures; check current NPS conditions before approaching

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn departure from Terror Basin camp to cross the glacier and snow gully on firm snow

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek trailhead to Terror Basin (~5,800 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, brushy approach gaining roughly 5,000 ft

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Terror Basin to the 7,200-ft col and both McMillan summits
sectionBreakdown.note: Glacier/snow-gully travel to the col, then ridge scrambling/climbing to each summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff, (360) 336-9450 (after-hours/SAR dispatch); call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245 / noca_wilderness@nps.gov
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA, (360) 856-6021 (closest); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: NPS emergency line (360) 856-5700 ext. 515 during business hours; after-hours Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37. Cell coverage is unreliable to absent in the Pickets backcountry — carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: The well-documented 1940 first ascent (Fred and Helmy Beckey, credited by Mountain Project to 8/29/1940) is specifically recorded for the West Ridge of the higher West McMillan Spire (the main/west summit, 8,004 ft). Available sources do not separately document a first-ascent party for a 'West Ridge/Southwest Face' line up the East summit itself — modern parties typically reach the East summit by continuing along the connecting ridge from the West summit, which AAC's 'Walking the Fence' article records elsewhere as a distinct line, 'East Ridge, II 5.6.' Approach stats (gainFt ~9,800 ft, distKm ~12.9 km one-way) are sourced from WTA's listing for West McMillan Spire via Goodell Creek (16 mi / 9,800 ft roundtrip) and applied here as the closest available proxy, since East McMillan Spire is reached via the same approach plus a short additional ridge traverse — true totals for the East summit specifically are likely somewhat higher. Peak elevation is listed as 7,989 ft in the given data versus 7,992 ft on Wikipedia/Mountain Project — a minor rounding difference between sources, not a substantive conflict.

blurb: Inspiration Peak (7,891 ft) is a steep, knife-edged summit in the Southern Pickets of North Cascades National Park, Washington, standing between Mount Degenhardt and the McMillan Spires above the Terror and McMillan glacier basins. Composed of Skagit Gneiss, it offers three quality technical rock lines (West Ridge, East Ridge, South Face) in one of the range's most remote alpine cirques. Fred Beckey and his brother Helmy made the first ascent via the West Ridge on August 29, 1940, while Fred was still in high school.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, August 29, 1940
routes.rappels: Multiple single-rope (60m) rappels down the west ridge crest back to the toe of the ridge/bergschrund
objHaz: remote location with slow rescue access
routes.overview: The West Ridge climbs the obvious ridgeline of Inspiration Peak directly to the summit via roughly 14 pitches of sustained 5.7 rock, starting about 300 ft above the bergschrund at the toe of the ridge.
routes.beta: From a high camp in Terror Basin, cross the glacier bench below Inspiration and Mount Degenhardt to reach the base of the west ridge. Climb the ridge crest for about 14 pitches (~1,300 ft) of sustained 5.7 face and crack climbing; rock quality is noticeably looser on the lowest pitches and improves higher on the route. Descend by rappelling back down the same ridge to the glacier.
routes.approach: Drive SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Goodell Creek trailhead/campground near Newhalem (~600 ft). Follow the old overgrown road bed along Goodell Creek for about 4.5 miles, then climb a steep, largely unmaintained boot path gaining roughly 3,000+ ft to treeline. Continue up to a high spur/notch near 6,200 ft, then descend slightly to a flat, gravelly camping area at about 5,800 ft in Terror Basin. From camp, cross the glacier bench toward Inspiration Peak, negotiating the bergschrund/moat via a collapsed or bridged section to reach the toe of the west ridge.
routes.descent: Rappel the west ridge crest (single 60m rope) back down to the toe of the ridge and across the glacier to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make a series of single-rope (60m) rappels down the climber's-left side of the west ridge crest, following the line of the ascent back toward the toe of the ridge above the bergschrund. Re-cross the glacier bench carefully, particularly late in the season when the moat/bergschrund has opened up, then descend the boot path back to Terror Basin camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from the Goodell Creek trailhead to a high camp in Terror Basin (roughly 6-8 hours, ~5,000-6,000 ft gain). Day 2: Early start; cross the glacier bench to the base of the west ridge, climb the ~14-pitch route to the summit, then rappel the ridge back to the glacier and return to camp. Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the route: rappel the west ridge crest back to the toe of the ridge and re-cross the glacier bench the way you came. Do not attempt to descend directly off the ridge into McMillan Cirque or the Terror Creek drainage.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, complex, largely unmaintained approach and the multi-rappel descent back across a crevassed glacier bench, set a conservative turnaround time based on remaining daylight — benightment high in this remote cirque with a difficult retreat is a serious hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the standard window, once the glacier bench has consolidated enough for safe travel and the bergschrund/moat at the base of the ridge has opened sufficiently to cross. The route has been climbed in winter only once on record (Colin Haley and Forrest Murphy, February 8, 2003), under full winter conditions with additional snow/ice hazard.
routes.comms: North Cascades NP backcountry areas including the Pickets have little to no reliable cell coverage; the NPS recommends carrying a satellite communicator for emergencies in this zone.
routes.detailedRack: A single set of cams from about 0.1 to 3 inches covers most placements, with doubles in the common 0.3-1 inch range given the sustained nature of the climbing. Bring extra slings/cord and a few pitons for older or marginal placements, plus a 60m rope for both leading and the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Standard traditional rack for sustained alpine 5.7 face and crack climbing; protection is generally adequate on sound rock higher up but sparser and the rock is looser on the initial pitches off the toe of the ridge.
gear: Glacier travel/crevasse-rescue kit (crampons, ice axe, rope, rescue gear)
whatToBring: Glacier travel and crevasse rescue kit
Overnight camping gear for the Terror Basin high camp (~5,800 ft)
Approach shoes plus rock shoes or sturdy alpine boots
Water treatment (limited water sources noted around ~5,200 ft on the approach)
Map/compass or GPS for route-finding on the unmaintained boot path
proTips: Rock quality improves significantly higher on the route; expect the loosest, junkiest climbing low on the ridge near the toe.

Anchors at the top were reportedly replaced in June 2024, but North Cascades NP's wilderness-wide moratorium on new fixed anchors means you can't place new bolts - carry cord/slings and don't pass up usable natural or existing gear.
A single 60m rope is sufficient for the rappel descent down the west ridge.
Most parties budget a full day (roughly 6-8 hours) just to reach the Terror Basin high camp, and climb the route over 2-3 days total.
watchOut: Crossing the crevassed glacier bench to reach the ridge requires roping up and crevasse-rescue readiness, especially as the bergschrund/moat opens later in the season.
The lowest pitches off the toe of the ridge have notably loose blocks - test holds carefully.
This is a remote Southern Pickets objective with a long, complex approach and unreliable cell coverage; self-rescue capability is essential.
knownHazards: Bergschrund/moat crossing at the base of the ridge can be tricky and changes through the season.
Loose rock on the lower pitches of the route.
Crevasse hazard on the glacier bench approach.
Long, remote approach on an unmaintained boot path with genuine route-finding difficulty.
Wildfire smoke has been reported affecting visibility/air quality on approach in late summer.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Goodell Creek trailhead (~milepost 119) is on the west side of the highway and is normally accessible even when the highway's high-elevation section is gated for winter.
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes seasonally at higher elevation due to snow/avalanche danger, typically with initial gates at Ross Dam trailhead (MP134) and Silver Star (MP171); in severe years the west-side gate can drop as low as Newhalem (MP120), just east of Goodell Creek.
road.driveNote: From Sedro-Woolley/Marblemount, drive SR 20 east to the Goodell Creek Campground/trailhead near Newhalem (~MP119).

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park (NWS Seattle/Marblemount zone forecast)
climate.typical: Cool, wet, and fast-changing weather typical of the North Cascades crest; the Southern Pickets cirque holds snow and glacier ice well into summer.

bySeason.spring: Approach and glacier bench still heavily snow-covered; route itself typically still snowed-in and not in condition.
bySeason.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep): most reliable rock and driest approach, though afternoon thunderstorms and wildfire smoke can occur.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and possible early snow by late September/October make the route increasingly committing.
bySeason.winter: Rarely attempted; the only known winter ascent was February 8, 2003, via the West Ridge, under full winter conditions.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry camping permit required for overnight stays, obtained at the NPS Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: Mandatory park-wide moratorium on installing new fixed anchors (bolts/hangers) in wilderness; clean/removable protection is required.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek trailhead (~600 ft) to Terror Basin camp (~5,800 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, largely trail-less boot path with roughly 5,000-6,000 ft of gain; most parties treat this as a full day.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Terror Basin camp to the summit (7,891 ft) and back to camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Involves a glacier-bench crossing to the ridge toe, ~14 pitches (~1,300 ft) of sustained 5.7, and a multi-rappel descent back to the glacier; round-trip time not consistently documented in trip reports.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit); approached via Skagit County (Goodell Creek trailhead near Newhalem)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: North Cascades NP Communications Center (360) 856-5700 ext. 515; after hours (360) 873-4590 ext. 37; or Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA - (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA - (360) 856-6021 (nearest); Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III trauma), Mount Vernon, WA - (360) 424-4111 (higher level of care)
emergency.notes: Remote glaciated cirque with little to no reliable cell coverage; NPS, Skagit County SAR, and Whatcom County SAR coordinate on North Cascades NP backcountry incidents.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,963 ft) conflicts with reputable sources: Wikipedia (7,891 ft / 2,405 m), Mountain Project's route GPS pin (elevation implied ~7,880-7,891 ft), and StephAbegg.com (7,880 ft) converge around 7,880-7,891 ft; corrected highPointFt to 7,891 ft (Wikipedia's precise figure, cross-checked against Mountain Project/Wikipedia coordinates which match to within ~10m). Given route text 'Grade IV, 5.7' overstates the commitment grade: Mountain Project's dedicated route page lists this exact route as Alpine Grade III, 5.7, and other summaries citing Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide give II-III 5.6 or III 5.7 for the West Ridge; corrected commitment to III. No independent source was found for a French-scale alpine adjectival grade (e.g., D/AD), so alpineGrade is left null rather than carrying over the unsourced prior value.

blurb: Spectre Peak (7,880+'–7,953') is a remote summit on the western slope of the Northern Pickets in Washington's Picket Range, North Cascades National Park, composed of biotite gneiss formed by metamorphism of sedimentary and volcanic rock roughly 100 million years ago. It sits among the range's cluster of jagged 7,500'+ summits (Fury, Challenger, Terror, Luna) that were first named on maps in 1931 and largely first climbed in the 1930s–40s; Spectre's own summit was first reached in 1980. Reaching it requires a multi-day, trail-less approach via Hannegan Pass and Easy Ridge, and its two documented technical routes — the 2006 "Haunted Wall" on the west face and the 2022 "Spirited Away" on the south ridge — both climb loose, remote alpine gneiss.

routes.fa: Sam Boyce & Joe Manning, July 2022 (South Ridge, "Spirited Away") — see corrections re: the catalog's original 1980/5.7 entry
routes.rappels: 1 rappel (30m) from summit ledges, then ~600 ft of 3rd/4th-class simul-downclimbing
routes.season: Jun-Aug (early July climbed by FA party, aided by heavy snow year)
routes.overview: Spirited Away climbs the previously unclimbed south ridge of Spectre Peak in the remote Northern Pickets, weaving through a section of delicate, hoodoo-like gneiss towers just below the summit.
routes.beta: Established by Sam Boyce and Joe Manning in July 2022, the route follows the crest and flank of Spectre Peak's south ridge for roughly 2,000 feet: about 12 pitches of 5.4-5.9 climbing (crux a short overhanging 5.9 choss section on pitch 2), followed by roughly 600 feet of 3rd/4th-class simul-climbing (up to 5.6) to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Pass Trailhead (end of Ruth Creek Rd/FR 32), hike the Hannegan Pass Trail and continue cross-country along Easy Ridge toward Easy Peak, then drop to the 'Imperfect Impasse' and climb back up through Challenger Col to reach the western slope of the Northern Pickets below Spectre Peak's south ridge. The first-ascent party's round trip covered roughly 46 miles and 17,500 ft of elevation gain over several days, with bivouacs near the Easy Peak summit and below Phantom Peak's west ridge.
routes.descent: Rappel (30m) from summit ledges, then simul-downclimb 3rd/4th-class terrain (~600 ft) onto steep snow slopes descending to the east.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make a single 30-meter rappel off summit ledges, then simul-downclimb roughly 600 feet of 3rd- and 4th-class terrain. From there, steep snow slopes drop away to the east; the first-ascent party found these in good condition during an early-July descent in a heavy snow year, which eased travel back toward the bivy sites below Phantom Peak.
routes.itinerary: Multi-day trip: approach day(s) from Hannegan Pass Trailhead along Easy Ridge with a bivy near the Easy Peak summit; a second approach day dropping to the Imperfect Impasse and climbing through Challenger Col to a bivy below Phantom Peak's west ridge; a climbing day on the South Ridge to the summit and descent via the east-side snow slopes; then multiple days to hike back out.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, trail-less approach through the Northern Pickets with no cell coverage and no easy escape once established on the upper ridge, parties should set a firm turnaround based on daylight, weather, and party pace, and be prepared to bivy or reverse pitches rather than push into deteriorating conditions on the ridge or hoodoo section.
routes.bestSeason: Early-to-mid summer (roughly June through August) is favored. The first-ascent party climbed in early July and benefited from a heavy snow year, which held snow on the east-side descent slopes and eased travel, though they noted limited water sources along the route and intense heat during the descent.
routes.detailedRack: Single 60m rope; singles of cams from about 0.1-2 (BD C4-equivalent sizing), doubles of 0.3-1, an optional #3, a light set of nuts, and roughly 50 ft of cord/webbing for slinging horns and chockstones.
routes.proNeeds: Rack leans toward small-to-mid cams for thin cracks in the gneiss, plus slings/cordage for horns and chockstones where the rock is too shattered for solid cam placements, especially through the hoodoo-tower section near the top.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for the multi-day approach
Snow-travel gear for early-season descent slopes
proTips: Climbing early season, as the FA party did in July after a heavy snow year, keeps snow on the east-side descent slopes and eases the approach traverse.
The first ascensionists noted that with some route-finding, it's likely possible to work around the isolated 5.9 sections and keep the climbing closer to 5.7-5.8.
watchOut: A short, overhanging 5.9 section of choss blocks on pitch 2 is the technical crux.
The hoodoo-like towers below the summit are soft and chalky and visibly shift ('vibrated and sung') under body weight — test holds carefully.
Overall rock quality is medium-low by Cascades standards, with short sections of extremely poor, exfoliating rock.
knownHazards: Loose, exfoliating gneiss in multiple sections of the route.
Unstable, hoodoo-like summit towers that move underfoot.
Extremely remote, multi-day-approach setting with no nearby rescue and no cell coverage.

pitchDetail.notes: Start of pitched climbing off the south ridge base
Crux pitch — short overhanging section of choss blocks
Remaining pitches of low-to-mid 5th class climbing along the ridge crest, weaving among hoodoo-like gneiss towers near the top
~600 ft of simul-climbing to the true summit

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32 / Ruth Creek Road)
road.status: Open seasonally to Hannegan Campground trailhead in summer
road.seasonalGate: Typically snowed-in through winter and spring; opens as snowpack clears, generally by early-mid summer
road.driveNote: From Hwy 542 about 13 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center, turn onto FR 32; at 1.3 miles bear left onto Ruth Creek Road and continue 5.4 miles to road's end at Hannegan Campground/trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Mt. Baker zone / NWS West Slopes North Cascades (WAZ567)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced winters with heavy snowpack and comparatively drier summers, typical of the North Cascades west-slope climate; snow lingers late into summer in the high Pickets.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; the approach road and trail may remain snow-covered into June.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season (roughly June-September); afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows with increasing storm frequency by October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard; road access to the trailhead is often closed by snow.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No park entrance fee
access.permit: Free backcountry/wilderness camping permit required for overnight trips, obtainable from the North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.closures: Mandatory park-wide moratorium on installing new fixed anchors — traditional, removable protection only

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Hannegan Pass Trailhead to Easy Ridge / bivy near Easy Peak summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Trail to Hannegan Pass, then cross-country along Easy Ridge

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Easy Ridge to bivy below Phantom Peak's west ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Drop to the 'Imperfect Impasse,' climb back up through Challenger Col to the western slope of the Northern Pickets

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: South Ridge base to summit, descend east snow slopes
sectionBreakdown.note: 12 pitches plus ~600 ft simul-climbing; single 30m rappel and downclimb on descent
Multiple days, reversing the approach

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office — 911 for emergencies; non-emergency line 360-778-6911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: Extremely remote, multi-day approach through the Northern Pickets with no cell coverage; self-rescue capability and a filed trip plan are essential.

routes.corrections: The catalog's given route grade ('Grade III, 5.7') and FA year (1980) for a 'South Route' do not match any documented technical route found for WA's Spectre Peak (Picket Range, North Cascades — 7,880+'/7,953', not to be confused with the unrelated 'The Spectre' in Antarctica, whose 1980 Stump-brothers FA appears to be a plausible source of the '1980' figure's origin). Wikipedia/Picket Range sources do confirm the PEAK's overall first ascent occurred in 1980, but name neither the party nor the line climbed. The only two documented technical routes on this peak are: (1) 'Spirited Away' (South Ridge, IV 5.9 per AAC / D+ 5.8 per the first ascensionists' own account, ~2,000 ft, 12 pitches) — FA Sam Boyce & Joe Manning, July 2022; and (2) 'Haunted Wall' (west face, IV 5.9+, 2,100 ft) — FA Wayne Wallace & Mike Layton, Aug 14, 2006. Since the catalog entry is named 'South Route,' this record has been populated with the South Ridge/'Spirited Away' data (the only documented south-aspect line) and the grade/FA corrected accordingly. If the catalog's original '5.7, 1980' entry was instead meant to represent an unrecorded original/easiest ascent line, that route's specific stats could not be sourced anywhere and remain undocumented. Peak elevation also varies by source: 7,880+' (AAC/Wikipedia, rounded) vs. 7,952-7,953' (listsofjohn.com, more precise) — the latter matches the catalog's given 7,952 ft.

blurb: West Twin Needle is one of two rugged Skagit-gneiss spires (the "Twin Needles") in the Southern Pickets of North Cascades National Park, rising about 0.55 mile west of Mount Terror with Himmelhorn immediately beyond it. The Twin Needles were first climbed on August 17, 1932 by William Degenhardt, James Martin, and Herb Strandberg, one of the pioneering ascents of the then-largely-unclimbed Picket Range. The peak's easiest terrain (class 2-3) is a common link in the classic Southern Picket enchainment/traverse, while a direct line from Crescent Creek Basin on the south side is a considerably harder, roped 5th-class outing.

routes.fa: William Degenhardt, James Martin, and Herb Strandberg, August 17, 1932 (first ascent of Twin Needles)
objHaz: loose rock/choss in the summit gullies
water scarcity high on the peak
routes.face: South face / Crescent Creek Basin side
routes.overview: The South Route climbs directly from Crescent Creek Basin up West Twin Needle's south side: a rising traverse beneath the Eye Col gully to the base of the peak, then broken buttresses and chockstone-filled gullies with sections of roped 5th-class rock (to 5.7) leading to the summit.
routes.beta: This line is considerably harder and less-traveled than the class 2-3 east flank / class 2 west slope used when West Twin Needle is simply tagged as a link in the Southern Picket Traverse. Climbing directly from Crescent Creek Basin on the south side puts parties on much more sustained, exposed ground; route-finding beta is scarce and the loose, chockstone-choked gullies require real roped pitching before easier scrambling gains the summit.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Trailhead on SR-20 near Newhalem, follow the Goodell Creek climbers' trail roughly 3.5+ miles up-valley into Terror Creek drainage, then leave the trail to gain and cross "The Barrier" (a class 2-3 rock rib), dropping into Crescent Creek Basin (typical camp near 6,050 ft). From the basin, a rising traverse below the Eye Col gully leads directly beneath West Twin Needle and neighboring Himmelhorn. The full approach-plus-climb is commonly described as a roughly 19.5-mile round trip spanning an elevation range of about 600-7,936 ft.
routes.descent: Rappel/downclimb to the Eye Col notch between East and West Twin Needle and continue down steep south-facing gullies, or reverse the easier class 2-3 east flank/west slope line used on the Southern Picket Traverse.
routes.descentText: Two options are reported: (1) downclimb and rappel to the Eye Col notch between the two Twin Needles, then work down steep south-facing gullies via 4th-class down-scrambling, rappelling, and snow travel back into Crescent Creek Basin; or (2) reverse the easier class 3 east flank / class 2 west slope terrain used when the peak is climbed as part of the Southern Picket Traverse. Both options involve loose rock and route-finding that can be slow, especially in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: A typical trip runs about 3 days: Day 1 approach via the Goodell Creek Trail and over The Barrier to camp in Crescent Creek Basin; Day 2 climb the South Route to the summit and return to camp; Day 3 hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally by reversing the ascent gullies with rappels; parties already committed to the upper terrain can often escape sideways/down toward the Eye Col notch and downclimb back into Crescent Creek Basin rather than pushing to the summit.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that leaves enough daylight margin to reverse the loose, chockstone-filled summit gullies and any rappels, plus recross The Barrier and Crescent Creek Basin terrain, before dark — this is reported as slow, disorienting ground even in good light.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions are mid-summer, roughly July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared the approach gullies and The Barrier crossing; recorded ascents and trip reports on this peak cluster in August.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack for cracks and short 5th-class pitches, plus a full 60m x2 rope setup to cover the rappel descent options; multi-night backpacking/camping gear is needed to stage from Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.proNeeds: Protection for mixed cracks and short roped pitches up to 5.7 in the summit gullies; doubled 60m ropes are recommended so either rappel-descent option (via Eye Col or the east flank) can be covered.
whatToBring: bivy/camping gear for Crescent Creek Basin
sturdy approach footwear for The Barrier crossing
map/GPS for route-finding through the gullies
proTips: The class 2-3 east flank / class 2 west slope line used on the Southern Picket Traverse is far more forgiving than climbing directly up from Crescent Creek Basin on the south side.
Camp in Crescent Creek Basin (roughly 6,050 ft) to stage the climb.
North Cascades National Park wilderness rules bar installing new fixed anchors — plan on clean, traditional protection.
watchOut: Route-finding on the south side is reported as scarce and disorienting.
Chockstone-filled gullies are loose and require careful, deliberate rope work.
Water is scarce high on the peak.
knownHazards: Loose rock and choss in the summit gullies increase rockfall risk to parties below.
Exposed rappels on the Eye Col descent option.
Rodent activity (marmots/pack rats) has been reported around Crescent Creek Basin camps.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Paved highway; the Goodell Creek Trailhead is directly off SR-20 near Newhalem and is generally accessible whenever this western section of the highway is open.
road.seasonalGate: The Newhalem-area section of SR-20 typically stays open much of the year, but the highway's high-elevation stretch further east (Washington/Rainy Pass) closes seasonally for winter avalanche hazard, roughly mid-November into spring; check NPS/WSDOT road-status pages before a trip.
road.driveNote: Goodell Creek Trailhead/Campground sits at about milepost 119 on SR-20, roughly 60 miles east of Burlington, WA.

climate.typical: Wet, glaciated North Cascades alpine climate; snow can linger on the approach gullies and The Barrier crossing well into early summer.

bySeason.summer: The main climbing window, though residual snow and afternoon thunderstorms are possible even in July.
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and increasing storm frequency by late September push most parties out of the range.

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No day-use entrance fee; wilderness permit reservation fees may apply for advance-reserved backcountry camps.
access.permit: A backcountry wilderness permit is required year-round for all overnight stays; obtain in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or via advance reservation on Recreation.gov (about 60% of sites are reservable in advance).
access.closures: Installation of new fixed anchors is prohibited park-wide in wilderness; traditional, removable protection only.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 676-6911; dial 911 for emergencies.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA; park HQ (360) 854-7200.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley/United General Hospital area, Sedro-Woolley/Mount Vernon, WA (Skagit Regional Health), roughly 60 miles west via SR-20.
emergency.notes: This is remote, glaciated, cross-country terrain inside North Cascades National Park; NPS Search and Rescue coordinates with the county sheriff for backcountry incidents. Cell coverage is not reliable in the Picket Range.

routes.corrections: Reputable sources (Wikipedia's Twin Needles article, and secondary listings/trip reports citing Peakbagger and Lists of John data) consistently give West Twin Needle's elevation as 7,936 ft, not the 7,927 ft supplied — highPointFt has been set to 7,936 ft accordingly. The supplied route fa of '1932' matches the documented August 17, 1932 first ascent of Twin Needles by Degenhardt, Martin, and Strandberg; sources do not explicitly confirm that ascent followed today's Crescent Creek Basin 'South Route' line exactly (one trip report references '1932's original route' near the Himmelhorn-West Twin col), so treat the FA-to-route match as reasonably likely but not fully certain.

objHaz: cornices near summit ridge in spring
routes.face: South Slope (also called the South Flank)
routes.overview: A long, mostly off-trail scramble up Mount Howard's broad south-facing slope above Crescent Lake on Nason Ridge, gaining a talus/scree/rock-band slope to the summit ridge rather than following any defined technical line.
routes.beta: From the Rock Mountain or Snowy Creek trailheads on US 2, climbers follow the Nason Ridge/Rock Mountain trail system to Rock Lake or Merritt Lake, then continue to Crescent Lake. From Crescent Lake's NE shore, ascend the open slope due north to the summit. A rock band partway up looks more formidable than it is and can be threaded without difficulty; above it a large bench leads to the final push to the top. Some parties choose class 3-4 variations near the summit rocks for more interest; easier ground can generally be found by traversing around.
routes.approach: Park at the Rock Mountain Trailhead (or Snowy Creek Trailhead) on the north side of US Highway 2 near milepost 73, about 27 miles west of Leavenworth / 8.5 miles east of Stevens Pass. Hike the Rock Mountain Trail #1587 or Merritt Lake Trail #1588 up to the Nason Ridge Trail #1583, then descend/traverse to Rock Lake or Merritt Lake and on to Crescent Lake, which sits below the peak's south flank.
routes.descent: Descend the same south-slope scramble route back to Crescent Lake and retrace the approach trail.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line down the south slope to the bench and rock band, then continue down to Crescent Lake. From there, pick up the Nason Ridge/Rock Mountain/Merritt Lake trail network back to the trailhead on US 2. In early season or after fresh snow, the upper slope can hold firm snow requiring an ice axe and traction for a safe glissade-free descent; a corniced summit ridge should be given a wide berth.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a very long single day (roughly 10-13 hours car-to-car) or split into a two-day trip with a camp near Rock Lake, Merritt Lake, or Crescent Lake to shorten summit day.
routes.bail: The route is a straightforward down-climb of the ascent slope essentially anywhere below the rock band; there is no technical commitment that prevents retreat directly back toward Crescent Lake at any point.
routes.turnaround: Given the length of the approach and the exposure of the open south slope to afternoon weather, set a firm turnaround time before starting and treat any lingering snow, wet rock, or building thunderheads as reasons to turn back rather than push for the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly June through September once the approach trails and Crescent Lake basin have shed most of their snow; a late, heavy snow year can leave the summit slope holding snow well into July. Fall visits are feasible but water sources near Crescent Lake can be less reliable and days are shorter.
whatToBring: map and compass/GPS (route-finding through the rock band and open slopes is nontrivial)
extra water (sources are limited above Crescent Lake)
sun protection for the exposed open slope
proTips: The rock band midway up the south slope looks intimidating from below but has an easy weakness through it once you're on it.
Combining Mount Howard with neighboring Mount Mastiff via the Nason Ridge crest is a popular one-way (car shuttle) option for parties wanting two summits in a day.
watchOut: This is a very long day with significant elevation gain and loss between trailhead, lake basins, and summit — budget generously.
Water is scarce along much of the Nason Ridge trail system outside the lake basins.
Corniced sections can persist on the summit ridge into early summer.
knownHazards: Loose, gneissic rock in the band partway up the south slope can shift underfoot.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common on the exposed open slope in summer.
Residual snow on the upper slope in early season can require an ice axe and traction to descend safely.

road.name: US Highway 2
road.status: Paved, maintained year-round
road.driveNote: Trailhead is on the north side of US 2 near milepost 73, about 27 miles west of Leavenworth and 8.5 miles east of Stevens Pass; parking at the Rock Mountain and Snowy Creek trailheads is very limited.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades climate: drier and sunnier than the west side, with hot summer days and cold, snowy winters; the open south-facing summit slope holds snow into early summer in heavy years.

bySeason.spring: Snow lingers on the upper slope and along shaded trail sections into June; expect a mix of trail and snow travel.
bySeason.summer: Prime season; watch for afternoon thunderstorms building over the exposed ridge.
bySeason.fall: Generally dry and stable but with shorter daylight and less reliable water near Crescent Lake.
bySeason.winter: Deep snow and avalanche terrain make the route a winter mountaineering/ski objective rather than a scramble.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District
access.fees: A Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency (America the Beautiful) pass, or day-use fee is required at the developed Nason Ridge Trailhead; the Rock Mountain Trailhead itself is listed as fee-free by the Forest Service.
access.permit: No climbing permit required.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency pass (trailhead-dependent)

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn given the length of the day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 non-emergency dispatch: (509) 663-9911 (dial 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center (critical access hospital), 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue unit is the primary SAR response resource for this area; cell coverage along the ridge and open summit slope is generally better than in the forested approach valleys but should not be relied upon.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt for the peak (7,068 ft) differs slightly from the figure reported by Wikipedia and Peakbagger (7,063 ft / 2,153 m); the Wikipedia/Peakbagger figure is used for highPointFt here. Also, the given route grade 'Class 2-3' undersells the difficulty per most sources: Wikipedia and trip reports describe class 3-4 scrambling opportunities near the summit, though the bulk of the route (per SummitPost's route description) is class 2-3 talus/slope walking with only optional harder variations.

blurb: Mount Howard is a 7,063-foot peak in the Wenatchee Mountains section of the Cascades, forming the high point of Nason Ridge between Stevens Pass and Lake Wenatchee in Chelan County. It sits on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land underlain by migmatitic gneiss of the Nason Terrane, and rises with 2,903 feet of prominence above the surrounding valleys. With no glaciers and no established technical rock routes, it's climbed as a long, strenuous off-trail scramble rather than a mountaineering objective, with class 3-4 terrain available near the summit for those who seek it.

waypoints.name: Rock Mountain summit / former lookout site

objHaz: Exposed, sun-baked switchbacks with no shade for most of the climb
No reliable water source on route
Loose/eroded tread in spots on the upper ridge
Lingering snow or cornices near the summit ridge in early season
routes.face: Southwest flank of Rock Mountain via Rock Mountain Trail #1587, the direct trail from US-2
routes.overview: A steep, direct out-and-back trail climb up Rock Mountain Trail #1587 on Nason Ridge's southwest flank, switchbacking more than 90 times through forest and open, granite-flecked meadows above Rock Lake to the mountain's old fire-lookout site and summit.
routes.beta: The West Route follows Trail #1587 from US-2 through a short stretch of forest and beneath power lines, leaves an old service road at about 0.5 mile, and begins a long, exposed switchbacking climb up Nason Ridge's flank (roughly 2,300 ft of gain in the first ~2 miles, 90+ switchbacks). Around 2-3 miles the grade eases as the trail contours into meadows near the Nason Ridge Trail junction (~6,000 ft); from there it continues past Rock Lake and a saddle near the Snowy Creek Trail junction, over a false summit, to the old lookout foundation and true summit.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth drive west on US-2 about 27 miles (or a few miles east from Stevens Pass) to a small, easy-to-miss pullout on the north side of the highway near milepost 73, about 0.4 mile east of the WSDOT maintenance yard. A short, rutted spur (roughly 75 ft) leads to trailhead parking for about 4 vehicles; no permit or fee is required.
routes.descent: Descend the same Rock Mountain Trail #1587 switchbacks back to the US-2 trailhead.
routes.descentText: There is no loop option; the standard descent simply reverses the ascent down 90+ switchbacks. Expect the lower and middle slopes to be hot and shadeless in the afternoon, and use care on loose tread.
routes.itinerary: Single long day: trailhead (~2,650 ft) -> steep switchbacks to the Nason Ridge Trail junction (~6,000 ft, ~3 mi) -> past Rock Lake and a saddle junction with the Snowy Creek Trail -> false summit -> true summit and old lookout site (~6,850 ft, ~4 mi) -> return via the same trail. Roughly 7-8 hours round trip.
routes.bail: This is a single trail with no technical shortcuts; the Nason Ridge Trail junction (about 3.2 mi, ~6,000 ft) is the most natural turnaround/bail point before the final push past Rock Lake to the summit.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, shadeless, dry climb with real heat- and weather-exposure risk on the open switchbacks and ridge. Start with enough margin to be off the exposed upper slopes before afternoon heat or building thunderstorms, since the only way down is the same trail you climbed.
routes.bestSeason: Because the West Route climbs south/west-facing slopes, it typically sheds snow earlier than the north-facing Snowy Creek approach; expect a rough window from June/July through October, with peak wildflowers a few weeks after snowmelt and huckleberries into early fall. Snow can still linger near the summit ridge into early summer in heavy years.
gear: Sun protection - hat and sunscreen (mostly unshaded)
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS (junctions with Nason Ridge and Snowy Creek trails near the top)
Extra layers for wind on the open ridge/summit
proTips: Carry all the water you'll need - the trail passes near Schilling Creek and within sight of Rock Lake but doesn't give direct access to either, making this effectively a dry trail (WTA).
Start early to beat the heat; the lower and middle switchbacks face south/west with almost no shade.
The Nason Ridge Trail junction (~3.2 mi, ~6,000 ft) is a good lunch spot or turnaround if you don't want the full summit push.
watchOut: Over 90 switchbacks climb an exposed slope with loose dirt/scree footing in places.
No dependable water on route - pack it all in.
A false summit appears before the true high point and old lookout site.
knownHazards: Trail is dry with no dependable water source along its length.
Long, sun-exposed switchback sections raise heat-exhaustion risk in summer.
Narrow, eroded tread in spots near the ridge; the connecting Nason Ridge Trail is not recommended for stock.
Snow and possible cornices can linger near the summit ridge/saddle into early summer, so early-season parties should check current conditions.

road.name: US Highway 2 (paved) to a roadside pullout near milepost 73
road.status: Paved highway access; trailhead itself reached via a short rutted spur
road.driveNote: Trailhead is an inconspicuous pullout on the north side of US-2 near milepost 73, about 0.4 mile east of the WSDOT maintenance facility; parking for roughly 4 vehicles.

climate.typical: Dry and hot on the exposed lower/mid switchbacks in summer; alpine meadows near the top see the afternoon convective weather typical of the East Cascades crest.

bySeason.spring: Upper mountain typically still snow-covered; the south-facing lower trail can melt out first.
bySeason.summer: Prime season - hot, dry, fully exposed climbing with wildflowers after snowmelt.
bySeason.fall: Cooler temperatures and huckleberries; generally the most comfortable season for the exposed switchbacks. Snow can return by late October.
bySeason.winter: Trail is snow-covered and not maintained; not a standard winter route.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District
access.fees: None - no parking pass or entry fee required at Rock Mountain Trailhead
access.permit: No wilderness permit required; the trailhead and route lie just north of (outside) the Alpine Lakes Wilderness boundary, which runs along US-2/Stevens Pass
access.passRequired: None (no-fee site; a Northwest Forest Pass is not required)

sectionBreakdown.section: Trailhead to Nason Ridge Trail junction
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: US-2 trailhead (~2,650 ft) to junction (~6,000 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Roughly 3 miles and 90+ switchbacks; about 2,300 ft of the gain comes in the first ~2 miles.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Nason Ridge Trail junction to Rock Mountain summit (~6,850 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 1 mile past Rock Lake and a saddle near the Snowy Creek Trail junction, over a false summit to the old lookout site and true summit.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 non-emergency dispatch (509) 663-9911 (dial 911 for emergencies); RiverCom is the sole 911 dispatch point for Chelan County agencies including the Sheriff's Office
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center (24-hour Emergency Department), Leavenworth, WA, roughly 27 miles east via US-2
emergency.notes: Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue is the primary backcountry rescue resource for this area; Cascade Ambulance (509-548-5815) also assists with backcountry rescues.

routes.corrections: Given data lists the peak elevation as 6,856 ft. Reputable sources disagree on the exact summit elevation: Wikipedia's USGS/prominence-based figure is 6,840 ft (prominence 640 ft, parent Mount Howard at 7,063 ft); WTA's GPS-based summit reading is 6,852 ft; another trail-guide GPS track (Must Hike Must Eat) logged 6,808 ft. No single authoritative benchmark elevation was found in this research; 6,840-6,852 ft is the best-supported range, used here for highPointFt. Approach stats also vary slightly by source (round-trip distance 8.0-8.2 mi, gain 3,947-4,177 ft); this entry uses WTA's commonly-cited 8.0 mi / 4,050 ft.

blurb: Rock Mountain is the second-highest point on Nason Ridge in the north-central Cascades of Chelan County, Washington, rising just north of US Highway 2 near Stevens Pass. Built from terrane-accreted volcanic and metamorphic rock uplifted roughly 50 million years ago and later carved by Pleistocene glaciers, its summit still holds the stone foundation of a former Forest Service fire lookout. No first-ascent record survives for the peak; it is reached today by maintained trail rather than technical climbing.

routes.overview: A non-technical off-trail scramble that gains Mastiff's south-facing scree and talus slopes above the Crescent Lake basin, then follows the connecting ridge (Wikipedia identifies the Southeast Ridge as the peak's easiest line) to the summit -- mostly Class 2 walking with occasional Class 3 moves around outcrops and short cliff bands.
routes.beta: Most parties start from the Merritt Lake Trailhead, hike the maintained trail to Merritt Lake, then leave the trail and work cross-country toward the Crescent Lake basin. From the head of the basin, scree/talus slopes lead up to the ridge connecting Mount Howard and Mount Mastiff (an alternate, less-documented line climbs Mastiff's northeast ridge via Lost Lake instead). From the ridge, follow the crest -- staying just below it to avoid small cliffs and dense subalpine trees -- to the summit. The route is entirely unmarked past Merritt Lake, so navigation, not climbing difficulty, is the main challenge. Many parties combine the peak with Mount Howard (1 mile west) as a single long day.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive US-2 west about 24 miles (from Stevens Pass, drive east about 11.5 miles) to the signed Merritt Lake Trailhead turnoff near milepost 76. Follow Forest Road 657 about 1.5 miles to its end at the Merritt Lake Trailhead (3,000-3,050 ft). Hike Merritt Lake Trail #1588 roughly 2.5 miles and about 2,000 ft of gain to Merritt Lake, then leave the maintained trail and traverse cross-country toward the Crescent Lake basin and the base of the south-facing scree slopes below the Howard-Mastiff ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down the ridge and south-facing scree/talus back toward the Crescent Lake basin and Merritt Lake, then out via the maintained Merritt Lake Trail #1588.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ridge crest toward the Howard-Mastiff col, staying below the crest to avoid cliff bands and dense trees, then descend the south-facing scree/talus slopes back toward the Crescent Lake basin. Pick up the cross-country line back to Merritt Lake and follow the maintained trail to the trailhead. Parties continuing on to summit Mount Howard as well typically descend Howard's steeper southwest slopes and gully systems instead, which involve mandatory bushwhacking near Crescent Lake -- parties turning around at Mastiff alone should simply retrace their ascent line.
routes.bail: There are no fixed rappel stations or established bail points; retreat is by reversing the ascent ridge/scree line back toward Merritt Lake. In poor visibility, or on corniced/snow-covered ridge sections (common into early summer), descend off the ridge crest onto the south-facing scree rather than risk the steep, cliffy east side of the peak.
routes.turnaround: Because the route is entirely off-trail with no fixed retreat points and afternoon thunderstorms are common on the east-slope Cascades in summer, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight, weather, and party pace before leaving the trailhead, and stick to it even short of the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in summer through early fall (roughly June-September) once seasonal snow clears from the Merritt Lake approach and the ridge; trip reports document ascents from June into late September. Snow and cornices can linger on north aspects and the ridge crest into early summer. The peak has also been climbed as a winter/spring snow route via a different line from a lower highway pullout, which requires avalanche awareness.
gear: helmet (recommended near talus and cliff bands)
map and compass or GPS (route is unmarked and off-trail)
whatToBring: extra water (reliable sources limited to Merritt/Lost/Crescent Lakes along the route)
sun protection for the exposed ridge and scree
layers for wind and afternoon storms
routes.proNeeds: None -- this is a non-technical talus/scree/ridge scramble with no fixed protection or belaying involved on the standard line.
proTips: Combine with neighboring Mount Howard (about 1 mile west, the high point of Nason Ridge) for a full Nason Ridge day if time and conditions allow.
Stay just below (east of) the ridge crest on the ascent/descent to avoid the worst cliff bands and dense tree thickets.
Watch which drainage you're ascending -- several bowls on the south side resemble Mount Howard's south face; straying into the Royal Creek drainage puts you on the wrong slope.
watchOut: Route-finding: travel past Merritt Lake is unmarked, cross-country, and involves open meadows, brush, and talus.
A roughly 2,000-foot cliffy east face and corniced ridge sections exist nearby -- avoid straying east of the ridge crest, especially with snow present.
Blueberry brush and alder can obscure the faint boot path near the Crescent Lake basin, especially later in the season.
knownHazards: Loose talus and scree on the south-facing slopes below the ridge.
Off-trail, boot-path-only navigation through meadows, brush, and talus with no signage.
Cornices and lingering snow on the ridge crest into early summer.
Steep, cliffy east face of the peak -- straying off-route to the east exposes climbers to significant fall hazard.
routes.face: Southeast Ridge / south-facing scree slopes below the Howard-Mastiff col (per Wikipedia, the peak's easiest route)

road.name: Forest Road 657 (Merritt Lake Rd)
road.status: Rough, native-surface forest road; high-clearance recommended, AWD/4WD advised when wet
road.driveNote: Turn off US-2 near milepost 76 (about 24 miles west of Leavenworth, or about 11.5 miles east of Stevens Pass) and follow FR 657 about 1.5 miles to its end at the Merritt Lake Trailhead. Expect rocks, potholes, and overgrown brush along the road edge.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Stevens Pass / East Slopes Central Cascades zone
climate.typical: East of the Cascade crest, noticeably drier than the west-side Cascades, with warm summers and cold, snowy winters.

bySeason.spring: Lingering snow on north aspects and the ridge crest into June; possible cornices near the summit ridge.
bySeason.summer: Warm and mostly dry with occasional afternoon thunderstorms; the main climbing season, documented June through September.
bySeason.fall: Cool, generally stable weather into September; snow returns by October.
bySeason.winter: Cold with significant snowfall on upper/west-facing slopes; the peak has been climbed as a winter snow scramble via an alternate lower-elevation approach, not the standard summer line described here.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District
access.fees: No fee at the Merritt Lake Trailhead per the official USFS recreation site page (self-registration only)
access.permit: Free self-registration at the trailhead
access.passRequired: None required at Merritt Lake Trailhead per USFS (note: WTA's trip listing shows a Northwest Forest Pass/park pass as required -- treat the USFS site page as authoritative)

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Merritt Lake TH (~3,050 ft) to Merritt Lake (~5,000 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 2.5 miles on maintained Merritt Lake Trail #1588 with roughly 2,000 ft of gain

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Merritt Lake to Mount Mastiff summit via Crescent Lake basin and the south scree/ridge
sectionBreakdown.note: Off-trail cross-country travel; documented car-to-summit times in trip reports vary widely (roughly 5 hours to a full day) depending on route variant and conditions

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom regional dispatch (509) 663-9911; Chelan County Sheriff's Office coordinates search and rescue via its SAR unit and Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue (CCVSAR)
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA, (509) 548-5815 (24-hour Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Route is off-trail past Merritt Lake with no established rescue infrastructure; report to the Chelan County Sheriff's Office (via 911/RiverCom) for backcountry emergencies in this area.

routes.corrections: Elevation: peak data lists 6,748 ft; most authoritative sources (Wikipedia/USGS-derived, Peakbagger) list 6,747 ft, and one narrative trailguide rounds to 6,741 ft -- treated 6,747 ft as the summit elevation here (negligible discrepancy, same peak). Access: WTA's trip listing implies a Northwest Forest Pass/park pass is required, but the official USFS Merritt Lake Trailhead recreation page (updated 2025) states no fee/pass is required, self-registration only -- flagged as a source conflict, defaulted to the USFS page. Route naming: no source uses the exact name "South Route"; multiple documented lines exist to Mastiff's summit from the Merritt Lake Trailhead (a south-side line via the Crescent Lake basin and the Howard-Mastiff ridge/Southeast Ridge -- used here as the closest match to "South Route" -- and a separate northeast-ridge line via Lost Lake), and approach/gain figures vary meaningfully by which line and whether Mount Howard is included in the same trip; figures given here (10 mi RT / 3,330 ft gain) correspond to the Mastiff-only option from the Merritt Lake Trailhead.

blurb: Mount Mastiff (6,747 ft) is the third-highest point on Nason Ridge in Chelan County, Washington, rising midway between Stevens Pass and Lake Wenatchee within the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, about one mile east of higher Mount Howard (7,063 ft). The peak is built of migmatitic gneiss from the Late Cretaceous Nason Terrane and was named by USFS topographer Albert H. Sylvester because its profile, seen from Lake Wenatchee, resembles the head of a mastiff. It sees no technical climbing; it's an off-trail talus/scree scramble most often paired with neighboring Mount Howard via the Merritt Lake approach.

routes.fa: Harry B. Hinman, Walter Eriksen, Louis Lesh — July 18, 1915 (fifth attempt; this is the peak's first ascent and established the standard Class 3-4 line matching today's Southeast Route)
routes.face: Southeast flank — south-facing boulder field leading to the 'hidden ramp' below the summit block
routes.overview: The Southeast Route is Gunn Peak's standard line: a steep, mostly unmaintained climbers' trail from Barclay Creek gains the peak's south-facing boulder fields, threads an exposed 'hidden ramp' (not visible from below) through Class 3-4 terrain, and finishes across a narrow, exposed ledge below the true summit.
routes.beta: From the Barclay Lake Trailhead area, a boot-path/climbers' trail switchbacks steeply through forest before breaking into open boulder/talus slopes on Gunn Peak's south side. Around 3,800 ft the route heads climber's-right off the main rockslide rather than straight up a rock face, onto the crux 'hidden ramp' — an exposed but moderate Class 3-4 traverse/scramble that isn't visible from below. Above the ramp, parties cross old rockslides and a col/notch, then work along a narrow, heavily exposed rock/heather ledge on the north side of the summit block before a short final Class 3 scramble to the top. The route sees relatively few ascents each year and has been the site of numerous search-and-rescue calls; a helmet is strongly advised.
routes.approach: Drive US Highway 2 to about milepost 41-42 in Baring and turn north onto Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road, also signed 635th Place NE at the highway). Follow FR-6024 roughly 3.9-4.3 miles toward the Barclay Lake Trailhead; near the trailhead an old spur road and boot path branch off toward Gunn Peak (rather than continuing to Barclay Lake) and lead through second-growth timber to the base of the peak's south-side boulder fields.
routes.descent: Descend the same Southeast Route: reverse the narrow summit ledge, down-climb the hidden ramp, and follow the boulder field and climbers' trail back to Barclay Creek Road.
routes.descentText: Most parties down-climb the ascent line rather than rappel. From the summit, carefully reverse the exposed heather/rock ledge below the true summit (extra caution if wet, snow-covered, or icy), down-climb the Class 3-4 'hidden ramp,' and pick back up the boot path through the boulder field and forest to the trailhead. A short rope can be used to protect the ledge or ramp for less-experienced parties; some trip reports note an optional rappel or hand-line above the ramp.
routes.itinerary: Most parties do it car-to-car in a single long day, budgeting 7-9 hours round trip (fit/fast parties have done it in around 6 hours). Some climbers split it into a 2-day trip via an alternate approach up Barclay Creek Road to Tailgunner Pass, camping near the Gunn Point bench (~5,450 ft) before a shorter summit push the next morning.
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the ascent line — there is no shorter bail option once on the upper boulder fields or the hidden ramp; down-climb back the way you came. Avoid getting cliffed out by leaving the main rockslide too early or late for the ramp turnoff, and if off-route in fog it is generally safest to retrace known ground rather than force a new line down unfamiliar terrain.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux narrow summit ledge is far more hazardous when wet, snow-covered, or in poor visibility, set a firm turnaround time before starting and be willing to retreat if rain, low cloud, or afternoon thunderstorms move in before the ledge is reached.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September) once the upper boulder fields and the narrow summit ledge have melted out. Parties attempting it in June have been turned back by lingering heavy snowpack, and the exposed ledge is especially dangerous when snow-covered or icy.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is required for the standard scramble. Some parties carry a short rope (roughly 30-40m) with a few slings/anchors to protect or rappel the exposed summit ledge or ramp section for less-confident members, though most climbers do the route unroped.
gear: Optional short rope (30-40m) for the ledge/ramp
Trekking poles for the steep, loose approach
Map/compass or GPS for route-finding above treeline
whatToBring: Plenty of water (long, steep, mostly shadeless climb)
Extra layers for wind and exposure near the summit
Headlamp for early starts or late finishes
Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent day-use pass for trailhead parking
proTips: Many parties climb nearby Mount Baring first as a gauge of readiness for Gunn Peak's harder scrambling and exposure.
Around 3,800 ft, look for where the boulder field opens up and the route breaks right (climber's right) rather than continuing straight up the rock face.
The 'hidden ramp' is not visible from below — resist the urge to keep climbing the rockslide and instead traverse onto the ramp when you reach it.
A short length of rope can protect the exposed heather/rock ledge below the summit for parties uncomfortable with the drop-off.
watchOut: The narrow summit ledge on the north side has essentially no safety net for a fall and should be avoided if wet, snow-covered, or icy.
The climbers' trail becomes faint above treeline; cairns mark the route through the boulder fields, and losing them in cloud/fog is a common way parties get off-route.
Early-season snowpack can linger into June and has stymied summit attempts; wait for it to melt out for safer passage of the ledge and ramp.
knownHazards: A narrow, highly exposed rock/heather ledge on the north side near the summit is the route's crux hazard, particularly dangerous when wet, snow-covered, or icy.
The 'hidden ramp,' an exposed Class 3-4 section not visible from below, requires careful route-finding through boulder and talus terrain.
Numerous search-and-rescue incidents have occurred on Gunn Peak; a helmet is strongly recommended for the scramble sections due to loose rock.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep, mostly unmaintained forest trail gaining roughly 1,500 ft before breaking into open terrain.
Talus/boulder slope on the south side; at ~3,800 ft the route heads climber's-right off the main rockslide.
Crux traverse/scramble not visible from below; can involve a steep gully with a chockstone (near-5th class) as an alternate line.
Narrow, exposed heather/rock ledge on the north side of the summit block, followed by a short scramble to the true summit.

road.name: Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road)
road.status: Gravel forest road through second-growth timber and clearcuts; can have blowdown and rough sections, especially after winter
road.seasonalGate: Not gated year-round, but unplowed and can be temporarily closed/impassable in winter and early spring until the Forest Service clears blowdown
road.driveNote: From US Highway 2 at Baring (near milepost 41-42), turn north onto FR-6024 (signed 635th Place NE at the highway) and follow it roughly 3.9-4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead area, where the Gunn Peak climbers' trail branches off.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Stevens Pass zone
climate.typical: Wet, mild winters with heavy snowpack and warmer, drier summers typical of the west-slope Cascades along the Skykomish/Stevens Pass corridor

bySeason.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack on upper slopes and the summit ledge into June; approach trail can be very wet and muddy
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul-Sep) once snow has melted from the boulder fields and ledge, though still subject to afternoon thunderstorms
bySeason.fall: Cooling temperatures and increasing rain, with possible early snow at elevation by October
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard, and an unplowed access road make the peak a winter mountaineering objective only for experienced parties

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30 annual day-use fee at the Barclay Lake Trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass and Interagency recreation passes accepted
access.permit: No permit required for day climbing; the trailhead accesses Wild Sky Wilderness, where self-issue wilderness registration may apply
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent interagency day-use pass
access.closures: Access road (FR-6024) is unplowed and can be closed or impassable in winter and early spring pending Forest Service blowdown clearing

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early morning, given the length, elevation gain, and exposure of the route

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Barclay Creek Road TH -> ~3,800 ft boulder field
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, mostly forested climbers' trail

sectionBreakdown.section: Boulder field to hidden ramp and notch
sectionBreakdown.note: Class 2-4 talus and the exposed 'hidden ramp'
Narrow, exposed ledge on the north side, then a short Class 3 scramble to the top

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office / Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR), (425) 388-3328
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe, Monroe, WA (nearest full-service hospital along US-2 west of the trailhead)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Barclay Creek drainage and on the upper mountain; the route has a history of search-and-rescue incidents, particularly at the narrow summit ledge.

routes.corrections: Reported summit elevation varies by source/datum: WTA lists 6,240 ft, ListsOfJohn 6,241 ft, Wikipedia/USGS (NAVD88) 6,244 ft; the input value of 6,238 ft falls within this typical NGVD29-vs-NAVD88 spread and was not overridden at the peak level. Reported round-trip stats for the 'standard' day-scramble also vary noticeably by exact start point/variant: WashingtonStateHiking reports 7.1 mi / ~4,000 ft gain from the gravel-road end, AlpineWanderlust reports 5.5 mi / 4,300 ft gain, and WTA (used here) reports 7.5 mi RT / 4,900 ft gain / 6,240 ft high point; a separate overnight approach via Tailgunner Pass (TrailCatJim) covers 6 mi / 4,700 ft gain/loss to a camp short of the summit. distKm (one-way, 6.0 km) is derived by halving WTA's round-trip mileage, not an independently surveyed one-way figure.

blurb: Gunn Peak is a rugged summit in the Cascade Range near Index, Washington, and the highest point in the Wild Sky Wilderness of the Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. It is built of erosion-resistant migmatitic gneiss within the North Cascades' eastern mélange belt and rises roughly 4,100 ft above South Fork Trout Creek in about a mile, giving it dramatic local relief. Named for Amos Gunn, an early homesteader/miner who founded nearby Index, it was first climbed July 18, 1915, by Harry B. Hinman, Walter Eriksen, and Louis Lesh on their fifth attempt, establishing what is now the standard Class 3-4 route.

routes.overview: A long, mostly-Class 1-2 trail hike up Round Mountain Trail #1529 through forest and an old burn, then a several-mile ridge walk on Nason Ridge Trail #1583 out to the staffed Alpine Lookout at the ridge's eastern end.
routes.beta: From the Round Mountain Trailhead, climb steadily through forest and a decades-old burn for about 1.4-1.6 miles to the junction with Nason Ridge Trail #1583. Turn left (north/east) and follow the ridge crest through meadows and past a notably narrow, exposed knife-edge saddle around the 9-mile mark (measured trailhead-to-trailhead style on some guides) before a final short climb to the lookout cabin at the summit. Return the same way.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive US-2 west about 17.7 miles (or from the Stevens Pass side, about 18 miles east of the pass) to an unsigned turn onto Butcher Creek Road/Forest Road 6910, just past the Nason Creek Rest Area. Follow FR 6910 about 4.4-4.5 miles, then turn onto spur road FR 6910-170 for roughly 0.1-0.5 mile to the Round Mountain Trailhead (small lot, privy).
routes.descent: Descend the same trail: Nason Ridge Trail back to the Round Mountain Trail junction, then down Round Mountain Trail #1529 to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: There is no loop option from this trailhead — descend by reversing the ascent route down Nason Ridge Trail #1583 to the Round Mountain Trail #1529 junction (~1.4-1.6 mi from the lookout), then continue down Round Mountain Trail to the trailhead. The steepest, most sustained downhill is the lower Round Mountain Trail section through the old burn.
routes.bail: The Round Mountain/Nason Ridge Trail junction (about 1.4-1.6 miles from the trailhead) is the last sheltered, low-exposure point before the hike commits to several miles of open ridge walking; retreat to it, and then to the trailhead, from anywhere along the ridge if weather or conditions turn.
routes.turnaround: Nason Ridge and the knife-edge saddle near the lookout offer no shelter from lightning for a long stretch of ridge walking — begin the return with enough margin to be off the exposed crest before afternoon thunderstorms typically build, and turn back if weather is deteriorating rather than pushing for the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Typically hikeable June through October; the trail sheds snow relatively early for a Cascades ridge hike, making it a good early-summer option, with wildflowers peaking in July-August and fall color later in the season. Snow, fog, and even early-season winter weather are possible in fall.
whatToBring: 3+ liters of water (trail is dry beyond a small spring near the trailhead)
layers for wind and sudden weather on the exposed ridge crest
Northwest Forest Pass, Digital Day Pass, or Interagency pass for trailhead parking
bright/blaze clothing if hiking during fall hunting season
proTips: Start early to be off the exposed ridge crest and knife-edge saddle before afternoon thunderstorms build
Fill up at the small spring near the trailhead -- it's effectively the only reliable water on the route
A high-clearance or AWD vehicle is recommended for the rutted final stretch of Forest Road 6910/6910-170
Give mountain goats a wide berth and never let them lick sweat, urine, or gear -- they are habituated and can be aggressive
watchOut: The saddle roughly 3+ miles along Nason Ridge narrows to about a 10-ft-wide knife edge with steep drop-offs to Highway 2 on one side and the Little Wenatchee River drainage on the other
Nason Ridge is highly exposed with no lightning shelter for over a mile of ridge walking
Water is scarce beyond one seasonal spring close to the trailhead
The unsigned access road (FR 6910, then FR 6910-170) has deep ruts and is easy to miss from Highway 2
knownHazards: Exposed, shelterless ridge crest for the final several miles increases lightning risk in summer thunderstorms
A narrow knife-edge saddle near the lookout has steep drop-offs on both sides of the trail
Habituated mountain goats frequent the summit area seeking salt from hikers
The trail is dry for most of its length, with only one seasonal spring near the trailhead as a water source

road.name: Forest Road 6910 (Butcher Creek Road) to spur FR 6910-170
road.status: gravel, unsigned turn off US-2, with deep ruts in the final stretch
road.driveNote: Turn is easy to miss on Highway 2 near the Nason Creek Rest Area; follow FR 6910 about 4.4-4.5 miles, then FR 6910-170 a short distance to a small trailhead lot with a privy. A couple of deep ruts/grooves on the final approach make high-clearance/AWD advisable.

climate.typical: Dry, warm summers with afternoon thunderstorm potential on the exposed ridge; fall brings variable weather including wind, rain, and possible early snow

bySeason.spring: Trail sheds snow relatively early for a Cascades ridge route, but upper sections and the saddle can hold snow into early summer
bySeason.summer: Warm and mostly dry with wildflower bloom in July-August; afternoon thunderstorms are the main hazard on the exposed ridge
bySeason.fall: Highly variable -- clear and cool days alternate with wind, rain, sleet, or early snow; hunting season is active in the area
bySeason.winter: Not a standard winter hiking route on this trailhead approach; snow-covered and used for backcountry ski/snowshoe travel by experienced parties in the broader Nason Ridge traverse

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District
