access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass, Digital Day Pass, or Interagency (America the Beautiful) pass accepted
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent interagency pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning so the exposed ridge and knife-edge saddle are behind you before typical summer afternoon thunderstorms build

sectionBreakdown.section: Round Mountain Trail to Nason Ridge Trail junction
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead (~3,950 ft) to ridge junction (~1.4-1.6 mi)
sectionBreakdown.note: Steepest, most sustained climbing of the hike, through forest and an old burn area

sectionBreakdown.section: Nason Ridge Trail to Alpine Lookout summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Ridge walk through meadows past the exposed knife-edge saddle to the lookout cabin

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 (Chelan/Douglas counties dispatch); Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 6,245 ft does not match the consensus of independent sources (Peakbagger, USFS-linked data, and multiple trip-report/guide sites), which cluster at 6,234-6,237 ft; used 6,235 ft as the sourced high point. Route elevation gain is reported inconsistently across sources (2,264-3,061 ft depending on start point/method); 2,600 ft was used as the figure supported by the largest number of independent sources. 'Round Mountain' is a separate, lower high point passed along the approach ridge, not the same summit as Alpine Lookout itself, despite the combined peak name.

blurb: Alpine Lookout is a 6,235-ft summit at the eastern end of Nason Ridge in the Wenatchee Mountains (Cascade Range), in the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest near Lake Wenatchee, Washington. A fire-lookout camp was first established on the summit in 1920; the original 1936 L-4 cab was replaced by the present R-6 flat-cab structure in 1975, and it remains one of only a handful of Cascade lookouts still staffed by the Forest Service each summer. The peak is reached by trail rather than technical climbing, via forested switchbacks and an old burn area up Round Mountain (a separate, lower high point on the same approach ridge) before a long, exposed ridge walk to the lookout.

waypoints.name: Barclay Lake Trailhead / Barclay Creek Rd (FR-6024) parking

objHaz: cliff bands / steep drop-offs above Gunn Lake
slide alder and devil's club brush on lower slopes
creek crossing on Barclay Creek and a waterfall-gully crossing
loose dirt/rock on an exposed hand-line section near the summit ridge
route-finding through unmarked, partly off-trail terrain
lingering or early-return snow on upper slabs (documented into late October and as a full snow/couloir climb in early March)
routes.face: Southeast slope, reached via Tailgunner Pass and Gunn Basin
routes.overview: A mostly off-trail Class 3 scramble to the second-highest point in the Wild Sky Wilderness: a boot path climbs from Barclay Creek through Tailgunner Ravine to Tailgunner Pass, then traverses talus, boulders, and heather below Gunn Peak's southwest buttress to Gunnshy's east ridge and rocky summit.
routes.beta: There is no official trail. From a spur off Barclay Creek Road (FR-6024) near an old fire pit, a faint climbers' path heads east into forest, crosses Barclay Creek on logs, and climbs a steep zigzag on the south slope past ~3,600 ft, dropping briefly into a waterfall gully before continuing through meadows along Tailgunner's south ridge to a ~5,400 ft saddle (Tailgunner Pass). The route then drops east around Peak 5,760, descends to ~5,200 ft to avoid Gunn Peak's southwest buttress, and rises in a talus/heather traverse above Gunn Lake to the 5,950 ft east ridge, finishing west to the summit. A helmet is recommended; one exposed spot near the top has been rigged with a hand line by guided scramble parties. Round trip involves roughly 5,000+ ft of cumulative elevation change.
routes.approach: From Monroe, take US-2 east toward Baring. Near milepost 41-42 (across from the old Der Baring Store), turn north onto 635th Place NE/Barclay Creek Road. Cross the railroad tracks, continue about 0.3-0.4 mile, then follow Forest Road 6024 roughly 4 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead (vault toilet, parking for ~20 cars). The Gunnshy climbers' path leaves from a spur/old roadbed near the trailhead rather than from the signed Barclay Lake trail itself.
routes.descent: Descend the ascent route in reverse (east ridge, talus/heather traverse above Gunn Lake, Tailgunner Pass, Tailgunner Ravine, Barclay Creek).
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the east ridge west-to-east reversal back down to the 5,950 ft ridge, then descend the talus/boulder/heather traverse below Gunn Peak's southwest buttress back toward Gunn Lake, climb back up to the ~5,400 ft Tailgunner Pass saddle, and drop through Tailgunner Ravine/meadows back to the steep zigzag trail above Barclay Creek. Use care on the exposed, dirt-covered section near the top (hand line recommended) and re-cross Barclay Creek on the same logs used on the approach.
routes.turnaround: Given the significant elevation gain, largely off-trail navigation, brushy terrain, and an exposed scrambling section near the summit, set a turnaround time based on remaining daylight, weather, and party pace rather than pushing for the top; the exposed hand-line section and the loose, dirt-covered ledges become notably more hazardous when wet.
routes.bestSeason: Late June/July through September offers the most reliable snow-free travel on the open upper slopes and ridge. Trip reports document successful ascents into late October, though upper slabs can already carry snow patches by then; a full snow/couloir ascent of the same general line has also been documented in early March, requiring ice axe, crampons, and avalanche awareness on Tailgunner Couloir.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed protection rack is used on the standard Class 3 route — it is a scramble, not a roped climb. A helmet is recommended for the exposed sections and loose rock/dirt near the top; guided parties have rigged a short hand line (about one rope length) on one exposed ledge.
gear: short hand line/rope for the exposed ledge (optional but used by guided parties)
ice axe and traction (early/late season snow)
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Recreation Pass, or day-use fee for Barclay Lake Trailhead ($5/vehicle day-use or $30 annual)
Green Trails Monte Cristo No. 143 map
gaiters or brush-resistant clothing for slide alder and devil's club
proTips: The climbers' path begins from a spur/old roadbed near a fire pit beyond the Barclay Lake trailhead parking, not from the signed Barclay Lake trail — watch for the faint path heading east into the forest.
Carry the Green Trails Monte Cristo No. 143 and USGS Baring quad; route-finding through brush, ledges, and cairns is the crux of this trip more than the scrambling itself.
The exposed section near the summit has 'ok footing but a lot of dirt' — a short hand line adds security even though the move itself is only Class 3-4.
watchOut: Cliff bands and steep drop-offs on the talus/heather traverse above Gunn Lake are marked by cairns — losing them can lead onto unnecessarily exposed terrain.
Slide alder and devil's club thickets on the lower slopes slow travel and can hide uneven footing.
Snow can linger into early summer and return to the upper slabs and ridge by mid-to-late October, adding a fall hazard to an otherwise dry Class 3 scramble.
knownHazards: No official trail exists for roughly half the route; the boot path fades into open talus, heather, and brush requiring active route-finding.
A Barclay Creek log crossing and a waterfall-gully crossing can be slick and are more hazardous with high water in early season.
Loose rock and dirt are present on the exposed hand-line section near the summit ridge.

road.name: Forest Service Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road)
road.status: Gravel, generally passable to passenger vehicles; watch for potholes
road.driveNote: From Baring on US-2 (milepost ~41-42, across from the former Der Baring Store), turn north onto Barclay Creek Road/FR-6024, cross the railroad tracks, and continue about 4 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades / Stevens Pass region (no peak-specific NWAC zone)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters above roughly 4,000 ft with drier, mild summers typical of the west-slope Cascades near Stevens Pass

bySeason.spring: Snow lingers on upper slopes and the pass into June; creek crossings can run high with snowmelt
bySeason.summer: Best conditions July-September; lower brushy sections open and upper slopes typically snow-free
bySeason.fall: Generally dry into early October; snow can return to upper slabs and the ridge by late October
bySeason.winter: Full winter mountaineering/avalanche conditions; a snow-and-couloir line via the same general approach has been climbed with axe and crampons in early March

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (climb lies within Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use fee or $30 annual pass at Barclay Lake Trailhead
access.permit: No wilderness permit required for day use; none of the sources found indicate a quota or self-issue permit
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Recreation Pass, Digital Pass, or day-use fee payment
access.closures: No maintained trail to the peak itself (Wilderness, no official route); Barclay Creek Trail/road access is closed to motorized vehicles, bicycles, and stock and is subject to normal seasonal snow at elevation

timing.recommendedStart: Early (pre-dawn/first light) start recommended given the long round trip, off-trail navigation, and brush

sectionBreakdown.section: Barclay Creek Rd trailhead to Tailgunner Pass
sectionBreakdown.note: Via Barclay Creek crossing and Tailgunner Ravine/Couloir; steep, brushy, off-trail
Traverse below Gunn Peak's SW buttress above Gunn Lake on talus/heather to the east ridge, then west to the summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (911 for emergencies); Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR) supports backcountry rescues, main line 425-388-3328
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (open Wed-Fri, 9:00 am-12:00 pm and 1:00-4:00 pm)
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe (formerly Valley General Hospital), 14701 179th Ave SE, Monroe, WA, near the US-2/SR-522 junction
emergency.notes: Cell coverage in the Barclay Creek/Gunn Basin drainage is not confirmed reliable by any source found; carrying a satellite messenger is prudent for this off-trail, low-traffic route.

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 6,211 ft appears to be a minor error: the most consistently cited figure across WTA, SummitPost, Mountaineers, and multiple trip reports is 6,218 ft ("Peak 6218"); ListsOfJohn lists a LIDAR-derived 6,221 ft for the same summit (there as "Jumpoff Ridge"). WTA's official stat block gives 4,295 ft of elevation gain for the standard route; a separate detailed trip report of a snow/couloir variant (same general approach, climbed in March) recorded about 5,450 ft of cumulative gain+loss and roughly 7 miles round trip — distKm above (5.6 km one-way) is derived from that single report and should be treated as approximate, not a confirmed stat for the standard summer line.

blurb: Gunnshy Peak — also called "Peak 6218" and, in Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, "Jumpoff Ridge" — is the second-highest summit in the Wild Sky Wilderness of the central Washington Cascades near Baring/Index, standing just 22 ft below neighboring Gunn Peak roughly 0.7 mile to the east. It and its neighbors are granitic/gneissic outcrops of the Index batholith and the Skykomish Mountains' eastern mélange belt. The peak has no maintained trail or documented technical first ascent; it is reached today by an unmaintained boot path and off-trail Class 3 scramble via Barclay Creek, Tailgunner Ravine/Pass, and Gunn Basin.

routes.fa: John Charlton & Albert H. Sylvester, July 28, 1897 (first recorded ascent of the peak, via the south-side line this route follows)
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly June through September once the summit gully sheds its winter snowpack; many parties still carry an ice axe into early summer for persistent snow/ice in the notch just below the summit.
knownHazards: Seasonal snow/ice lingers in the notch/gully just below the summit, sometimes into summer
Lower boot path is brushy and indistinct through forest, easy to lose
Loose rock on the final class 3-4 scramble section
watchOut: Persistent snow in the summit notch/gully — carry an ice axe for spring/early-summer ascents
The unmaintained climber's path is easy to lose in the trees before the ridge
Loose rock near the top
proTips: Save maps/waypoints offline before starting — cell/data connections are unreliable in this drainage
The climbing route leaves near the Barclay Lake parking lot on an old abandoned logging road, not the main Barclay Lake trail
An ice axe is commonly recommended for spring and early-summer ascents
routes.overview: The standard route climbs Baring Mountain's south side from the Barclay Lake trailhead, following an unmaintained climber's path up steep forest to the ridge, then traversing into a basin below the peak before a class 3-4 scramble through a snow-prone notch to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Barclay Lake trailhead parking area, an old abandoned logging road/climber's path (not the maintained Barclay Lake trail) climbs steeply, crossing a creek several times, to reach a ridge around 4,000 ft after about a mile. From there the route traverses below on the south side, crosses another ridge into a basin, and climbs toward the saddle/notch between Baring's north and south summits before a final class 3-4 scramble to the true summit.
routes.approach: Drive US Highway 2 to the small town of Baring (roughly milepost 41-42); turn north across the railroad tracks onto Barclay Creek Road (Forest Road 6024) and continue about 4.3-4.5 miles on gravel to the Barclay Lake trailhead, bearing left at a fork about 0.2 miles from the highway. From the trailhead, leave the main lake trail and find the older, unmaintained climber's path (an old logging road) that goes straight up toward the ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route.
routes.descentText: Descend by reversing the south-side scramble line back through the basin and down the ridge to the climber's path; downclimb the summit notch/gully with care, as it can hold snow or ice well into the season.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a long single day from the Barclay Lake trailhead; some parties split the effort by camping near Barclay Lake.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the boot path for most of the route; the crux notch below the summit and any snow in the gully are the only sections that call for careful downclimbing.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before committing to the summit notch/gully if it is snow- or ice-covered without an axe, or if weather is closing in — the upper ridge and basin offer little shelter, and the brushy, indistinct lower path is easy to lose in fog or after dark.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the Barclay Lake/Baring Mountain drainage; download maps and directions before leaving cell coverage.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is typically placed; the route is an unroped scramble for most parties, though a rope may be prudent for less experienced climbers at the class 3-4 notch.
whatToBring: Map/compass or offline GPS — cell service is unreliable
Northwest Forest Pass or interagency pass for trailhead parking
Extra water — most of the route is forested with limited water sources

road.name: Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road)
road.status: Open, gravel
road.driveNote: From US Highway 2 in Baring, turn north across the railroad tracks onto FR 6024 and continue about 4.3-4.5 miles on gravel to the Barclay Lake trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: Cascades of Snohomish and Northern King Counties (NWS)
climate.typical: West-slope Cascades climate: wet, mild winters with heavy snowpack and drier, warmer summers; the most reliable dry-route window is July through September.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack typically lingers into June, including in the summit notch/gully.
bySeason.summer: Driest, most stable window (Jun/Jul-Sep); shaded snow patches can persist into August.
bySeason.fall: Feasible into September/early October before rain and early-season snow return.
bySeason.winter: Deep snowpack and frequent storms make this a non-standard, serious winter undertaking.

emergency.county: Snohomish County (summit area is near the Snohomish/King county line)
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF — (360) 677-2414, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288 (Wed-Fri, 9-12 & 1-4)
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett — Snohomish County's Level II trauma center and primary regional trauma referral hospital
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Barclay Lake/Baring Mountain area; download maps/directions before leaving cell range.

routes.corrections: Elevation gain to the summit is cited variably across sources: ~3,500 ft (WTA), ~3,700 ft (Willhite Web), ~4,000 ft (The Mountaineers) — likely reflecting different start points on the approach; no single figure could be confirmed as definitive, so gainFt is left null rather than guessed. Wikipedia and The Mountaineers label the standard scramble the 'Northwest Ridge' route, while Mountain Project calls the same south-side line the 'South Scramble' — both appear to describe the same route as the given 'South Route,' approaching from the south to the saddle between the north and south summits.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper & Don Gordon (Claunch), with Fred Beckey on the final push, July 9-13, 1960 (after a decade of prior attempts by Pete Schoening, Richard Berge, and others)
routes.bestSeason: Historically climbed in July; the central couloir separating the two rock ribs often holds snow as late as August, and the face is prone to extreme heat given its exposure — a narrow dry-rock window is typical.
objHaz: Extreme heat on the sun-exposed wall
Severe water scarcity high on the route
Unusually hard rock that resists piton placement, requiring bolts in places
Dense brush (alder, vine maple, devil's club) on the lower approach
knownHazards: Loose rock and difficulty placing protection reported by first-ascent parties
A fatality occurred during a 1952 retreat attempt (Richard Berge)
Dense brush and steep, slide-alder-choked terrain in the lower ~2,000 feet
watchOut: Rock is unusually hard and resisted piton placement historically — expect slow, technical protection
Severe water scarcity high on the face
The central couloir commonly holds snow into August
Extreme heat exposure on the sun-facing wall
proTips: The lower-approach brush-fighting is described by historic parties as more exhausting than the technical climbing itself
This route has claimed a life during a retreat (Richard Berge, 1952) — plan retreat options seriously before committing
A large couloir separating the two ribs often still holds snow in mid-summer
routes.overview: A serious, historically aid-dependent big-wall route up Baring's massive north face, climbed over four vertical rock steps separated by a brushy, snow-holding couloir — one of the most sustained and committing alpine walls in the Cascades.
routes.beta: The route gains the first 1,000 ft up a steep, forested and brushy hillside to 'Couloir Camp.' The next 1,300 ft climbs two precipitous parallel rock ribs separated by a large couloir that often holds snow as late as August; the left rib is followed until it merges with another rib further left, from which a rightward ledge traverse reaches the base of the first rock step. From there, four vertical steps of roughly 300, 400, 220, and 250 feet, separated by spacious ledges, lead to the summit, requiring sustained grade V-VI climbing with delicate, marginal aid on the first ascent.
routes.approach: Approach from the Barclay Lake trailhead (via Forest Road 6024) up steep, forested and brushy terrain toward the base of the north face and 'Couloir Camp'; expect significant brush-fighting through alder, vine maple, and devil's club on the lower ~2,000 feet before reaching the technical climbing.
routes.itinerary: The first ascent took five days (July 9-13, 1960); it is a multi-day, bivy-dependent undertaking with Couloir Camp as a key staging point.
routes.turnaround: Given the severe water scarcity, sustained difficulty, and brush-choked lower approach, parties should have a firm turnaround plan and enough daylight/supplies to retreat from any point on the face — a 1952 attempt ended in a fatality during retreat.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the Barclay Lake/Baring Mountain drainage.
routes.proNeeds: The first ascent required extensive aid climbing with pitons on unusually hard rock that resisted placements, plus at least one bolt at a high point reached on an earlier attempt; a specific modern free/aid rack has not been confirmed by a contemporary source.
gear: Aid gear (historically pitons; a bolt kit was used on early attempts)
whatToBring: Extra water — the route is noted for severe water scarcity high on the face
Bivy/overnight gear (the first ascent took 5 days)
Brush-fighting layers/gaiters for the lower approach

pitchDetail.notes: ~1,000 ft up a steep, forested and brushy hillside to Couloir Camp
~1,300 ft up two parallel rock ribs separated by a large couloir that often holds snow into August
~300 ft vertical rock step
~400 ft vertical rock step
~220 ft vertical rock step
~250 ft vertical rock step to the summit

climate.forecastZone: Cascades of Snohomish and Northern King Counties (NWS)
climate.typical: West-slope Cascades climate: wet, mild winters with heavy snowpack and drier, warmer summers; the face's central couloir is documented as holding snow as late as August in some years.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snow and lingering ice in the couloir make this a non-standard season for the route.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season, though the couloir may still hold snow into August and the face is exposed to extreme heat.
bySeason.fall: Narrow window before rain/early snow returns; not well documented in sources.
bySeason.winter: Not a standard season; deep snowpack and storm exposure.

access.fees: Day-use parking fee at the Barclay Lake trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful / interagency pass

emergency.county: Snohomish County (summit area is near the Snohomish/King county line)
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF — (360) 677-2414, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288 (Wed-Fri, 9-12 & 1-4)
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett — Snohomish County's Level II trauma center and primary regional trauma referral hospital
