routes.corrections: Given data listed alpineGrade 'IV-V' and rockGrade '5.9+ (variations harder)'. The primary first-ascent account (AAC Publications) describes the original 1960 line as requiring 'constant grade V and VI climbing' with delicate, marginal aid — a more serious/committing rating than IV-V, 5.9+. No source was found confirming a distinct, more moderate 5.9+ named 'North Face' line on Baring; the given grade may conflate the classic route with an unconfirmed variation, so alpineGrade has been corrected to 'V-VI' and rockGrade left null pending a source-confirmed YDS number. The AAC account also cites the peak's elevation as 6,200 ft, an older figure superseded by the current official 6,127 ft (Wikipedia/USGS). A separate, modern bolted route on the same wall ('Vanishing Point,' 5.12b, ~14-20 pitches, FA Burdo/Stanton 1999) exists but is a distinct line from the given North Face route and was not used to fill this route's fields.

blurb: Baring Mountain (6,127 ft) rises steeply above the Skykomish River valley near US Highway 2, within the Wild Sky Wilderness of the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest's Cascade Range. Its north face — roughly 3,500 feet of migmatitic gneiss towering over Barclay Lake — is one of the largest, most sustained alpine walls in the Cascades. The peak's south side was first climbed in 1897 by John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester; the imposing north face resisted a decade of attempts before Ed Cooper, Don Gordon (Claunch), and Fred Beckey completed its first ascent in July 1960.

routes.rappels: One rappel (or a careful downclimb) at the crux, chockstone-blocked waterfall in the gully; fixed rappel rings have been reported there.
objHaz: lingering snow/ice in the gully (early season)
routes.overview: A steep, direct Class 3 scramble up a loose granite gully system on Merchant Peak's south side, gaining roughly 3,800 vertical feet over about 3 miles from the Barclay Lake Trailhead, with a short technical crux at a chockstone-blocked waterfall partway up.
routes.beta: From the Barclay Lake Trail, climbers leave the maintained trail at a rocky, Y-shaped creek bed just past the Barclay Creek bridge and follow the drainage upward, bypassing three waterfall steps (left, then right, then a technical squeeze at the third) before breaking out into upper heather meadows and a final rock scramble to the summit block. The rock throughout is exceptionally loose, sandy granodiorite, and route-finding through the upper meadows requires care once the faint climbers' trail fades near the top.
routes.approach: Drive US 2 to Baring (~MP 41-42); turn north onto Barclay Creek Road (FR-6024), across from the store, and follow it roughly 3.9-4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead (day-use fee site). Hike the Barclay Creek Trail (#1055) about 0.3-0.5 mile — a short, mostly flat stretch — then watch for a rocky, Y-shaped creek-bed drainage a few hundred feet past the Barclay Creek bridge (roughly 47.789, -121.436); leave the trail here and follow the wash uphill into the climbing route.
routes.descent: Descend the same south gully, downclimbing the loose rock sections and rappelling (or very carefully downclimbing) the crux waterfall/chockstone, where fixed rappel rings have been reported.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ascent line back down through the upper meadows to the top of the gully system. Downclimb the loose rock steps, using the fixed rappel rings at the crux third-waterfall/chockstone obstacle if downclimbing looks too insecure. Continue down the drainage past the lower waterfalls to where the route rejoins the Barclay Creek Trail, then hike the remaining short, flat stretch back to the trailhead. Descent is reported to take noticeably less time than the climb (roughly 2.5 hrs vs. 3.5-4 hrs) but still demands continuous caution on loose rock.
routes.itinerary: Single long day from the Barclay Lake Trailhead: early-morning start, roughly 3.5-4 hours up the gully to the summit, roughly 2.5 hours back down, for a 6-8 hour car-to-car day.
routes.bail: Below treeline, the only realistic retreat is back down the ascent gully — there is no safer alternate descent once committed above the lower waterfalls. Turn back before entering the crux waterfall section if the party is uncomfortable with loose, essentially unprotectable rock.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on the slowest member's pace through the gully, since the loose rock becomes more dangerous to downclimb in fading light or wet conditions, and there is no quick bail option once above the lower waterfalls.
routes.bestSeason: Peak scrambling conditions are roughly July through September, once the gully has melted out; the route holds snow into early summer in many years, and parties climbing before mid-June should expect firm snow or ice in the gully and should carry an ice axe. An outlier January ascent with snow cover has been documented, but that is a winter mountaineering undertaking, not the standard Class 3 scramble.
routes.detailedRack: No traditional protection rack is needed for the standard scramble; some parties carry a short rope (around 30m) plus a few slings or rap rings as backup for the crux waterfall/chockstone section, since fixed rap anchors have been reported there.
routes.proNeeds: The route is essentially unprotected scrambling for its entire length; the crux chockstone squeeze has small, unreliable holds on loose, sandy rock where trustworthy gear placements are difficult to find.
gear: Sturdy approach shoes or light hiking boots with aggressive tread
30m rope plus a few slings/rap rings (optional, for the crux waterfall)
Ice axe (needed for early-season snow in the gully)
whatToBring: Detailed map/GPS track — the creek-bed turnoff is easy to miss
Extra water — none reliable once you leave Barclay Creek
Gloves for handling loose, sharp rock
Sun protection for the open upper meadows and summit block
Headlamp for margin on a long day
proTips: Look for the rocky, Y-shaped creek bed a few hundred feet past the Barclay Creek bridge (roughly 47.789, -121.436) — the correct drainage has noticeably more loose rock than the surrounding forest, and picking the wrong gully leads to a frustrating bushwhack.
Start early: fewer parties above you means less rockfall risk in the loose gullies, and it buys time margin for route-finding through the upper meadows.
Carry rap rings/webbing as backup at the third-waterfall crux even if you plan to downclimb it — several parties have used fixed rings there.
watchOut: The gully rock is exceptionally loose and sandy; rockfall from other parties, or your own footing, is a serious hazard throughout the climb.
The crux chockstone/chimney section has small, insecure holds on loose rock over sandy mud and is hard to protect — a fall here would be serious.
The faint climbers' trail through the upper meadows disappears in places, and poor visibility can make it easy to get off-route on the upper mountain.
knownHazards: Loose, sandy granodiorite throughout the approach gully and crux waterfall sections makes rockfall a near-constant hazard.
Lingering snow/ice in the gully in spring and early summer adds fall hazard on already-loose terrain.
Exposed scrambling near the summit block carries consequential fall potential.

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend the creek-bed wash from the trail turnoff; bypass the first waterfall via a use trail on the left.
Work up and right onto the cliffs, passing over the top of the falls.
Large jammed boulders block the gully except for a loose, ~15-20 ft vertical chimney/chockstone squeeze on small holds and sandy rock; fixed rap rings have been reported for the descent.
Faint climbers' trail through heather meadows trending climbers-left, finishing with roughly 300 ft of firmer rock scrambling to the summit.

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (FR-6024)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service road; generally drivable but rough enough that some sources recommend high-clearance/4WD
road.driveNote: From US 2 at roughly milepost 41-42 in Baring, turn north onto FR-6024 (across from the store) and follow it about 3.9-4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead.

climate.typical: The Skykomish River valley/Stevens Pass corridor has a wet, snowy maritime Cascades climate; the route's gully typically holds snow later into the season than the sun-exposed upper meadows and summit.

bySeason.spring: Snow and meltwater fill the gully; footing on already-loose rock becomes more hazardous, and this is not recommended as a standard Class 3 scramble.
bySeason.summer: Prime season once the gully melts out, typically by July; generally warm and mostly dry, with possible afternoon thunderstorms.
bySeason.fall: Can remain climbable into September with stable weather but shortening daylight; early snow is possible by October.
bySeason.winter: Full winter mountaineering conditions; ascents have been documented but are rare and require snow/ice travel gear and avalanche awareness.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use fee (or $30 annual) at the Barclay Lake Trailhead
access.permit: No permit required for day use in the Wild Sky Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass accepted in lieu of the day-use fee

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (predawn/first light) to reduce rockfall exposure from other parties and leave margin for careful downclimbing

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Barclay Lake Trailhead -> creek-bed gully turnoff
sectionBreakdown.note: About 0.3-0.5 mi of flat trail on the Barclay Creek Trail before leaving it for the gully
Three waterfall/chockstone obstacles, then meadows and summit-block scrambling
Downclimb/rappel the same line back to the trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: (425) 407-3999 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288, (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe Emergency Department, 14701 179th Ave SE, Monroe, WA, (360) 794-7497
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Barclay Creek drainage. Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR) supports SAR operations in the Wild Sky Wilderness.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation is consistently cited as 6,113 ft (USGS GNIS / Peakbagger / Wikipedia), not 6,108 ft as given; highPointFt has been set to 6,113 ft accordingly.

blurb: Merchant Peak (6,113 ft per USGS/Peakbagger data) rises above Barclay Lake in the Skykomish Mountains of the central Washington Cascades, within the Wild Sky Wilderness of Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. Like its neighbors Gunn Peak and Baring Mountain, it is built of the granodiorite of the Index batholith, which produces the notoriously loose, sandy rock that characterizes its scramble routes. The peak's name was officially adopted in 1918 for miner Andrew Merchant, and in 1965 it was the site of a small-plane crash that killed Seattle city councilman Wing Luke and two others, whose wreckage was not located for three years.

objHaz: avalanche terrain on open slopes above ~5,250 ft
dense brush and downed timber in the lower forest (snow-free conditions)
active BNSF railroad tracks crossed near the highway
routes.overview: A remote, mostly off-trail winter/spring scramble on a minor Chiwaukum Mountains summit near Stevens Pass, climbing from US Highway 2 through steep timber to a shoulder around 3,500 ft, then the northeast ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: No maintained trail exists. Parties leave Highway 2 near the WSDOT maintenance yard / East Portal tunnel pullouts east of Stevens Pass, cross Nason Creek and the BNSF railroad tracks, then climb steep forest to gain a ridge around 4,600-4,800 ft that is followed to the summit. The route is almost always done with snow on the ground; bare-ground conditions expose brushy, log-choked terrain that is considerably harder to travel.
routes.approach: From a Highway 2 roadside pullout near the WSDOT maintenance yard or the BNSF East Portal tunnel (about 7 miles east of Stevens Pass), cross the highway, cross the Nason Creek bridge, and follow an old powerline/logging-road corridor (near Forest Road 687) paralleling Nason Creek and the railroad tracks for roughly 0.75 mile before turning to climb steeply south through dense forest.
routes.descent: Descend the same ascent line back down the northeast ridge and forest slope to Highway 2.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent ridge and timbered slope, watching for minor cliff bands near 3,400 ft. The open terrain from the summit down to about 5,250 ft (roughly 30-34 degrees) is sometimes skied or glissaded by winter parties; below that, re-enter the forest, cross the BNSF tracks and Nason Creek, and return to the Highway 2 pullout.
routes.itinerary: Single-day car-to-car scramble; no camping reported.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same ascent ridge/forest line toward Highway 2; there is no shorter technical bail-out, and off-route retreat is discouraged given steep, brushy, cliff-banded terrain.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that accounts for short winter daylight and worsening avalanche conditions on the open upper slopes; turn back if snow stability deteriorates or if route-finding in the dense lower forest becomes uncertain.
routes.bestSeason: Most parties climb this as a winter-into-spring (roughly December-May) snowshoe or ski scramble, when snow cover fills in the brushy, log-choked lower forest and firms up travel; a snow-free ascent is possible but reported as steep, brushy, and harder to travel.
gear: avalanche gear (beacon, probe, shovel) in winter conditions
whatToBring: Green Trails Wenatchee Lake No. 145 map
proTips: Roadside pullouts near the WSDOT maintenance yard or the BNSF East Portal tunnel on Highway 2 serve as the de facto trailhead; there is no official parking area, so watch for no-parking signage.
Snow-covered conditions are strongly preferred: multiple trip reports describe the lower forest as steep, brushy, and choked with downed logs when bare.
watchOut: Minor cliff bands around 3,400 ft require careful route-finding to avoid.
The approach crosses active BNSF railroad tracks close to the highway.
Open slopes above roughly 5,250 ft reach 30-34 degrees and can present avalanche hazard after new snow.
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain on open slopes above about 5,250 ft.
Tree wells and cornices on the upper ridge in winter.
Dense brush and downed timber in the lower forest, especially without snow cover.
Active BNSF railroad tracks crossed near the trailhead.

road.name: US Highway 2
road.status: Paved and open year-round, subject to winter storm/avalanche-control delays near Stevens Pass.
road.driveNote: Park in a highway shoulder pullout near the WSDOT maintenance yard or the BNSF East Portal tunnel, roughly 7 miles east of Stevens Pass; there is no official trailhead or parking area.

climate.forecastZone: Stevens Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Wet, heavy-snowfall maritime winters typical of the Cascade crest near Stevens Pass.

bySeason.spring: Consolidating snowpack into May keeps snowshoe/ski access viable; wet loose-snow avalanches possible during warm-ups.
bySeason.summer: Snow-free by mid-summer; the lower forest reverts to brushy, log-choked terrain that is harder to travel.
bySeason.fall: Similar to summer until early-season snow returns, typically by October or November.
bySeason.winter: Deep snowpack and frequent storms; this is the preferred season for the scramble, with avalanche hazard on the open slopes above about 5,250 ft.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District)
access.fees: No fee at the roadside pullouts typically used for this route.
access.permit: None required for day use.
access.passRequired: Not required at the roadside pullouts; a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass is required only if parking at a developed nearby trailhead (e.g., the Stevens Pass Nordic area).

timing.recommendedStart: An early/pre-dawn start is recommended given multi-hour car-to-car times and short winter daylight.

sectionBreakdown.section: Highway pullout to powerline/logging-road crossing of the BNSF tracks
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: ~2,950 ft to a 2,650 ft local high point
sectionBreakdown.note: Follows an old powerline road roughly 0.75 mile before crossing the railroad.
Steep, dense forest travel; minor cliff bands near 3,400 ft.
Curves southeast to gain the ridge crest.
Follows the ridge crest, with open glades above ~5,250 ft, to the summit.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff (509) 667-6851; RiverCom 911 regional dispatch non-emergency (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24-hour Emergency Department, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Route parallels active BNSF railroad tracks near US Highway 2; cell coverage in the Stevens Pass corridor is inconsistent.

routes.corrections: Given alpineGrade 'F' could not be independently verified against a reputable source and is returned as null rather than repeated unsourced. Reported round-trip elevation gain varies across trip reports (3,000-3,554 ft) depending on the exact trailhead pullout and route variant used; 3,300 ft (musthikemusteat, corroborated by an independent onehikeaweek elevation-range calculation) was used as the representative figure. One source (wenatcheeoutdoors) lists an 'Arrowhead peak' elevation of 5,909 ft, likely referring to a sub-point along the ridge rather than the true 6,030 ft summit confirmed by Wikipedia/USGS-derived data and consistent with the given peak elevation.

blurb: Arrowhead Mountain (6,030 ft) is a minor summit in the Chiwaukum Mountains, a subrange of the central Washington Cascades, rising above US Highway 2 about 6 miles east of Stevens Pass on the boundary of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness within the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. The peak is a Late Cretaceous tonalitic pluton and was named by pioneering Forest Service surveyor Albert H. Sylvester for an arrowhead-shaped clearing visible on its south slopes. It has no maintained trail and is most commonly climbed as a steep, off-trail winter or spring snowshoe/ski scramble via its northeast ridge.

routes.overview: A mostly-trail approach via the Smithbrook Trail and Pacific Crest Trail to Lake Valhalla, followed by an unmaintained boot path and open slopes/ridge on the mountain's southeast side to the summit ridge — a classic non-technical Class 2 scramble above Lake Valhalla and Lichtenwasser Lake.
routes.beta: Standard summer/fall line to the summit: trail to Lake Valhalla, then off-trail travel up southeast-facing slopes to a saddle on the southeast ridge and along the ridge to the top. No rope or technical rock gear is needed in dry conditions; the same slopes are also skinned/skied or snowshoed as a separate winter tour when snow-covered.
routes.approach: From the Smith Brook Trailhead (about 4,000 ft, on Smithbrook Road/FR 6700) follow Smithbrook Trail #1590 roughly 0.9 mile to the Pacific Crest Trail. Head north (left) on the PCT about 1.7 miles to a ridge overlooking Lake Valhalla, then continue about 0.3 mile farther. Leave the maintained trail and follow an unmaintained boot path along Lake Valhalla's west shore for about 0.5 mile, then climb open slopes/a gully on the mountain's southeast side to the ridge above Lichtenwasser Lake.
routes.descent: Descend the same slopes/ridge back to Lake Valhalla and return via the PCT and Smithbrook Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ascent line down the southeast-side slopes/ridge to the shoulder above Lichtenwasser Lake, drop to Lake Valhalla's west shore on the unmaintained boot path, and rejoin the PCT. Follow the PCT south to the Smithbrook Trail junction and descend Smithbrook Trail #1590 to the trailhead. No rappels or technical descent gear are needed in dry conditions.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a single long day: trailhead to Lake Valhalla via Smithbrook Trail/PCT (roughly 2.9 miles one-way of trail), then off-trail slopes and ridge to the summit, and back out the same way.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward and non-technical: reverse the ridge/slope line back down to Lake Valhalla and out the PCT/Smithbrook Trail. There are no fixed rappel anchors on this route.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting and stick to it — the unmaintained boot path above Lake Valhalla and the open summit ridge are considerably harder to navigate in poor visibility or after dark, and route-finding fog has caused parties trouble on this peak in the past.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a dry Class 2 scramble roughly July through October, after seasonal snow has cleared the upper slopes and the Lichtenwasser Lake basin. The same terrain is climbed as a separate backcountry ski/snowshoe objective in winter, which requires avalanche gear, training, and current conditions assessment rather than scrambling gear.
routes.face: Southeast slopes/ridge (opposite the northeast ridge and west face lines also described for this peak)
knownHazards: The boot path beyond Lake Valhalla is unmaintained and can be hard to follow, especially in fog.
Upper slopes and the final gully/ridge have sections of loose rock and steep terrain.
In winter/spring the same slopes carry real avalanche hazard: a D1.5 soft-slab avalanche caught and partially buried two skiers on Lichtenberg's north aspect near 5,780 ft in March 2023.
Access via Smithbrook/Rainy Creek Road (FR 6700) has been subject to storm-damage closures affecting vehicle approach to the trailhead.
watchOut: Losing the unmaintained path along Lake Valhalla's west shore in fog or low visibility.
Loose rock underfoot on the steeper sections above the lake.
Wind-loaded snow and cornicing near the summit if attempted with any snow present.
proTips: Parties report the southwest-facing bowl/ridge line is generally more reliable than the northeast ridge, which some groups have found does not lead cleanly to the true (west) summit.
In winter, north-facing aspects tend to hold better snow quality than the crustier south/southwest-facing terrain lower down.
routes.detailedRack: None required — this is a non-technical Class 2 walk-up/scramble in dry conditions; no rope or rack is carried.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed or removable protection needed; it's a hands-occasionally-on-rock Class 2 scramble, not a roped climb.
gear: Map/compass or GPS (unmaintained path past Lake Valhalla)
whatToBring: Layers for the exposed summit ridge
Extra time/margin for route-finding if fog moves in

road.name: Smithbrook Road / Rainy Creek Road (Forest Road 6700)
road.status: Reported closed to vehicles due to storm damage per a Washington Trails Association alert dated 2026-06-01 — verify current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District or WTA before driving up.
road.driveNote: From US-2 near Stevens Pass, turn onto Smithbrook Road/FR 6700 and follow it about 3 miles (paved to gravel) to the Smith Brook Trailhead at road's end.

climate.forecastZone: Stevens Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Marine west-slope Cascades climate: Pacific frontal systems are forced upward over the Cascade crest, producing heavy orographic precipitation and, in winter, wet, heavy snowfall.

bySeason.spring: Snowpack commonly lingers into June-July on the upper slopes and around Lichtenwasser Lake; an ice axe can still be useful.
bySeason.summer: Driest and most reliable scrambling window (roughly Jul-Sep), though afternoon thunderstorms are possible on the open summit ridge.
bySeason.fall: Good scrambling conditions typically continue into September/October before the first significant snowfall.
bySeason.winter: Deep, wind-loaded snowpack; a documented D1.5 slab avalanche caught two skiers on the north aspect near 5,780 ft in March 2023 (NWAC).

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District; the route lies within the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness.
access.fees: The official Forest Service Smithbrook Trailhead page states no fee is required there; some third-party trip-planning sites (AllTrails, Wenatchee Outdoors) instead state a Northwest Forest Pass is required for parking — confirm current trailhead signage before you go.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness boundary.
access.passRequired: None per the official USFS trailhead listing; some other sources list a Northwest Forest Pass (see fees note above).
access.closures: Smithbrook/Rainy Creek Road (FR 6700) was reported closed to vehicles due to storm damage as of a 2026-06-01 WTA alert — check current road status before driving.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom 911 dispatch at (509) 663-9911 (dispatches Chelan County Sheriff/SAR)
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24/7 emergency department, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Cascade Medical's ambulance service covers the Stevens Pass-to-Blewett Pass corridor, which includes this trailhead.

routes.corrections: Reported distance/elevation-gain figures vary meaningfully by approach variant across sources: the Smithbrook Road/Lichtenwasser-Lake line to the southeast-ridge saddle is cited around 7 mi round trip / 2,100 ft gain (used here for this 'Southeast Slopes' route), while the PCT/Lake Valhalla approach is cited anywhere from ~5 mi/1,000 ft (rounded trailhead-sign style figure) up to a GPS-corrected ~9 mi round trip / ~2,000-2,100 ft by a trip-report commenter — the higher, GPS-based gain figure is consistent with the 2,100 ft figure used here. The peak's summit coordinate on WTA's overview page (47.7989, -121.0651) diverges somewhat from the GNIS/Wikipedia-derived coordinate (47.7878, -121.0908) used for the summit waypoint here. Fee/pass requirement is also inconsistently reported (see access.fees).

blurb: Lichtenberg Mountain (5,844 ft) rises 2.8 miles north of Stevens Pass in the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, within the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness, standing steeply above Lake Valhalla's east shore and more gently over Lichtenwasser Lake to the north. Like much of the central Cascades crest, its rock was assembled by volcanic and intrusive activity roughly 50 million years ago (late Eocene) and later carved by repeated Pleistocene alpine glaciation. It is a popular non-technical scramble via the Smithbrook Trail/PCT and Lake Valhalla in summer and fall, and an equally popular backcountry ski and snowshoe objective in winter.

objHaz: lingering snow and cornices into early summer
Barclay Creek crossing (footlog removed, ford or logjam crossing required)
routes.season: Jul-Sep (snow-free scramble); snow/cornices can linger on the upper mountain into June
routes.face: West Ridge / south slope of Tailgunner's summit dome
routes.overview: An off-trail Class 3 scramble that gains Tailgunner's summit dome from Tailgunner Pass via the peak's west ridge/south slope, most commonly climbed as a bonus summit alongside Gunn Peak or Gunn Point.
routes.beta: From Barclay Lake Trailhead, follow Barclay Creek Trail then leave it to climb the Tailgunner Creek drainage/couloir to Tailgunner Pass (~5,320 ft), a broad saddle between Gunn Peak's ridge and Tailgunner. From the pass, scramble up Tailgunner's west ridge and south-facing scree/rock slopes to the summit dome; a narrow summit ledge is the crux and can be exposed or icy if snow lingers. In late-spring snow conditions parties have instead cramponed up a north-slope snow route and surmounted a corniced west-ridge headwall — a more technical, roped variation not represented by the Class 3 rating.
routes.approach: Drive US-2 to Baring (MP 41-42), turn onto Barclay Creek Road (FS-6024) and follow it roughly 4 miles to Barclay Lake Trailhead (2,300 ft). Hike the maintained Barclay Creek Trail a short distance, then leave the trail, cross Barclay Creek (the footlog has been removed; expect a ford or logjam crossing), and follow the Tailgunner Creek drainage and an old overgrown logging road/couloir up to Tailgunner Pass.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route down the west ridge/south slope to Tailgunner Pass, then descend the Tailgunner Creek drainage/couloir back to Barclay Creek Trail and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the west ridge/south slope scramble terrain back to Tailgunner Pass. From the pass, descend the broad Tailgunner Couloir/Tailgunner Creek drainage on grassy slopes and scree (firm snow patches possible early season), rejoin the old logging-road tread, cross Barclay Creek again near the logjam, and follow Barclay Creek Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this route: reverse to Tailgunner Pass and descend the couloir/drainage back toward Barclay Creek at any point below the summit ledge; there is no fixed protection or rappel infrastructure on the standard line.
routes.turnaround: Because the route is off-trail with route-finding, a narrow exposed summit ledge, and a creek crossing without a footlog, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight for the descent and re-crossing of Barclay Creek, and turn back if the summit ledge is snow- or ice-covered and unprotected.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a dry Class 3 scramble from mid/late summer into September once snow has cleared from the upper mountain and the summit ledge; a lingering snow-covered cornice near the summit has been reported into early summer and adds real technical difficulty (crampons/rope) outside the dry-scramble window.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection on the standard dry scramble; parties climbing earlier in the season when snow/cornices linger near the summit have carried a rope, a picket, and slings for running belays on the exposed headwall.
whatToBring: map/GPS track (route is unmarked and off-trail)
ice axe/crampons if climbing before the upper mountain is snow-free
proTips: Most parties tag Tailgunner as a bonus/consolation summit on the way to or from Gunn Peak via Tailgunner Pass
Visit the small 'infinity tarn' on Tailgunner's summit shoulder, a popular photo spot overlooking Baring Mountain
Download or carry a GPS track since the route is entirely off-trail with no cairns or signage
watchOut: The footlog over Barclay Creek has been removed, so the creek crossing may require fording or using a logjam upstream of the old crossing
The narrow summit ledge can be dangerous if snow- or ice-covered
Cornices and firm snow can persist on the upper mountain well into early summer
knownHazards: Off-trail bushwhacking and route-finding through brush and old logging roads on the approach
Loose rock and scree on the summit scramble
A relic cornice/snow headwall has been encountered on the west ridge in late-spring conditions, requiring crampons and rope

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (Forest Service Road 6024)
road.status: Open, unpaved forest road; watch for potholes
road.driveNote: From US-2 in Baring (near milepost 41-42, across from a convenience store), turn onto Barclay Creek Road/FS-6024 and follow it about 4 miles, crossing railroad tracks, to Barclay Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: Stevens Pass / West Slopes Central Cascades (NWAC)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy winter snowfall and wet, changeable weather typical of the western Cascades near Stevens Pass

bySeason.spring: Significant snowpack persists on the upper mountain; snow climbing/crampons often needed near the summit
bySeason.summer: Primary dry-scramble season once snow clears, typically July onward
bySeason.fall: Generally dry and stable into September, shortening daylight and earlier snow possible late in the season
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; not a standard season for this route

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.fees: $5 per vehicle per day, or $30 annual pass, at Barclay Lake Trailhead
access.permit: No wilderness entry permit found required; a Recreation Pass (Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Pass, or day-use fee) is required at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass / day fee (Washington Discover Pass NOT accepted)
access.closures: Footlog over Barclay Creek has been removed for safety with no confirmed replacement timeline; trail closed to motorized vehicles, bicycles, and stock

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start recommended given the long off-trail approach and afternoon weather buildup common in the Cascades

sectionBreakdown.section: Barclay Lake Trailhead to Tailgunner Pass
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Trailhead (2,300 ft) to Tailgunner Pass (~5,320 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Off-trail; includes creek crossing, bushwhacking, and couloir climbing; ~3,200 ft gain per one trip report

sectionBreakdown.section: Descent, Tailgunner Pass/summit area to trailhead
sectionBreakdown.note: Per a single reported round trip; times vary with conditions and route-finding

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County 911 non-emergency: 425-407-3999 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, (360) 677-2414 (Wed-Fri, 9am-12pm and 1-4pm)
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe, Monroe, WA
emergency.notes: Remote off-trail terrain with no cell coverage confirmed; carry a means of emergency communication (e.g., satellite messenger) given the off-trail nature of the route.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 5,839 ft conflicts with multiple independent sources (Listsofjohn, Peakery, AllTrails, Bivouac, trip reports), which consistently report Tailgunner's summit at 5,842 ft; highPointFt set to 5,842 ft accordingly.

blurb: Tailgunner is a 5,842-foot summit in the Skykomish Mountains of Snohomish County, Washington, within the Wild Sky Wilderness of the Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. It sits on Jumpoff Ridge just west of much taller Gunn Peak (6,244 ft) and is composed of the same rugged Cascade Range rock typical of the area's glacially-carved ridgelines; it has no recorded technical first ascent and is most often tagged as a side trip from Gunn Peak or Tailgunner Pass. Its summit shoulder holds a small, photogenic "infinity tarn" overlooking Baring Mountain.

objHaz: seasonal steep snow and cornices near the summit
routes.overview: A steep, brushy off-trail approach from Barclay Lake Trailhead climbs into the shared alpine basin below Gunn, Tailgunner, and Wing peaks; Wing's north ridge/summit-block scramble is typically combined with Gunn Peak and/or Tailgunner Peak in the same outing since all three share the same approach and basin.
routes.beta: Class 3 scramble reached via the Gunn Peak climbers' trail and the basin/saddle shared with neighboring Tailgunner Peak. Multiple trip reports (July-September) describe it as more exposed than it looks from below, with loose rock and route-finding through brush low on the approach; at least one report documents lingering hard snow and cornices near the summit requiring an axe and crampons.
routes.approach: From the road-end Barclay Lake Trailhead (2,300 ft) on FR 6024 (signed 635th Pl NE off US-2 in Baring), follow the unmaintained Gunn Peak climbers' trail: cross Barclay Creek, climb steeply through forest and brush, and continue above treeline into the alpine basin shared by Gunn, Tailgunner, and Wing peaks. From the basin, work toward the saddle area between Tailgunner and Wing before turning onto Wing's north ridge/summit scramble.
routes.descent: Reverse the scramble route to the Tailgunner-Wing saddle, then retrace the climbers' trail back to Barclay Lake Trailhead.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at any point along the ridge/basin and retraced back down the climbers' trail to Barclay Lake Trailhead; there is no fixed rappel/bail infrastructure documented for this line.
routes.turnaround: As with any exposed Class 3 scramble with a long brushy approach, set a firm turnaround time before starting so the descent through the basin and forest can be completed in daylight, and be prepared to retreat if the summit block is holding snow, ice, or wet rock.
routes.bestSeason: Trip reports document ascents from mid-July through September, after the approach gullies and basin have mostly cleared of snow; a February report treats the peak as a ski-mountaineering objective, indicating north-facing terrain can hold snow well into early summer in some years.
routes.proNeeds: Most parties climb this Class 3 line unroped; a light rack (a few slings, a short rope) can be useful for added security on the exposed summit block, and an ice axe with crampons are warranted if snow lingers in the approach gullies or on the summit ridge.
gear: approach shoes or light boots with good grip
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
extra water — no reliable water sources above the basin
layers for exposed alpine ridge weather
bug protection for early-summer basin travel
proTips: Combine with Gunn Peak and/or Tailgunner Peak in the same trip — they share the same Barclay Lake approach and basin.
Carry a GPS track or detailed map; the climbers' trail is unsigned and route-finding through the brush and basin is a common source of confusion.
Check recent trip reports for snow conditions on the summit block before going light — several reports describe unexpectedly firm, steep snow near the top.
watchOut: Early-season parties have encountered hard, steep snow and cornices near the summit requiring an ice axe and crampons despite the Class 3 rating.
The approach involves brush, deadfall, and a crossing of Barclay Creek that can be difficult at high water.
Loose rock is present on the scramble sections; a helmet is recommended.
knownHazards: Steep, hard snow and cornices can persist near the summit into early summer.
Brush, deadfall, and an unbridged Barclay Creek crossing complicate the lower approach.
Loose rock and exposure are present on the upper scramble/summit block.

road.name: Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road, signed 635th Pl NE from US-2)
road.driveNote: From US-2 in Baring, turn north onto 635th Pl NE / FR 6024 and follow it about 4.3 miles to its end at the Barclay Lake Trailhead (2,300 ft).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Cascade West / Stevens Pass forecast zone

bySeason.summer: Trip reports from July-September generally describe dry scrambling conditions, though lingering hard snow and cornices have been documented on north-facing terrain near the summit into July.

access.landManager: U.S. Forest Service — Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) to park at Barclay Lake Trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line 425-407-3999
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest — (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe, 14701 179th Ave SE, Monroe, WA — nearest hospital serving the US-2/Skykomish Valley corridor
emergency.notes: Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue (SCVSAR) supports mountain rescues in this area; the Gunn/Tailgunner/Wing massif has seen multiple SAR incidents given its steep, exposed, off-trail terrain.

blurb: Wing Peak (5,761 ft) is a minor granite/granodiorite summit in the western Cascades' Skykomish River area, part of the rugged Gunn Peak massif within Snohomish County's Wild Sky Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest). It sits immediately southeast of Tailgunner Peak and south of Gunn Peak on the same alpine ridge above Barclay Creek, and is rarely climbed on its own — most parties tack it onto a Gunn Peak or Gunn-Tailgunner-Wing scramble day via the same steep, brushy, off-trail approach from Barclay Lake Trailhead.

objHaz: loose rock and eroded tread on the final push
avalanche terrain on the south ridge in winter/spring
poor visibility crossing Highway 2 at the Smithbrook Road turnoff
unmarked boot path easily lost in fog or fresh snow
routes.season: Jun-Oct (snow-free window); also a moderate winter/spring snowshoe or ski tour
routes.overview: An off-trail hike-scramble that leaves the Pacific Crest Trail at the saddle above Lake Valhalla, climbs Mount McCausland's south ridge, then cuts onto an unmaintained boot path through heather meadows to the small, view-packed 5,747-ft summit.
routes.beta: From the Smithbrook Trailhead, follow Smithbrook Trail #1590 and the Pacific Crest Trail south to the saddle (roughly 5,100 ft) between Lichtenberg Mountain and Mount McCausland just above Lake Valhalla. Leave the PCT and start up McCausland's south ridge; before the ridge crests at an unnamed gap, an obvious but unsigned boot path breaks off through heather meadows for about half a mile, finishing with a short, steep, occasionally brushy Class 2 scramble (hands-on in spots) to the summit, marked by a large summit rock with a register in a steel lockbox.
routes.approach: Drive US-2 to the Smithbrook Road/FR-6700 junction (about 31 miles west of Leavenworth, or roughly 3.5 miles east of the Stevens Pass summit) and follow the gravel road 3 miles to the Smithbrook Trailhead (about 4,000 ft). Hike Smithbrook Trail #1590 roughly 0.9-1.2 miles and about 650 ft of switchbacks up to Union Gap, where it meets the Pacific Crest Trail inside the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness. Turn south (left) on the PCT and follow it about 1.4-1.5 miles, with views toward Nason Ridge and Lichtenberg Mountain, to the saddle above Lake Valhalla.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the heather-meadow boot path to the south-ridge gap, then back to the PCT/saddle and out via Union Gap and Smithbrook Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the boot path south back down through the heather meadows to the unnamed gap on McCausland's south ridge, then drop to rejoin the PCT at the saddle above Lake Valhalla (worth a short detour to the lakeshore). Follow the PCT north back to Union Gap and descend Smithbrook Trail's switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single day: trailhead to Union Gap (0.9-1.2 mi), PCT to the Lake Valhalla saddle (1.4-1.5 mi, with an optional detour to the lake), then the south-ridge/heather-meadow scramble to the summit (about 0.5 mi), reversing the same line to descend.
routes.bail: The route is entirely Class 2 terrain on an established boot path with no technical sections; retreat at any point simply means reversing back down the ridge/meadow path to the PCT saddle and out via Union Gap - no rappels or fixed retreat points are required.
routes.turnaround: Because the final push is loose, steep, and can hold snow well into early summer, turn around if the boot path becomes icy or snow-covered without proper traction, if visibility drops in cloud or fog near the ridge, or if the party's pace won't allow a return to the trailhead before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Most comfortable July through October once the trail is snow-free; WTA and local hikers highlight September for huckleberries and fall color in the heather meadows below the summit. The route also serves as a moderate winter/spring snowshoe or ski objective when avalanche conditions on the south-ridge approach are stable.
gear: map/GPS or a downloaded track (the final boot path is unmarked)
whatToBring: extra water (no reliable water source above Lake Valhalla)
microspikes/traction and an ice axe for early-season or winter snow on the south ridge
proTips: Don't skip a stop at Lake Valhalla itself - most trip reports call it the scenic highlight of the outing.
Time the trip for September to catch huckleberries and turning heather and mountain-ash color in the meadows below the summit.
Note your turn-off point near the ~5,100-ft gap where the unmarked summit boot path leaves the ridge, so you don't miss it on the descent.
watchOut: The last half-mile is steep, loose, and has some Class 2 hand-and-foot scrambling.
The unmarked summit boot path through the heather meadows can be lost in fog or after fresh snow.
Watch your footing and traffic when crossing Highway 2 to reach the Smithbrook Road turnoff.
knownHazards: Loose rock and eroded, steep trail tread on the final summit push.
Lingering snow on the south-ridge approach into early summer creates slip-and-fall exposure.
The south ridge crosses avalanche terrain, making the route a stable-conditions-only objective in winter and spring.

pitchDetail.notes: 0.9-1.2 mi, about 650 ft gain on Smithbrook Trail #1590 switchbacks to the PCT junction.

pitchDetail.pitch: Union Gap to Lake Valhalla saddle
pitchDetail.notes: About 1.4-1.5 mi south on the PCT to the saddle between Lichtenberg Mountain and Mount McCausland, just above Lake Valhalla.
Climb the south ridge toward a roughly 5,100-ft unnamed gap, then take the unmarked boot path north through heather meadows, steep and brushy, about 0.5 mi to the 5,747-ft summit.

road.name: Smithbrook Road / Forest Road 6700
road.status: Gravel, generally passable for passenger cars with some rough/pothole sections; not recommended for stock trucks or trailers
road.driveNote: Turn off US-2 onto Smithbrook Road/FR-6700 (about 31 miles west of Leavenworth, or 3.5 miles east of the Stevens Pass summit) and follow it 3 miles to the Smithbrook Trailhead. The road has been subject to periodic storm-damage closures - check current Wenatchee River Ranger District conditions before driving.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Stevens Pass avalanche forecast zone
climate.typical: East-slope Cascades crest climate: cool, wet, snow-heavy winters and warm, mostly dry summers.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack often lingers into June on the south ridge and summit meadows; avalanche hazard requires caution on the ridge approach.
bySeason.summer: Warm and largely dry July-September; the primary hiking/scrambling season.
bySeason.fall: Cool and clear, popular in September-October for huckleberries and foliage; first snow can arrive by late October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall turns the route into a snowshoe or ski tour with avalanche exposure on the south ridge.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District; Henry M. Jackson Wilderness (designated 1984)
access.fees: No fee currently listed for the Smithbrook Trailhead per the U.S. Forest Service site; some third-party trail sites still reference a Northwest Forest Pass, so check current trailhead signage.
access.permit: No permit required; standard Wilderness regulations apply within the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness (no campfires at Lake Valhalla, camp at least 200 ft from shorelines).
access.passRequired: None required per current USFS trailhead page (historically some sources list a Northwest Forest Pass).
access.closures: Access road FR-6700 has seen periodic storm-damage closures - verify current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start recommended for the roughly 7-mile, 1,800-ft round trip with an unmarked final scramble.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Smithbrook Trailhead (~4,000 ft) to Union Gap (~4,600 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: 0.9-1.2 mi, about 650 ft gain via Smithbrook Trail #1590 switchbacks.

sectionBreakdown.section: Union Gap to Lake Valhalla saddle
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Union Gap to the saddle above Lake Valhalla (~5,100 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 1.4-1.5 mi south on the PCT.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Saddle to Mount McCausland summit (5,747 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 0.5 mi, steep Class 2 boot path through heather meadows.

emergency.county: Chelan County (trailhead and approach); the summit ridge lies on the Chelan-Snohomish county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 667-6851, dispatched via RiverCom 911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826 - (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center (24-hr ED), 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA; EvergreenHealth Monroe serves the west/Snohomish County side of Stevens Pass

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 5,752 ft is not well supported. Multiple independent, reputable sources (WTA, USFS-linked trip descriptions, Mountaineers, Hike of the Week, Wandering Backpack) consistently give the summit as 5,747 ft; Wikipedia lists 5,741 ft (1,750 m) and PeakVisor lists about 5,748 ft (1,752 m). 5,747 ft is the best-supported figure and is used here as highPointFt.

blurb: Mount McCausland (5,747 ft) rises immediately north of Lake Valhalla in the Cascade Range east of Stevens Pass, straddling the Chelan-Snohomish county line inside the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness. It is a minor, non-technical summit of forest, heather meadow, and rocky ridgeline reached off the Pacific Crest Trail from the Smithbrook Trailhead, popular with hikers, scramblers, and off-season snowshoers/skiers for its views of Lake Valhalla, Lichtenberg Mountain, and Glacier Peak. The peak is named for Norm McCausland, a longtime U.S. Forest Service ranger who served the Skykomish Ranger District for 44 years before retiring in 1969.

objHaz: Avalanche potential on all aspects of the peak outside of full summer conditions
Route-finding/navigation through a bushwhack and faint climbers' path beyond the PCT junction
Steep, tree-covered rib gaining the southeast ridge between roughly 4,500 and 5,200 ft
routes.overview: A short, non-technical Class 2 scramble that leaves the Smithbrook Trail/PCT junction near Union Gap, climbs a steep, forested rib to gain Union Peak's southeast ridge, and follows that ridge to the broad, meadow-like summit plateau.
routes.beta: From the Smithbrook Road trailhead, hike the Smithbrook Trail roughly 0.9 mile up through old-growth forest to its junction with the Pacific Crest Trail near Union Gap. Leave the trail after a short distance and bushwhack northeast to gain Union Peak's southeast ridge at about 5,200 ft, then follow the ridge to the 5,696-5,700 ft summit. The line is an easy-to-moderate, non-technical alpine scramble in summer/fall and a moderate winter scramble or backcountry ski/snowshoe objective the rest of the year.
routes.approach: Drive US-2 to Smithbrook Road (Forest Road #6700), about 4.2 miles east of Stevens Pass (roughly 31 miles northwest of Leavenworth). Follow Smithbrook Road about 2.7-2.8 miles to the trailhead/big switchback near the base of the peak (a Northwest Forest Pass is required for summer trailhead parking). In winter the road itself is not plowed; parties instead park on the extra-wide, plowed shoulder of US-2 near milepost 68.9 and approach on foot, snowshoes, or skis. From the road-end trailhead, hike the Smithbrook Trail about 0.9 mile to its junction with the Pacific Crest Trail at Union Gap.
routes.descent: Descend the same southeast ridge and bushwhack line back to the PCT/Smithbrook Trail junction at Union Gap, then follow the maintained Smithbrook Trail back down to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the southeast ridge down to about 5,200 ft, then drop back through the bushwhack/steep rib section to rejoin the Smithbrook Trail near the PCT junction at Union Gap. Follow the trail (about 0.9 mile) back down through old-growth forest to the trailhead. The upper trail and ridge can be snow-covered and hard to follow outside of summer, and the faint boot path off-trail is easy to lose in poor visibility, so a map/compass or GPS track is recommended.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward from anywhere on this route: descend the southeast ridge/rib back to the PCT junction near Union Gap and follow the maintained Smithbrook Trail back to the trailhead.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting based on your group's pace and the forecast, and turn back if deteriorating weather, low visibility, or lingering snow on the ridge make the off-trail route-finding or avalanche exposure unacceptable — sources note that all aspects of Union Peak carry avalanche potential outside of fully melted-out summer conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a hiking/scramble objective in summer and fall (roughly July through October) once the bushwhack section and ridge are snow-free. The same slopes are a popular backcountry ski and snowshoe tour in winter and spring, when avalanche hazard on all aspects requires appropriate gear, training, and current forecast awareness.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed — this is an unroped Class 2 scramble on maintained trail, a short bushwhack, and an open ridge/meadow to the summit.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection required for the standard route; footwear with good traction and trekking poles are the main 'gear' concern for the steep, loose rib section.
gear: Map and compass or GPS (for the off-trail bushwhack/ridge section)
Layered clothing for exposed ridge and summit
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking (summer access)
Extra water and sun protection on the open ridge/summit plateau
Navigation aid (map/compass or GPS track) given the faint climbers' path beyond the PCT junction
Winter travelers: snowshoes/skis and avalanche safety gear if approaching in winter/spring conditions
proTips: At the Pacific Crest Trail junction, take a right and then an immediate left before leaving the trail to start the bushwhack toward the southeast ridge

Consider combining with neighboring Jove Peak (6,007 ft) via the roughly 5,180-5,200 ft saddle on Union Peak's north ridge — several sources note the views from Jove Peak surpass those from Union Peak
A Northwest Forest Pass is required to park at the summer (road-end) trailhead
watchOut: The path beyond the PCT junction is a faint, unofficial climbers' route and easy to lose in socked-in weather
No toilet facilities at the trailhead
Trailhead access differs by season: drive to the road-end trailhead in summer, but park on the US-2 shoulder and approach on foot/skis in winter — check current road status before you go
knownHazards: WTA states that 'ALL SIDES of this mountain are avalanche prone,' which drives serious winter/spring caution even though the summer scramble itself is non-technical.
The Mountaineers describe a steep, tree-covered rib between about 4,500 and 5,200 ft with 'some avalanche potential' on the way to gaining the southeast ridge.
The upper trail and off-trail sections can be snow-covered and difficult to follow outside of summer conditions (USFS Smithbrook Trail page).

road.name: Smithbrook Road (Forest Road #6700)
road.status: Gravel forest road, drivable to the trailhead in snow-free months; not plowed in winter
road.seasonalGate: Effectively closed to vehicles by snow in winter/spring; winter parties park on the US-2 shoulder near milepost 68.9 instead and approach on foot or skis
road.driveNote: Turn off US-2 about 4.2 miles east of Stevens Pass (or ~31 miles northwest of Leavenworth) and follow Smithbrook Road roughly 2.7-2.8 miles to the trailhead/switchback; use caution crossing the westbound highway lanes to reach the turn.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Stevens Pass avalanche forecast zone
climate.typical: Cascade-crest climate at Stevens Pass: heavy winter snowfall, cool and often wet/cloudy weather, with the trailhead itself around 3,200-4,000 ft elevation.

bySeason.spring: Lingering, often unstable snowpack at higher elevations; avalanche hazard persists on the peak's slopes.
bySeason.summer: Typically snow-free by mid-summer, the standard window for the non-technical Class 2 scramble.
bySeason.fall: Generally cool and stable into October, though snow can return by late fall.
bySeason.winter: Deep, avalanche-prone snowpack on all aspects of the peak; a popular backcountry ski and snowshoe tour.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (per the USFS's own Smithbrook Trail page); some hiking sources instead attribute the area to the neighboring Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, reflecting its location right at the Cascade-crest forest boundary.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent interagency day-use pass) required to park at the Smithbrook Road trailhead in summer.
access.permit: No wilderness permit is required for this specific route; the upper Smithbrook Trail does enter designated wilderness near the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness boundary, where standard wilderness regulations apply.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Smithbrook Road (FR 6700) is not plowed and is effectively closed to vehicles in winter; the trailhead itself has no toilet facilities.

timing.recommendedStart: An early start is advisable to allow time for the off-trail bushwhack/route-finding and to be off the ridge before afternoon weather moves in, though no source gives a specific recommended clock time.

sectionBreakdown.note: About 0.9 mile on the maintained Smithbrook Trail through old-growth forest (USFS).
Leave the trail after roughly 0.3 mile past the junction and bushwhack northeast up a steep, tree-covered rib to gain the ridge (The Mountaineers).
Follow the southeast ridge to the broad, gentle summit plateau (WTA, The Mountaineers).

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff's Office / RiverCom 911 dispatch, non-emergency (509) 667-6860
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-5815 — 24-hour ER; its hospital district's service area is explicitly described as extending to the summit of Stevens Pass.
emergency.notes: Cell coverage along the US-2/Stevens Pass corridor is inconsistent. Search and rescue in this area falls under Chelan County Sheriff's Office jurisdiction (supported by Chelan County Mountain Rescue).

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 5,700 ft is a rounded figure; WTA and ListsOfJohn both list Union Peak at 5,696 ft, which was used for highPointFt. Land-manager attribution varies by source: WTA and several hiking sites call the area part of Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, but the USFS's own Smithbrook Trail page places the trailhead/approach in the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest's Wenatchee River Ranger District — the latter was used as the primary access/landManager value since it is the official agency source.

blurb: Union Peak (5,696 ft per USGS-derived sources) is a minor summit on the Cascade Crest in Chelan County, Washington, just east of Stevens Pass near the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness boundary and the Pacific Crest Trail crossing at Union Gap. It sits on forested, granitic Cascade-crest terrain and is best known as an easy backcountry ski/snowshoe objective in winter and a short, non-technical Class 2 scramble in summer and fall; its north ridge connects via a saddle to the taller, better-viewed Jove Peak (6,007 ft). No documented first-ascent history was found for this minor, non-technical summit.

blurb: Himmelhorn (7,880 ft) is a remote summit in the Southern Pickets, part of the Picket Range within North Cascades National Park, standing about 0.7 mile west of Mount Terror near the range's western Crescent Creek Spires. Built of Skagit Gneiss, it shows the steep, broken faces typical of the Pickets and was first climbed on September 8, 1961 by Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, and George Whitmore, who found an unexpectedly broken class IV line up the north side after approaching via a west notch. Originally recorded as "Himmelgeisterhorn" ("Horn of the Sky Spirit"), it remains one of the range's less-frequently climbed peaks, summited roughly every couple of years via its standard line or the popular Wild Hair Crack.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, George Whitmore — September 8, 1961 (Himmelhorn's first ascent; the peak's original/standard line)
objHaz: chossy moats near snow/rock margins late season
remote, serious commitment far from help
routes.aspect: N (summit pitch); approach trends up SE-facing terrain through Crescent Creek Basin to the Himmel-Otto col before crossing to the north face
routes.face: North Face (final pitch, reached via the west/Himmel-Otto notch)
routes.overview: The peak's original 1961 first-ascent line and its standard route: a long approach through Crescent Creek Basin to the Himmel-Otto col, then a broken, class-IV scramble up Himmelhorn's north side to the small summit, discovered only after the party judged the peak unreachable from the notch itself.
routes.beta: Considered Himmelhorn's standard/easiest way up, alongside the more technical Wild Hair Crack; the peak sees an ascent roughly every couple of years by one or the other. Early season the gully to the col is mostly snow with limited scrambling; by late summer it can present icy snow and chossy moats, with an overhanging boulder near the top sometimes requiring a loose class 3/4 bypass.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Trailhead on SR-20 (near Newhalem, ~MP 119), follow the 'stump hollow' approach: an old roadbed for about 4 miles, then a faint boot path through forest, crossing Terror Creek and climbing 'the Barrier' into Crescent Creek Basin (traverse around ~5,800 ft). From the basin, ascend the narrow gully toward the Himmel-Otto col between Himmelhorn and neighboring peaks, then work onto the class-IV north-face terrain to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb/rappel back to the col, then retrace the Crescent Creek Basin approach to Goodell Creek. Parties in the Southern Pickets commonly combine downclimbing with one or more short rappels off the notch/col area rather than a single walk-off.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the class-IV terrain on the north side back to the west/Himmel-Otto notch, downclimbing or making short rappels as needed depending on conditions (snow vs. bare rock, moat development). From the col, descend the gully back into Crescent Creek Basin and retrace the Barrier/Terror Creek approach to the old roadbed and Goodell Creek Trailhead. Conditions vary greatly by season — snow coverage in the descent gully can make travel faster and safer early season, while late-season choss and moats slow the descent and add rockfall exposure.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip is 3-4 days: Day 1 approach from Goodell Creek TH to Crescent Creek Basin camp (a full, strenuous day; some parties split this into two days); Day 2 climb Himmelhorn (and often combine with Mount Terror or other Southern Pickets summits given the shared approach); Day 3-4 additional summits and/or the return hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally back the way you came: downclimb to the Himmel-Otto col and descend the approach gully into Crescent Creek Basin. There is no shorter bail option once established on the peak's broken upper terrain — the approach itself is the retreat route.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, strenuous multi-day approach and the loose, weather-exposed nature of Pickets terrain, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight margin for the descent and the return through Crescent Creek Basin before dark or before afternoon weather typical of the North Cascades builds.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in mid-to-late summer (roughly July through September) once the approach gully and col are more consolidated; early-season ascents face mostly-snow conditions with less scrambling but require snow-travel skill, while late-season trips deal with icy snow, choss, and moats as the snowpack recedes.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable throughout this part of North Cascades National Park; the park explicitly warns not to rely on cell phones for emergency plans in this terrain, though occasional signal may be caught from high points.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack is typical for this class-IV/low-5th line: a small set of nuts and cams, a few slings/draws, and a lightweight rope for the short technical sections and any rappels off the col; carry an ice axe and crampons for the approach gully and col when snow-covered (common through mid-summer).
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are modest and intermittent — mostly natural gear placements on broken, blocky rock for short exposed sections rather than sustained crack climbing.
gear: mountaineering boots or sturdy approach shoes
whatToBring: bivy/overnight gear for the multi-day approach
map and compass or GPS (route-finding through Crescent Creek Basin is nontrivial)
satellite communicator (InReach or similar) given unreliable cell coverage
extra food and fuel for a 3-4 day trip
sun/weather protection for exposed basin travel
proTips: Early-season snow coverage in the approach gully to the Himmel-Otto col generally makes for easier, faster travel with less scrambling than late season.
Fit parties familiar with the approach can reach Crescent Creek camp in a single long day from Goodell Creek; otherwise plan on two.
Many parties combine Himmelhorn with Mount Terror and/or other Southern Pickets summits given the shared, demanding approach.
watchOut: Late-season conditions bring icy snow and chossy moats along the approach gully.
An overhanging boulder near the top of the approach gully can force a loose, exposed class 3/4 bypass.
Rockfall risk on the broken, loose gneiss typical of the Pickets — helmets and careful party spacing are standard advice from NPS for this range.
knownHazards: Remote, multi-day approach far from help with slow (24-48 hr) non-emergency response.
Loose rock and rockfall on the class-IV summit terrain and approach gully.
Glacier-adjacent travel near the Mustard Glacier on the peak's north side.
Route-finding difficulty through brush and the Barrier en route to Crescent Creek Basin.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Goodell Creek
road.status: Goodell Creek Trailhead (near Newhalem, ~milepost 119) is west of SR-20's seasonal high-country closure gate and is generally accessible year-round by road.
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 itself closes seasonally at higher passes further east (Washington Pass area) due to avalanche danger, but this does not affect access to Goodell Creek/Newhalem on the west side.
road.driveNote: Goodell Creek Campground/Trailhead sits directly off SR-20, about 60 miles east of Burlington, WA.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center / NOAA North Cascades zone forecasts (park-recommended resources); no Pickets-specific NWAC zone confirmed
climate.typical: Maritime North Cascades climate: substantial rain and snow, rapidly changing mountain weather, and heavy winter snowpack persisting into summer at this elevation.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; approach and gully largely snow-covered.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep) but still subject to sudden North Cascades weather changes and afternoon buildups.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early snow at elevation.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; not a realistic climbing season for this route.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: No specific route fee; backcountry permit reservation fees apply through Recreation.gov for peak-season overnight stays.
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry permit is required year-round for all overnight stays; obtainable in advance (partial inventory) via Recreation.gov or in person at an NPS Wilderness Information Center.
access.closures: Standard NPS backcountry regulations apply (Goodell Creek Cross-Country Zone); check current NPS alerts for any temporary closures (e.g., bear activity closures have affected the Goodell Creek Campground in some seasons).

sectionBreakdown.section: Goodell Creek TH to Crescent Creek Basin camp
sectionBreakdown.note: A full, strenuous day for a fit, approach-savvy party; many parties split this over two days.

sectionBreakdown.section: Crescent Creek camp to base of climbing
sectionBreakdown.note: Even fast parties typically need about 2-3 hours from Crescent Creek camp to the base of routes on peaks around the basin.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff (non-emergency dispatch): 360-384-5390; or 911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center: 360-854-7245 (Marblemount); Park HQ: 360-854-7200 (Sedro-Woolley)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA — 360-856-6021 (24-hour ER)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable throughout this area per NPS guidance; non-emergency park response can take 24-48 hours given the remote terrain. Report SR-20 highway incidents to Washington State Patrol; incidents in Newhalem/Diablo go to Whatcom County Sheriff.

routes.corrections: Given elevation context (peak listed at 7,901 ft) conflicts with multiple concurring sources (Wikipedia, StephAbegg.com, Alpinist) which all list Himmelhorn at 7,880 ft; used 7,880 ft as highPointFt. No source we found names a distinct 'Southeast Route' on Himmelhorn — the only well-documented original/standard line is the 1961 FA route (Cooper-Denny-Firey-Firey-Whitmore), whose primary AAC account describes the summit pitch as a 'class IV' line found on the NORTH face after approaching via a west notch, not a southeast-facing line; treated this route as that standard/FA line and flagged the aspect discrepancy rather than inventing a separate southeast route. Similarly, the primary FA source's 'class IV' terminology is an old-style scrambling-class descriptor, not a confirmed modern 5.8 roped rating — the given rockGrade of 5.8 and alpineGrade D+ could not be independently corroborated by any source found and should be treated as approximate/unconfirmed rather than verified.

blurb: The Rake (7,840–7,869 ft depending on source) is a serrated biotite-gneiss summit on the crest of the Southern Picket Range in North Cascades National Park, Washington, between Mount Terror and the Twin Needles. Like the rest of the Pickets, it formed from metamorphism of sedimentary and volcanic rock roughly 100 million years ago and sits in some of the most remote, glaciated alpine terrain in the Lower 48. Its only documented technical line, the East Ridge, was first climbed on July 26, 2003 by Mark Bunker, Colin Haley, and Wayne Wallace during the first complete traverse of the Southern Pickets.

routes.fa: Mark Bunker, Colin Haley, Wayne Wallace — East Ridge, July 26, 2003 (during the first complete Southern Pickets Traverse)
objHaz: Loose rock in the gully guarding the base of the ridge
Extreme remoteness — self-rescue is the only realistic option for most incidents
Sustained exposure on an overhanging/vertical ridge crest
Lingering snow into midsummer requiring ice axe/crampon travel on the approach
Afternoon thunderstorms common on the exposed crest in summer
routes.overview: A serrated, sustained crest of gneiss climbing the East Ridge of The Rake from the col near Mount Terror toward the Twin Needles — the technical crux link of the Southern Pickets Enchainment rather than a stand-alone objective.
routes.beta: The Rake is climbed almost exclusively as part of the multi-day Southern Pickets Traverse. The line ascends steep-to-overhanging rock above a loose lower gully (originally protected on pitons), then follows a long, exposed, sustained ridge crest — described by the first-ascent party as 'a half-mile of pure ecstasy, classic alpine ridge climbing' — to the summit, where parties rappel to continue the traverse.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek trailhead (~600 ft) off SR 20 just west of Newhalem, follow the old roadbed and climber's trail roughly 7.4 miles and about 5,835 ft of gain, crossing a ~6,200 ft notch, down to the flat camp at Terror Basin (~5,800 ft). From camp, traverse on snow, talus, and slabs beneath Mount Terror to reach the base of the gully separating Mount Terror from The Rake.
routes.descent: Rappel from the summit back down the East Ridge/gully line.
routes.descentText: Parties completing the full enchainment rappel from The Rake's summit to continue toward the next objective. Climbing it as an out-and-back requires reversing the ridge and rappelling the approach gully; no fixed/maintained rappel anchors exist in this designated wilderness (bolting is prohibited), so expect to build gear anchors and leave rappel tat.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan: Day 1 hike in to Terror Basin camp; Day 2 traverse to the Rake–Terror col, climb the East Ridge and rappel back to camp (or continue the traverse); Day 3 hike out. Most parties encounter The Rake mid-traverse on a longer Southern Pickets itinerary rather than as a single-day objective.
routes.bail: Retreat is by rappelling the line just climbed — there is no easier alternate way off the ridge crest, and the loose gully below the first pitches is the most direct way back down to the talus above Terror Basin.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour, off-trail approach required just to reach the base of the route, begin early enough to complete the ridge and rappel back with margin before dark, and retreat before afternoon thunderstorms typical of Cascades summers reach the exposed crest.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in August, after the Pickets' notoriously heavy, late-lingering snowpack has melted off the approach and route. Snow can still cover approach terrain into July, and increasing storm frequency narrows the window again by mid-to-late September.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in the deep, glaciated terrain of the Pickets; the NPS advises climbers not to rely on a phone as a sole emergency plan and to carry a satellite communicator or PLB instead.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of cams from finger to hand size (roughly 0.3–3 in), a set of stoppers, a few pitons (knifeblades/Lost Arrows) for thin cracks and to back up tat anchors, plenty of slings, and a 60m rope (a second rope or tag line eases rappels) covers the technical climbing. Carry an ice axe and light crampons for approach snow.
routes.proNeeds: Small-to-medium cams and nuts protect most of the ridge climbing; a couple of pitons are useful around the loose lower gully and for backing up rappel anchors, since no fixed hardware exists on this wilderness route.
gear: Single rack of cams (finger to hand size)
Snow picket for approach snow crossings
whatToBring: Bivy gear or tent for Terror Basin camp
Bear-resistant food storage (canister/Ursack) — required in some Pickets cross-country zones, confirm at the Wilderness Information Center
Sturdy approach shoes for the bushwhack, swapped for rock shoes on route
Extra food/fuel buffer for weather or route-finding delays
proTips: Budget extra time on the ridge itself — the first-ascent party called it 'a half-mile of pure ecstasy,' but that length plus route-finding on unfamiliar rock adds up.
Water and flat bivy sites are scarce right at The Rake; plan camps at Terror Basin or established cols instead of near the summit.
The loose gully below the ridge crux was historically avoided by climbing steeper, more solid rock to its side, protected on pitons.
watchOut: The lower gully guarding the ridge is loose and rockfall-prone — helmet mandatory, avoid stacking parties above/below.
This is remote, largely off-trail, glaciated terrain with no realistic chance of fast rescue; full self-sufficiency is required.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in Cascades summers and this exposed rock ridge offers little shelter.
knownHazards: Loose rock in the approach gully beneath the ridge, historically avoided via steeper, more solid rock protected with pitons.
Extreme remoteness of the Southern Pickets means any accident requires lengthy self-extraction or a multi-day rescue response.
Lingering snow on the approach and route into midsummer requires ice axe/crampon travel and adds rockfall exposure on adjacent terrain.

road.name: Goodell Creek Road (spur off SR 20 / North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Short, rough-but-passenger-car-accessible gravel spur off SR 20 near Newhalem, signed for Goodell Creek Campground.
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 itself is gated for winter between the west (Newhalem, MP 120) and east (Early Winters/Silver Star/Mazama) sides, typically from late November into spring depending on snowpack — the Goodell Creek turnoff near Newhalem stays open longer since it's on the west (year-round-accessible) side of the pass closure.
road.driveNote: Drive SR 20 to just west of Newhalem, turn north on the signed Goodell Creek spur, and park near the campground turnout; the trailhead itself is reachable in a standard passenger car.

climate.forecastZone: NWS/NOAA North Cascades zone forecast and Northwest Avalanche Center mountain weather (West Slopes North); Mountain-Forecast.com publishes a dedicated Mount Terror point forecast for the immediate Southern Pickets area.
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short, relatively dry summer climbing window typical of the western North Cascades crest; the Pickets hold snow later into the season than most other Washington ranges.

bySeason.spring: Still largely snow-covered; approach and route are snow/glacier travel, not a rock objective.
bySeason.summer: The primary climbing season, especially August, once approach snow has consolidated or melted and the ridge is mostly dry rock.
bySeason.fall: A shrinking window through September before storm frequency increases and early snow returns.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible for climbing; the range sees heavy snowfall and the drive-in season is limited.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: $10/person plus a $6 nonrefundable permit fee for backcountry permits, charged mid-May through early October (no charge outside that window); no fee for youth 15 and under.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay. Reserve via Recreation.gov (about 60% of sites reservable in advance) or obtain a walk-up permit at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: No state Discover Pass needed — this is NPS land, not WA State land; only the NPS backcountry permit applies.
access.closures: Designated wilderness fixed-anchor moratorium in effect since July 2013 — no new bolts may be placed anywhere in the park's backcountry, including this route.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (the peak sits near the Whatcom/Skagit line; the Goodell Creek trailhead itself is in Skagit County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff dispatch: 360-384-5390. Skagit County Sheriff dispatch (covers the trailhead approach): 360-336-9450. 911 also routes to the correct agency.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245; main park line (360) 854-7200.
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA — 24/7 Level IV emergency department, (360) 856-6021.
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable throughout the Pickets; NPS recommends carrying a satellite communicator/PLB as the primary emergency backup rather than relying on a phone.
