routes.corrections: Seed data listed fa='1951' with rockGrade 5.7 / alpineGrade 'D' for the 'Ridge Traverse Route.' Reliable sources (AAC Publications 'Walking the Fence,' corroborated by multiple trip reports) document only one established technical route on The Rake — the East Ridge, graded IV 5.9 — first climbed July 26, 2003 by Mark Bunker, Colin Haley, and Wayne Wallace during the first complete Southern Pickets Traverse, not 1951/5.7. Wikipedia separately lists a peak (not this route's) first ascent year of 1951, but no source could confirm the party or the exact line climbed then, so that year should not be attached to this 5.9 ridge route. Also, sources disagree on summit elevation: Lists of John gives 7,869 ft (matching this app's stored peak elevation) while Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and StephAbegg.com all independently give 7,840 ft — flagging for review since 3 sources agree on the lower figure.

blurb: East Twin Needle is a slender ~7,840–7,868 ft Skagit Gneiss spire in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Washington, rising just west of Mount Terror and separated from its slightly taller neighbor West Twin Needle (7,936 ft) by the notch known as Eye Col. The pair's rugged, glacier-carved topography (Mustard Glacier lies on their north slopes) reflects terrane uplift and Pleistocene glaciation, and they rank among the most remote, rarely-visited summits in the Cascades. Twin Needles was first climbed on August 17, 1932 by Seattle Mountaineers William Degenhardt, James Martin, and Herb Strandberg, part of a wave of first ascents these climbers made throughout the Southern Pickets that summer.

routes.fa: William Degenhardt, James Martin, Herb Strandberg — August 17, 1932
routes.overview: The South Route follows the moderate south side of East Twin Needle up from Crescent Creek Basin to the summit. It is understood to be the original 1932 line and remains the easiest way to the top, in contrast to the much harder routes established decades later on the peak's other faces (a 5.9 North Buttress in 2022 and a 5.10a southeast-ridge/east-arête line climbed during the 2003 Southern Pickets traverse).
routes.beta: A full-value Cascades alpine outing: several days of approach through Terror Creek and Crescent Creek Basins to reach the base, followed by a day of mostly moderate, broken rock climbing on the south side with a short 5.7 crux section guarding the summit. Loose rock, complex route-finding around Eye Col, and the remoteness of the Southern Pickets are the defining features rather than sustained technical difficulty.
routes.approach: From the North Cascades Highway (SR 20) near Newhalem, drive the short Goodell Creek spur road to the trailhead/group campground (elev. ~600 ft). Follow an old roadbed roughly 4 miles to a cairned turnoff, then a steep boot-beaten path climbing through forest to treeline (~5,000-5,200 ft) and on to a high camp in Terror Creek Basin (~5,800 ft; about 7 hours from the car per trip reports). Reaching Crescent Creek Basin beneath the Twin Needles requires an additional crossing of 'The Barrier,' a rocky ridge separating the two basins that has turned back numerous parties; an alternate approach used for the North Buttress route goes via the Lower Goodell Creek trailhead to a col near the Chopping Block (~6+ miles, ~6,000 ft gain) on day one, then traverses Crescent Creek Basin, crossing the Ottohorn-Himmelhorn col, on day two.
routes.descent: Descend the South Face to Eye Col, then continue down south-facing gullies to Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb and rappel the roughly 1,200-foot south face back down to Eye Col, the notch between East and West Twin Needle. From there, connect a sequence of steep south-facing gullies via fourth-class down-scrambling, rappelling, and snow travel (conditions-dependent) to regain Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same south-facing gullies used on the descent; from most points on the route it is fastest and safest to downclimb/rappel back toward Eye Col rather than push on over the summit.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, complex approach and the loose, route-finding-intensive descent, set a firm turnaround time that leaves a full daylight margin for the technical downclimb/rappels and the return crossing of The Barrier; do not linger on the summit if weather is building.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-summer through early fall (roughly July through September), once seasonal snow has cleared enough from the approach basins and the rock for reasonable travel; conditions crossing The Barrier and traveling through Crescent Creek Basin vary significantly year to year with snowpack, and glaciers in the area (e.g., Mustard Glacier) become more broken/crevassed later in summer.
proTips: The crossing of 'The Barrier' between Terror Basin and Crescent Creek Basin is a major crux of the approach and has turned back several parties — scout it carefully rather than committing blind.
Parties have described route-finding through the south-facing gully system between Eye Col and Crescent Creek Basin as disorienting, with beta scarce; build in extra time for the descent.
watchOut: Rock quality on East Twin Needle varies and some bands have been described by climbers as rotten.
Both the approach and descent pass near glaciated/snow terrain (Mustard Glacier area), where crevasse hazard can persist into mid-summer.
knownHazards: Extremely remote location deep in the Southern Pickets means any accident requires a long, difficult self-rescue or helicopter-dependent response.
Loose rock and complex, multi-notch terrain around Eye Col create ongoing rockfall and route-finding hazards.
Approach basins are separated by 'The Barrier,' a rocky ridge crossing that is itself a significant navigational and physical obstacle.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Goodell Creek Road
road.status: Goodell Creek Road is a short spur off SR 20 near Newhalem leading to the trailhead/group campground
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 itself closes seasonally at the Washington Pass/Ross Lake stretch (roughly mid-November to mid/late April) due to avalanche danger, but the Newhalem/Goodell Creek area on the park's west side is on the year-round-open portion of the highway
road.driveNote: Park at the Goodell Creek trailhead/group campground just off SR 20 near Newhalem; the approach begins on an old roadbed.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Mount Baker area (NWS Seattle forecast office; NWAC covers nearby zones for winter/spring avalanche forecasting)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced alpine climate typical of the North Cascades; snow lingers into July on north-facing terrain and glaciers, and the best rock/weather window is mid-summer before fall storms return in October.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack and high avalanche/cornice hazard; approach basins largely snow-covered.
bySeason.summer: Best climbing window (roughly July-September) as snow recedes from rock, though glaciers and gullies remain more broken and crevassed later in the season.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency from October onward.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain; essentially inaccessible for technical rock climbing.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (National Park Service)
access.fees: No entrance fee for North Cascades National Park; no specific day-use fee identified for this approach.
access.permit: A free backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay in North Cascades National Park backcountry, including camps in Terror Creek and Crescent Creek Basins.
access.closures: Standard NPS wilderness regulations apply; check current North Cascades National Park conditions/closures before a trip.

sectionBreakdown.section: Trailhead to Terror Creek Basin camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek trailhead (~600 ft) to Terror Creek Basin camp (~5,800 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Approx. 4 miles of old roadbed then a steep boot path; per a Steph Abegg trip report for the general Southern Pickets approach.

sectionBreakdown.section: Terror Basin to Crescent Creek Basin
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Terror Creek Basin across 'The Barrier' to Crescent Creek Basin
sectionBreakdown.note: Time highly variable with conditions and route-finding; this crossing has turned back multiple parties.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit area); trailhead access is via Skagit County near Newhalem
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 — after-hours contact if NPS emergency lines are unreachable
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.notes: Search and rescue is coordinated by North Cascades National Park: business-hours SAR line (360) 856-5700 ext. 515; after-hours NPS Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37; or Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 if park lines are unreachable. This is one of the most remote peaks in the North Cascades — any rescue will be slow and may require helicopter access.

routes.corrections: Peak elevation given (7,868 ft) is broadly consistent with sourced figures describing East Twin Needle as the shorter of the pair at roughly '7,840+ ft' (contour-based estimate), versus West Twin Needle at a more precisely surveyed 7,936 ft — no correction needed. The route fa carries the confirmed peak first-ascent party/date (Degenhardt, Martin, Strandberg; Aug 17, 1932, per Wikipedia and AAC sources); this is documented as the first ascent of Twin Needles overall and is presumed to correspond to this south-side line (the peak's easiest known route), but the exact 1932 route description is not preserved in available online sources, so treat that specific attribution as reasonably likely rather than fully documented.

blurb: Little Mac Spire is a minor rock summit (7,736 ft) on the ridge connecting East and West McMillan Spire in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington. It rises above the divide between Terror Creek Basin and Stettatle Basin and is built of the same gneissic/metamorphic rock typical of the Southern Pickets, a range known for slender spires above small glaciers and famously unpredictable weather. Though overshadowed by its higher McMillan Spire neighbors, it is periodically linked with them in longer ridge outings, notably during the 2003 complete Southern Picket enchainment by Wayne Wallace, Mark Bunker, and Colin Haley.

routes.overview: A minor, moderate (5.4) alpine rock line up the Southwest Arête of Little Mac Spire, the smallest and westernmost of the McMillan Spires group; most parties climb it as part of a longer day linking Little Mac with East and West McMillan Spire rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.beta: The Southwest Route (Southwest Arête) is the standard, moderate way up Little Mac Spire, given a first ascent date of 1969 (party not identified in available sources). It sees far fewer ascents than the neighboring McMillan Spires and is most often documented as part of longer Southern Pickets link-ups: during the 2003 complete Southern Picket enchainment, Wayne Wallace, Mark Bunker, and Colin Haley climbed Little Mac Spire via the Southwest Arete, then continued over East McMillan Spire (East Ridge) and West McMillan Spire (Southeast Face) in a single day before bivouacking near West McMillan's notch. A secondary account of the same climb (AAC's 'Walking the Fence') instead describes the day's ascent of 'Little McMillan Spire' as via the 'southeast face, III 5.8' -- see corrections.
routes.approach: Access is via the Goodell Creek Trailhead / Upper Goodell Creek Group Campground near Newhalem, off the North Cascades Highway (SR-20). Follow the old road roughly 4 miles (relatively flat, with blowdowns), then a steep, unmaintained climbers' path that gains roughly 3,000-4,000 ft over about 2 miles through heather, boulders, and open forest to a ridge viewpoint near 5,800 ft with the first views into Terror Basin. Descend loose terrain (stay skier's-right) into Terror Basin, then traverse benches and slabs toward the McMillan Spires ridge system to reach the base of Little Mac Spire. This approach is shared with the standard route on West McMillan Spire.
routes.descent: Reverse the Southwest Route/Arete back to the connecting ridge, then retrace the approach out through Terror Basin and the climbers' path to the Goodell Creek Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/reverse the Southwest Arete back to the ridge or notch used on ascent. From there retrace the approach: traverse back through Terror Basin, regain the ~5,800 ft ridge overlook, then descend the steep, loose climbers' path back to the old road and out to the trailhead. The gully/notch section near the ridge overlook is repeatedly described in trip reports as the loosest and most exposed part of the round trip -- favor the skier's-right side to avoid the worst debris.
routes.itinerary: Typical pattern based on standard McMillan Spires trip reports (Little Mac Spire is not separately documented): Day 1 -- hike from Goodell Creek Trailhead to a high camp near the ridge overlook (~5,800 ft) or down into Terror Basin. Day 2 -- climb Little Mac Spire via the Southwest Route, often combined with East and West McMillan Spire in one long day by strong parties (as in the 2003 enchainment); return to camp. Day 3 -- hike out.
routes.bail: Once on the ridge connecting the McMillan Spires, retreat options are limited to reversing the line of ascent back toward the notch/basin and then out via the same approach gully -- there is no quick escape given how remote and complex the Southern Pickets terrain is.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour, route-finding-heavy approach and the loose, exposed ridge terrain connecting the McMillan Spires, set a firm turnaround time based on weather and remaining daylight before starting, and be conservative -- retreat from high on this ridge is slow and involves the same loose terrain climbed on the way up.
routes.bestSeason: Generally climbed July through September; NPS notes snow persists on higher North Cascades terrain into July, and the approach gullies/notch can hold snow into early summer. Most documented ascents of the McMillan Spires group (including the 2003 Little Mac Spire ascent) occurred in late July.
routes.comms: No formal cell-coverage data is available for this area. NPS describes many North Cascades NP backcountry areas, including the Southern Pickets, as 'remote and seldom visited, with few or no current conditions reports,' and emphasizes climber self-sufficiency.
objHaz: nearby glaciers (Terror Glacier / McMillan Creek Glaciers)
whatToBring: Helmet (loose rock reported on the approach gullies and connecting ridge)
Ice axe for early-season snow in the approach gullies (snow can persist into July per NPS)
Bear canister (required by NPS for designated Terror Basin-area camps)
Sturdy approach shoes or light boots for scree, heather, and slab travel
proTips: Little Mac, East McMillan, and West McMillan Spires are often linked in a single long day by strong parties approaching from Terror Basin, as in the 2003 Southern Picket enchainment.
Weather in the Southern Pickets can turn from clear to socked-in fog within minutes -- don't linger on exposed sections of the ridge.
On the steep gully descending toward Terror Basin, staying skier's-right avoids the worst of the loose rock.
watchOut: Loose rock and choss on the approach gullies and the ridge connecting the McMillan Spires.
Rapid, unpredictable weather changes typical of the Southern Pickets.
Very limited current-conditions information -- NPS notes the area is remote and seldom visited.
knownHazards: Loose/rotten rock on the connecting ridge and approach gullies, reported across multiple Southern Pickets trip accounts.
Glacial terrain (Terror Glacier and McMillan Creek Glaciers) nearby if variations off the standard line are attempted.
Long, arduous, multi-day approach that increases exposure and complicates retreat.
No reliable cell coverage in this remote wilderness area.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Goodell Creek Trailhead / Upper Goodell Creek Group Campground near Newhalem
road.status: Paved highway; the Newhalem/Goodell Creek area (near milepost 120) lies west of the standard winter closure gates (Ross Dam TH, MP134, to Silver Star/Early Winters, MP171) and typically stays accessible longer than the high-pass section.
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally each winter, typically between Ross Dam Trailhead (MP134) and the Early Winters/Silver Star gate (MP171), roughly early December through April/May depending on conditions; in severe years the closure has extended down to the Newhalem gate (MP120), which would also block this approach.
road.driveNote: Parking at the Goodell Creek/Upper Goodell Creek Group Campground trailhead is limited, and multiple campgrounds in the area share similar names -- confirm the correct trailhead before arrival.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ301 -- Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit
climate.typical: Wet, storm-prone maritime mountain climate typical of the west-side North Cascades; NPS advises being prepared for rain and wind even in summer, with heavy snow/rain at high elevations in the off-season.

bySeason.spring: Snow lingers at elevation into early summer; approach gullies and the notch above Terror Basin can still hold snow, increasing difficulty and avalanche concern on steep sections.
bySeason.summer: The main climbing window (roughly July-September); west-side terrain like the Pickets stays cooler and wetter than the east-side Cascades, with storms common even in clear-looking weather.
bySeason.fall: Shoulder season with more variable, increasingly wet and cold conditions; NPS notes fall is a popular but weather-variable time to visit.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow and rain at high elevations, with avalanche hazard throughout the steep Southern Pickets terrain; SR-20 access is typically closed.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; NPS backcountry/climbing permit costs $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee as of the 2025 fee schedule (ages 15 and under free).
access.permit: NPS wilderness permit required for overnight backcountry/climbing trips; reserve in advance via Recreation.gov or obtain in person (walk-up) at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: No America the Beautiful/entrance pass required; the separate backcountry permit fee applies instead.

sectionBreakdown.section: Goodell Creek Trailhead to climbers' path turnoff
sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 miles of relatively flat old-road trail with blowdowns; ~1 hr at a fast/trail-running pace per one 2019 trip report -- expect longer at a standard backpacking pace.
Steep, unmaintained path gaining ~3,000-4,000 ft over ~2 miles; ~2.5 hrs at a fast pace in the same report.

sectionBreakdown.section: Ridge overlook down into Terror Basin
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep, loose descent (favor skier's-right); not separately timed in available sources.

sectionBreakdown.section: Terror Basin to Little Mac Spire summit via Southwest Route
sectionBreakdown.note: Not documented with specific timing in available sources; typically climbed as part of a longer McMillan Spires ridge day.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office -- 911 for emergencies; 360-676-6911 non-emergency. Search and rescue coordinated through Whatcom County Search & Rescue Council.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA 98267.
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284 (nearest hospital to the North Cascades Highway corridor).
emergency.notes: Extremely remote area with essentially no cell coverage and, per NPS, few or no current-conditions reports; self-sufficiency and a pre-trip check-in with the Wilderness Information Center are strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Given peak/route data listed this as 'Southwest Route, Grade II-III, 5.4' with FA 1969, which is consistent with the primary source found (Colin Haley's Northwest Mountaineering Journal trip report, which names the line the 'Southwest Arete'). However, a secondary AAC account of the same July 25, 2003 climbing day ('Walking the Fence: The Southern Picket Enchainment, Washington') instead describes that day's ascent of Little Mac/Little McMillan Spire as via the 'southeast face, III 5.8.' This is flagged as an unresolved discrepancy between two secondary/primary sources describing the same 2003 ascent, not a confirmed correction to the given Southwest Route data -- no source was found giving the first-ascent party's name for the 1969 first ascent.

blurb: Ottohorn is a summit in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Washington, sitting on the ridge between Himmelgeisterhorn and Frenzelspitz above Crescent Creek Basin. Like the rest of the notoriously rugged, choss-prone Pickets, it is built of biotite gneiss and is rarely climbed on its own — it is typically taken in via the Otto-Himmel Col as part of a broader Southern Pickets traverse. It was first climbed on September 10, 1961 by Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, and George Whitmore, part of a multi-day first-ascent push that also claimed neighboring Himmelgeisterhorn and Frenzelspitz; the peak's whimsical name (along with its neighbors') came from labels on a mustard bottle the Fireys carried on the trip.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, George Whitmore — September 10, 1961 (via the East Ridge from the Otto-Himmel Col)
routes.overview: A moderate, mostly Class 3-4 scramble up Ottohorn's east ridge from the Otto-Himmel Col, capped by a short pitch of 5.6-5.7 rock climbing where later parties have had to detour around a rockfall scar near the original 1961 line.
routes.beta: First climbed in 1961 as part of a multi-day first-ascent push that also claimed neighboring Himmelgeisterhorn and Frenzelspitz, Ottohorn's standard line climbs the east ridge from the Otto-Himmel Col — mostly unroped scrambling with a short technical crux near the summit. It is rarely climbed as a stand-alone objective; nearly all recorded ascents happen as part of a larger Southern Pickets traverse/enchainment or as a side trip from a Crescent Creek Basin camp.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Trailhead/Campground (about mile 119 on SR 20 near Newhalem), follow the Goodell Creek trail roughly 4-4.5 miles, then branch onto the steep, often-faint Terror Basin climbers' path, gaining about 4,700 ft in the final 2.5 miles to camp in Terror Basin. From there, cross the Barrier (Class 3 terrain with moat/glacier sections) into Crescent Creek Basin, the standard camp for Ottohorn and its neighbors. From Crescent Creek Basin, ascend glacier/snow and talus to the Otto-Himmel Col between Ottohorn and Himmelhorn, then climb the east ridge to the summit.
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the East Ridge back to the Otto-Himmel Col, then down into Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the east ridge back toward the Otto-Himmel Col, watching for loose blocks and reversing (or short-rappelling, if needed) the 5.6-5.7 crux near the top. From the col, descend the moat/glacier terrain on its south side back into Crescent Creek Basin, then retrace the approach over the Barrier to Terror Basin and out via the Goodell Creek climbers' trail.
routes.itinerary: Typical 3-4 day plan: Day 1 — hike in to Terror Basin camp via Goodell Creek; Day 2 — cross the Barrier into Crescent Creek Basin and establish camp; Day 3 — climb to the Otto-Himmel Col and up Ottohorn's east ridge to the summit, return to camp; Day 4 — hike out. Many parties instead fold Ottohorn into a longer Southern Pickets enchainment.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the east ridge back to the Otto-Himmel Col, downclimbing (or short-rappelling) the crux section, then descending to Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, high-commitment approach and notoriously fast-changing Pickets weather, set a firm turnaround based on time and forecast rather than pushing for the summit — a stuck party can be weather-bound for days, and the retreat involves the same loose rock and glacier hazards as the ascent.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly July through September, after the Crescent Creek Basin glacier and approach gullies lose most of their winter snow. The Southern Pickets carry a well-earned reputation for unpredictable, fast-changing weather even in mid-summer, so parties should build slack into any itinerary.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in the Southern Pickets backcountry; parties should carry a satellite messenger/communicator for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack suffices for the short technical section: a thin rack of nuts and cams up to about 2 inches, a handful of slings, and a single 30-60m rope for the crux pitch(es) and any short rappels/downclimbs on retreat. Glacier travel gear (harness, crevasse-rescue kit, ice axe, crampons) is needed for the Crescent Creek Basin glacier and moats below the Otto-Himmel Col.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal — a single short pitch (or two) of 5.6-5.7 rock near the top, often taken around a rockfall scar; natural gear (small-to-medium cams and nuts) protects it adequately. The bulk of the route is unroped 3rd/4th-class scrambling.
whatToBring: Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach/SPOT) — no reliable cell coverage
Bivy/camping gear for the multi-day approach
Map and GPS/compass — the Pickets are notoriously easy to get off-route in
proTips: Ottohorn is rarely climbed as a stand-alone objective — nearly every recorded ascent has been part of a broader Southern Pickets traverse or enchainment out of Crescent Creek Basin.
Expect to find an alternate line around a fresh rockfall scar near the summit ridge; several parties have reported unplanned 5.6-5.7 pitches here that weren't part of the original 1961 line.
Rope up for the final scramble to the ridge even though most of the route is 3rd/4th class — loose blocks and exposure add up.
watchOut: The rock in the Southern Pickets, including on Ottohorn, is notoriously loose, choss-prone biotite gneiss — test every hold.
The north side of the Otto-Himmel Col has loose rock and moats that worsen as the season's snow melts out.
Weather in the Pickets changes fast and can strand parties for days; build slack into any itinerary.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock across the route and approach gullies.
Glacier travel with crevasses and moats near the Otto-Himmel Col, especially late season.
Extremely remote setting with no cell coverage — self-rescue capability and a satellite communicator are effectively required.
Difficult, multi-day approach with significant route-finding challenges through brush and over the Barrier.

pitchDetail.notes: From the Otto-Himmel Col, unroped or simul-climbed scrambling up the east ridge/gullies toward the summit ridge.
A short technical section near the top, historically encountered where a rockfall scar undercuts the easier original line; parties rope up here.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Goodell Creek Trailhead
road.status: Goodell Creek Trailhead (about mile 119 on SR 20, near Newhalem) sits west of the highway's winter closure gates and is generally reachable whenever SR 20 is open between Sedro-Woolley/Marblemount and Newhalem.
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 itself closes each winter (typically late November to mid/late spring, snowpack-dependent) between Ross Dam (MP 134) and the Methow Valley side, well east of Goodell Creek, so it does not block trailhead access.
road.driveNote: From Sedro-Woolley/Marblemount, drive SR 20 east to Newhalem (about mile 119); Goodell Creek Campground/trailhead is just west of Newhalem.

climate.typical: A wet, maritime alpine climate typical of the North Cascades: heavy winter snowpack, a short climbing season, and weather that can turn fast even in summer.

bySeason.spring: Still deep in winter snowpack; approach and route are not in typical rock-climbing condition.
bySeason.summer: The main climbing window (roughly July-September) once the Crescent Creek Basin glacier and gullies open up, though storms and cold fronts can still move through with little warning.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early snow by late September/October.
bySeason.winter: Not a winter objective; the approach and highway access are effectively closed by snow.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee. Overnight backcountry/wilderness camping requires a $10 per-person, per-night camping fee (youth 15 and under free) plus a $6 non-refundable permit reservation fee.
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay in the Terror Basin or Crescent Creek Basin zones, obtained in person or via advance reservation (Recreation.gov) through the park's Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: North Cascades NP climbing areas fall under NPS wilderness climbing management (including a fixed-anchor policy); no permanent closures specific to Ottohorn are documented.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek Trailhead to Terror Basin
sectionBreakdown.note: Roughly 4-4.5 mi of mostly graded trail paralleling Goodell Creek, then a steep, often-faint climbers' path gaining about 4,700 ft over the final 2.5 mi to Terror Basin; commonly reserved as its own day.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Terror Basin over the Barrier to Crescent Creek Basin
sectionBreakdown.note: Class 3 terrain and glacier/moat crossings on the Barrier separate Terror Basin from Crescent Creek Basin, the standard camp for Ottohorn.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Crescent Creek Basin to Ottohorn summit via the Otto-Himmel Col
sectionBreakdown.note: Glacier/snow travel to the col, then Class 3-4 terrain (with a short 5.6-5.7 section) along the east ridge to the summit.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office / Skagit 911 non-emergency (360) 428-3211; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7200 (permits); NPS Park Dispatch (360) 873-4500 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E. Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage in the Southern Pickets backcountry; a satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin inReach) is strongly recommended. Rescues here are difficult and can take days given the remoteness and unpredictable weather.

routes.corrections: Every reputable source found (AAC Publications' 'First Ascents in the Southern Pickets,' rhinoclimbs.com, and multiple Southern Pickets trip reports) describes this line as the 'East Ridge' from the Otto-Himmel Col, Class III, not a 'Southeast Route' — likely the same route under a different name, since the FA year/party (Cooper, Denny, J. & J. Firey, Whitmore, Sept. 10 1961) and general character (3rd/4th-class terrain with a short 5.6-5.7 crux where a rockfall scar forces a detour around the original line) match closely. Peak elevation also varies by source and could not be confirmed precisely: Wikipedia lists 7,640 ft, rhinoclimbs.com lists '7,680+' ft (topo-contour reading) — both differ somewhat from the 7,703 ft given — so highPointFt was left null rather than guessed. The given alpineGrade 'D' (French adjectival scale) could not be independently verified from any source found, so it was left null pending confirmation.

blurb: Whatcom Peak (7,574 ft) rises in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA, immediately north of Mount Challenger and its Challenger Glacier, with the Whatcom Glacier draining the peak's northeast slopes. It is built of the metamorphic gneiss/schist and granitic intrusive rock typical of the North Cascades crystalline core, and presents a mix of glacier/snow travel and loose Class 3 rock scrambling rather than sustained technical climbing. A remote, seldom-visited summit reached only by long cross-country approaches, it was first climbed in 1936 by Fred Berry and Lawrence Buchanan.

routes.fa: Fred Berry, Lawrence Buchanan — 1936 (recorded as the peak's first ascent; no source ties it to a specific line, but this is the standard/easiest route and almost certainly the FA line for the era)
routes.overview: From the Perfect Pass camp, a moderate-angle snow slope on Whatcom Peak's south face leads to a short but exposed Class 3 scramble along the south ridge crest to the summit.
routes.beta: Whatcom Peak is usually climbed as a side trip from a Perfect Pass base camp reached via the multi-day Easy Ridge approach from Hannegan Pass. From Perfect Pass a long, straightforward snowfield/glacier slope gains the south side of the peak, followed by an exposed but short Class 3 scramble along the south ridge to the summit; the peak has two summit humps, with the northeast one being the true high point. Parties continuing toward Mount Challenger instead traverse the Whatcom Glacier on the peak's east side to bypass the summit, or go up-and-over via the North Ridge (Class 3-4) from Whatcom Pass — guidebooks and trip reports treat the south-side/Perfect Pass line as the easiest standard route.
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Trailhead (end of Ruth Creek Road/FS Road 32, ~3,100 ft), hike over Hannegan Pass and drop to the Chilliwack River (a thigh-deep ford in mid-summer), then climb the old climbers' trail up Easy Ridge. Follow the ridge over Easy Peak and through the 'Imperfect Impasse' — a section of deep moats and down-sloping slabs that often forces a detour through brush and Class 4 rock — to reach Perfect Pass (~6,200 ft), the standard base camp for the peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down-climb the south ridge scramble and descend the snowfield back to Perfect Pass camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the Class 3 south-ridge scramble down to the top of the snow slope, then descend the moderate-angle snowfield/glacier back toward Perfect Pass camp. Multi-day parties then retrace the Easy Ridge approach (via the Imperfect Impasse, over Easy Peak, down to the Chilliwack River ford, and over Hannegan Pass) back to the Hannegan Trailhead, or continue the trip as a loop via Whatcom Pass and other exit trails.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip is 2-4 days: Day 1 backpack from Hannegan Trailhead over Hannegan Pass, ford the Chilliwack River, and climb to a camp on Easy Ridge or continue to Perfect Pass; Day 2 establish/confirm Perfect Pass camp and climb Whatcom Peak (round trip from camp is short, roughly 90 minutes per trip reports); remaining day(s) used to hike back out via Easy Ridge, or to continue on toward Mount Challenger/Whatcom Pass if extending the trip into a loop.
routes.bail: Retreat to Perfect Pass camp is straightforward by reversing the snow slope and ridge scramble; from Perfect Pass, bail out is via the Easy Ridge trail back over Hannegan Pass to the Hannegan Trailhead.
routes.turnaround: Because the final ridge is exposed Class 3 terrain above glaciated slopes with a long, multi-day approach back to the trailhead, parties should set a turnaround time before starting the summit push from Perfect Pass and be willing to retreat if weather, visibility, or party pace put a safe return to camp in doubt.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the typical window, once the Chilliwack River ford and high snowfields are most manageable; several guidebook and trip-report sources note that glacier and snow-bridge conditions on the approaches around Whatcom Peak (including the Whatcom Glacier traverse) become more treacherous later in the season as bridges melt out, so timing and year-to-year conditions matter.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service is reported in this part of the North Cascades National Park backcountry; parties commonly carry a satellite messenger (e.g., Garmin inReach) for emergency communication given the remote, multi-day approach.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit rather than a rock rack: a 30-40m rope per rope team, crampons, a mountaineering ice axe, climbing harness, 2-3 locking carabiners, prusik cords or mechanical ascenders, and a pulley for crevasse rescue. No technical rock protection is needed for the Class 3 scrambling near the summit; a helmet is recommended given reports of loose rock on the final ridge.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed or placed protection is used on the scrambling sections; the only rope work is for glacier crossings, handled as a standard 2-person (or larger) glacier rope team with crevasse-rescue gear.
whatToBring: Bear canister (required in certain North Cascades NP backcountry zones)
Satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin inReach) — no cell coverage
Gaiters for stream fords and snow travel
proTips: The Easy Ridge approach is considered the more scenic way in; the North Ridge from Whatcom Pass is faster but less aesthetic, and some parties combine the two as an up-one, down-the-other loop.

From Perfect Pass camp the summit push is short — roughly 90 minutes round trip per trip reports — making Whatcom Peak a worthwhile side objective for parties camped there en route to Mount Challenger.
Whatcom Peak has two summit humps; the northeast one is the true (higher) summit.
watchOut: The final south-ridge scramble to the summit is exposed Class 3 with real consequences for a fall.
The 'Imperfect Impasse' section of the Easy Ridge approach has deep moats and down-sloping slabs that often force a Class 4 detour with brush.
Loose rock has been noted on the summit ridge in multiple trip accounts.
The Chilliwack River ford on the approach can be thigh-deep and swift in mid-summer.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel is involved on the Whatcom Glacier and nearby Challenger Glacier for parties extending beyond the summit.
Guidebooks warn that snow-bridge and glacier conditions around Whatcom Peak deteriorate and become more treacherous after mid-summer.
The approach is long and remote with no cell coverage, increasing the consequence of any injury or delay.

pitchDetail.notes: Hannegan Trailhead to Perfect Pass via Hannegan Pass, Chilliwack River ford, and Easy Ridge (multi-day backpack).

pitchDetail.pitch: Perfect Pass to base of snow slope
pitchDetail.notes: Cross-country travel from camp toward the south side of the peak.
Long, straightforward snowfield/glacier climb up the south side.
Short, exposed scramble along the south ridge crest; two summit humps, the northeast one higher.

road.name: Ruth Creek Road / Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Service Road 32)
road.status: Open seasonally; reported potholes and flood-damage-limited access for larger/low-clearance vehicles
road.seasonalGate: Closed by snow in winter and into spring; check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts for current status
road.driveNote: From WA-542 (Mt. Baker Highway) near Glacier, WA, turn onto FS Road 32 just before the third Nooksack River bridge, then take the left fork onto Ruth Creek Road and continue to the road's end at Hannegan Campground/Trailhead (~5.4 mi on FS 32).

climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short alpine summer window (roughly July-September) typical of the North Cascades crest, which produces strong orographic precipitation.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; approach roads and the Easy Ridge/Chilliwack approach are typically still snow-covered into June or July.
bySeason.summer: The main climbing window; snow bridges and glacier conditions on approaches around the peak deteriorate as the season progresses, per multiple trip reports.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and the possibility of early season snow by September-October.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall, avalanche hazard, and road/trail closures make this outside the standard climbing season.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee for the backcountry permit; youth 15 and under are free
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for all overnight stays; reserve in advance via Recreation.gov or obtain a walk-up permit at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, WA
access.closures: Camp quotas apply at popular sites (e.g., Perfect Pass); check the NPS Wilderness Trip Planner for current zone availability

sectionBreakdown.note: Typically a multi-day (2+ day) backpack via Hannegan Pass, the Chilliwack River ford, and Easy Ridge

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Perfect Pass camp -> Whatcom Peak summit -> Perfect Pass camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Reported as roughly 90 minutes round trip from Perfect Pass camp, not cumulative time from the trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 360-676-6911 (non-emergency); 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245; NPS backcountry emergency line 360-873-4590
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA — the only hospital in Whatcom County
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage is reported in this part of the North Cascades NP backcountry; a satellite communicator is strongly recommended given the remote, multi-day approach.

routes.corrections: Elevation: multiple reputable sources (Wikipedia, mountain-forecast.com, and other aggregators citing USGS/Peakbagger data) list Whatcom Peak's summit at 7,574 ft, not 7,579 ft as given in the source data; highPointFt above uses 7,574 ft. Route identity: the standard easy route to the summit ascends from Perfect Pass on the south side (moderate snowfield + exposed Class 3 scramble along the south ridge) — guidebooks and trip reports call this the 'South Side'/'South Ridge' route. The Whatcom Glacier itself sits on the peak's northeast side and is more commonly used by parties bypassing/traversing around the peak between Whatcom Pass and Perfect Pass (e.g., en route to Mount Challenger) than as the summit line, so the catalog's combined label 'Southwest Route (Whatcom Glacier)' conflates two related but distinct lines; content above describes the south-side/Perfect Pass summit line specifically. Separately, cascadeclimbers.com documents a much harder, unrelated technical first ascent on Whatcom's SW ridge ('Imperfect Bypass,' IV 5.10+R, 2023) and a South Buttress route ('Castle in the Sky,' IV 5.10b TD, 2022) — these are distinct technical rock routes, not this Class 3/PD scramble route, and are not reflected in the fields above.

blurb: Frenzel Spitz (7,440 ft per Wikipedia, StephAbegg, and multiple peak databases — see corrections) is the westernmost and lowest of the three Crescent Creek Spires, alongside Himmelhorn and Ottohorn, in the remote Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park. Like the rest of the Pickets it is composed of biotite gneiss formed by metamorphism roughly 100 million years ago, giving it steep, often loose, rugged terrain. It was first climbed on September 10, 1961 by Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, and George Whitmore, part of a multi-summit Southern Pickets first-ascent campaign that day; the peak's name (along with Himmelgeisterhorn/Himmelhorn and Ottohorn) was reportedly taken from the label of a mustard bottle the Fireys carried on the trip.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, George Whitmore — September 10, 1961 (peak first ascent; AAC Publications' account of the 1961 Southern Pickets first-ascent campaign does not name the aspect climbed, but the date matches the peak FA and this is the only route on record)
objHaz: loose, exfoliating biotite gneiss typical of Picket Range rock
exposed Class 3 scrambling on steep, loose grass/rock ledges crossing the Barrier (~6,100 ft) en route to Crescent Creek Basin
multiple cliff bands on the forested descent, sometimes requiring tree rappels
faint, brush-choked climbers' trail that is easy to lose on both ascent and descent
uncertain creek crossing at Terror Creek (no reliable log bridge)
routes.overview: The South Route is the original 1961 line up Frenzel Spitz, the westernmost of the Crescent Creek Spires group (with Himmelhorn and Ottohorn) in the Southern Pickets — a Grade III, 5.6 alpine rock outing whose real crux is the remote, complex approach rather than the climbing itself.
routes.beta: Frenzel Spitz sits off the main Pickets crest between the Northern and Southern subranges, close under Ottohorn's north face; the 1961 first-ascent party reached it by descending roughly 500 feet from Ottohorn after climbing that peak the same day. It is rarely climbed in isolation — most parties link it with Himmelhorn, Ottohorn, and/or other Crescent Creek Basin objectives given the difficulty of getting there at all.
routes.approach: Access is via the Goodell Creek Trailhead (~600 ft) off SR 20 near Newhalem. Follow the old roadbed along Goodell Creek for about 4 miles to a stone cairn/arrow marking the Terror Creek Basin turnoff (~1,700 ft); from there a boot-beaten, often-fading climbers' trail climbs steeply through timber, breaking out into meadows and slabs near 5,000-6,400 ft. To reach Crescent Creek Basin (below Frenzel Spitz, Himmelhorn, and Ottohorn) parties either cross the Barrier — a steep, loose, exposed Class 3 traverse/scramble around 6,100 ft — from Terror Basin, or follow a separate, even fainter Crescent Creek climbers' trail that splits off before the Terror Basin turn and climbs the steep timbered ridge between Terror and Crescent Creeks.
routes.bail: There is no quick bail off Frenzel Spitz or out of Crescent Creek Basin — retreat means re-crossing the Barrier's loose, exposed terrain or descending the steep, cliff-banded, brush-choked climbers' trail back toward Terror or Goodell Creek, both slow and hazard-prone in poor weather or light.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, complex, and partly unmaintained approach/retreat (loose Barrier scrambling, cliff bands, a fading brush trail), build in a generous margin and turn back with enough daylight to clear the technical descent terrain before dark rather than pushing a summit attempt against the clock.
routes.bestSeason: Trip reports for this corner of the Southern Pickets cluster from July through September (the peak's 1961 FA was September 10; nearby Crescent Creek Basin objectives have been climbed in July and August in recent years). Snow can linger on the upper approach into early summer, and the range is essentially inaccessible in winter once SR 20 closes.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage is typical in this remote North Cascades National Park backcountry; a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly advised.
proTips: The Terror/Crescent Creek trail turnoff is an easy-to-miss stone cairn near the end of the ~4-mile Goodell Creek roadbed — watch for it closely.
Water and shade are scarce on the brushy, blueberry-choked ridge crest above timberline; carry enough for a long, hot approach day.
The climbers' trails into Terror/Crescent Creek basins are unmaintained and shift year to year — recent trip reports (StephAbegg.com, Dr. Dirtbag) are the best current beta.
watchOut: The Barrier crossing is serious, exposed Class 3 scrambling on steep, loose grass and rock around 6,100 ft — not a place to slip.
The forested descent below the Barrier crosses several cliff bands; some parties have needed tree rappels to get through.
The approach trail fades in and out above timberline, and many parties have gotten lost on both ascent and descent.
knownHazards: Loose, exfoliating biotite gneiss is characteristic of Picket Range rock and can make scrambling terrain unstable.
The Terror Creek crossing has no reliable log bridge and may require wading.
The Southern Pickets are among the most remote ranges in the Lower 48 — an accident here means a long, difficult self-rescue or SAR response.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Goodell Creek Trailhead
road.status: Paved highway open seasonally; the trailhead/campground sit directly off SR 20 a few miles east of Newhalem
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes for winter (conditions-based, typically early Dec through Apr/May) between roughly Ross Dam (MP 134) and the Silver Star Gate (MP 171); closure points can extend further west/east (Newhalem MP 120 / Early Winters MP 178) later in the season
road.driveNote: Goodell Creek Trailhead is reached directly off SR 20 near Newhalem; no unpaved approach road is required.

climate.forecastZone: NWS North Cascades National Park mountain zone forecast
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and cooler, drier but still unsettled summers typical of the North Cascades; the Pickets sit in one of the most heavily glaciated, snow-loaded parts of the range.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack typically persists into June/July on the upper approach and basins.
bySeason.summer: Jul-Sep is the primary climbing window, though lingering snow patches and afternoon thunderstorms are common.
bySeason.fall: Conditions deteriorate quickly after September as storms return and SR 20 closure looms.
bySeason.winter: Essentially inaccessible for casual climbing once SR 20's seasonal closure cuts off the Goodell Creek Trailhead.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 processing fee for the backcountry/wilderness permit; visitors 15 and under are free
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required year-round for overnight stays; reserve via Recreation.gov, watch the mandatory safety video, and email noca_wilderness@nps.gov up to 3 days before the trip for ranger review and permit issuance
access.closures: SR 20 (the only road access to the Goodell Creek Trailhead) closes seasonally in winter due to avalanche hazard, typically early December through April/May depending on conditions

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Goodell Creek Trailhead (~600 ft) to Terror/Crescent Creek basin camp (~6,200 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: ~4 mi old roadbed, then a steep boot-beaten climbers' trail through timber breaking into meadows/slabs near 5,000-6,400 ft; figure drawn from recent trip reports (StephAbegg.com, Dr. Dirtbag) using the same Goodell Creek corridor to nearby Southern Pickets objectives, not a Frenzel Spitz-specific report.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Skagit County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (360) 416-1911 (business hours) / (360) 428-3211 (after hours)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, is also within range)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in this remote NCNP wilderness area; carry a satellite communicator or PLB. Report emergencies via 911 or the NPS Law Enforcement line (360) 854-7255 for non-emergencies.

routes.corrections: The peak's elevation is given as 7,482 ft, but Wikipedia, StephAbegg.com, and other peak references consistently list Frenzel Spitz at 7,440 ft — used as highPointFt here. No source found documents this route's aspect/face as literally 'south'; 'S' is inferred from the route's name pending a guidebook (e.g., Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide Vol. 3) confirmation, which was not accessible online. No pitch count, route length, rack, or descent-specific beta could be sourced for this route specifically — those fields are left null rather than estimated.

blurb: Mount Crowder is a remote 7,082-foot summit in the Picket Range of the North Cascades, within North Cascades National Park and the Stephen Mather Wilderness in Whatcom County. It sits on the ridge south of Phantom Peak between Goodell and Picket creeks, is built of the biotite gneiss typical of the Picket Range's crystalline core, and was first climbed (via its north ridge over an adjacent 7,020-ft sub-peak) by a Mountaineers party led by Cal Magnusson with Jack Ardussi, Don Mech, and Don Schmechel; the peak was then known as "Old Brownie" and was renamed in 1970 to honor USGS geologist-mountaineer Dwight F. Crowder (1929-1970).

routes.corrections: Sources conflict on the peak's first-ascent year: Wikipedia and Peakbagger list 1962, but the primary source — the American Alpine Journal (1963 edition, Northern Pickets report) — documents the summit reached on August 9, 1963, by Ardussi, Magnusson, Mech, and Schmechel via 'the north ridge over the top of the adjacent peak (7020 feet),' not via a southwest line. Because the documented first-ascent route was the north ridge (not this Southwest Route), 'fa' is left null for this route rather than attributing the FA to the SW line; the peak-level FA party is as described in the blurb. Given rockGrade/pitches/routeFt were not independently confirmed and are left null; grade 'Class 4' and alpineGrade 'PD' are plausible for this scramble but were not directly corroborated by a source giving an explicit numeric grade, so they are left as originally provided rather than being overridden.
routes.overview: A remote, non-technical alpine scramble up Crowder's southwest flank from heather-bench camps on the west side of the peak. It is the standard, easiest way to the summit of this seldom-visited Picket Range peak and is usually climbed as one objective within a longer Northern Pickets traverse rather than as a standalone trip.
routes.beta: The Southwest Route climbs blocky gneiss and steep heather benches on Crowder's SW flank to the summit ridge. There is no trailhead approach; nearly all parties reach the mountain via a multi-day cross-country approach (Hannegan Pass-Easy Ridge-Perfect Pass, or Big Beaver-Whatcom Pass) as part of a broader Picket Range outing. Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide description of the route is minimal, and even Beckey himself was reportedly unsure of its exact source/origin, so treat published beta as approximate.
routes.approach: No direct trailhead reaches Crowder. A common approach starts at the Hannegan Pass Trailhead (end of FS Road 32 off Mt. Baker Highway/SR 542), follows the Hannegan Pass Trail toward Easy Ridge, crosses the 'Imperfect Impasse' chasm to reach Perfect Pass, then continues cross-country toward Crowder's west side, where heather benches provide camping below the southwest flank. An alternate approach follows the Big Beaver Trail to Whatcom Pass and around to the Wild Lake basin below the peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the southwest flank scramble back to the heather-bench camps for a round trip; parties continuing a Northern Pickets traverse instead descend via the steep northeast ridge or a documented bypass around the north side of the peak.
routes.descentText: For a simple round trip, downclimb the southwest flank the way you ascended, back to the heather-bench camps. Traverse parties bound for Swiss Peak/Access Creek instead descend Crowder's northeast ridge, described in a 2012 trip report as grassy ledges and loose rock split by a large chasm (possibly a fault) — class 3-4 with brief class 5 downclimbing, 'don't-slip-here terrain.' A separate published account describes a north-side bypass first pieced together by a USGS survey party: from camp, carefully backing down a roughly 45-degree snow gully to a sloping bench that leads around Crowder's north side, per the out-of-print guide 'Routes and Rocks in the Mt. Challenger Quadrangle' by Tabor and Crowder.
routes.bail: From the southwest flank, retreat is straightforward — downclimb back to the heather-bench camps the way you came. Parties who have already committed to the northeast ridge or the north-side snow-gully bypass have a much harder time reversing course and should expect to continue rather than bail.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and descent options off Crowder (loose ridges, a steep snow gully, wet heather) are all more hazardous than the ascent line itself, set a turnaround time that gets the whole party off the summit and back onto easier ground well before afternoon weather, fatigue, or failing light complicate the descent.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most favorable weather and driest heather/rock on this peak, per regional climate data for the North Cascades high country; earlier in the season the SW flank's heather and gullies are more likely to hold snow or be wet, which the standard trip report for this route calls outright dangerous.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Picket Range backcountry; a satellite communicator/messenger is recommended for any Northern Pickets objective.
routes.detailedRack: Not a technical rock route — most parties carry little to no rack. A short length of rope, a few slings, and basic snow/scramble gear (ice axe, helmet, light crampons or microspikes for lingering snow) cover the terrain encountered on the SW flank and any descent variation.
routes.proNeeds: Essentially no fixed or placed protection is used; the line is a scramble on heather, dirt, and blocky gneiss. Any rope carried is for occasional handline/rappel use on the steeper snow or downclimbing sections rather than sustained pitched protection.
whatToBring: multi-day backpacking/bivy gear for the cross-country approach
route description (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide Vol. 3, or Tabor & Crowder's 'Routes and Rocks in the Mt. Challenger Quadrangle')
map and compass/GPS for cross-country navigation
proTips: Camp on the heather benches on Crowder's west side to break the summit push into a shorter day, as documented in a published account of a Northern Pickets traverse.
Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide description of this route is minimal — treat it as a rough pointer rather than precise beta and be ready to route-find.
Plan the trip for dry conditions; the SW flank's steep heather is reported as genuinely dangerous when wet.
watchOut: Steep heather on the southwest flank becomes 'downright dangerous' when there is any moisture on it, per a 2012 trip report.
The northeast ridge descent alternative has loose rock, grassy ledges, and a major chasm/fault line — described as 'don't-slip-here terrain.'
A roughly 45-degree snow gully guards the documented north-side bypass around the peak and requires careful, controlled downclimbing.
knownHazards: Loose, blocky gneiss typical of the Picket Range.
Remote, seldom-traveled terrain with difficult and slow self-rescue.
No cell coverage; help is far away if something goes wrong.
Route-finding hazards approaching via Easy Ridge, including the exposed 'Imperfect Impasse' chasm crossing.

pitchDetail.notes: Multi-day cross-country approach via Easy Ridge/Perfect Pass or Whatcom Pass to heather-bench camps on Crowder's west side.
Ascend steep heather slopes on the southwest flank; easy in dry conditions, hazardous when wet (per 2012 trip report of this line).
Final scrambling to the summit ridge and top.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (FS Road 32)
road.status: Seasonal USFS gravel access road, generally open during the summer climbing season
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive east on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) about 12.5 miles, then turn onto Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32) and continue 5.3 miles to the Hannegan Pass Trailhead.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate typical of the North Cascades high country, with wet winters and a short, drier alpine season centered on midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Snowpack lingers on approach slopes and gullies well into June.
bySeason.summer: July-September is the most favorable window for this route, with the best chance of dry heather and snow-free scrambling.
bySeason.fall: Weather deteriorates and days shorten from late September; early snow can return to the high country.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snowfall and long approaches make this a non-objective in winter.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park, Stephen Mather Wilderness); trailhead access is via Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest.
access.fees: No park entrance fee for climbing; a USFS Recreation Pass (Washington State Discover Pass not accepted) must be displayed at the Hannegan Pass Trailhead.
access.permit: Free backcountry/climbing permit required for overnight stays in North Cascades National Park, issued at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: USFS Recreation Pass / NW Forest Pass at the Hannegan Pass Trailhead.
access.closures: A fixed-anchor moratorium is in effect throughout the Stephen Mather Wilderness (no new bolts/fixed gear); standard NPS wilderness backcountry regulations apply.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: 360-676-6911 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA — the nearest 24-hour hospital, roughly a 90-minute drive from the park
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Picket Range backcountry; North Cascades NP climbing rangers coordinate search and rescue with the Whatcom County Sheriff's Office. Carry a satellite communicator.

blurb: The Chopping Block (officially named Pinnacle Peak, elevation 6,819 ft) is a Skagit-Gneiss summit in the Southern Picket Range, North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA, sitting at the end of the Barrier ridge that separates the Terror and Crescent Creek basins. It offers a panoramic view of the Southern Pickets from West McMillan Spire to Mount Terror and was first climbed in 1932 by Seattle Mountaineers climbers William Degenhardt and Herbert (H.V.) Strandberg during their early exploration of the range.

routes.fa: William Degenhardt & Herbert (H.V.) Strandberg, 1932 (Seattle Mountaineers) — the peak's original line, climbed the summer after the pair first glimpsed the Southern Pickets in 1931
objHaz: loose rock in the south-side access gully
exposed low-5th-class moves near the top
chokestones/moats in nearby gullies (route-finding hazard for variants)
routes.season: May-Oct (per Mountaineers.org); most comfortable once the approach brush and any snow have cleared, roughly Jul-Sep
routes.overview: The South Route follows a loose access gully and then the 'obvious line' up the Chopping Block's sloping south face, staying near the peak's east side through a couple of short, low-5th-class (5.4) exposed steps to reach the summit plateau — the peak's easiest and most commonly climbed line.
routes.beta: From a camp in the Terror or Crescent Creek basin, cross onto the triangular plateau at the base of the Barrier and continue toward the Chopping Block's south side. A loose but moderate gully gains the base of the sloping south face; existing rappel tat on this face confirms the line. A couple of short, exposed sections (low 5th class) lead through the upper face; staying near the east side of the peak leads most directly to the summit plateau, where a summit register is kept in a cylinder. Descend by downclimbing the same line to a heather traverse back toward the Barrier crossing.
routes.approach: Drive SR 20 to Newhalem; the trailhead is on the north side of SR 20 across from the Newhalem/Goodell Creek campground — take an immediate left after crossing Goodell Creek, keep left past the gravel pit, and park at the road's end by the creek. Follow the old Goodell Creek logging road, then pick up a climbers' trail that leaves Goodell Creek to climb through the woods south of Terror Creek. The trail is faint and disintegrates in places; it eventually crosses Terror Creek (no reliable log bridge — expect to ford, reported calf-deep in summer), then climbs an exceptionally steep, unmaintained 'Stump Hollow' trail straight up the ridge crest between Terror and Crescent Creeks. The brushy, blueberry-choked crest (no reliable water) leads to the sloping plateau forming the base of the Barrier, from which the Chopping Block's south side is reached.
routes.descent: Downclimb the same south-face line back to the heather ledge/traverse that leads north toward the Barrier; existing rap tat allows a short rappel through the exposed steps instead of downclimbing if preferred.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, reverse the ascent line down the east side of the sloping south face, using the existing fixed tat to rappel the short exposed low-5th-class steps if desired (most parties downclimb). Continue down the loose access gully to the base, then pick up the heather traverse leading north back toward the Barrier crossing and camp in the Terror or Crescent Creek basin.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as an overnight (or multi-day) trip: hike the Goodell Creek road and Terror/Crescent Creek climbers' trail to a camp in the Terror or Crescent Creek basin on day one (a long, steep day with brush and route-finding), climb the Chopping Block's South Route (often combined with other Southern Pickets objectives) on day two, then hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is essentially reversing the route — downclimb the ascent line; the short low-5th-class steps have existing rap tat for a quick rappel if downclimbing feels unsafe. There is no easier alternate descent off this face.
routes.turnaround: The approach alone is a long, route-finding-dependent day over steep brush, a creek ford, and talus with no reliable water on the ridge crest — set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to reverse the entire approach, since cell coverage for calling for help is unreliable to absent in this area.
routes.bestSeason: The Mountaineers list a May-October season for this peak; in practice the brushy, exposed approach ridge and access gully are most reasonable once seasonal snow has cleared, roughly July through September.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the Southern Pickets/Goodell Creek drainage; NPS guidance for the park advises against relying on cell phones for emergency communication here and recommends a satellite messenger or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack is sufficient — the route is mostly 3rd/4th-class terrain with only a couple of short, low-5th-class (5.4) exposed steps. A small set of nuts/cams plus a few slings for the tat-marked sections covers it; many parties treat most of the climb as an unroped scramble with a rope carried for the exposed steps.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear needs — natural protection (slings on horns/chockstones) plus a small rack for the brief 5.4 section; existing fixed tat marks short raps for those who prefer rappelling over downclimbing the exposed steps.
whatToBring: Water treatment (no reliable water on the brushy ridge crest)
Approach shoes/boots suited to steep brush, talus, and a creek ford
Satellite communicator or PLB given unreliable cell coverage
Bear canister if camping overnight (required in certain NPS zones)
proTips: The line stays near the east side of the peak's south face — following it (rather than gullies farther west) leads most directly to the summit plateau.
Existing rappel tat on the south face is a good confirmation you're on the standard line if you're following the 'obvious' route.
Most parties approach via a camp in the Terror Creek or Crescent Creek basin rather than attempting the brutal Stump Hollow approach car-to-car.
watchOut: The Stump Hollow approach trail up the Terror-Crescent Creek ridge is exceptionally steep, and trip reports describe rappelling through cliff bands when the climbers' trail is lost on descent.
The ridge crest is choked with blueberry brush and has no reliable water — carry enough for the dry stretch.
There is no bridge across Terror Creek; expect to ford (reported calf-deep in summer).
Nearby gullies on adjacent Southern Pickets summits are chossy and moated with chokestones — stick to the known south-face line rather than improvising a variation.
knownHazards: Loose rock in the south-side access gully and on the approach ridge.
Persistent route-finding difficulty — the climbers' trail disintegrates repeatedly above Terror Creek and on the brushy ridge crest.
Exposed low-5th-class moves on the upper south face, largely reliant on natural protection.
Remote location deep in the Southern Pickets with unreliable-to-absent cell coverage for emergencies.

pitchDetail.notes: A loose but moderate gully on the south side gains the base of the sloping south face.

Follow the 'obvious line' up the sloping south face; a couple of short, exposed low-5th-class steps; stay near the east side of the peak to reach the summit plateau. Existing rappel tat marks the line.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Newhalem
road.status: Paved highway; the Newhalem section is west of the winter closure gates and stays open year-round for vehicle access
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes seasonally each winter east of Newhalem (typically late Nov through Apr/May) at Ross Dam/Newhalem and Early Winters gates; the Goodell Creek trailhead itself, at Newhalem, is unaffected by this closure
road.driveNote: From Newhalem, cross Goodell Creek and turn onto the old logging road on the north side of SR 20 (across from the Newhalem/Goodell Creek campground); keep left past the gravel pit and park at the road's end by the creek.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate; snow-free access to the peak generally requires waiting until early-to-mid summer given the north-facing approach brush and lingering snow in the Barrier/basin terrain.

bySeason.spring: Significant snow typically still covers the approach ridge and basins; not the normal season for this route.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season once brush and snow allow passage of the approach ridge and gully (roughly Jul-Sep).
bySeason.fall: Climbable into September/October per the Mountaineers' listed season, weather permitting, before early winter snow returns.
bySeason.winter: Not a winter objective; SR 20 access beyond Newhalem is also closed.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance or parking fee at the Goodell Creek trailhead (within the NPS complex, not National Forest land); a backcountry permit fee of $10/person plus a $6 reservation fee applies for any overnight camping/bivy.
access.permit: A backcountry permit is required year-round for any overnight stay in the park, climbs included. Reserve via Recreation.gov, watch the mandatory safety video, and pick up the permit (about 60% of permits are reservable in advance; the rest held for walk-ups at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount).
access.passRequired: No Northwest Forest Pass or entrance pass needed for day parking at Goodell Creek (NPS complex trailhead); only the paid backcountry permit applies for overnight trips.

timing.recommendedStart: Most parties camp in the Terror or Crescent Creek basin rather than attempting the climb car-to-car, given the long, steep, brushy, route-finding-heavy approach.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit area); Skagit County (Newhalem trailhead approach)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff dispatch: 360-336-9450; Whatcom County Sheriff dispatch: 360-384-5390; 911 also connects but may route through a distant dispatch center in the backcountry
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — climbing/wilderness info; park communications center after-hours at 360-873-4590 ext. 37; park main line 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III Emergency Dept.), 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA, 360-424-4111 (roughly 1.5-2 hr drive from Newhalem); PeaceHealth United General Hospital ED in Sedro-Woolley is closer but smaller
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable to absent through most of the park; NPS advises against relying on it as a sole evacuation plan and recommends a satellite communicator or PLB for remote objectives like the Southern Pickets.

routes.corrections: The existing record lists the peak's elevation as 6,809 ft; the best-supported figure is 6,819 ft, per Wikipedia's infobox and The Mountaineers' page for this peak ('High Point: 6,819 ft'). Mountain Project's area page separately lists 6,739 ft for its route-finder GPS point, which appears to be a less precise/older geocode and is not used here. The peak is officially named Pinnacle Peak on USGS maps; 'The Chopping Block' is the common climbers' name used almost universally in trip reports and guides. Note Mountain Project documents a distinct, harder 'Northeast Ridge' route (5.5, Grade II, 4 pitches) on this same peak — not to be confused with the easier South Route covered here, which corresponds to the peak's original 1932 line up the south-side gullies/face.

blurb: Sitkum Spire is a minor rock/pumice outcrop (roughly 9,355-9,412 ft, with only about 28 m/92 ft of prominence per Peakbagger) on the west summit ridge of Glacier Peak in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, North Cascades. It separates the Sitkum Glacier on its south side from the steep, heavily crevassed Scimitar Glacier to the north, and sits directly on the standard Sitkum Glacier approach route to Glacier Peak's true summit (10,541 ft) — most parties pass the spire via a saddle at roughly 9,500 ft rather than tag its actual summit block. No dedicated first-ascent record for the spire itself was found in available sources.

objHaz: Crevasses on the Sitkum Glacier (roped travel standard)
Steep, heavily crevassed Scimitar Glacier immediately north of the spire
Loose rock on the moraine below the glacier
Unstable pumice on the summit ridge
routes.face: West summit ridge of Glacier Peak, between the Sitkum Glacier (S) and Scimitar Glacier (N)
routes.overview: A minor rock/pumice summit on Glacier Peak's west ridge, reached by climbing the Sitkum Glacier from Boulder Basin to a saddle just east of the spire, then ascending the loose pumice ridge; most parties pass beside the spire's actual summit block on their way to Glacier Peak's true summit rather than climbing the spire itself.
routes.beta: From Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), head SE onto the Sitkum Glacier, staying in the corridor left of the feature that divides its lower section, then trend NE across the glacier to a saddle at roughly 9,500 ft just east of Sitkum Spire. From the saddle, the standard line follows the pumice Sitkum ridge upward, keeping right of and slightly below the true summit toward a snow chute. Rope up for glacier travel; carpet of loose pumice makes the final ridge unpleasant underfoot. The route is described on Mountain Project as Grade III, Easy Snow, with ice axe and crampons the only gear most parties report using.
routes.approach: Start at the White Chuck River Trailhead (end of FS Road 23) near Darrington. Hike roughly 5.5 miles to the former Kennedy Hot Springs area (~3,300 ft; the hot springs themselves were destroyed in a November 2003 flood), then about 1.5 more miles to Sitkum Creek and the Pacific Crest Trail junction (~3,850 ft). Follow the PCT north about 0.5 mile to a forested campsite (~4,100 ft), then take the steep climbers' path (sometimes called the Boulder Basin Trail) up a ridge south of Sitkum Creek to Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), the standard base camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down the pumice ridge to the saddle, back down the Sitkum Glacier to Boulder Basin, then out the climbers' path to the PCT and White Chuck River Trail.
routes.descentText: From the saddle east of Sitkum Spire, descend the same pumice ridge and glacier corridor used on ascent back to Boulder Basin. Glacier travel should be roped both directions; crevasse bridges are more likely to be softened and less trustworthy on the return in the afternoon. From Boulder Basin, retrace the climbers' path down to the PCT junction and out the White Chuck River Trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a 2-day trip: Day 1, hike in to Boulder Basin and set up camp; Day 2, pre-dawn (~4 a.m.) start up the Sitkum Glacier to the saddle and ridge, then descend back to Boulder Basin and out, or camp a second night and hike out the following day.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ascent line back to the saddle and down the Sitkum Glacier to Boulder Basin. Do not attempt to escape north onto the Scimitar Glacier, which is steep and heavily crevassed.
routes.turnaround: Glacier conditions on the Sitkum Glacier soften through the morning; parties report starting around 4 a.m. to cross while snow and crevasse bridges are still firm. Build in a turnaround time that gets the party off the glacier before afternoon softening, and be prepared to retreat if visibility drops on the broad, featureless upper glacier and ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Mountain-Forecast lists May-August as the general climbing window for Glacier Peak. Documented Sitkum Glacier trip reports were strongest in July, when the glacier had firm, climbable snow; an early-season (May) attempt found unconsolidated snow and difficult travel conditions. Late June through September is the most consistently reported window for this route.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: ice axe, crampons, and a rope for the Sitkum Glacier crossing with a crevasse rescue kit. No dedicated technical rock rack is reported for the standard line, since most parties bypass technical rock on the spire's actual summit block.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
Tent/bivy gear for the Boulder Basin camp
Water treatment (glacial silt in melt sources)
proTips: Camp at Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft) to split the approach into two manageable days.
Start around 4 a.m. from Boulder Basin to cross the Sitkum Glacier before the snow softens.
Confirm you're on the correct (climber's-right) moraine approaching the glacier — parties have lost significant time after mistakenly taking the left moraine into unstable terrain.
watchOut: Hidden crevasses on the upper Sitkum Glacier, especially later in the season.
The Scimitar Glacier immediately north of the spire is steep and heavily crevassed — stay off it.
Loose, unconsolidated pumice on the final summit ridge.
knownHazards: Crevasse fall hazard on the Sitkum Glacier; unroped travel is not recommended.
Rockfall and loose talus/pumice on the ridge leading to the saddle and spire.
Rapid weather changes and whiteout risk on the broad, featureless glacier and ridge.

road.name: White Chuck River Road (FS Road 23)
road.status: Closed as of the 2025-2026 seasons between milepost 3.7 and its junction with FS Road 27 (Forest Order #06-05-25-02), due to flood damage and proximity to the river.
road.driveNote: The road has a long history of storm-damage closures: washed out by a November 2003 flood (closed ~8 years), reopened October 2011 only as far as just past the Rat Trap Pass/FS 27 junction (the remainder to the original trailhead was decommissioned and will not reopen), then closed again after December 2024/2025 flooding. Confirm current status with the Darrington Ranger District before driving.

climate.typical: Mountain-Forecast lists May-August as the typical climbing window for Glacier Peak; the route mixes snow/glacier travel with routine crevasse hazard on the Sitkum and Scimitar glaciers.

bySeason.spring: Cold and often unconsolidated; a May ski/climb attempt found difficult, unstable skinning conditions on approach.
