bySeason.summer: Firm snow in early morning that softens by midday to early afternoon; the main reported climbing window (Jun-Sep).
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows; early snowfall can close approach roads and trails.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: No fee or permit required to climb or camp on Glacier Peak; a Northwest Forest Pass is required to park at the trailhead.
access.permit: No formal climbing permit; wilderness trailhead self-registration is typical.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: White Chuck River Road (FS 23) is closed for the 2025-2026 season due to December 2024/2025 flood damage (Forest Order #06-05-25-02); check current Darrington Ranger District alerts before approaching.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (~4 a.m.) departure from Boulder Basin camp to cross the Sitkum Glacier while snow and crevasse bridges are firm.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: White Chuck River Trailhead -> Sitkum Creek/PCT junction (~3,850 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: About 7 miles of maintained trail past the former Kennedy Hot Springs site, per Mountain Project.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: PCT campsite (~4,100 ft) -> Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Steep unmaintained climbers' path, sometimes called the Boulder Basin Trail.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Boulder Basin -> saddle east of Sitkum Spire (~9,500 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Roped travel across the Sitkum Glacier.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (425) 388-3393; Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue coordinated via the Sheriff's Office at (425) 388-3328.
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155.
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (Level IV ER), (360) 435-2133.
emergency.notes: White Chuck River Road has a history of repeated storm-damage closures (2003-2011, and again 2025-2026) that can cut vehicle access to the trailhead and lengthen response times; the approach and glacier are remote with no cell infrastructure along the route.

routes.corrections: Elevation conflict: Peakbagger and skimountaineer.com list Sitkum Spire at 9,355 ft (2,851 m) with only 28 m (92 ft) of prominence, versus the 9,412 ft in this dataset — the dataset figure was retained but the discrepancy is flagged here. The seed alpineGrade 'AD' could not be corroborated by any source found and was nulled; Mountain Project grades the Sitkum Glacier line (which passes the spire's saddle en route to Glacier Peak's true 10,541 ft summit) as 'Grade III, Easy Snow' — used here as the commitment grade. No source documents a dedicated technical grade for actually topping out Sitkum Spire's own summit block specifically (as opposed to passing it); the given 'Class 4 / low 5th' rock grade is plausible given the terrain described but is unverified by an explicit source, since most parties bypass the spire's true summit via the saddle rather than climb it.

blurb: Dome Peak (8,926 ft) is a massive, heavily glaciated summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Washington's North Cascades, at the extreme southeast corner of Skagit County, marking the southern terminus of the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. Built of quartz diorite, it has two rock summits linked by a narrow, exposed connecting ridge (the crux move is nicknamed "the Cheval") and is ringed by the Dome, Chickamin, and Dana glaciers. The higher northeast summit was first climbed by George Freed and Eric Larson on August 1, 1936, about three weeks after Forest Farr, Norval Grigg, and Don Blair topped the slightly lower southwest summit on July 5, 1936.

routes.rockGrade: 4th class (exposed 3rd–4th class summit-ridge scramble; Mountain Project grades the technical portion 4th class / Grade II)
routes.iceGrade: Optional M1b mixed step reported on the glacier in some seasons/conditions
objHaz: avalanche-prone slopes on the early-season approach
exposed unroped scrambling on the summit ridge
sustained bushwhacking on the unmaintained Bachelor Creek Trail
routes.face: Standard line via Itswoot Ridge and the (west-side) Dome Glacier, topping out at a notch/col roughly 8,560–8,600 ft just north of the true summit
routes.overview: A glacier mountaineering line up Dome Peak's west-side Dome Glacier, gaining a notch just north of the summit before finishing on an exposed 3rd–4th class scramble along the summit ridge to the peak's famous "Cheval" — a straddled, low-holds move linking the two summit horns.
routes.beta: The standard route follows the long Downey Creek/Bachelor Creek approach to Cub Lake and Itswoot Ridge, then descends to cross the Dome Glacier on a rising traverse toward the ~8,560–8,600 ft col just north of the summit. From the col, a short snow slope gains the summit ridge, followed east through exposed but moderate (3rd–4th class) terrain to the true (northeast) summit, crossing the narrow connecting ridge's "Cheval" move between the two summit horns. Most parties rope up for glacier travel and carry a light rock rack (slings, a cam or two) for the final ridge.
routes.approach: From the Downey Creek Trailhead (1,440 ft) on the Suiattle River Road, follow Downey Creek Trail #768 roughly 6–6.5 miles to "Sixmile Camp," then turn onto the unmaintained, often heavily overgrown Bachelor Creek Trail. Continue through brush, avalanche debris, and meadows past a forest camp (~3.5 miles up Bachelor Creek) to Cub Lake (~5,338 ft, ~12 miles from the trailhead). From Cub Lake, climb onto Itswoot Ridge (good camps with water) before dropping to traverse snow, slabs, and scree toward the Dome Glacier at roughly 7,600 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the summit ridge to the col, recross the Dome Glacier, and retrace the Itswoot Ridge / Cub Lake / Bachelor Creek / Downey Creek approach back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, downclimb the exposed 3rd–4th class ridge back across the Cheval to the notch, then descend the short snow slope onto the Dome Glacier. Re-cross the glacier (roped, watching for the bergschrund and any crevasses that have opened since the ascent) to regain Itswoot Ridge, then descend to Cub Lake and reverse the Bachelor Creek/Downey Creek trail approach. Parties commonly allow 1.5+ hours from the summit back to high camp, plus a long full day to hike out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: A common plan is 3 days: Day 1 — hike the Downey Creek/Bachelor Creek trails to Cub Lake or a high camp on Itswoot Ridge (a long day given the overgrown Bachelor Creek section); Day 2 — climb from high camp across the Dome Glacier to the summit and return to camp; Day 3 — hike out to the trailhead. Strong parties sometimes compress this into a long 2-day push.
routes.bail: There is no fixed rappel/bail infrastructure on this route; retreat from anywhere on the glacier or summit ridge means reversing/downclimbing the line back to the col and down the Dome Glacier toward Itswoot Ridge.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and glacier crossing are long and the final ridge is exposed and unprotected in places, set a firm turnaround time before starting summit day — deteriorating weather, softening snow bridges, or a slow pace on the brushy approach are reasons to turn back rather than push into the col/summit ridge late in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly June through September, with conditions shifting significantly across the window: earlier season (May–June) snow can ease the bergschrund crossing and glacier travel but adds avalanche and creek-crossing hazard on the approach, while late season (August–September) melt-out exposes more crevasses and can leave the final approach to the col as talus/scree rather than snow.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse-rescue gear, crampons, ice axe) plus a light rock rack for the summit ridge — a handful of slings and one or two small-to-medium cams/nuts (one trip report notes a single 0.5" cam sufficed) to protect the exposed "Cheval" move and any short mixed steps.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is needed — the crux is exposure rather than difficulty. Slings for the Cheval move plus a small rack of nuts/small cams covers the rest; some parties rig a hand line or short fixed rope on the exposed ridge bulge near the summit.
whatToBring: bivy gear for a 2–3 day trip
bug protection for the brushy lower approach
extra water capacity for the Cub Lake / Itswoot Ridge camp
GPS track or careful notes for glacier route-finding
proTips: Camp at Cub Lake or on Itswoot Ridge to shorten summit day; Itswoot Ridge camps have water and cut real time off the push to the glacier.
Follow a GPS track or careful route-finding through the Dome Glacier's crevasse field — several trip reports note the safe line shifts through the season.
Rope up for the Cheval move on the summit ridge even though it's rated only 3rd–4th class — exposure, not difficulty, is the real hazard.
watchOut: The Bachelor Creek Trail section is unmaintained and heavily overgrown — budget significant extra time versus the maintained Downey Creek Trail.
The bergschrund below the summit col can be a major obstacle late season and has been reported as nearly impassable without a good snow bridge.
The final summit ridge scramble is narrow and exposed with real fall consequences despite its modest technical grade.
knownHazards: Glacier travel on the Dome Glacier involves real crevasse hazard; some crevasses are only safely passable via known snow bridges.
A bergschrund forms below the summit col and has been reported as spanning the full width of the glacier late in some seasons.
Avalanche-prone slopes are present on the approach to Itswoot Ridge/the glacier in early-season snow conditions.

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse the Dome Glacier from Itswoot Ridge to the ~8,560–8,600 ft notch/col just north of the summit; roped travel recommended, crevasses and a bergschrund present depending on season.
A short snow slope gains the summit ridge east of the true summit.
Narrow, exposed ridge scramble to the true (northeast) summit; crux is a straddled "cheval" move between the two summit horns — many parties place slings or a hand line here.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.status: Closed to motorized vehicles beyond milepost 4 due to major flood/washout damage; Downey Creek Trailhead itself is listed as "temporarily closed" for vehicle access as of the closure order
road.driveNote: Forest Order closure is effective April 2, 2026 through January 1, 2028 (unless rescinded sooner). From Darrington, FR 26 is driveable only to about milepost 4; reaching Downey Creek Trailhead (~mile 20) currently requires walking or biking the remaining road distance beyond the closure.

climate.typical: Deep North Cascades wilderness climate — wet, snowy winters and springs with a shorter, drier summer climbing window; weather can change quickly and whiteout conditions are possible even with a favorable forecast.

bySeason.spring: Heavy snowpack persists into June; avalanche-prone slopes on the Itswoot Ridge/glacier approach are a documented hazard.
bySeason.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul–Aug); glacier crevasses more open but generally passable, lower trail sections lush and often wet/overgrown.
bySeason.fall: Shorter weather windows into September; the bergschrund and glacier crevasses are most exposed after summer melt-out.
bySeason.winter: Not a realistic climbing objective — road and trail access effectively closed by snow and, currently, by the road washout.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.fees: $5 per vehicle day-use fee (or $30 annual pass) at Downey Creek Trailhead
access.permit: No special overnight permit beyond standard Glacier Peak Wilderness backcountry regulations; day-use fee payable via the Recreation.gov app (Scan & Pay) or a physical pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (for the day-use fee)
access.closures: Suiattle River Road (FR 26) closed to motorized vehicles beyond milepost 4 under a Forest Order effective April 2, 2026 – January 1, 2028 (unless rescinded sooner); Downey Creek Trailhead reachable only on foot or by bike beyond the closure as of 2026.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start from high camp on summit day, to cross the glacier while snow is firm and be off the exposed summit ridge before afternoon weather deteriorates

sectionBreakdown.section: Downey Creek Trailhead to Sixmile Camp
sectionBreakdown.note: ~6–6.5 miles on maintained trail; ~2 hours per one trip report

sectionBreakdown.section: Bachelor Creek Trail to Cub Lake
sectionBreakdown.note: Unmaintained, overgrown trail; one party took ~9.5 hours total from trailhead to Cub Lake camp
Highly variable by conditions and camp location; not consistently reported across sources
Allow 1.5+ hours from summit back to camp per one detailed trip report

emergency.county: Skagit County (summit area); approach and trailhead access via Snohomish County (Darrington)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 416-1911; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241 (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF, Darrington Ranger District)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — 24-hour Level III Emergency Department, (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Deep wilderness approach in the Glacier Peak Wilderness; expect no cell coverage on the approach or climb and long search-and-rescue response times given the remote, brushy, multi-day approach.

routes.corrections: Approach elevation-gain figures vary by source (~7,500 ft per ericsbasecamp.net, ~8,132 ft per WTA's official listing, ~8,400 ft per jeffreyjhebert.com); WTA's curated figure (8,132 ft) is used here. Round-trip distance is reported as ~30–31 miles depending on source and exact camp used, so a one-way distKm was left null rather than estimated. The input grade "Class 3, Glacier" understates the route: Mountain Project officially grades the technical summit-ridge portion 4th class (Grade II, ~450 ft), and multiple trip reports describe the final scramble as class 3–4 with an exposed, often-roped "Cheval" move; an optional M1b mixed step has been reported in some conditions. Route first ascent is unmarked/unknown per Mountain Project — do not confuse with the peak's first ascents (NE summit: Freed & Larson, Aug 1 1936; SW summit: Farr, Grigg & Blair, Jul 5 1936), which are not specific to this route line.

blurb: Dark Peak (8,518 ft, cited 8,504-8,520 ft across sources) is a remote, glaciated satellite summit on the high connecting ridge to 9,511 ft Bonanza Peak, the highest non-volcanic peak in the Cascade Range, within the "Stehekin Triangle" bounded by Lake Chelan, the Stehekin River, and Agnes Creek in Chelan County, Washington. Its crevassed Dark Glacier, which descends the peak's north side into Swamp Creek, forms the standard route; the mountain lies deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness and ranks 56th on Washington's Bulger List of the state's 100 highest peaks. No verifiable route-specific first-ascent record was found in available sources.

routes.overview: The standard route climbs the crevassed Dark Glacier on Dark Peak's north side to a short Class 2-3 scramble finish, reached via a long, remote multi-day approach from Stehekin through the Agnes Creek and Swamp Creek drainages.
routes.beta: A moderate glacier climb whose real difficulty is remoteness and access rather than technical terrain: a multi-day approach from Stehekin, brushy off-trail travel up Swamp Creek to a lower basin, then roped ascent of the crevassed Dark Glacier (the right side is reported as the cleanest line) to easy terrain and the summit.
routes.approach: From Stehekin (reached via the Lake Chelan ferry, about 2.5-4 hrs from Chelan, or by floatplane), take prearranged shuttle transport up the Stehekin Valley Road to the Agnes Creek trailhead at High Bridge (~1,700 ft). Follow the Agnes Creek Trail/PCT about 5 miles to Five Mile Camp (2,200 ft), then continue roughly 3 more miles to Swamp Creek Camp (2,800 ft). From Swamp Creek Camp pick up the signed Swamp Creek Trail, which switchbacks up about 800 ft over 1-1.5 miles before travel becomes cross-country up the brushy Swamp Creek drainage to the lower basin below the Dark Glacier (~4,300 ft, roughly 4-5 hours from Swamp Creek Camp). An alternate approach leaves the PCT near Hemlock Camp and climbs open forest onto Dark Peak's northwest ridge, avoiding some of the worst Swamp Creek brush.
routes.descent: Descend the same glacier and Swamp Creek drainage route used on ascent.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent: down-climb/rope down the Dark Glacier watching for crevasses, drop into the lower basin, then descend the brushy Swamp Creek drainage back to Swamp Creek Camp and out the Agnes Creek Trail to High Bridge for the return shuttle.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-day trip: Day 1 - ferry/shuttle to High Bridge and hike in to Swamp Creek Camp; Day 2 - climb through the lower basin, up the Dark Glacier, to the summit, and partway back down; Day 3 - hike out to High Bridge for the return shuttle and ferry.
routes.bail: Retreat down the Dark Glacier and back through the Swamp Creek drainage the way you came; there is no shorter bail-out option given the remote, off-trail nature of the route.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, brushy off-trail travel, and crevassed glacier crossing, set a firm turnaround time before the summit push so the glacier and lower-basin descent are not done in the dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed May through October. Early season (May-June) travel can be faster because snow covers much of the Swamp Creek brush and bridges glacier crevasses; late season exposes the brush and open crevasses but tends to bring more stable weather.
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage in the Stehekin Valley or the Agnes Creek/Swamp Creek backcountry; carry a satellite messenger (inReach/SPOT) given the remote, multi-day approach.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: harness, glacier rope, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks or mechanical ascenders, pulley, a picket or two), ice axe, and crampons. No fixed protection or rock rack is needed on the standard route.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel and crevasse-rescue protection only (rope, pickets/screws as conditions dictate); no rock protection is typically placed on the standard line.
whatToBring: Water filter or treatment for creek crossings
Sturdy brush-resistant layers/gaiters for the Swamp Creek bushwhack
Map/compass or GPS for routefinding through the lower basin
proTips: Early-season snow cover over the Swamp Creek brush and glacier crevasses can make the approach and climb noticeably faster than in a snow-free late-season attempt.
The right side of the Dark Glacier is reported by multiple parties as the cleanest line through the crevasses, though the best line depends on the year's conditions.
An alternate line leaving the PCT near Hemlock Camp and climbing open forest onto Dark Peak's northwest ridge avoids some of the worst Swamp Creek brush.
watchOut: Finding the log crossing of Swamp Creek and the correct exit from the lower basin are the cruxes of the approach; missing them leads to serious bushwhacking.
The Dark Glacier is crevassed and requires roped travel; crevasses can be well-hidden in early season and open up as the season progresses.
This is a remote, multi-day approach with no cell coverage and limited rescue access, so solid navigation and glacier travel skills are essential.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel on the Dark Glacier requires roped travel and crevasse-rescue skills.
Dense, difficult brush in the lower Swamp Creek drainage, worse once snow melts out later in the season.
Loose, lichen-covered rock on the connecting ridge toward Bonanza Peak for parties continuing the Dark-Bonanza traverse.

pitchDetail.notes: High Bridge to Swamp Creek Camp via the Agnes Creek Trail/PCT, about 8 miles
Off-trail ascent through brush to the lower basin below the Dark Glacier, roughly 4-5 hrs from Swamp Creek Camp
Roped glacier travel up the crevassed Dark Glacier; the right side is reported as the cleanest line
Short scramble from the top of the glacier to the summit

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road (to High Bridge)
road.status: Not reachable by outside road; access is by Lake Chelan ferry or floatplane to Stehekin, then a shuttle bus up the Stehekin Valley Road to the High Bridge trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Shuttle bus service is seasonal; the road beyond High Bridge (at Car Wash Falls) has been washed out and impassable to vehicles since 2003
road.driveNote: No public road connects Stehekin to the rest of Washington; the Lady of the Lake ferry from Chelan takes about 2.5-4 hours

climate.typical: East-side Cascades rain-shadow climate with warm, relatively dry summers; snow can linger on the Dark Glacier and in the upper basin into July

bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; best snow/crevasse-bridge conditions are typically in June before crevasses open up fully
bySeason.fall: Possible into October in good weather but with less predictable snow bridges and colder conditions

access.landManager: Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (NPS) near High Bridge, transitioning into the Glacier Peak Wilderness, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Chelan Ranger District)
access.fees: No trailhead fee; a Lake Chelan ferry or floatplane fare to Stehekin is required since no road connects Stehekin to the outside
access.permit: NPS backcountry/wilderness permit required for overnight camping in North Cascades National Park/Lake Chelan NRA, reservable via recreation.gov or obtained in person at the Golden West Visitor Center in Stehekin
access.passRequired: None at the trailhead itself; an NPS wilderness permit is required for overnight stays

timing.recommendedStart: An early start on summit day is recommended so the glacier crossing and brushy lower-basin descent are not done in failing light

sectionBreakdown.section: Swamp Creek Camp to lower basin
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Swamp Creek Camp (2,800 ft) to lower basin (~4,300 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Off-trail, brushy travel up the Swamp Creek drainage

sectionBreakdown.section: Lower basin to summit via Dark Glacier
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Lower basin (~4,300 ft) to summit (8,518 ft)
sectionBreakdown.note: Roped glacier travel plus a short Class 2-3 finish

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom Dispatch at 509-663-9911 (dispatches Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: Golden West Visitor Center / North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Stehekin
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health Hospital, Chelan, WA (24-hour ER; reachable from Stehekin only by boat, floatplane, or trail - no road)
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Stehekin Valley or the Agnes/Swamp Creek backcountry; a satellite communicator is strongly recommended given the remote, multi-day approach

routes.corrections: Peak elevation varies by source: 8,518 ft (given), 8,504 ft (Country Highpoints trip report), 8,513 ft (WTA), 8,507 ft (PeakVisor), 8,520 ft (ListsOfJohn) - all within normal survey/LiDAR variance, kept the given 8,518 ft. WTA lists the trailhead's managing agency as Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District, but USFS trail pages place the Agnes Creek/Swamp Creek drainage in the Glacier Peak Wilderness under the Chelan Ranger District, with the initial trail segment from High Bridge inside North Cascades National Park's Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (NPS); used the latter. Reported round-trip mileage also varies widely by source (WTA: 16.0 mi; a detailed trip report: 27.7 mi) depending on start point and exact line taken, so distKm was left null rather than guessed.

blurb: Sinister Peak (8,440+ ft) rises along the high glacier-carved divide of the North Cascades' Glacier Peak Wilderness, about a mile east of Dome Peak between the Chickamin and Garden glaciers. Like most of the range it is built of rugged, glacially sculpted granite and metamorphic rock, and it sits along a natural extension of the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. It was first climbed in 1939 by Lloyd Anderson, Jim Crooks, and Clint Kelley, and remains one of Washington's more remote "hundred highest" summits, guarded by long, brushy approaches.

objHaz: Crevasses on the Dome and Chickamin glaciers
Bergschrund at the Dome-Sinister col that can be difficult or impassable by late season
Loose rock in the summit gully
Exposed class 3+ scrambling on the upper west ridge
Wet-slide/avalanche potential on approach slopes in early season
routes.overview: From a high camp near the Dome-Chickamin col, the Southwest Route descends the Chickamin Glacier to the Dome-Sinister col, then climbs a loose gully on Sinister's south side to gain the upper west ridge, finishing with a class 2-3 scramble to the summit.
routes.beta: Most parties reach the Dome-Sinister col (~7,640 ft) by descending the Chickamin Glacier from a high camp near the Dome-Chickamin col and traversing right; a bergschrund near the col can be a late-season obstacle. From the col, climbers either take the exposed class 3+ ridge directly or wrap around to the south side and climb a loose, unpleasant gully to gain the upper west ridge, then follow class 2-3 terrain to the broad summit.
routes.approach: From the Downey Creek trailhead (end of the Suiattle River Road/FR 26), follow the Downey Creek Trail roughly 6.5 miles to Bachelor Creek, then the unmaintained, brushy Bachelor Creek climbers' path through Bachelor Meadows and over Cub Pass to Cub Lake (about 14 miles, 5,338 ft) — a common first camp. From Cub Lake, gain Itswoot Ridge and traverse east on snow and talus to the Dome Glacier (~7,600 ft), ascend and trend southwest across the glacier to a flat shelf near 8,000 ft, then continue to the Dome-Chickamin col (~8,450 ft) for high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down-climb the gully/ridge back to the Dome-Sinister col, then re-cross the Chickamin and Dome glaciers back to high camp.
routes.descentText: Descent follows the same line as the ascent — down-climb the loose gully and ridge terrain back to the Dome-Sinister col, then re-cross the bergschrund (where present) and traverse back over the Chickamin and Dome glaciers to high camp. There is no established rappel line on the standard route; parties down-climb roped for glacier travel and unroped on the scrambling sections in good conditions.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-4 day trip: Day 1, hike Downey Creek and Bachelor Creek trails to Cub Lake or Bachelor Meadows camp (a long day, reported at 12+ hours by one party); Day 2, continue over Itswoot Ridge and the Dome Glacier to a high camp near the Dome-Chickamin col; Day 3, climb Sinister Peak (often combined with nearby Dome Peak) and return to high camp; Day 4, hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the route: descend the gully/ridge back to the Dome-Sinister col and re-cross the glacier to camp. The loose gully and exposed ridge above the col are the main commitment points once past the col.
routes.turnaround: Because the route crosses crevassed glaciers and a bergschrund before any technical climbing begins, set a turnaround time based on weather, visibility, and party pace on the glacier crossing rather than the summit scramble itself — deteriorating weather or whiteout on the Dome/Chickamin glaciers is the more serious hazard than the terrain near the top.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through early July is widely reported as the sweet spot: enough snow remains to bridge the bergschrund at the Dome-Sinister col and cover the approach glaciers, while the summit gully is still reasonably firm. Some parties report good early-season (May) snow climbing conditions on nearby faces; by late summer the bergschrund can open significantly and the gully becomes looser and more rockfall-prone.
routes.detailedRack: Full glacier travel gear is required for the Dome and Chickamin glacier crossings: rope, harness, crevasse-rescue kit, crampons, and ice axe. The route itself is normally scrambled or simul-climbed rather than pitched out; some parties carry a small rack of light rock protection for the loose gully but a full trad rack is not standard.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is used on route; a rope is carried primarily for glacier travel and short belays through the gully rather than for pitched rock climbing.
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS for the unmaintained Bachelor Creek path
proTips: Time the trip for late June-early July when the Dome-Sinister col bergschrund is still snow-bridged
The Bachelor Creek trail is unmaintained — expect brush, blowdown, and route-finding below Bachelor Meadows
Many parties combine Sinister Peak with nearby Dome Peak from the same high camp
watchOut: Loose rock in the summit gully, especially with other parties above
The bergschrund at the Dome-Sinister col can become difficult or impassable by late summer
Crevasse hazard crossing the Dome and Chickamin glaciers
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel on the Dome and Chickamin glaciers
Loose, unpleasant rock in the ascent gully
Exposed class 3+ scrambling on the upper west ridge
Avalanche/wet-slide potential on approach slopes in early-season snow

pitchDetail.notes: Chickamin/Dome glacier crossing from high camp to the Dome-Sinister col (~7,640 ft); bergschrund possible near the col
South-side gully gaining the upper west ridge
Scramble along the upper west ridge to the summit

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.status: Closed to motor vehicles beyond approximately milepost 4 due to washout damage
road.seasonalGate: Historically a seasonal gravel forest road to the Downey Creek trailhead near road's end (~MP 20); current washout closure supersedes normal seasonal status
road.driveNote: From Darrington, drive Suiattle River Road (FR 26) east. As of the closure order effective April 2, 2026 (through January 1, 2028 unless rescinded sooner), motorized access ends around MP 4 due to flood/washout damage; reaching the Downey Creek trailhead requires hiking or biking the closed road beyond that point.

climate.typical: Marine west-slope Cascades climate: wet, heavy-snowfall winters and drier but changeable summers; extensive glacier and open-slope exposure means weather can shift quickly at elevation.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack on the approach trail and glaciers; avalanche and wet-slide hazard.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing window (Jun-Aug); generally the most stable weather, though afternoon convection is possible.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing new-snow risk by October.
bySeason.winter: Road and trail access effectively closed; heavy snow accumulation at elevation.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required to park at the Downey Creek trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Suiattle River Road (FR 26) closed to motor vehicles beyond ~MP 4 since an April 2, 2026 closure order (in effect through January 1, 2028) following a Dec 2025 atmospheric-river flood event; Downey Creek trailhead reachable only by foot or bike beyond the closure point.

sectionBreakdown.section: Downey Creek trailhead to Cub Lake camp
sectionBreakdown.note: One trip report logged this as a long first day (~12+ hours) via the Downey Creek and Bachelor Creek trails

emergency.county: Snohomish County (Darrington-area approach); the summit lies near the Skagit-Chelan county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 425-388-3393 (dial 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, 330 S. Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA (Level IV Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Remote multi-day wilderness approach with no expected cell coverage in the Downey/Bachelor Creek valleys; a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,444 ft) differs slightly from the figure used consistently across Wikipedia, Peakbagger, WTA, and multiple trip reports (8,440+ ft) — treat 8,440+ ft as the better-supported value. The peak (not necessarily this specific route) is reported first-climbed in 1939 by Lloyd Anderson, Jim Crooks, and Clint Kelley; secondary sources citing the same 1939 Mountaineer bulletin account disagree on the exact day (May 26 vs. May 29), so no specific date is asserted here.

blurb: Spire Point (8,264 ft) is a remote granite summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, rising along the classic Ptarmigan Traverse between Cub Lake/Itswoot Ridge and Dome Peak, with the Spire and Dana Glaciers on its northwest and east flanks. The peak offers several moderate technical rock routes on generally solid but lichen-covered granite, capped by a famously tiny, knife-edge summit that holds only one climber at a time. Its deep-wilderness setting — a full day or more from the nearest road — makes it one of the more committing objectives in the Washington Cascades.

routes.overview: A roughly 5-pitch, 5.6 line up Spire Point's southwest aspect on generally solid granite; it is one of only two established technical routes on the peak (with the East Face) and is reported to be the second most-climbed line to the summit.
routes.beta: Spire Point sits deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness along the Ptarmigan Traverse. The Southwest Face gives sustained low-5th-class granite climbing (5 full pitches at 5.6 per SummitPost) to the peak's tiny, extremely exposed summit horn, which seats only one climber at a time. Black lichen coats much of the rock and can make holds slick even when dry. Given the multi-day approach, most parties combine the climb with other Ptarmigan Traverse objectives (Dome Peak, Old Guard, Sentinel Peak, etc.) rather than visiting Spire Point alone.
routes.approach: From the Downey Creek Trailhead (1,440 ft, on Suiattle River Road/FR 26), hike the Downey Creek Trail and Bachelor Creek Trail toward Cub Lake, then follow the Ptarmigan Traverse route over Itswoot Ridge to a high camp near Cub Lake or Spire Col (~7,760 ft) below the peak's north side. The Southwest Face is reached by traversing around/below the peak toward its south side, typically via snow/glacier terrain near the Dana Glacier and the Dana–Dome col; parties approaching from the south (e.g., coming from Dome Peak/White Rock Lakes) reach the face directly off the Dana Glacier. The trailhead-to-Cub-Lake leg alone is about 11.1 miles one way with roughly 4,327 ft of gain (Gaia GPS recorded track); reaching the actual climb start from camp adds further distance and elevation gain not separately quantified in available sources.
routes.descent: Rappel descent, typically via the same anchors used on the standard East Face line.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via the East Face rappel line rather than down-climbing the Southwest Face: rappel from a horn anchor near the 3rd ledge (about 28 m) to an anchor above the Class 4 entry chimney, then a second ~28 m rappel to easy talus at the chimney's base. A 60 m rope is commonly carried for these raps.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-4 day trip: Day 1 hike Downey Creek/Bachelor Creek Trail to a camp near Bachelor Meadows or Cub Lake; Day 2 continue via Itswoot Ridge to a high camp near Spire Col; Day 3 climb the Southwest Face and return to camp (or continue the Ptarmigan Traverse toward Dome Peak); Day 4 hike out via Downey Creek.
routes.bail: Retreat down the climbed pitches or the standard rappel line is generally straightforward in good weather; from high camp, the only way out is to reverse the approach over Itswoot Ridge and the Bachelor Creek/Downey Creek Trails back to the trailhead — there is no shorter bail option given the wilderness setting.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting the face climb: the rappel descent, glacier crossing, and long hike back to high camp should not be done after dark or once weather deteriorates, and trip reports note that conditions on the exposed summit block can turn quickly.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September; earlier-season attempts face a longer, more snow-covered approach and firmer glacier/snowfield crossings on the Dana and Spire Glaciers, while the granite is driest and the approach trail most passable by mid-to-late summer.
knownHazards: Approach and climb start cross glacier/snow terrain (Dana Glacier and Spire Glacier) that can have open crevasses, especially later in the season.
Black lichen coats much of the granite and can make holds slick even in dry conditions.
The summit is a narrow, highly exposed horn with room for only one climber at a time.
The approach is a full day or more from the nearest trailhead through remote wilderness with no easy bail-out.
watchOut: Loose rock has been reported in sections of the class 4/5 terrain near the top of the peak.
Route-finding on the upper face has multiple class 4/5 variations; parties have found some lines (e.g., on the northeast side) harder than expected (5.7+).
Weather can change quickly at this exposed elevation, complicating an already committing rappel descent.
proTips: Camp at Cub Lake or on Itswoot Ridge/near Spire Col to shorten the summit push.
Given the long approach, most parties tag Spire Point as part of a multi-summit Ptarmigan Traverse itinerary rather than a standalone objective.
The standard descent rappels off a horn anchor near the 3rd ledge rather than the true summit, giving more room to organize the rappel.
gear: Rack of nuts and small-to-medium cams
whatToBring: Glacier travel gear (rope, crevasse rescue kit) for Dana/Spire Glacier crossings
Sturdy approach footwear; rock shoes optional for the 5.6 pitches
Crampons and ice axe for early-season snow/glacier sections
Multi-day backpacking/camping gear for the wilderness approach
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of nuts and small-to-medium cams (roughly finger- to hand-size) is sufficient for the 5.6 face climbing; a 60m rope covers both the roughly 5 pitches of climbing and the standard rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Moderate protection needs — small-to-medium cams and nuts protect the low-5th-class face climbing; the standard descent uses natural horn/rock anchors rather than fixed gear.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate: Pacific weather systems are forced upward by the Cascade crest, producing heavy winter/spring snowfall and comparatively drier, clearer conditions in summer.

bySeason.summer: Best window (Jun-Sep); driest rock and most passable trail/glacier travel, though afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving weather changes are still reported at the summit.
bySeason.spring: Approach trail and glacier crossings still largely snow-covered; longer, more strenuous access.
bySeason.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency; early snow can close out the route.
bySeason.winter: Not a viable climbing season; deep wilderness access effectively closed.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District), within the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent interagency pass) required at the Downey Creek Trailhead.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness travel permit at the trailhead; overnight backcountry camping in Glacier Peak Wilderness requires a permit.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass
access.closures: As of 2026, Suiattle River Road (FR 26) is closed to motor vehicles at a large washout about 4.5 miles in (roughly 130 ft wide, 70 ft deep, caused by an atmospheric river flood event); the Downey Creek Trailhead remains reachable on foot or bike beyond the closure, adding distance to the approach. Access into Glacier Peak Wilderness overall has been described as extremely limited following a December 2025 storm event; a closure order for the road segment runs from April 2, 2026 until January 1, 2028 unless rescinded sooner.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.status: Closed to motor vehicles at a washout roughly 4.5 miles from Highway 530
road.driveNote: Drive Highway 530 north from Darrington (or south from Highway 20 near Rockport) to FR 26, the Suiattle River Road; the road is closed to vehicles at a major washout about 4.5 miles in, so the Downey Creek Trailhead (normally ~20 miles up FR 26) must currently be reached by walking or biking the closed road segment.

emergency.county: Trailhead/approach area is near Darrington, Snohomish County; the summit lies close to the Skagit-Chelan county line.
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (911 emergency; non-emergency 425-388-3393), supported by Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue (425-388-3328).
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, (360) 436-1155.
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (Level IV ED) or Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III ED) — both a significant drive from the trailhead.
emergency.notes: This is a remote wilderness objective a full day or more from the trailhead; self-rescue capability and a float/trip plan left with someone are essential.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Downey Creek Trailhead to Cub Lake
sectionBreakdown.note: 11.1 miles one-way, ~4,327 ft gain per a Gaia GPS recorded track; typically split over 1-2 days as part of a multi-day approach, not done in a single push before climbing.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (8,262) is a minor mismatch with the commonly cited figure of 8,264 ft (Wikipedia, multiple trip reports) — recommend using 8,264 ft. Note also that 'Southwest Face' and the peak's 'East Face' (the mountain's standard/scramble route) are distinct named lines; most sourced descent beta (rappel count/length) comes from East Face trip reports, since parties commonly climb one line and descend via the East Face rappels rather than reversing the Southwest Face — flagged here rather than presented as Southwest-Face-specific rappel data with false certainty. Approach distance/gain figures given (11.1 mi / 4,327 ft) cover only the trailhead-to-Cub-Lake leg, not the full trailhead-to-summit distance, since no single reputable source gives a combined total.
routes.rappels: 2 x ~28m rappels (standard East Face descent line, shared by most parties regardless of ascent route)

blurb: Old Guard Peak (8,260 ft) rises directly above the Le Conte Glacier in the North Cascades, immediately west of Sentinel Peak, along the classic Ptarmigan Traverse between Cascade Pass and Dome Peak. The peak sits within the Glacier Peak Wilderness on notably high-friction gabbro rock typical of the surrounding Dome Peak massif. It is almost always climbed as a side-summit from a Le Conte–Sentinel col glacier camp during multi-day traverses of the range rather than as a standalone objective.

objHaz: Crevasses on the Middle Cascade and Le Conte Glaciers
Cornices on the eastern rim of Cache Col along the standard approach
Steep, exposed snow on the Red Ledge crux of the approach
Loose rock possible on the final gabbro scramble
routes.face: West/Northwest side of the Le Conte Glacier cirque
routes.overview: A short, moderate Class 3 scramble on solid gabbro from the Sentinel-Old Guard col or upper Le Conte Glacier, reached only after a multi-day approach deep in the North Cascades; almost always tacked on as a side-summit during a Ptarmigan Traverse rather than climbed in isolation.
routes.beta: From a glacier camp near the Le Conte-Sentinel col (~7,300 ft), cross onto the upper Le Conte Glacier and climb a steep snowfinger/snow ramp on the northwest side toward the Sentinel-Old Guard saddle, then finish with Class 3 scrambling on solid, high-friction gabbro to the summit. Round trip from a col/glacier camp runs roughly 2.5-3 hours and is commonly combined with an ascent of adjacent Sentinel Peak the same day.
routes.approach: There is no trailhead approach to Old Guard Peak itself - it is reached via the multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse. From the Cascade Pass trailhead (end of Cascade River Road, Forest Road 15, out of Marblemount), travel south over Cascade Pass, Cache Col, Kool-Aid Lake, the Red Ledge, the Middle Cascade Glacier, the Formidable-Spider col, and Yang Yang Lakes to the Le Conte Glacier, then camp near the Le Conte-Sentinel col (~7,300 ft). Old Guard is then a roughly 1,000 ft side-trip of gain from where the route crosses onto the Le Conte Glacier. An alternate, longer and more remote approach follows the Stehekin River trail up Agnes Creek and bushwhacks to the east shoulder of the Old Guard/Sentinel massif.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line - downclimb the Class 3 scramble and descend the snow ramp/snowfinger back to the glacier or col camp; no rappels are typically needed for the standard line.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same Class 3 gabbro terrain back to the Sentinel-Old Guard saddle, then descend the snow ramp/snowfinger back onto the upper Le Conte Glacier to the col camp. The larger commitment on this objective is the multi-day glacier approach/exit rather than the summit scramble itself; parties typically continue the Ptarmigan Traverse south toward Dome Peak or retrace their steps north over Cache Col and the Red Ledge.
routes.itinerary: Typical multi-day plan: Day 1, Cascade Pass trailhead to a camp near the Le Conte-Sentinel col (~7,300 ft) via Cascade Pass, Cache Col, Kool-Aid Lake, the Red Ledge, the Middle Cascade Glacier, and Yang Yang Lakes. Day 2, climb Old Guard Peak (and often Sentinel Peak) as a side-trip from camp, then either continue the traverse toward Dome Peak or retrace the route north.
routes.bail: Retreat from the summit scramble itself is straightforward - reverse to the col/glacier camp. The approach has few quick bail options once past Cache Col and the Red Ledge crux; retreating north over those sections in poor weather or soft snow is the main commitment to plan around.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that leaves enough margin to re-cross the Red Ledge and Cache Col sections of the approach before dark or before afternoon sun softens snow and increases rockfall/cornice risk, since those sections are far more hazardous in low visibility or deteriorating conditions than the summit scramble itself.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, once the Cache Col and Red Ledge snow has consolidated enough for safe travel but before early-season crevasse bridges on the Middle Cascade and Le Conte Glaciers deteriorate; most parties climb it in July-August as part of a full Ptarmigan Traverse.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit for the approach: crampons, ice axe, harness, rope, and crevasse-rescue gear for the Middle Cascade and Le Conte Glacier crossings. No technical rock rack is needed for the Class 3 summit scramble itself; a helmet is recommended for the scramble and for cornice/rockfall exposure on the approach.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is placed - it is an unroped Class 3 scramble once off the glacier. The gabbro is high-friction and generally solid, but a helmet is recommended given loose rock and overhead hazard near Cache Col.
whatToBring: multi-day backpacking/bivy gear (the traverse typically runs 4+ days)
sun protection for extended glacier travel
proTips: Climb Old Guard together with adjacent Sentinel Peak from the same Le Conte-Sentinel col camp to bag both with one glacier crossing.
The gabbro rock here is notably high-friction, so the Class 3 scrambling feels more secure than the grade alone suggests.
watchOut: Cornices can overhang the eastern rim of Cache Col on the approach - stay well back and favor the rock ramp on climber's right.
The Red Ledge crossing is the crux of the approach and is far more hazardous in low visibility or soft afternoon snow.
Crevasses on the Middle Cascade and Le Conte Glaciers require roped travel and careful route-finding.
knownHazards: Glacier travel with crevasse hazard on the Middle Cascade and Le Conte Glaciers en route to the peak.
Cornice hazard at Cache Col on the standard approach.
Loose rock possible on the final gabbro scramble to the summit.

pitchDetail.notes: Cross the upper Le Conte Glacier and ascend a snowfinger/snow ramp on the northwest side toward the Sentinel-Old Guard saddle.
Short, exposed scramble on solid, high-friction gabbro from the saddle/upper slopes to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.seasonalGate: Sometimes gated roughly 2 miles short of the Cascade Pass trailhead due to washout damage, adding a road walk to the approach.
road.driveNote: Gravel/paved Forest Service road running from Marblemount (SR 20) to the Cascade Pass trailhead; it is historically prone to washout damage - check current North Cascades National Park / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest road-condition pages before driving.

bySeason.summer: Glacier travel with consolidating snow on the Cache Col and Red Ledge sections; typical window for the traverse and this peak.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness) near Cascade Pass, transitioning south into the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest) around the Le Conte Glacier / Old Guard Peak.
access.fees: No entrance fee for the backcountry approach.
access.permit: A free, quota-limited NPS wilderness permit is required for overnight camping within North Cascades National Park (e.g., Cache Col/Kool-Aid Lake area); no permit is required once the route continues south into the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.passRequired: None required at the Cascade Pass trailhead itself (NPS land).
access.closures: Cascade River Road is periodically closed by washouts/storm damage; check NPS Cascade River Road condition updates before travel.

sectionBreakdown.section: Cascade Pass TH to Le Conte-Sentinel col camp
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass trailhead to ~7,300 ft col camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Multi-day approach via Cache Col, the Red Ledge, Middle Cascade Glacier, and Yang Yang Lakes.

sectionBreakdown.section: Col camp to Old Guard summit and back
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Le Conte-Sentinel col camp to summit round trip
sectionBreakdown.note: Snow ramp/snowfinger plus Class 3 scramble; reported round-trip time from Sentinel Pass area.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park - Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
emergency.notes: This is a remote, multi-day glaciated approach deep in the North Cascades with no maintained trail beyond Cascade Pass and no reliable cell coverage; a satellite communicator is strongly advised.

blurb: Sentinel Peak (8,257 ft) is a dark, pyramidal summit on the crest of the North Cascades in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, straddling the Chelan–Snohomish county line between the South Cascade Glacier (west) and LeConte Glacier (northeast). It is one of the signature side-summits of the Ptarmigan Traverse, the classic high route first completed in July 1938 by Ptarmigan Climbing Club members Bill Cox, Calder Bressler, Ray W. Clough, and Tom Myers, and it is almost always climbed as a detour from that traverse rather than as a standalone objective.

routes.overview: The easiest line climbs from the LeConte–Sentinel col up a steep snow ramp on the west side, tops out on a short 4th/low-5th-class rock step, then follows a class 2-3 ridge to the summit; it is normally tagged on to the Ptarmigan Traverse rather than climbed as an isolated objective. A separate, less-common approach reaches the west face directly from the South Fork Cascade Glacier, crossing a bergschrund before 4th-class scrambling and a class 2-3 ledge to the top.
routes.beta: Sentinel is a side-trip peak on the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. From the LeConte Glacier, parties climb to the LeConte–Sentinel col (roughly 7,300 ft), then take a steep snow ramp beginning around 7,400 ft, a short low-5th-class rock step, and a class 2-3 ridge walk to the 8,257 ft summit. Old Guard Peak, its near-twin just to the south, is commonly combined with Sentinel on the same outing.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road 23.1 mi from SR 20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass trailhead. Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 mi and 1,700 ft to Cascade Pass, then continue cross-country over Cache Col, down to Kool-Aid Lake, across the exposed Red Ledge, over the Middle Cascade Glacier, past Yang Yang Lakes and the Spider–Formidable col, and up the LeConte Glacier (staying climber's right to avoid rockfall from above) to the LeConte–Sentinel col. This is the standard 2-3 day Ptarmigan Traverse approach; an alternate, shorter but harder line reaches the west face directly via the South Fork Cascade River trail and South Cascade Glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to the LeConte–Sentinel col.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the ridge and rock step back to the LeConte–Sentinel col, then either continue south along the Ptarmigan Traverse toward Old Guard Peak and Dome Peak, or retreat the way you came over the LeConte, Middle Cascade, and Cache Col glaciers back to Cascade Pass. Parties on the west-face approach can exit via Drop Creek, a cross-country emergency-exit route cut in the 1960s for the Ptarmigan Traverse.
routes.itinerary: Typical 3-day plan: Day 1 — Cascade Pass trailhead to Cascade Pass, over Cache Col, camp near Kool-Aid Lake or on the Middle Cascade Glacier. Day 2 — traverse Red Ledge, Yang Yang Lakes, and the LeConte Glacier to a camp near the LeConte–Sentinel col; climb Sentinel (and often Old Guard) that day or the next. Day 3 — exit via the Ptarmigan Traverse or back over Cache Col.
routes.bail: There is no quick bail off the peak itself; from high on the route, downclimb the rock step and snow ramp back to the LeConte–Sentinel col and reverse the glacier approach toward Cache Col and Cascade Pass.
routes.turnaround: Because the route crosses multiple glaciers on a multi-day approach, set a turnaround based on current snow/glacier conditions, weather, and party speed rather than a fixed clock time, and budget extra margin for the return over Cache Col and Red Ledge.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly July through September, after the Cache, Middle Cascade, and LeConte glaciers have consolidated and the Red Ledge and Cascade Pass trail are mostly snow-free. The Cascade River Road and Cascade Pass trailhead themselves typically are not accessible until late June, and sometimes not until early July in heavy-snow years.
whatToBring: Glacier travel rope and crevasse-rescue kit (Cache, Middle Cascade, and LeConte glaciers are all crossed)
Overnight backpacking gear for the 2-3 day approach
Sun protection for extended glacier travel
proTips: On the LeConte Glacier below Sentinel Peak, move quickly and hug the climber's-right side of the crevassed section — the left side is exposed to rockfall from above and has no reasonable alternate line.
watchOut: Rockfall danger on the LeConte Glacier approach below Sentinel Peak — stay to climber's right, away from the exposed left side.
Cornices form along the ridges and cols on the traverse, including near Sentinel.
The Red Ledge, a key link on the approach, is exposed 3rd-class terrain that can be icy early in the season.
knownHazards: Multiple glaciated crossings (Cache Col/Glacier, Middle Cascade Glacier, and LeConte Glacier) with real crevasse hazard.
A bergschrund guards the base of Sentinel's west face on the South Cascade Glacier approach.
Loose rock on the summit scrambling sections.

pitchDetail.notes: Cascade Pass trailhead to Cascade Pass (3.7 mi, 1,700 ft gain), then cross-country over Cache Col.
Exposed ledge traverse past Kool-Aid Lake, then a glacier crossing toward Yang Yang Lakes and the Spider-Formidable col.
Climb the LeConte Glacier, staying climber's right to avoid rockfall, to the LeConte-Sentinel col (~7,300 ft).
A steep snow ramp above ~7,400 ft leads to a short low-5th-class rock step and a class 2-3 ridge to the 8,257 ft summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first third, then gravel with narrow, steep sections near the end
road.seasonalGate: Gated in winter near Eldorado (MP 20); the road/trailhead typically doesn't open until late June, sometimes not until early July in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: 23.1 mi from SR 20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass trailhead, about a 1-hour drive

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North (NWAC)
climate.typical: Best climbing window is roughly May through September; in practice the road and trailhead usually aren't snow-free and open until late June, pushing the practical season to July-September.

bySeason.summer: Warmest and driest window; approach glaciers most consolidated July-September.
bySeason.winter: Cascade River Road is gated and the approach is under deep snow — not a winter objective.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park Complex / Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount)
access.fees: Wilderness/backcountry permit fee: $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for overnight stays
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay in the park; obtain it at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center or via advance reservation on Recreation.gov
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated in winter; the Cascade Pass trailhead is typically inaccessible until late June or early July

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass trailhead to Cache Col / Kool-Aid Lake area
sectionBreakdown.note: Trail to Cascade Pass, then cross-country over Cache Col.
Red Ledge, Middle Cascade Glacier, Yang Yang Lakes, Spider-Formidable col, LeConte Glacier; climb Sentinel (and often Old Guard) from a camp near the col.
Continue the Ptarmigan Traverse south, or retreat over Cache Col back to Cascade Pass.

emergency.county: Skagit County (Cascade River Road / Marblemount approach); the summit itself sits on the Chelan-Snohomish county line
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Remote, multi-day glaciated approach; cell coverage is unreliable to absent along most of the route — carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: Older/secondary sources disagree on Sentinel Peak's elevation (Wikipedia infobox 8,266 ft; Wikipedia's Ptarmigan Traverse article 8,205 ft; various trip reports 8,260-8,261 ft). A 2025 differential-GPS survey (countryhighpoints.com) measured Sentinel Peak at 8,256.9 ft ± 0.1 ft NAVD88 — matching the given 8,257 ft — and found neighboring Old Guard Peak to be very slightly taller at 8,259.6 ft, contrary to older lists that rank Sentinel higher. No specific first-ascent party/year could be confirmed for this route itself (as distinct from the 1938 first traverse of the Ptarmigan Traverse as a whole), so 'fa' is left null.

blurb: Kololo Peaks (roughly 8,240 ft) rise on the Cascade Crest in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, straddling the Snohomish–Chelan county line about 3.3 miles south of Glacier Peak. Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide describes the twin-summited massif as "almost a nunatak" because it is nearly encircled by the White River, White Chuck, Suiattle, and Honeycomb Glaciers. Like the rest of the North Cascades crest here, the peak sits on accreted-terrane rock uplifted and later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, within a marine-west-coast climate zone of heavy orographic snowfall.

routes.rappels: None typically needed; descend the ascent line
objHaz: Crevasses / shallow icefall on the upper glacier
Rockfall on talus and ledges below the summit
Whiteout navigation on the featureless glacier
Swift, unbridged creek crossings on the approach
Long, remote approach with few bail options
routes.overview: The standard line climbs Kololo Peaks' south side from the White River Trail: a long forested/meadow approach, a cross-country ridge and talus climb to a flat shoulder near 6,000 ft, then a rope-up crossing of the upper White River Glacier to Class 2-3 summit rock. An alternate approach reaches the same summit area from the west via the North Fork Sauk River Trail, White Pass/Foam Basin, and the White Chuck Glacier–Glacier Gap traverse, at similar overall length.
routes.beta: Kololo Peaks is a remote, glacier-girdled objective with no maintained trail to the summit; every route finishes with off-trail travel and roped glacier crossing. From the east, the White River Trail approach leaves the trail around 3,000-3,500 ft and climbs open slopes/talus onto the south ridge, over a bulge near 7,200 ft, into a small basin below the east summit (Pt. 8160+), then west to a 7,400 ft saddle before ascending the ridge forming the east side of the White River Glacier cirque and dropping onto the upper glacier to angle up toward the summit rocks (Class 2+). From the west, parties cross White Pass and Foam Basin from the North Fork Sauk Trail, gain the White Chuck Glacier, cross Glacier Gap, descend onto the White River Glacier, and approach the twin summits via the west ridge; this variant crosses open crevasses and a shallow icefall with exposed blue ice by late summer. Both approaches run 12+ miles one-way and are typically done as 2-3 day trips with a high camp.
routes.approach: From the White River Trailhead (2,300 ft; end of FS Road 6400, reached via US-2 to Coles Corner, WA-207/Lake Wenatchee Highway, then FS Road 6400, about 35 miles from Leavenworth), follow White River Trail #1507 up the White River valley for roughly 10-11 miles of maintained trail, gaining elevation gradually. Leave the trail and climb cross-country (open slopes, talus, brush lower down) toward a flat shoulder at about 6,000 ft on Kololo's south ridge, which makes a good high camp below the final glacier/scramble pitch. (The alternate western approach starts at the North Fork Sauk River Trailhead, elev. ~2,050-2,070 ft, off Sloan Creek/FS Road 49 from the Mountain Loop Highway, and follows North Fork Sauk Trail #649 through Red Creek and White Pass to White River Meadows and Foam Basin before gaining the White Chuck Glacier.)
routes.descent: Descend the same south-side glacier and talus/ridge line used on the ascent back to the flat 6,000 ft shoulder and the White River Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ascent: downclimb the summit ledges and talus, re-cross the upper White River Glacier roped up (watching for the crevasses/icefall encountered on the way up), regain the east-side ridge of the glacier cirque, and drop back over the 7,400 ft saddle and 7,200 ft bulge to the flat shoulder at 6,000 ft, then follow the ascent line back down to the White River Trail. Parties using the western approach instead reverse the Glacier Gap–White Chuck Glacier–Foam Basin–White Pass line back to the North Fork Sauk Trail.
routes.itinerary: A common plan is a 3-day trip: Day 1 hike in (White River Trail or North Fork Sauk Trail) to a high camp near 6,000 ft or at White River Meadows/Foam Basin; Day 2 climb to the summit via the glacier and return to camp; Day 3 hike out. Strong parties in good conditions have done the climb car-to-car in a single very long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on the south-side line: downclimb/retrace the ascent track off the glacier onto the ridge and back to the 6,000 ft shoulder before the trail. On the western line via the White Chuck Glacier, retreat over Glacier Gap the way you came rather than continuing to descend toward the White River side.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before committing to the glacier crossing — deteriorating visibility, softening snow bridges over crevasses, or afternoon thunderstorms are the main reasons to bail on this long, remote approach, and the round trip back to a trailhead is long regardless of which side you're on.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in mid-summer (roughly July into September) once approach creeks have dropped and enough snow remains to bridge crevasses on the White River/White Chuck Glaciers; earlier in the season (June) means firmer snow bridges but harder creek crossings and more snow-covered brush, while late season exposes more open crevasses and bare glacier ice.
routes.comms: The approach and summit lie deep inside the Glacier Peak Wilderness, well away from cell infrastructure on both the White River and North Fork Sauk sides; climbers should not rely on cell coverage and should carry a satellite messenger or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for this Class 2-3 scramble; the gear list is glacier-travel gear — a rope per team (30m+), harness, a couple of ice screws or pickets for anchors/crevasse-rescue, and crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks or mechanical ascenders, pulley, extra slings and carabiners).
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are limited to glacier travel and crevasse-rescue anchors (pickets/screws) rather than rock gear; there is no fixed protection or bolted anchors on this route.
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the long approach and creek crossings
Bug protection for the forested approach
Map/compass/GPS for glacier navigation in whiteout
Overnight camping gear if doing it as a multi-day trip
proTips: Go earlier in the summer (June-July) for firmer, better-bridged crevasses on the White River/White Chuck Glaciers; by late summer the icefall and crevasses are more exposed.
The White River and North Fork Sauk approaches are roughly the same overall length; the White River Trail keeps you on maintained trail longer before you go cross-country.
The flat shoulder near 6,000 ft on the south ridge makes a good high camp and cuts the final summit push to about two hours.
watchOut: Open crevasses and a shallow icefall with exposed blue ice can appear on the upper glacier by late summer.
Creek crossings on the approach (e.g. Lightning Creek, Red Creek) can be difficult in high water or early-season snowmelt, sometimes requiring washed-out bridge workarounds.
Dense, chest-high brush low in some approach drainages slows travel significantly.
Cornices and unstable snow can linger on the summit ridge into early summer.
knownHazards: Crevasse fall while crossing the White River or White Chuck Glacier
Rockfall on talus and ledges below the twin summits
Whiteout/navigation hazard on the largely featureless upper glacier
Difficult, sometimes bridge-less creek crossings on the multi-mile approach
Overall remoteness — a 12+ mile one-way approach with few realistic bailout points

pitchDetail.notes: White River Trail #1507 (or North Fork Sauk Trail #649 on the west side) for roughly 10-12 miles to the point you leave maintained trail.
Off-trail travel up open slopes and talus to the flat shoulder near 6,000 ft on the south ridge (or to White River Meadows/Foam Basin on the west-side variant).
Roped travel across the upper White River Glacier (or White Chuck Glacier via Glacier Gap on the west variant), negotiating crevasses and, late season, a shallow icefall.
Scramble talus and ledges to the true summit; Kololo Peaks has twin summits (an east summit near 8,160+ ft and the higher west/main summit).

road.name: Forest Service Road 6400 (White River Road)
road.status: Open seasonally in summer; unpaved forest road to the trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Access can be blocked by snow outside summer/early fall; check current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest road alerts before driving (nearby roads in the district, e.g. the Suiattle River Road, have also seen multi-year washout closures).
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth: US-2 west 15 miles to Coles Corner, right on WA-207/Lake Wenatchee Highway for 10.5 miles, then continue on FS Road 6400 for 10 miles to the trailhead at road's end (about 35 miles total). The alternate North Fork Sauk Trailhead is reached via Sloan Creek Road (FS 49) off the Mountain Loop Highway, about 19.7 miles from the Darrington Ranger Station or 26.5 miles from the Verlot Public Service Center, then 6.6 miles up Sloan Creek Road.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy Pacific-front orographic precipitation, especially on west-facing (Snohomish County) slopes; temperatures west of the Cascade Crest rarely fall below 0°F or exceed 80°F.

bySeason.spring: Deep lingering snowpack; both approach roads/trails are often still snow-covered and avalanche hazard can affect approach slopes.
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing season; snow bridges over glacier crevasses are more reliable in June-July and increasingly open/bare by August.
bySeason.fall: Shorter days and colder creek crossings; first snow can arrive by late September-October, shortening the season.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible for climbing; access roads are typically snow-gated and the approach is a multi-day winter expedition.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District) and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District), within the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.fees: $5 per vehicle per day (or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass) at both the North Fork Sauk and White River trailheads.
access.permit: Free, self-issue wilderness permit available at the trailhead; no quota or advance reservation required.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (physical or digital) for trailhead parking.
access.closures: Access roads are subject to seasonal snow closure and occasional washout closures on nearby forest roads; check current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from high camp so the glacier crossing is done on firm snow, with margin for the long approach in and out.

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: White River Trailhead (2,300 ft) to ~6,000 ft shoulder / high camp
sectionBreakdown.note: Via White River Trail then cross-country; one recorded GPS-tracked trip took 9h40m over 14.6 miles with 7,222 ft gain — times vary widely with route choice, creek conditions, and brush.
South-side glacier crossing plus Class 2+ scramble to the top.

emergency.county: Peak straddles Snohomish County (west, North Fork Sauk approach) and Chelan County (east, White River approach)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff non-emergency: (425) 407-3999; Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency: (509) 667-6851; dial 911 for emergencies.
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF: (360) 436-1155 (west-side/North Fork Sauk access); Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee NF, Leavenworth: (509) 548-2550 (east-side/White River access).
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (24-hour ER, Level V trauma) for the White River/east side; Providence Regional Medical Center Everett for the North Fork Sauk/west side.
emergency.notes: Both trailheads are in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, well beyond cell coverage; rescues are typically coordinated by the county Sheriff's Office with volunteer search-and-rescue (e.g., Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue) and may require helicopter access given the remoteness.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation is given inconsistently across sources: the seed data lists 8,240 ft, Wikipedia rounds to "8,200+ ft," and ListsOfJohn.com's more precise figure is 8,243 ft (used here for highPointFt); these are within normal survey variance, not a substantive conflict. Approach gain/loss/distance (gainFt 7,222 / lossFt 1,333 / distKm 23.5) are drawn from one specific GPS-tracked trip report via the White River Trail and a cross-country Lightning Creek variant, not an official/averaged figure — expect meaningful trip-to-trip variation depending on exact line taken.

blurb: Gunsight Peak (~8,185–8,198 ft, sources vary) is a remote, heavily glaciated granite peak on the Cascade crest in Chelan County, Washington, rising between the Chickamin and Dome/Blue Glaciers just east of Dome Peak. It anchors the Gunsight Range, a cluster of granite spires (North, Center, and South Gunsight) prized for high-quality alpine granite but rarely visited because of a rugged multi-day approach. Hermann Ulrichs and Dwight Watson reached the edge of the Chickamin Glacier in 1936, and Fred Beckey (with Leif Patterson) climbed new routes on the range's North and Center peaks in 1968.

routes.rappels: Multiple double-rope (60m) rappels down the West Face gully/dihedral system back to the glacier snow finger below.
objHaz: moat/bergschrund crossing at base of face
loose rock in gully and basalt-dike sections
routes.overview: The standard line climbs Gunsight Peak's West Face: parties descend from a glacier high camp to a snow finger between the summit spires, then follow a broken granite rib past a black-rock dihedral and an exposed summit ridge to the top, mixing Class 3 scrambling with a short low-5th-class crux.
routes.beta: A multi-day glaciated alpine outing rather than a single-day climb: several days of approach through brush and over two glaciers lead to a high camp, from which the West Face itself involves a short wall to a ledge, a steep dihedral with an exposed 5.6 crux near its top, a Class 3 traverse through a reddish-black basalt dike, a down-climb/traverse at the main-north notch, a series of short Class 5.5 chimneys and cracks, and a wildly exposed but generally solid Class 3-to-low-5th ridge scramble to the summit horn. Parties then reverse the technical ground with double-rope rappels back to the glacier.
routes.approach: From the Downey Creek Trailhead on the Suiattle River Road (FR 26), follow the Downey Creek Trail, then the unmaintained, brushy Bachelor Creek Trail to Bachelor Flats and Bachelor Meadows. Cross Cub Pass to Cub Lake/Cub Lake Meadows, gain Itswoot Ridge, and cross the Dome Glacier to the Chikamin-Dome col (roughly 8,500 ft). Descend onto the Chickamin Glacier and traverse to a camp/bench (roughly 7,000 ft) below Gunsight Peak's West Face.
routes.descent: Rappel the West Face (several double-rope rappels) back down to the glacier snow finger, then retrace the approach: back over the Chikamin-Dome col, across the Dome Glacier and Itswoot Ridge, and out via Bachelor Creek and Downey Creek.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/belay the exposed ridge crest back to the large horn with rappel slings, then make a diagonal double-rope rappel with directional protection to reach the snow finger between the spires in one long rappel where terrain allows. From there, descend soft snow back to the glacier camp. The full round trip from high camp is reported at roughly 7 hours up and 3 hours back down in good conditions.
routes.itinerary: Typical parties take 5-6 days total: Day 1 hike in to a forest camp near Bachelor Flats; Day 2 continue over Bachelor Meadows/Cub Pass/Itswoot Ridge and across the Dome Glacier to a high camp at the Chikamin-Dome col or a bench on the Chickamin Glacier; Day 3 descend to the base of the West Face, climb the route, and rappel back to camp; remaining days retrace the approach out, sometimes combined with an ascent of neighboring Sinister Peak.
routes.bail: The technical section can be reversed by rappelling the West Face (double-rope rappels) back to the glacier at any point below the crux. Below the face, retreat follows the same glacier/ridge approach back over the Chikamin-Dome col to Bachelor Creek and Downey Creek.
routes.turnaround: As on any glaciated alpine route, plan to be off exposed glacier terrain and back at camp before afternoon warming softens snow bridges and opens the moat further; turn around rather than force a crossing of the moat/bergschrund or push the summit ridge if conditions or weather deteriorate.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August is the usual window, once enough snow remains to bridge the moat between the glacier and the base of the West Face but before the glaciers become too broken up; trip reports describe conditions varying sharply by year, from about 95% snow-covered in a big-snow late-June ascent to an impassable moat in leaner years.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rock rack (small-to-medium cams and stoppers) for the short 5.6 finger/hand cracks and belays on the dihedral and upper chimneys, plus a full glacier-travel kit (harness, crevasse-rescue gear, pickets), crampons, ice axe, and two 60m ropes for the double-rope rappels off the summit ridge and West Face.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is light and short — a small rack for the crux dihedral and a couple of short 5.5 chimney/crack pitches — supplemented by natural rappel anchors and slings reported at stations along the upper ridge.
gear: Glacier travel kit (harness, crevasse-rescue gear, pickets)
whatToBring: Multi-day backpacking/camping gear for a 4-6 day trip
Tent or bivy suitable for a glacier camp
Sun protection for extended glacier travel
Gaiters and sturdy boots for the brushy, unmaintained approach trail
Bear-aware food storage (bears reported in the Bachelor/Downey Creek valleys)
proTips: Time the trip for after the moat between the glacier and the base of the West Face has closed enough to cross safely — multiple parties have been turned back by an impassable moat in lean years.
A camp on the Chickamin Glacier bench below the West Face shortens the summit push considerably compared to camping at the Chikamin-Dome col.
Carry two ropes specifically for the double-rope rappels required to retreat down the West Face.
Budget extra time (and expect slow going) on the unmaintained, brushy Bachelor Creek climbers' path, especially early in the season.
watchOut: The moat/bergschrund where the glacier meets the base of the West Face can be difficult or outright impassable depending on the snow year.
Loose rock is reported through the basalt-dike section and the upper gully system.
Route-finding on the exposed, jumbled summit ridge (Class 3 to low 5th) requires care, especially in poor visibility.
knownHazards: Crevasse fields on the Dome and Chickamin Glaciers require standard glacier travel and rescue precautions.
The bergschrund/moat crossing below the West Face has been reported as everything from an easy step-across to fully impassable, depending on the year.
Loose rock through the basalt dike and gully sections poses rockfall risk to parties below.
The current Suiattle River Road washout (2025-2026) removes vehicle access, adding miles of road walking/biking before the trail even begins, and lengthens any rescue response.

pitchDetail.notes: Short wall from the base up to a broad ledge.
Leftward to a steep black-rock dihedral; the uppermost ~15 ft is exposed 5.6 on solid granite.
Scramble through a distinctive reddish-black basalt dike.
20-ft downclimb followed by a rightward traverse across steep, well-broken rock.
