Series of short chimneys and cracks up to Class 5.5 leading to the ridge crest, near a large horn with rappel slings.
Wildly exposed but generally solid scrambling over granite boulders to the summit.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (FR 26)
road.status: Closed to motor vehicles beyond roughly milepost 4 due to major washouts (as of Dec 2025); hiking and cycling on the road are still permitted.
road.driveNote: From Darrington, the drive is normally SR 530 north ~8 mi, then FR 26 east ~20 mi to the Downey Creek Trailhead — but the road is currently impassable to vehicles past mile ~4, so climbers must walk or bike the remaining ~16+ miles of road before starting the trail, under a closure order in effect through Jan 1, 2028 unless rescinded sooner.

climate.typical: A remote, heavily glaciated alpine environment on the Cascade crest; fast-moving Pacific storms can bring sudden weather changes even in summer, and glacier surface conditions vary a great deal year to year.

bySeason.spring: Deep, often-unstable snowpack; the approach trail and road are typically still snow-covered and avalanche-prone, making this an atypical season for the route.
bySeason.summer: Late June through August is the primary climbing window; snow bridges and the moat below the West Face are most likely to be passable in this period, though conditions vary significantly by year.
bySeason.fall: By September glaciers are more broken and crevassed and the moat below the West Face can become difficult or impassable; weather is often more stable but the glacier margin for error narrows.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible for climbing given heavy snowpack, avalanche hazard, and a snowed-in approach road.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District) — Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use or $30 annual pass at Downey Creek Trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit available at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency (America the Beautiful) Pass, or day-use fee
access.closures: Suiattle River Road (FR 26) closed to motor vehicles past roughly milepost 4 due to washouts, under a closure order in effect through Jan 1, 2028 unless rescinded; open to hikers/cyclists.

sectionBreakdown.note: Includes glacier travel, the moat crossing, and the technical West Face.
Double-rope rappels down the West Face back to the snow finger and glacier.

emergency.county: Snohomish County (trailhead and approach via Darrington); the summit lies near the Chelan County line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (425) 407-3999 (dial 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest: (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Skagit Regional Health), 330 S Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA — 24-hour Level IV Emergency Department, (360) 435-2133
emergency.notes: Very remote, glaciated terrain reached by a multi-day approach; the current Suiattle River Road washout adds significant road-walking mileage before the trail even begins, and helicopter evacuation is often the only practical rescue option from high camp or the peak itself.

routes.corrections: Original seed data listed rockGrade as 'Class 4'. Multiple independent trip reports (Wenatchee Outdoors, Jim Brisbine/trailcatjim.com, Eileen's Travel & Mountain Adventures) and Wikipedia describe the standard West Face route as involving short pitches of roped fifth-class rock, with a crux of about 15 ft of exposed 5.6 plus Class 5.5 chimneys higher up — not just Class 4 scrambling. Corrected rockGrade to '5.6'. Reported summit elevation varies by source (Wikipedia 8,198 ft; listsofjohn.com 8,184 ft; a USGS-derived figure of ~8,012 ft also appears); the given 8,185 ft falls within this range and is closest to listsofjohn.com, so it was left unchanged.

blurb: Agnes Mountain is a remote, seldom-climbed summit in the North Cascades' Agnes Creek drainage (Glacier Peak Wilderness / Lake Chelan National Recreation Area), Chelan County, Washington. Fred Beckey described it as "an immense Matterhorn-shaped massif," with its north face dropping roughly 1,800 feet to Agnes Creek in only 1.5 miles. It was first climbed in 1936 by W. Ronald Frazier and Dan O'Brien via the West Fork of Agnes Creek, and only a handful of parties are known to have reached the summit since.

routes.fa: W. Ronald Frazier and Dan O'Brien, 1936, via the West Fork of Agnes Creek
routes.overview: The West Route follows Agnes Mountain's original 1936 first-ascent line: from Fivemile Camp on the Pacific Crest Trail, the largely vanished West Fork Agnes Creek route heads west to the foot of the mountain, from which class 3-4 terrain leads up the peak's west side to the summit, avoiding the roped 5.6-5.8 climbing found on the mountain's south ridge/northeast ridge lines.
routes.beta: Agnes Mountain is a remote, seldom-climbed North Cascades summit that Fred Beckey described as 'an immense Matterhorn-shaped massif.' The West Route retraces the peak's 1936 first-ascent line via the West Fork of Agnes Creek. Multiple trip accounts of this peak's other approaches agree that the real difficulty lies in the multi-day approach — an all-but-vanished trail, heavy brush, and creek crossings — rather than in the class 3-4 summit scrambling itself; treat it as a serious wilderness undertaking even though the climbing grade is moderate.
routes.approach: Reach Stehekin via the Lake Chelan Boat Company ferry (Lady of the Lake II or Lady Express) from Chelan, then take the Stehekin Shuttle Bus roughly 11 miles up-valley to High Bridge (about 1,600 ft), where the Pacific Crest Trail (Agnes Creek Trail) heads south. Follow the PCT about 5.5 miles to Fivemile Camp, then leave the maintained trail and follow the West Fork Agnes Creek route — described by WTA as 'all but vanished' — west through brush along the West Fork drainage toward the base of Agnes Mountain (roughly 17 miles round trip to the base alone), before continuing off-trail up the peak's west side to the summit.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach itself is a multi-day, off-trail undertaking through brush and creek crossings with no maintained trail, set a firm turnaround time for the summit push that accounts for the slow pace of brushy travel and leaves margin to re-cross Agnes and Spruce Creek and regain the PCT before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July-September, after early-season high water recedes on Agnes and Spruce Creek and snow has cleared from the approach; trip reports covering this peak's approaches consistently describe the drainage trails as heavily overgrown regardless of month, so brush is a factor throughout the climbing window.
proTips: The approach, not the summit scrambling, is the crux of this climb — the West Fork Agnes Creek trail is officially described by WTA as 'all but vanished,' so budget significant extra time for bushwhacking and route-finding.
Nearby trip reports on this peak's other approaches describe crossing Agnes Creek and Spruce Creek on downed logs; expect similar unbridged crossings en route to the West Fork drainage.
watchOut: Fivemile Camp to the base of Agnes Mountain is roughly 17 miles round trip on an unmaintained, brush-choked route with no formal trail.
Early-season snow can linger in gullies on the mountain's flanks well into summer.
Creek crossings of Agnes Creek and Spruce Creek can be hazardous at high water on downed-log crossings.
knownHazards: Steep, dense brush along the largely vanished West Fork Agnes Creek route
Unbridged log crossings of Agnes Creek and Spruce Creek
Loose rock in the upper mountain's scrambling terrain
Remote location with no maintained trail beyond Fivemile Camp, making self-rescue slow and difficult

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road (shuttle bus only, via Lake Chelan ferry)
road.status: No public highway access — Stehekin is reached only by boat, floatplane, or trail
road.driveNote: From Chelan, ride the Lake Chelan Boat Company ferry (Lady of the Lake II or Lady Express) to Stehekin Landing, then the Stehekin Shuttle Bus about 11 miles up Stehekin Valley Road to the High Bridge trailhead.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced North Cascades climate; the West Fork Agnes Creek approach is described as overgrown/brushy in essentially all trip accounts covering this peak.

bySeason.spring: High creek flows and lingering snow make the approach hazardous
bySeason.summer: Primary climbing window (Jul-Sep); most reliable creek crossings and driest brush
bySeason.fall: Shortening days and possible early snow
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible — no practical ferry-based approach and deep snow

access.landManager: NPS — North Cascades National Park Service Complex / Lake Chelan National Recreation Area
access.fees: $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for a reserved backcountry permit; free walk-up permits available at ranger stations
access.permit: Overnight wilderness/backcountry permit required, via recreation.gov reservation or first-come at the Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount) or Golden West Visitor Center (Stehekin)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom Dispatch 509-663-9911 (Chelan County Sheriff's Office Search & Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, (360) 854-7245; Golden West Visitor Center, Stehekin
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health (Lake Chelan Community Hospital), Chelan, WA — 24-hour emergency care, (509) 682-3300
emergency.notes: Stehekin and the Agnes Creek drainage are accessible only by boat, floatplane, or trail; cell coverage is minimal to nonexistent in the approach — carry a satellite communicator/PLB.

routes.corrections: The catalog lists elevation 8,131 ft. Wikipedia (citing a USGS NAVD88 survey) gives 8,119 ft, while listsofjohn.com's listing text indicates 8,133 ft and at least one trip report cites 8,115 ft — sources vary by roughly 15-18 ft, likely reflecting different surveys/datums. 8,119 ft (Wikipedia/USGS) is used here as the best-sourced figure; the differences are minor and not evidence of a wrong peak.

blurb: Elephant Head is a rugged, ice-sculpted sub-summit of the Dome Peak massif in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Washington's North Cascades, part of a chain of jagged granite crags roughly 1.1 miles northwest of Dome Peak's twin summits that divides the Dana and Chickamin Glaciers. Its cliffs drop into the remote cirque at the head of Agnes Creek's West Fork, and it is best known as a landmark passed along the classic Ptarmigan Traverse and the Dome Peak approach rather than as a stand-alone climbing destination. No documented first-ascent record specific to Elephant Head (as distinct from Dome Peak itself) was found in available climbing literature.

routes.overview: A remote glacier-and-scramble line: parties cross the Dana Glacier along the ridge system linking Dome Peak and Spire Point, then finish with Class 3-4 rock over broken, ice-polished granite to Elephant Head's summit.
routes.beta: Elephant Head sits on the crest dividing the Dana and Chickamin Glaciers, about 1.1 miles northwest of Dome Peak's summits (Beckey, 'Cascade Alpine Guide'). It is rarely climbed as an independent objective; most parties encounter it while crossing the Dana Glacier on the Ptarmigan Traverse or on the glacier approach to Dome Peak, and a side trip to its summit adds glacier travel plus a short, broken rock scramble. No published pitch-by-pitch route description or fixed protection beta for the peak itself was located.
routes.approach: Two approaches reach the Dana Glacier basin below Elephant Head. (1) Via the Ptarmigan Traverse: start at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (Cascade River Road), follow the Traverse south to a camp at White Rock Lakes, then climb west/southwest onto the Dana Glacier. (2) Via Downey Creek/Bachelor Creek: from the Downey Creek Trailhead (1,440 ft) follow Downey Creek Trail 768 roughly 6.5 miles to Bachelor Creek, continue past Cub Lake to a high camp near 6,200 ft on the ridge toward Spire Point, then cross onto the Dana Glacier to reach the Elephant Head ridge. Both approaches are multi-day and cross unmaintained, brushy trail sections.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down-climb/glissade the Dana Glacier to the approach ridge and return to camp by the same route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the Class 3-4 scramble back onto the Dana Glacier and descend the glacier to the ridge crest used on the ascent, roping up for crevasse-prone sections. From there, either continue the Ptarmigan Traverse toward White Rock Lakes or drop back to a Bachelor Creek/Cub Lake camp and out via Downey Creek Trail 768, depending on which approach was used.
routes.itinerary: A typical plan mirrors the Dome Peak massif itinerary from the Downey Creek side: Day 1 - hike in to a camp near Bachelor Creek/Cub Lake (~6.5-8 mi); Day 2 - continue to a high camp (~6,200 ft), cross the Dana Glacier, and climb Elephant Head, returning to camp; Day 3 - hike out via Downey Creek Trail 768. Parties approaching via the Ptarmigan Traverse instead build the Elephant Head side-trip into a multi-day traverse itinerary from Cascade Pass, typically summiting from a White Rock Lakes camp.
routes.bail: Retreat down the Dana Glacier along the ascent track to the ridge/camp; there is no faster escape from this remote cirque, so turning around early is the main safety margin.
routes.turnaround: Glacier travel on the Dana Glacier and the exposed summit scramble should be finished before afternoon whiteout, thunderstorm build-up, or softening snow bridges make crevasse crossings unsafe; because the approach is multi-day and far from help, set a conservative turnaround well before deteriorating weather or visibility closes in.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, with August typically offering the driest brush on the approach trails and the most stable glacier conditions on the Dana Glacier, per Mountaineers/SummitPost guidance for this Dome Peak massif area.
routes.face: Cliffs plunge southeast into the cirque at the head of Agnes Creek's West Fork; the glacier approach comes from the north/northwest via the Dana Glacier.
objHaz: Loose/broken rock on the summit scramble
Washed-out access road adding approach mileage
watchOut: Snow bridges over Dana Glacier crevasses can be hidden and unreliable, especially late season.
The final scramble is over loose, ice-polished granite blocks with real exposure.
The Downey Creek/Bachelor Creek approach trail has not been consistently maintained and includes brush, downed timber, and damaged puncheon.
Suiattle River Road (FR-26) access has been subject to washout closures — confirm current road status before relying on a vehicle approach.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel is required to reach the peak, with real fall/fall-in hazard on the Dana Glacier.
The remoteness (multiple days from a trailhead) means self-rescue capability and a wide weather margin are essential.
Access roads in this drainage have experienced repeated washouts, which can add many extra miles of walking to reach the trailhead.
proTips: Most parties combine Elephant Head with the Ptarmigan Traverse rather than approaching it as a stand-alone objective.
A camp at White Rock Lakes (on the Traverse) sets up a reasonable side-trip onto the Dana Glacier toward Elephant Head.
Go in August for the firmest snow bridges and driest brush on the Downey Creek approach.
gear: Light rock rack for the summit scramble
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
Map and compass/GPS for cross-country glacier navigation
Bug protection for the brushy approach
Extra food/fuel for a multi-day carry
routes.detailedRack: No published protection beta specific to Elephant Head was found. Parties carry standard glacier-travel gear (rope, pickets, crevasse-rescue kit, ice axe, crampons) for the Dana Glacier crossing, plus a light rack of slings and optional small cams for the short, broken Class 3-4 rock finish, consistent with general guidance for glaciated objectives on the Dome Peak massif (Mountaineers Books, Cascade Alpine Guide).
routes.proNeeds: Primarily glacier-travel protection (pickets, rope, crevasse-rescue gear) for the Dana Glacier; the summit scramble is unroped Class 3-4 for most parties, with no documented fixed anchors or rappels.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.status: Subject to washout closures — closed to motor vehicles roughly 4.5 miles in as of recent reports, and the Downey Creek Trailhead recreation site was listed 'Temporarily Closed' by the Forest Service per a December 2025 alert due to multiple washouts.
road.driveNote: From Darrington drive north about 8 miles, then east on FR-26 toward Downey Creek Campground/Trailhead (normally ~19.5-20 miles total). Washouts have periodically closed the road to vehicles partway in, requiring hikers/bikers to cover the remaining distance on foot; verify current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest road/trailhead alerts before driving. The alternate Ptarmigan Traverse approach starts from Cascade River Road to the Cascade Pass Trailhead, a separate seasonal gravel road.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Glacier Peak Wilderness (NWS Seattle forecast office)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short, drier climbing window typical of the west-slope Cascade crest; the Dana/Chickamin glacier cirque holds snow late into the season.

bySeason.summer: July-September is the recommended climbing window for this Dome Peak massif area, with August typically driest and most stable (SummitPost/Mountaineers).

access.landManager: Glacier Peak Wilderness, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful interagency pass required for trailhead parking at Downey Creek Trailhead.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness/backcountry permit required, obtained in person at the trailhead or Darrington Ranger Station on the day of or day before entry; party size is limited to 12 within Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Downey Creek Trailhead was listed 'Temporarily Closed' by the Forest Service due to Suiattle River Road washouts (alert current as of December 2025); the road is closed to motor vehicles at a washout roughly 4.5 miles in, though the trailhead has remained reachable by foot/bike beyond that point per other trip reports. Confirm current status before a trip.

emergency.county: Skagit County, WA (peak/summit area, per Wikipedia and ListsOfJohn for the adjacent Dome Peak); the Downey Creek Trailhead access point near Darrington is in Snohomish County.
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, 1405 Emens Avenue North, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Skagit Regional Health), 330 South Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA 98223 — Level IV Emergency Department, (360) 435-2133
emergency.notes: This is a remote wilderness area multiple days from a trailhead; expect no reliable cell coverage and plan for self-rescue/extended response times.

waypoints.name: Slate Peak Trailhead (Harts Pass / Slate Pass access road)

blurb: Blizzard Peak sits in the Hozameen Range on the crest of the Pasayten Wilderness, on the Whatcom–Okanogan county line just a few miles south of the Canadian border near Manning Provincial Park, BC. Its upper slopes are grass, scree, and broken talus rather than technical rock, and the mountain is one of several nearby names — along with Three Fools Creek, Freezeout Pass, and Nightmare Camp — commemorating an 1800s prospecting party that came to grief searching for gold in this remote corner of the range. No documented technical first ascent exists; the summit is reached almost entirely by long cross-country travel from Frosty Pass, and multiple surveying sources place its true elevation at 7,622 ft rather than 7,871 ft.

routes.overview: A long, largely off-trail journey through the Pasayten Wilderness to a remote, easy summit; from Frosty Pass the final push is a straightforward Class 1-2 to 2-3 walk over grass, scree, and talus with no technical climbing.
routes.beta: Blizzard Peak has no maintained summit trail and sees very few ascents. Parties reach it via Frosty Pass on the Boundary Trail, then travel cross-country south over open grass and talus slopes to the summit. The peak is most often combined with nearby Mt. Winthrop as part of a multi-day Pasayten backpacking loop rather than climbed as a standalone objective.
routes.approach: Two long approaches are used. From the US side: either drive to Harts Pass (via Mazama and Forest Road 5400) and follow the PCT north over Woody/Rock/Hopkins Passes to Castle Pass, then the Boundary Trail east to Frosty Pass; or use the Three Fools Creek trailhead and follow the Boundary Trail up and over to the same area. Both approaches are 15+ miles one-way through remote, partly unmaintained trail. A shorter but border-crossing option starts at the Monument 78/83 trailhead off Highway 3 in Manning Provincial Park, BC, and follows trail/cross-country travel east to Frosty Pass. From Frosty Pass or a camp in Upper Frosty Basin (~6,100 ft), head off-trail toward the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to Frosty Pass and out via whichever approach trail was used.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the same grass/talus slopes back to Frosty Pass. There is no shortcut or alternate descent line documented; retrace the ascent to Frosty Pass and then follow the Boundary Trail/PCT (or the BC trail system, if approached from Manning Park) back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-5 day backpacking trip: Day 1-2 hike in to a camp near Frosty Pass/Upper Frosty Basin via Harts Pass/PCT/Castle Pass, Three Fools Creek, or the Manning Park (BC) trailhead; Day 3 climb Blizzard Peak (often paired with Mt. Winthrop) as a day trip from camp; remaining day(s) to hike out.
routes.bail: There is no technical terrain to bail from; simply turn around and reverse the cross-country line to Frosty Pass and the trail system. The main constraint is the long, remote approach itself rather than the summit terrain.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and exit are both very long and largely off-trail through unmaintained terrain, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather rather than pushing to the summit — deteriorating weather or fading light can make the cross-country return to camp difficult to navigate.
routes.bestSeason: Summer through early fall is best. Eastern Pasayten trails are typically snow-free by early July, while higher terrain can hold snow into early August; a documented ascent in early October found clear, calm, dry conditions, though nights were cold and the window for good weather narrows quickly late in the season. Snow returns by October-November and access roads close by late fall.
routes.comms: No cell coverage should be expected in this remote section of the Pasayten Wilderness; the nearest reliable cell service is in the Methow Valley towns of Winthrop and Twisp, WA.
routes.detailedRack: No rope or rack is needed for the standard line; this is a non-technical scramble over grass, scree, and talus.
routes.proNeeds: None — the route is unroped scrambling terrain with no fixed protection or established technical pitches.
gear: Map and compass or GPS/GPX track
whatToBring: Sleeping bag rated for cold alpine nights
Extra warm layers and rain shell
Passport or required documentation if approaching via the Manning Provincial Park, BC trailhead
proTips: The route sees very few visitors — expect no trail beyond Frosty Pass and to route-find cross-country over grass and talus to the summit.
A documented early-October ascent found stable, clear, calm conditions, but nights were cold at this elevation and the fall weather window is short.
Camping at Upper Frosty Basin (~6,100 ft) near Frosty Pass puts the summit within an easy half-day round trip and doubles as a base for nearby Mt. Winthrop.
watchOut: The Boundary Trail between Castle Pass and Frosty Pass has been unmaintained since 2006, with significant blowdown and faint, hard-to-follow tread through meadow sections.
No maintained trail leads to the true summit — final travel is off-trail route-finding over grass, scree, and talus.
This is one of the most remote corners of the Pasayten Wilderness; help is many miles and likely a full day or more away.
knownHazards: Unmaintained, hard-to-follow trail sections (deadfall, faint tread) on the Boundary Trail approach
Off-trail talus/scree travel with rockfall and ankle-injury potential
Remote location far from cell coverage and emergency services
Early- or late-season snow lingering on shaded slopes
An international border crossing (with required documentation) if approaching via the Manning Provincial Park, BC trailhead

road.name: Harts Pass Road (Forest Road 5400) from Mazama, WA, or Highway 3 to the Monument 78/83 trailhead in Manning Provincial Park, BC
road.status: Harts Pass Rd is a narrow, unpaved forest road to the highest road-accessible point in Washington State; it has been subject to storm-damage closures, so current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts should be checked before driving
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed by snow from late October through June or early July
road.driveNote: From Mazama on SR 20, follow Lost River Road/Forest Road 5400 about 20 miles to Harts Pass.

climate.typical: Snow typically covers the area from October through May/June; eastern Pasayten trails are usually snow-free by early July, while higher west-side terrain can hold snow into early August.

bySeason.spring: Deep snow persists into June or July at this elevation; travel requires mountaineering skills and gear.
bySeason.summer: Prime season, mid-July through September, with snow-free cross-country travel and long days.
bySeason.fall: Stable, clear, cold conditions are possible into early October (as documented on one ascent), but weather can turn quickly and days shorten fast.
bySeason.winter: Effectively inaccessible; heavy snowpack and closed access roads (e.g. Harts Pass Road closes by late October).

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District (Pasayten Wilderness)
access.fees: No wilderness permit fee; a Northwest Forest Pass may be required at some trailheads
access.permit: Free self-issuing wilderness permit, available at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead-dependent); passport/proper documentation required if approaching via the Manning Provincial Park, BC trailhead
access.closures: The Castle Pass/Boundary Trail segment has been unmaintained since 2006 with heavy blowdown; access roads such as Harts Pass Road are subject to seasonal and storm-damage closures, so check current Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts before a trip

emergency.county: Whatcom County (the summit sits on the Whatcom/Okanogan county line); most approach trailheads and services are in Okanogan County
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff non-emergency dispatch: (509) 422-7232
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 West Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA; (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA, (509) 689-2517, or Mid-Valley Hospital, Omak, WA, (509) 826-1760 — both roughly 30 miles from Methow Valley trailheads, and a full day or more of travel from this remote summit
emergency.notes: No cell coverage should be expected in this part of the Pasayten backcountry; Aero Methow Rescue Service provides EMS response in the Methow Valley area.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,871 ft) conflicts with the USGS GNIS survey point (48.9407522, -120.7553147, feature ID 1516656) and independently converging figures from Peakbagger, ListsOfJohn, and PeakVisor, all of which give 7,622 ft — set route highPointFt to 7,622. The given rockGrade 'Class 2-3' was left as-is since it matches a plausible peakbagging classification, but a detailed firsthand 2012 trip report (trailcatjim.com) describes the final scramble to the summit as easier 'Class 1-2' terrain over grass/scree; noting the discrepancy here rather than overwriting on a single source.

blurb: Le Conte Mountain is a 7,762-foot summit straddling the Skagit–Chelan county line on the crest of the North Cascades, within the Glacier Peak Wilderness on the shared border of the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests. Carved by the Le Conte and South Cascade Glaciers and built of granite and metamorphic terrane rock uplifted roughly 50 million years ago, it forms a two-mile ridge above the Yang Yang Lakes basin and is one of the signature side-summits of the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. It was first climbed on July 23, 1938 by Calder T. Bressler, Ralph W. Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers — the same party that pioneered the Ptarmigan Traverse itself.

routes.overview: Le Conte's standard line is a side-summit climbed from a Yang Yang Lakes camp on the Ptarmigan Traverse: cross the fast-melting Le Conte Glacier on the peak's southeast flank, then scramble broken, loose Class 3-4 rock up to a notch and along an exposed ridge crest to the true summit.
routes.beta: Most parties climb Le Conte as a roughly 2-hour round-trip side trip from a Yang Yang Lakes (or nearby) camp rather than carrying full packs over it. From camp, cross snowfields/the glacier below the peak's southeast side, then work up ledges toward an obvious notch in the ridge skyline before a short, exposed Class 3-4 finish to the summit. The Le Conte Glacier melts to bare, hard blue ice by mid-to-late summer even though the slope angle is modest, so some parties rope up or crampon a section that would otherwise be a walk.
routes.fa: Calder T. Bressler, Ralph W. Clough, Bill Cox, Tom Myers — July 23, 1938 (first ascent of the peak, made by the same Ptarmigan Climbing Club party during the first traversal of the Ptarmigan Traverse; period accounts describe them gaining the ridge, passing a false summit, and traversing below the crest to the true summit — closely matching today's standard line, though it is not certain the exact modern ledge system was followed).
routes.rappels: None — the standard line has no rappels; retreat is by downclimbing the ascent route.
routes.face: Southeast side / Le Conte Glacier and ridge above Yang Yang Lakes
routes.approach: Reached only as part of the multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse. From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (end of Cascade River Road, 23 mi from Marblemount/SR 20), hike the Cascade Pass Trail (3.7 mi, ~1,700 ft gain) to Cascade Pass, continue up and over Cache Col (~6,900-7,000 ft), then descend snowfields toward Kool-Aid Lake (~6,100 ft) and on to Yang Yang Lakes (~5,840 ft), the customary camp from which Le Conte is climbed as a round-trip side excursion.
routes.descent: Downclimb the same Class 3-4 ledges and ridge back to the glacier, then re-cross the Le Conte Glacier to Yang Yang Lakes camp; no rappels needed.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ascent: downclimb the short exposed ridge section, pick your way back down the loose ledges to the notch, and descend the snow/glacier slope on the peak's southeast side back to Yang Yang Lakes. Parties continuing the full Ptarmigan Traverse then proceed south toward Spider-Formidable Col and beyond; parties treating Le Conte as an out-and-back simply return to their traverse camp.
routes.itinerary: Typical multi-day plan: Day 1 - Cascade Pass Trailhead to Cache Col/Kool-Aid Lake camp (~6-8 mi, ~2,800 ft gain, roughly 6 hrs: 2 hrs car-to-pass, 3 hrs pass-to-Cache Col, 1 hr col-to-camp). Day 2 - move to Yang Yang Lakes (roughly 8 hrs via Spider-Formidable Col) and climb Le Conte as a ~2-hour round trip from camp. Most parties continue the Ptarmigan Traverse southward on subsequent days rather than climbing Le Conte in isolation.
routes.bail: Retreat from anywhere on the route is straightforward — reverse the ascent back across the glacier to Yang Yang Lakes; there is no forced downclimbing or rappelling on the standard line.
routes.turnaround: Because this is a glaciated side-summit reached from a remote multi-day camp, set a turnaround time that gets your party off the glacier and loose upper ridge before afternoon thunderstorms build or snow bridges/ice soften, and before fatigue from the broader traverse compromises footing and route-finding.
routes.bestSeason: The peak is realistically climbable only during the short window the Ptarmigan Traverse itself is in condition — roughly late July through September, after the Cascade Pass approach and Cache Col snowfields have opened up. North Cascades National Park does not allow advance high-country reservations for trips starting before July 1 because of lingering snow.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to nonexistent in the Cache Col/Yang Yang Lakes basin and on the summit; NPS guidance for North Cascades climbers is to never rely on a phone as the sole means of summoning rescue.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard scramble line. Parties typically carry a single 30 m (or half-length) rope for the Le Conte Glacier crossing, a couple of snow pickets, and ice axes/crampons for the glacier and any lingering snow on the approach gullies; the Class 3-4 rock above is normally climbed unroped by competent parties.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are limited to glacier-travel gear (pickets; screws optional) for crevasse hazard on the Le Conte Glacier and its late-season hard-ice section. The upper rock scramble is generally unprotected — parties wanting a belay on the exposed ridge finish should bring a small rack of slings/light gear.
whatToBring: North Cascades NP overnight wilderness permit
Bear canister (required for overnight backcountry stays in North Cascades NP)
Map/compass or GPS with the Ptarmigan Traverse route loaded
proTips: Treat Le Conte as a light, fast round trip from a Yang Yang Lakes camp rather than carrying full overnight loads over it.
Cross the glacier early in the day while any snow is firmer; by late summer it can be bare blue ice even on the modest-angle sections.
Aim for the obvious notch in the ridge skyline above the glacier, then follow ledges toward the true summit for the short exposed finish.
watchOut: Loose, blocky rock on the final scramble — wear a helmet and test holds.
The Le Conte Glacier melts to bare ice by mid-to-late summer; some parties rope up for the crossing even where the slope angle is modest.
A false summit and a narrow, exposed ridge crest just below the true summit have caught parties expecting a simple walk-up off guard.
knownHazards: Rockfall on the loose gully/ledge terrain below the summit ridge.
Crevasses on the Le Conte Glacier, especially early season.
Hard, bare blue ice on the glacier surface late in the season, increasing fall risk without crampons.
Remote, multi-day approach with little to no cell coverage, which extends any self-rescue timeline.

pitchDetail.notes: Snow/glacier approach from Yang Yang Lakes camp across the Le Conte Glacier; crevasse hazard early season, bare/blue ice possible by late summer.
Scramble broken, loose rock and ledges up toward a notch on the ridge crest — helmet essential.
Short, exposed ridge-crest finish to the true summit, past/near a false summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally to the Cascade Pass Trailhead, typically by late June/July once snow clears
road.seasonalGate: Gated at Eldorado Creek (mile 20) during late fall through spring, ~3 mi before road's end
road.driveNote: 23.1 mi from Marblemount (SR 20) to the Cascade Pass Trailhead; paved for roughly the first third, then gravel with narrow, steep sections near the end (~1 hr drive). When the seasonal gate is closed, add ~6 mi round trip plus elevation.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ301 (North Cascades Zone Area), per NPS guidance
climate.typical: Cool, wet, changeable weather typical of the North Cascades crest; high country is normally snow-covered until roughly July and can see sudden afternoon thunderstorms even in midsummer.

bySeason.spring: Deep snowpack and avalanche hazard; road/trail largely inaccessible above ~2,000 ft.
bySeason.summer: Mid-June through late September is the primary window; still showery at times, west side cooler/wetter than the east (Stehekin) side which can reach the 90s F.
bySeason.fall: Increasing storms and early snow possible by late September/October; shoulder-season access to Cascade Pass itself but the traverse becomes unreliable.
bySeason.winter: Heavy snow, road gated, significant avalanche terrain — not a winter objective.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness) for the Cascade Pass/Cache Col approach; Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests) for the peak itself
access.fees: No fee or pass required to drive Cascade River Road or day-hike to Cascade Pass
access.permit: NPS Wilderness/Backcountry Permit required for any overnight stay along the traverse — about 60% of sites reservable on Recreation.gov (2+ days ahead), remainder issued walk-up at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.passRequired: None for parking/day use; overnight backcountry permit required for camping
access.closures: Cascade River Road gated seasonally (roughly November-June) near mile 20 (Eldorado Creek); high-country camps (Cascade Pass, Cache Col area) cannot be reserved for trips starting before July 1 due to snow

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from Yang Yang Lakes camp to cross the glacier while snow/ice is firmer and to be off the exposed ridge before afternoon storms

sectionBreakdown.note: 3.7 mi, ~1,700 ft gain

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Cascade Pass → Cache Col → Kool-Aid Lake
sectionBreakdown.note: 3 hrs pass-to-col, 1 hr col-to-camp; ~2,800 ft gain for the day overall

sectionBreakdown.section: Kool-Aid Lake to Yang Yang Lakes
sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Kool-Aid Lake → Spider-Formidable Col → Yang Yang Lakes
sectionBreakdown.note: 3 hrs to col, 2 hrs descent, 3 hrs col-base to camp

sectionBreakdown.fromTo: Yang Yang Lakes ↔ Le Conte summit
sectionBreakdown.note: Round trip across the Le Conte Glacier and Class 3-4 ridge

emergency.county: Skagit County (trailhead/approach side); the summit sits on the Skagit-Chelan county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: 360-336-9450 (per NPS North Cascades climb-safely guidance); Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatch (alternate NOCA number): 360-384-5390
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — permits/info: 360-854-7245; park dispatch business hours 360-856-5700 ext. 515, after-hours Communications Center 360-873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III ED), 1415 E Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA — roughly 1.5-2 hrs drive from the Cascade Pass Trailhead; United General/PeaceHealth in Sedro-Woolley is closer to Marblemount
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in the Cache Col/Yang Yang Lakes basin and on the peak; carry a satellite communicator and never rely on a phone as the sole rescue plan. Call 911 or the numbers above with location, injury details, and terrain description.

routes.corrections: The given peak elevation of 7,773 ft does not match reputable sources: Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and ListsOfJohn all give 7,762 ft (USGS-derived) for Le Conte Mountain; used 7,762 ft for highPointFt. The route's technical grade (Class 3-4, glacier travel, alpineGrade PD as given) is consistent with all sourced descriptions and was not changed.
